⚙️ CHAPTER I — PRELIMINARY
§1 — Short Title, Extent and Commencement
⚖️ Section 1 — CGST Act, 2017
Short Title | Extent | Commencement
§1(1)
Short Title:
Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017
§1(2)
Extent:
Whole of India
(J&K originally excluded — now included w.e.f. 08.07.2017)
📝 Originally J&K was excluded. After the CGST (Extension to J&K) Act 2017, it now covers all of India including J&K.
§1(3)
Commencement:
Date notified by Central Govt in Official Gazette
Different dates may be appointed for different provisions
📝 The Act was not automatically effective from the date of Parliament's passing. Each provision came into force only on the date separately notified by the Government.
🔍 GP Singh: "shall come into force" + "on such date as may be notified" = commencement is contingent, not automatic. Effective date of each provision may differ — critical for limitation computation.
⚔️ Word War: "shall come into force" — "shall" is mandatory (the Govt MUST notify; it cannot not bring the Act into force). But the date itself is discretionary. Contrast with an Act that says "shall come into force immediately upon assent" — there, the date is not discretionary.
📋 Corresponding Rules: Rules 1–2, CGST Rules 2017 (Page 161)
§2 — Key Definitions | Part A: Clauses 1–30 (Litigation-Critical)
Clause Term Statutory Definition (Gist) Plain English 📝 Litigation Point ⚠️ GP Singh / Word War 🔍⚔️
§2(1) Actionable Claim Same meaning as §3, Transfer of Property Act, 1882 📝 A right to sue for money/debt that is not based on possession — e.g., lottery tickets, betting rights, insurance claims. ⚠️ Actionable claims are GOODS under §2(52). But most actionable claims are EXEMPT except "specified actionable claims" (§2(102A)) — betting, casino, lottery, online gaming — these attract GST at 28%. 🔍 Word imported from TPA — gives TPA meaning automatically. Courts may apply TPA jurisprudence to determine scope.
§2(4) Adjudicating Authority Any authority appointed to pass any order/decision — EXCLUDES CBIC, Revisional Authority, AAR, AAAR, NAAR, Appellate Authority, GSTAT, Anti-Profiteering Authority 📝 The officer who first decides a SCN — typically Deputy/Joint Commissioner. The exclusion list tells you who is NOT an adjudicating authority. ⚠️ Critical for jurisdiction challenges: if an order is passed by a non-adjudicating authority — it is without jurisdiction and void. Check whether the officer who issued the order is the proper officer. ⚔️ Word War: "does not include" — exclusions are exhaustive. Any body not in the exclusion list is an adjudicating authority.
§2(6) Aggregate Turnover All taxable supplies + exempt supplies + exports + inter-state supplies by persons with same PAN — computed on all-India basis — EXCLUDES tax, cess, and inward RCM supplies 📝 Your total business size across India under one PAN. Used to decide registration threshold, composition eligibility, and e-invoicing applicability. ⚠️ Inward supplies under RCM are EXCLUDED from aggregate turnover. Exempt supplies ARE included. This distinction is critical for composition scheme eligibility and annual return filing. 🔍 "Aggregate" connotes summation across all GSTINs of same PAN — not individual GSTIN-level. A company with 5 state registrations must sum all for threshold purposes.
§2(7) Agriculturist Individual or HUF — cultivating land — by own/family labour OR by servants on wages OR by hired labour under personal supervision 📝 Farmers who grow crops themselves or through family / hired labour — they are completely outside GST (no registration needed). ✓ Agriculturists are exempt from registration obligation even if turnover exceeds threshold — important for landowners renting agricultural land. 🔍 "Personal supervision" test — absentee landlords who only lease out land may NOT qualify as agriculturists. Supervision is a factual inquiry.
§2(11) Assessment Determination of tax liability — includes self-assessment, re-assessment, provisional, summary, best-judgment assessment 📝 Any process of calculating how much GST is owed — whether done by you (self-assessment) or by the department (various types of departmental assessments). ⚠️ Self-assessment u/s 59 is the primary mode. All departmental assessments (§73/74/74A, §75, §76) are secondary — triggered when self-assessment fails. Limitation periods attach to each type differently.
§2(17) Business Includes trade, commerce, manufacture, profession, vocation, adventure, wager, ANY similar activity — whether or not for pecuniary benefit — includes activities by Govt as public authority, club/society/association services, race clubs 📝 "Business" under GST is very wide — even a one-time adventure or wager is "business". Not-for-profit activities of clubs/associations are also business. Govt public authority activities are included. ⚠️ "Whether or not for a pecuniary benefit" — even charitable activities can be "business" if they involve supply for consideration. This is broader than the common law definition of business. 🔍 GP Singh: In taxing statutes, "includes" is expansive — the list in §2(17)(a)–(i) is illustrative, not exhaustive. Any activity resembling trade/commerce may be swept in.
§2(19) Capital Goods Goods whose value is capitalised in books of accounts AND used/intended to be used in course/furtherance of business 📝 Fixed assets that go into your balance sheet AND used for business — machines, computers, equipment. NOT capitalised stock-in-trade. ⚠️ TWO conditions BOTH must be met: (i) capitalised in books + (ii) used for business. If capitalised but not used for business — not capital goods. ITC on capital goods available (subject to §17(5) restrictions). ⚔️ Word War: "capitalised" vs "used" — Both are separately required. Capitalisation is an accounting test; "used for business" is a functional test. Failure of either condition = not capital goods.
§2(20) Casual Taxable Person Person who occasionally supplies goods/services in a State/UT where he has NO FIXED PLACE OF BUSINESS 📝 A Delhi-based businessman who occasionally sells goods at a Pune exhibition — he has no Pune office, so he is a "casual taxable person" in Maharashtra. Must take temporary registration before supply. ⚠️ Must register BEFORE supply (§24) — even if below threshold. Advance deposit of estimated tax required. Non-registration = ITC blocked for both supplier and recipient.
§2(30) Composite Supply Two or more taxable supplies — naturally bundled — supplied in conjunction — in ordinary course of business — ONE of which is principal supply 📝 Goods packed and transported with insurance = composite supply; supply of goods is the principal. The entire bundle is taxed at the principal supply's rate. ⚠️ "Naturally bundled" is a factual test — not a contractual choice. Artificially separating or bundling to manipulate tax rate is not allowed. Compare with §2(74) mixed supply (no natural bundling). 🔍 GP Singh: "naturally bundled" must be interpreted from the perspective of the ordinary recipient — not the supplier. Would the ordinary recipient expect these to be supplied together? That is the test.
Continued → Part B
📋 No separate Rules for §2 definitions — definitions apply throughout the Act
§2 — Key Definitions | Part B: Clauses 31–70 (Litigation-Critical)
◄ Back to Part A
Clause Term Statutory Definition (Gist) Plain English 📝 Litigation Point ⚠️ GP Singh / Word War 🔍⚔️
§2(31) Consideration Any payment (money or otherwise) made in respect of / in response to / for inducement of supply — by recipient OR by any other person — EXCLUDES govt subsidies

Proviso: Deposit is NOT consideration unless applied as such by supplier		 📝 Money or anything valuable paid for a supply — even if paid by a third party (e.g. employer paying for employee's services). But govt subsidies don't count. Deposits (like security deposits) are NOT consideration unless the supplier treats them as payment. ⚠️ "Any other person" — third-party consideration is still consideration. E.g., insurance company paying supplier on behalf of recipient = consideration. Also: non-monetary consideration (barter, contra services) is included. 🔍 GP Singh: "In response to" vs "in respect of" — wider than cause-and-effect. Even a voluntary payment made in appreciation of a supply already made can be consideration if connected to the supply.
§2(32) Continuous Supply of Goods Goods provided continuously/recurrently under a contract — via wire/cable/pipeline etc. OR as notified by Govt — with periodic invoicing by supplier 📝 Supply of electricity, gas, water through pipes — supplied all the time, invoiced periodically. Time of supply rules work differently for this category. ⚠️ Time of supply for continuous supply = date of invoice OR date of payment (whichever earlier) per §12(4). Failure to invoice periodically can push the time of supply to the last date of the contract period.
§2(33) Continuous Supply of Services Services provided continuously/recurrently under contract exceeding 3 months with periodic payment obligations — OR as notified by Govt 📝 Long-term service contracts — annual maintenance, software subscriptions, security services — where payment happens monthly/quarterly. ⚠️ "Periodic payment obligations" — if contract has no periodic payment clause, it may not qualify as continuous supply of services. Time of supply rules in §13(4) apply. ⚔️ Word War: §2(32) goods — "3 months" not required; §2(33) services — "exceeding 3 months" required. The asymmetry is deliberate — goods pipelines are always continuous regardless of duration.
§2(47) Exempt Supply Supply attracting nil rate OR wholly exempt u/s 11 CGST OR u/s 6 IGST — INCLUDES non-taxable supply 📝 Three types of exempt supplies: (i) nil-rated (0% in schedule), (ii) exempted by notification, (iii) non-taxable (alcohol, petrol — outside GST). All affect ITC reversal under §17(2). ⚠️ ITC on inputs used for exempt supply must be reversed under Rule 42/43. Misclassifying nil-rated as taxable (or vice versa) triggers ITC reversal demands. "Includes non-taxable supply" expands the definition — important for §17(2) calculations. 🔍 GP Singh: "includes" in definition — non-taxable supply is included within exempt supply for ITC reversal purposes even though non-taxable supply is conceptually different (not subject to GST at all, vs zero-rated).
§2(52) Goods Every kind of movable property other than money and securities — INCLUDES actionable claims, growing crops, grass, things attached to land agreed to be severed before/under supply 📝 Everything physical that can be moved — except money and shares/bonds. Also includes crops that will be cut, and rights like lottery tickets (actionable claims). ⚠️ "Things attached to land agreed to be severed" = standing timber (sale of trees to be cut) is goods, not immovable property. Contrast: uncut trees with right to enter land = profit-à-prendre = immovable property. ⚔️ Word War: "goods" vs "services" — §2(52) vs §2(102). The entire GST structure depends on this classification. Works contract — where both goods and services involved — is deemed service (§2(119)/Schedule II).
§2(59)/(60) Input / Input Service Input: Any goods (≠ capital goods) used/intended for use in business
Input Service: Any service used/intended for use in business		 📝 Raw materials, consumables = inputs. Professional fees, rent, freight = input services. Both attract ITC subject to §17(5) restrictions. ⚠️ "Intended to be used" — ITC available even before actual use, at the time of receipt of goods/services. But reversal required if not actually used for business purposes later.
§2(61) Input Service Distributor (ISD) Office of supplier receiving tax invoices for input services (including RCM services under §9(3)/9(4)) for/on behalf of distinct persons u/s 25 — liable to distribute ITC as per §20 [Amended w.e.f. 01.04.2025] 📝 The head office that receives invoices for services used by branch offices (distinct persons). It collects the ITC and distributes to branches in proportion to their turnover. ⚠️ Post-01.04.2025 amendment: ISD must now also distribute ITC on RCM inward supplies received on behalf of distinct persons. Earlier, this was done through cross-charge. ISD and cross-charge are now separate mechanisms. 🔍 GP Singh: "for or on behalf of distinct persons" — the ISD definition now has wider scope post-amendment. The phrase "on behalf of" includes situations where HO procures services for PAN-India use.
§2(62)/(63) Input Tax / Input Tax Credit Input Tax: CGST/SGST/IGST/UTGST charged on supply to registered person + IGST on imports + RCM tax payable u/s 9(3)/9(4) — EXCLUDES composition levy tax
ITC: Credit of input tax		 📝 GST you paid as a buyer — either directly on purchases or as RCM — which you can use to offset your output GST liability. Composition dealer's tax is NOT input tax — so no ITC chain. ⚠️ RCM tax paid IS input tax and qualifies for ITC (subject to conditions) — but only in the same or later month. ITC on composition levy paid = NIL. This affects subcontractor/GTA chains significantly.
Continued → Part C
§2 — Key Definitions | Part C: Clauses 71–121 (Litigation-Critical)
◄ Back to Part B
Clause Term Statutory Definition (Gist) Plain English 📝 Litigation Point ⚠️ GP Singh / Word War 🔍⚔️
§2(72) Manufacture Processing of raw materials/inputs resulting in emergence of a new product having DISTINCT name, character AND use 📝 Three-part test: new name + new character + new use. All three must be present. Mere processing without change in character is NOT manufacture. ⚠️ This definition matters for: (i) composition scheme manufacturer rate (1%), (ii) ITC reversal where goods are "manufactured" for exempt supply, (iii) Works contract distinction. 🔍 GP Singh: Conjunctive "AND" — all three elements must be present simultaneously. A product with new name but same character/use is not "manufactured". This is the classic Central Excise test imported into GST.
§2(74) Mixed Supply Two or more individual supplies — supplied in conjunction — for a single price — where it does NOT constitute composite supply 📝 A gift hamper with chocolates + dry fruits + beverages for a single price = mixed supply. Each item can be supplied separately. Taxed at the HIGHEST rate among all supplies in the bundle. ⚠️ Taxed at highest rate — unlike composite supply (taxed at principal supply rate). Identifying whether a supply is composite or mixed is the key battleground. Department often argues "mixed" (higher rate); assessee argues "composite". ⚔️ Word War: "mixed" vs "composite" — composite = naturally bundled (test: would recipient insist on all together?). Mixed = artificially put together at seller's choice. Rate consequence is enormous.
§2(75) Money Indian legal tender OR foreign currency + cheques, promissory notes, bills of exchange, LOC, drafts, pay orders, TT, money orders, PayOrder, electronic remittances — EXCLUDES currency held for numismatic value 📝 Cash and all instruments that function as cash. Money is excluded from the definition of "goods" and "services" — no GST on exchange of money itself (except forex conversion charges). ⚠️ Currency dealers: the exchange fee/spread charged on forex conversion = "service" under §2(102) (relating to use of money for conversion) — taxable. But the currency itself = money = not goods = no GST.
§2(84) Person Includes: individual, HUF, company, firm, LLP, AOP/BOI (inc. or uninc.), corporation, govt company, foreign body corporate, co-op society, local authority, Central/State Govt, society, trust, and every artificial juridical person 📝 "Person" for GST is extremely wide — includes trusts, societies, government bodies, even foreign companies. All these can be suppliers or recipients of supply. ⚠️ "Every artificial juridical person not falling within any of the above" — catch-all entry. Even new forms of legal entity (like LLPs at the time of TPA) are covered. A joint venture without separate legal personality may still be a "person" here. ⚔️ Word War: "person" vs "taxable person" (§2(107)) — "person" is broader; "taxable person" is a registered or registrable person. A person may be outside GST ambit entirely (e.g., agriculturist, small businessman below threshold).
§2(98) Reverse Charge Liability to pay tax by RECIPIENT of supply instead of supplier — under §9(3) or §9(4) or IGST §5(3)/5(4) 📝 Normally the seller pays GST. Under RCM, the buyer pays GST directly to the government. This applies to specific notified goods/services (§9(3)) and purchases from unregistered persons (§9(4)). ⚠️ RCM liability is SELF-ASSESSED — recipient must issue self-invoice, declare in GSTR-3B, pay through cash ledger (NOT ITC). ITC on RCM available only in the same month of payment. 🔍 GP Singh: "instead of supplier" — RCM completely replaces supplier liability. Supplier need not collect or remit. But buyer must prove payment of RCM tax to claim ITC — documentary burden is on buyer.
§2(102) Services Anything other than goods, money and securities — INCLUDES activities relating to use/conversion of money for which separate consideration is charged
Explanation: Facilitating/arranging transactions in securities = services		 📝 Services = anything that is not a physical product, money, or shares/bonds. The Explanation (added in 2018) clarifies that stock brokers / sub-brokers facilitating securities transactions are providing services — taxable. ⚠️ "Anything other than" is a residual definition — if it's not goods, not money, not securities, it's services. This catches: software downloads (now also goods if on physical media), cloud computing, IP licensing, etc. ⚔️ Word War: "goods" vs "services" for software — physical CD = goods; cloud subscription = services. For GST rate purposes, same software can attract different rates depending on mode of delivery.
§2(102A) Specified Actionable Claim Actionable claim in/by way of: betting, casinos, gambling, horse racing, lottery, or online money gaming [Inserted w.e.f. 01.10.2023] 📝 These six categories of actionable claims are TAXABLE at 28% on face value of bets/entry fees — unlike other actionable claims which are exempt. ⚠️ §2(105) — supplier of specified actionable claims includes platform operators. Even if the platform does not receive consideration directly, it is deemed the supplier if consideration flows through it. Full §105 proviso applies. 🔍 GP Singh: Deeming fiction in §2(105) proviso — "shall be deemed to be a supplier" is strict construction territory. Once the platform is deemed supplier, all GST obligations (registration, return, payment) apply to it.
§2(119) Works Contract Contract for building, construction, fabrication, completion, erection, installation, fitting out, improvement, modification, repair, maintenance, renovation, alteration or commissioning of ANY IMMOVABLE PROPERTY — wherein transfer of property in goods is involved in execution 📝 Any contract where you build/fix/improve something on land — and materials are also used — is a "works contract". It is treated as SUPPLY OF SERVICE under Schedule II Para 6(a), taxed at 18% (or 12% for residential). ⚠️ ITC on works contract service for immovable property blocked under §17(5)(c) UNLESS: (i) it is plant and machinery, or (ii) it is for further supply of works contract service. ITC on goods procured for works contract available. ⚔️ Word War: "immovable property" vs "plant and machinery" — if the construction is for plant and machinery (functional, not structural), §17(5)(c) ITC block does not apply. The characterisation of the asset determines ITC eligibility.
📋 §2 definitions — apply throughout the Act and Rules. No corresponding separate rules.
🏛️ CHAPTER II — ADMINISTRATION
§3 — Officers under this Act
🏛️ Section 3 — Officers Appointed by Government via Notification
(a) Principal Chief Commissioner /
Principal DG of Central Tax
(b) Chief Commissioner /
DG of Central Tax
(c) Principal Commissioner /
Principal Addl. DG
(d) Commissioner /
Addl. DG
(e) Additional Commissioner /
Additional Director
(f) Joint Commissioner /
Joint Director
(g) Deputy Commissioner /
Deputy Director
(h) Assistant Commissioner /
Assistant Director
(i) Any other class of officers as
the Govt deems fit
DEEMED OFFICERS
Officers appointed under the Central Excise Act, 1944 are deemed to be officers appointed under CGST Act — continuity of service, rank and powers
📝 When GST replaced Central Excise in 2017, the existing Central Excise officers automatically became CGST officers without fresh appointment — ensuring no disruption in administration.
🔍 GP Singh: Jurisdiction of an officer flows from notification under §3 read with §5. An order passed by an officer not properly assigned the function = without jurisdiction = void. Always verify the officer's rank and function assignment.
📋 No corresponding separate Rules for §3
§4 & §5 — Appointment of Officers & Powers of Officers
⚙️ §4 — Appointment | §5 — Powers of Officers
§4 — Board's Power to Appoint
§4(1) Board may appoint such persons as it thinks fit as officers — in addition to Government's notifications under §3
§4(2) Board may by ORDER authorise officers (clauses a–h of §3) to appoint subordinate officers below Assistant Commissioner rank
📝 The Board (CBIC) has independent power to appoint GST officers beyond what the Government directly appoints — and can delegate power to appoint field-level officers.
§5 — Powers of Officers
§5(1) Officer may exercise ALL powers under the Act — subject to Board's conditions/limitations
§5(2) Senior officer may exercise powers of all subordinate officers
§5(3) Commissioner may DELEGATE powers to subordinate officers — subject to conditions
§5(4) CRITICAL LIMITATION
Appellate Authority SHALL NOT exercise powers or duties of any OTHER officer of Central Tax — Appellate authority's powers are EXCLUSIVE to appellate functions
📝 The Appellate Authority (Commissioner Appeals) cannot play the role of the original adjudicating officer. It can only HEAR APPEALS — cannot investigate, issue SCN, or adjudicate in original jurisdiction.
🔍 GP Singh: §5(4) is a restriction on the powers otherwise conferred by §5(1)–(3). "Notwithstanding anything" opens §5(4) — this override clause makes §5(4) absolute. Appellate Authority stepping into adjudicatory role = ultra vires.
📋 No separate Rules for §§4–5. Powers assigned by Board circulars and notifications.
§6 — Authorisation of State/UT Tax Officers as Proper Officers
🔁 Section 6 — Cross-Empowerment: State/UT Officers ↔ CGST Proper Officers
📝 GST has both Central and State officers. To avoid duplication of proceedings, §6 allows each jurisdiction's officers to act as "proper officers" for the other's Act — but with a one-proceeding-one-subject rule.
§6(1) — GRANT
State/UT tax officers are authorised as proper officers for CGST purposes — subject to notified conditions
📝 A State GST officer can also issue orders/notices under CGST Act — they are deemed "proper officers" for Central GST as well.
§6(2)(a) — PARALLEL ORDER
If a proper officer issues order under CGST — he must ALSO issue corresponding order under SGST/UTGST — and intimate the jurisdictional State/UT officer
💡 When a CGST officer issues a demand order, he must simultaneously issue a demand under SGST — both Central and State taxes must be covered in the same adjudication.
§6(2)(b) — CRITICAL: ONE PROCEEDING RULE
If State/UT officer has INITIATED PROCEEDINGS on a subject matter → Central proper officer SHALL NOT initiate proceedings on the SAME subject matter
📝 If State GST officer starts investigating a transaction — Central GST officer cannot also start a separate investigation on the same transaction. This prevents double jeopardy and harassment.
🔍 GP Singh: "initiated any proceedings" — "initiated" is a wider word than "issued SCN". Even a summons, inspection, or letter of enquiry may constitute initiation of proceedings. The moment either jurisdiction "starts", the other is barred.
💡 State GST officer issues summons to examine books on ITC reversal claim → Central GST officer cannot issue separate SCN for the same ITC reversal in the same period. §6(2)(b) bars the second proceeding.
§6(3) — APPELLATE EXCLUSIVITY
Any rectification / appeal / revision against orders passed by CGST officers CANNOT lie before SGST/UTGST officers — appellate jurisdiction follows original jurisdiction
📝 If a CGST officer passed an order, the appeal goes to the CGST appellate authority — not to any State GST authority, even if State GST officers are cross-empowered for original proceedings.
📋 No specific rules for §6. Cross-empowerment governed by Notification No. 39/2017-CT and subsequent amendments.
📊 CHAPTER III — LEVY AND COLLECTION OF TAX
§7 — Scope of Supply (The Foundation of GST)
⚡ Section 7 — "Supply" — The Charging Concept of GST
The entire edifice of GST rests on this section
📝 GST is a tax on "supply". Unless there is a "supply", there is no GST. §7 defines what "supply" is. It has 4 sub-sections: §7(1) = what IS supply; §7(2) = what is NOT supply; §7(1A) = whether goods or services; §7(3) = Government power to notify.
📌 §7(1) — What Constitutes "Supply" — FOUR LIMBS
§7(1)(a)
All forms of supply — sale, transfer, barter, exchange, licence, rental, lease or disposal — FOR CONSIDERATION — IN COURSE/FURTHERANCE OF BUSINESS
📝 The main category. Two conditions: (i) for consideration + (ii) in business. Without both — not supply u/s 7(1)(a).
🔍 GP Singh: "or" between each mode (sale/transfer/barter/etc.) = disjunctive. Each mode independently constitutes supply. Even a "disposal" (e.g., writing off goods) may be supply if consideration is involved.
§7(1)(aa) [w.r.e.f. 01.07.2017]
Activities/transactions by a person (≠ individual) to its MEMBERS/CONSTITUENTS or vice-versa — for cash/deferred payment/other valuable consideration
Person and members = DEEMED two separate persons
📝 Club services to members, co-operative society services, association services to constituents — all are supply. Inserted with retrospective effect from 01.07.2017 to overturn court decisions that held member-club relationship as no supply.
💡 RWA charges maintenance fees from members — this is supply of services under §7(1)(aa). Members and RWA = two separate persons in law. GST applies on excess of ₹7,500/month per member.
§7(1)(b)
Import of services — FOR CONSIDERATION — whether or not in course/furtherance of business
📝 Importing services is supply even if NOT for business. E.g., an individual importing a personal service from abroad (Netflix subscription) — still GST supply. Business requirement is absent for import of services.
🔍 Word War: §7(1)(a) requires "in course of business" BUT §7(1)(b) import of services does NOT require this. The deliberate omission makes ALL import of services for consideration taxable regardless of purpose.
§7(1)(c)
Activities in Schedule I — WITHOUT CONSIDERATION — still treated as supply
(e.g., supplies between related persons, permanent transfer of business assets, principal-agent supply)
📝 Normally "supply" needs consideration. Schedule I creates exceptions — certain activities are supply even without payment. Key example: HO to Branch transfer of goods/services even without charging each other.
💡 Head Office at Mumbai provides marketing services to Pune branch (same PAN, different GSTIN = distinct persons under §25(4)). No inter-company charge. Still = supply under Sch. I Para 2. GST on deemed value.
§7(1A) — Where activities constitute supply under §7(1) → SCHEDULE II determines if it is supply of goods or supply of services
📝 Once something is a "supply" — Schedule II tells you whether it is goods-supply or services-supply. This matters for rate, place of supply, time of supply, and ITC eligibility.
⚔️ Word War: "shall be treated as" in Schedule II = deeming provision. Even if something is factually goods (e.g., software on disk), if Schedule II deems it as service — it is service for ALL GST purposes. Strict construction of deeming provisions applies.
§7(2) — WHAT IS NOT SUPPLY
(a) Schedule III activities — treated NEITHER as goods nor services supply
(b) Activities of Central/State Govt or local authority as public authorities — as may be notified
📝 Schedule III = Negative List under GST. These are activities that look like supply but the law declares they are NOT supply — e.g., services by employee to employer, funeral services, sale of land (Para 5), actionable claims (except specified).
💡 An employee's salary — however large — is NOT supply under Schedule III Para 1 (services by employee to employer in course of employment). No GST on salaries, perquisites, or ESOPs.
§7(3) — NOTIFICATION POWER
Govt may notify transactions to be treated as — (a) goods supply and NOT service supply, OR (b) service supply and NOT goods supply
🔍 GP Singh: §7(3) is a classification override power. It overrides the natural character of the supply. Such notifications must be strictly construed — if the transaction does not fall squarely within the notification's language, the natural classification prevails.
📋 No specific Rules for §7. Schedule I, II, III appended to the Act are operative directly.
§8 — Tax Liability on Composite and Mixed Supplies
📦 Section 8 — Tax Rate on Bundled Supplies
Two rules — one for composite, one for mixed
§8(a) — COMPOSITE SUPPLY
Taxed as supply of PRINCIPAL SUPPLY
Principal supply rate applies to the entire bundle
📝 Goods + packing + insurance + transport = composite supply. Principal supply = goods. GST rate for goods applies to the full value — you don't separately charge GST on packing or transport.
💡 Textile company supplies fabric + freight + insurance for one price. Fabric = principal supply. GST rate for fabric (5% or 12%) applies to entire bill. Transport alone would be 5% — here it follows fabric's rate.
🔍 GP Singh: "Principal supply" must be the supply that gives the composite supply its essential character. Department often argues the highest-rated component is the principal supply — incorrect. Principal supply = the element that defines the purpose of the entire transaction.
§8(b) — MIXED SUPPLY
Taxed at rate of the supply which attracts the HIGHEST RATE OF TAX
📝 Gift hamper with chocolates (18%), juice (12%), and dry fruits (5%) sold for single price = mixed supply. Tax at 18% on the entire hamper value because chocolate has the highest rate.
💡 Department challenges a maintenance service (12%) bundled with spare parts supply (18%) as mixed supply → entire bundle taxable at 18%. Assessee must prove natural bundling to classify as composite supply (rate = 12% on service).
⚔️ Word War: "particular supply which attracts the highest rate" — the taxing unit is the "supply" type, not the "item". Identify which supply (not which item) attracts the highest rate. If the supply type is works contract (18%), all items within it follow 18%.
📋 No specific Rules for §8. Classification determined by §2(30) and §2(74) definitions.
§9 — Levy and Collection of Central GST
⚡ Section 9 — The Charging Section of CGST
Intra-State Supply | Rate | Petroleum deferment | RCM | ECO liability
📝 This is the heart of the CGST Act — the section that actually imposes the tax. It says: there SHALL be levied a tax called CGST on all intra-State supplies of goods or services. Every other section in the Act is either in furtherance of this levy or provides an exception to it.
§9(1) — MAIN LEVY
CGST on ALL intra-State supplies of goods/services
Rate: not exceeding 20%
Excludes: Alcoholic liquor for human consumption + un-denatured ENA/rectified spirit for manufacture of alcoholic liquor [added w.e.f. 01.11.2024]
Payable by taxable person
🔍 GP Singh: "There shall be levied" = mandatory levy. No discretion. Once intra-State supply of taxable goods/services exists — tax MUST be levied. Exemption is the exception (u/s 11), not the rule.
📖 "Intra-State supply" — defined in §2(64) read with IGST §8. Supply where both supplier and place of supply are in the same State. IGST §8 determines what is intra-State for complex cases.
§9(2) — PETROLEUM DEFERMENT
CGST on petroleum crude, HSD, MS (petrol), natural gas, ATF — levied from DATE TO BE NOTIFIED by Govt
Not yet brought under GST as of date
📝 Petrol, diesel, natural gas, ATF are in the GST law but NOT yet taxed under GST — they continue under State VAT and Central Excise. GST Council must recommend the date to bring these into GST.
💡 ITC on petrol/diesel purchases = BLOCKED. Manufacturer buying diesel for factory generators cannot claim ITC — as diesel is outside GST ambit. This is a significant cost for many industries.
§9(3) — RCM: NOTIFIED SUPPLIES
Govt notifies categories of supply → Tax paid by RECIPIENT on reverse charge basis
All Act provisions apply to recipient as if he is the person liable
📝 For notified supplies (GTA, lawyer, advocate, sponsorship services etc.) — the recipient pays GST, not the supplier. Recipient issues self-invoice and pays from cash ledger.
💡 Company pays freight to a GTA (Goods Transport Agency). GTA does not collect GST. Company (recipient) must pay 5% GST under RCM on freight. Company can then claim ITC of that RCM-paid tax.
§9(4) — RCM: UNREGISTERED SUPPLIER
Notified class of registered persons receiving notified categories of goods/services from UNREGISTERED supplier → Pay tax on RCM basis [Amended 2018 — now narrowed to specific notified categories]
📝 Originally ALL purchases from unregistered suppliers triggered RCM on the registered recipient. This was amended in 2018 — now only notified categories (like real estate) attract this RCM.
💡 Builder (registered) procures cement/sand from unregistered dealer — if notified under §9(4) — must pay RCM on such purchase. If not notified — no RCM obligation.
§9(5) — ECO LIABILITY
Govt notifies categories of services → Tax paid by ELECTRONIC COMMERCE OPERATOR (ECO) instead of service supplier
If ECO has no physical presence in India → appointed representative pays tax
📝 For services like Ola/Uber (cab aggregators), Swiggy/Zomato (restaurant delivery) — the app (ECO) pays GST, not the individual driver/restaurant. If foreign ECO has no India presence, its Indian representative is liable.
💡 Ola collects ride fare — Ola (the app/ECO) pays GST under §9(5) for cab services. Individual driver/taxi operator need not separately register or pay GST for this category.
⚔️ Word War: §9(3) vs §9(4) RCM — §9(3) = based on nature of SUPPLY (e.g., all GTA services attract RCM regardless of supplier's registration). §9(4) = based on SUPPLIER's status (unregistered). Different triggers; different ITC implications; different self-invoice requirements.
📋 Corresponding Rules: Rules 3–7, CGST Rules 2017 (Pages 162–165) | RCM notifications: Notification 13/2017-CT(Rate) for §9(3); Notification 7/2019-CT(Rate) for §9(4)
§10 — Composition Levy
🔄 Section 10 — Composition Scheme
Small business alternate tax — simplified compliance, lower rate, NO ITC
📝 Small businesses below ₹1.5 crore (goods) or ₹50L (services) can opt for composition — pay tax at a low flat rate on turnover instead of normal GST. Trade-off: NO ITC can be claimed OR passed on.
Sub-Section Provision Key Condition / Rate Plain English 📝 Litigation Point ⚠️
§10(1) Eligibility — Goods Dealers Aggregate turnover in preceding FY ≤ ₹50L (extendable to ₹1.5 crore by notification). Rates: Manufacturer=1%, Restaurant=2.5%, Other suppliers=0.5% of turnover in State/UT 📝 A manufacturer with turnover below ₹1.5 crore can opt composition at 1% instead of 12%/18% normal GST. No ITC — but far less compliance. Only for intra-State supply. ⚠️ Proviso: Can also supply services up to 10% of previous year's State turnover or ₹5L (whichever higher) without losing composition eligibility. Interest/discount on deposits is excluded from turnover for this purpose.
§10(2) Eligibility Conditions — WHO CANNOT opt Cannot opt if: (a) supplies goods/services not leviable to GST, (b) makes inter-state outward supplies, (c) supplies notified goods, (d) supplies through ECO under §52, (e) not manufacturer of notified goods, (f) not casual/non-resident taxable person 📝 Six conditions — ALL must be met to be eligible. Even one violation (e.g., one inter-State supply) disqualifies entire composition eligibility. ⚠️ Multi-GSTIN same PAN rule: If one registration opts composition, ALL registrations with same PAN must opt. Selective composition is not allowed.
§10(2A) Composition for Service Providers
[Inserted by Finance (No.2) Act, 2019]		 Registered persons NOT eligible under §10(1)/(2) but turnover ≤ ₹50L — can opt at 3% of turnover in State/UT — subject to same conditions as §10(2) 📝 Small service providers (pure service suppliers — consultants, freelancers, small contractors) with turnover below ₹50L can opt for composition at 3% — no ITC, simple quarterly return. ⚠️ §10(2A) rate is 3% (compared to max 1% for goods dealers). Service providers who cross ₹50L must exit immediately — no grace period.
§10(3) Automatic Exit Option lapses from the day turnover EXCEEDS the limit during the financial year 📝 The moment you cross the threshold mid-year — that very day, you exit composition and become a normal GST payer. You must register under regular scheme and comply with all normal GST provisions. ⚠️ Must immediately start collecting GST on outward supplies. Cannot retrospectively claim ITC on stocks held at the time of crossing threshold (§18(1)(c) allows ITC on opening stock — but only prospectively).
§10(4) NO ITC / NO Collection from Recipient Composition dealer: (i) CANNOT collect tax from recipient, (ii) NOT entitled to any ITC 📝 Composition dealer bears the tax cost himself — cannot bill it to customer, cannot recover it from government. The entire composition tax is a cost to the dealer. ⚠️ Any recipient who purchases from composition dealer cannot claim ITC — composition dealer cannot issue a proper tax invoice. Purchases from composition dealers are a "stuck cost" for the buyer in the supply chain.
§10(5) Penalty for Wrongful Use If proper officer has reason to believe composition scheme opted despite ineligibility → tax + penalty → §73/74/74A applies mutatis mutandis 📝 If you fraudulently opt for composition while being ineligible — the officer can demand full normal GST + penalty. Normal SCN/adjudication procedures apply. ⚠️ "Reasons to believe" — the threshold for invoking §10(5) is a subjective but reasoned belief. Mere suspicion is insufficient — there must be specific material on record. Orders passed without such material are liable to be set aside.
🔍 GP Singh: §10(5) uses "reasons to believe" — same phrase as in §67 (search). This is a jurisdictional fact: the existence of valid reasons is a condition precedent to invoking the provision. Courts have held that reasons cannot be vague, generalised, or a mere reproduction of the section.
📋 Corresponding Rules: Rules 3–7, CGST Rules 2017 (Pages 162–165) | Composition notification: Notification No. 14/2019-CT
§11 — Power to Grant Exemption from Tax
🛡️ Section 11 — Government's Power to Exempt from GST
General Exemption | Special Exemption | Explanation Insertion
§11(1) — GENERAL EXEMPTION
Where satisfied that it is necessary in PUBLIC INTEREST → exempt goods/services of any specified description — absolutely or subject to conditions — by NOTIFICATION
📝 The main exemption power. Government issues a notification (Notification 12/2017-CT for services; Notification 2/2017-CT for goods) listing exempt categories. Must be in public interest — a policy decision, not an administrative one.
🔍 GP Singh: "Satisfied that it is necessary in the public interest" — this is the condition precedent. Courts have held that satisfaction must be real and not colourable. An exemption notification that is arbitrary or motivated by extraneous considerations can be challenged.
💡 Healthcare services by clinical establishments — exempt by Notification 12/2017. If a hospital charges for diagnostic services, it must prove the service falls within the exempt category's exact wording. Any deviation (e.g., cosmetic procedure) = taxable.
§11(2) — SPECIAL ORDER EXEMPTION
Under circumstances of EXCEPTIONAL NATURE (stated in order) → exempt by SPECIAL ORDER in each case
📝 For one-off situations — not a general exemption category but a specific case. The exceptional nature must be stated in the order itself.
§11(3) — EXPLANATION INSERTION
Govt may insert an EXPLANATION within ONE YEAR of original notification/order — to clarify scope or applicability
Explanation has effect as if it always formed part of the notification
📝 The government can retrospectively clarify an exemption notification by adding an Explanation — within one year. The Explanation is deemed to have been part of the original notification from day one.
🔍 GP Singh: Retrospective operation of Explanation — "as if it had always been part" is a legal fiction. Strictly construed: Explanation cannot expand the scope of exemption; it can only clarify ambiguity. An Explanation that creates a new condition = ultra vires.
§11 EXPLANATION — NO EXCESS COLLECTION
Where absolute exemption is granted → registered person CANNOT collect tax in excess of effective rate on exempt supply
📝 If a supply is 100% exempt — the supplier cannot still charge and collect GST from the customer. Any excess collection must be deposited to the government (anti-profiteering principle).
💡 A hospital wrongly charges GST on exempt diagnostic services and collects it from patients. This is impermissible — the hospital must refund GST to patients OR deposit to government under §76 (excess collection).
⚔️ Word War: "absolutely" vs "subject to conditions" — §11(1) allows both absolute and conditional exemptions. A conditional exemption: the condition is a mandatory prerequisite; failure to satisfy = no exemption, full tax. Conditions are to be construed strictly.
📋 No specific CGST Rules for §11. Exemptions via Notifications 12/2017-CT (Services) and 02/2017-CT (Goods)
§11A — Power Not to Recover GST Not Levied or Short-Levied Due to General Practice [w.e.f. 01.11.2024]
🛡️ Section 11A — Relief from Retrospective Tax Demand
Inserted by Finance (No.2) Act, 2024 | w.e.f. 01.11.2024
📝 This is a new provision modelled on the similar provision in the Central Excise Act. Where the entire industry has followed a particular tax practice (e.g., treating a service as exempt or at a lower rate), and the government later determines the correct rate is higher — §11A allows the Government to waive the differential demand. It protects honest businesses that followed general trade practice.
📋 Pre-conditions — Both must be satisfied:
CONDITION A
A practice was/is generally prevalent regarding levy of CGST (including non-levy) on any supply
📝 "Generally prevalent" = the entire industry/trade was following this practice — not just one or two businesses. A widespread, common, industry-wide practice.
📖 "Generally prevalent" — no definition in CGST Act. By analogy to Central Excise Act (§11C) precedent, courts have held that this requires evidence of widespread adoption: trade circulars, industry association practices, CA/tax consultant advice consistently given.
CONDITION B
Such supplies were/are liable to — (i) CGST not levied per the practice, OR (ii) Higher CGST than levied per the practice
📝 The practice resulted in either: (i) no GST being paid when some was due, OR (ii) lower GST being paid than what was actually due — but everyone in the industry did this.
RELIEF
Government may, on Council recommendation, notify that the unpaid/short-paid CGST on such supplies SHALL NOT be required to be paid — for ALL supplies where tax was not/short levied per the general practice
📝 In plain terms: Government can issue a notification saying — "We know the whole industry underpaid GST on this category due to common practice. We are waiving the arrears." This saves the entire industry from massive retrospective demands.
💡 Example: If the entire textile trade consistently classified a fabric (say, unstitched suit material) as exempt based on a particular heading, and later it is held taxable — §11A enables the Government to waive past demands if the misclassification was a general industry practice. Without §11A, the entire industry would face retrospective demands for 5 years back.
🔍 GP Singh: Deeming relief provision — strictly construed in favour of taxpayer (as it is a beneficial provision). The Government's satisfaction about "generally prevalent practice" is a jurisdictional fact — challengeable by Department if it tries to restrict the relief to fewer taxpayers than entitled. Also: §11A applies only to CGST (central tax) — corresponding SGST relief requires parallel State notification.
⚔️ Word War: "shall not be required to be paid" — this is an absolute waiver, NOT a conditional exemption. Once the Government issues the notification, the tax liability is extinguished. This is stronger language than "may be remitted" or "may be condoned".
📋 §11A inserted by Finance (No.2) Act 2024, w.e.f. 01.11.2024. Modelled on §11C, Central Excise Act 1944. No specific CGST Rules yet.
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal — CGST Act Sections 1–11A
Statutory arguments and principles for use in personal hearings. State them confidently with section reference.
Section 7(1)(aa) — Member-Club Transactions: Deemed Separate Persons
CGST Act, 2017 | Inserted w.r.e.f. 01.07.2017 | Finance Act, 2021
Key Statutory Principle
"Notwithstanding anything contained in any other law for the time being in force or any judgment, decree or order of any Court, tribunal or authority, the person and its members or constituents shall be deemed to be two separate persons and the supply of activities or transactions inter se shall be deemed to take place from one such person to another."
When to Use — When assessee (RWA, club, co-operative) argues that services to members are not "supply". Counter: Parliament has specifically deemed member and body to be two separate persons. There is no "mutuality" defence available post this insertion. The retrospective effect (from 01.07.2017) covers all past demands as well.
Section 6(2)(b) — One Proceeding Rule: Bar on Parallel Proceedings
CGST Act, 2017 | Chapter II | Administration
Key Statutory Principle
Where a proper officer under the State Goods and Services Tax Act or the Union Territory Goods and Services Tax Act has initiated any proceedings on a subject matter, no proceedings shall be initiated by the proper officer under this Act on the same subject matter.
When to Use — When both CGST and SGST authorities issue separate SCNs for the same transaction/period. Invoke §6(2)(b) immediately to quash the Central proceedings if State has already initiated. This is a statutory bar — not a discretionary stay. "Initiated" is a low threshold — even a summons may suffice.
Section 5(4) — Appellate Authority Cannot Exercise Adjudicatory Powers
CGST Act, 2017 | Chapter II | Powers of Officers
Key Statutory Principle
Notwithstanding anything contained in this section, an Appellate Authority shall not exercise the powers and discharge the duties conferred or imposed on any other officer of central tax.
When to Use — When a Commissioner (Appeals) steps beyond appellate powers and issues fresh findings on facts not part of original order, or remands with new grounds of demand not raised in original SCN. §5(4) bars this — any such exercise is ultra vires. The remand cannot add new charges; it can only re-examine original charges.
Section 9(1) — Tax Leviable on Intra-State Supply Only
CGST Act, 2017 | Chapter III | Charging Section
Key Statutory Principle
There shall be levied a tax called the central goods and services tax on all intra-State supplies of goods or services or both — at such rates not exceeding twenty per cent, as may be notified — and collected in such manner as may be prescribed — and shall be paid by the taxable person.
When to Use — When Department demands CGST on what is actually an inter-State supply (where IGST should have been paid). §9(1) charges CGST only on intra-State supply. Payment of IGST instead of CGST is wrong-head (not non-payment) — remedy is §77 CGST (refund of wrong head), not re-payment of CGST.
Section 8 — Composite vs Mixed Supply: Rate Determination
CGST Act, 2017 | Chapter III | §8 read with §2(30) and §2(74)
Key Statutory Principle
A composite supply comprising two or more supplies, one of which is a principal supply, shall be treated as a supply of such principal supply. A mixed supply shall be treated as a supply of that particular supply which attracts the highest rate of tax.
When to Use — When Department argues a bundled supply should be taxed at the highest component's rate (treating it as mixed supply). Counter: if the elements are naturally bundled in ordinary course of business and one is the principal, it is composite supply taxable at the principal rate. The onus is on Department to show lack of natural bundling before invoking §8(b) highest rate rule.
Section 11A — General Practice Relief: No Retrospective Recovery
CGST Act, 2017 | §11A inserted w.e.f. 01.11.2024 by Finance (No.2) Act, 2024
Key Statutory Principle
Where a practice was generally prevalent regarding levy of central tax (including non-levy thereof) on any supply, and such supplies were liable to central tax which was not being levied according to such practice, the Government may direct that the whole of the central tax payable on such supplies shall not be required to be paid in respect of the supplies on which the central tax was not, or is not being, levied, in accordance with said practice.
When to Use — When facing retrospective demand on a tax treatment that the entire industry has been consistently following (e.g., a particular classification, an exemption claim, a valuation method). Invoke §11A framework: document that the practice was "generally prevalent" and request the Government to exercise §11A power. Also cite the analogous Central Excise §11C precedents before the SC/HC.
Section 10(5) — "Reasons to Believe": Jurisdictional Threshold for Composition Demand
CGST Act, 2017 | §10(5) | Read with SC decisions on "reasons to believe" (ITO v. Lakhmani Mewal Das, AIR 1976 SC 1753)
Key Principle
"Reasons to believe" in a taxing statute is a jurisdictional fact — not a matter of whim or surmise. The existence of valid reasons on record is a condition precedent to invoking the provision. Reasons cannot be vague, generalised, or merely restate the statutory language. The proper officer must have specific, articulable material to form such belief.
When to Use — When order u/s 10(5) is challenged. Ask the Adjudicating Authority / Appellate Authority to produce the reasons on record that formed the basis of the "reasons to believe". If the file shows no such material — the order is without jurisdiction and liable to be quashed.
Section 2(47) — "Exempt Supply" includes Non-Taxable Supply: ITC Reversal Scope
CGST Act, 2017 | §2(47) read with §17(2) and Rule 42/43
Key Statutory Principle
"Exempt supply" as defined includes non-taxable supply. For purposes of §17(2) (proportionate ITC reversal on common inputs), both nil-rated supplies AND non-taxable supplies (petroleum, alcohol) must be included in "exempt supply" for the denominator computation.
When to Use — When Department computes ITC reversal under Rule 42 and excludes either nil-rated or non-taxable supplies from the "exempt supply" calculation. Invoke §2(47) — both categories are included in exempt supply — ITC reversal must be computed correctly considering all exempt supplies including non-taxable supply in the denominator.
For professional litigation use | Pawan Soni & Associates, Ichalkaranji
CGST Act — Ch.IV–VI (Sec.12–30)

⚖️ CGST Act, 2017 — Chapters IV, V & VI | Sections 12–30

Source: CGST Act, 2017 / GST Bare Law ICAI Nov 2025 | CA Pawankumar Soni, Ichalkaranji

Permitted / Valid
Prohibited / Blocked
Condition / If / Unless
Optional / Exception
Neutral Reference / Statutory
📝 Plain English
💡 Example
🔍 GP Singh Interpretation
📋 Rule Cross-Reference
📋 Index of Sections
Chapter IV — Time & Value of Supply
§12 — Time of Supply of Goods
§13 — Time of Supply of Services
§14 — Change in Rate of Tax
§15(1)–(3) — Value: Transaction Value & Inclusions
§15(4)–(5) & Related Persons
Chapter V — Input Tax Credit
§16(1)–(2) — Eligibility & Conditions
§16(3)–(6) — Special ITC Rules
§17(1)–(4) — Apportionment of Credit
§17(5) — Blocked Credits
§18 — ITC in Special Circumstances
§19 — ITC for Job Work
§20 — ISD Distribution
§21 — Recovery of Excess ISD Credit
Chapter VI — Registration
§22 — Persons Liable for Registration
§23 — Persons Not Liable
§24 — Compulsory Registration
§25 — Procedure for Registration
§26 — Deemed Registration
§27 — Casual / Non-Resident Taxable Person
§28 — Amendment of Registration
§29 — Cancellation / Suspension
§30 — Revocation of Cancellation
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal
§12 — Time of Supply of Goods
⏱️ Section 12 — Time of Supply of Goods
Liability to pay tax arises at time of supply
§12(1) Liability to pay tax on goods arises at time of supply as determined under this section
§12(2) — Normal Supply (Forward Charge): Earlier of —
§12(2)(a)
Date of issue of invoice
OR
Last date by which invoice ought to be issued under §31
🔍 GP Singh: "Shall" in §31 = mandatory obligation. Last date = law-determined deadline, not supplier's choice. Time of supply can be triggered even if invoice NOT issued.
§12(2)(b)
Date on which supplier receives payment
📝 Whichever happens first — invoice date or payment date — that is your time of supply. Most disputes arise when advance is received before invoice.
💡 Advance received on 1 Jan, invoice issued on 15 Jan → Time of supply = 1 Jan. GST liability arises on 1 Jan itself.
PROVISO — ₹1,000 Tolerance
If supplier receives amount up to ₹1,000 in excess of invoice amount → at supplier's option, time of supply for excess = date of next invoice covering that excess
📝 Small rounding differences up to ₹1,000 above invoice can be covered by the next invoice — saves the supplier from filing revised returns for minor overages.
EXPLANATION 1 "Supply" deemed made to extent covered by invoice or payment (whichever applies)

EXPLANATION 2 "Date of payment received" = date entered in supplier's books of account OR date credited to bank account — whichever is earlier
🔍 GP Singh / Word War: "Whichever is EARLIER" — legislature chose strictest trigger. Bank credit date can precede book entry date → bank date governs. Cannot delay TOS by delaying book entries.
§12(3) — Reverse Charge: Earliest of —
(a) Date of receipt of goods
(b) Date of payment (books of recipient / bank debit — earlier)
(c) Date immediately following 30 days from date of supplier's invoice / any other document
📝 If supplier issues invoice but recipient does not pay within 30 days — TOS automatically triggers on 31st day. Designed to prevent indefinite deferral of tax in RCM.
FALLBACK (§12(3) Proviso) If none of the above can be determined → date of entry in recipient's books of account
§12(5) — Residual Rule
If TOS cannot be determined under §12(2) or §12(3):
(a) If periodical return → date return is to be filed
(b) Any other case → date tax is paid
§12(6) — Interest / Late Fee / Penalty
TOS for addition in value by way of interest, late fee or penalty for delayed payment = date supplier receives such addition
📝 Interest received later on delayed payment — GST applies on that interest too. Time of supply = date the supplier actually receives such extra amount (not the original invoice date).
§12(4) — OMITTED w.e.f. 01.10.2025 (Finance Act 2025)
Earlier dealt with: Vouchers — date of issue (if supply identifiable) or date of redemption (otherwise). Now deleted.
📋 Corresponding Rules: Rule 47 CGST Rules | Pages 159 (Points to Note)
§13 — Time of Supply of Services
⏱️ Section 13 — Time of Supply of Services
§13(2) — Forward Charge: Earliest of —
§13(2)(a)
Invoice issued within prescribed period (§31):
Date of invoice OR date of receipt of payment — whichever earlier
📝 If invoice is issued on time (within 30 days for services), the earlier of invoice or payment triggers TOS.
§13(2)(b)
Invoice NOT issued within prescribed period:
Date of provision of service OR date of receipt of payment — whichever earlier
📝 If supplier delays invoice, TOS falls back to actual date of service. Penalty for late invoicing: you lose the benefit of invoice date.
💡 Service provided 1 Jan, invoice issued 15 Feb (after 30-day deadline) → TOS = 1 Jan (service date), not 15 Feb. Liability is from 1 Jan.
§13(2)(c)
Where neither (a) nor (b) applies:
Date on which recipient shows receipt of services in books of account
📝 Residual trigger — if no invoice and no payment, go by the recipient's books. Relevant for intercompany transactions.
PROVISO — ₹1,000 Tolerance (same as §12)
Excess up to ₹1,000 over invoice amount → at supplier's option, TOS = date of next invoice for excess
EXPLANATION
(i) Supply deemed made to extent of invoice or payment
(ii) "Date of receipt of payment" = entry in supplier's books OR credit to bank — whichever earlier
§13(3) — Reverse Charge: Earlier of —
(a) Date of payment (books / bank debit — earlier)
(b) Date immediately following 60 days from supplier's invoice / other document
[w.e.f. 01.11.2024: "where supplier is required to issue invoice"]
🔍 Key amendment: 60-day rule now applies only where supplier is required to issue invoice. Captures scenarios where no invoice obligation exists (e.g., unregistered supplier).
(c) — NEW w.e.f. 01.11.2024
Date of issue of invoice by recipient (in cases where recipient is required to issue invoice)
💡 RCM on legal services from advocate — recipient (company) issues self-invoice. TOS = date of self-invoice.
PROVISO 1 (§13(3)) Cannot determine → date of entry in recipient's books

PROVISO 2 — Associated Enterprises Supplier outside India → TOS = entry in recipient's books OR date of payment — whichever earlier
📝 For import of services from group companies abroad — even if invoice is delayed, TOS triggers on receipt entry or payment, whichever is earlier. Prevents deferral of IGST on intercompany service imports.
§13(5) — Residual Same as §12(5) — return due date or tax payment date
§13(6) — Interest / Late Fee / Penalty TOS = date supplier receives such addition
[§13(4) re: vouchers also omitted w.e.f. 01.10.2025 — Finance Act 2025]
📋 Corresponding Rules: Rule 47 CGST Rules
§14 — Change in Rate of Tax
📊 Section 14 — Time of Supply when Rate of Tax Changes
Overrides §12 and §13 — applies only during rate transition
📝 When the GST rate changes (say 5% → 12%), a transaction may straddle the change date — supply before change, invoice/payment after, or vice versa. Section 14 lays out which rate applies depending on when each event (supply / invoice / payment) occurred.
Scenario Supply? Invoice? Payment? Time of Supply (= TOS) Rate Applicable
§14(a)(i) Before change After change After change Earlier of: invoice date OR payment date New rate
§14(a)(ii) Before change Before change After change Date of invoice Old rate
§14(a)(iii) Before change After change Before change Date of payment Old rate
§14(b)(i) After change Before change After change Date of payment New rate
§14(b)(ii) After change Before change Before change Earlier of: payment date OR invoice date Old rate
§14(b)(iii) After change After change Before change Date of invoice New rate
⚠️ BANK CREDIT PROVISO Date of receipt of payment = date of credit in bank account. But if bank credit occurs AFTER 4 working days from rate change date → TOS shifts to date of bank credit (not earlier date). Designed to prevent manipulation via cheque timing.
📝 "Date of receipt of payment" for §14 = earlier of: entry in supplier's books OR bank credit (same as §12 Expl. 2).
📋 Overrides §12 / §13 | No separate Rule — apply §14 matrix directly
§15 — Value of Taxable Supply [Part A: §15(1)–(3)]
💰 Section 15 — Value of Taxable Supply
Transaction Value Rule + Inclusions + Exclusions
§15(1) — PRIMARY RULE: Transaction Value
VALUE = Transaction Value
= Price actually paid or payable for the supply
Conditions: (i) Supplier & recipient are NOT related, AND (ii) Price is the sole consideration
🔍 GP Singh — "sole consideration": If any non-monetary benefit flows from recipient to supplier (free supply of inputs, preferential treatment) — price is NOT sole consideration → §15(1) fails → §15(4) or Rules 27–35 apply.
§15(2) — Mandatory Inclusions in Value of Supply:
(a) Other taxes / duties / cesses / fees under any other law
(except GST Acts and Compensation Cess)
📝 E.g., Custom duty, excise duty on an element of composite supply — gets included in GST base value.
(b) Amounts supplier is liable to pay but borne by recipient and not included in price
💡 Freight payable by seller but paid by buyer on seller's behalf — taxable as part of supply value.
(c) Incidental expenses — commission, packing — charged at time of or before delivery
(d) Interest, late fee, penalty for delayed payment of consideration
💡 Moratorium interest, delayed payment charges — all form part of taxable value.
(e) Subsidies directly linked to price — excluding Central & State Govt subsidies
📝 Expl: Subsidy amount included in value of supplier who RECEIVES the subsidy (not buyer's value).
§15(3) — Discounts EXCLUDED from Value (if conditions met):
§15(3)(a) — Pre-supply discount
Discount given before or at time of supply AND duly recorded in invoice → Excluded from value
📝 Trade discount printed on invoice — simply not charged GST on discounted amount. Clean exclusion.
§15(3)(b) — Post-supply discount
Discount given after supply — excluded ONLY IF:
(i) Established in terms of agreement at or before time of supply, specifically linked to relevant invoices
(ii) Recipient has reversed ITC attributable to discount
🔍 GP Singh — Strict construction: Both conditions conjunctive ("and"). If recipient does NOT reverse ITC, supplier cannot exclude discount from value. This is a critical battleground in Volume discounts / year-end credit notes.
💡 Year-end volume discount credit note — supplier reduces liability, but if distributor fails to reverse ITC proportionately → §15(3)(b) conditions not met → demand on supplier for full GST.
Continued → §15(4)(5) & Related Persons
📋 Corresponding Rules: Rules 27–35 CGST Rules, 2017 (Pages 187–192)
§15 — Value of Taxable Supply [Part B: §15(4)(5) & Related Persons]
◄ Back to §15(1)–(3)
§15(4) & (5) — Alternate Valuation & Deemed Related Persons
§15(4) If value cannot be determined under §15(1) → determined as prescribed (Rules 27–35)
📝 When §15(1) fails (related parties, non-monetary consideration) → go to Valuation Rules. Hierarchy: Comparison Value → Computed Value → Residual Method.
§15(5) Specific supplies notified by Govt on Council recommendation → value determined as prescribed (notwithstanding §15(1) or §15(4))
📝 Notified supplies (e.g., lottery, alcohol, petroleum — when GST applies) have mandatory special valuation regardless of transaction value.
Explanation — "Related Persons" [§15(1) Explanation(a)]
#Relationship ConditionLitigation Note
(i)Officers or directors of one another's businessesCommon in group companies — cross-directorships
(ii)Legally recognised partners in businessPartnership firms — all partners are related
(iii)Employer and employeeAll employer-employee supply transactions fall under related party valuation
(iv)One directly/indirectly owns ≥25% voting stock of bothSubsidiary holding — common in MNCs
(v)One directly/indirectly controls the otherBroader than shareholding — control by agreement also covered
(vi)Both controlled by a third person (directly/indirectly)Common parent → both subsidiaries are related to each other
(vii)Together they control a third personJoint venture scenario
(viii)Members of the same familyFamily business transactions — always scrutinise valuation
"Person" includes legal personsCompanies, LLPs, AOP, BOI all covered
Sole agent / distributor / concessionaire = Deemed relatedEven if no shareholding link — exclusive distributor is "related" for GST valuation purposes
📋 Corresponding Rules: Rules 27–35 CGST Rules, 2017 (Pages 187–192)
§16(1)–(2) — Eligibility & Conditions for ITC
🔑 Section 16 — Eligibility & Conditions for ITC
The Gateway Provision for Input Tax Credit
§16(1) — ENTITLEMENT
Every registered person shall be entitled to take credit of input tax on supply of goods or services or both used or intended to be used in the course or furtherance of business
→ Credited to Electronic Credit Ledger
🔍 GP Singh — "Used or intended to be used": Future/intended use is sufficient — ITC not restricted to supplies already consumed. "Course or furtherance of business" = wide standard. Key battleground: whether expense has nexus to business activity.
§16(2) — CUMULATIVE CONDITIONS (All must be satisfied):
Clause Condition Plain English Key Litigation Issue GP Singh Node
§16(2)(a) Possession of tax invoice / debit note / prescribed documents Must have the invoice in hand. No document = no ITC. E-invoicing compliance — whether GSTIN mismatch invalidates invoice 🔍 "Possession" — physical OR constructive? Courts split on electronic records.
§16(2)(aa) w.e.f. 01.01.2022 Supplier must have furnished invoice details in GSTR-1 AND details communicated to recipient under §37 ITC is now document-linked to supplier's GSTR-1 filing. If supplier doesn't file → recipient's ITC blocked. Constitutional validity of §16(2)(aa) — penalises innocent recipient for supplier's default. Major litigation. 🔍 "Communicated to recipient" — §38 auto-populated data. If GSTN portal fails to communicate — does condition fail?
§16(2)(b) Goods or services actually received Physical receipt of goods / actual provision of services required. Bill-to-ship-to transactions — covered by Explanation (deemed receipt). Direct delivery to third party = deemed receipt by registered person.
§16(2)(ba) w.e.f. 01.10.2022 ITC communicated under §38 must not be restricted If GSTN flags the credit as restricted (based on department analysis), cannot claim it. New blocking mechanism — operationalised through §38. Scope of "restriction" is not fully defined in Act.
§16(2)(c) Tax actually paid to Govt — in cash OR through utilisation of eligible ITC Supplier must have actually paid tax. Uncollected/unpaid GST by supplier cannot be claimed by recipient. Fraudulent invoicing — if supplier collected GST but did not deposit → ITC denial to recipient + recovery u/§74. Hardship provision lacking. 🔍 "Actually paid" — strict construction. No fictional deemed payment. Circular trading exploits this condition.
§16(2)(d) Return filed under §39 Must have filed your GSTR-3B for the period. Filing after due date — ITC available only if within limitation u/§16(4).
⚠️ GOODS IN LOTS / INSTALMENTS PROVISO ITC available only on receipt of LAST LOT or instalment — not partial receipt.
180-DAY PAYMENT PROVISO [§16(2) 2nd Proviso]
If recipient fails to pay supplier (for non-RCM supplies) within 180 days from invoice date → amount equal to ITC availed shall be paid back with interest (§50)
Recipient can re-avail ITC upon subsequent payment to supplier. [3rd Proviso — re-availment right]
💡 Invoice dated 1 Jan 2024 → if not paid by 29 Jun 2024 → ITC must be reversed with interest. On payment made later → ITC can be re-claimed. Common issue in MSME creditor disputes.
Continued → §16(3)–(6)
📋 Corresponding Rules: Rules 36–45 CGST Rules, 2017 (Pages 193–213)
§16(3)–(6) — Depreciation, Limitation & Special ITC Rules
◄ Back to §16(1)–(2)
§16(3)–(6) — Additional ITC Restrictions & Special Situations
§16(3) — DEPRECIATION BAR
If depreciation claimed on tax component of capital goods under Income Tax Act 1961 → ITC on that tax component NOT allowed
📝 You cannot claim both: income tax depreciation on GST amount AND GST credit. Choose one benefit.
💡 Machine costs ₹100 + GST ₹18. If company claims depreciation on ₹118 (includes GST) → ITC of ₹18 blocked. If depreciation on ₹100 only → ITC of ₹18 available.
§16(4) — TIME LIMITATION
ITC not available after 30th November following end of FY to which invoice pertains OR filing of relevant annual return — whichever earlier
🔍 "30th November" substituted by Finance Act 2022 for earlier "due date of Sep return". Absolute time bar — no extension of time. Constitutional validity challenged in multiple HCs (burden of §16(4) on assessee).
💡 Invoice of FY 2023-24 → last date to claim ITC = 30 Nov 2024 (or date of GSTR-9 if earlier). After that, ITC permanently lost.
§16(5) — SPECIAL RELIEF FOR FY 2017-21 [Inserted by Finance (No.2) Act 2024]
Notwithstanding §16(4), for FY 2017-18, 2018-19, 2019-20 & 2020-21 → ITC can be availed in any return under §39 filed up to 30 Nov 2021
📝 Retrospective relief for initial GST years — recognising GSTN teething problems. ITC for those years saved if return filed by 30 Nov 2021. This section inserted to give effect to numerous court orders / amnesty.
§16(6) — ITC AFTER REVOCATION OF CANCELLATION [Inserted by Finance (No.2) Act 2024]
Where registration cancelled and later revoked → ITC not restricted on date of cancellation = eligible ITC
Can be availed in return filed up to:
(i) 30th Nov after FY of invoice (normal deadline), OR
(ii) Return filed within 30 days of revocation order (for period of cancellation)
— whichever is later
💡 Registration cancelled 1 Apr 2023, revoked 1 Oct 2024. Returns for cancellation period (Apr–Sep 2023) can be filed within 30 days of 1 Oct 2024 = by 31 Oct 2024 → ITC available.
📋 Corresponding Rules: Rules 36–45 CGST Rules (Pages 193–213)
§17(1)–(4) — Apportionment of Credit
⚖️ Section 17 — Apportionment & Blocked Credits
Restrictions on ITC when supply is mixed / partial
§17(1) — Business vs Non-Business Use
Goods or services used partly for business + partly for other purposes → ITC restricted to portion attributable to business purposes
💡 Company car used 60% for business, 40% personal → ITC allowed only on 60% portion. Rule 42/43 prescribes the calculation method.
§17(2) — Taxable vs Exempt Supplies
Goods or services used for taxable supplies (incl. zero-rated) + exempt supplies → ITC restricted to portion attributable to taxable & zero-rated supplies
📝 Common ITC reversal requirement — when a business makes both taxable and exempt supplies, common credits must be bifurcated using formula in Rule 42.
§17(3) — "Value of Exempt Supply" Includes:
• RCM supplies (where recipient pays tax)
• Transactions in securities
• Sale of land
• Sale of building (subject to Schedule II Para 5(b) caveat)
EXPLANATION [inserted vide 2018 Amendment + 2023 Amendment]
"Value of exempt supply" for §17(2) purpose does NOT include activities in Schedule III — EXCEPT:
(i) Para 5 of Schedule III (sale of land and completed building)
(ii) Such activities as may be prescribed under Para 8 of Schedule III
🔍 Word War — "Except" in the Explanation: Critical carve-back. Schedule III activities (non-supply) normally don't trigger reversal. But sale of land / completed building (Para 5) DO trigger reversal of common ITC. This is a deliberate legislative choice — real estate sector must reverse ITC for land/completed flat sales.
§17(4) — Special Option for Banks / NBFCs / Financial Institutions
Option to either:
(A) Comply with §17(2) — full proportionate reversal, OR
(B) Avail 50% of eligible ITC every month (remaining 50% lapses)

Option once exercised — cannot be changed in same FY
50% restriction does NOT apply to tax paid on intra-entity supplies (same PAN entities)
📝 Banks deal in both taxable (fee income) and exempt (interest income) services. Calculating exact bifurcation is complex. The 50% blanket option simplifies compliance.
Continued → §17(5) Blocked Credits
📋 Corresponding Rules: Rules 42–43 CGST Rules | "Plant and Machinery" definition at end of §17
§17(5) — Blocked Credits [Ineligible ITC]
◄ Back to §17(1)–(4)
Clause Blocked Item Exception (ITC Available) Litigation Note
§17(5)(a) Motor vehicles for transport of persons with approved seating ≤13 persons (incl. driver) ✓ Further supply of such vehicles
✓ Transportation of passengers
✓ Imparting driving training		 Passenger cars, SUVs — blocked unless used in above businesses. Company car = blocked ITC.
§17(5)(aa) Vessels and aircraft ✓ Further supply of vessels/aircraft
✓ Transportation of passengers
✓ Navigation/flying training
✓ Transportation of goods		 Airlines, shipping companies — ITC available. Corporate jets for executive travel — blocked.
§17(5)(ab) Insurance, servicing, repair & maintenance of motor vehicles / vessels / aircraft in §17(5)(a) or (aa) ✓ If vehicles/vessels/aircraft used for permitted purposes above
✓ Manufacturer of such vehicles
✓ General insurance supplier insuring such vehicles		 If car is blocked → insurance & servicing of that car are also blocked. Linked blocking.
§17(5)(b)(i) Food & beverages, outdoor catering, beauty treatment, health services, cosmetic/plastic surgery, leasing/renting/hiring of vehicles/vessels/aircraft (as in a/aa), life insurance, health insurance ✓ If inward supply used to make same category outward taxable supply
✓ Or as element of taxable composite/mixed supply		 Restaurant services purchased for employee canteen — blocked (unless restaurant business itself). Life/health insurance for employees — blocked (unless Govt mandates under law).
§17(5)(b)(ii) Membership of club, health & fitness centre No exception provided Gym membership, club fees — absolute block. No business nexus argument works.
§17(5)(b)(iii) Travel benefits to employees on vacation — LTC / HTC ✓ Where obligatory under law for employer to provide LTA provided voluntarily — blocked. LTA mandated by service law/contract — available if law mandates it.
§17(5)(c) Works contract services for construction of immovable property (other than plant & machinery) ✓ Input service for further supply of works contract service WCS for building construction — blocked. Sub-contractor receiving WCS from main contractor — available (it IS a WCS input for WCS output).
§17(5)(d) Goods or services for construction of immovable property on own account (other than plant & machinery) No exception — even if used in course of business Explanation 2 [Finance Act 2025, retrospective w.e.f. 01.07.2017]:
"Plant or machinery" in §17(5)(d) = "plant AND machinery" as defined in §17 Explanation.
Overrides all court judgments contrary to this — retrospective clarification.
🔍 CRITICAL — Retrospective amendment to nullify SC/HC judgments allowing ITC on P&M. Department argument now strengthened. Assessee must show item falls within "plant and machinery" definition in §17 Explanation.
§17(5)(e) Goods or services on which tax paid under §10 (Composition Scheme) No exception Composition dealers cannot claim ITC.
§17(5)(f) Non-resident taxable person — except on goods imported by him ✓ Imported goods only NRTP can get ITC only on goods imported — not on local services.
§17(5)(fa) w.e.f. 01.10.2023 Goods/services used for CSR activities under §135 Companies Act 2013 No exception CSR expenditure ITC blocked from Oct 2023. Prior period — contested. Retrospective effect denied by courts.
§17(5)(g) Personal consumption No exception Business entity using goods personally — blocked.
§17(5)(h) Goods lost, stolen, destroyed, written off, gifted, free samples No exception Inventory lost in fire — ITC blocked. Samples given to clients — ITC blocked. Reversal required if ITC already availed.
§17(5)(i) Tax paid under §74 [for demands upto FY 2023-24]
[amended by Finance (No.2) Act 2024 w.e.f. 01.11.2024]		 No exception Demand confirmed under fraud/suppression provisions — ITC of such tax also blocked. Earlier: also covered §129 and §130 demands.
§17 EXPLANATION — "Plant and Machinery" Definition
Apparatus, equipment, and machinery fixed to earth by foundation or structural support used for making outward supply. Includes foundation and structural supports.
Excludes: (i) Land, building, civil structures (ii) Telecommunication towers (iii) Pipelines outside factory premises
📋 Corresponding Rules: Rules 42–43 CGST Rules (Pages 193–213)
§18 — Availability of Credit in Special Circumstances
🔄 Section 18 — ITC on Opening Stock & Special Events
§18(1) — Entitlement to ITC on pre-existing stock (subject to conditions & restrictions):
§18(1) ClauseSituationITC Available OnCondition
(a) Person applied for registration within 30 days of becoming liable → registration granted Inputs in stock / semi-finished / finished goods on day immediately preceding date from which liable to pay tax Applied within 30 days
(b) Voluntary registration under §25(3) Inputs in stock / semi-finished / finished goods on day before grant of registration Voluntary — no turnover threshold crossed
(c) Registered person ceases to pay under §10 (exits composition) Inputs in stock / semi-finished / finished goods / capital goods on day before switching to normal scheme Capital goods: ITC reduced by prescribed % (Rule 40)
(d) Exempt supply becomes taxable supply Inputs relatable to exempt supply + capital goods used exclusively for exempt supply — on day before becoming taxable Capital goods: ITC reduced by prescribed %
§18(2) — 1 Year Bar
No ITC if invoice more than 1 year old from date of issue
📝 Even in special circumstances — if invoice is older than 1 year, ITC is denied. Absolute cut-off.
§18(3) — Transfer of ITC on Business Transfer
Merger / demerger / amalgamation / lease / transfer with transfer of liabilities → unutilised ITC in ECL can be transferred to new entity
📝 M&A transactions — ITC balance doesn't die with old entity. Transfers to acquiring entity along with liabilities.
§18(4) — ITC Reversal on Exit Events
Opts for composition OR supplies become wholly exempt → must pay amount = ITC on inputs/semi-finished/finished goods/capital goods (reduced by %) by debit in ECL/ECash
📝 Cannot enjoy ITC accumulated while in normal scheme and then switch to composition. Must reverse and pay back.
§18(6) — Capital Goods Supply
Sale of capital goods on which ITC taken → Pay higher of:
(a) ITC taken minus % reduction (as prescribed)
(b) Tax on transaction value under §15
💡 Machine bought for ₹100 + ₹18 GST, ITC ₹18 taken. Sold for ₹70 after 2 years. Calculate ITC after 5% reduction per quarter. Pay higher of reduced ITC or GST on ₹70.
📋 Corresponding Rules: Rules 40–44 CGST Rules
§19 — ITC for Inputs & Capital Goods Sent for Job Work
🏭 Section 19 — Job Work ITC
"Principal" = person who sends goods for job work (ref §143)
§19(1) — INPUTS
Principal entitled to ITC on inputs sent to job worker

§19(2): Even if inputs sent DIRECTLY to job worker (without first coming to principal's premises) — ITC available
📝 Manufacturer sends raw material directly from supplier to job worker — still gets ITC. Removes practical hardship of routing goods through own warehouse.
§19(3) — 1 YEAR RULE (Inputs)
Inputs sent for job work — NOT returned within 1 year of sending out →
Deemed supply by principal to job worker on the day inputs were sent out
📝 Inputs not returned in 1 year = treated as if sold to job worker. GST liability triggers on principal. Clock starts from date of sending (if via job worker: from date job worker received).
💡 Inputs sent 1 Jan 2024 → must return by 1 Jan 2025. Not returned → deemed supply on 1 Jan 2024 → GST on invoice value.
§19(4) — CAPITAL GOODS
Principal entitled to ITC on capital goods sent to job worker

§19(5): Direct dispatch to job worker also allowed (without bringing to principal's place)
§19(6) — 3 YEAR RULE (CG)
Capital goods NOT returned within 3 years → deemed supply on day sent out
📝 Longer window for capital goods (3 years vs 1 year for inputs) — recognising that tooling, dies, moulds may remain with job worker for extended periods.
§19(7) — EXCEPTION §19(3) and §19(6) do NOT apply to moulds, dies, jigs & fixtures, or tools sent to job worker
These can remain permanently with job worker — no deemed supply.
📋 Corresponding Rules: Rules 45 CGST Rules | Read with §143 (Job Work Procedure)
§20 — Manner of Distribution of Credit by ISD
