📤 Section 20 — Input Service Distributor (ISD)
Amended & Substituted by Finance Act 2024 — effective 01.04.2025
ISD — What is it?
An office of the supplier of goods/services which receives tax invoices for input services — for or on behalf of distinct persons (branches in different States/UTs under same PAN) → Required to be registered as ISD under §24(viii) → Distributes ITC to distinct persons
📝 HO receives common services (IT, legal, audit) on behalf of branches across India. HO registers as ISD, collects ITC and distributes it to branches in correct proportions.
§20(1) [w.e.f. 01.04.2025]
ISD office shall be required to register — mandatory, not optional
Distributes ITC of invoices received including RCM supplies (§9(3) / §9(4) of CGST Act and §5(3)/§5(4) of IGST Act)
§20(2) — Distribution Rules:
• Credit shall be distributed to distinct persons registered in the same State as ISD
• In prescribed manner, within prescribed time, subject to restrictions
• CT distributed as CT or IGST; IGST distributed as IGST or CT
📝 HO in Mumbai distributes to Mumbai branches only (same State). Cross-state distribution done via IGST mechanism.
§20(3) Credit of CGST distributed as CGST or IGST; IGST distributed as IGST or CGST — by issue of document containing ITC amount
🔍 Amendment significance: Earlier ISD only distributed common service credits. From 01.04.2025 — also distributes RCM credits received at HO level. Major operational change for large corporates. Pro-rata formula based on turnover of recipients remains (via Rules).
📋 Corresponding Rules: Rules 39 CGST Rules | Old §20 = pre-2024 rules; New §20 = w.e.f. 01.04.2025
§21 — Recovery of Excess ISD Credit
⚠️ Section 21 — Recovery of Excess Credit Distributed by ISD
TRIGGER ISD distributes credit in contravention of §20 → excess distribution to one or more recipients
CONSEQUENCE Excess credit recovered from such recipients along with interest
Provisions of §73 or §74 or §74A (as the case may be) apply mutatis mutandis for determination of amount to be recovered
📝 "Mutatis mutandis" = with necessary modifications. §73 = non-fraud cases, §74 = fraud cases, §74A = new provision for FY 2024-25 onwards. ISD mistakes = recipient's liability. §74A inserted by Finance (No.2) Act 2024 w.e.f. 01.11.2024.
🔍 GP Singh — "mutatis mutandis": Does not mean §73/§74 apply verbatim — only applicable provisions apply with modifications. Penalties and extended limitation under §74 should not automatically apply to innocent ISD distribution errors.
📋 Corresponding Rules: Rules 36–45 CGST Rules
§22 — Persons Liable for Registration
📋 Section 22 — Who Must Register?
Turnover-based registration threshold
§22(1) — General Rule
Every supplier making taxable supply from any State/UT (non-special category) →
Aggregate turnover in FY exceeds ₹20 lakhs → liable for registration
📝 ₹20 lakh = turnover threshold for normal states. All taxable + exempt + export + inter-state supplies of taxable person across India counted together.
Special Category States
Threshold = ₹10 lakhs

Government may enhance to ₹20 lakhs at State's request (Proviso 1)

For exclusive goods suppliers — threshold can be enhanced to ₹40 lakhs at State's request (Proviso 2)
📝 "Exclusively in supply of goods" — even if engaged in exempt services by way of interest/discount on deposits/loans, still treated as exclusive goods supplier.
Other Liability Triggers under §22:
§22(2)
Registered under existing law (pre-GST) on appointed day → liable to register under GST within appointed day
§22(3)
Business transferred as going concern (succession/transfer) → transferee/successor liable to register from date of transfer
§22(4)
Amalgamation/demerger by HC/Tribunal order → transferee liable to register from date Registrar of Companies issues certificate of incorporation
EXPLANATION — "Aggregate Turnover" [§22 Explanation]
(i) Includes all supplies by taxable person — own account + on behalf of all principals
(ii) Job worker's supply of goods after job work = supply of principal (§143) — NOT included in job worker's turnover
(iii) "Special category States" = States under Art. 279A(4)(g) of Constitution — except J&K
[Includes: Manipur, Mizoram, Nagaland, Tripura, Arunachal Pradesh, Assam, Himachal Pradesh, Meghalaya, Sikkim, Uttarakhand]
🔍 Word War — "aggregate" in §22: Legislature chose "aggregate" not "taxable" turnover for threshold. Even exempt supply, non-taxable supply counts for crossing ₹20L threshold. But once registered → ITC restricted to taxable portion (§17(2)).
📋 Corresponding Rules: Rules 8–26 CGST Rules (Pages 166–186)
§23 — Persons Not Liable for Registration
🚫 Section 23 — Exemption from Registration
§23(1)(a)
Person engaged exclusively in supply of goods/services that are:
(i) Not liable to tax, OR
(ii) Wholly exempt from tax
📝 If your entire business is making exempted or nil-rated supplies — no registration needed regardless of turnover. E.g., exempt healthcare, exempt education.
🔍 "Exclusively" — even one taxable supply (even accidental) can destroy this exemption. Strict construction: not partially exempt.
§23(1)(b)
Agriculturist — to the extent of supply of produce out of cultivation of land
📝 Farmer selling own farm produce — no GST registration needed. But if farmer processes or trades in others' produce — that portion may become taxable.
§23(2) [Substituted by Finance Act 2023 w.e.f. 01.10.2023]
Notwithstanding §22(1) and §24 — Govt may on Council's recommendation, by notification, specify category of persons exempted from obtaining registration subject to conditions and restrictions
📝 Earlier wording was simpler. New wording explicitly overrides even compulsory registration (§24) — gives Govt power to exempt even persons who would otherwise compulsorily register (e.g., small e-commerce suppliers notified in Oct 2023).
🔍 §23(2) overrides §24 — interpretation: Can Govt use §23(2) to exempt persons mandatorily registrable under §24? New wording says yes. Earlier — debatable. The "notwithstanding §24" language is a deliberate amendment to settle this.
📋 Corresponding Rules: Rules 8–26 CGST Rules
§24 — Compulsory Registration in Certain Cases
⚠️ Section 24 — Mandatory Registration
Regardless of turnover threshold — these persons MUST register
#Category of PersonPlain EnglishLitigation Note
(i)Persons making any inter-State taxable supplyEven ₹1 of inter-state supply → must register. No threshold.Exception: Small suppliers of services exempted by Notification.
(ii)Casual taxable persons making taxable supplyOccasional business in a State where no fixed establishmentApply 5 days before commencement. Advance tax deposit required (§27(2)).
(iii)Persons required to pay tax under reverse chargeRCM payer must register — even if own supplies are exempt/nil.Common issue: Unregistered service recipients (exempt from §22) who receive RCM supplies.
(iv)Persons required to pay tax under §9(5)E-commerce operators collecting/paying tax on behalf of suppliers
(v)Non-resident taxable personsForeign persons supplying in India without fixed establishmentRegister on advance tax deposit basis.
(vi)TDS deductors under §51Government, PSUs, local bodies deducting TDSMandatory even if no outward supply.
(vii)Agents making taxable supply on behalf of principalsDel credere agents, commission agents — must register
(viii)Input Service Distributor (ISD)HO distributing common service credits to branchesSeparate ISD registration alongside regular registration.
(ix)Persons supplying through ECO required to collect TCS under §52E-commerce suppliers (Amazon, Flipkart sellers) — must registerException: Small sellers exempted by §23(2) notification from Oct 2023.
(x)Every Electronic Commerce Operator required to collect TCS under §52Amazon, Flipkart, Swiggy etc. — mandatory registration
(xi)OIDAR service providers from outside India to non-registered persons in IndiaNetflix, Spotify, LinkedIn, online courses from abroadRegister in India despite no physical presence.
(xia) w.e.f. 01.10.2023Person supplying online money gaming from outside IndiaOnline gaming platforms abroad targeting Indian usersNew category added by CGST Amendment Act 2023 — addresses offshore online gaming (28% GST on full face value).
(xii)Any other person notified by Govt on Council recommendationResidual category for future notifications
📋 Corresponding Rules: Rules 8–26 CGST Rules (Pages 166–186)
§25 — Procedure for Registration
📝 Section 25 — Registration Procedure, Single/Multiple Registrations & Aadhaar Authentication
§25(1)
Apply within 30 days from becoming liable
Casual/NRTP: at least 5 days prior to business commencement
SEZ unit/developer: separate registration from non-SEZ business in same State
📝 30-day window from liability date. Territorial waters: register in coastal State nearest to baseline.
§25(2) — Single Registration per State
Single registration per State/UT

PROVISO Multiple places of business in same State/UT → separate registration for each place permitted (subject to conditions)
🔍 Amended from "business vertical" (pre-2019) to "place of business" — broader scope for separate registrations. Each registration = distinct person under §25(4).
§25(3) — Voluntary Registration
Person below threshold may voluntarily register
All provisions applicable to registered person apply
💡 Small vendor below ₹20L threshold voluntarily registers to give ITC benefit to B2B customers.
§25(4) — Distinct Persons
Multiple registrations (same or different States) → each registration = distinct person for GST purposes
§25(5) — Distinct Establishments
Establishment in one State + establishment in another State = distinct persons
Aadhaar Authentication Requirements [§25(6A)–(6D)]:
§25(6A) Every registered person shall undergo Aadhaar authentication or furnish proof of possession of Aadhaar number
Failure → registration deemed invalid → person treated as unregistered

§25(6B) Every individual applicant → Aadhaar authentication mandatory from notified date
§25(6C) Other persons (companies, LLPs etc.) → Aadhaar authentication of Karta/MD/WTD/Partners/Members etc. from notified date
§25(6D) Govt may exempt specified persons/States/UTs from §25(6A)/(6B)/(6C)
🔍 §25(6A) — "deemed invalid": Deeming fiction — strict construction. Registration not cancelled (different process under §29) but "deemed invalid". What is the consequence? Treated as unregistered → all supplies = unregistered supplier's liability? Active litigation on this point.
§25(6) PAN mandatory for registration
Exception: TDS deductor may use TAN
§25(7) NRTP: registration on basis of other documents (no PAN requirement)
§25(8) Proper officer may suo motu register person who failed to apply
§25(9) UN agencies, Consulates, Embassy → Unique Identity Number (UIN) for refund of taxes
§25(10)–(12) Registration/UIN granted or rejected in prescribed period. Deemed granted on expiry if no deficiency communicated. Certificate issued in prescribed form.
📋 Corresponding Rules: Rules 8–26 CGST Rules (Pages 166–186)
§26 — Deemed Registration
🔄 Section 26 — Cross-Act Registration Parity
§26(1) — Deemed Grant
Grant of registration under SGST or UTGST Act = deemed granted under CGST Act
(Subject to: application not rejected under CGST within §25(10) period)
📝 If State GST registers you → Central GST also registers you simultaneously. No separate Central registration application needed.
§26(2) — Deemed Rejection
Rejection under SGST/UTGST = deemed rejection under CGST also
📝 Mirror principle — State rejection blocks Central registration too. Single window system — one acceptance/rejection applies to both Acts.
📋 Corresponding Rules: Rules 9–10 CGST Rules
§27 — Casual Taxable Person & Non-Resident Taxable Person
🗓️ Section 27 — Special Provisions for CTP & NRTP
§27(1) — Validity
Certificate valid for: period specified in application OR 90 days from effective date — whichever is earlier

Extension: Proper officer may extend by further 90 days on sufficient cause

Taxable supplies allowed only after certificate issued
§27(2) — Advance Deposit Mandatory
At time of registration application → CTP/NRTP must deposit advance tax = estimated tax liability for registration period

Extension sought → additional advance deposit = estimated tax for extension period
📝 No advance deposit = no registration. Designed to ensure tax compliance from temporary/visiting business operators. Amount credited to Electronic Cash Ledger.
💡 Foreign company participating in India trade fair registers as NRTP. Estimates GST liability of ₹5L for 15-day fair → deposits ₹5L upfront before registration granted.
§27(3) — Credit to ECash Ledger
Advance deposited → credited to Electronic Cash Ledger → utilised as per §49
📋 Corresponding Rules: Rules 13–16 CGST Rules
§28 — Amendment of Registration
✏️ Section 28 — Amendment of Registration Particulars
§28(1) — Obligation to inform
Every registered person must inform proper officer of any change in registration information — in prescribed form, manner & period
§28(2) — Officer's Action
Proper officer may approve or reject amendments in prescribed manner
Proviso 1: Certain minor particulars (as prescribed) — no prior approval needed
Proviso 2: Cannot reject without opportunity of being heard (natural justice)
§28(3) — Cross-Act Parity
Rejection/approval under SGST/UTGST = deemed rejection/approval under CGST
📝 Key change: Additional place of business, trade name change, partner change etc. — inform within prescribed time. Delay in informing can lead to consequences under §29(2)(a) (contravention of rules).
📋 Corresponding Rules: Rules 19–20 CGST Rules
§29 — Cancellation / Suspension of Registration
❌ Section 29 — Cancellation & Suspension of Registration
§29(1) — Voluntary Cancellation (Application by Taxpayer or Legal Heirs):
(a) Business discontinued, transferred, amalgamated, demerged or disposed of
(b) Change in constitution of business
(c) Person no longer liable under §22 / §24 OR intends to opt out of voluntary registration
§29(1) PROVISO During pendency of cancellation proceedings — registration may be suspended in prescribed manner
§29(2) — Suo Motu Cancellation by Proper Officer:
(a) Contravened provisions of Act or Rules (as prescribed)
(b) Composition dealer — not filed return for FY beyond 3 months from due date
(c) Other registered persons — not filed returns for such continuous tax period as may be prescribed
(d) Voluntary registrant — not commenced business within 6 months from registration
(e) Registration obtained by fraud, wilful misstatement or suppression of facts
§29(2) Proviso (Natural Justice) Proper officer shall NOT cancel without giving opportunity of being heard

2nd Proviso: During pendency → proper officer may suspend registration in prescribed manner
🔍 "Fraud, wilful misstatement, suppression" in §29(2)(e): Same language as §74. These are threshold words for coercive action. "Suppression" = deliberate concealment, not mere non-disclosure. "Wilful" imports mens rea. Cancellation u/§29(2)(e) can only be for deliberate wrongdoing — not procedural lapses.
§29(3)
Cancellation does NOT affect liability to pay tax and dues for period prior to cancellation
📝 Cancellation is not amnesty. All past liabilities survive.
§29(4)
Cancellation under SGST/UTGST = deemed cancellation under CGST
§29(5) — ITC Reversal on Cancellation
Must pay higher of:
(i) ITC on inputs in stock / semi-finished / finished goods / capital goods / P&M on day before cancellation
(ii) Output tax payable on such goods
💡 Stock of finished goods: ITC availed = ₹10L, output tax on same goods = ₹12L → pay ₹12L (higher). For capital goods — pay ITC minus % reduction OR tax on transaction value — whichever higher.
📋 Corresponding Rules: Rules 20–22 CGST Rules
§30 — Revocation of Cancellation of Registration
🔄 Section 30 — Getting Registration Back After Cancellation
§30(1) — Who Can Apply
Any registered person whose registration is cancelled by proper officer on his own motion may apply for revocation

Manner, time & conditions as prescribed
[Amended by Finance Act 2023 — removed fixed 30-day time limit, replaced with "such time as prescribed"]
📝 Only suo motu cancelled registrations can be revoked by this section. Voluntary cancellation under §29(1) → no §30 revocation (person chose to cancel).
🔍 "On his own motion" — cancellation under §29(2) only. If person's cancellation was pursuant to application under §29(1) — §30 remedy not available. Jurisdiction point in revocation matters.
§30(2) — Officer's Disposition
Proper officer may revoke or reject in prescribed manner and period

Proviso 1: Application cannot be rejected without giving applicant opportunity of being heard

Proviso 2 [Finance (No.2) Act 2024 w.e.f. 01.11.2024]: Revocation subject to conditions and restrictions as may be prescribed
§30(3) — Cross-Act Parity
Revocation under SGST/UTGST = deemed revocation under CGST
PRACTICAL NOTE Non-filing of returns + accumulated demand → cancellation → revocation possible ONLY after pending returns are filed and dues paid. GSTN portal conditions are linked to statutory compliance.
💡 See §16(6) — ITC eligibility post-revocation. Once revoked, returns for cancellation period can be filed within 30 days of revocation order and ITC claimed therein.
📋 Corresponding Rules: Rules 23–26 CGST Rules (Pages 166–186)
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal — CGST Act | Sections 12–30
These statutory ratios and principles are for use in personal hearings before GST authorities, Appellate Authority & GSTAT. State them confidently and cite the section.
Section 16(2)(aa) — ITC Restriction on Supplier's Non-Filing
Statutory Provision | CGST Act, 2017 | w.e.f. 01.01.2022 | Major Constitutional Challenge Pending
Key Principle
Section 16(2)(aa) conditions ITC availability of a bona fide recipient on supplier's GSTR-1 filing. An innocent recipient is penalised for a default that is entirely within supplier's control. Multiple High Courts have held that denial of ITC to innocent buyers for supplier's fault violates Article 14 (equality) and Article 300A (property rights). The Legislature has not provided any mechanism for recipient to compel supplier to file returns.
When to Use — When Department denies ITC solely on ground that supplier did not file GSTR-1 or details not reflecting in GSTR-2B, and recipient has all other evidence (invoice, payment proof, goods receipt). Cite High Court decisions challenging constitutional validity of §16(2)(aa) in relevant jurisdiction.
Section 16(4) — Time Limit for ITC: Absolute Bar vs. Procedural Restriction
Statutory Provision | CGST Act | 30th November Deadline | Constitutionality Challenged
Key Principle
Section 16(4) imposes an absolute time bar — ITC must be claimed by 30th November following the financial year or annual return date, whichever is earlier. The provision operates as a penal provision: genuine ITC earned through legitimate transactions is permanently forfeited for a mere procedural delay. Several High Courts have held that a provision which results in permanent forfeiture of legitimately earned ITC, without any mechanism for extension or condonation, is disproportionate and potentially violates fundamental rights.
When to Use — When ITC denied solely on ground of §16(4) time limitation where underlying transaction and tax payment are undisputed. Argue: (i) genuine hardship / system errors; (ii) §16(5) relief for initial years; (iii) HC/SC directions in jurisdiction. Also: §16(6) relief where registration was cancelled/revoked.
Section 17(5)(d) — Blocked Credit on Construction: Plant and Machinery Exception
Statutory Provision | Finance Act 2025 Retrospective Amendment | w.e.f. 01.07.2017
Key Principle (Post Finance Act 2025)
Section 17(5)(d) blocks ITC on goods and services for construction of immovable property on own account — except for "plant and machinery" as defined in the Explanation to §17. Explanation 2 (inserted by Finance Act 2025, effective retrospectively from 01.07.2017) clarifies that "plant or machinery" in §17(5)(d) must always be construed as "plant and machinery" — overriding any contrary court judgment, decree or order. The definition requires: (a) apparatus/equipment/machinery (b) fixed to earth by foundation/structural support (c) used for making outward supply (d) not being land/building/civil structures/telecom towers/outside-factory pipelines.
When to Use — When claiming ITC on factory machinery, industrial equipment embedded in factory floor. Argue: item qualifies as "plant and machinery" under §17 Explanation — ITC available. Counter-argue to Department's reliance on pre-2025 court judgments: retrospective amendment overrides those judgments.
Section 15(3)(b) — Post-Supply Discounts: Strict Dual Conditions
Statutory Provision | CGST Act §15(3)(b) | Value Disputes in Distribution Network
Key Principle
Section 15(3)(b) allows exclusion of post-supply discounts from taxable value only if both conditions are met conjunctively: (i) discount established in terms of agreement at or before time of supply, specifically linked to relevant invoices, AND (ii) recipient has reversed ITC attributable to the discount based on supplier's documentation. Failure of either condition — particularly recipient's ITC reversal — means supplier cannot reduce GST liability through credit note. The "and" between the two conditions is conjunctive, not alternative.
When to Use — When Department challenges year-end volume discounts / trade scheme credit notes. To save discount: ensure ITC reversal proof from distributor. When opposing Department's demand: if recipient has reversed ITC → both conditions met → credit note is valid → no demand.
Section 12(2) / 13(2) — Time of Supply: "Earlier / Earliest" — Strict Trigger
Statutory Provision | CGST Act | §12(2) for Goods | §13(2) for Services
Key Principle
The time of supply under §12(2) is the earlier of invoice date (or last date invoice ought to be issued) and payment date. The Explanation clarifies that "date of payment received" is the date entered in supplier's books of account OR the date credited to bank account — whichever is earlier. The legislature deliberately chose the earliest possible trigger. A supplier cannot delay tax liability by delaying book entries if the bank has already credited the amount. Similarly, if invoice is not issued by the statutory deadline, the last date of issue itself becomes the time of supply — irrespective of whether invoice is actually issued.
When to Use — In disputes about advance payment treatment, pre-GST era advances, time of supply for ongoing contracts, interest on delayed payments. Argue: TOS determined by statute — not by supplier's convenience of invoicing or book entry.
Section 29(2) — Cancellation: "Wilful Misstatement / Suppression" — Mens Rea Required
Statutory Provision | CGST Act §29(2)(e) | Read with §74 language | Applies in Registration Cancellation Challenges
Key Principle
Section 29(2)(e) empowers cancellation of registration obtained by fraud, wilful misstatement or suppression of facts. The words "wilful" and "fraud" import a requirement of mens rea — deliberate, conscious wrongdoing. "Suppression" means active concealment, not mere non-disclosure or inadvertent error. A registration cannot be cancelled retrospectively under §29(2)(e) for procedural lapses, technical non-compliances or inadvertent omissions that do not rise to the level of deliberate concealment. Natural justice is preserved — no cancellation without hearing (§29(2) Proviso).
When to Use — When Dept seeks to cancel registration for alleged false particulars. Argue: no mens rea, no deliberate suppression → §29(2)(e) not attracted. Even if grounds exist → cancellation only prospective, not automatic retroactive cancellation of all past supplies.
Section 13(3) RCM — 60-Day Rule: Self-Invoice as Alternative Trigger (w.e.f. 01.11.2024)
Statutory Provision | §13(3)(c) inserted by Finance (No.2) Act 2024 | w.e.f. 01.11.2024
Key Principle
For RCM services, where the recipient is required to issue invoice (self-invoice scenario) — the time of supply is the date of issue of invoice by the recipient (§13(3)(c)). For cases where supplier IS required to issue invoice — 60-day rule in §13(3)(b) applies only to such cases. This amendment clarifies that where no invoice obligation exists for supplier — the 60-day clock does not run; instead, recipient's self-invoice date controls. The amendment prevents unintended time of supply acceleration for RCM on unregistered supplier services where supplier never issues invoice.
When to Use — In RCM disputes on legal services, security services, GTA where self-invoicing is norm. Argue: TOS = date of self-invoice issued by recipient — not 60 days from any imaginary supplier invoice. Relevant for avoiding interest liability for period before self-invoice date.
Section 16(2)(c) — Tax Actually Paid: Bona Fide Recipient Protection
Statutory Provision | §16(2)(c) CGST Act | ITC Fraud Supply Chain Disputes
Key Principle
Section 16(2)(c) requires that tax in respect of the supply has been actually paid to the Government. While the statute imposes this condition, the Supreme Court and various High Courts have consistently held that denial of ITC to a bona fide recipient who has paid consideration and tax to the supplier, and who had no knowledge of the supplier's default in depositing tax with the Government, may amount to unjust enrichment of the state and violation of recipient's rights. The purchasing taxpayer must be given an opportunity to produce alternate evidence of good faith and due diligence before ITC is denied.
When to Use — When ITC denied on ground that supplier collected GST but did not deposit it (fake invoice / fraudulent supplier chain cases). Argue: recipient was bona fide, exercised due diligence, paid price including GST, and had no means of knowing of supplier's default.
For professional litigation use | Pawan Soni & Associates, Ichalkaranji
CGST Act — Ch.VII–X (Sec.31–53A)

⚖️ CGST Act, 2017 — Chapters VII to X | Sections 31–53A

Tax Invoice · Accounts & Records · Returns · Payment of Tax | Source: CGST Act, 2017 / GST Litigation Reference | CA Pawankumar Soni, Ichalkaranji

Permitted / Valid
Prohibited / Void / Penal
Condition / If / Unless
Optional / Discretionary
Neutral Reference
Rule Reference
📝 Plain English
💡 Example
🔍 GP Singh Interpretation
📋 Index of Sections — CGST Act Ch. VII to X
Chapter VII — Tax Invoice, Debit & Credit Notes (§31–34)
§31(1)–(2) Tax Invoice — Goods & Services
§31(3)–(7) Special Situations & Timing
§31A Digital Payment Facility
§32 Prohibition — Unauthorised Tax Collection
§33 Tax Amount to be Indicated
§34 Credit & Debit Notes
Chapter VIII — Accounts & Records (§35–36)
§35 Accounts & Records
§36 Retention of Accounts
Chapter IX — Returns (§37–48)
§37 Outward Supplies — GSTR-1
§38 Inward Supplies & ITC Communication
§39 Furnishing of Returns
§40 First Return
§41 Availment of ITC
§44 Annual Return
§45 Final Return
§46–48 Notices, Late Fee & GST Practitioners
Chapter X — Payment of Tax (§49–53A)
§49 Payment — Ledgers & ITC Utilisation
§49(5) ITC Cross-Utilisation Rules
§49A & 49B ITC Utilisation Priority
§50 Interest on Delayed Payment
§51 Tax Deduction at Source (TDS)
§52 Tax Collection at Source — ECO (TCS)
§53 & 53A Transfer of ITC / Amounts
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal
📄 CHAPTER VII — TAX INVOICE, DEBIT AND CREDIT NOTES (§31–34)
§31(1)–(2) — Tax Invoice: Goods & Services | Timing & Content
🧾 Section 31 — Tax Invoice
Obligation on Registered Person to Issue Invoice
§31(1) — SUPPLY OF TAXABLE GOODS
TIMING
Issue invoice BEFORE or AT THE TIME of:
§31(1)(a)
Removal of goods — where supply involves movement
§31(1)(b)
Delivery / making available — in any other case
📝 For goods that travel — invoice when goods leave. For goods handed over (e.g., installation at site) — invoice when delivered.
💡 Textile: Dispatch of fabric rolls from Ichalkaranji mill → invoice on removal. Generator installation at buyer's factory → invoice on commissioning/delivery.
§31(2) — SUPPLY OF TAXABLE SERVICES
TIMING
Issue invoice BEFORE or AFTER provision of service — but within prescribed period
📝 Services have more flexibility — invoice can come before or after the service, but within the time limit notified by Government.
PROVISO 1 (FA 2020)
Govt may specify categories of services where:
(i) Other document = deemed tax invoice
(ii) Tax invoice need not be issued
📝 E.g., bank passbook entry, insurance premium receipt — can be treated as invoice by notification. Or for some services — no invoice needed at all.
💡 Insurance company: Premium receipt = deemed tax invoice. CA professional services: Invoice must be raised within 30 days of completion.
MANDATORY CONTENTS of Tax Invoice: Description | Quantity (goods) | Value | Tax charged thereon | Other prescribed particulars (HSN, GSTIN, etc.)
🔍 GP Singh: "Shall" in §31 is mandatory. Non-issuance of invoice creates strict liability. However, "within prescribed period" in §31(2) — the word "prescribed" delegates timing to Rules, making Rules part of the statute for compliance purposes.
Continued →
📋 Corresponding Rules: Rules 46–48 CGST Rules, 2017 (Pages 214–217) | Rule 46: Tax Invoice particulars | Rule 47: Time limit for Services | Rule 48: Manner of issuing invoice
§31(3)–(7) — Special Situations: Revised Invoice, Bill of Supply, Vouchers & Timing
◄ Back to §31(1)–(2)
Clause Situation Document to Issue Plain English Litigation Point
§31(3)(a) Registration obtained — invoices already issued between effective date & certificate date Revised Invoice — within 1 month of certificate; in prescribed manner 📝 Gap between effective date & certificate date — revise all earlier invoices. Pre-registration invoices can be regularised — ITC available to recipient after revision
§31(3)(b) Value of goods/services < ₹200 May NOT issue tax invoice (subject to conditions) 📝 Small transactions exempted from invoice requirement. No invoice ≠ No supply. Tax still payable. Cannot be used to suppress turnover.
§31(3)(c) Supplier of exempted goods/services OR paying tax u/s 10 (Composition) Bill of Supply (not tax invoice) — prescribed particulars
No bill if value < ₹200		 📝 Exempt/composition dealers issue "Bill of Supply" — no tax line item shown. Issuing tax invoice instead of bill of supply = §32 violation = penalty
§31(3)(d) Advance received for any supply of goods/services Receipt Voucher evidencing advance receipt 📝 Advance = Point of taxation. Receipt voucher = proof of tax event. 💡 Builder collects advance on booking — receipt voucher mandatory. If not issued → §31 default → penalty u/s 122.
§31(3)(e) Advance received → Receipt voucher issued → Supply NOT made → No tax invoice issued Refund Voucher against advance payment 📝 Order cancelled, advance refunded — issue refund voucher. GST liability reversed. Refund voucher = reversal of receipt voucher's tax liability. Critical in real estate booking cancellations.
§31(3)(f) RCM liable person (§9(3)/9(4)) receives supply from unregistered supplier (incl. those registered only for TDS u/s 51) Invoice to be issued by recipient — within prescribed period 📝 Under RCM, the buyer becomes the invoicing party. Unregistered seller cannot issue GST invoice. Failure to issue self-invoice = §31(3)(f) default. No self-invoice = No ITC on RCM → blocked u/s 16(2).
§31(3)(g) RCM liable person makes payment to unregistered supplier Payment Voucher at time of payment 📝 Payment to unregistered vendor under RCM — issue payment voucher. Together with self-invoice (§31(3)(f)) forms complete RCM documentation trail.
§31(4) Continuous supply of goods — successive statements/payments Invoice before or at the time of each statement / each payment 📝 Running contracts — coal, chemicals, bulk commodities — invoice every statement/payment cycle. If invoice delayed beyond statement date → default in reporting period → interest u/s 50(1).
§31(5) Continuous supply of services — three scenarios (a) Due date ascertainable → invoice on/before due date
(b) Not ascertainable → before/at payment
(c) Event-linked → on/before event completion		 📝 Service contracts with payment milestones — invoice timing follows contract structure. AMC/retainership contracts: invoice = tax point. Late invoice → delayed tax → interest.
§31(6) Service contract terminated BEFORE completion Invoice at time of cessation — to extent of supply made before cessation 📝 Mid-term termination — invoice only for services actually rendered. Partial performance invoicing — key in construction/event management disputes.
§31(7) Goods sent on approval for sale-or-return BEFORE supply takes place Invoice before/at time of supply OR 6 months from removal — whichever is earlier 📝 Approval sale: goods sent out but ownership not transferred yet. Invoice must come before 6 months. 6-month rule: If no supply & no invoice within 6 months → deemed supply → tax payable even without sale.
Explanation "Tax invoice" includes revised invoice issued in respect of a supply made earlier.
📋 Corresponding Rules: Rule 49 (Receipt Voucher) | Rule 50 (Refund Voucher) | Rule 51 (Payment Voucher) | Rule 52 (Bill of Supply) | Rule 55 (Delivery Challan — approval sale) CGST Rules, 2017
§31A — Facility of Digital Payment to Recipient
📱 Section 31A — Digital Payment Facility
Inserted vide Finance (No.2) Act, 2019 | w.e.f. 01.01.2020
Government MAY prescribe a class of registered persons who SHALL provide prescribed modes of electronic payment to recipient of supply
Recipient shall be given option to make payment accordingly — in prescribed manner and subject to conditions/restrictions
📝 This enables the government to mandate digital payment options (UPI, NEFT, etc.) for specific categories of suppliers — to promote digital economy and audit trail.
🔍 GP Singh: "May prescribe" = Government discretion. "Shall provide" = mandatory once prescribed. Notice the shift — Government has discretion whether to activate; but once activated, the obligation is strict.
📋 No corresponding Rule notified as of Nov 2025 — Section operative but class of persons not yet prescribed.
§32 — Prohibition of Unauthorised Collection of Tax
🚫 Section 32 — Prohibition of Unauthorised Tax Collection
§32(1)
Unregistered person SHALL NOT collect any amount by way of tax on any supply
📝 If you're not registered under GST, you cannot charge GST to your customer. Doing so is illegal — not a technicality.
💡 Unregistered vendor charges 18% GST on invoice → §32(1) violated → buyer cannot claim ITC (unregistered supplier's invoice invalid) → demand + penalty on vendor.
§32(2)
Registered person SHALL NOT collect tax EXCEPT in accordance with GST Act and Rules
📝 Even a registered person cannot collect tax arbitrarily — must follow the rate, classification and conditions prescribed.
💡 Registered person charges 28% on supply actually taxable at 12% → excess collection = §32(2) violation → §76 action — mandatory deposit of excess tax collected.
⚠️ CONSEQUENCE — §76 CGST Act: Person who has collected excess tax is mandatorily required to pay it to the Government. Cannot retain wrongly collected tax — even if collected by mistake.
🔍 GP Singh: "Shall not" = absolute prohibition. §32 uses "shall" for both prohibitions — equally mandatory. Violation is not merely procedural — it triggers separate penal provisions u/s 122(1)(iv).
📋 Corresponding Provision: §76 CGST Act — Mandatory deposit of excess tax collected | §122(1)(iv) — Penalty
§33 — Amount of Tax to be Indicated in Tax Invoice and Other Documents
📋 Section 33 — Tax Disclosure in Invoice & Documents
Notwithstanding anything in this Act or any other law
Where supply is made for a consideration — EVERY PERSON liable to pay tax SHALL prominently indicate in ALL documents relating to:
assessment | tax invoice | other documents
— the amount of tax which shall form part of the price
📝 Tax must be visibly shown on invoice as part of price — not hidden. "Prominently" means clear, visible, not buried in fine print.
KEY PHRASE — "notwithstanding anything in this Act or any other law" — overrides any other law that might have allowed non-disclosure of tax component
🔍 GP Singh: "Notwithstanding" clause = §33 is a non-obstante provision. It overrides even the contract between parties. Even if contract says "inclusive of taxes" — separate disclosure in invoice is mandatory.
💡 Service contract says "lump sum ₹1,00,000 all inclusive" — §33 still requires invoice to show GST separately as part of that price. Non-disclosure = default u/s 122.
📋 Read with: Rule 46 CGST Rules (mandatory particulars in tax invoice) | §122(1) — Penalty for non-issuance
§34 — Credit Notes and Debit Notes
📝 Section 34 — Credit Notes & Debit Notes
Adjustments to Tax Invoices Already Issued
§34(1) — CREDIT NOTE
WHEN — One or more invoices issued and:
• Taxable value/tax EXCEEDS actual payable, OR
• Goods returned by recipient, OR
• Goods/services found deficient
📝 Credit note = downward adjustment. You charged more than you should have, or goods came back, or quality was defective — issue credit note to reduce tax liability.
💡 Manufacturer invoiced ₹1,00,000 + GST 18% = ₹18,000. Buyer returns 20% goods. Credit note for ₹20,000 + GST ₹3,600 to be issued.
§34(2) REPORTING
Declare in return for month of issuance BUT
NOT LATER THAN 30th November of next FY OR date of annual return — whichever earlier
PROVISO (FA 2025 — w.e.f. 01.10.2025)
NO reduction in output tax liability if:
(i) ITC attributable to CN not reversed by registered recipient, OR
(ii) Tax incidence passed on to any other person (non-registered)
📝 Supplier cannot reduce his tax just by issuing credit note — recipient must reverse his ITC first. This prevents double benefit.
🔍 GP Singh: Amendment in FA 2025 sharpens the condition. "Has not been reversed" = past fact. Supplier must verify before reducing liability. Burden shifts to supplier to confirm reversal.
§34(3) — DEBIT NOTE
WHEN — One or more invoices issued and:
• Taxable value/tax charged is LESS THAN payable
📝 Debit note = upward adjustment. You under-charged — issue debit note to increase tax liability. Recipient gets enhanced ITC.
💡 Contract price revised upward after invoice — issue debit note for differential. Also includes "supplementary invoice" (per Explanation to §34).
§34(4) REPORTING
Declare in return for month of issuance — tax liability adjusted accordingly.
No cut-off date restriction for debit notes
📝 Debit notes — no November deadline. Can be declared in any subsequent return. But delay means delayed tax — interest applies.
Explanation — "Debit note" includes supplementary invoice
📋 Corresponding Rules: Rule 53 CGST Rules (Credit Note) | Rule 53A (Debit Note) | Timeline: 30th November following FY (w.e.f. 01.10.2022, Finance Act 2022)
🗄️ CHAPTER VIII — ACCOUNTS AND RECORDS (§35–36)
§35 — Accounts and Other Records
Sub-section Obligation / Provision Who is Covered Plain English Litigation Point
§35(1) Keep TRUE & CORRECT account at principal place of business (as in registration certificate) of:
(a) Production/manufacture
(b) Inward & outward supplies
(c) Stock
(d) ITC availed
(e) Output tax payable & paid
(f) Other prescribed particulars		 Every registered person 📝 The "books of account" requirement under GST. Must be at the registered principal place. §35(1) is the foundation for §73/74 demands. Failure to maintain accounts = presumption that goods/services suppressed. "True and correct" = auditable, reconcilable accounts.
§35(1) Proviso 1 Multiple places of business — accounts to be kept at EACH respective place Registered person with multiple GSTINs / places 📝 If you have branches, each branch keeps its own records at that branch. During inspection — each place can be independently inspected. Accounting at only one place for all = §35(1) default.
§35(1) Proviso 2 Accounts may be kept in electronic form in prescribed manner All registered persons 📝 Digital books are allowed — but must follow prescribed format. ERP/Tally data = valid records. But must be in format importable by tax authorities during audit.
§35(2) Maintain records of consigner, consignee and relevant goods details Every owner/operator of warehouse, godown, storage place AND every transporter — WHETHER registered or not 📝 Even unregistered transporters/warehouses must keep GST-relevant records. Scope extends beyond registered persons. Transporter without records = §122(1)(xiv) penalty. E-way bill enforcement: §35(2) is the statutory basis.
§35(3) Commissioner may notify a class of taxable persons to maintain additional accounts/documents for specified purpose Specific classes notified by Commissioner 📝 Government can impose extra record-keeping on specific sectors (e.g., real estate, precious metals). Additional accounts requirement under notification has same force as statutory requirement. Non-compliance = §35(6) consequences.
§35(4) If taxable person unable to maintain accounts per §35(1) — Commissioner may for reasons in writing PERMIT simplified maintenance Class of taxable persons specified by Commissioner 📝 Small/informal sector relief — simplified books allowed by permission only. Permission required in writing. Informal arrangement with inspector ≠ §35(4) permission. If no formal order → full §35(1) compliance mandatory.
§35(5) OMITTED vide Finance Act 2021 (w.e.f. 01.08.2021) — Earlier required GST audit by CA/CMA for turnover exceeding prescribed limit and submission of GSTR-9C reconciliation statement. Now omitted — GSTR-9C is self-certified.
§35(6) If registered person fails to account for goods/services per §35(1) → proper officer SHALL determine tax payable as if such goods/services were supplied — §73/74/74A apply mutatis mutandis Registered person who failed to account 📝 Unaccounted goods = deemed supply = tax demand. The officer reconstructs the supply and raises demand. §35(6) is the statutory basis for demands on unaccounted stock. "Mutatis mutandis" = §73/74 procedure applies. Limitation, notice, hearing — all apply. Not a summary power.
📋 Corresponding Rules: Rules 56–58 CGST Rules, 2017 (Pages 225–227) | Rule 56: Accounts to be maintained | Rule 57: Generation and maintenance of records | Rule 58: Records to be maintained by owner/operator of godown and transporters
§36 — Period of Retention of Accounts
📁 Section 36 — Retention of Accounts
How Long Must Records Be Kept?
GENERAL RULE
72 months from the due date of furnishing annual return for the year pertaining to those accounts
📝 6 years retention. Annual return for FY 2023–24 due Dec 31, 2024 → records to be kept till Dec 31, 2030.
💡 FY 2023–24 books: Annual return due 31.12.2024 → retain till 31.12.2030. Destruction before this = §36 default.
EXTENDED RETENTION — PROVISO
If registered person is PARTY to:
• Appeal / revision
• Proceedings before Appellate Authority / Revisional Authority / GSTAT / Court (filed by him OR Commissioner)
• Under investigation for offence under Ch. XIX
THEN: Retain accounts pertaining to subject matter for:
1 year after FINAL DISPOSAL of appeal/revision/proceedings/investigation
OR 72 months — whichever is LATER
📝 If you're fighting a GST case — keep all related records until the case is fully closed + 1 year. Court order alone is not enough — "final disposal" means all remedies exhausted.
⚠️ LITIGATION ALERT — Premature destruction of records during pending appeal = contempt + obstruction of proceedings + separate offence u/s 122(1)(xv).
🔍 GP Singh: "Shall retain" = mandatory. The extended retention proviso uses "whether filed by him or by the Commissioner" — this covers situations where the department appeals against an order in assessee's favour. Records must be retained even for department-initiated appeals.
📋 Corresponding Rules: Rules 56–58 CGST Rules, 2017 (Pages 225–227)
📊 CHAPTER IX — RETURNS (§37–48)
§37 — Furnishing Details of Outward Supplies (GSTR-1)
Sub-section Provision Key Condition / Restriction Plain English Litigation Point
§37(1) Every registered person (except ISD, NR taxable person, §10/§51/§52 persons) shall electronically furnish details of outward supplies in prescribed form, manner, within time, subject to conditions On or before 10th day of succeeding month — for monthly filers
Details communicated to recipient per prescribed conditions		 📝 GSTR-1 filing — outward supply details every month by 10th (or quarterly under QRMP). This is what auto-populates recipient's GSTR-2B. Delay in GSTR-1 → ITC not reflecting in GSTR-2B of recipient → recipient faces ITC denial. Cause of action for recovery from supplier.
§37(1) Proviso Commissioner may extend time limit for class of taxable persons — reasons in writing required. State/UT Commissioner extension deemed as Central Commissioner extension. Class of taxable persons specified 📝 Mass extension possible for technical failures, disasters, etc. Extension notification must be challenged if wrongly worded. "Reasons in writing" = mandatory — absent reasons = invalid notification.
§37(3) Error/omission discovered after furnishing — rectify in prescribed manner + pay tax & interest if applicable NO rectification after 30th November following end of FY OR furnishing of annual return — whichever earlier 📝 Corrections allowed — but only upto November of the next year. Post-November correction = impossible. Must disclose in annual return GSTR-9 with payment. If not done → demand u/s 73/74 at risk.
§37(4) Registered person CANNOT file GSTR-1 for a tax period if GSTR-1 for ANY previous tax period has not been filed Proviso: Govt may allow filing even if prior periods missed — for class of persons, with conditions 📝 Sequential filing requirement — can't skip months. This creates cascading blockage — missing one month blocks all subsequent filings. Recipient's ITC locked too.
§37(5) GSTR-1 cannot be filed after 3 years from the due date of furnishing Proviso: Govt may allow even after 3 years for class of persons 📝 3-year outer limit for GSTR-1 filing — after that, permanently locked. 3-year bar is statutory — extendable only by notification. If missed permanently → recipient cannot claim ITC from that period → significant loss.
Explanation "Details of outward supplies" includes details of invoices, debit notes, credit notes and revised invoices issued in respect of outward supplies during any tax period.
📋 Corresponding Rules: Rules 59–60 CGST Rules, 2017 (Pages 228–232) | Rule 59: Form and manner of furnishing details of outward supply (GSTR-1)
§38 — Communication of Details of Inward Supplies and ITC (GSTR-2B)
📥 Section 38 — Inward Supplies & ITC Communication
Substituted vide Finance Act 2022 w.e.f. 01.10.2022 — GSTR-2B Auto-Generated System
§38(1) — Data Feed: What is Made Available to Recipient
Details furnished by registered persons u/s 37 (outward supplies of supplier) PLUS other prescribed supplies + a statement (earlier: auto-generated — amended to "a statement" vide FA 2025 w.e.f. 01.10.2025) containing ITC details → made available electronically to RECIPIENT
📝 GSTR-2B: Supplier files GSTR-1 → system auto-populates buyer's GSTR-2B → buyer sees available ITC. This is the matching mechanism.
§38(2) — The Statement: Two Categories of ITC
§38(2)(a)
Inward supplies where ITC MAY BE AVAILABLE to recipient
📝 Green zone ITC — supplier has filed, matched, no issues.
§38(2)(b)
Supplies where ITC CANNOT be availed — where supplier:
(i) Newly registered (within prescribed period)
(ii) Tax payment defaulter
(iii) Outward tax payable exceeds paid by prescribed limit
(iv) ITC availed exceeds eligible by prescribed limit
(v) Defaulted in tax liability discharge
(vi) Other prescribed class
📝 Red zone ITC — supplier has compliance issues. System blocks or flags ITC from such suppliers.
💡 Supplier defaults on GST payment for 3 months → GSTR-2B of all buyers marks ITC from that supplier as "blocked" → buyers cannot claim ITC.
§38(2)(c) [FA 2025 — NEW] Such other details as may be prescribed — expanding the scope of statement
§38(3) & (4) — Modifications by recipient communicated back to supplier | §38(3): GSTR-2 era modifications communicated in prescribed time
🔍 GP Singh: §38(2)(b) introduces a risk-based ITC blocking mechanism — ITC denial based on supplier's compliance status, not recipient's. Constitutional validity debated — HC's split on whether "shall" in GSTR-2B blockage is absolute or subject to §16(2)(c) CGST Act.
📋 Corresponding Rules: Rules 60–61 CGST Rules, 2017 (Pages 232–234) | Rule 60: Form and manner of furnishing details of inward supply | GSTR-2B: System-generated statement
§39 — Furnishing of Returns (GSTR-3B / GSTR-4 / GSTR-5 / GSTR-7)
Sub-section Category of Person Return Type / Period Timing Plain English / Litigation Point
§39(1) Regular registered persons (NOT ISD, NR, §10/§51/§52) GSTR-3B — inward & outward supplies, ITC, tax payable & paid Every calendar month (or quarter under QRMP proviso) — within prescribed time, subject to conditions & restrictions (FA 2025 amendment) 📝 GSTR-3B = monthly tax payment return. Monthly filers: by 20th (GSTN) / 22nd / 24th (staggered). Quarterly filers: by last day of month after quarter.
§39(2) Composition dealers (§10) Annual return with quarterly tax payment Each financial year — prescribed form and time 📝 Composition dealers file one annual return (CMP-08 quarterly payment, GSTR-4 annual).
§39(3) TDS deductors (§51) GSTR-7 — monthly deductions For every calendar month — whether or not deductions made Must file even if no deductions. Nil GSTR-7 mandatory. Late fee applicable for non-filing.
§39(4) Input Service Distributors ISD Return (GSTR-6) Within 13 days after end of each calendar month 📝 ISD must file by 13th — earlier deadline than other filers.
§39(5) Non-resident taxable persons GSTR-5 Within 13 days after end of calendar month OR within 7 days after last day of registration period — whichever earlier 📝 Short-term foreign suppliers — return linked to registration validity.
§39(7) Regular filers (§39(1)), TDS (§39(3)), NR (§39(5)) Tax payment due Not later than LAST DATE of furnishing return
Composition dealers: Prescribed form/manner/time		 ⚠️ Tax payment = by return due date. Late payment = interest u/s 50(1) from next day. For QRMP: monthly advance payment + reconciliation at quarter end.
§39(8) Regular (§39(1)) and Composition (§39(2)) Nil return mandatory For EVERY tax period — whether or not supplies made ⚠️ No supply ≠ No return. Nil GSTR-3B mandatory. Late fee for non-filing applies even for Nil periods.
§39(9) All return filers Rectification of omission/incorrect particulars Rectify in subsequent return + pay tax & interest. NOT LATER THAN 30th November following end of FY OR annual return date — whichever earlier. (Except when discovered during scrutiny/audit/enforcement) Self-correction window: up to 30th November. Post-November corrections = departmental demand terrain. Rectifications arising from enforcement ≠ eligible for §39(9) self-correction.
§39(10) All return filers Blocking of current period return CANNOT file GSTR-3B for current period if ANY PREVIOUS PERIOD return OR GSTR-1 for that period not filed Cascading blockage. One unfiled return → all subsequent locked → ITC of all downstream buyers impacted.
§39(11) All return filers 3-year outer bar CANNOT furnish return after 3 years from due date of that return. Proviso: Govt may allow by notification. 3-year lapse = permanent ITC loss for recipient + no self-correction possible. Hardship petitions before HC post this.
📋 Corresponding Rules: Rules 61–84 CGST Rules, 2017 (Pages 234–250) | Rule 61: GSTR-3B | Rule 62: Composition return (CMP-08/GSTR-4) | Rule 66: GSTR-5 (NR) | Rule 67: GSTR-6 (ISD) | Rule 68: GSTR-7 (TDS)
§40 — First Return | §41 — Availment of Input Tax Credit
§40 — FIRST RETURN
Every registered person who made outward supplies between:
Date of becoming liable to registration → Date of grant of registration
→ SHALL declare same in FIRST RETURN after grant
📝 Gap period supplies: made before registration was granted but after liability arose — must be reported in first GSTR-1/GSTR-3B.
💡 Liability arose 01.07.2024 (turnover crossed ₹40L). Registration granted 20.08.2024. Supplies from July 1 to Aug 19 = first return must include all of them. Tax with interest from liability date.
📋 Rule 40 CGST Rules: First return form
§41 — AVAILMENT OF ITC (Substituted — FA 2022 w.e.f. 01.10.2022)
§41(1) Every registered person — subject to conditions/restrictions — entitled to avail credit of eligible input tax as self-assessed in return → credited to electronic credit ledger
📝 ITC is self-assessed — you declare it in GSTR-3B based on invoices. It goes to your credit ledger automatically.
§41(2) REVERSAL ITC availed in respect of supplies where tax NOT paid by supplier → SHALL BE REVERSED with applicable interest
PROVISO TO §41(2) If supplier later pays tax → RE-AVAIL the reversed credit in prescribed manner
📝 If your supplier defaults on GST payment — you must reverse ITC. But if supplier pays later — you can take it back.
💡 Buyer claims ITC ₹1,00,000 from Supplier A. Supplier A fails to pay GST. Buyer gets notice → reversal of ₹1,00,000 + interest u/s 50(3). Supplier A later pays → Buyer re-avails ₹1,00,000.
🔍 GP Singh: "Self-assessed" = provisional character of ITC claim. §41(2) creates the reversal obligation — it is independent of §16(2)(c). Both operate. Reversal under §41(2) = specific ITC reversal mechanism for supplier default.
📋 Rule 37 CGST Rules: Reversal of ITC on non-payment by supplier
§44 — Annual Return | §45 — Final Return
§44 — ANNUAL RETURN (Substituted FA 2021 w.e.f. 01.08.2021)
WHO MUST FILE
Every registered person EXCEPT:
• ISD
• §51 TDS deductor
• §52 TCS (ECO)
• Casual taxable person
• Non-resident taxable person
§44(1) Annual return including self-certified reconciliation statement reconciling supplies declared in monthly returns with audited annual financial statements — electronically — within prescribed time & form
📝 GSTR-9 + GSTR-9C (self-certified) — annual reconciliation. Replaces earlier CA-certified audit. Due date: December 31 of following year.
PROVISO 1 Commissioner may exempt a class from filing — by notification with Council recommendation
PROVISO 2 Govt departments / local authorities whose accounts audited by CAG or appointed auditor → exempt from §44
§44(2) [FA 2023 w.e.f. 01.10.2023] CANNOT file annual return after 3 years from due date. Proviso: Govt may allow by notification.
💡 GSTR-9 for FY 2021-22: Due 31.12.2022. 3-year bar = 31.12.2025. After that → permanently locked → no self-correction.
📋 Rule 80 CGST Rules: Annual return (GSTR-9, GSTR-9A, GSTR-9C)
§45 — FINAL RETURN
WHO Every registered person required to file §39(1) return whose registration has been cancelled
TIMING Within 3 months of:
• Date of cancellation, OR
• Date of order of cancellation
— whichever is LATER
📝 After registration is cancelled — one last return (GSTR-10) must be filed. Shows all assets, ITC to be reversed, etc.
💡 Registration cancelled 01.03.2025. GSTR-10 must be filed by 31.05.2025. Non-filing → late fee + ITC reversal demand.
🔍 GP Singh: "Within three months" = peremptory. No extension provision in §45 unlike §39(6). Late filing = mandatory late fee u/s 47. Deliberately strict to prevent exit without compliance.
📋 Rule 81 CGST Rules: Final return (GSTR-10)
§46 Notice to Return Defaulters | §47 Late Fee | §48 GST Practitioners
Section Provision Quantum / Period Plain English Litigation Point
§46 If registered person fails to furnish return u/s 39, §44, §45 → notice shall be issued requiring return within 15 days 15 days from notice 📝 Non-filer gets a notice — must file within 15 days or face best judgment assessment u/s 62. §46 notice is the trigger for §62 best judgment assessment. If §46 notice not issued → §62 assessment itself is invalid. Notice is a mandatory precedent.
§47(1) Failure to furnish GSTR-1 (§37) / returns u/s §39 / §45 / §52 by due date → LATE FEE ₹100/day of default — maximum ₹5,000 per return
(Reduced by notifications for Nil returns and below ₹5Cr turnover)		 📝 ₹100/day for late filing — capped at ₹5,000. Government routinely waives/reduces by notification. Late fee waiver notifications frequently issued. Late fee waived ≠ offence condoned for §74 purposes. Check notification scope before advising waiver of penalty.
§47(2) Failure to furnish Annual Return (§44) by due date → LATE FEE ₹100/day — maximum = 0.25% of turnover in State/UT 📝 Annual return late fee capped at 0.25% of turnover — much higher cap than GSTR-3B. Large turnover entities: 0.25% cap can be significant. E.g., ₹50Cr turnover → max late fee ₹12.5L. Waiver notification essential to track.
§48 GST Practitioners — eligibility, duties, obligations, removal by prescription
Registered person MAY authorise GSTPs to file GSTR-1, GSTR-3B, §44 & §45 returns and other functions		 Per prescribed conditions 📝 GSTPs are like TRPs under Income Tax — authorised intermediaries who can file returns. §48(3): RESPONSIBILITY for correct particulars REMAINS WITH REGISTERED PERSON even if GSTP files. GSTP error ≠ defence against penalty. Principal cannot escape by blaming GSTP.
📋 Corresponding Rules: Rules 82–84 CGST Rules | Rule 83: Conditions for enrolment as GSTP | Rule 83A: Examination for GSTPs | Rule 84: Functions of GSTP
💰 CHAPTER X — PAYMENT OF TAX (§49–53A)
§49 — Payment of Tax: Three Ledgers, Utilisation & Priority of Discharge
💳 Section 49 — Payment Architecture
Three Electronic Ledgers + ITC Utilisation Rules
The Three Ledgers — GST Payment Infrastructure
ELECTRONIC CASH LEDGER §49(1)
Deposits via internet banking, debit/credit card, NEFT, RTGS etc. → credited to Cash Ledger → maintained in prescribed manner
📝 Your GST bank account. Cash deposited here for paying tax, interest, penalty, fees.
ELECTRONIC CREDIT LEDGER §49(2)
ITC self-assessed in return per §41 → credited to Credit Ledger
📝 Your ITC bank account. Only output tax can be paid from here — not interest, penalty or fees.
ELECTRONIC LIABILITY REGISTER §49(7)
All liabilities recorded & maintained in liability register — in prescribed manner
📝 The debit side. Tax dues, interest, penalty — all posted here. Must be discharged from Cash/Credit ledger.
Utilisation Rules — What Can Be Paid From Where
CASH LEDGER §49(3)
May be used for ANY payment: tax + interest + penalty + fees + other amounts
CREDIT LEDGER §49(4)
May be used ONLY for output tax payment under CGST/IGST Acts — subject to conditions & restrictions
📝 ITC ≠ cash. Cannot pay interest, penalty, late fee from ITC. Only output tax.
§49(6) REFUND
Balance remaining after payment of all dues → may be refunded u/s 54
§49(8) — Mandatory Order of Discharge of Dues
FIRST: Self-assessed tax + dues of previous tax periods' returns
SECOND: Self-assessed tax + dues of current tax period return
THIRD: Any other amount incl. demands u/s 73, 74, 74A
📝 You cannot pay a new demand and leave old returns unpaid. System mandates clearing arrears first. This prevents selective payment of fresh dues while keeping old defaults alive.
🔍 GP Singh: §49(8) uses "shall discharge...in the following order" — mandatory sequencing. Court-directed payment toward a specific demand does not override §49(8) unless court specifically orders deviation from the prescribed sequence.
§49(9) — DEEMED PASS-ON Every person who has paid tax under this Act shall, unless contrary is proved, be DEEMED to have passed on the full incidence of such tax to the recipient of goods/services
📝 Presumption of unjust enrichment — tax paid is deemed to have been passed on to buyer. For refund claims, you must rebut this presumption by proving you didn't charge tax to your customer.
💡 Manufacturer claims refund of excess tax paid. Department says: you must have charged it to the buyer → unjust enrichment → refund to be credited to Consumer Welfare Fund, not you. Must prove tax not passed on via certified CA statement.
Continued →
📋 Corresponding Rules: Rules 85–88 CGST Rules, 2017 (Pages 251–255) | Rule 85: Electronic Liability Register | Rule 86: Electronic Credit Ledger | Rule 87: Electronic Cash Ledger | Rule 88: Matching of payment
§49(5) — ITC Cross-Utilisation Rules | §49(10) Transfer | §49(12) Cap on Credit Ledger Use
◄ Back to §49 Ledgers
🔄 §49(5) — ITC Cross-Utilisation Rules
Which ITC Can Pay Which Tax Head?
ITC Type Available First: Pay This Tax Then: Remaining Can Pay CANNOT PAY Key Proviso
IGST ITC §49(5)(a) First: IGST Remaining → CGST, then SGST/UTGST (in that order) — IGST ITC is most flexible — can cross-utilize.
CGST ITC §49(5)(b) First: CGST Remaining → IGST Cannot pay SGST / UTGST CGST → IGST: only after CGST exhausted.
SGST ITC §49(5)(c) First: SGST Remaining → IGST (with proviso) Cannot pay CGST Proviso: SGST → IGST only if CGST balance NOT AVAILABLE for IGST payment
UTGST ITC §49(5)(d) First: UTGST Remaining → IGST (with proviso) Cannot pay CGST Proviso: UTGST → IGST only if CGST balance NOT AVAILABLE for IGST payment
§49(5)(e)&(f) ABSOLUTE RESTRICTION: CGST ≠ SGST/UTGST | SGST/UTGST ≠ CGST — NEVER cross-payable between State and Central taxes This is the sacrosanct ring-fencing of Centre vs State revenues.
§49(10) — Transfer Between Distinct Persons / Tax Heads
§49(10)(a) Registered person may transfer excess amount in Cash Ledger between tax heads (IGST/CGST/SGST/UTGST/Cess) on the portal

§49(10)(b) Transfer to Cash Ledger of a distinct person (same PAN, different GSTIN — e.g., transfer from Maharashtra entity to Gujarat entity)
📝 Excess cash in CGST ledger? Transfer to IGST head, or to your another branch's GSTIN. No refund process needed — direct portal transfer. This is deemed a refund from ledger.
PROVISO to §49(10)(b) Transfer to distinct person NOT allowed if transferor has unpaid liability in electronic liability register
📝 You cannot transfer excess cash to your branch if you have outstanding dues. First clear your own liabilities.
§49(12) [FA 2022] Government may prescribe MAXIMUM PROPORTION of output tax liability that may be discharged through Credit Ledger — for registered person or class of persons
📝 Government can cap how much ITC you can use (e.g., 99% cash, max 1% ITC for certain sectors). Used as anti-fraud measure in fake ITC cases.
🔍 GP Singh: §49(12) is an exceptional power. "Maximum proportion" = delegates capping to Government. Constitutional validity questioned — amounts to restricting a statutory right (ITC) by subordinate legislation. Subject of ongoing litigation.
📋 Corresponding Rules: Rule 86B CGST Rules — Restriction on use of Credit Ledger (applicable to specified taxpayers) | Rule 88A: Order of utilisation of ITC (now governed by §49A/49B)
§49A — ITC Utilisation: Integrated Tax First | §49B — Order of Utilisation
§49A — IGST FIRST RULE
Inserted vide CGST Amendment Act 2018 w.e.f. 01.02.2019
OVERRIDES §49 — ITC on CGST, SGST, UTGST shall be utilised towards IGST, CGST, SGST, UTGST ONLY AFTER ITC available on account of IGST has been FIRST UTILISED FULLY
📝 You must exhaust ALL your IGST ITC first before using CGST or SGST ITC. No selective utilisation of smaller credit while IGST sits unused.
💡 Assessee has: IGST ITC ₹5L, CGST ITC ₹3L, SGST ITC ₹3L. Output: CGST ₹2L, SGST ₹2L. Must first use IGST ₹4L (CGST ₹2L + SGST ₹2L), not own CGST/SGST credit.
🔍 GP Singh: "Shall be utilised...only after...has first been utilised fully" = mandatory sequencing. "Fully" is key — even ₹1 of IGST ITC remaining → CGST/SGST ITC cannot be used. Strict construction applies.
§49B — ORDER OF UTILISATION
Inserted vide CGST Amendment Act 2018 w.e.f. 01.02.2019
OVERRIDES entire Chapter X (subject to §49(5)(e)&(f)) — Government may on Council recommendation prescribe the order and manner of ITC utilisation of IGST, CGST, SGST, UTGST towards any tax
📝 §49B is the enabling provision that gives Government power to prescribe ITC utilisation order by Rule — current order is in Rule 88A CGST Rules.
💡 Rule 88A: IGST ITC → IGST first → CGST → SGST. CGST ITC → CGST → IGST. SGST → SGST → IGST. This Rule derives power from §49B.
📋 Rule 88A CGST Rules: Order of utilisation of input tax credit | Read with §49(5) cross-utilisation rules
§50 — Interest on Delayed Payment of Tax
💹 Section 50 — Interest on Delayed Payment
Three Distinct Interest Scenarios
§50(1) — GENERAL INTEREST ON DELAYED TAX
Rate: Up to 18% p.a. (notified @ 18%)
When: Tax not paid within prescribed time
Period: From day succeeding due date to actual payment
Liability: Pay suo motu on own
📝 Standard delay interest. You owe tax by 20th, you pay on 25th → interest for 5 days at 18% p.a. Auto-calculated.
PROVISO (FA 2021 — retrospective from 01.07.2017)
For late return (§39) not during enforcement proceedings — interest only on portion paid by DEBIT FROM CASH LEDGER
NOT on portion paid from Credit Ledger (ITC)
📝 Critical relief: Interest only on net cash payment, not on ITC. If you had enough ITC all along but filed late, interest = zero (because no cash outgo).
💡 Late GSTR-3B: Tax ₹5L via ITC + ₹1L via cash. Interest = 18% on ₹1L only (cash portion) — NOT on ₹5L ITC portion.
🔍 GP Singh: "Except where return furnished after commencement of proceedings u/s 73/74" — once proceedings initiated, full gross tax interest applies. The proviso is available only for voluntary late filers, not when caught by department.
§50(2) — CALCULATION BASIS
Interest calculated in prescribed manner
From: Day succeeding the day tax was due
To: Actual payment date
📝 Due date = 20th. Payment on 25th. Interest starts 21st. Not 20th itself.
§50(3) — INTEREST ON WRONG ITC (FA 2022 — retro 01.07.2017)
Rate: Up to 24% p.a. (notified @ 24%)
When: ITC wrongly availed AND UTILISED
Calculated in prescribed manner
📝 Higher rate for abuse. If you claimed fake/ineligible ITC and actually used it to offset tax → 24% interest. Just claiming without using = 18% debate (earlier).
💡 Fake ITC of ₹10L claimed and used to pay output tax → §50(3) interest @ 24% from date of utilisation till recovery.
🔍 GP Singh: "Wrongly availed AND utilised" = conjunctive. Both elements must be present for §50(3). If ITC availed but reversed before utilisation → §50(3) may not apply. FA 2022 amended "undue or excess claim" to "wrongly availed and utilised" — significant narrowing of scope.
📋 Rate notifications: CGST Notification 13/2017-CT (18%) | 13/2017-CT (24%) | Rule 88B CGST Rules: Manner of interest calculation (inserted 2022)
§51 — Tax Deduction at Source (TDS)
Sub-section Provision Key Threshold / Condition Plain English Litigation Point
§51(1) Government mandates following to deduct TDS:
(a) Dept/establishment of Central/State Govt
(b) Local authority
(c) Governmental agencies
(d) Such persons as notified		 Contract value EXCEEDS ₹2,50,000
Rate: 1% (0.5% CGST + 0.5% SGST or 1% IGST)
Deduct from payment/credit to supplier

EXEMPT: If supplier location and place of supply are in a different State/UT from recipient's registration		 📝 Government buyers must deduct 1% GST TDS on contracts above ₹2.5L. Like TDS under Income Tax but only for specified entities. Deductor files GSTR-7. TDS deducted but not deposited → interest u/s 50(1). Supplier cannot claim credit until reflected in deductor's GSTR-7. Supply value EXCLUDING tax for TDS calculation (per Explanation).
§51(2) Amount deducted shall be PAID TO GOVERNMENT within 10 days after end of month of deduction 10 days from month end 📝 Deposit TDS fast — 10 days from month end. Same urgency as Income Tax TDS deposit. Late deposit → interest u/s 50(1) at 18%. No grace period. Even 1 day late = interest liability.
§51(3) TDS certificate to be issued in prescribed form and manner (amended FA 2020) Prescribed form & manner 📝 Deductor must issue TDS certificate. Supplier uses it to claim credit. Earlier: Failure to issue certificate within 5 days → ₹100/day late fee (§51(4) — now omitted vide FA 2020). TDS credit to supplier available only after GSTR-7 filed by deductor.
§51(5) Deductee (supplier) claims credit in electronic cash ledger — from amount reflected in deductor's GSTR-7 Per prescribed manner 📝 Supplier claims TDS credit → appears as cash in supplier's Cash Ledger → can use for tax payment. TDS credit ≠ ITC credit. Goes to Cash Ledger, not Credit Ledger. Can use for any liability — tax, interest, penalty.
§51(6) If deductor fails to deposit TDS → pays interest per §50(1) In addition to TDS amount 📝 Non-deposit = interest. Plus recovery proceedings under §73/74. §51(7): Determination of default under §51 → §73/74/74A procedure applies. Notice, hearing, order — full adjudication required before recovery.
§51(8) Refund of excess/erroneous deduction → dealt with per §54
Proviso: No refund to DEDUCTOR if TDS already credited to supplier's Cash Ledger		 §54 procedure for refund 📝 If TDS deducted excess → refund under §54. But if already credited to supplier → refund to deductor blocked (unjust enrichment). Proviso prevents double benefit — supplier already got credit, deductor cannot also claim refund. Section 77 does NOT apply here.
📋 Applicable from: 01.10.2018 (Notification 50/2018-CT) | Corresponding Rules: Rule 66 (GSTR-7 return) | Rule 67 (TDS certificate — GSTR-7A)
§52 — Collection of Tax at Source by Electronic Commerce Operators (TCS)
🛒 Section 52 — TCS by Electronic Commerce Operators (ECO)
Amazon, Flipkart, Zomato, Swiggy etc. — collect 1% on net taxable supplies
Key Provisions at a Glance
§52(1) RATE
Every ECO (not being agent) SHALL collect an amount calculated at up to 1% (notified @ 0.5% CGST + 0.5% SGST or 1% IGST)
On: Net value of taxable supplies through it by other suppliers where consideration collected by ECO
📝 ECO = marketplace platform. For each third-party seller's supply through the platform — ECO collects 1% TCS before paying the seller.
💡 Seller on Amazon makes ₹1,00,000 supply. Amazon deducts 1% TCS = ₹1,000. Pays seller ₹99,000. Deposits ₹1,000 to govt.
EXPLANATION to §52(1)
"Net value of taxable supplies" = Aggregate value of taxable supplies by all registered suppliers through ECO during month MINUS aggregate value of supplies returned through ECO
📝 TCS on net sales — returns are deducted. Cancellations and returns reduce TCS base.
§52(3) DEPOSIT
Amount collected → paid to Government within 10 days after end of month of collection
§52(4) MONTHLY STATEMENT
Furnish monthly statement electronically — details of outward supplies through it, returns, TCS collected — within 10 days after end of month (GSTR-8)
§52(5) ANNUAL STATEMENT
Annual statement by 31st December following end of FY
§52(7) SUPPLIER CREDIT
Supplier claims credit of TCS in his Cash Ledger — from amount reflected in ECO's GSTR-8
📝 Seller gets TCS credit in Cash Ledger — just like TDS. Use it for any liability.
§52(9) MISMATCH
If ECO's GSTR-8 details don't match supplier's GSTR-1/GSTR-3B → discrepancy communicated to both
§52(10) ADDITION
Unresolved discrepancy (ECO shows higher supply than supplier) → added to supplier's output tax liability in next month's return
📝 If ECO shows you made more supply than you reported → difference added to your tax liability. Interest also applies.
§52(12) NOTICE POWER Officer not below Deputy Commissioner may serve notice to ECO for details of supplies effected, stock of goods in managed warehouses — within 15 working days of notice. Non-compliance → penalty up to ₹25,000 (§52(14))
§52(15) [FA 2023 w.e.f. 01.10.2023] ECO CANNOT file GSTR-8 after 3 years from due date. Proviso: Govt may allow by notification.
📋 Corresponding Rules: Rule 67 (GSTR-8 statement) | Rule 78 (Matching of TCS details) CGST Rules, 2017
§53 — Transfer of ITC | §53A — Transfer of Amounts Between State & Central Governments
§53 — TRANSFER OF ITC BETWEEN CGST AND IGST ACCOUNTS
When CGST ITC is utilised for payment of IGST (per §49(5)(b)) → amount collected as central tax stands REDUCED by amount of credit utilised → Government TRANSFERS equal amount from Central Tax Account to Integrated Tax Account
📝 Back-end government accounting: When you use CGST ITC to pay IGST, the Centre moves money from its Central Tax bucket to the IGST bucket. Seamless settlement between revenue streams.
§53A — TRANSFER OF AMOUNTS TO STATE/UT ACCOUNT
Inserted vide Finance (No.2) Act 2019 w.e.f. 01.01.2020
Where any amount transferred from Electronic Cash Ledger under CGST Act to Electronic Cash Ledger under SGST/UTGST Act → Government SHALL transfer to State/UT Tax Account an amount equal to amount so transferred — in prescribed manner and time
📝 When you transfer excess CGST cash to SGST ledger (inter-head transfer) — the Centre deposits equivalent amount into State's account. Government-to-Government settlement matching your inter-ledger transfer.
💡 Maharashtra GSTN holder transfers ₹5L from CGST Cash Ledger to SGST Cash Ledger on portal. Central Govt simultaneously transfers ₹5L to Maharashtra's State Tax Account. System remains balanced.
📋 Corresponding Rules: Rule 88 CGST Rules — Matching of payment with IGST account | Rules 85–88D CGST Rules (Pages 251–258)
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal — CGST Act Ch. VII–X | §31–53A
These statutory arguments are for use in personal hearings before GST Officers, Appellate Authorities and GSTAT. State them confidently and cite the section.
Section 31(3)(f) — Self-Invoice Mandatory for RCM | No Self-Invoice = No ITC
Statutory Provision | CGST Act, 2017 | Read with §16(2) — ITC Conditions
Key Principle
Under Section 31(3)(f), a registered person liable to pay tax under reverse charge (Section 9(3) or 9(4)) is mandatorily required to issue an invoice in respect of goods or services received from an unregistered supplier. Failure to issue self-invoice results in non-compliance of Section 16(2)(a) — which requires possession of a tax invoice as a condition for availing ITC. Accordingly, ITC on RCM purchases cannot be availed without self-invoice.
When to Use — When Department denies ITC on RCM purchases citing absence of self-invoice. Also use when assessee claims ITC on oral/unregistered RCM transactions — self-invoice is not optional, it is statutory. Cite to establish that ITC is legitimately available once self-invoice is issued.
Section 31(7) — Six-Month Rule for Approval Sale
Statutory Provision | CGST Act, 2017 | Read with §12 — Time of Supply for Goods
Key Principle
Section 31(7) provides that where goods are sent on approval for sale or return before supply takes place, the invoice shall be issued before or at the time of supply or six months from the date of removal, whichever is earlier. Consequently, if no supply occurs and no invoice is issued within six months — it becomes a deemed supply — taxable even without actual sale. Tax liability crystallises at the 6-month mark.
When to Use — In disputes involving goods sent on consignment, approval sale, sale-or-return arrangements. Counter Department's demand that tax arose at removal: argue supply not yet complete. Alternatively, use to defend against demand on expired 6-month goods by confirming deemed supply treatment.
Section 32 — Excess Tax Collected: Mandatory Deposit Under Section 76
Statutory Provision | §32 CGST Act read with §76 CGST Act
Key Principle
Section 32(2) prohibits any registered person from collecting tax except in accordance with the provisions of this Act and the rules made thereunder. Where excess tax is collected, Section 76 mandates its deposit with the Government irrespective of whether the supplier has paid excess tax in error or by design. The person collecting excess tax cannot retain it even if collected as a bona fide mistake.
When to Use — When assessee collected higher GST rate (e.g., 28% on 18% supply) and deposited it. Use to argue that refund under §54 is available only if unjust enrichment is rebutted. Also use when Department issues demand for excess collected tax: direct attention to §76 mandatory deposit obligation.
Section 34(2) Proviso (FA 2025) — Credit Note: Supplier Cannot Reduce Tax Without Recipient Reversal
Statutory Provision | §34(2) Proviso | CGST Act as amended by Finance Act 2025 w.e.f. 01.10.2025
Key Principle
No reduction in output tax liability of the supplier shall be permitted if the input tax credit as is attributable to such credit note, if availed, has not been reversed by the recipient (where recipient is a registered person), or where the incidence of tax has been passed on to any other person (in other cases). The amended provision explicitly makes the supplier's output tax reduction conditional on recipient's ITC reversal — a linked mechanism.
When to Use — When assessee (supplier) issues credit note and reduces output tax liability in GSTR-3B. Counter Department's objection by showing recipient has reversed ITC. Alternatively, challenge a supplier's credit note reduction where recipient's reversal is unverified — important in audit proceedings.
Section 35(6) — Unaccounted Goods: §73/74 Procedure Mandatory Before Demand
Statutory Provision | §35(6) CGST Act read with §73 / §74 / §74A
Key Principle
Section 35(6) provides that where a registered person fails to account for goods or services in accordance with Section 35(1), the proper officer shall determine the amount of tax payable on such unaccounted goods as if they had been supplied — and the provisions of Section 73 or Section 74 (or Section 74A), as the case may be, shall apply mutatis mutandis. This means the full adjudication procedure — notice, opportunity of hearing — is mandatory even for unaccounted stock demands.
When to Use — When Department issues summary demand for unaccounted stock found during inspection without following §73/74 procedure. Cite §35(6): "mutatis mutandis" application of §73/74 is not optional — full adjudication is mandatory. Demand without SCN = void. Raise jurisdictional objection on this ground.
Section 36 — Records Must Be Retained for Pending Appeals: Extended 1-Year Post-Final Disposal
Statutory Provision | §36 CGST Act | Critical in Assessment & Investigation Proceedings
Key Principle
Section 36 provides that where a registered person is a party to an appeal or revision or proceedings before any Appellate Authority, Revisional Authority or Appellate Tribunal or Court, or is under investigation for an offence under Chapter XIX — the books of account and records pertaining to the subject matter shall be retained for a period of one year after final disposal of such proceedings, or for the period of 72 months, whichever is later.
When to Use — When assessee disposes of records after 5-6 years thinking limitation has run out — but appeal is still pending. Cite §36 to show retention obligation continues. Also use in reverse: challenge Department's demand that relies on records assessee was not obligated to retain after 72 months + 1 year post-final disposal.
Section 39(9) — Self-Correction Only Upto 30th November: Post-Enforcement Corrections Outside §39(9)
Statutory Provision | §39(9) CGST Act
Key Principle
Section 39(9) enables rectification of omissions or incorrect particulars in returns — however, it expressly excludes rectifications that are a result of scrutiny, audit, inspection or enforcement activity by tax authorities. Such errors discovered by enforcement cannot be self-corrected; they fall within the domain of Section 73 or Section 74 adjudication. Further, no rectification is allowed after the 30th day of November following the end of the financial year.
When to Use — When assessee attempts voluntary correction after Department issues audit objection — argue §39(9) is unavailable for audit-detected errors. Conversely, use to defend assessee's pro-active self-correction before any enforcement proceedings — such corrections are valid and should not be treated as admissions of wrong-doing.
Section 41(2) — ITC Reversal on Supplier Default: Right to Re-Avail After Supplier Pays
Statutory Provision | §41(2) CGST Act (Substituted FA 2022 w.e.f. 01.10.2022)
Key Principle
The credit of input tax availed by a registered person in respect of supplies where the tax payable has not been paid by the supplier shall be reversed along with applicable interest.
