However, where the said supplier subsequently makes payment of the tax payable, the registered person may re-avail the amount of credit so reversed in such manner as may be prescribed.
When to Use — When Department demands permanent denial of ITC based on supplier's default. Cite §41(2) proviso: denial is only temporary — if and when supplier pays, ITC is legitimately re-availed. Permanent denial = contrary to the statutory scheme. Use to challenge Circular 183/2022 interpretations that deny re-availment.
Section 50(1) Proviso — Interest Only on Cash Portion: ITC-Backed Late Return Not Subject to Full Interest
Statutory Provision | §50(1) Proviso | CGST Act — Applicable retrospectively from 01.07.2017 (Finance Act 2021)
Key Principle
The proviso to Section 50(1), as substituted by the Finance Act, 2021 with retrospective effect from 01.07.2017, provides that for late-furnished returns under Section 39, interest shall be levied only on that portion of the tax that is paid by debiting the electronic cash ledger — and not on the portion paid through the electronic credit ledger (ITC). This proviso does not apply where the return is furnished after commencement of proceedings under Section 73 or Section 74 in respect of the said period.
When to Use — When Department raises interest demand on full tax amount for late GSTR-3B filing, including ITC portion. Cite §50(1) proviso: interest restricted to cash-paid portion only. For FY 2017-18 to 2020-21 (pre-FA 2021 periods) — retrospective application applies. Also use to argue zero interest where entire tax was discharged from ITC with no cash payment.
Section 50(3) — Wrong ITC: "Wrongly Availed AND Utilised" — Conjunctive Requirement
Statutory Provision | §50(3) CGST Act (Substituted FA 2022 — retrospective 01.07.2017)
Key Principle
Section 50(3) as substituted by the Finance Act, 2022 imposes interest at 24% on ITC that has been wrongly availed AND utilised. The use of the conjunctive "and" is deliberate — both conditions must be satisfied. ITC that has been wrongly availed but reversed before utilisation does not attract 24% interest under Section 50(3). The rate of 18% under Section 50(1) — applicable to delayed tax — would in any event apply only to the cash portion per the proviso.
When to Use — When Department levies 24% interest on ITC claimed and subsequently reversed voluntarily. Counter: §50(3) requires both "wrongly availed" AND "utilised" — voluntary reversal before utilisation breaks the chain. 24% interest does not apply. Important defence in fake ITC cases where some portion was reversed before offset.
Section 49(9) — Deemed Pass-On: Unjust Enrichment Must Be Disproved for Refund
Statutory Provision | §49(9) CGST Act | Read with §54(8)(d) — Refund Conditions
Key Principle
Section 49(9) creates a statutory presumption that every person who has paid the tax shall be deemed to have passed on the full incidence of such tax to the recipient of goods or services, unless the contrary is proved by him. This presumption of unjust enrichment applies to refund claims — the claimant must affirmatively prove, through a CA certificate or documentary evidence, that tax was not passed on to customers.
When to Use — In refund proceedings where Department invokes unjust enrichment and proposes to credit amount to Consumer Welfare Fund. Cite §49(9) to understand the burden — then provide CA certificate under Rule 89 to rebut the presumption. Also use when advising clients: at the time of invoicing, maintain clear evidence of whether GST was charged to customer (for future refund claims).
Section 46 — Notice to Return Defaulter is Mandatory Precedent to §62 Best Judgment Assessment
Statutory Provision | §46 read with §62 CGST Act
Key Principle
Section 46 mandates issuance of a notice to a registered person who has failed to file returns under Section 39, 44, or 45, requiring such person to furnish the return within fifteen days. Section 62 empowers best judgment assessment only after the registered person fails to comply with such notice. Consequently, a best judgment assessment under Section 62 made without prior issuance of a Section 46 notice is without jurisdiction and liable to be set aside.
When to Use — When Department issues §62 assessment order for non-filing without issuing prior §46 notice. Challenge the order as jurisdictionally void — §46 notice is a mandatory statutory precedent. Order passed without following mandatory procedure = nullity. Cite principle of mandatory procedural compliance before coercive action.
For professional litigation use | Pawan Soni & Associates, Ichalkaranji
CGST Act — Ch.XI–XIII (Refunds, Assessment, Audit)

⚖️ Chapters XI–XIII — Refunds | Assessment | Audit

Sections 54–66 | Source: CGST Act, 2017 | CA Pawankumar Soni, Ichalkaranji

Permitted / Valid
Prohibited / Restriction
Condition / If / Unless
Optional / Discretionary
Neutral Reference
Procedural / Notice
📝 Plain English
💡 Example
🔍 GP Singh Interpretation
📋 Rule Cross-Reference
📖 Legal Dictionary
⚔️ Word War
📋 Index of Sections
Chapter XI — Refunds (§54–58)
§54(1–4) — Application for Refund
§54(5–9) — Processing & Payment
§54(10–15) — Withholding & Restrictions
§54 Explanation — Refund & Relevant Date
§55 — Refund in Certain Cases (UN/Embassy)
§56 — Interest on Delayed Refunds
§57–58 — Consumer Welfare Fund
Chapter XII — Assessment (§59–64)
§59 — Self-Assessment
§60 — Provisional Assessment
§61 — Scrutiny of Returns
§62–63 — Assessment of Non-Filers & Unregistered
§64 — Summary Assessment
Chapter XIII — Audit (§65–66)
§65 — Audit by Tax Authorities
§66 — Special Audit
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal
§54(1–4) — Section 54: Refund of Tax — Application & Eligibility
💰 Section 54 — Refund of Tax
Chapter XI | CGST Act, 2017
§54(1) — WHO CAN APPLY
Any person claiming refund of:
(a) Tax + interest paid on such tax, OR
(b) Any amount paid by him
⚔️ Word War: "Any person" vs "Registered person" — §54(1) says "any person" (wider scope — includes unregistered buyers, exporters, SEZ units) but §54(3) says "registered person" (narrower, for ITC refund). Legislature made a deliberate distinction — "any person" in §54(1) cannot be restricted to registered persons.
📝 Anyone who has overpaid GST or paid tax that is refundable — including an unregistered buyer who paid RCM tax — can apply for refund. Not just registered dealers.
⏱ TIME LIMIT
Must apply before expiry of 2 years from the relevant date
(Amended from "the return" to "the date" w.e.f. 01.10.2022 by Finance Act 2022)
🔍 GP Singh: "Shall" in limitation provision is mandatory. The 2-year limit is a condition precedent — failure bars the claim absolutely. Courts cannot extend it by equity or hardship unless specific provision exists. Read strictly against assessee.
💡 Exporter filed refund claim on 15.10.2024 for shipping bill dated 01.09.2022. Date of export = relevant date. 2 years = 01.09.2024. Claim is time-barred by 45 days. No relief available.
PROVISO — Electronic Cash Ledger Balance
Registered person claiming refund of balance in Electronic Cash Ledger u/s 49(6) → may claim in prescribed form & manner
📝 If you have excess cash sitting in your GST cash ledger (e.g., you paid too much TDS/TCS) — you can claim it back. This is different from ITC refund. No "relevant date" concept — just file in the prescribed form.
§54(2) — SPECIALISED AGENCIES
UN/Multilateral Institutions notified under UN Privileges Act 1947 | Consulates | Embassies | Any notified person
→ Entitled to refund of tax paid on inward supplies
⏱ Apply within 2 years from last day of quarter in which supply was received
📝 Foreign embassies and UN bodies are sovereign — they shouldn't bear GST. They can claim back whatever GST their vendor charged them, but must file quarterly refund claims within 2 years.
§54(3) — ITC REFUND (UNUTILISED)
Registered person may claim refund of unutilised ITC at end of any tax period
GENERAL BAR
No refund of unutilised ITC except in 2 permitted cases (below)
CASE 1 — §54(3)(i)
Zero-rated supplies made without payment of tax (LUT/Bond route)
💡 Exporter under LUT — no IGST paid on exports — accumulated ITC of inputs can be refunded.
CASE 2 — §54(3)(ii)
Inverted duty structure — rate on inputs > rate on output supplies
(except nil-rated / fully exempt / notified goods)
💡 Fabric (ITC @ 12%) used to make school uniforms (output @ 5%) — accumulated ITC eligible for refund.
FURTHER PROVISO — DRAWBACK BAR
No refund of ITC if supplier avails drawback of central tax OR claims refund of integrated tax paid on such supplies.
(Cannot double-dip — either drawback or GST refund, not both)
NEW — w.e.f. 01.11.2024
§54(3) proviso (export duty goods): Omitted by Finance (No.2) Act, 2024 — prior bar on ITC refund for goods subjected to export duty removed.
§54(4) — DOCUMENTS TO ACCOMPANY APPLICATION
(a) Documentary evidence as prescribed — to establish refund is due to applicant
(b) Evidence to establish: (i) Tax & interest was paid by applicant, and (ii) Incidence of tax was NOT passed on to any other person
📖 "Incidence of tax" (Black's): The final burden of tax — who actually bears it economically. If seller recovered GST from buyer → seller cannot claim refund, as economic burden passed on. Unjust enrichment doctrine.
💡 Contractor paid GST on a contract. Client (Government) disallowed the amount in bill. Contractor absorbed the tax — no passing on → eligible for refund.
SMALL REFUND EXCEPTION — PROVISO TO §54(4)
Where refund amount < ₹2 lakhs → No documentary evidence required
Applicant may file a self-declaration certifying incidence of tax has not been passed on
📝 Small refund claims (under ₹2 lakh) don't need proof — just a self-declaration from the applicant. Legislature's effort to reduce compliance burden for small amounts.
Continued → §54(5–9)
📋 Corresponding Rules: Rules 89–97A, CGST Rules, 2017 (Pages 259–281)
§54(5–9) — Processing & Payment of Refund
◄ Back to §54(1–4)
⚙️ §54(5–9) — How Refund is Processed & Paid
§54(5) — DEFAULT ROUTE — Credit to Consumer Welfare Fund
If proper officer satisfied that whole/part amount is refundable → passes order → Amount credited to Consumer Welfare Fund (§57)
📝 By default, refund money goes to the Consumer Welfare Fund — NOT directly to the applicant. Direct payment to applicant only in the cases listed in §54(8). This is the general rule; §54(8) is the exception list.
§54(6) — PROVISIONAL REFUND — Zero-Rated Supplies
Proper officer may grant provisional refund of 90% of total claim
Applicable only to: Zero-rated supply by registered persons (except notified categories)
Final settlement after verification of documents u/s 54(5)
⚔️ Word War: "may" vs "shall" — §54(6) uses "may" (discretionary). Proper officer is NOT obligated to grant provisional refund. However, if conditions are met and no adverse finding — denial may be challenged as arbitrary exercise of discretion. Contrast with §54(7) which uses "shall" (mandatory 60-day timeline).
📝 To give exporters quick cash flow relief — 90% of claimed refund can be paid upfront within days, before full verification. The remaining 10% is settled after documents are checked. Amendment removed the "excluding provisionally accepted ITC" qualification w.e.f. 01.10.2023.
💡 Exporter files refund claim of ₹10 lakh for IGST on exports. Proper officer may release ₹9 lakh provisionally within days; balance ₹1 lakh after document verification.
§54(7) — MANDATORY 60-DAY TIMELINE
Proper officer SHALL issue order u/s 54(5) within 60 days from date of receipt of application complete in all respects
🔍 GP Singh: "Shall" = mandatory obligation. Failure to decide within 60 days triggers interest liability u/s 56. The phrase "complete in all respects" has been litigated — department cannot reject on deficiencies it failed to raise within the processing period. If no deficiency raised within 15 days (Rule 90), application deemed complete.
📖 "Complete in all respects" — Per Rule 90(3) CGST Rules: If deficiency memo not issued within 15 days, application is deemed complete from date of filing. Department cannot later claim incompleteness to extend 60-day clock.
§54(8) — DIRECT PAYMENT TO APPLICANT (Exception to CWF route)
(a) Refund of tax on export of goods/services or inputs/services used in exports
(b) Refund of unutilised ITC u/s 54(3)
(c) Refund of tax on supply not provided, no invoice issued, or refund voucher issued
(d) Refund of tax in pursuance of §77 (tax paid in wrong head)
(e) Tax & interest paid by applicant who has NOT passed on incidence
(f) Such other class of applicants as Government notifies
§54(8A) — STATE TAX REFUND
Government may disburse refund of State Tax in prescribed manner
(Inserted by Finance (No.2) Act, 2019 w.e.f. 01.09.2019)
§54(9) — OVERRIDING BAR — No Refund Except Under §54(8)
Notwithstanding anything in any judgment, decree, order or direction of AT/Court or any other provision of this Act or rules
→ No refund shall be made EXCEPT in accordance with §54(8)
🔍 GP Singh: Non-obstante clause ("notwithstanding anything") overrides even court orders directing refund — unless the payment mode falls within §54(8). This is a deeming provision narrowly confining the modes of refund payment. Assessee must identify under which sub-clause of §54(8) the refund falls.
📝 Even if a High Court says "give this person a refund" — the only way the government can actually pay is through the categories in §54(8). This clause prevents refund by any other route including Court orders.
Continued → §54(10–15)
📋 Corresponding Rules: Rules 89–97A, CGST Rules, 2017 (Pages 259–281)
§54(10–15) — Withholding, Restrictions & Special Cases
◄ Back to §54(5–9)
🚫 §54(10–15) — When Refund Can Be Withheld or Barred
§54(10) — WITHHOLDING WHERE DEFAULTER
Where refund is due to a registered person who has:
(a) Defaulted in furnishing return, OR
(b) Is required to pay tax/interest/penalty not stayed by court/tribunal/AA by specified date
→ Proper officer may — (a) withhold refund, OR (b) deduct dues from refund
⚔️ Word War: "may" in §54(10) — Discretionary, not mandatory. If dues are minor and refund is large — officer must apply mind; blanket withholding of entire refund for small dues = disproportionate. "Specified date" = last date for filing appeal under the Act.
📝 If you haven't filed returns or have pending dues — your refund can be held back until you comply. But "may" means officer must exercise judgment — not automatic. Prior to 01.10.2022, §54(10) also said "under sub-section (3)" which was omitted — now applies to all refunds.
💡 Exporter has ₹5 lakh ITC refund claim pending. Also has ₹50,000 unpaid demand from a SCN (no stay). Officer can deduct ₹50,000 from refund and pay ₹4.5 lakh. Cannot withhold entire ₹5 lakh for a ₹50,000 default.
§54(11) — WITHHOLDING DURING APPELLATE PROCEEDINGS
Where refund order is subject matter of appeal/proceedings OR other proceedings pending under the Act
AND Commissioner is of opinion that refund is likely to adversely affect revenue on account of malfeasance or fraud
→ After giving applicant opportunity of being heard → Commissioner may withhold refund till determined time
🔍 GP Singh: Two conditions BOTH required: (1) proceedings pending + (2) likelihood of adverse revenue impact due to malfeasance/fraud. Neither condition alone is sufficient. Word "malfeasance or fraud" sets a HIGH threshold — mere pendency of appeal insufficient. Opportunity of hearing is a condition precedent (natural justice).
📖 "Malfeasance" (Black's): Wrongful or unlawful conduct by a public officer, OR intentional wrongdoing. Here applied to taxpayer — connotes active wrongdoing, not mere error. Courts have held that disputed tax liability ≠ malfeasance.
§54(12) — INTEREST ON WITHHELD REFUND (§54(11) cases)
If refund withheld u/s 54(11) AND as result of appeal/proceedings assessee becomes entitled to refund
→ Interest payable at rate not exceeding 6% (as notified by Council)
📝 If department wrongly withheld your refund under §54(11) and you ultimately win the appeal — you get interest on the withheld amount. Rate is capped at 6% p.a. (lower than the 9% available for delayed refund under §56).
§54(13) — CASUAL/NON-RESIDENT TAXPAYER — ADVANCE REFUND BAR
Advance deposit of casual taxable person / non-resident taxable person u/s 27(2)
→ NOT refunded unless all returns under §39 for entire registration period are furnished
📝 A casual or non-resident taxpayer must file ALL their GST returns for the entire period they were registered before they can get back their advance deposit. No partial refund for filing some returns — 100% compliance required.
§54(14) — MINIMUM REFUND THRESHOLD
No refund under §54(5) or §54(6) shall be paid if amount is less than ₹1,000
📝 Petty refund claims below ₹1,000 are simply not paid — to avoid administrative costs exceeding refund value.
§54(15) — NEW w.e.f. 01.11.2024 | EXPORT DUTY GOODS BAR
No refund of unutilised ITC for zero-rated supply of goods subject to export duty
No refund of integrated tax paid on zero-rated supply of goods subject to export duty
(Inserted by Finance (No.2) Act, 2024)
📝 If your exported goods attract export duty — you cannot claim GST refund (either ITC or IGST). Legislature's policy: if goods are export-dutiable, government will not also give GST refund. Prevents double incentive.
💡 Iron ore (subject to export duty) exported — neither ITC accumulated on inputs, nor IGST paid on export is refundable from 01.11.2024.
📋 Corresponding Rules: Rules 89–97A, CGST Rules, 2017 (Pages 259–281)
§54 Explanation — "Refund" & "Relevant Date" Defined
Clause Situation Relevant Date = … Plain English Litigation Point / GP Singh
Exp(1) "Refund" includes… Tax on zero-rated supplies + inputs used therein | Tax on deemed exports | Unutilised ITC u/s 54(3) The definition of "refund" is inclusive — not exhaustive. Other forms of refund may exist. 🔍 Inclusive definition ("includes") — cannot be read restrictively. What is not in the list may still be "refund" if it falls within the spirit of the chapter.
Exp(2)(a)(i) Goods exported by Sea or Air Date on which ship/aircraft leaves India Date of departure of vessel — not date of loading or bill of lading. Key date for 2-year limitation — if exported goods are still on ship within India on a particular date, that date is not yet the relevant date.
Exp(2)(a)(ii) Goods exported by Land Date on which goods pass the frontier Crossing the land border of India. Date of crossing customs frontier — not date of invoice or LR.
Exp(2)(a)(iii) Goods exported by Post Date of despatch by Post Office Postal department dispatch date. Relevant for e-commerce small exporters.
Exp(2)(b) Deemed Exports Date on which return relating to deemed exports is furnished When the supplier files the return reporting the deemed export supply. 2-year clock starts from return filing date — not supply date. Important for suppliers to EOU/EPCG holders.
Exp(2)(ba) Zero-rated supply to SEZ developer/unit Due date for furnishing return u/s 39 for period of such supplies
(Inserted by Finance Act 2022 w.e.f. 01.10.2022)		 The due date of GSTR-3B for the period in which supply was made — not actual filing date. 🔍 "Due date" — not actual filing date. Beneficial to supplier — clock starts on due date even if return filed late.
Exp(2)(c)(i) Services exported — payment received in convertible forex (supply completed before payment) Date of receipt of payment When foreign currency hits exporter's bank account. If payment received in advance before supply — use invoice date (below). Critical sequencing.
Exp(2)(c)(ii) Services exported — payment received in advance before invoice Date of issue of invoice When invoice is raised — even though payment already received. Advance payment exporters must track invoice date as limitation trigger.
Exp(2)(d) Refund pursuant to order of appellate authority / tribunal / court Date of communication of such judgment/decree/order Date you actually receive the appellate order — not the date of judgment pronouncement. 🔍 "Communication" — receiving copy, not the date of order. Important for calculating 2-year limitation from appellate success.
Exp(2)(e) Refund of unutilised ITC under inverted duty structure — §54(3)(ii) Due date for furnishing return u/s 39 for period in which claim for refund arises
(Substituted by CGST (Amendment) Act 2018)		 Due date of GSTR-3B for the period showing accumulated ITC. Prior to 2018 amendment — was "end of financial year in which claim arises." Now much more favorable — monthly period-based.
Exp(2)(f) Tax paid provisionally u/s 60 Date of adjustment of tax after final assessment When the provisional assessment is finalized and difference is computed. 2-year period starts only after final assessment order — not provisional payment date.
Exp(2)(g) Refund to a person other than supplier Date of receipt of goods/services by such person E.g., recipient who paid RCM tax. Relevant for RCM payers claiming refund.
Exp(2)(h) Any other case Date of payment of tax Residual — when tax was actually paid in cash. Catch-all clause — for situations not covered above.
📋 Corresponding Rules: Rules 89–97A, CGST Rules, 2017 (Pages 259–281)
§55 — Refund in Certain Cases (UN / Embassy / Notified Persons)
🌐 Section 55 — Sovereign & Privileged Entity Refunds
📝 Some bodies — like the UN, World Bank, IMF, foreign embassies — are exempt from paying taxes by international law. But in India's GST system, their vendors still charge GST. Section 55 lets Government notify these entities so they can claim back the GST paid.
WHO IS NOTIFIED
• UN Specialised Agencies
• Multilateral Financial Institutions (UN Act 1947)
• Consulate or Embassy of foreign countries
• Any other notified person
ENTITLEMENT
Refund of taxes paid on notified supplies of goods or services or both received by them
📝 Not all purchases — only "notified supplies". Government specifies which category of purchases qualify for refund.
CONDITIONS
Subject to conditions and restrictions as prescribed in rules
File application as prescribed
Time limit: 2 years from last day of quarter (§54(2))
📋 Corresponding Rules: Rules 89, 95, CGST Rules, 2017 | Notification No. 13/2017-CT (Rate) and related notifications
§56 — Interest on Delayed Refunds
💸 Section 56 — Interest on Delayed Refunds
NORMAL CASE
Refund ordered u/s 54(5) NOT refunded within 60 days from receipt of application
Interest @ up to 6% p.a.
For period of delay beyond 60 days from receipt of application till date of refund
(Amended w.e.f. 01.10.2023 — now "for the period of delay beyond 60 days" — clearer computation)
📝 You filed a complete refund application. Department did nothing for 3 months. Interest runs from day 61 till the day refund is actually paid. At up to 6% per year.
APPELLATE ORDER CASE
Refund arises from order of Adjudicating Authority / AA / AT / Court that has attained finality
Application filed consequent to such order
Interest @ up to 9% p.a.
From day after expiry of 60 days from receipt of application (filed after the final order) till date of refund
📝 Higher rate (9% vs 6%) because department delayed even after losing in appeal — this penalises wilful delay post-adjudication. "Attained finality" = no further appeal pending.
💡 HC order in assessee's favour dated 01.06.2024, application filed 10.07.2024. No refund by 09.09.2024. Interest @ 9% runs from 10.09.2024 till payment date.
EXPLANATION — Deeming Fiction
Where refund order is made by AA/AT/Court against proper officer's order u/s 54(5)
→ Such order by AA/AT/Court is deemed to be order passed under §54(5)
(So 60-day clock runs from applicant's application under §54(1) — not from the appellate order date)
🔍 GP Singh: Deeming provisions must be strictly construed — only for the purpose stated. Here the fiction is limited: appellate order treated as §54(5) order ONLY for calculating interest period. The 60-day clock runs from the application consequent to the final order. Multiple applications not permitted — only one application after finality.
📋 No separate rule — governed by Section 56 directly. Rate notified by Notification No. 13/2017-CT.
§57–58 — Consumer Welfare Fund
🏛️ Sections 57 & 58 — Consumer Welfare Fund (CWF)
📝 When a refund cannot be directly paid to the taxpayer (e.g., because unjust enrichment doctrine applies — the tax incidence was passed on to the consumer), the refund amount goes into a Fund created for consumer welfare. This prevents taxpayers from getting windfall gains by claiming refunds they effectively recovered from end-consumers.
§57 — WHAT GOES INTO CWF
(a) Amount under §54(5) — refunds credited to Fund
(b) Income from investment of Fund amounts
(c) Such other monies
§58(1) — UTILISATION
All CWF amounts utilized by Government for welfare of consumers in prescribed manner
§58(2) — ACCOUNTS
Government / specified authority to maintain proper separate accounts and annual statement in consultation with CAG
💡 Manufacturer collected GST from customer, paid to government. Later claims refund on ground of excess tax. If investigation shows GST was collected from buyer → unjust enrichment → refund goes to CWF, not manufacturer. Manufacturer "made no loss" since it was the buyer who bore the tax.
📋 Corresponding Rules: Rules 97–97A, CGST Rules, 2017 (Pages 279–281)
§59 — Self-Assessment | Chapter XII: Assessment
📋 Section 59 — Self-Assessment
Chapter XII | CGST Act, 2017
Every registered person SHALL self-assess the taxes payable under this Act
AND furnish a return for each tax period as specified under §39
⚔️ Word War: "shall" — Mandatory. Self-assessment is not optional. Every registered person has the statutory obligation to compute their own liability correctly. This is the foundation of GST compliance — the system trusts the taxpayer to self-declare. Any deviation from honest self-assessment exposes the taxpayer to scrutiny u/s 61, demand u/s 73/74/74A.
🔍 GP Singh: §59 is the umbrella provision — all assessment chapters (§61–64) are triggered by failures in self-assessment. Self-assessment correctly done = safe harbour. The return filed under §39 is itself the self-assessed tax declaration.
📝 GST is built on trust — you compute your own tax, declare it in your return, and pay it. Department checks later. If you get it wrong — whether by error or intention — sections 61 to 74A are the consequences.
📋 Corresponding Rules: Rules 98–100, CGST Rules, 2017 (Pages 282–283)
§60 — Provisional Assessment
🔄 Section 60 — Provisional Assessment
When taxpayer is unable to determine value or rate of tax
TRIGGER CONDITIONS — §60(1)
Taxable person is unable to determine:
(a) The value of goods/services, OR
(b) The rate of tax applicable
📝 This provision exists for genuine uncertainty — e.g., where goods are being custom-made and value is not yet fixed, or where the GST rate classification is genuinely disputed. Not for deliberate evasion.
💡 Complex software development contract where value depends on usage metrics to be determined at year-end. Developer can apply for provisional assessment, pay tax provisionally, and settle finally after value is determined.
STEP 1 — Application
Taxable person requests in writing giving reasons → requests payment on provisional basis
STEP 2 — Officer's Order §60(1)
Proper officer passes order within 90 days from receipt of request → allowing payment at specified rate/value
🔍 "Shall pass" within 90 days — mandatory timeline. If officer does not act → applicant can file writ mandamus.
STEP 3 — Bond + Surety §60(2)
Taxable person executes bond in prescribed form with such surety/security as officer deems fit → binding for payment of difference on final assessment
📝 Security deposit — ensures that if final tax is higher, government can recover the difference. The bond is a guarantee.
STEP 4 — Final Assessment §60(3)
Within 6 months from communication of provisional order → final assessment order passed
Extension: JC/AC → +6 months | Commissioner → +4 years
🔍 Extension up to 4 years (Commissioner) — extremely wide. Must record reasons in writing. After 4 years + 6 months, cannot be extended further.
§60(4) — INTEREST ON UNDERPAYMENT
Taxpayer liable to pay interest u/s 50(1) on tax payable under provisional assessment but not paid on due date
From: First day after due date u/s 39(7)
Till: Date of actual payment
Regardless of whether amount is paid before or after issuance of final assessment order
📝 If you provisionally paid less than the actual tax — interest runs from the ORIGINAL due date of payment, not from the final assessment order. The interest clock never stops — even if the department took 3 years to finalize.
§60(5) — REFUND IF OVER-PAID
If registered person entitled to refund consequent to final assessment order u/s 60(3)
→ Interest paid as provided in §56
📝 If on final assessment it turns out you paid too much provisionally — you get a refund + interest under §56. The interest clock for refund also runs from the original overpayment.
📋 Corresponding Rules: Rules 98–100, CGST Rules, 2017 (Pages 282–283)
§61 — Scrutiny of Returns
🔎 Section 61 — Scrutiny of Returns
First-level departmental examination
STEP 1 §61(1)
Proper officer MAY scrutinize return and related particulars
→ Verify correctness of return
→ Inform taxpayer of discrepancies noticed
→ Seek explanation
⚔️ "may" — Discretionary power. No obligation to scrutinize every return. But if undertaken, must follow prescribed procedure.
STEP 2 §61(2) — IF EXPLANATION ACCEPTABLE
Registered person informed accordingly
→ No further action
📝 If you satisfactorily explain the discrepancy — matter closed. No SCN, no demand. This is the most favorable outcome.
STEP 3 §61(3) — IF NO SATISFACTORY EXPLANATION
Within 30 days (or extended period) — no explanation
OR taxpayer accepts discrepancies but fails to correct
→ Proper officer may initiate action
Consequences under §61(3) — Officer's Options
Action u/s 65 — Audit by tax authorities
Action u/s 66 — Special Audit
Action u/s 67 — Inspection, Search, Seizure
Proceed to determine tax under §73 or §74 or §74A
(§74A inserted w.e.f. 01.11.2024)
🔍 GP Singh: §61 is the "notice to cure" provision. The 30-day period is a valuable taxpayer safeguard — use it to correct mistakes or provide explanations. Once §73/74 proceedings begin under §61(3), the taxpayer loses the benefit of voluntary payment without penalty. Difference between §61(3) → §73 route (bonafide error) and §74 route (fraud) is critical — demand under §74 carries 100% penalty.
💡 GSTR-3B shows higher ITC than GSTR-2B. Officer issues scrutiny notice. Taxpayer explains — vendor filed GSTR-1 late but ITC was eligible. Officer accepts → case closed. If unexplained → §73 demand for ITC reversal + interest.
📋 Corresponding Rules: Rules 98–100, CGST Rules, 2017 (Pages 282–283)
§62–63 — Assessment of Non-Filers & Unregistered Persons
Feature §62 — Non-Filer Assessment §63 — Unregistered Person Assessment
Trigger Registered person fails to furnish return u/s 39 or §45 even after notice u/s 46 Taxable person failed to obtain registration though liable to do so, OR registration cancelled u/s 29(2) but was liable to pay tax
Non-obstante clause Notwithstanding §73 or §74 or §74A (inserted w.e.f. 01.11.2024) Notwithstanding §73 or §74 or §74A (inserted w.e.f. 01.11.2024)
Method of Assessment Best judgment assessment — taking into account all relevant material available or gathered Best judgment assessment — for relevant tax periods
Time Limit for Order Within 5 years from date specified u/s 44 for furnishing annual return for the FY in which tax not paid relates Within 5 years from date specified u/s 44 for furnishing annual return for FY in which tax not paid relates
Remedy for taxpayer §62(2): File valid return within 60 days of service of assessment order → order deemed withdrawn
Interest u/s 50(1) + late fee u/s 47 continue even after withdrawal		 Must be given opportunity of being heard before passing order (proviso to §63)
Extended time for return filing NEW Proviso (Finance Act 2023 w.e.f. 01.10.2023): If valid return not filed in 60 days, can file within FURTHER 60 days on payment of ₹100/day additional late fee → order deemed withdrawn No such extended period — assessment stands unless challenged in appeal
Interest/Fee Liability after Withdrawal CONTINUES — Interest u/s 50(1) and late fee u/s 47 remain payable even after §62 order is withdrawn by filing return No specific provision — general interest u/s 50 applies
🔍 GP Singh on Best Judgment Assessment: "Best of judgement" is not arbitrary assessment — it must be based on material available. Officer cannot make a random estimate. If estimate is excessive or without basis — assessee can attack it in appeal as perverse. The fact that it is "best judgment" does not insulate it from judicial review for reasonableness.
💡 Reg. person does not file GSTR-3B for 6 months. After §46 notice, still no return. §62 assessment order passed for ₹50 lakh based on GSTR-2A data. Taxpayer files valid return within 60 days showing actual liability of ₹30 lakh → §62 order deemed withdrawn, but late fee + interest on ₹30 lakh continue.
📋 Corresponding Rules: Rules 98–100, CGST Rules, 2017 (Pages 282–283)
§64 — Summary Assessment in Certain Special Cases
⚡ Section 64 — Summary Assessment
Emergency / Urgency Assessment to Protect Revenue
📝 This is the most powerful and quickest assessment tool — available when there is urgency and evidence of tax liability. Unlike §62/63 which follow a process, §64 can be passed instantly with prior permission of Add. Commissioner/JC. But it is also the most prone to misuse — hence the safeguard in §64(2).
§64(1) — CONDITIONS FOR SUMMARY ASSESSMENT
Evidence showing tax liability coming to notice
📖 "Evidence showing" — Lower threshold than "reasons to believe" (§67). Mere documentary evidence sufficient. Courts have held §64 cannot be triggered on suspicion alone.
Previous permission of Additional Commissioner or Joint Commissioner
🔍 GP Singh: Prior permission is a condition precedent — not a formality. If order passed without proper prior sanction, it is void ab initio. Challenge at the threshold.
Sufficient grounds to believe that delay may adversely affect revenue interest
📖 "Sufficient grounds to believe" — Higher threshold than "evidence showing." Both conditions must co-exist: evidence + urgency grounds. Not a routine assessment tool.
PROVISO — WHEN LIABLE PERSON IS UNASCERTAINABLE
Where taxable person to whom liability pertains is not ascertainable
AND liability pertains to supply of goods
→ Person in charge of goods shall be deemed the taxable person liable to pay tax
📝 If goods are found in transit with no owner identified — the truck driver or person in physical custody is treated as the taxable person. This enables seizure and recovery where the actual owner is evading identification.
💡 Unaccounted goods found in a warehouse. Owner cannot be traced. Person holding keys to warehouse = taxable person liable under §64 proviso.
§64(2) — SAFEGUARD: WITHDRAWAL OF SUMMARY ASSESSMENT
On application by taxable person within 30 days from receipt of order
OR on suo motu motion of Add. Commissioner / JC
IF: Such officer considers order to be erroneous
→ MAY withdraw the order AND follow procedure u/s 73 or §74 or §74A
🔍 GP Singh: "Erroneous" is wider than "without jurisdiction." An order based on correct facts but wrongly applying the law is erroneous. §64(2) safeguard available only within 30 days — strict limitation. After 30 days, only appeal/writ available. The Add. Commissioner's suo motu power to withdraw is a check on the very power they granted.
💡 Summary assessment passed in haste for ₹80 lakh. Taxpayer files application within 30 days showing goods were inter-branch transfers (not supply). Add. Commissioner finds order erroneous → withdraws → follows §73 process where taxpayer gets full hearing.
📋 Corresponding Rules: Rules 98–100, CGST Rules, 2017 (Pages 282–283)
§65 — Audit by Tax Authorities | Chapter XIII: Audit
🔍 Section 65 — Departmental Audit
Chapter XIII | CGST Act, 2017
§65(1) — WHO CONDUCTS
Commissioner OR any officer authorised by him
Via general or specific order
For such period, at such frequency, in such manner as prescribed
§65(2) — WHERE
(a) At place of business of registered person, OR
(b) In their office
📝 Two choices of venue — taxpayer's premises or department office. If audit at taxpayer's premises — more disruptive but more thorough. If at office — taxpayer must bring records.
§65(3) — MANDATORY NOTICE
Registered person must be informed via notice
Not less than 15 working days prior to audit conduct
🔍 GP Singh: "Not less than 15 working days" — mandatory safeguard. Audit conducted without requisite advance notice is void. Distinction between "working days" and "calendar days" — holidays excluded. Failure = procedural infirmity.
§65(4) — COMPLETION PERIOD
Audit SHALL be completed within 3 months from date of commencement
Extension: Commissioner may extend by further 6 months if satisfied audit cannot be completed within 3 months — with recorded reasons
📖 "Commencement of audit" [Explanation to §65(4)]: The LATER of: (a) Date on which records/documents called for are made available by the registered person, OR (b) Actual institution of audit at place of business. This definition is crucial — departmental inaction in calling for records or commencing audit cannot extend the 3-month window by making "commencement" flexible.
📝 Total maximum time = 3 + 6 = 9 months from commencement. After 9 months — audit period closes. Any audit report or findings communicated after this may be challenged as beyond the statutory time frame.
💡 Audit notice given 01.04.2024. Taxpayer provides records on 01.05.2024. Audit deemed to commence on 01.05.2024. Must be completed by 01.08.2024 (3 months). With extension → latest 01.02.2025.
§65(5) — DURING AUDIT — OBLIGATIONS OF TAXPAYER
(i) Afford necessary facility to verify books of account or other documents as required
(ii) Furnish such information as required and render assistance for completion of audit
§65(6) — POST-AUDIT COMMUNICATION — MANDATORY
On conclusion of audit → proper officer SHALL, within 30 days, inform the registered person about:
(a) Findings of audit
(b) His rights and obligations
(c) Reasons for such findings
🔍 GP Singh: §65(6) is a natural justice requirement embedded in statute. The 30-day mandate for communicating findings means: (1) Taxpayer has right to know findings before any demand; (2) Any demand based on undisclosed audit findings violates §65(6); (3) "Reasons for findings" must be meaningful — not vague conclusions. Failure to comply with §65(6) before initiating §73/74 proceedings is a procedural defect.
💡 Audit completed 15.08.2024. Officer does not issue §65(6) notice. Issues SCN under §73 on 20.09.2024. Challenge: SCN is premature — §65(6) notice of findings is a condition precedent to §73/74 proceedings arising from audit.
§65(7) — CONSEQUENCE OF AUDIT FINDINGS
If audit results in detection of:
(a) Tax not paid / short paid
(b) Erroneously refunded
(c) ITC wrongly availed or utilised
→ Proper officer shall initiate action under §73 or §74 or §74A
(§74A inserted w.e.f. 01.11.2024 by Finance (No.2) Act, 2024)
⚔️ Word War: §73 vs §74 vs §74A — §73 = no fraud, up to 3 years, 10% penalty | §74 = fraud/suppression, up to 5 years, 100% penalty | §74A = new provision inserted 2024, likely consolidating demands with new thresholds. Choice of section affects entire demand quantum and penalty exposure. Attack the section chosen by department first.
📋 Corresponding Rules: Rules 101–102, CGST Rules, 2017 (Pages 283–284)
§66 — Special Audit by Chartered Accountant / Cost Accountant
📊 Section 66 — Special Audit
Expert examination by nominated CA / CMA when value/credit raises concern
📝 When the regular GST officer feels the case is too complex (complex transactions, large amounts, irregular credit) — they can direct the taxpayer to get a special audit by a CA or CMA nominated by the Commissioner. The cost is borne by the government, not the taxpayer. The special audit report is then used as basis for further proceedings.
§66(1) — TRIGGER CONDITIONS (ALL must be met)
Stage: Any stage of scrutiny, inquiry, investigation or any other proceedings
📝 "Any other proceedings" — extremely wide. Includes assessment, refund proceedings, even appeal stage. Can be directed at any point.
Officer: Not below rank of Assistant Commissioner
Opinion: Value has not been correctly declared OR credit availed is not within normal limits
📖 "Normal limits" — not defined. Gives wide discretion. Courts have held this must mean industry/sector norms, not officer's personal benchmark. Vague trigger susceptible to challenge.
Prior approval of Commissioner required before directing special audit
🔍 GP Singh: Prior approval of Commissioner = condition precedent. Unlike §64 where order itself has prior permission, here only the direction needs Commissioner's prior approval. Without this approval — §66 direction is void.
WHO AUDITS §66(1)
CA or Cost Accountant nominated by Commissioner
📝 Not taxpayer's own CA — the government nominates. Taxpayer has no say in selection. Nominated expert is independent.
TIMELINE §66(2)
Submit audit report within 90 days from direction
Extension: AC may extend by further 90 days on application
🔍 Extension available on application — from either the registered person OR the CA/CMA themselves. Total: 180 days max.
COST §66(5)
Expenses of examination and audit — including remuneration of CA/CMA — determined and paid by Commissioner
Such determination is FINAL
📝 Government bears the cost — not the taxpayer. The fee fixation by Commissioner is not appealable ("final"). But the amount payable to the CA/CMA is public expenditure — taxpayer does not need to pay.
§66(3) — OVERRIDING EFFECT (Protects Revenue)
§66(1) shall have effect notwithstanding that accounts of registered person have been audited under any other provision of this Act or any other law
📝 Even if the company has a statutory CA audit under Companies Act, even if there was a previous GST audit — §66 special audit can still be directed. It is an independent GST-specific examination.
§66(4) — NATURAL JUSTICE SAFEGUARD
Registered person SHALL be given opportunity of being heard in respect of any material gathered on basis of special audit
BEFORE being used in proceedings against him
🔍 GP Singh: §66(4) is a statutory embodiment of audi alteram partem (hear the other side). Any SCN issued based on §66 audit report without first giving the registered person opportunity to respond to the audit material = violation of §66(4) = void proceedings. The "material gathered" includes not just the audit report but also workpapers and underlying data.
💡 Special audit report says ITC of ₹2 crore wrongly availed. Department issues SCN directly without sharing audit material with taxpayer. Challenge: §66(4) violated — SCN is void as opportunity of being heard not given before use of audit material in proceedings.
§66(6) — CONSEQUENCE OF SPECIAL AUDIT FINDINGS
If special audit results in detection of:
Tax not paid / short paid | Erroneously refunded | ITC wrongly availed or utilised
→ Proper officer may initiate action under §73 or §74 or §74A
(§74A inserted w.e.f. 01.11.2024)
📋 Corresponding Rules: Rules 101–102, CGST Rules, 2017 (Pages 283–284)
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal — Sections 54–66 | Refunds, Assessment & Audit
These statutory arguments and principles are for use in personal hearings before GST Officers, Appellate Authorities, GSTAT and High Courts. State them with citation of the sub-section.
Section 54(1) — "Any Person" Scope of Refund
Statutory Provision | CGST Act, 2017 | Relevant for RCM payers, buyers, unregistered claimants
Key Argument
Section 54(1) uses "any person" — not "registered person." The Legislature's deliberate choice of the wider expression "any person" over "registered person" (used in §54(3)) shows the Legislature intended to make the basic refund remedy available to any person who has paid tax, not just registered persons. An unregistered buyer who has borne GST under reverse charge cannot be denied refund by restricting the section to registered persons.
When to Use — When department rejects refund of an unregistered person or a person whose registration was cancelled, arguing they are "not a registered person." Counter: §54(1) says "any person" — registration is not a prerequisite for basic refund claim.
Section 54(7) — Mandatory 60-Day Processing Timeline
Statutory Provision | CGST Act, 2017 | Read with Rule 90 CGST Rules
Key Argument
Section 54(7) uses the word "shall" — the proper officer shall issue the order within 60 days from the date of receipt of application complete in all respects. This is a mandatory, not directory, provision. Under Rule 90(3), if no deficiency memo is issued within 15 days of filing, the application is deemed complete from date of filing. The department cannot belatedly raise deficiencies to reset the 60-day clock after allowing 15 days to pass. Any delay beyond 60 days automatically triggers interest liability under Section 56.
When to Use — When department delays processing refund beyond 60 days without issuing any deficiency memo, then suddenly raises objections. Cite §54(7) + Rule 90(3) together to establish deemed completeness and trigger §56 interest from day 61.
Section 56 — 9% Interest for Post-Appellate Delays
Statutory Provision | CGST Act, 2017 | Higher rate available after appellate victory
Key Argument
Where a refund claim arises from an order that has attained finality — passed by an Adjudicating Authority, Appellate Authority, Appellate Tribunal or Court — and the refund is not paid within 60 days of application consequent to such final order, interest at up to 9% per annum is payable as per Section 56 proviso. The Legislature prescribed a higher interest rate for this situation specifically to penalise departments that delay even after losing at the appellate stage.
When to Use — After winning an appeal/writ that directs a refund, and department still delays payment beyond 60 days from your application. Demand 9% interest (not merely 6%) by citing the proviso to §56. "Attained finality" = no further challenge pending.
Section 54(11) — High Threshold for Withholding Refund
Statutory Provision | CGST Act, 2017 | Two cumulative conditions required
Key Argument
Section 54(11) requires BOTH: (i) proceedings pending on account of the specific refund order, AND (ii) the Commissioner's opinion that grant of refund is likely to adversely affect revenue on account of MALFEASANCE OR FRAUD. The word "malfeasance or fraud" sets a very high threshold — a mere disputed tax liability, pending appeal, or administrative error is NOT malfeasance. Further, the registered person must be given an opportunity of being heard before the refund is withheld. Withholding refund for administrative convenience or mere pendency of appeal — without establishing fraud or malfeasance — is beyond the scope of §54(11).
When to Use — When Commissioner withholds your refund citing §54(11) merely because an appeal is pending, without alleging fraud. Argue: No malfeasance established; opportunity of hearing not given; conditions precedent not satisfied; withholding is illegal.
Section 61(3) — 30-Day Window is a Substantive Safeguard
Statutory Provision | CGST Act, 2017 | Gateway to voluntary compliance without penalty
Key Argument
Section 61(3) grants the registered person 30 days (or extended period) to respond to the discrepancy notice. This is not a formality — it is a statutory opportunity to correct errors and pay taxes before the heavy consequences of §73/74/74A proceedings begin. If the registered person accepts the discrepancy and pays the tax with interest during this period, proceedings under §73 or §74 should not be initiated — the purpose of scrutiny has been served. Using §61(3) response stage to correct errors and pay tax — with 10% or no penalty — avoids the 100% penalty exposure of §74 proceedings.
When to Use — During scrutiny notice reply stage. Use §61 window to pay correct tax + interest → this closes the matter at scrutiny level and prevents escalation to §73/74 SCN. Also argue that after taxpayer's payment on §61 notice, no fresh SCN under §74 (fraud) can be issued for the same discrepancy unless fresh evidence of fraud emerges.
Section 62(2) — Self-Assessment Restoration after §62 Order
Statutory Provision | CGST Act, 2017 | Critical remedy for non-filers
Key Argument
Section 62(2) provides a statutory right to restore the self-assessment regime by filing a valid return within 60 days of service of the §62 assessment order (extendable by further 60 days under the Finance Act 2023 amendment, on payment of ₹100/day additional late fee). Upon valid return filing within the prescribed period, the best-judgment assessment order under §62(1) stands deemed withdrawn. However, the liability to pay interest under §50(1) and late fee under §47 continues — these are not wiped out by the withdrawal of the assessment order.
When to Use — When client has received a §62 assessment order for non-filing. Advise immediate filing of valid return within 60/120 days to avail deemed withdrawal. The assessment order will vanish but interest + late fee survive — assess economics before deciding.
Section 64(2) — Summary Assessment is Withdrawable within 30 Days
Statutory Provision | CGST Act, 2017 | Emergency challenge window
Key Argument
Section 64 summary assessment — passed without prior hearing — carries a built-in 30-day challenge window under §64(2). The taxable person can, within 30 days of receipt, apply to the Additional Commissioner/Joint Commissioner to withdraw the order on grounds that it is erroneous. If the authority considers the order erroneous, it must withdraw the order and follow the full procedure under §73 or §74 or §74A — giving the taxpayer a proper hearing. This 30-day window must be exploited before filing an appeal — it is faster, costs nothing, and results in complete withdrawal rather than modification.
When to Use — Immediately upon receiving a §64 summary assessment order. Within 30 days, file §64(2) application before the sanctioning Add. Commissioner. If refused, then proceed to appeal. Do not let 30-day window lapse waiting for further documents.
Section 65(3) — 15 Working Days Advance Notice is Mandatory
Statutory Provision | CGST Act, 2017 | Audit without notice = void
Key Argument
Section 65(3) mandates that the registered person shall be informed by notice not less than 15 working days prior to the conduct of audit. The word "shall" makes this a mandatory requirement. An audit conducted without proper advance notice of 15 working days (not calendar days — working days excludes holidays and Sundays) is procedurally defective and the resulting audit report — including any findings — cannot form the basis of proceedings under §73/74/74A. The 15-day window is not just a courtesy — it is a statutory right to prepare for audit, organize records, and avail legal counsel.
When to Use — When audit was conducted with less than 15 working days' notice, or with no notice at all. Raise the procedural infirmity at the first opportunity in §65(6) response, and again in any SCN reply arising from audit findings. Demand that the entire audit be conducted afresh with proper notice.
Section 65(6) — Audit Findings Communication is Condition Precedent
Statutory Provision | CGST Act, 2017 | Natural justice codified in statute
Key Argument
Section 65(6) mandates that on conclusion of audit, the proper officer shall, within 30 days, communicate to the registered person: (a) the findings, (b) the registered person's rights and obligations, and (c) the reasons for such findings. This statutory obligation of communication — including reasons — must be fulfilled BEFORE initiating proceedings under §73/74/74A arising from audit. A SCN issued based on audit findings, without a prior §65(6) communication of findings with reasons, bypasses the natural justice safeguard that the Legislature embedded in the law. The proper officer cannot merge the §65(6) communication with the SCN itself.
When to Use — When SCN is issued directly from audit findings without a prior §65(6) notice communicating findings and reasons. Challenge the SCN as premature and procedurally infirm. §65(6) is a pre-condition, not a parallel process.
Section 66(4) — Special Audit Material Must be Shared Before Use
Statutory Provision | CGST Act, 2017 | Audi alteram partem — statutory embodiment
Key Argument
Section 66(4) provides that the registered person shall be given an opportunity of being heard in respect of any material gathered on the basis of special audit under §66(1), which is proposed to be used in proceedings against him. The words "any material" and "proposed to be used" are significant — not just the final audit report, but all underlying material (workpapers, data, computations) must be shared. And the opportunity must be given BEFORE the material is used — not during or after the SCN. Any SCN based on §66 special audit, where the underlying audit material was not first shared with the registered person for response, is void for violation of §66(4).
When to Use — When department issues SCN based on special audit without first sharing the audit workpapers/material and seeking response. File a preliminary objection in SCN reply: §66(4) violated; SCN void; audit material must first be shared and opportunity given before proceedings can continue.
Section 54(3) — Two-Condition Bar on ITC Refund (Inverted Duty)
Statutory Provision | CGST Act, 2017 | Read with Explanation(2)(e) for relevant date
Key Argument
Section 54(3) allows refund of unutilised ITC only in two cases — zero-rated supplies without tax payment, and inverted duty structure. For the inverted duty structure case under §54(3)(ii), the relevant date is the DUE DATE for furnishing return under §39 for the period in which the refund claim arises — not the actual filing date, and not the end of the financial year (as it was prior to the 2018 amendment). This means each monthly period generates a fresh relevant date for refund purposes. The 2-year limitation runs from each period's due date — giving the assessee a rolling refund window period by period.
When to Use — When department argues ITC refund for inverted duty structure is time-barred for older periods. Counter: each period has its own relevant date (due date of GSTR-3B for that period). Period-specific analysis required — blanket time-bar argument is legally incorrect.
For professional litigation use | Pawan Soni & Associates, Ichalkaranji

§ 67 — Power of Inspection, Search & Seizure (Overview)
🔍 Section 67 — Inspection, Search & Seizure
Triggering Officer: Joint Commissioner or above | Written authorisation mandatory
📝 This is the GST department's entry power — the right to walk into premises, inspect, search and seize. But it is NOT a free-wheeling power. Two strict conditions: (1) Officer rank minimum JC; (2) Must have "reasons to believe" — not mere suspicion.
🚦 Two Separate Trigger Scenarios
§67(1)(a) — TAXABLE PERSON
Has suppressed supply transactions
OR suppressed stock in hand
OR claimed excess ITC
OR contravened any provision to evade tax
Power: INSPECTION of place of business
§67(1)(b) — TRANSPORTER / WAREHOUSE
Owner/operator keeping goods on which tax has escaped payment
OR keeping accounts/goods likely to facilitate tax evasion
Power: INSPECTION of place
📶 Escalation: Inspection → Search & Seizure (§67(2))
TRIGGER Pursuant to §67(1) inspection OR otherwise — JC has reasons to believe goods/documents/books liable to confiscation or useful for proceedings are secreted
📖 "SECRETED" — The Critical Threshold Word in §67(2) Black's Law Dictionary (10th Ed.): "Secreted = to conceal (a person or thing) from discovery; to put away or hide." Implies active concealment — not mere non-disclosure. Stroud's Judicial Dictionary: "Secreted implies that the thing is put in a place of hiding deliberately, away from ordinary view."
⚖️ SC in ITO v. Lakhmani Mewal Das (1976) 3 SCC 757: Words in taxing statutes bear their natural, plain meaning. "Secreted" requires deliberate hiding — goods merely unaccounted for, or stored in a warehouse, are NOT "secreted" without additional facts of concealment.
⚖️ Bombay HC in Jitendra Dilipbhai Jain v. Union of India (2022): Mere allegation that goods are secreted without specific information about the hiding place does not satisfy §67(2) threshold. Search order quashed.
🔑 LITIGATION WEAPON: If search is authorised only because goods are "suspected unaccounted" — not specifically secreted — challenge under §67(2). Unaccounted ≠ Secreted.
POWER CONFERRED Authorise in writing any CGST officer to Search & Seize — goods, documents, books, things
📝 "Reasons to believe" is a legal threshold — higher than suspicion. Courts have held this must be based on tangible material. A vague tip-off is not enough.
🔍 GP Singh: "Reasons to believe" must be objectively justifiable — not merely subjective satisfaction. If reasons are absent/vague, the search itself is illegal and all seizures from it tainted (Madhya Pradesh HC — Bhera Impex).
📖 "REASONS TO BELIEVE" — The Constitutional Gateway to §67 Power Black's Law Dictionary: "Reason to believe = A standard requiring the existence of facts or circumstances that would lead a reasonable person to conclude that something exists or has occurred." It is an OBJECTIVE standard — not what the officer thinks, but what a reasonable man in that position would think.
⚖️ SC in ITO v. Lakhmani Mewal Das (1976) 3 SCC 757: "Reasons to believe" must be based on information in possession of officer — must have a rational nexus with the belief formed. Mere suspicion, gossip or rumour is not "reason to believe".
⚖️ SC in Rajesh Jhaveri Stock Brokers v. SEBI (2007): The court can examine whether reasons to believe existed — though it cannot substitute its own opinion. If no reasons recorded = search order void.
🔑 ACTION POINT: In reply to search/seizure — file RTI / formal request seeking the written reasons to believe recorded by JC before authorising search. Absence of recorded reasons = jurisdictional defect.
Continued: §67 Sub-section Details ➜
§ 67 — Sub-section Details (Part B): Procedural Safeguards & Provisos
◀ Back: §67 Overview
Sub-section Provision Key Condition / Safeguard Plain English 📝 Litigation Point ⚔️ GP Singh 🔍
§67(2) Proviso 1 If seizure of goods not practicable → serve order on owner/custodian: do not remove / deal with goods without prior permission Not every seizure needs physical taking away — a deemed seizure by order is valid Like putting a court seal on goods without moving them. You can't sell/transfer sealed goods. If Department moves goods without order — challenge under §67(2) Proviso. Seek return under §67(6). Purposive: Legislature contemplated practical difficulties of seizing heavy/immovable goods — order suffices.
§67(2) Proviso 2 Seized documents/books/things shall be retained only as long as necessary for examination, inquiry or proceedings Duration of retention limited to necessity — not indefinite Department cannot hold your books forever. Once proceedings are over, you get them back. If books retained beyond necessity → file writ for return. Use this proviso. Harmonious reading with §67(3): 30-day return window post-notice issuance. These are two distinct timelines.
§67(3) Documents not relied upon in notice — shall be returned within 30 days of notice issuance Only relied-upon documents can be retained post-notice If you seized 50 folders but used only 5 in the SCN — remaining 45 must come back within 30 days of SCN. Demand return of non-relied documents proactively. Failure to return = grounds for writ + adverse inference argument. GP Singh — "relied upon" must be explicitly stated in SCN. Implicit reliance does not satisfy the condition.
§67(4) Officer may seal/break open doors, almirahs, electronic devices, boxes if access denied and documents/goods suspected concealed Precondition: access must have been denied Department can break a lock to enter — but only if you refused entry. They cannot just barge in. If officer breaks open premises without prior request for access → challenge: precondition not met. Excess of power. Strict construction: "denied" must mean explicit refusal, not mere absence of persons. Night raids on unoccupied premises under §67(4) are challengeable.
§67(5) Person from whose custody docs seized is entitled to copies — at place/time indicated by officer
Except: if copies prejudicially affect investigation		 Right to copies is the default
Denial = exception requiring reasons		 You have a right to photocopy whatever they took from you — unless the officer gives specific reasons why copies would harm investigation. Denial of copies without recording reasons in writing = violation of §67(5). File objection on record. This is a valuable safeguard. "Prejudicially affect" must be recorded — vague assertion insufficient (CPC analogy — SC Prasar Bharati).
§67(6) Seized goods shall be provisionally released on bond + security (as prescribed) OR on payment of tax + interest + penalty Assessee's right — not Department's discretion You can get your goods back during the proceedings by giving a security or paying disputed tax. Use this proactively to prevent business disruption. Provisional release does not prejudice your legal rights. GP Singh — "shall be released" = mandatory, not discretionary. Department cannot deny provisional release if conditions met.
§67(7) If goods seized and no notice within 6 months → goods shall be returned
Extendable by further 6 months for sufficient cause		 Maximum: 12 months. Beyond this, goods must be returned. If they seized your goods but didn't issue a notice within 6 months (max 12), they must give your goods back. Track seizure date. File for return if 6 months pass without notice. No notice = no justification for continued detention. Purposive: Legislature balanced investigation needs against assessee's right to property. Prolonged seizure without notice = violation of Article 300A.
§67(11) Proper officer may seize accounts, registers, documents produced before him — if reasons to believe evasion — with receipt granted Receipt mandatory. Documents can be retained for prosecution. During examination, the officer can retain documents — but must give you a receipt. Demand receipt immediately. No receipt = no valid seizure. Failure to give receipt is procedural illegality. Distinction from §67(2): §67(2) is pursuant to formal search; §67(11) is from voluntary production during inquiry — different procedural chain.
§67(12) Test purchase — Commissioner/authorised officer may cause purchase of goods/services to check tax invoice issuance — Refund to be given by taxable person after cancelling invoice Purpose: verify compliance, not entrapment Tax officer can secretly buy from your shop to check if you issue a proper tax invoice. After check, you refund the money and cancel the invoice. Test purchase report is evidence in SCN. Challenge: was purchase genuine or artificial? Was it properly documented? Analogous to decoy purchases under excise/customs. Narrow power — only to check invoice compliance, not broader tax evasion.
Corresponding Rules: Rules 138–141 CGST Rules, 2017 (Pages 304–320) — E-way bill, conveyance inspection procedures
§ 68 — Inspection of Goods in Movement
🚚 Section 68 — Goods in Movement
E-Way Bill | Conveyance Documents | Interception
📝 This section is the legal basis for E-Way Bill and interception of vehicles carrying goods. It empowers the Government to require that all goods above a threshold must be accompanied by specified documents — the E-Way Bill is the primary document under this.
§68(1)
Government may require person in charge of conveyance carrying goods above notified value to carry prescribed documents & devices
📝 The "prescribed documents" = E-Way Bill (Rule 138). "Devices" = RFID tag on conveyance (Rule 138A).
Rule 138: E-Way Bill generation, validity, cancellation, extension rules
§68(2)
Details of documents shall be valid in such manner as prescribed
📝 Validity of E-Way Bill is distance-based (Rule 138(10)). An expired E-Way Bill is as good as no E-Way Bill.
💡 Goods detained for expired E-Way Bill. Officer invokes §68(3). Assessee must argue validity of extension or contest on facts.
§68(3)
Proper officer intercepts conveyance → requires person in charge to
(i) Produce documents
(ii) Allow inspection of goods
If documents not produced / goods not matching → Detention under §129 | Confiscation risk under §130
🔍 GP Singh: Interception must be on reasonable grounds — random interceptions without any basis have been challenged as arbitrary (Orissa HC — Hindustan Steel).
§ 69 — Power to Arrest
🔒 Section 69 — Power to Arrest
Highest coercive power under CGST Act | Commissioner's Order | Links to §132
📝 Arrest in GST is the nuclear option. Only the Commissioner (not JC or below) can authorise arrest. And only for specific serious offences under Section 132. This is NOT a routine power for all tax evasion cases.
PRECONDITION Commissioner must have reasons to believe that person has committed offence under §132(1)(a), (b), (c), or (d) which is punishable under §132(1)(i), (ii) OR §132(2)
§69(2) — COGNIZABLE OFFENCE (§132(5))
Officer must inform person of grounds
Produce before Magistrate within 24 hours
📝 Cognizable = Police (or authorised officer) can arrest without warrant. Serious offences like tax fraud exceeding ₹5 crore (§132(1)(i)).
🔍 GP Singh: Right to be informed of grounds = constitutional right under Article 22. Non-compliance renders arrest illegal.
§69(3)(a) — COGNIZABLE OFFENCE
Admitted to bail
OR in default of bail → forwarded to Magistrate's custody
§69(3)(b) — NON-COGNIZABLE & BAILABLE
DC/AC has powers of officer in charge of police station for bail purposes
📝 Non-cognizable = Less serious offences (tax fraud ₹1–5 crore). Here bail is a right, not discretion.
§132(1) OFFENCE CHART
(a) Fake invoices / ITC fraud
(b) Issue invoice without supply
(c) Obstruction/prevent/tamper
(d) Failure to supply after collecting tax
Threshold:
>₹5Cr = Cognizable (§132(1)(i))
₹2–5Cr = Non-cognizable (§132(1)(ii))
<₹2Cr = §132(2)
💡 Assessee evades GST of ₹6 crore via fake ITC claims. Commissioner authorises arrest. Officer must produce before Magistrate within 24 hours. Bail application before Sessions Court.
⚠️ CRITICAL SAFEGUARD Before arrest, Commissioner's order in writing is mandatory. Verbal arrest order by Commissioner not valid. Authorisation to "any officer of central tax" must be in writing — not oral direction.
🔍 GP Singh on purposive construction: Arrest power under taxing statute to be construed strictly. Safeguards in §69 (written order, 24-hour production, bail rights) are substantive rights — not procedural technicalities. Violation → habeas corpus (Gujarat HC — Vimal Yashwantgiri Goswami).
§ 70 — Power to Summon Persons to Give Evidence and Produce Documents
📜 Section 70 — Power to Summon
Civil Court Powers | §193 & §228 IPC | Finance (No.2) Act 2024 Amendment
⚔️ WORD WAR — "Inquiry" vs "Enquiry" in §70 — A Deliberate Legislative Choice
ENQUIRY
Administrative asking
Informal information gathering
No judicial consequences
No perjury risk
INQUIRY
Formal quasi-judicial proceeding
Governed by CPC standards
§193 IPC applies (Perjury)
§228 IPC applies (Contempt)
⚖️ §70 uses "INQUIRY" — not "enquiry". This is NOT accidental. Legislature deliberately elevated the GST summons proceeding to the level of a judicial inquiry. Consequence: (1) Full CPC norms apply; (2) False statement = perjury under §193 IPC; (3) Disrespect to officer = contempt under §228 IPC. Courts have held that this distinction is substantive, not merely semantic.
📖 "INQUIRY" — Legal Dictionary Meaning (as used in §70) Black's Law Dictionary: "Inquiry = A request for information; a formal proceeding, usu. Conducted by a government body, to gather facts about a particular matter." Garner's Dictionary of Legal Usage: "Enquiry = mere asking. Inquiry = formal investigation or official examination — implies institutional authority and procedure."
⚖️ SC in Bhopal Sugar Industries v. ITO AIR 1961 SC 182: The choice of the word "inquiry" in a taxing statute is significant — it imports all the procedural safeguards of a judicial proceeding including the right to be heard, right to cross-examine, and obligation to speak the truth.
⚖️ SC in Poolpandi v. Superintendent, Central Excise (1992) 3 SCC 259: Proceedings before an excise officer under summons power = judicial proceedings. False statement = perjury. Applies with equal force to GST §70.
🔑 DUAL USE: (1) FOR ASSESSEE — "inquiry" imports natural justice rights. Department cannot conduct §70 inquiry without affording opportunity. (2) FOR DEPARTMENT — false statement during §70 inquiry = criminal prosecution under §193 IPC.
§70(1) — CORE POWER
Proper officer has power to summon any person
→ to give evidence
→ to produce document / any other thing
in inquiry — same as civil court (CPC)
📝 The GST officer has the same power as a civil court judge to call you and make you talk or produce documents. This is a quasi-judicial power.
§70(1A) — NEW (Finance Act 2024 | w.e.f. 01.11.2024)
All persons summoned are bound to attend — in person or through authorised representative
Must speak the truth during examination
Must produce documents/things required
📝 New amendment: You can no longer ignore a summons or send anyone you like. You must attend personally or through authorised rep and you must tell the truth.
🔍 GP Singh: §70(1A) codifies constitutional obligation to speak truth in judicial proceedings. "Authorised representative" — appears to include advocates, CA — but scope under litigation.
§70(2) — JUDICIAL PROCEEDINGS
Every inquiry under §70(1) is deemed to be "judicial proceedings" under §193 IPC (giving false evidence) and §228 IPC (insult to court/officer)
📝 Lying to a GST officer during summons = same as lying in court = perjury. This is a serious criminal offence under IPC.
💡 Assessee summoned under §70 to explain ITC claims. Gives false statement. Officer can initiate prosecution under §193 IPC for perjury — independent of GST proceedings.
⚠️ PRACTICAL ADVICE On receiving summons: (1) Verify officer's identity and jurisdiction; (2) Attend with CA/advocate; (3) Do not give false or speculative answers; (4) Request copies of all statements recorded; (5) Record objections if any in writing before/after examination
§ 71 — Access to Business Premises | § 72 — Officers to Assist
🏢 §71 — Access to Business Premises & §72 — Assistance by Other Officers
§71(1) — Authorisation
WHO Any officer authorised by proper officer (not below Commissioner rank)
ACCESS TO: Books of account | Documents | Computers | Computer programs | Computer software | Any other things at business premises
PURPOSE Audit | Scrutiny | Verification | Checks to safeguard revenue interest
📝 This is the audit access provision — different from §67 search. Here the purpose is audit/verification, not investigation/seizure.
🔍 GP Singh: §71 and §67 must be read harmoniously — §71 is for planned audit access; §67 is for urgent/emergency enforcement action. Using §67 for routine audit = misuse of power.
§71(2) — Records to be Made Available
(i) Records as prescribed by proper officer
(ii) Trial balance or its equivalent
(iii) Audited Annual Financial Statements
(iv) Cost Audit Report (§148, Companies Act 2013)
(v) Income-tax audit report (§44AB, IT Act 1961)
(vi) Any other relevant record
TIMELINE Within 15 working days from demand date (or extended as allowed by officer/audit party/CA/CMA)
📝 You must provide these within 15 working days. But you can request extension. Non-cooperation with audit team can be used against you in assessment.
💡 IT audit report (Form 3CA/3CB) and ITR are specifically listed. Department routinely cross-verifies GST turnover with IT returns using this access.
§72 — Officers to Assist
MANDATORY (§72(1))
Police | Railways | Customs | Land Revenue | Village Officers | State Tax | UT Tax Officers
→ SHALL assist proper officers
DISCRETIONARY (§72(2))
Government may by notification empower any other class of officers to assist when called upon by Commissioner
📝 If a GST officer needs police help to execute a search/arrest — police are legally obligated to assist. This section gives them the legal cover to do so.
📋 Chapter XV — Demands & Recovery (Sections 73–84) | Rules 142–161
📊 Chapter XV — Broad Overview Tree (All Sections at a Glance)
⚖️ Chapter XV — Demands & Recovery
CGST Act §§ 73–84 | Rules 142–161 CGST Rules, 2017
§73
📋 Non-Fraud Demand
(upto FY 2023-24)
3 yr limitation | 10% penalty
§74
🔴 Fraud Demand
(upto FY 2023-24)
5 yr limitation | 100% penalty
§74A
🆕 UNIFIED SECTION
(FY 2024-25 onwards)
42 mth notice | 12+6 mth order | Finance Act 2024
§75
📐 General Provisions
Stay, opportunity, demand cap, deemed conclusion
§76
💰 Tax Collected Not Paid
Irrespective of taxability | 1 yr order | Interest
§77
↔️ Wrong Head Tax
IGST vs CGST+SGST | Refund | No interest
§78-79
🚨 Recovery
3 months payment window | 6 modes of recovery
§80-84
🛡️ Payment/Protection
Installments | Void transfers | First charge | Provisional attachment
§ 73 — Tax Determination: Non-Fraud / Non-Suppression (upto FY 2023-24)
📋 Section 73 — Tax Not Paid / Short Paid / ITC Wrongly Availed
Non-Fraud | Non-Wilful Misstatement | Non-Suppression | Applicable upto FY 2023-24
📝 Section 73 is the "normal case" — where tax was not paid due to genuine errors, oversight or honest mistakes. No allegation of deliberate fraud. This has a shorter limitation and lower penalty. For FY 2024-25 onwards, §74A applies instead.
Sub-section What Happens Time Limit Penalty Plain English 📝 GP Singh 🔍
§73(1)
Show Cause Notice		 SCN served requiring assessee to show cause why tax + interest + penalty should not be paid SCN ≥ 3 months before order date Penalty as per §73(9) The starting point. Tax shortfall identified → SCN issued. You have right to reply. SCN must specify: exact amount, exact basis, exact period. Omnibus/vague SCN can be challenged (SC — Kesar Enterprises).
§73(5)
Voluntary Payment		 Assessee pays tax + interest before SCN on self-assessment or officer's ascertainment Before SCN NO PENALTY — No SCN to be issued thereafter If you discover a shortfall yourself and pay with interest before SCN — complete immunity from penalty AND no notice issued. GP Singh: "shall not serve any notice" = mandatory prohibition. Department cannot issue SCN after valid §73(5) payment.
§73(8)
Payment within 30 days of SCN		 Assessee pays tax + interest within 30 days of SCN 30 days from SCN NO PENALTY — All proceedings deemed concluded Even after receiving SCN, if you pay within 30 days — zero penalty. Best option when demand is correct. Purposive: Legislature incentivises early compliance by zero penalty under §73. "Deemed concluded" is absolute — no reopening.
§73(9)
Order after Adjudication		 Proper officer determines tax + interest + penalty Within 3 years from due date of annual return for that FY Penalty = 10% of tax OR ₹10,000 — whichever is HIGHER If you don't pay voluntarily, officer passes order after hearing. Penalty is 10% of tax (minimum ₹10,000). Important: 10% of tax or ₹10,000 — higher. So even for very small tax amounts, minimum penalty is ₹10,000.
§73(11)
Exception to §73(8)		 Notwithstanding §73(6) or §73(8) — penalty payable if self-assessed tax OR tax collected remains unpaid beyond 30 days from due date 30 days from due date of payment Penalty mandatory even if paid later Exception: If you had already computed the tax in your return (self-assessed) but didn't pay — penalty applies even if you pay within 30 days of SCN. GP Singh: §73(11) is a carve-out from the benefit of §73(8). Failure to pay self-assessed tax = civil default, not a genuine error — hence penalty warranted.
§73(12) NEW Section 73 applicable only for periods upto FY 2023-24 Inserted vide Finance (No.2) Act 2024 w.e.f. 01.11.2024 — For FY 2024-25 and beyond, §74A is the applicable provision — §73 and §74 are now sunset for future periods. Transitional: SCNs issued under §73 for periods upto FY 2023-24 continue under §73. New periods covered by §74A.
Rules 142–161 cover adjudication, SCN format, reply format, personal hearing, order formats, recovery modes — all under Chapter XV Rules.
§ 74 — Tax Determination: Fraud / Wilful Misstatement / Suppression (upto FY 2023-24)
⚠️ Section 74 — Fraud / Wilful Misstatement / Suppression
Extended limitation | 100% Penalty | Applicable upto FY 2023-24
📝 Section 74 is the "fraud case" — allegation that assessee deliberately hid facts or misrepresented to evade tax. This carries heavier penalties and longer time limits. Key battleground: Can Department prove "suppression" or "wilful misstatement"? If not, §74 proceedings must be converted to §73.
📖 "SUPPRESSION" — The Most Contested Word in GST Demand Law §74A Explanation 2 (statutory definition): "Suppression = non-declaration of facts or information which a taxable person is REQUIRED to declare in the return / statement / report / document, OR failure to furnish information on being ASKED FOR IN WRITING by the proper officer."
⚖️ SC in Pushpam Pharmaceuticals Co. v. CCE (1995) Supp (3) SCC 462: "Suppression means failure to disclose full information with the intent to evade payment of duty. Mere failure to pay duty does not amount to suppression. There must be some positive act of suppression on the part of the assessee." RATIO: Classification dispute, legal interpretation difference = NOT suppression.
⚖️ SC in Anand Nishikawa Co. v. CCE (2005) 7 SCC 749: "Suppression of facts can have only one meaning — that the manufacturer should have known that the facts were required to be stated but he did not state them. There was a deliberate act of omission." RATIO: Innocent omission ≠ suppression. Guilty mind (mens rea) required.
⚖️ SC in Continental Foundation Jt. Venture v. CCE (2007) 5 SCC 239: "Willful misstatement and suppression of facts are coupled with the expression 'to evade payment of duty'. Therefore, unless there is intent to evade, mere non-disclosure is not suppression."
🔑 THE TRINITY TEST FOR SUPPRESSION (from case law): (1) Non-disclosure of facts; (2) Which assessee KNEW were required to be disclosed; (3) WITH INTENT to evade tax. All three must coexist. If any one absent — §74 fails → §73 applies → 10% penalty only.
Sub-section What Happens Time Limit Penalty Plain English 📝 GP Singh 🔍
§74(1)
SCN — Fraud		 SCN alleging fraud / wilful misstatement / suppression — requiring tax + interest + penalty equivalent to full tax SCN ≥ 6 months before order date Full tax amount as penalty (100%) When Department alleges you deliberately hid supply/ITC — they can issue SCN with up to 100% penalty. GP Singh: Allegation of fraud must be specifically pleaded and proved — not inferred. Mere non-payment is not fraud (SC — Pushpam Pharmaceuticals).
§74(3)
Statement for Additional Periods		 Where notice issued for any period under §74(1), proper officer may serve a statement for other periods — containing details of tax not paid / ITC wrongly availed — instead of issuing fresh SCN for each period Same proceedings — no separate fresh SCN required for each additional period As per applicable sub-section Department can cover multiple years in one shot — instead of issuing separate SCNs for each year, they issue one main SCN and then a statement for remaining years. GP Singh: The statement under §74(3) is a procedural device to save multiplicity of notices — but it is strictly governed by the conditions in §74(4). A statement without satisfying §74(4) conditions is not a valid deemed notice.
§74(4) ⭐ CRITICAL
Deemed §73(1) Notice
