— The Hidden Weapon		 Service of statement under §74(3) shall be deemed to be service of notice under §73(1) — subject to condition that the grounds relied upon for such additional periods, EXCEPT the ground of fraud / wilful misstatement / suppression, are the same as mentioned in the earlier §74(1) notice ⚠️ DEEMING FICTION:
Additional periods covered by §74(3) statement → Travel under §73, NOT §74
(unless fraud grounds separately established for those periods too)		 CONSEQUENCE = Only 10% penalty (§73 rate) for additional periods — NOT 100% penalty (§74 rate)
Unless Department separately pleads and proves fraud for each additional period		 This is the hidden weapon. Department issues main SCN for FY2021-22 alleging fraud (100% penalty). Then issues §74(3) statement for FY2020-21 and FY2019-20. For those additional years — §74(4) says they are treated as §73 notices — meaning penalty can only be 10%, NOT 100%. Department often wrongly applies 100% penalty to all years. 🔍 GP Singh — Deeming provisions must be construed strictly and cannot be extended beyond their express terms. §74(4) explicitly carves out the additional periods into §73 track. This is a substantive right — not a technicality. The condition "except the ground of fraud" means: fraud must be independently established for each additional period, not borrowed from the main SCN period. (Compare: §73(4) has same structure for non-fraud statements — both §73(4) and §74(4) work on the same "same grounds" principle).
§74(5)
Voluntary Payment Before SCN		 Pay tax + interest + 15% penalty before SCN on own ascertainment Before SCN Only 15% — No SCN thereafter Even in fraud cases, if you pay voluntarily before SCN with 15% penalty — proceedings stop. Saves from 100% penalty. Compare with §73(5) — zero penalty. Under §74, even voluntary payment attracts 15% (acknowledgment of wrongdoing presumed).
§74(8)
Payment within 30 days of SCN		 Pay tax + interest + 25% penalty within 30 days of SCN 30 days from SCN 25% penalty — All proceedings deemed concluded Post-SCN early payment cuts penalty from 100% to 25%. Legislative policy: Reward early compliance even in fraud cases — but minimum acknowledgment of wrongdoing via 25% penalty.
§74(9)
Order after Adjudication		 Proper officer determines tax + interest + penalty = tax amount Within 5 years from due date of annual return for that FY 100% of tax as penalty If you don't settle — full 100% penalty confirmed in order after hearing. 5 years vs 3 years under §73. Extended period justified by fraud — but burden of proving fraud is on Department (SC — Nizam Sugar Factory).
§74(11)
Payment within 30 days of Order		 Pay tax + interest + 50% penalty within 30 days of order communication 30 days from order 50% penalty — All proceedings deemed concluded (saves appeal costs) Even after losing at adjudication — paying within 30 days cuts penalty from 100% to 50%. This is a settlement incentive at post-order stage. "All proceedings deemed concluded" includes appeals — important for finality.
§74(12) NEW Section 74 applicable only for periods upto FY 2023-24 Inserted vide Finance (No.2) Act 2024 w.e.f. 01.11.2024 — Like §73 — §74 is now sunset. FY 2024-25 onwards → §74A covers both fraud and non-fraud. Important transitional challenge: Which section governs SCN issued after 01.11.2024 for period upto FY 2023-24? Answer: §73/§74 as applicable.
§ 74A — NEW Unified Section: All Demands for FY 2024-25 Onwards (Finance Act 2024)
🆕 Section 74A — Inserted by Finance (No.2) Act 2024 | w.e.f. 01.11.2024
Unified Provision | Applicable from FY 2024-25 Onwards | No bifurcation into §73/§74
📝 This is the biggest change in GST enforcement in 2024. Instead of having two separate tracks (§73 for non-fraud, §74 for fraud), §74A creates ONE unified SCN procedure for all demands from FY 2024-25. Fraud vs non-fraud distinction remains ONLY for penalty quantum — not for notice/order timelines.
🔍 GP Singh on §74A: Unification is legislative simplification — but it removes the clear pre-notice signals of "fraud" or "non-fraud" from the SCN stage. Assessees must now carefully watch penalty clauses in SCN to understand which sub-section of §74A(5) is being invoked.
Sub-section Provision Time Limit Plain English 📝 Litigation Point ⚔️
§74A(1)
SCN		 Unified SCN for all cases — tax not paid / short paid / ITC wrongly availed — regardless of whether fraud or not
Proviso: No notice if tax shortfall < ₹1,000 per year		 — Single SCN for all cases. New de-minimis limit: no notice if tax gap < ₹1,000/year (saves small-taxpayer harassment). Check ₹1,000 threshold carefully — Department may try to cumulate multiple periods to cross threshold.
§74A(2)
Notice Deadline		 Notice must be issued within 42 months from due date of annual return OR from date of erroneous refund 42 months (3.5 years) — applicable to both fraud and non-fraud Under old law: §73 = 3 yr, §74 = 5 yr. Now unified at 42 months (3.5 yr) for all cases — regardless of fraud. Major change: Fraud cases now have SHORTER limitation than before (42 months vs 5 years). Track notice dates carefully.
§74A(5)
Penalty Structure		 Non-Fraud: 10% of tax or ₹10,000 (whichever higher)
Fraud/Wilful/Suppression: 100% (= full tax amount) 		— Penalty distinction retained — only the route to SCN is unified. Non-fraud = 10%; Fraud = 100%. SCN must clearly specify which limb of §74A(5) is being invoked. If SCN is ambiguous, challenge specificity.
§74A(7)
Order Deadline		 Order within 12 months from notice date
Extendable by further 6 months with Commissioner/Jt.Commissioner approval (reasons in writing, before expiry)		 Max: 18 months from notice If order not passed within 12 months (or 18 months with extension) — proceedings deemed concluded (§75(10) as amended). Track 12-month order deadline. If order not passed in time → file application for proceedings to be deemed concluded.
§74A(8)
Benefit for Non-Fraud		 (i) Pay before notice → No penalty, no notice issued
(ii) Pay within 60 days of SCN → No penalty, proceedings concluded		 60 days (vs 30 days under §73) Non-fraud assessee gets 60 days to pay (vs 30 under old §73). Extended window is an improvement. Use 60-day window strategically. Paying within 60 days = zero penalty and proceedings end.
§74A(9)
Benefit for Fraud		 (i) Pay before notice: 15% penalty → no notice
(ii) Pay within 60 days of SCN: 25% penalty → concluded
(iii) Pay within 60 days of order: 50% penalty → concluded		 60 days at each stage Fraud cases: 15% → 25% → 50% at successive stages. Same structure as §74 but with 60 days instead of 30 days. 60 days is more reasonable than 30 days. But 15%/25%/50% rates are same as §74. Fraud classification challenge remains critical.
§74A(12) Applicable for FY 2024-25 onwards w.e.f. 01.11.2024 §74A covers FY 2024-25+; §73/§74 cover upto FY 2023-24. Clean cut-off. Transitional disputes expected: SCNs for FY 2023-24 issued after 01.11.2024 — still under §73/§74, not §74A.
⚖️ Side-by-Side Comparison: §73 vs §74 vs §74A
🔑 Master Comparison — Demands & Penalty Structure (The Critical Chart for Every Hearing)
Parameter §73 — Non-Fraud
(upto FY 2023-24)		 §74 — Fraud
(upto FY 2023-24)		 §74A — Unified
(FY 2024-25 onwards)
Trigger Tax not paid — any reason EXCEPT fraud/wilful misstatement/suppression Tax not paid BY REASON of fraud / wilful misstatement / suppression Any reason — unified. Fraud vs non-fraud relevant ONLY for penalty.
SCN Advance Notice before Order Minimum 3 months before order Minimum 6 months before order No "advance" rule — Notice within 42 months from ARD
Order Limitation 3 years from due date of AR 5 years from due date of AR 12 months from notice (+6 mth extension = max 18 months)
Voluntary payment before SCN 0% penalty — No SCN 15% penalty — No SCN Non-fraud: 0% penalty
Fraud: 15% penalty
Payment within days of SCN 0% penalty — within 30 days 25% penalty — within 30 days Non-fraud: 0% — within 60 days
Fraud: 25% — within 60 days
Payment after Adjudication Order No specific concession post-order under §73 50% penalty — within 30 days of order Fraud: 50% — within 60 days of order
Full Penalty (if no concession) 10% of tax or ₹10,000 (higher) 100% of tax Non-fraud: 10% or ₹10,000
Fraud: 100%
Time to Pay (post-SCN) 30 days 30 days 60 days (improved)
Deemed Conclusion Rule If order not within 3 yrs — deemed concluded If order not within 5 yrs — deemed concluded If order not within 12 mths (or 18 mths) — deemed concluded (§75(10))
Min. Notice Threshold No minimum No minimum ₹1,000 per FY minimum — no notice below this
§ 75 — General Provisions Relating to Determination of Tax
📐 Section 75 — General Safeguards & Procedural Rules in Adjudication
📝 Section 75 contains the procedural fairness rules that apply to ALL adjudication (§73, §74, §74A). These are the assessee's procedural rights — opportunity of hearing, demand cap, interest auto-applies, deemed conclusion, etc.
Sub-section Provision Key Point Litigation Use ⚔️
§75(1) Period of court/tribunal stay excluded from limitation computation under §73(2)/(10), §74(2)/(10), §74A(2)/(7) Stay stops the clock — Department gets time back after stay is vacated Check if any previous stay on same period was granted — it extends Department's limitation. Often missed in limitation calculations.
§75(2) If §74 charges (fraud/suppression) NOT established by AA/Tribunal/Court → Proper officer shall determine tax under §73 §74 → §73 conversion mandated when fraud not proved Critical: When contesting §74 SCN — argue fraud not established → §73 applies → 10% penalty instead of 100%. Use §75(2) explicitly in reply.
§75(2A) NEW If §74A(5)(ii) fraud penalty NOT established → penalty under §74A(5)(i) (non-fraud = 10%) applies Same conversion principle under §74A — new equivalent of §75(2) For §74A SCNs — argue fraud not proved → §74A(5)(i) penalty = 10% only. Cite §75(2A) in reply.
§75(4) Opportunity of hearing shall be granted where:
(a) Written request received from assessee, OR
(b) Any adverse decision contemplated		 Mandatory — not discretionary If PH denied or ignored → order is void for violation of natural justice (SC — Maneka Gandhi principle). File objection on record.
§75(5) Proper officer shall grant time to assessee if sufficient cause shown — adjourn for reasons in writing
Proviso: Maximum 3 adjournments per proceedings		 3 adjournments maximum — not unlimited Use 3 adjournments wisely. After 3rd, officer can proceed ex-parte. Track adjournment count — Department cannot deny adjournment without written reasons.
§75(7) Demand confirmed in order shall NOT exceed the amount in SCN. No new grounds in order not in SCN. Order bound by SCN — cannot travel beyond it If order raises new ground / new amount not in SCN → void. Challenge under §75(7). SC principle: Demand > SCN = jurisdictional error (Union of India v. Hindustan Development Corp.).
§75(9) Interest on tax short paid shall be payable whether or not specified in the order Interest is automatic — not dependent on officer specifying it Cannot argue "order doesn't mention interest so no interest payable." Interest under §50 runs automatically.
§75(10) AMENDED Adjudication proceedings deemed concluded if order NOT issued within time specified in §73(10), §74(10), or §74A(7) Barred-by-limitation order = void + proceedings concluded Track order deadline precisely. If deadline passes without order → file letter/application citing §75(10) and §74A(7) — proceedings deemed concluded. Department cannot subsequently pass order.
§75(11) Pending appeal against revenue-unfavorable decision at higher court → period between decisions excluded from §73(10)/§74(10)/§74A(7) limitation Appellate pendency also stops the clock Complex calculation when appeals pending. Keep track of all pending appeal dates that may affect limitation.
§75(12) Self-assessed tax that remains unpaid (wholly or partly) or interest thereon → recovered directly under §79 without invoking §73/§74/§74A No SCN needed for self-assessed unpaid tax — direct recovery Critical: If you filed GSTR-3B showing tax payable but didn't pay → Department can directly issue recovery notice under §79 without any SCN. No procedural safeguards of §73/§74.
§75(13) No double penalty for same act under other provisions if penalty already imposed under §73/§74/§74A No double jeopardy for same act If penalised under §73 — Department cannot separately penalise for same act under §122 or §125. Cite §75(13) to bar double penalty.
§ 76 — Tax Collected But Not Paid | § 77 — Tax on Wrong Head
💰 §76 & §77 — Two Special Demand Scenarios
§76 — TAX COLLECTED NOT PAID
§76(1) Any person who collected any amount from another person as tax under GST and has NOT paid it to the Government — must forthwith pay it
Irrespective of whether supplies were taxable or not
📝 If you collected GST from your customer but didn't deposit it — you must pay it to the Government. Even if it turns out the supply was exempt and you shouldn't have charged GST at all — you still must pay what you collected.
🔍 GP Singh: "Forthwith" = immediately, without waiting for any notice or order. This is strict liability — no defence of "supply was exempt" once you collected the tax.
§76(6) Order within 1 year from notice date
§76(4) Interest from date of collection to date of payment to Government (§50 rate)
💡 Service provider charges GST on a transaction later held exempt. Collected ₹10 lakh GST. Must pay ₹10 lakh to Government under §76 + interest, even though ultimately no tax was due. Separately can apply for refund under §76(11).
§76(9)/(10)/(11) Surplus after adjustment → credited to Fund OR refunded to person who bore incidence
Person may apply for refund under §54
§77 — TAX ON WRONG HEAD
§77(1) — Intra-State paid, later held Inter-State
Paid CGST+SGST on transaction considered intra-state → later held inter-state → refund of CGST+SGST
§77(2) — Inter-State paid, later held Intra-State
Paid IGST on transaction considered inter-state → later held intra-state → No interest on CGST+SGST now due
📝 This is the "honest mistake" provision — paid tax under wrong head (IGST vs CGST+SGST). Government protects you: get refund of wrong head AND no interest on correct head for the period of honest mistake.
🔍 GP Singh: §77 read with §50 — Interest relief under §77(2) applies only when the wrong IGST was actually paid. If not paid — §77 protection does not flow.
💡 Supplier treats services as intra-state and pays CGST+SGST. Later GSTAT holds it inter-state. §77(1) allows refund of CGST+SGST. Then pays IGST without interest (protection of §77(2)).
§ 78 — Initiation of Recovery | § 79 — Recovery of Tax (6 Modes)
🚨 Recovery of Tax — §78 (When) + §79 (How)
§78 — WHEN Recovery Starts
GENERAL RULE
Payment due within 3 months from date of order service
If not paid → recovery proceedings initiated
EXPEDITED
If proper officer considers it expedient for revenue → may require payment in less than 3 months (reasons in writing)
📝 Normally 3 months after order to pay. But if officer thinks money will disappear — can require earlier payment. "Expedient for revenue" must have factual basis.
🔍 GP Singh: Expedited recovery power to be exercised judiciously. Mechanical exercise without specific reasons = challengeable by writ (Bombay HC precedent on similar excise provisions).
§79(1) — SIX MODES OF RECOVERY
(a) Deduction from money owed TO taxable person — under proper officer's control
(b) Detention & sale of goods belonging to taxable person
(c) Notice to third party owing money to taxable person (Garnishee Notice) → bank, debtor, insurer must pay Government directly
(d) Distress & sale of movable/immovable property
(e) Certificate to District Collector → recover as arrear of land revenue
(f) Application to Magistrate → recover as fine imposed by Magistrate
💡 Department sends Garnishee Notice (§79(1)(c)) to bank directing it to deduct from assessee's bank account. Bank must comply — no need for FD/passbook. Assessee can challenge via writ if order itself is under appeal.
§79(1)(c) — GARNISHEE NOTICE KEY RULES
(iii) Third party ignoring notice = defaulter for that amount
(vii) If third party proves no money was owed to defaulter at time of notice — not liable
(v) Payment by third party in compliance = good discharge of liability to defaulter
(vi) If third party pays defaulter AFTER notice — personally liable to Government
📝 If someone owes you money (your customer) and Department issues garnishee notice to them — they must pay Government directly. If they pay you instead — they become personally liable.
🔍 GP Singh: Garnishee notice is not a demand on the third party — it is a diversion of existing liability. Third party must verify genuinely owed money at time of notice before complying.
§ 80–84 — Instalments | Void Transfers | First Charge | Provisional Attachment | Recovery Continuation
🛡️ Sections 80–84 — Special Provisions: Payment, Property Protection & Recovery Continuity
Section Provision Key Condition Plain English 📝 GP Singh 🔍 / Litigation ⚔️
§80
Instalments		 Commissioner may allow payment of tax (other than self-assessed) in instalments — maximum 24 instalments — with interest under §50 Application required | Reasons in writing
Default in ANY instalment → entire balance becomes immediately payable (no further notice)		 If you cannot pay the full GST demand at once — apply to Commissioner for 24-month instalment plan. But miss one instalment → full amount due immediately. Instalment facility does NOT stop interest under §50. Interest runs on outstanding balance. "Self-assessed" tax not covered — only adjudicated demands. Default clause is automatic — no notice needed.
§81
Void Transfer		 Any charge / transfer / sale / mortgage / exchange of property made by taxable person after tax becomes due — with intention to defraud Government revenue — is VOID as against Government Exception: Transfer is NOT void if: (i) Made for adequate consideration + (ii) In good faith + (iii) Without notice of pending proceedings/tax liability + (iv) With prior permission of proper officer If you're trying to evade GST recovery by transferring property to relatives/associates after tax demand — that transfer is void. Government can still attach and recover from that "transferred" property. GP Singh: "Intention to defraud" — burden of proof on Department. Mere transfer after demand ≠ fraudulent transfer. Must show: no consideration OR undervalued + close relationship + proximate timing. (CA Pvt Ltd v. UOI — Madras HC).
§82
First Charge		 Any amount payable as tax/interest/penalty to Government = FIRST CHARGE on property of taxable person — overrides all other laws
Exception: Insolvency and Bankruptcy Code, 2016		 Overrides banks, secured creditors, all laws except IBC Government's tax dues have first priority over all your property — senior to bank mortgage, secured lenders — with one exception: IBC proceedings. Critical: In insolvency/resolution plans — IBC charge rank above GST. In all other situations (attachment, recovery) — GST is first charge. SC — Rainbow Papers case on IBC vs GST priority.
§83
Provisional Attachment		 Commissioner may provisionally attach property (including bank accounts) belonging to taxable person or §122(1A) person — where proceedings pending under Ch.XII, XIV or XV
Cease automatically after 1 year from order date		 Purpose: Protect revenue interest
Max duration: 1 year
Covers: Any property incl. bank accounts		 During GST proceedings — Commissioner can freeze your bank account or attach property as a protective measure. But this attachment automatically expires after 1 year. GP Singh: Provisional attachment is a draconian power — must be used sparingly and with due application of mind. Courts (SC — Radha Krishan Industries) have held that mechanical exercise of §83 power is impermissible. Must show: specific reasons why property will be dissipated. 1-year automatic expiry is a safeguard — cannot be renewed without fresh order.
§84
Recovery Continuation		 Where Government dues are in appeal/revision and:
(a) Enhanced → Commissioner issues fresh notice for enhanced amount; existing proceedings continue for original amount
(b) Reduced → No fresh notice; Commissioner gives intimation; recovery continues for reduced amount		 Recovery proceedings survive the appeal — they continue from where they stood If your tax demand is appealed and outcome changes the amount — recovery proceedings don't restart from zero. They continue from where they were, for the revised amount. Important: If demand reduced on appeal — Department must give formal intimation under §84(b)(ii). Without intimation, recovery proceedings for original amount may continue — leading to over-recovery. Watch for this in practice.
Rules 142–161 CGST Rules (Pages 321–331) cover: Rule 142 (Notice/Statement/Order Forms), Rule 143 (Recovery by deduction), Rule 144 (Recovery by selling goods), Rule 145 (Recovery of tax debts by garnishee), Rule 146–161 (various recovery and payment procedures)
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal — Chapters XIV & XV | Inspection, Search, Seizure & Demands
These ratios and statutory arguments are for use in personal hearings, appeals, and replies to SCN. State them confidently and cite the provision/case name. Applicable before Adjudicating Authority, First Appellate Authority, GSTAT, and High Court.
Section 67 — "Reasons to Believe" Threshold
CGST Act, §67(1) & §67(2) | Read with Constitutional requirements of Article 19 & Article 300A
Key Principle
The power of search and seizure under Section 67 is not a free-wheeling power. The Joint Commissioner must have "reasons to believe" — which is a higher threshold than mere suspicion. Such reasons must be based on tangible, objective material and must be recorded in writing before authorising the search. A search conducted without objectively justifiable reasons to believe is illegal, and all seizures made pursuant to such an illegal search are tainted and liable to be returned.
When to Use — When challenging legality of search/seizure where Department acted on vague information or suspicion. Demand production of written reasons to believe. If reasons absent — challenge seizure, seek return of documents under §67(3) and provisional release under §67(6).
Section 67(3) — 30-Day Return of Non-Relied Documents
CGST Act, §67(3) | Mandatory statutory obligation on Department
Key Principle
The documents, books or things seized under Section 67(2) which have not been relied upon in the notice issued under the Act shall be returned to the person within a period not exceeding thirty days of the issue of the said notice. This is a mandatory obligation — not a discretion. The Department cannot retain non-relied documents indefinitely as a pressure tactic.
When to Use — After receipt of SCN. Proactively demand return of all documents not cited in the SCN within 30 days. If not returned — file formal objection citing §67(3). Non-return is procedural illegality and can be used as ground in appeal/writ.
Section 67(7) — Automatic Return of Goods on Non-Issue of Notice
CGST Act, §67(7) | Mandatory return within 6 months (max 12 months)
Key Principle
Where any goods are seized under sub-section (2) and no notice in respect thereof is given within six months of the seizure of the goods, the goods shall be returned to the person from whose possession they were seized. The maximum extension is six months for sufficient cause shown — making the absolute maximum twelve months. Beyond twelve months, continued retention of goods without notice is illegal detention of property.
When to Use — Track seizure date. If 6 months pass without notice — file application for return of goods citing §67(7). File writ if not returned. Article 300A (Right to Property) grounds available if goods retained beyond 12 months without notice.
Section 70(1A) — Summons: Rights and Obligations (New — w.e.f. 01.11.2024)
CGST Act, §70(1A) inserted by Finance (No.2) Act 2024 | Read with §70(2) and Article 22
Key Principle
Under Section 70(1A), all persons summoned are bound to attend either in person or through an authorised representative as directed, and the person appearing shall speak the truth during examination. Every inquiry under Section 70(1) is deemed to be judicial proceedings under Sections 193 and 228 of the IPC. Consequently, false statements before a GST officer during summons proceedings are equivalent to perjury before a court. The right to attend through an authorised representative is equally protected — any restriction on this right undermines the constitutional safeguard.
When to Use — When assessee is summoned under §70: (1) Assert right to attend through authorised representative (CA/Advocate); (2) Request advance copy of questions/issues; (3) Request copy of statement recorded; (4) If officer restricts representative's presence — object in writing on record citing §70(1A).
Section 75(2) / §75(2A) — Conversion from Fraud to Non-Fraud Track
CGST Act, §75(2) | §75(2A) (new) | Supreme Court — Pushpam Pharmaceuticals v. CCE [1995 Supp (3) SCC 462]
Key Principle
Where any Appellate Authority or Appellate Tribunal or court concludes that the notice under sub-section (1) of section 74 is not sustainable for the reason that the charges of fraud or any wilful-misstatement or suppression of facts to evade tax has not been established against the person to whom the notice was issued, the proper officer shall determine the tax payable by such person, deeming as if the notice were issued under sub-section (1) of section 73. The Supreme Court in Pushpam Pharmaceuticals has held that suppression means conscious withholding of information — mere non-payment or filing a different classification is NOT suppression.
When to Use — When Department issues SCN under §74 (fraud/suppression) without specific factual basis. Argue: (1) No suppression — all transactions were disclosed in returns; (2) Classification dispute ≠ fraud; (3) If SCN under §74 fails — §75(2) mandates conversion to §73 with only 10% penalty. This converts 100% penalty to 10% — a 90% savings. For §74A SCNs, use §75(2A) similarly.
Section 75(7) — Demand Cannot Exceed SCN Amount
CGST Act, §75(7) | Affirmed repeatedly — Union of India v. Hindustan Development Corporation [1994 (3) SCC 499]
Key Principle
The amount of tax, interest and penalty demanded in the order shall not be in excess of the amount specified in the notice and no demand shall be confirmed on the grounds other than the grounds specified in the notice. An adjudication order that travels beyond the show cause notice — in quantum or in grounds — is without jurisdiction and void ab initio. The show cause notice defines and limits the adjudicating officer's jurisdiction.
When to Use — When adjudication order: (i) demands more tax than SCN; or (ii) raises new grounds not in SCN; or (iii) adds new period not covered by SCN. Cite §75(7) — entire excess demand is void. This is a jurisdictional point — can be raised at any stage including before GSTAT/HC.
Section 75(10) — Deemed Conclusion for Time-Barred Orders
CGST Act, §75(10) as amended by Finance (No.2) Act 2024 | Read with §73(10), §74(10), §74A(7)
Key Principle
The adjudication proceedings shall be deemed to be concluded if the order is not issued within the period specified in sub-section (10) of section 73 or in sub-section (10) of section 74 or in sub-section (7) of section 74A. When proceedings are deemed concluded by efflux of time, the adjudicating officer loses jurisdiction to pass any order. Any order passed after the deemed conclusion date is void and without jurisdiction — not merely irregular.
When to Use — Track limitation dates precisely: §73 = 3 years from ARD; §74 = 5 years from ARD; §74A = 12 months from notice (18 months with extension). If order is passed beyond the deadline — file objection citing §75(10). If ignored — challenge in appeal/writ. Deemed conclusion = death of proceedings.
Section 75(4) — Mandatory Opportunity of Personal Hearing
CGST Act, §75(4) | Principles of Natural Justice | Maneka Gandhi v. Union of India [1978 (1) SCC 248] | Reasoned order requirement
Key Principle
An opportunity of hearing shall be granted where a request is received in writing from the person chargeable with tax or penalty, or where any adverse decision is contemplated against such person. This statutory mandate to provide personal hearing is in addition to the constitutional requirement of natural justice. An order passed without granting personal hearing despite a written request, or where an adverse order was passed without any opportunity, is void for violation of natural justice.
When to Use — Always file written request for personal hearing in SCN reply. If order passed without PH despite request — challenge at first appeal level. §75(6) also requires order to set out relevant facts and basis of decision — non-speaking/vague orders are equally challengeable.
Section 83 — Provisional Attachment: Radha Krishan Industries Principle
CGST Act, §83 | Supreme Court — Radha Krishan Industries v. State of Himachal Pradesh [2021 (5) SCC 299]
Key Principle (SC Ratio)
Provisional attachment under Section 83 is a drastic power which has a serious civil consequence of depriving the taxable person of the use and benefit of their assets. It must be exercised with utmost care and caution. The formation of opinion by the Commissioner must be based on tangible material showing that the taxable person is likely to dissipate assets to defeat revenue claims. A mechanical or routine exercise of the power of provisional attachment — without specific application of mind — is impermissible. The remedy of the aggrieved party is to approach the High Court under Article 226.
When to Use — When bank account or property attached under §83: (1) File objection demanding reasons for attachment; (2) Argue no tangible material showing dissipation of assets; (3) Cite Radha Krishan Industries — mechanical attachment is impermissible; (4) File writ under Article 226 for release; (5) Provisional attachment expires automatically after 1 year — track date and file for release.
⭐ Section 74(4) — The "Deemed §73 Notice" — Hidden Weapon Against 100% Penalty on Additional Periods
CGST Act, §74(4) | Read with §73(1) and §75(2) | Applicable to all SCNs under §74 for periods upto FY 2023-24
Key Statutory Principle
The service of statement under sub-section (3) of Section 74 shall be deemed to be service of notice on such person under sub-section (1) of Section 73 — subject to the condition that the grounds relied upon for such tax periods, other than those covered under sub-section (1) of Section 74, are the same as are mentioned in the earlier notice — EXCEPT the ground of fraud, or any wilful-misstatement or suppression of facts to evade tax. This means: When Department issues one §74(1) SCN for a primary period (say FY 2021-22) alleging fraud, and then issues a §74(3) statement covering additional periods (FY 2020-21, FY 2019-20) — those additional periods are DEEMED to be under Section 73(1), not Section 74(1). The consequence is that the maximum penalty applicable to those additional periods is only 10% (§73 rate) — not 100% (§74 rate) — unless the Department separately establishes fraud for each of those additional periods independently.
Practical Impact
Department routinely issues §74(3) statements for 4-5 years and then confirms 100% penalty for all years in the adjudication order — riding on the fraud allegation of the main SCN year. This is legally untenable. The deeming fiction in §74(4) expressly confines the additional-period statement to §73(1) track. Any order confirming 100% penalty for §74(3) statement years — without independently establishing fraud for those years — is bad in law and liable to be set aside.
When to Use — When SCN covers multiple years with §74 fraud allegations: (1) Identify which year has the main §74(1) SCN and which years are covered by §74(3) statement; (2) In SCN reply — specifically invoke §74(4) for statement-covered years; (3) Argue: for statement years → only §73 penalty (10%) applies; (4) If order confirms 100% for statement years — challenge in appeal citing §74(4) + §75(2); (5) This single argument can reduce penalty by 90% for all years except the primary SCN year.
Section 74A — New Unified Demand Procedure (FY 2024-25 onwards)
CGST Act, §74A inserted by Finance (No.2) Act 2024 | w.e.f. 01.11.2024
Key Principles — Three Tactical Points
First: Under §74A, notice must be issued within 42 months from the due date of annual return — this is applicable to BOTH fraud and non-fraud cases, bringing fraud limitation DOWN from 5 years to 3.5 years. Second: The order must be passed within 12 months of notice (extendable to 18 months by Commissioner/JC level officer with reasons in writing) — this creates a hard deadline unlike the older 3/5 year outer limit. Third: For non-fraud cases, no penalty if paid within 60 days of SCN — giving 30 more days than under §73. These three features together create a tighter, faster adjudication cycle with more pressure on Department to act promptly.
When to Use — For all cases FY 2024-25 onwards: (1) Challenge SCN issued beyond 42 months (§74A(2) limitation); (2) File for deemed conclusion if order not passed within 12/18 months (§74A(7) + §75(10)); (3) Use 60-day window for zero-penalty payment in non-fraud cases; (4) Demand that SCN specify which limb of §74A(5) (fraud or non-fraud) is being invoked — ambiguity in SCN → attack specificity of notice.
For professional litigation use | Pawan Soni & Associates, Ichalkaranji
CGST Act — Ch.XVI–XVIII (Liability, AR, Appeals)

⚖️ Chapters XVI · XVII · XVIII — CGST Act, 2017

Liability in Certain Cases · Advance Ruling · Appeals & Revision (Sections 85–121)

Source: CGST Act, 2017 (Bare Law — ICAI Nov 2025) | CA Pawankumar Soni, Ichalkaranji

Permitted / Valid
Prohibited / Void / Penalty
Condition / Unless / If
Optional / Discretionary
Neutral Reference
Rule / Procedural Cross-ref
📝 Plain English
💡 Example
🔍 GP Singh Interpretation
📋 Rule Cross-Reference
📋 Index — Chapters XVI · XVII · XVIII
CHAPTER XVI — LIABILITY TO PAY IN CERTAIN CASES
§85 Transfer of Business
§86 Agent & Principal
§87 Amalgamation / Merger
§88 Company in Liquidation
§89 Directors of Pvt. Company
§90 Partners of Firm
§91 Guardians, Trustees
§92 Court of Wards
§93 Special Provisions — Death / HUF / Dissolution
§94 Liability in Other Cases
CHAPTER XVII — ADVANCE RULING
§95 Definitions
§96–97 Authority & Application
§98 Procedure on Receipt
§99–101 Appeal to AAAR
§101A–101C National Appellate Authority (NAAR)
§102–106 Rectification, Applicability, Void, Powers
CHAPTER XVIII — APPEALS AND REVISION
§107 First Appeal — Appellate Authority
§108 Revisional Authority
§109 Constitution of GSTAT
§110 Qualifications & Appointment of Members
§111–112 Procedure & Appeals to GSTAT
§113–116 Orders, Interest on Refund, Authorised Rep.
§117–121 High Court, SC, Non-Appealable Orders
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal
CHAPTER XVI — LIABILITY TO PAY IN CERTAIN CASES (§§ 85–94)
Section 85 — Liability in Case of Transfer of Business
🏭 §85 — Transfer of Business
Joint & Several Liability on Transferor + Transferee
TRIGGER Taxable person transfers his business — wholly or in part — by sale, gift, lease, leave & licence, hire or any other manner
📝 It doesn't matter HOW the business is transferred — sale, gift, even a lease. The moment ownership or control shifts, both parties are on the hook for past dues.
§85(1) — PRE-TRANSFER LIABILITY
Transferor + Transferee → Jointly & Severally liable for tax, interest & penalty due up to time of transfer
(whether determined before or after transfer)
📝 Even if the demand order comes AFTER the sale is done — BOTH seller and buyer are personally liable for old dues. Department can chase either one.
💡 A sells his business to B on 1 Jan 2025. Department later raises demand for FY 2023-24 GST of ₹50 lakh. Both A and B are jointly liable — Department can recover from B even though A was the defaulter.
§85(2) — POST-TRANSFER LIABILITY
Transferee carrying on the business → liable for GST on supplies effected by him from date of transfer
If registered — apply for amendment of registration within prescribed time
📝 After the transfer date, new owner pays GST on his own supplies. He must also update his GST registration to reflect the change.
🔍 GP Singh: "Jointly and severally liable" — creditor (Revenue) may sue either or both — liability is not proportionate to share. The words "or any other manner whatsoever" give the provision the widest possible scope — ejusdem generis cannot restrict it when legislature uses such sweeping language.
📋 Corresponding Rules: Rules 19(2) (Amendment of Registration) | CGST Rules, 2017
Section 86 — Liability of Agent and Principal
🤝 §86 — Agent & Principal | Joint & Several Liability
TRIGGER Agent supplies or receives taxable goods on behalf of principal
CONSEQUENCE Agent AND Principal — Jointly & Severally liable to pay tax on such goods
📝 If an agent sells goods on your behalf — both of you owe the GST. The Department doesn't have to prove who was more at fault — it can demand from either party.
🔍 GP Singh: Note "goods" only — §86 applies to taxable GOODS, not services. For services through agent, principal-agent rules under Schedule I Para 3 and §7(1) read with §2(93) apply. This limited word "goods" may be a litigation point in service-sector agency arrangements.
💡 Commission agent receives agricultural produce (taxable goods) and sells on behalf of farmer-principal. Both the agent and the farmer are jointly liable for GST on the turnover — agent cannot say "I only charge commission, principal owes the tax."
Section 87 — Liability in Case of Amalgamation or Merger
🔀 §87 — Amalgamation / Merger of Companies
Retrospective Effect & GST Implications
TRIGGER Order of Court / Tribunal (or otherwise) for amalgamation/merger — with retrospective effective date earlier than order date
§87(1) — INTER-COMPANY SUPPLIES IN GAP PERIOD
Between effective date and order date — supplies between the merging companies → included in respective turnovers → each company liable for GST separately
📝 If the merger is supposed to be effective from 1 Jan but court order comes on 1 June — the supplies made between Jan and June BETWEEN the merging companies count as separate taxable supplies. They cannot escape GST by claiming "we were one company".
💡 Company A merges into Company B. NCLT order dated 1 June 2024 gives effect from 1 Jan 2024. All goods supplied between A and B during Jan–June 2024 are taxable — both companies' GST returns for that period must include such supplies.
§87(2) — DISTINCT COMPANIES UNTIL ORDER DATE
Notwithstanding the merger order — for GST purposes companies treated as distinct until order date → Registration certificates cancelled with effect from order date
📝 GST law ignores the court-ordered retrospective merger — it treats companies as separate until the actual court order. This avoids GST disputes on past inter-company transactions.
🔍 GP Singh / Word War: "Notwithstanding" in §87(2) overrides the merger order for GST purposes. This creates an overriding fiction — a clear deeming provision that must be strictly construed. Courts cannot extend this fiction beyond its express terms.
Section 88 — Liability in Case of Company in Liquidation
🏚️ §88 — Liquidation | Liquidator's Obligations & Director Liability
§88(1) — LIQUIDATOR'S INTIMATION DUTY
Liquidator appointed → must intimate Commissioner within 30 days of appointment
📝 The moment a court appoints a liquidator, that liquidator has 30 days to inform the GST Commissioner. Failure to do so creates personal liability.
§88(2) — COMMISSIONER'S RESPONSE
Commissioner — after inquiry — notifies Liquidator within 3 months of intimation: amount sufficient to provide for any tax/interest/penalty then due or likely to become due
📝 Commissioner tells the liquidator: "Set aside this much money for GST dues." The liquidator CANNOT distribute assets without keeping this aside.
§88(3) — DIRECTOR LIABILITY (Pvt. Co.)
Private company wound up → tax/interest/penalty NOT recoverable from company → Every director during the period of tax liability: Jointly & Severally liable
UNLESS proves non-recovery not due to gross neglect/misfeasance/breach of duty by him
📝 If a private company is wound up and GST can't be recovered — directors personally pay. But a director can escape liability by PROVING he was not responsible for the default — the BURDEN OF PROOF is on the director.
🔍 GP Singh: "Unless he proves" — burden shifts to director. This is an exception clause and must be strictly construed. Mere resignation from directorship is insufficient — must show no active role in affairs at relevant time.
💡 Director A resigned 2 years before GST default period. Can establish he had no role. Director B was MD during default period. Director A may escape; Director B cannot.
📋 Read with IBC 2016 — Liquidator's duties under IBBI Regulations override GST provisions where IBC applies
Section 89 — Liability of Directors of Private Company
👔 §89 — Director Liability | Private Company
Notwithstanding Companies Act, 2013
§89(1) — TRIGGER Tax/interest/penalty due from private company for any period CANNOT BE RECOVERED → Every director during such period: Jointly & Severally liable
Unless proves non-recovery not due to any gross neglect/misfeasance/breach of duty on his part
🔍 GP Singh — "Cannot be recovered": This is the GATEWAY CONDITION. Department must first establish that recovery from the company has FAILED — not merely that it is difficult. If company has assets, department cannot directly proceed against directors. The word "cannot" is the threshold — not "may not" or "is unlikely to".
📝 Section 89 OVERRIDES the Companies Act — even if the Companies Act would protect a director, GST law doesn't care. But the key protection for directors is: prove you had no role in the default.
§89(2) — CONVERSION: Pvt → Public Company
If private company converts to public before tax can be recovered → §89(1) does NOT apply after conversion
Exception: Personal penalty imposed on a director still applies even after conversion
📝 Once a private company becomes public, its directors are no longer personally liable for the company's GST dues — EXCEPT for any personal penalty specifically imposed on that director.
💡 XYZ Pvt Ltd converts to XYZ Ltd on 1 April 2024. GST dues for FY 2022-23 still unpaid. Post-conversion, directors NOT personally liable. BUT if Director D had a personal penalty imposed in 2023 — that remains recoverable from him.
📋 Compare with §88(3) — §88 applies on WINDING UP; §89 applies while company is GOING CONCERN but tax is unrecoverable
Section 90 — Liability of Partners of Firm
🤝 §90 — Partnership Firm | Partner Liability
GENERAL RULE — §90(1) Notwithstanding any contract to the contrary and any other law → Firm + each of the partners Jointly & Severally liable for tax/interest/penalty
📝 The partnership agreement cannot override this. Even if partners agree internally that "Partner A pays all GST" — the Department is not bound. It can recover from any or all partners.
Retirement of Partner — Special Rules
STEP 1 — Intimation Required
Retiring partner / firm must intimate Commissioner in writing of retirement
IF INTIMATED WITHIN 1 MONTH
Retiring partner liable for dues up to date of retirement only
IF NO INTIMATION WITHIN 1 MONTH
Liability of retiring partner continues until Commissioner receives intimation
📝 Fail to inform the Commissioner — you stay liable even after you've left the firm. The one-month clock runs from date of retirement.
💡 Partner C retires on 1 March 2024 but firm forgets to inform Commissioner. GST demand raised for April 2024. Partner C is STILL liable because no intimation was given in time.
📋 Note: "Limited Liability Partnership" under LLP Act 2008 is also treated as a FIRM under Explanation to §94 — §90 applies to LLPs too
Section 91 — Liability of Guardians, Trustees, etc.
👶 §91 — Guardian / Trustee / Agent of Minor or Incapacitated Person
WHEN APPLICABLE Business is carried on by guardian/trustee/agent on behalf of & for benefit of a minor or incapacitated person
CONSEQUENCE Tax/interest/penalty levied upon and recoverable from the guardian/trustee/agent — in the SAME manner and to the SAME extent as it would be from the minor/incapacitated person IF he were a major/capacitated conducting business himself
📝 If you run a business for a minor child — you become the taxpayer. The GST law treats you exactly as if you WERE the minor, personally conducting the business. All provisions of the Act apply to you accordingly.
💡 A father (guardian) runs a trading business for his minor son. GST registration, returns, payments — all in the guardian's name. Department can issue notice to the guardian and recover from him directly — cannot say "my son is the real owner."
Section 92 — Liability of Court of Wards, etc.
🏛️ §92 — Court of Wards / Administrator General / Official Trustee / Court-Appointed Receiver
WHEN APPLICABLE Estate (or portion of estate) of a taxable person owning a business is under control of: Court of Wards | Administrator General | Official Trustee | Receiver / Manager appointed by court
CONSEQUENCE Tax/interest/penalty levied upon and recoverable from such statutory authority / court-appointed manager — same manner and same extent as from the taxable person himself
📝 When a court puts someone else in charge of a business (e.g., insolvency proceedings before IBC, or estates under Court of Wards) — that court-appointed person steps into the shoes of the original owner for GST purposes.
Section 93 — Special Provisions: Death / HUF Partition / Firm Dissolution / Trust Termination
Sub-section Event / Trigger Who is Liable? Extent of Liability Plain English / Notes Litigation Point
§93(1)(a) Person dies — business CONTINUED by legal representative or another person Legal representative / other person continuing business All tax/interest/penalty due from deceased 📝 Business carries on after death — the person carrying on steps into the dead person's shoes for GST. Whether estate executor who continues business is "legal representative" — relevant for personal liability
§93(1)(b) Person dies — business DISCONTINUED (before or after death) Legal representative — from the ESTATE Out of estate to the extent estate can meet the charge — all pre-death dues (determined before or after death) 📝 Business stops on death. Legal heir pays from estate assets — but NOT from personal pocket beyond estate value. Heir's personal assets protected — liability capped to estate value
§93(2) HUF / Association of Persons — property partitioned Each member / group of members Jointly & Severally liable for dues up to time of partition (whether determined before or after partition) 📝 Family partition doesn't wipe out GST dues. Each family member personally liable — Department can go after any member. Save as provided by IBC 2016 — insolvency resolution supersedes §93(2)
§93(3) Firm — dissolved Every person who was a partner Jointly & Severally for dues up to time of dissolution 📝 Dissolve the firm — partners still pay old GST dues. Dissolution is not a shield. IBC override applies — if firm goes into insolvency, IBC resolution plan prevails
§93(4)(a) Guardian of minor — guardianship terminated Ward (minor, now grown) Dues up to time of termination of guardianship 📝 When guardianship ends (minor becomes major), the ward takes over liability from the guardian.
§93(4)(b) Trustee carrying on business — trust terminated Beneficiary Dues up to time of termination of trust 📝 When the trust ends, the beneficiary (who benefits from the business) becomes liable for past GST dues.
📋 All sub-sections: Save as otherwise provided in IBC, 2016 (31 of 2016) — insolvency proceedings under IBC override §93 provisions
Section 94 — Liability in Other Cases (Firm / AOP / HUF — Discontinuance & Reconstitution)
📋 §94 — Discontinuance / Reconstitution of Firm / AOP / HUF
§94(1) — DISCONTINUANCE
(a) Tax/interest/penalty may be determined as if NO discontinuance has taken place
(b) Every partner / member at time of discontinuance → Jointly & Severally liable — even after discontinuance
📝 "Business stopped" doesn't stop GST assessment. Department can complete assessment proceedings as if the firm/AOP never stopped, and recover from ex-partners.
§94(2) — RECONSTITUTION
Old + new partners/members of reconstituted firm/AOP → Jointly & Severally liable for dues for any period before reconstitution
📝 New partners joining a firm inherit old GST liabilities jointly with the old partners. Due diligence is essential before joining a firm.
💡 New partner joins old firm. Later a GST audit uncovers FY 2021-22 liability (pre-joining). New partner is STILL jointly liable under §94(2) — he cannot say "I wasn't a partner then".
§94(3) — DISSOLUTION / PARTITION
§94(1) provisions apply mutatis mutandis to dissolution of firm and partition of HUF — references to "discontinuance" = dissolution / partition
📝 "Mutatis mutandis" = with necessary changes. Dissolution and partition are treated exactly like discontinuance for tax liability purposes.
EXPLANATION (§94)
(i) LLP under LLP Act 2008 = treated as a FIRM for Chapter XVI purposes
(ii) "Court" = District Court, High Court or Supreme Court
📋 LLP Partners — personal liability exposure under §94 read with §90 and §93(3) — important for LLP due diligence
CHAPTER XVII — ADVANCE RULING (§§ 95–106)
Sections 95–97 — Definitions, Authority & Application for Advance Ruling
📖 Advance Ruling Framework — Definitions & Scope (§§ 95–97)
§95 — Key Definitions
Advance Ruling
Decision by AAR / AAAR / NAAR to applicant on questions specified in §97(2) or §100(1) or §101C
Applicant
Any person registered or desirous of obtaining registration under the Act
Authority (AAR)
AAR constituted under SGST / UTGST Act — deemed AAR under CGST for that State/UT
NAAR
National Appellate Authority for Advance Ruling — §101A (Finance Act 2019, date to be notified)
§97(2) — Questions on which Advance Ruling can be Sought (7 Categories)
(a) Classification of goods or services or both
(b) Applicability of any notification
(c) Time and value of supply
(d) Admissibility of ITC
(e) Liability to pay tax
(f) Whether applicant is required to be registered
(g) Whether any particular thing done = supply of goods/services/both
🔍 GP Singh — "In relation to supply … being undertaken or proposed to be undertaken": Advance ruling is PROSPECTIVE. It is for FUTURE or ONGOING supply — not past transactions. A ruling on past supplies is outside scope of §97 — key argument to challenge retrospective application of adverse ruling.
📋 Corresponding Rules: Rules 103–107A CGST Rules, 2017 (Page 285)
Section 98 — Procedure on Receipt of Application for Advance Ruling
⚙️ §98 — AAR Procedure: From Application to Ruling
STEP 1 Application received → AAR forwards copy to concerned officer → may call for relevant records (records to be returned ASAP)
STEP 2 — §98(2) AAR examines application + records → Hearing applicant + concerned officer → Admit OR Reject
REJECT — IF
Question already pending or decided in any proceedings in the case of the applicant under any provision of this Act
Opportunity of hearing mandatory before rejection
Reasons must be specified in rejection order
📝 If the same question is being litigated elsewhere (in a notice, appeal, audit) — AAR cannot give ruling on it. This prevents parallel proceedings on same issue.
🔍 "Already pending or decided" — if proceedings were INITIATED before application, AAR must reject. But if assessment proceedings START after AR application — the bar may not apply. Timing is crucial.
ADMIT → STEP 3 — §98(4)
Further material + hearing (applicant + concerned officer) → Pronounce Advance Ruling
§98(6) Ruling to be pronounced in writing within 90 days from receipt of application
§98(7) Copy sent to applicant, concerned officer, jurisdictional officer
§98(5) — DIFFERENCE OF OPINION If members of AAR DIFFER on any question → refer to Appellate Authority (AAAR) for hearing and decision
Sections 99–101 — Appellate Authority for Advance Ruling (AAAR)
SectionProvisionKey Condition / TimeframePlain EnglishLitigation Point
§99 AAAR constituted under SGST/UTGST = deemed AAAR for CGST State/UT specific 📝 Same as AAR — one authority, two hats (State + Central).
§100(1) Concerned officer / jurisdictional officer / aggrieved applicant may appeal to AAAR against §98(4) ruling Within 30 days of communication of ruling (+ 30 days condonation by AAAR for sufficient cause) 📝 Both the taxpayer AND the department can appeal an AAR ruling. Appeal window is short — 30 days only. If AAR gives ruling against assessee — immediately examine appeal within 30 days. No court can extend this beyond 60 days total.
§101(1) AAAR: confirm or modify the ruling — after giving parties opportunity of being heard Order within 90 days of filing of appeal 📝 AAAR has wide powers — can confirm, modify or reverse AAR ruling.
§101(3) If AAAR members DIFFER on any point → deemed no advance ruling can be issued on that question Split bench = no ruling 📝 If the two AAAR members disagree — the entire question is left open. No ruling is issued — the original uncertainty continues. Powerful position for assessee — deadlock means no binding adverse ruling. The legal uncertainty remains — assessee may take conservative or aggressive position with protection.
📋 Corresponding Rules: Rules 103–107A CGST Rules, 2017 (Page 285)
Sections 101A–101C — National Appellate Authority for Advance Ruling (NAAR)
🏛️ NAAR — National Appellate Authority for Advance Ruling
Inserted by Finance (No. 2) Act, 2019 — Effective from date to be notified
📝 NAAR was created to solve the CONFLICT RULING problem — when two States give opposite rulings on the same question for the same taxpayer (distinct person). Without NAAR, such taxpayers faced impossible situations. NAAR gives one binding national answer. Note: NAAR is not yet operationalized as of 2025.
§101A — CONSTITUTION
President: Former SC Judge / CJ of HC / HC Judge (5+ yrs)
Technical Member (Centre): IRS (C&CE) Group A — 15+ yrs
Technical Member (State): State officer (Addl. Commr. VAT or above) — 3+ yrs
§101B — WHEN CAN NAAR BE APPROACHED?
Conflicting Advance Rulings from AAAR of 2+ States/UTs on same question → applicant (distinct person u/s 25) OR authorized officer may appeal
Within 30 days (officer: 90 days) + 30 days condonation
💡 PAN-India company gets AAR ruling in Maharashtra that "X service is exempt" but AAR ruling in Karnataka that "X service is taxable." Now company can approach NAAR for a single binding national ruling.
§101C — NAAR ORDER
All parties + all Chief Commissioners (Centre & State) heard
Majority decision
Order within 90 days (as far as possible)
Binding on all distinct persons with same PAN + their officers
🔍 Binding on "registered persons having same PAN" — this extends NAAR ruling to ALL GSTINs of the company across India. One ruling covers all states — both sword and shield.
Sections 102–106 — Rectification · Applicability · Void Ruling · Powers
SectionSubjectKey ProvisionPlain English / Litigation Point
§102 Rectification AAR/AAAR/NAAR may amend order to rectify any error apparent on face of record — on own accord or on notice from concerned officer / applicant — within 6 months from date of order
Proviso: No rectification enhancing tax/reducing ITC without opportunity of hearing		 📝 6-month window — if adverse ruling has an obvious error (wrong section cited, math error) — applicant must bring it to AAR's notice within 6 months. After that: window closed. 🔍 "Error apparent on face of record" — narrow standard. Error must be obvious without external evidence or argument.
§103(1) Applicability of AAR / AAAR Ruling Binding ONLY on:
(a) Applicant — in respect of the matter ruled on
(b) Concerned officer / jurisdictional officer — in respect of that applicant only		 📝 An advance ruling is NOT general law — it binds ONLY the applicant and his officers. A third party cannot use another assessee's AAR ruling as precedent. 🔍 This is a crucial distinction — Department sometimes misapplies other taxpayers' rulings to unrelated assessees. Contest it.
§103(1A) Applicability of NAAR Ruling Binding on:
(a) Applicants (distinct persons) who sought ruling + ALL registered persons with same PAN
(b) Concerned + jurisdictional officers of such persons		 📝 NAAR ruling has pan-India binding effect for the same company (all GSTINs). Unlike AAR ruling — NAAR ruling IS effectively a national precedent for that company.
§103(2) Ruling ceases to be binding If law, facts or circumstances supporting the original ruling have changed 📝 Even a favorable ruling can be rendered void if GST law changes or facts change. Always review the basis of a favorable ruling before relying on it. 🔍 "Circumstances" — broad word. A change in business model may be enough to invalidate even a NAAR ruling.
§104 Ruling void if obtained by fraud AAR/AAAR/NAAR may declare ruling void ab initio if obtained by fraud / suppression / misrepresentation
→ All Act provisions apply as if ruling was never made
→ Limitation period (§73/§74/§74A) excludes the period of such ruling		 📝 "Void ab initio" = never existed. Company cannot rely on a fraudulently-obtained ruling — entire past protection disappears. The time during which the void ruling was in effect does NOT count towards limitation for the Department — very dangerous for assessee.
§105 Powers of AAR / AAAR / NAAR All powers of Civil Court under CPC 1908: discovery & inspection, enforcing attendance, issuing commissions, production of books
Deemed civil court for §195 CrPC (not Chapter XXVI)
Proceedings = judicial proceedings (IPC §§193, 228, 196)		 📝 The advance ruling authority has court-like powers — it can compel a witness to appear and can demand your books. Proceedings before it are treated as court proceedings — false statements = perjury.
§106 Procedure Subject to provisions of Chapter XVII — AAR/AAAR/NAAR have power to regulate their own procedure 📝 The authority makes its own rules of procedure — as long as it stays within the statutory framework.
📋 Corresponding Rules: Rules 103–107A CGST Rules, 2017 (Page 285)
CHAPTER XVIII — APPEALS AND REVISION (§§ 107–121)
Section 107 — First Appeal to Appellate Authority (Commissioner Appeals)
🏛️ §107 — First Appeal | Appellate Authority
Any person aggrieved by decision / order of Adjudicating Authority
WHO CAN APPEAL
Any person aggrieved → within 3 months of communication of order
Commissioner → call for record; direct officer to appeal within 6 months (suo motu)
Condonation: +1 month by Appellate Authority for sufficient cause
PRE-DEPOSIT §107(6)
(a) 100% of admitted tax/interest/fine/fee/penalty
(b) 10% of remaining disputed tax — max ₹20 crore
Penalty-only orders: 10% of penalty (no tax demand involved) [Finance Act 2025]
📝 Before filing appeal — pay admitted amount in full + 10% of disputed amount (up to ₹20 Cr cap). Without this, appeal is not maintainable.
💡 Order: Tax ₹10 lakh admitted + ₹90 lakh disputed. Pre-deposit = ₹10 lakh + ₹9 lakh = ₹19 lakh. Recovery of ₹81 lakh balance STAYS automatically on payment.
§107(7) — AUTOMATIC STAY
Payment of §107(6) amount → recovery of balance amount deemed stayed
§107(13) — TIMELINE
Appellate Authority to hear & decide within 1 year from date of filing
(Court stay period excluded)
Key Powers of Appellate Authority — §107(11)
Confirm / Modify / Annul the decision appealed against
CANNOT refer case back to adjudicating authority
If enhancing fee/penalty/confiscation/reducing refund/ITC — must give opportunity of hearing to appellant (show cause)
If finding underpaid/short-paid/wrong refund/wrong ITC — must issue notice to show cause + decide within §73/§74/§74A time limit
🔍 GP Singh — "Shall not refer the case back": Unlike many appellate statutes, §107(11) explicitly bars remand to adjudicating authority. This is a significant procedural right — once at AA level, case must be decided by AA. Department cannot get fresh bite at cherry through remand. Similarly, enhancement by AA without show cause notice = procedural infirmity vitiating the order.
📋 Corresponding Rules: Rules 108–110 CGST Rules, 2017 (Pages 286–287) | Form GST APL-01 (Appeal), APL-02 (Acknowledgement)
Section 108 — Powers of Revisional Authority
🔄 §108 — Revisional Authority
Suo Motu or on Information / State Commissioner Request
POWER Call for and examine record of proceedings — if decision/order is:
(i) Erroneous in so far as prejudicial to interest of revenue AND
(ii) Illegal or improper OR has not taken into account material facts (available at time of order OR in consequence of CAG observations)
📝 Both conditions must be satisfied — the order must be BOTH prejudicial to revenue AND either illegal/improper/missing material facts. An order that is merely prejudicial to revenue but legally correct cannot be revised. This dual requirement is the assessee's shield against revision.
🔍 GP Singh — Conjunctive "and": "Erroneous … AND … illegal or improper" — BOTH limbs must be satisfied. Many High Courts have held that revision cannot lie merely because the authority disagrees with the view taken — the order must be perverse or contrary to law. An order in favour of assessee that is debatable is NOT revisable.
§108(2) — BARS TO REVISION (when RA cannot act)
(a) Order already under appeal (§107/§112/§117/§118)
(b) Period for Department appeal (§107(2)) not yet expired — OR more than 3 years after passing of original order
(c) Order already revised at earlier stage under this section
(d) Order passed in exercise of §108(1) itself
📝 If the Department already has an appeal route open but hasn't filed — it cannot use revision as a substitute. And if 3 years have passed — revision is time-barred. But if appeal was decided, revision can lie on fresh issues not raised in appeal within 1 year of appeal order or 3 years, whichever is later.
📋 Corresponding Rules: Rules 109A CGST Rules, 2017 | Revisional Authority = Principal Commissioner or Commissioner
Section 109 — Constitution of GSTAT (GST Appellate Tribunal)
⚖️ §109 — GSTAT | Goods & Services Tax Appellate Tribunal
Finance Act 2023 restructured GSTAT (w.e.f. 01.08.2023)
PRINCIPAL BENCH
New Delhi | President + Judicial Member + TM (Centre) + TM (State)
Exclusive jurisdiction:
• Place of supply disputes
• Anti-profiteering (§171) — if notified
Appeals from Principal Bench → Supreme Court (§118)
STATE BENCH / AREA BENCHES
One per State (at places notified)
Judicial Member + TM (Centre) + TM (State)
Junior-most JM = State President
Jurisdiction: All matters except place of supply
Appeals from State Bench → High Court (§117)
SINGLE MEMBER BENCH
Tax/ITC/fine/penalty ≤ ₹50 lakh AND no question of law → Single Member can hear (with President approval)
§109(10)
📝 Small cases can be decided by one member — faster disposal. But if a question of law arises even in a small case, bench must be of two members.
§109(14) — SAVING CLAUSE No act or proceedings of GSTAT shall be invalid merely on ground of vacancy or defect in constitution
🔍 Finance Act 2023 restructured GSTAT completely — eliminated National/Regional Bench nomenclature, introduced Principal Bench + State Bench + Area Bench structure. Place of supply cases now exclusively before Principal Bench (New Delhi) — significant for disputes involving cross-state services.
Section 110 — Qualifications, Appointment & Service Conditions of GSTAT Members
Member TypeQualificationTenureRetirement AgeKey Notes
President Former SC Judge OR Chief Justice of HC 4 years (re-appointment up to 2 more years) 70 years (amended — was 67) Appointed by Govt. on Search-cum-Selection Committee recommendation. Not eligible to appear before Principal/State Bench after retirement.
Judicial Member (i) HC Judge; OR (ii) District/Addl. District Judge (combined 10 yrs); OR (iii) Advocate — 10 yrs substantial indirect tax experience (HC / SC / CESTAT / VAT Tribunal) [Min. age 50 yrs] 4 years (re-appointment up to 2 more years) 67 years (amended — was 65) Advocate track newly added by CGST Second Amendment Act 2023 — expanded pool. Min. age 50 yrs for appointment.
Technical Member (Centre) IRS (Customs & Indirect Taxes) Group A OR All India Service — 25+ years Group A service with 3+ yrs GST/existing law experience 4 years (re-appointment up to 2 more years) 67 years State Bench TM (Centre) appointed by Central Govt.
Technical Member (State) State Govt. officer (not below Addl. Commr. VAT / SGST or lower rank as notified) — 25+ years Group A equivalent (relaxable by State if no eligible officer with 25 yrs but 20 yrs service available) 4 years (re-appointment up to 2 more years) 67 years First preference: officers who worked in that State's Government. Appointed by State Govt.
REMOVAL — §110(12) On recommendations of Search-cum-Selection Committee: insolvency / moral turpitude / physical/mental incapacity / financial interest conflict / position abuse. Removal for clauses (d)/(e) requires prior hearing. Proved misbehaviour/incapacity: Central Govt. + CJI nominated SC Judge inquiry (for President/JM/TM Centre); State Govt. + Chief Justice HC inquiry (for JM/TM State of State Bench).
📋 Corresponding Rules: Rules 110–112 CGST Rules, 2017 | Finance Act 2023 restructured entire GSTAT w.e.f. 01.08.2023
Sections 111–112 — Procedure Before GSTAT & Filing of Appeal to GSTAT
⚙️ §§111–112 — GSTAT: Procedure & How to Appeal
§111 — PROCEDURE
NOT bound by CPC — guided by natural justice
Has civil court powers: summoning witnesses, discovery & inspection, evidence on affidavit, commissions, public records
Orders enforceable as court decrees
Proceedings = judicial proceedings (IPC §§193, 228, 196)
§112(8) — PRE-DEPOSIT FOR GSTAT
(a) 100% of admitted dues
(b) 10% of remaining disputed tax (in addition to §107(6) pre-deposit) — max ₹20 crore [amended Finance Act 2024 — was 20% capped at ₹50 Cr]
Penalty-only: 10% of penalty (Finance Act 2025)
📝 Total pre-deposit for GSTAT = §107(6) amount (10% at AA level) + another 10% at GSTAT level. So effectively 20% of disputed tax paid before GSTAT (subject to ₹20 Cr cap at each stage).
💡 Disputed tax ₹5 Cr. At §107: pay 10% = ₹50 lakh. Appeal fails. At §112: pay another 10% = ₹50 lakh. Total pre-deposit = ₹1 Cr. Balance ₹4 Cr: stayed automatically.
§112(1) — TIME LIMIT & LIMITATION
Appeal within 3 months of order (+ 3 months condonation for sufficient cause)
Department: 6 months from order (or notified date, whichever later) [Finance Act 2024 amendment]
Cross-objection by respondent: within 45 days of notice of appeal
🔍 Word War — §112(8): "ten per cent" (amended from "twenty per cent"): Finance Act 2024 reduced GSTAT pre-deposit from 20% to 10%. This is a significant taxpayer relief — the word change from "twenty" to "ten" halved the financial burden of accessing GSTAT. Any demand raised before this amendment but appeal filed after — benefit of 10% applies prospectively from notification date (01.11.2024).
📋 Corresponding Rules: Rules 110–113 CGST Rules, 2017 (Pages 287–290) | Form GST APL-05 (GSTAT Appeal)
Sections 113–116 — GSTAT Orders · Interest on Refund · Authorised Representative
SectionSubjectKey ProvisionPlain English / Litigation Point
§113(1) GSTAT Orders Confirm / Modify / Annul OR Remand (to AA/RA/original authority for fresh adjudication with directions, additional evidence if necessary) 📝 Unlike §107(11), GSTAT CAN remand the case back. This is a key difference between First Appeal (no remand) and GSTAT (remand permitted). 🔍 Remand is discretionary — "as it thinks fit." Court can refuse remand if facts are clear. Use this: if GSTAT seeks remand on a pure legal question, argue for final decision instead.
§113(3) Rectification by GSTAT GSTAT may rectify error apparent on face of record — within 3 months of order — on own accord or on notice from Commissioner/State/UT Commissioner or other party. Enhancement of liability / reduction of refund/ITC only after hearing.
§113(4) Timeline GSTAT to decide as far as possible within 1 year from date of filing
§113(6) Finality GSTAT orders final and binding — save as provided in §117 (HC) or §118 (SC)
§114 Financial & Administrative Powers of President President exercises financial and administrative powers over GSTAT as may be prescribed [amended FA 2023]
§115 Interest on Refund of Pre-Deposit If pre-deposit (§107(6) or §112(8)) is refunded pursuant to order of AA / GSTAT → interest at §56 rate payable from date of payment till date of refund 📝 You paid pre-deposit, you won the appeal, the pre-deposit must be refunded WITH INTEREST. Interest rate = same as §56 rate (currently 6% p.a.). This is an automatic right — no separate application needed. 💡 Always claim §115 interest when getting refund of pre-deposit — Department sometimes ignores this. Cite §115 specifically in your refund claim.
§116 Authorised Representatives Who can appear: (a) relative/regular employee; (b) Advocate (entitled to practice, not debarred); (c) CA/CMA/CS (with certificate of practice, not debarred); (d) Retired Commercial Tax Officer not below Group-B Gazetted (1 year bar from retirement/resignation); (e) GST Practitioner 📝 CA/CMA/CS can appear as authorized representative before Adjudicating Authority, AA and GSTAT. No specific GST degree required — certificate of practice + not debarred is sufficient.
📋 Corresponding Rules: Rules 114–116 CGST Rules, 2017 (Pages 291–293) | Form GST APL-07 (Cross-objection)
Sections 117–121 — High Court · Supreme Court · Non-Appealable Orders
SectionForumTrigger / ConditionTimeframeKey Rules / Litigation Points
§117(1) High Court Appeal from State Bench orders of GSTAT → HC may admit if substantial question of law is involved Within 180 days of receipt of GSTAT order (HC may condone delay for sufficient cause) 🔍 "Substantial question of law" = a question that is not merely academic, is debatable and has not been conclusively settled by SC. PURE FACT questions cannot go to HC. 💡 Frame grounds of appeal carefully — mix of fact and law arguments. HC will only hear the law portion.
§117(3) High Court HC must formulate the question of law before hearing — appeal heard only on formulated question HC can hear on other substantial questions of law even if not formulated — if satisfied they are present. Respondent can argue "no such question."
§117(6) High Court HC appeal heard by bench of minimum 2 judges — decided by majority
§118(1)(a) Supreme Court Appeal from Principal Bench of GSTAT — as of right Place of supply disputes decided by Principal Bench → direct appeal to Supreme Court. No HC in between — significant for place of supply disputes.
§118(1)(b) Supreme Court Appeal from HC judgment — if HC certifies case as fit for SC appeal (on its own or on application of aggrieved party)
§119 Payment despite Appeal Even after appeal to HC/SC — dues determined by Principal Bench (§113(1)) or State Bench (§113(1)) or HC (§117) shall be paid as per order 📝 Filing an appeal to HC/SC does NOT automatically stay the order. Must separately seek stay from HC/SC. §119 makes the duty to pay explicit. 💡 Always apply for stay from HC along with the main appeal to protect against recovery action.
§120 Monetary Limits Board may fix monetary limits for filing appeals by Central Tax officers — below that limit, officers not to file appeals 📝 Small cases — Department bound by CBIC instructions not to litigate below monetary threshold. Non-filing in one case does NOT mean Department has accepted that position in similar cases (§120(3) — no acquiescence). GSTAT/HC must consider why officer didn't file (§120(4)).
§121 Non-Appealable Orders No appeal lies against:
(a) Transfer of proceedings (Commissioner order)
(b) Seizure/retention of books/documents
(c) Order sanctioning prosecution
(d) Order under §80 (payment in instalments)		 📝 These four categories cannot be challenged through the appeal mechanism under Chapter XVIII. Challenge route = writ petition before High Court under Article 226/227 of Constitution. 💡 If Department seizes books (§67) — no appeal under §107. Must file writ immediately. This is a critical procedural trap.
📋 Corresponding Rules: Rules 110–116 CGST Rules, 2017 (Pages 286–293)
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal — Chapters XVI · XVII · XVIII | CGST Act
These statutory ratios are for use in personal hearings, adjudication proceedings, first appeals, and GSTAT. State them confidently with the section reference.
Section 85 — Joint & Several Liability on Transfer of Business
CGST Act, 2017 | Statutory Provision | Chapter XVI
Key Principle
Where a taxable person transfers his business in whole or in part by sale, gift, lease, leave and licence, hire or in any other manner whatsoever — the transferor and the transferee shall, jointly and severally, be liable to pay the tax, interest or penalty due from the transferor upto the time of such transfer, whether such tax has been determined before or remains unpaid or is determined thereafter.
When to Use — When a successor-purchaser receives a GST demand for the predecessor's dues. Transferee must conduct thorough GST due diligence before acquisition. Also use to argue that Department must pursue transferor FIRST — joint and several means Department has discretion but equitable principles suggest proceeding against the actual defaulter first.
Sections 88(3) & 89(1) — Gateway Condition for Director Liability
CGST Act, 2017 | Statutory Provision | Chapter XVI | Critical for Director SCN Defence
Key Principle
Under §89(1), before invoking director liability, the Department must first establish that tax, interest or penalty cannot be recovered from the private company. The word "cannot" is the threshold — not "may not" or "is difficult to recover." Further, the director may escape liability by proving that the non-recovery cannot be attributed to any gross neglect, misfeasance or breach of duty on his part in relation to the affairs of the company.
When to Use — When director receives personal SCN under §89. First line of defence: company still has assets — "cannot be recovered" condition not met. Second line: director had no role in financial affairs — prove through board resolutions, delegation of authority, lack of signing authority on GST returns. Also: if director had resigned before default period — period of directorship must cover the period of tax liability.
Section 90 — Retiring Partner's Liability & Intimation Requirement
CGST Act, 2017 | Statutory Provision | Chapter XVI | Critical for Partnership GST
Key Principle
Where any partner retires from the firm, he or the firm shall intimate the retirement to the Commissioner in writing within one month from the date of retirement. The retiring partner shall be liable to pay tax, interest or penalty due up to the date of his retirement whether determined or not on that date. If no such intimation is given within one month, the liability of such partner shall continue until the date on which such intimation is received by the Commissioner.
When to Use — When a retired partner receives a demand for post-retirement period GST dues. Argue: intimation was given within one month — liability extinguished as on retirement date. Also: check if intimation was actually given — if yes, Department cannot demand for post-retirement period. If firm failed to intimate: fight on limitation — Department's delay in proceeding.
Section 93 — IBC Override on Liability in Death / Partition / Dissolution
CGST Act, 2017 | Statutory Provision | Read with IBC, 2016
Key Principle
Save as otherwise provided in the Insolvency and Bankruptcy Code, 2016 — where a person liable to pay tax dies / HUF is partitioned / firm is dissolved / guardianship or trust is terminated — the legal representative / members / partners / ward or beneficiary shall be liable to pay the tax, interest or penalty. However, in all cases the IBC, 2016 takes precedence — if insolvency proceedings are pending, GST claims must be filed before the Resolution Professional and are governed by the IBC waterfall mechanism.
When to Use — When Department issues personal demands to legal heirs, HUF members or ex-partners for dissolved entity dues. If IBC proceedings are on, resist proceedings before GST authorities — direct Department to file claim before RP. For heirs: liability capped to estate value under §93(1)(b) — personal assets of heir are protected if business is discontinued.
Section 95(a) & 97(1) — Advance Ruling is Prospective Only
CGST Act, 2017 | Statutory Provision | Chapter XVII
Key Principle
Advance ruling means a decision on questions in relation to the supply of goods or services or both being undertaken or proposed to be undertaken by the applicant. An applicant means any person registered or desirous of obtaining registration. The advance ruling mechanism is prospective in nature — it is for future or ongoing supplies. It cannot and does not decide the tax position for past completed transactions.
When to Use — When Department relies on an adverse AAR ruling to raise a demand for past periods. Argue: AAR ruling is prospective — it cannot determine tax liability for periods prior to the ruling. Also: AAR ruling binds only the applicant and his officers — cannot be applied against a third party (§103(1)). If adverse ruling came for another assessee — it is not binding on your client.
Section 103(1) — Binding Effect of Advance Ruling Limited to Applicant
CGST Act, 2017 | Statutory Provision | Chapter XVII
Key Principle
The advance ruling pronounced by the Authority or the Appellate Authority shall be binding only — (a) on the applicant who had sought it in respect of any matter referred to in sub-section (2) of section 97; and (b) on the concerned officer or the jurisdictional officer in respect of the applicant.
When to Use — When Department applies an adverse AAR ruling given in another assessee's case to your client's matter. Counter firmly: §103(1) limits binding effect strictly to the applicant. An AAR ruling in ABC Pvt Ltd's case does NOT bind XYZ Pvt Ltd — even on identical facts. The provision is explicit and unambiguous.
Section 101(3) — AAAR Member Disagreement = No Advance Ruling Issued
CGST Act, 2017 | Statutory Provision | Chapter XVII
Key Principle
Where the members of the Appellate Authority differ on any point or points referred in the appeal or reference, it shall be deemed that no advance ruling can be issued in respect of the question under the appeal or reference.
When to Use — When AAAR has a split opinion on your question. The legal effect is that NO ruling has been issued — the original uncertainty continues. This means the assessee is NOT bound by either opinion. In such situation, take a well-reasoned conservative or business-appropriate position independently — cannot be said to have ignored a binding ruling since none exists.
Section 107(6) & (7) — Mandatory Pre-deposit & Automatic Stay
CGST Act, 2017 | Statutory Provision | Chapter XVIII | Critical for Every First Appeal
Key Principle
No appeal shall be filed unless the appellant has paid in full such admitted part of the amount and a sum equal to ten per cent of the remaining amount of tax in dispute subject to a maximum of twenty crore rupees. Where the appellant has paid the amount under sub-section (6), the recovery proceedings for the balance amount shall be deemed to be stayed.
When to Use — In every first appeal filing. Key argument: on payment of pre-deposit, balance DEEMED STAYED by statute — no separate stay application needed. If Department initiates recovery despite appeal filing and pre-deposit payment — invoke §107(7) and file writ for contempt/mandamus. Also: cap of ₹20 Cr per appeal is crucial for large demands — beyond ₹200 Cr demand, still only ₹20 Cr pre-deposit.
Section 107(11) — Appellate Authority Cannot Remand
CGST Act, 2017 | Statutory Provision | Chapter XVIII
Key Principle
