The Appellate Authority shall, after making such further inquiry as may be necessary, pass such order as it thinks just and proper, confirming, modifying or annulling the decision or order appealed against — but shall not refer the case back to the adjudicating authority that passed the said decision or order.
When to Use — When Appellate Authority attempts to remand the case back to the adjudicating authority. Such order is ultra vires — §107(11) expressly prohibits remand. The AA must decide the case on merits itself. File writ against illegal remand order. Also: if AA seeks to enhance tax without issuing show cause notice — same section requires prior notice — challenge such enhancement order as violating §107(11) proviso.
Section 108(1) — Dual Test for Invoking Revisional Jurisdiction
CGST Act, 2017 | Statutory Provision | Chapter XVIII
Key Principle
The Revisional Authority may, if it considers that any decision or order passed by any officer subordinate to him is erroneous in so far as it is prejudicial to the interest of the revenue AND is illegal or improper or has not taken into account certain material facts — pass such order as it thinks just and proper. Both conditions must be jointly satisfied for valid exercise of revisional jurisdiction.
When to Use — When assessee receives a revision notice. First question: Is the original order in assessee's favour ALSO illegal/improper? If the order is legally correct (though it benefits assessee) — revision does not lie merely because it is prejudicial to revenue. The word "and" makes both limbs mandatory. Cite general principle: a debatable view taken by adjudicating authority in favour of assessee cannot be characterised as "erroneous" merely because a different view is possible.
Section 115 — Interest on Refund of Pre-Deposit on Successful Appeal
CGST Act, 2017 | Statutory Provision | Chapter XVIII | Often missed by practitioners
Key Principle
Where an amount paid by the appellant under sub-section (6) of section 107 or sub-section (8) of section 112 is required to be refunded consequent to any order of the Appellate Authority or the Appellate Tribunal, interest at the rate specified under section 56 shall be payable in respect of such refund from the date of payment of the amount till the date of refund of such amount.
When to Use — On winning the appeal (at AA or GSTAT level), when applying for refund of pre-deposit amount. Specifically cite §115 to claim interest. The interest is automatic — not discretionary. Rate = §56 rate (currently 6% per annum). Department sometimes issues refund without interest — always check and demand §115 interest separately. Compute from actual date of payment (not appeal filing date) to actual refund date.
Section 121 — Non-Appealable Orders — Writ Jurisdiction is the Only Remedy
CGST Act, 2017 | Statutory Provision | Chapter XVIII | Read with Article 226 Constitution of India
Key Principle
Notwithstanding anything to the contrary in any provisions of this Act, no appeal shall lie against any decision taken or order passed if it relates to: (a) transfer of proceedings from one officer to another; (b) order pertaining to seizure or retention of books of account, registers and documents; (c) order sanctioning prosecution; (d) order under section 80 (payment in instalments).
When to Use — When books are seized under §67 or prosecution is sanctioned or instalment payment order is made. No appeal available under Chapter XVIII — the only remedy is a writ petition under Article 226/227 before the High Court. File immediately — do not waste time filing a §107 appeal (which will be dismissed as not maintainable). In writ: challenge on grounds of unreasonableness, non-application of mind, violation of natural justice, or lack of jurisdiction.
For professional litigation use | Pawan Soni & Associates, Ichalkaranji
CGST Act — Ch.XIX–XX (Offences, Transitional)

Sections 122–142 | CGST Act, 2017 | Source: ICAI Bare Law Nov 2025 | CA Pawankumar Soni, Ichalkaranji

Permitted / Valid
Prohibited / Penalty
Condition / If / Unless
Discretionary / Optional

Chapter XIX — Offences & Penalties | Tier 1 Overview
⚖️ Chapter XIX — Offences & Penalties
Sections 122 to 138 | CGST Act, 2017
§122
21 civil offences
Penalty: ₹10K or tax evaded whichever higher
§122A
Machine non-registration
₹1 lakh/machine + seizure
§122B
Track & Trace failure
₹1 lakh or 10% tax whichever higher
§123
Non-furnishing of information return
₹100/day, max ₹5,000
§124
Non-furnishing of statistics
Fine up to ₹10K + ₹100/day (max ₹25K)
§125
General penalty (residual)
Up to ₹25,000
§126
General disciplines on penalty
Proportionality + PH + Mitigating factors
§127
Power to impose penalty
Outside §62/63/64/73/74/74A/129/130
§128
Power to waive penalty / fee
By Government notification
§128A
Waiver of interest/penalty
Jul 2017 – Mar 2020 | Pay tax only
§129
Detention, Seizure & Release
200% tax penalty or 50% value
§130
Confiscation of goods/conveyance
+ levy of penalty
§131
Confiscation without prejudice
to other punishments
§132
Criminal punishment
Imprisonment up to 5 years + fine
§133
Liability of officers
for wilful disclosure of info
§134
Cognizance of offences
Only with Commissioner's sanction
§135
Presumption of culpable mental state
Court presumes; accused to rebut
§136
Relevancy of statements
Summons responses as evidence
§137
Offences by companies
Directors / Partners deemed guilty
§138
Compounding of offences
25%–100% of tax involved
📋 Corresponding Rules: Rules 162–164, CGST Rules, 2017 (Pages 332–337)
§122 — Penalty for Certain Offences
⚖️ Section 122 — Penalty for Offences
Sub-sections (1), (1A), (1B), (2), (3)
§122(1) — 21 Civil Offences by Taxable Person → Penalty: ₹10,000 OR Tax Evaded, whichever higher
Clause Offence Plain English Litigation Point
(i)Supplies goods/services without invoice or with incorrect/false invoiceNo bill issued or wrong bill issued for actual supply.Common SCN ground — check if invoice issued but not uploaded on portal; that alone is not "no invoice".
(ii)Issues invoice without actual supplyFake billing — bill created but no actual delivery of goods/services.Bogus ITC chain cases. Physical verification of goods receipt is key defence.
(iii)Collects tax but fails to pay within 3 months of due dateCollected GST from customer but kept it — did not pay to government.Mere delayed payment? Argue §73/§74 route — interest under §50 sufficient; penalty only if wilful.
(iv)Collects tax in contravention of Act but fails to pay within 3 monthsCollected GST on an exempt supply and retained it.Defence: Tax collected in good faith — return it under §76. Penalty discretionary.
(v)Fails to deduct TDS u/s 51 or deducts short or deducts but doesn't payApplicable to government entities. Didn't deduct tax at source.Contractor receiving payment — verify whether deductor was covered under §51 notification.
(vi)Fails to collect TCS u/s 52 or collects short or collects but doesn't payE-commerce operators — TCS default.§1B inserted w.e.f. 01.10.2023 — unregistered seller allowed on platform = penalty.
(vii)Takes/utilises ITC without actual receipt of goods or servicesITC claimed but goods/services never received — paper credit without physical supply.Most common GST fraud case. Defence: Prove actual receipt — e-way bill, GRN, payment trail.
(viii)Fraudulently obtains refundFalse refund claim — bogus export invoices, inflated refund amount.Fraud must be proved — mere excess claim by mistake ≠ fraud. Argue §73 route.
(ix)Takes/distributes ITC in contravention of §20 or rules (ISD)Input Service Distributor distributing credit wrongly across units.ISD provisions — verify GSTIN-wise distribution formula and timelines.
(x)Falsifies records / produces fake accounts / furnishes false information to evade taxManipulation of books or returns with intent to evade.Intent to evade must be proved — accidental error in returns ≠ falsification.
(xi)Liable to register but fails to obtain registrationCrossed ₹20 lakh threshold (or ₹10 lakh for special category states) but never registered.Registration taken voluntarily before notice? Argue mitigation under §126(5).
(xii)Furnishes false information at time of or after registrationWrong address, wrong constitution, wrong PAN provided.SCN must specify which particular was false — vague allegation not sufficient.
(xiii)Obstructs or prevents officer in discharge of dutiesPhysically blocking search, seizing officer from entering premises.Mere asking for legal counsel ≠ obstruction. Right to lawyer before statement.
(xiv)Transports taxable goods without cover of specified documentsGoods in transit without E-way bill or other required documents.Generates §129 proceedings separately. §122 penalty is additional/cumulative.
(xv)Suppresses turnover leading to tax evasionSales/receipts deliberately hidden from returns.Key word: "suppression" — must be wilful concealment, not mere omission. Dict node below.
(xvi)Fails to keep/maintain/retain books of accountNo books of account maintained as required under §35.Best judgment assessment under §62/63 possible; penalty independent.
(xvii)Fails to furnish information/documents called for or furnishes false info during proceedingsNot responding to summons/letters or giving wrong documents.Notice under §70 summons — right to counsel. Documents produced under protest may be challenged.
(xviii)Supplies/transports/stores goods he knows are liable to confiscationKnowingly dealing in contraband or tax-evaded goods."Knows or has reasons to believe" — state of mind must be proved. Mere handling insufficient.
(xix)Issues invoice using registration number of another personGSTIN fraud — issuing bills with someone else's GST number.Identity fraud under GST. Criminal prosecution under §132 also likely.
(xx)Tampers with or destroys material evidence or documentsDestroying books, files, digital records during an inquiry.Contemptuous conduct — also liable under §135 (presumption of culpable mental state).
(xxi)Disposes of or tampers with detained/seized/attached goodsAfter goods are seized under §67/§129 — selling or hiding them.Contempt + additional penalty. Custody order must be verified before any dealing.
📖 Word: "SUPPRESSION" [§122(xv)]
Black's Law Dictionary: "The act of concealing or withholding something from public notice or from disclosure." Stroud's: "Suppression implies a wilful or deliberate act — a positive act of hiding."
SC Ratio: Pushpam Pharmaceuticals v. CCE (SC) — "Suppression" means wilful mis-statement or deliberate failure to disclose facts. Mere error/miscalculation is NOT suppression.
Litigation Action: When Department invokes §122(xv), demand proof of wilful concealment — not mere non-disclosure. Wrong turnover in return ≠ suppression.
§122(1A) — Benefit Retainer Penalty [Inserted by Finance Act 2020, w.e.f. 01.01.2021]
WHO Any person who retains benefit from transactions under §122(1) clauses (i), (ii), (vii), (viii), (ix) — AND at whose instance such transaction was conducted
PENALTY Amount equivalent to tax evaded / ITC availed / passed / refund obtained
📝 This catches the real beneficiary — the masterminds behind bogus billing who themselves don't appear in the GST chain but benefit financially. Even non-registered persons can be penalised.
💡 XYZ Ltd. arranges fake invoices from shell companies. XYZ's management "retains the benefit" of the ITC availed. §122(1A) penalty applies to XYZ's directors even if they are not the GSTIN holders.
§122(1B) — E-Commerce Operator Penalty [Inserted FA 2023, w.e.f. 01.10.2023; substituted FA(No.2) 2024]
ClauseViolation by E-Commerce OperatorPenalty
(i)Allows supply through it by unregistered person (unless exempt notification applies)₹10,000 or equivalent tax amount, whichever higher
(ii)Allows inter-State supply by person not eligible to make it₹10,000 or equivalent tax amount, whichever higher
(iii)Fails to furnish correct details in §52 statement for exempt-from-registration suppliers₹10,000 or equivalent tax amount, whichever higher
§122(2) — Penalty for Short/Non-Payment of Tax
NON-FRAUD (a) For any reason other than fraud/wilful misstatement/suppression
→ Penalty: ₹10,000 OR 10% of tax due, whichever higher
🔍 GP Singh: "shall be liable" — mandatory penalty. No discretion once short-payment proved. But assessee can argue good faith / interpretation dispute to invoke only this sub-section and not §122(2)(b).
FRAUD (b) For reason of fraud / wilful misstatement / suppression to evade tax
→ Penalty: ₹10,000 OR 100% of tax due, whichever higher
🔍 The jump from 10% to 100% penalty turns on ONE word: "fraud." Demand Department prove fraud — mere error in returns does NOT satisfy the burden under §74/122(2)(b). SC in Dharmendra Textiles confirmed: burden on department.
§122(3) — Third Party Liability (Aiders & Abettors)
ClauseWho is CoveredPenalty
(a)Aids or abets any of the offences in §122(1)(i)–(xxi)Up to ₹25,000
(b)Acquires possession of / transports / deposits / conceals / supplies / purchases goods liable to confiscation knowing soUp to ₹25,000
(c)Receives / concerned with supply of services known to be in contravention of ActUp to ₹25,000
(d)Fails to appear before officer when summoned under §70Up to ₹25,000
(e)Fails to issue invoice or fails to account for invoice in booksUp to ₹25,000
📝 The "may extend to ₹25,000" language means it is discretionary — the officer "may" impose, not "shall". Cite §126 and argue proportionality + mitigating circumstances to reduce to minimum.
📋 Corresponding Rules: Rules 162–164, CGST Rules, 2017 (Pages 332–337)
§122A — Penalty for Failure to Register Machines [Inserted FA 2024, w.e.f. 01.10.2024]
🏭 Section 122A — Machine Registration under Special Procedure (§148)
TRIGGER
Person engaged in manufacture notified under §148 special procedure — machine NOT registered as per that procedure
§122A(1) PENALTY
₹1,00,000 per machine not registered
(in addition to any other penalty under Chapter XV or Chapter XIX)
§122A(2) SEIZURE
Every unregistered machine liable to seizure and confiscation
PROVISO — ESCAPE FROM CONFISCATION
(a) Penalty paid, AND
(b) Registration done within 3 days of receipt of penalty order
📝 The 3-day window is extremely tight. Failure to register the machine within 3 days even after paying penalty = confiscation proceeds. Act immediately on receipt of order.
§122B — Penalty for Track & Trace Mechanism Failure [Inserted FA 2025, w.e.f. 01.10.2025]
📦 Section 122B — Track & Trace Mechanism (§148A)
TRIGGER Person covered under §148A(1)(b) who acts in contravention of §148A provisions (track & trace mechanism for goods)
PENALTY ₹1,00,000 OR 10% of tax payable on such goods — whichever is HIGHER
(in addition to any penalty under Chapter XV or Chapter XIX)
📝 This is a newly inserted provision (FA 2025). Track & trace is a physical marking/tagging mechanism for specific goods. Non-compliance = severe penalty. Businesses covered under §148A should note compliance is mandatory.
§123 — Penalty: Information Return | §124 — Fine: Statistics
§123 — INFORMATION RETURN (§150)
Fails to furnish information return within period specified in §150(3) notice

→ Penalty: ₹100 per day of default
→ Maximum: ₹5,000

Proper officer issues direction → penalty runs from notice period expiry
📝 Information return under §150 relates to data furnished by financial institutions, banks, etc. This is NOT the regular GSTR return. The cap of ₹5,000 limits the damage.
§124 — STATISTICS (§151)

(a) Without reasonable cause, fails to furnish info/return under §151
OR
(b) Wilfully furnishes false information

→ Fine: Up to ₹10,000 for initial offence
→ Continuing offence: ₹100/day for each day after first day
→ Maximum: ₹25,000
⚔️ WORD WAR: "Penalty" vs "Fine"
Penalty (§122/125)
Administrative, recoverable as tax arrear under §73/74		Fine (§124)
Criminal/quasi-criminal; imposed by court/officer; not recoverable as tax arrear
Legislature deliberately used "Fine" in §124 — signals quasi-criminal nature of statistics offence. Cannot be adjusted against credit ledger.
§125 — General Penalty (Residual)
🔧 Section 125 — General / Residual Penalty
CONDITION Any person who contravenes any provision of the Act or Rules — for which NO separate penalty is provided
PENALTY Which may extend to ₹25,000
📝 This is the catch-all provision. "May extend to" = discretionary. The officer need not impose the maximum. §126 principles apply to restrain disproportionate use.
🔍 GP Singh on residual provisions: A general/residual penal provision must be read harmoniously with specific provisions. If the act complained of is already covered by a specific penal section (e.g., §122), §125 cannot be invoked to circumvent the specific provision. "Generalia specialibus non derogant."
💡 Department invokes §125 for failure to update address on portal. Argue: §126(1) "minor breach" exemption applies if no tax consequence arises + "easily rectifiable" criterion met.
§126 — General Disciplines Related to Penalty (Assessee's Arsenal Section)
🛡️ Section 126 — General Disciplines on Penalty
The Assessee's Protective Shield — Six Key Disciplines
Sub-sectionDiscipline / RightPlain EnglishHow to Use in Hearing
§126(1) NO penalty for minor breaches — omissions/mistakes easily rectifiable without fraudulent intent or gross negligence
Minor breach = tax involved < ₹5,000
Easily rectifiable = error apparent on face of record		 If the mistake is small (less than ₹5K tax) and can be fixed easily — no penalty should be imposed. Use aggressively for clerical errors in returns, wrong GSTIN in invoice, typographic errors — penalty should be Nil under §126(1).
§126(2) Penalty shall be commensurate with degree and severity of breach Penalty must fit the crime. A small default cannot attract maximum penalty. Challenge disproportionate maximum penalties. SC ratio: Penalty must be proportional. Cite §126(2) when ₹10K minimum is imposed on ₹100 tax short-payment.
§126(3) No penalty without opportunity of being heard Before imposing penalty, the officer must give you a chance to explain your side. Any penalty order passed without show cause notice + personal hearing = void ab initio on natural justice grounds.
§126(4) Penalty order must specify: nature of breach + applicable regulation + penalty amount under that regulation The penalty order must explain exactly what rule was broken and why that specific amount was fixed. If penalty order merely says "penalty imposed" without specifying the breach and the applicable section — challenge on §126(4) non-compliance. Order is bad in law.
§126(5) Voluntary disclosure prior to discovery by officer = Mitigating factor in quantification If you report your own mistake before the officer finds it — this should reduce the penalty. Always document voluntary disclosures — letters, amended returns, rectification applications. Use as mitigation in hearing: "We self-disclosed — penalise at minimum."
§126(6) §126 does NOT apply where penalty is a fixed sum or fixed percentage If the law says "penalty = 100% of tax" (no discretion), then §126 principles don't apply — that fixed penalty runs as-is. Limitation — §126 is powerful but cannot override fixed-amount penalty provisions like §122(2)(b) (100% for fraud) or §129 (200% for transit detention).
🔍 GP Singh on Natural Justice (§126(3)): "Audi alteram partem" is a constitutional principle — no order adverse to a person can be passed without giving him opportunity to be heard. §126(3) is merely the statutory expression of this constitutional mandate. Even if statute is silent, courts will read in the PH requirement. An order in breach of this = void, not merely voidable.
§127 — Power to Impose Penalty | §128 — Power to Waive | §128A — Amnesty Scheme
§127 — Power to Impose Penalty (Standalone)
TRIGGER Proper officer of view that person is liable to penalty AND case is NOT covered under any proceedings u/s §62, §63, §64, §73, §74, §74A, §129 or §130
POWER Issue order levying penalty after giving reasonable opportunity of being heard
📝 §127 is the residual penalty-imposition power — used when the particular case doesn't fit into any of the eight listed proceedings. Personal hearing is mandatory before any §127 order.
💡 Wrong address maintained on GST portal — no tax demand arises. Neither §73 nor §129 applies. Officer can use §127 to impose penalty. But must issue notice + allow hearing first.
§128 — Power to Waive Penalty / Late Fee
POWER Government may by Notification (on GST Council recommendation) waive — in part or full — any penalty under §122, §123, §125 or late fee under §47
CONDITIONS For such class of taxpayers + under such mitigating circumstances as specified in the Notification
📝 This is the legal basis for various amnesty/waiver notifications issued by CBIC. If a waiver notification is in force for your period/category — claim it as of right; it is not discretionary once notified.
§128A — Waiver of Interest/Penalty | §73 Demands for Jul 2017–Mar 2020 [Inserted FA(No.2) 2024, w.e.f. 01.11.2024]
🎯 AMNESTY SCHEME — Pay Only Tax, Get Full Waiver of Interest + Penalty
For §73 Demands covering period 01.07.2017 to 31.03.2020
ClauseStage of ProceedingsCondition to AvailBenefit
§128A(1)(a) Notice u/s 73(1) or Statement u/s 73(3) — Order u/s 73(9) NOT yet passed Pay full tax as per notice/statement on or before notified date Full waiver of interest + penalty; proceedings deemed concluded
§128A(1)(b) Order u/s 73(9) passed — Appeal order u/s 107(11) or 108(1) NOT yet passed Pay full tax as per §73(9) order on or before notified date Full waiver of interest + penalty; proceedings deemed concluded
§128A(1)(c) Order u/s 107(11) or 108(1) passed — SC/HC order u/s 113(1) NOT yet passed Pay full tax as per appellate order on or before notified date Full waiver of interest + penalty; proceedings deemed concluded
First Proviso §74 notice → Order u/s 75 directing as per §73 — such notice/order treated as §73 notice/order for amnesty Available even if originally §74 notice issued
Second Proviso Where appeal/writ pending and not withdrawn — benefit available only if additional tax (as per appellate order) paid within 3 months of such order Must withdraw pending appeal to avail amnesty
Third Proviso Interest/penalty already paid — NO REFUND available Past payments not refundable
§128A(2) Erroneous refund cases — NOT eligible for amnesty Erroneous refunds excluded
§128A(3) Cases where appeal/writ pending and NOT withdrawn by notified date Excluded from amnesty
§128A(4) Once proceedings concluded under §128A(1) — no fresh appeal lies against the order Finality — cannot reopen concluded proceedings
💡 ABC Ltd. received §73 SCN for FY 2018-19 (ITC mismatch). Order u/s 73(9) passed for ₹5 lakh tax + ₹2 lakh interest + ₹50K penalty. ABC pays ₹5 lakh tax before notified date → ₹2.5 lakh of interest + penalty WAIVED. Proceedings concluded. Major benefit for non-fraud GST disputes from pre-2020 period.
📋 Corresponding Rules: Rules 162–164, CGST Rules (Pages 332–337)
§129 — Detention, Seizure and Release of Goods and Conveyances in Transit
🚛 Section 129 — Transit Detention & Seizure
Amended significantly by Finance Act 2021 w.e.f. 01.01.2022
TRIGGER Any person transports goods OR stores goods while in transit in contravention of Act/Rules → Goods + Conveyance + Documents liable to detention/seizure
Release Conditions — After Detention/Seizure
§129(1)(a) — OWNER COMES FORWARD
Taxable goods: Penalty = 200% of tax payable
Exempted goods: 2% of value OR ₹25,000 (whichever less)
📝 If you are the owner and you come forward — penalty is 200% of tax. For an ₹1 lakh tax consignment, penalty = ₹2 lakhs. Steep but lower than (b).
§129(1)(b) — OWNER DOESN'T COME FORWARD
Taxable goods: Penalty = 50% of value OR 200% of tax (whichever higher)
Exempted goods: 5% of value OR ₹25,000 (whichever less)
📝 If owner doesn't come forward — higher penalty applies. "50% of value" can far exceed 200% of tax for low-tax-rate goods. Always come forward and pay (a) rate.
§129(1)(c) — SECURITY OPTION
Furnish security equivalent to (a) or (b) amount — in prescribed form/manner
Procedure After Detention
§129(3) Proper officer to issue notice within 7 days of detention/seizure specifying penalty → Pass order within 7 days of notice service
§129(4) No penalty determined without opportunity of being heard
§129(5) On payment of penalty under §129(1) → all proceedings deemed concluded
§129(6) Failure to pay penalty within 15 days of receiving copy of §129(3) order → Goods/conveyance liable to be sold/disposed to recover penalty
Proviso: Conveyance released on payment of penalty under §129(3) OR ₹1 lakh, whichever less
Proviso 2: Perishable/hazardous goods — 15-day period may be reduced
NOTE Detention/seizure notice MUST be served before detention — no detention without notice
💡 Truck detained carrying goods without E-way bill. Tax payable = ₹50,000. Owner comes forward (§129(1)(a)) → Penalty = ₹1,00,000 (200% of tax). Owner does not come forward → Penalty = higher of 50% of value or 200% of tax. If goods worth ₹20 lakh → penalty could be ₹10 lakh.
🔍 GP Singh on strict construction: §129 is a penal provision — to be strictly construed. "Contravention of provisions" must be specific and proved — a mere technical lapse in e-way bill (Part B not filled) vs deliberate evasion are different. Courts have distinguished procedural non-compliance from substantive tax evasion in §129 proceedings.
§130 — Confiscation of Goods or Conveyances and Levy of Penalty
🔒 Section 130 — Confiscation Proceedings
§130(1) — Five Grounds for Confiscation
ClauseGround for ConfiscationKey Element
(i)Supplies/receives goods in contravention of Act/Rules with intent to evade taxIntent to evade — must be proved
(ii)Does not account for goods on which tax is payableOff-books goods — unaccounted stock
(iii)Supplies goods liable to tax without having applied for registrationUnregistered taxable supply
(iv)Contravenes provisions of Act/Rules with intent to evade taxGeneric intent-to-evade ground
(v)Uses conveyance for carriage of goods in contravention of Act/Rules — unless owner proves ignorance/non-connivance of himself, agent & person in chargeOwner can escape by proving ignorance + diligence
§130(2) — Fine in Lieu of Confiscation (Redemption Fine)
OWNER'S OPTION Pay redemption fine instead of confiscation (quantum at officer's discretion)
LIMITS
Redemption fine ≤ Market value of confiscated goods + tax chargeable
Aggregate of fine + penalty ≥ 100% of tax payable on such goods
§130(4) No order of confiscation/penalty without opportunity of being heard
§130(5) On confiscation — title of goods/conveyance vests in Government
§130(6)/(7) Proper officer takes possession; Police assist on requisition; disposal after 3 months notice to pay fine
📝 §130 is distinct from §129. §129 is for transit detention (procedural violation). §130 is for substantive evasion. §130 can apply without §129 proceedings if the case involves evasion at source.
§131 — Confiscation/Penalty Not to Interfere with Other Punishments
⚖️ Section 131 — Non-Obstante / Cumulative Punishment Principle
PRINCIPLE Without prejudice to CrPC 1973 provisions — confiscation or penalty under this Act shall NOT prevent infliction of any other punishment under this Act or any other law
📝 GST penalty (civil) AND criminal prosecution (§132) can run simultaneously. Being penalised under §129 does not prevent prosecution under §132 for the same goods movement. Both can proceed independently.
💡 Goods detained under §129 + penalty paid. Prosecution under §132(1)(a) for same transaction still possible. The assessee cannot argue "I already paid penalty — double jeopardy." GST law allows cumulative action.
§132 — Punishment for Certain Offences (Criminal Prosecution)
⛓️ Section 132 — Criminal Prosecution
Imprisonment + Fine | Amended by FA 2020, FA 2023
§132(1) — Punishable Offences (whoever commits, causes to commit AND retains benefit)
ClauseOffenceThreshold → Punishment
(a)Supplies without invoice with intention to evade tax>₹500 lakh → Up to 5 years imprisonment + fine
₹200–500 lakh → Up to 3 years + fine
₹100–200 lakh → Up to 1 year + fine (only clause (b))
(b)Issues invoice without supply — leading to wrongful ITC/refund avail
(c)Avails ITC using invoice referred to in (b) OR fraudulently avails ITC without any invoiceSame thresholds as above
(d)Collects tax but fails to pay within 3 monthsSame thresholds as above
(e)Evades tax or fraudulently obtains refund (not covered in (a)–(d))Same thresholds as above
(f)Falsifies financial records / fake accounts / false information to evade taxSame thresholds as above
(h)Acquires/transports/deposits/conceals/supplies goods liable to confiscationSame thresholds as above
(i)Concerned with supply of services known to contravene ActSame thresholds as above
(l)Attempts to commit or abets commission of (a)–(f), (h), (i)Same punishment as principal offender
Catch-allAny offence not in above — committed/abettedUp to 6 months OR fine OR both
§132(2)
Second conviction = Up to 5 years + fine for every subsequent offence
§132(3)
Minimum 6 months imprisonment for clauses (i), (ii), (iii) — unless special & adequate reasons recorded in judgment
§132(4)
General rule: All offences NON-COGNIZABLE and BAILABLE (except §132(5))
§132(5)
Clauses (a), (b), (c), (d) punishable under clause (i) [>₹500 lakh] = COGNIZABLE and NON-BAILABLE
§132(6)
No prosecution without prior sanction of Commissioner
🔍 GP Singh on criminal provisions: Penal statutes must be strictly construed — every element of the offence must be independently proved. "Commits or causes to commit and retains benefit" (amended 2020) — the word "AND" is conjunctive. Both commission AND benefit-retention must be proved for the principal offender. Merely retaining benefit without commission does not attract §132 — that is §122(1A).
📖 Word: "COGNIZABLE" [§132(5)]
CrPC §2(c): An offence for which police may arrest without warrant and investigate without magistrate's order. Black's: Cognizable offences allow immediate arrest and are serious in nature.
Impact: If amount >₹500 lakh AND offence under (a)/(b)/(c)/(d) — arrest WITHOUT warrant possible. This is the highest-risk zone in GST. Any allegation of bogus invoicing involving >₹5 crore = cognizable + non-bailable.
§133 to §137 — Procedural Criminal Law Provisions
SectionSubjectKey ProvisionLitigation Point
§133 Liability of Officers & Portal Persons for Disclosure of Information Wilful disclosure of information/return contents other than in execution of duties → Imprisonment up to 6 months OR fine up to ₹25,000 OR both
Prosecution: Govt. servant = Govt. sanction; others = Commissioner sanction		 Valuable protection — any GST officer who leaks your return data to competitors or press = criminally liable. Can be used in contempt/writ proceedings.
§134 Cognizance of Offences by Courts No court shall take cognizance of any offence punishable under Act/Rules except with prior sanction of Commissioner
No court inferior to a Magistrate of First Class shall try such offence		 Commissioner's sanction is a jurisdictional prerequisite — without it, even a cognizable offence cannot be tried. Challenge complaint at threshold stage if sanction absent/defective.
§135 Presumption of Culpable Mental State Court SHALL presume existence of culpable mental state in any prosecution requiring it.
Defence: Accused to prove he had NO such mental state — beyond reasonable doubt.
Culpable mental state = intention, motive, knowledge, belief, reason to believe		 Reversal of normal criminal burden of proof. Normally prosecution proves guilty mind. Under §135, Court presumes it — accused must rebut. Prepare affirmative defence documenting absence of intent.
§136 Relevancy of Statements (§70 Summons Responses) Statement made/signed under §70 summons during inquiry = relevant evidence for proving truth of its contents IF: (a) Person dead/untraceable/incapacitated/kept out of way/undue delay; OR (b) Person examined as witness and court considers admission in interest of justice Crucial: Statements under §70 can be used against you in criminal prosecution. Always take legal counsel before §70 appearance. Retract incorrect statements immediately in subsequent communication.
§137 Offences by Companies / Firms / HUF / Trust §137(1): Every person in charge of & responsible for company conduct = deemed guilty
§137(2): Director/Manager/Secretary who consented/connived/was negligent = deemed guilty (in addition)
§137(3): Partner/Managing Trustee = deemed guilty for firm/HUF/trust
§137(4): DEFENCE — Prove offence committed WITHOUT knowledge or that he exercised all due diligence		 Defence under §137(4) is critically important. Document all compliance systems, internal controls, delegation of authority. "Due diligence" = active compliance system, not passive ignorance.
🔍 GP Singh on §135 (Reverse Burden): Reversal of burden of proof in penal statutes is constitutionally valid (Article 20(3) not applicable) but must be expressly stated. §135 does so expressly. However, the presumption is rebuttable — the standard is proof on preponderance of probability (not beyond reasonable doubt). Accused need only show his case is more probable than not.
§138 — Compounding of Offences
🤝 Section 138 — Compounding
Settlement of Criminal Offences by Payment
§138(1) BASIC RULE Commissioner may compound any offence — either before or after institution of prosecution — on payment of compounding amount
Prerequisite: Payment of tax + interest + penalty involved FIRST, then compounding
§138(1) Proviso — WHO CANNOT COMPOUND
(a) ONCE-COMPOUNDED
Person already compounded once for offences under §132(1)(a)–(f), (h), (i), (l) — CANNOT compound again for same category
(c) BOGUS INVOICE ACCUSED
Person accused of §132(1)(b) offence — CANNOT compound
(Fake invoice issuers permanently barred)
(d) CONVICTED
Person convicted for ANY offence under this Act by a court — CANNOT compound
(f) PRESCRIBED CLASS
Any other class of persons/offences as prescribed by Rules
§138(2) QUANTUM
Minimum: 25% of tax involved
Maximum: 100% of tax involved
(Amended by FA 2023 — earlier was "₹10K or 50% of tax min, ₹30K or 150% of tax max")
§138(3) EFFECT On payment of compounding amount: No further proceedings initiated; criminal proceedings (if already initiated) stand abated
📝 Compounding = paying a sum to close criminal proceedings. It is not an admission of guilt for civil purposes. It is available BEFORE or AFTER prosecution is started. The FA 2023 reduced the minimum compounding amount significantly — making it a more accessible settlement option.
💡 Tax evaded = ₹20 lakh. Criminal prosecution initiated under §132. Compounding amount = Minimum 25% of ₹20 lakh = ₹5 lakh. On payment, criminal case abates. Note: Must first pay the ₹20 lakh tax + interest + penalty under civil proceedings, THEN pay compounding amount separately.
📋 Corresponding Rules: Rules 162–164, CGST Rules, 2017 (Pages 332–337)
Chapter XX — Transitional Provisions | Tier 1 Overview
🔄 Chapter XX — Transitional Provisions
Sections 139 to 142 | CGST Act, 2017 | Bridge between Pre-GST Era and GST Era
§139
Migration of Existing Taxpayers
Provisional → Final registration certificate
§140
Transitional ITC
CENVAT credit carryforward — 8 scenarios
§141
Transitional Job Work
Goods sent for job work before 01.07.2017
§142
Misc. Transitional Provisions
Returns, Refunds, Appeals, Price revisions, Price revisions
⚠️ IMPORTANT Though transitional provisions relate to the appointed day (01.07.2017), disputes under §140 (transitional ITC carryforward) are still actively litigated before GSTAT and High Courts. These sections remain live litigation battlegrounds.
📋 Corresponding Rules: Rules 117–121, CGST Rules, 2017 (Pages 294–296)
§139 — Migration of Existing Taxpayers
🔄 Section 139 — Migration of Existing Taxpayers to GST
§139(1) — AUTOMATIC PROVISIONAL CERT
Every person registered under existing law with valid PAN → issued provisional registration certificate on appointed day
Valid until replaced by final certificate OR cancelled for non-compliance
📝 On 01.07.2017, all existing excise/VAT/service tax registrants got a provisional GSTIN automatically. No fresh application needed. But final certificate required conditions to be met.
§139(2) — FINAL CERTIFICATE
Final registration certificate in prescribed form, manner and conditions
§139(3) — DEEMED NOT ISSUED
Provisional certificate deemed NOT issued IF registration cancelled pursuant to application that person was NOT liable to register u/s 22 or 24
📝 If a person migrated but was actually below threshold and applied for cancellation — the provisional registration is treated as never having been granted. Important for determining whether registration existed during transition.
§140 — Transitional Arrangements for Input Tax Credit
💰 Section 140 — CENVAT Credit Carryforward to GST
Most Litigated Transitional Provision | Still active before GSTAT/HC
Sub-section Category of Person Credit Available Key Conditions Litigation Point
§140(1) Registered persons (not §10 composition) — continuing in GST CENVAT credit of eligible duties carried forward in last pre-GST return Credit must be admissible under GST Act; all returns of last 6 months filed; not from exemption notifications Failure to file even one return in 6 months → entire carry forward credit denied. SC in Union of India v. Filco: §140(1) must be read with conditions strictly.
§140(2) Registered persons — capital goods CENVAT credit not carried forward Unavailed CENVAT credit on capital goods as of appointed day Must be admissible under both existing law and GST Act
Unavailed = Total entitlement minus already availed		 Capital goods in transit on 01.07.2017 — verify partial credit already availed and claim only balance.
§140(3) Persons NOT under existing law — first stage dealers, second stage dealers, unregistered manufacturers, works contract availing 26/2012-ST benefit, reimporters Credit of eligible duties on inputs held in stock / in semi-finished / finished goods on appointed day (i) Used for taxable supplies; (ii) Eligible for ITC; (iii) Invoice not older than 12 months before appointed day; (iv) Supplier not eligible for abatement
If no invoice: Rule 117 alternative — reduced credit allowed; pass-on benefit to recipient		 Massive litigation around 12-month invoice condition. If invoice older than 12 months = no credit. Date of invoice critical. Also: manufacturer converting from exemption to taxable — this is the route.
§140(4) Manufacturer of BOTH taxable + exempt goods under existing law — both taxable under GST (a) CENVAT from last return [§140(1)] + (b) CENVAT on exempt goods inputs in stock [§140(3)] Both §140(1) and §140(3) conditions must be satisfied for respective portions Double benefit available — claim both §140(1) and §140(3) credit on appropriate inputs.
§140(5) All registered persons — credit on goods/services received after appointed day but tax paid under existing law Credit of eligible duties + taxes on inputs/input services received on/after appointed day (tax paid by supplier under existing law) Invoice/document recorded in books within 30 days of appointed day (extendable by Commissioner for another 30 days) Straddle supplies — goods ordered before GST, received after 01.07.2017, with pre-GST invoice. 30-day window for book entry is strict. Late entry = no credit.
§140(6) Persons paying tax at fixed rate or fixed amount under existing law Credit on inputs in stock / semi-finished / finished goods on appointed day Must not pay tax under §10 (composition); eligible for ITC; invoice available; invoice not older than 12 months Composition taxpayers under VAT converting to regular GST — this is the transition credit route.
§140(7) Input Service Distributor (ISD) Credit of services received prior to appointed day — available for distribution under GST Whether invoices received before, on or after appointed day — credit available [amended by FA(No.2) 2024 w.r.e.f. 01.07.2017] FA 2024 retrospective amendment resolved earlier controversy — ISD can distribute even for pre-GST service invoices received after 01.07.2017.
§140(8) Centralised-registration entities migrating to GST CENVAT from centralised return for period ending day before appointed day Return filed within 3 months of appointed day (original or revised with reduced credit); credit admissible under GST; may transfer to any PAN-linked GSTIN Large service providers (IT, banks) with centralised registrations — can distribute accumulated CENVAT across all GST units under same PAN.
§140(9) Person who reversed CENVAT credit on input services due to non-payment within 3 months under existing law Reversed credit can be reclaimed under GST if consideration paid within 3 months from appointed day Consideration must be paid within 3 months from 01.07.2017 [i.e., by 30.09.2017] Rarely availed. Useful for those who had service tax credit reversal but paid supplier after GST commenced.
⚠️ KEY DEFINITIONS — §140 Explanations
Eligible Duties (Exp. 1 — for §140(1),(3),(4),(6)): ADE [§3 of ADE(GSI) Act 1957] + ADD CVD [§3(1) of CT Act 1975] + SAD CVD [§3(5) of CT Act 1975] + Basic Excise [1st + 2nd Schedule CETA 1985] + NCCD [§136 Finance Act 2001] — on inputs in stock on appointed day
Eligible Duties & Taxes (Exp. 2 — for §140(1),(5)): Above + Service Tax [§66B of Finance Act 1994] — on inputs and input services received on/after appointed day
Note: Exp. 3 (inserted 2018): "Eligible duties" EXCLUDES cess not specified in Exp.1 or Exp.2 and cess collected as CVD.
🔍 GP Singh on transitional ITC: SC in Filco Trade Centre v. Union of India held that §140(1) read with Rule 117 imposes a time limit for filing TRAN-1. However, technical portal issues preventing filing can be condoned — equitable relief available. Legislature cannot deprive assessee of credit that was legally earned under existing law merely due to technical filing failure. This ratio is the backbone of all transitional ITC writ petitions.
📋 Corresponding Rules: Rules 117–121, CGST Rules, 2017 (Pages 294–296)
§141 — Transitional Provisions Relating to Job Work
🔧 Section 141 — Job Work Goods Straddling Appointed Day
Sub-sectionCategory of GoodsReturn WindowConsequence if Not ReturnedSpecial Option
§141(1) Inputs removed to job worker for processing/testing/repair BEFORE appointed day 6 months from appointed day (extendable by Commissioner for 2 more months) ITC recoverable under §142(8)(a) After completion of job work — returned to place of business; no tax payable
§141(2) Semi-finished goods removed to manufacturer's other premises before appointed day 6 months from appointed day (extendable 2 months) ITC recoverable under §142(8)(a) Manufacturer may transfer to any registered person for sale — on payment of tax in India or without payment for exports, within the 6-month window
§141(3) Excisable goods sent for tests/processes (not amounting to manufacture) before appointed day 6 months from appointed day (extendable 2 months) ITC recoverable under §142(8)(a) Manufacturer may transfer to any other person for sale on tax payment (or export) within window
§141(4) Common condition for all three sub-sections Manufacturer + Job Worker MUST jointly declare details of inputs/goods held in stock by job worker as of appointed day — in prescribed form, manner and time
💡 Textile unit sent fabric to dyeing job worker on 15.05.2017. Appointed day = 01.07.2017. Fabric returned on 20.10.2017 (within 6 months). No GST payable on return. If returned on 15.01.2018 (beyond 6 months even with extension) → ITC originally taken reversed under §142(8)(a).
§142 — Miscellaneous Transitional Provisions
Sub-sectionSubjectKey RuleLitigation Point
§142(1) Return of goods on which duty paid (within 6 months before appointed day) Registered person gets refund of pre-GST duty paid if goods returned by unregistered person within 6 months; identifiable to officer's satisfaction If returned by registered person → deemed supply under GST. Useful for stock return disputes.
§142(2)(a)/(b) Price revision pursuant to pre-GST contract — upward/downward (a) Upward revision: Issue supplementary invoice/debit note within 30 days of price revision — deemed outward supply under GST
(b) Downward revision: Issue credit note within 30 days — reduce tax liability subject to recipient reducing ITC		 Post-GST price revisions for pre-GST contracts → GST applicable on the supplementary document. Cannot claim service tax rate on revision.
§142(3)/(4)/(5) Refund claims under existing law filed before/after appointed day All disposed as per existing law; amount paid in cash (§11B second proviso of CEA not applicable); rejected CENVAT → lapses; carried-forward credit → no refund Pre-GST refund claims still pending — disposed under old law. But any rejection cannot be credited to GST ledger.
§142(6)(a)/(b) Appeals/reviews/references on CENVAT credit claims (a) Refund allowed → paid in cash; amount rejected → not admissible as ITC under GST
(b) Recovery proceedings → if recovered = not ITC under GST		 Pending CENVAT litigation outcome: Refund = cash payment. Cannot take as ITC credit.
§142(7)(a)/(b) Appeals on output duty/tax liability Recovery → as GST arrear under this Act (not as pre-GST recovery); Refund → in cash under existing law Pre-GST demand confirmed in appeal after 01.07.2017 → recovered as GST arrear. Can be significant for limitation and recovery proceedings.
§142(8)(a)/(b) Assessment/adjudication under existing law (a) Amount recoverable → as GST arrear; amount recovered → NOT ITC under GST
(b) Amount refundable → paid in cash under old law; rejected → not ITC		 Ongoing central excise / service tax assessments → recoveries treated as GST arrear. Track outcome of all pre-GST litigation.
§142(9)(a)/(b) Revised returns under existing law filed after appointed day (a) Additional recovery → as GST arrear
(b) Refund → in cash under old law; rejected → not ITC		 Belated revision of pre-GST returns for refunds — must be within time limits under old law.
§142(10) Supplies under pre-GST contracts Goods/services supplied on or after appointed day pursuant to pre-GST contract → GST applicable Pre-GST long-term contracts (construction, supply) where supply happens after 01.07.2017 → GST charged. Cannot continue under VAT/service tax.
§142(11)(a)/(b)/(c) Supplies already taxed under VAT/Service Tax (a) If VAT already levied on goods → no GST
(b) If service tax already levied on services → no GST
(c) If both levied → GST applicable + credit of VAT/service tax for future supplies		 No double taxation for straddling transactions. Important for works contracts / composite supplies where pre-GST law charged both VAT and service tax.
§142(12) Goods sent on approval basis within 6 months before appointed day If rejected/not approved and returned within 6 months from appointed day → no GST payable
If returned after period → tax payable by recipient/sender depending on who has goods		 Common in jewellery/textile trade. Document rejection/return within 6-month window to avoid GST liability.
§142(13) TDS on supplies where invoice issued before appointed day Supplier issued invoice before 01.07.2017 + payment made on/after 01.07.2017 → No GST TDS under §51 Avoids double levy — VAT TDS already done at State level on such transaction. No additional GST TDS.
📋 Corresponding Rules: Rules 117–121, CGST Rules, 2017 (Pages 294–296)
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal — Chapters XIX & XX | Offences, Penalties & Transitional
These ratios and statutory principles are for use in personal hearings. State them confidently — cite section and authority.
Section 126(1) — Minor Breach Shield
Statutory Provision | CGST Act, 2017 | Primary Defence Against Disproportionate Penalties
Key Ratio
No officer shall impose any penalty for minor breaches of tax regulations or procedural requirements, and in particular any omission or mistake in documentation which is easily rectifiable and made without fraudulent intent or gross negligence. A breach is "minor" if tax involved is less than ₹5,000. An omission is "easily rectifiable" if it is an error apparent on the face of record.
When to Use — When Department imposes ₹10,000 minimum penalty for typographic errors, wrong GSTIN in invoice, incorrect HSN code, or any clerical error where tax involved is less than ₹5,000. State §126(1) and demand Nil penalty.
Section 126(2) — Proportionality Principle
Statutory Provision | CGST Act, 2017 | Applied universally in penalty proceedings
Key Principle
The penalty imposed under this Act shall depend on the facts and circumstances of the case and shall be commensurate with the degree and severity of the breach. A maximum penalty imposed for a minor breach = disproportionate = bad in law.
When to Use — When Department imposes maximum or fixed-percentage penalty without considering facts. Cite §126(2) and argue: penalty must be commensurate with severity. Demand itemised reasoning in penalty order.
Section 126(4) — Penalty Order Specification Requirement
Statutory Provision | CGST Act, 2017 | Procedural invalidity ground
Key Principle
The officer shall, while imposing penalty in an order for a breach of any tax regulation or procedural requirement, specify the nature of the breach and the applicable regulation or procedure under which the amount of penalty for the breach has been specified.
When to Use — When penalty order merely says "penalty imposed for contravention" without specifying the exact breach and the section under which the penalty quantum is fixed. File objection: order is bad in law for non-compliance with §126(4) mandate.
Section 126(5) — Voluntary Disclosure as Mitigating Factor
Statutory Provision | CGST Act, 2017 | Practical tool in every hearing
Key Principle
When a person voluntarily discloses to an officer the circumstances of a breach of the tax law, regulation or procedural requirement prior to the discovery of the breach by the officer, the proper officer may consider this fact as a mitigating factor in quantifying a penalty for that person.
When to Use — In every hearing where the client had amended returns, filed voluntary rectifications, or informed the Department before receiving any notice. Produce documentary evidence of voluntary disclosure. Argue for minimum penalty under §126(5).
Section 122(2)(b) — Fraud Must Be Proved (Not Presumed)
CGST Act, 2017 | Read with Dharmendra Textiles Processors v. CCE (SC) | Universal application
Key Principle
Under §122(2)(b), 100% penalty applies only "for reason of fraud or wilful misstatement or suppression of facts to evade tax." The legislature has deliberate distinction between (a) non-fraud (10%) and (b) fraud (100%). The burden of proving fraud is on the Department — mere short payment or wrong classification does not constitute fraud. Suppression means wilful concealment — not mere error or omission.
When to Use — When Department imposes 100% penalty under §122(2)(b) or §74 (100% penalty provision) for what is actually a classification dispute, ITC reversal, or interpretation-based short-payment. Argue: No fraud — penalty should be §122(2)(a) at 10%, not (b) at 100%.
Section 132(4) — Non-Cognizable and Bailable (General Rule)
CGST Act, 2017 | Critical for arrest/bail situations
Key Principle
Notwithstanding anything contained in the Code of Criminal Procedure, 1973, offences under this Act, except the offences referred to in sub-section (5), shall be non-cognizable and bailable. Only offences under §132(1)(a), (b), (c), (d) involving amount exceeding ₹500 lakh (5 crores) are cognizable and non-bailable under §132(5).
When to Use — When any officer attempts to arrest without specifying §132(5) offence or arrest without warrant for a case where amount is below ₹5 crores. Cite §132(4): offence is bailable. Demand bail as of right. Challenge arrest if offence is non-cognizable.
Section 132(6) — Prior Sanction of Commissioner Mandatory for Prosecution
Statutory Provision | CGST Act, 2017 | Jurisdictional prerequisite
Key Principle
A person shall not be prosecuted for any offence under this section except with the previous sanction of the Commissioner. Similarly, §134 requires Commissioner's sanction before any court takes cognizance. These are independent, cumulative requirements — both must be satisfied. Prosecution without prior sanction = void ab initio.
When to Use — When facing criminal prosecution under §132. Immediately verify: (1) Was Commissioner's sanction obtained before filing complaint? (2) Did Commissioner apply his mind independently? (3) Was sanction order a speaking order? A rubber-stamp sanction without application of mind = invalid. Challenge at threshold.
Section 137(4) — Due Diligence Defence for Directors/Partners
CGST Act, 2017 | Applicable in all company/firm prosecution cases
Key Principle
Nothing contained in §137 shall render any such person liable to any punishment if he proves that the offence was committed without his knowledge or that he had exercised all due diligence to prevent the commission of such offence. The burden is on the person seeking protection — document compliance systems, board delegations, and internal controls.
When to Use — When a non-executive director, sleeping partner, or board member is named in prosecution under §137 for acts of operations team. Prove: (a) No knowledge; OR (b) Active compliance system in place — GST compliance team, regular reviews, delegated authority. "Deemed guilty" under §137(1) is rebuttable.
Section 128A — Amnesty: Pay Only Tax, Close All Interest & Penalty
Statutory Provision | Inserted FA(No.2) 2024 | w.e.f. 01.11.2024 | For §73 demands Jul 2017 – Mar 2020
Key Principle
Where the full amount of tax as per the notice or order under §73 pertaining to the period 01.07.2017 to 31.03.2020 is paid on or before the date notified by the Government, no interest under §50 and no penalty under this Act shall be payable and all proceedings shall be deemed to be concluded. Applies at all three stages: (a) Pre-order stage; (b) Post-order pre-appeal stage; (c) Post-first-appellate pre-High-Court stage.
When to Use — For any non-fraud §73 demand for FY 2017-18, 2018-19, 2019-20. Calculate tax amount payable. Verify notified date. Pay tax and file application. Get proceedings closed. Major litigation-cost reduction tool — avoids years of appeal with interest accumulating.
Section 140 Transitional ITC — Filco Trade Centre Ratio
Union of India v. Filco Trade Centre Pvt. Ltd. | Supreme Court | 2022
Key Ratio
A registered person who could not file TRAN-1 within the prescribed period due to technical glitches on the common portal or other valid reasons beyond his control shall be permitted to claim the transitional ITC. The right to carry forward legitimately earned CENVAT credit is a vested right and cannot be extinguished by technical/procedural filing failures. Constitutional protection under Article 300A applies.
When to Use — When transitional ITC claim under §140 was rejected solely because TRAN-1 was not filed within Rule 117 time limit due to portal errors or technical issues. Cite Filco SC ratio. Produce evidence of attempts to file: screenshots, error logs, IT cell complaints. Claim as vested right.
Section 135 — Reverse Burden Is Rebuttable
CGST Act, 2017 | Section 135 | To be used in criminal proceedings
Key Principle
It shall be a defence for the accused to prove the fact that he had no such culpable mental state with respect to the act charged as an offence in that prosecution. A fact is proved only when the court believes it to exist beyond reasonable doubt — and not merely when its existence is established by a preponderance of probability. The accused need only prove absence of culpable mental state on preponderance of probability — not beyond reasonable doubt.
When to Use — In prosecution under §132 where mens rea (guilty mind) is being presumed under §135. Affirmatively prove: bona fide belief, reasonable interpretation adopted, absence of motive, genuine business transaction. The standard to rebut is lower — balance of probabilities, not beyond reasonable doubt.
Section 129(1)(a) vs (b) — Owner Should Always Come Forward
Practical Litigation Strategy | CGST Act §129 | Applicable in all transit detention cases
Key Principle
When goods are detained in transit, §129(1)(a) provides release on payment of 200% of tax payable when owner comes forward. §129(1)(b) imposes penalty equal to 50% of value OR 200% of tax (whichever higher) when owner does not come forward. For high-value low-tax-rate goods (e.g., agricultural commodities), 50% of value can be astronomically higher than 200% of tax. The differential between (a) and (b) can be lakhs.
When to Use — Advise transporter/owner immediately on detention news: come forward under §129(1)(a) WITHOUT admitting any liability. State "coming forward for release under §129(1)(a) without prejudice to all rights." Pay penalty, get release, then challenge in appeal. Do NOT let it proceed under (b) — the financial consequences are far worse.
For professional litigation use | Pawan Soni & Associates, Ichalkaranji
CGST Act — Ch.XXI + Schedules I–III
