🏗️ TIER 1 — Chapter XXI Miscellaneous: Overview of All Sections (§143–174)
BIRDS-EYE VIEW — Click Section Links to Jump to Detailed Cards
SectionShort TitleWhat It DoesTypeLitigation Relevance
§143Job Work ProcedurePrincipal may send inputs/capital goods for job work without tax; must return/supply within 1 yr (inputs) / 3 yrs (CG). Deemed supply on expiry.RIGHT+OBLIGATIONHigh — ITC, deemed supply, additional place of business issues
§144Presumption as to DocumentsCourt shall presume truthfulness of seized/received documents — signature, contents, execution — unless contrary proved.EVIDENTIARYHigh — Used against assessee in investigations; can be rebutted
§145Admissibility of Electronic RecordsMicrofilm, facsimile, computer printouts, electronically stored data — deemed documents — admissible without original.EVIDENTIARYHigh — GSTN data, portal records, e-way bills admitted as evidence
§146Common PortalGovernment may notify Common GST Electronic Portal for registration, payment, returns, e-way bill etc.PROCEDURALModerate — Portal glitches, technical errors in filings
§147Deemed ExportsGovernment may notify certain domestic supplies as deemed exports (goods must be manufactured in India).BENEFITHigh — Refund claims, LUT, export benefits for EOU/SEZ suppliers
§148Special ProcedureGovernment may notify certain classes of registered persons for special procedures (registration, return, payment).PROCEDURALModerate — E-commerce, composition, specific sector rules
§148ATrack and TraceNew (Finance Act 2025 w.e.f. 01.10.2025) — unique identification marking on specified goods; persons must affix, maintain records, pay system charges.COMPLIANCEEmerging — Track & trace violations, penalties; tobacco/pharma likely
§149GST Compliance RatingEvery registered person may be assigned compliance rating based on record; rating updated periodically and published publicly.ADMINISTRATIVEModerate — ITC restriction linked to low rating
§150Obligation to Furnish Information ReturnWide list of entities (stock exchanges, RBI, banks, Registrar, income tax authorities etc.) must furnish information returns to GST authorities.OBLIGATIONHigh — Cross-verification, notices based on third-party data
§151Power to Call for Information(Amended 2022) Commissioner or authorised officer may by order direct ANY person to furnish information in specified time/form/manner.POWERHigh — Broad investigative power; no restriction to taxable persons only
§152Bar on DisclosureInformation given u/s 150/151 cannot be published identifying particular person without written consent; cannot be used in proceedings without giving opportunity of hearing.SAFEGUARDHigh — Assessee right to be heard before info used against them
§153Expert AssistanceOfficer not below Asst. Commissioner may take expert assistance at any stage of scrutiny/inquiry/investigation.PROCEDURALModerate — Challenge to expert used without proper appointment
§154Power to Take SamplesCommissioner/authorised officer may take samples from taxable person's goods with receipt.INVESTIGATIONModerate — Receipt mandatory; basis for demand on test results
§155Burden of Proof — ITCWhere any person claims ITC under CGST Act, burden of proving the claim lies on SUCH PERSON.CRITICALVery High — Dept uses this in every ITC denial; assessee must prove entitlement
§156Persons Deemed Public ServantsAll persons discharging functions under CGST Act = public servants (IPC §21).STATUSModerate — Prosecution, bribery angles
§157Protection of OfficersNo suit/prosecution against Appellate Tribunal or officers for anything done in good faith under the Act.PROTECTIONModerate — Limitation on suing officers; good faith defence
§158Disclosure of Information by Public ServantAll statements, returns, accounts, documents produced under Act are CONFIDENTIAL. 12 specific exceptions listed (prosecution, audit, govt purposes etc.).SAFEGUARDHigh — Confidentiality of assessee data; exceptions for audit/prosecution
§158AConsent-Based Data Sharing(Finance Act 2023 w.e.f. 01.10.2023) — Common portal may share taxable person's data with other govt systems with CONSENT. No liability on Govt for sharing.DATAModerate — GSTN data sharing with income tax, banks etc.
§159Publication of NamesCommissioner may publish name of persons in pending proceedings. But NOT until appeal time expires or appeal is disposed of.RESTRICTIONHigh — Cannot publish during appeal; safeguard against premature naming
§160Invalid Proceedings — SavingsProceedings not invalid merely due to mistake/defect/omission IF in substance they conform to the intent of the Act. Service of notice not questionable if already acted upon.CAUTIONHigh — Dept uses to save defective SCNs/orders; assessee challenge still valid for substantial defects
§161Rectification of ErrorsAny authority may rectify errors apparent on face of record — suo motu or on notice — within 6 months of issue. No restriction for pure clerical errors. AUDI ALTERAM PARTEM if adverse.REMEDYHigh — Assessee can seek rectification; Dept can correct orders
§162Bar on Civil CourtsNo civil court jurisdiction on any matter arising under CGST Act (except §§117, 118).JURISDICTIONModerate — Writ jurisdiction of HC survives
§164Power to Make RulesGovt may make rules on recommendations of Council. Rules can have retrospective effect from commencement date.LEGISLATIVEHigh — Retrospective rules; validity challenge
§165–168Regulations / Laying / Delegation / InstructionsBoard may make regulations; every rule/regulation/notification must be laid before Parliament; Commissioner may delegate powers; Board may issue instructions to officers.ADMINISTRATIVEModerate — Circulars binding on officers (§168); assessee can rely
§168AForce Majeure Extension(Inserted 2020 retrospectively from 01.03.2020) — Govt may extend time limits for force majeure (war, epidemic, flood, drought, fire, cyclone, earthquake etc.).RELIEFVery High — COVID extensions; validation of time-barred actions
§169Service of Notice6 modes of service: direct/messenger, registered post/speed post, email, common portal, newspaper, affixation. Deemed service provisions.PROCEDURALHigh — Challenge to service; deemed service on portal upload
§170Rounding OffTax, interest, penalty, fine, refund — round to nearest rupee; ≥50 paise → ₹1; <50 paise → ignored.PROCEDURALLow
§171Anti-ProfiteeringBenefit of tax rate reduction or ITC must be passed on to recipient by commensurate price reduction. NAA/Appellate Tribunal may examine. Penalty = 10% of profiteered amount (payable within 30 days to avoid penalty).CRITICALVery High — Profiteering demands; penalty; appeal; new sunset clause inserted
§172Removal of DifficultiesGovt may remove difficulties by general/special order within 5 years of commencement.LEGISLATIVEModerate — Used for urgent corrections without full amendment
§173–174Amendment & Repeal + SavingFinance Act 1994 Chapter V omitted. Central Excise Act 1944 and 4 other Acts repealed. Savings for all prior proceedings, rights, liabilities, duties.TRANSITIONALModerate — Legacy proceedings under service tax / central excise
🏗️ TIER 1 — Schedules I, II, III: Overview
SCHEDULES ANNEXED TO SECTION 7 — DEFINING SCOPE OF "SUPPLY"
ScheduleParaSubjectConsequenceLitigation Relevance
Schedule I
(§7)		Para 1Permanent transfer/disposal of business assets where ITC availedSupply even without consideration → GST payableHigh — Asset disposal, write-offs, ITC-availed assets
Para 2Supply between related persons / distinct persons u/s 25 — in course of businessSupply even without consideration → GST payable. Exception: gifts ≤ ₹50,000/year to employeeVery High — Branch transfers, holding-subsidiary transactions
Para 3Principal to agent / agent to principal — goods supplySupply even without considerationHigh — Commission agents, del credere agents
Para 4Import of services by person from related person / establishments outside IndiaSupply even without consideration → GST under RCMHigh — Intra-group service imports, shared services from parent
Schedule II
(§7)		Para 1Transfer — title in goods / right without title / agreement to sellClassifies as goods supply or serviceHigh — Hire purchase, financial lease, conditional sale
Para 2Land and Building — lease/tenancy/easement/licence to occupy land; lease/letting of buildingBoth are supply of SERVICESVery High — Renting disputes, commercial vs residential
Para 3Treatment or process on another person's goodsSupply of SERVICESHigh — Job work, finishing work
Para 4Transfer of business assets / private use of business goods / cessation of taxable personVarious — goods supply or service depending on sub-clauseModerate — Deemed supply on cessation
Para 5Supply of services — renting of immovable property, construction, IP, IT software, tolerate/refrain, right to use goods, works contract, food supplyAll listed activities = supply of SERVICESVery High — Real estate, construction, IP, works contract rates
Para 6Composite supply — works contract and food supply with serviceSupply of SERVICESVery High — Works contract tax rate, restaurant composite supply
Schedule III
(§7)		Para 1Employee services to employer in course of employmentNEITHER goods NOR services → outside GSTHigh — Perquisite vs supply distinction
Para 2Services by court/tribunalOutside GSTLow
Para 3Functions by MPs, MLAs, Panchayat/Municipality members, constitutional post holdersOutside GSTLow
Para 4Funeral, burial, crematorium, mortuary services including transport of deceasedOutside GSTLow
Para 5Sale of land and (subject to Sch II Para 5(b)) sale of buildingOutside GST — with condition for under-construction buildingsVery High — Land sale, completed building sale, JDA issues
Para 6Actionable claims other than specified actionable claims (lottery, betting, gambling substituted w.e.f. 01.10.2023)Outside GSTHigh — Online gaming, lottery, gambling; specified AC now taxable
Para 7Supply of goods from non-taxable territory to another non-taxable territory without entering IndiaOutside GST (retrospective w.e.f. 01.07.2017)High — High seas sales, ship/aircraft stores
Para 8(a)Supply of warehoused goods before clearance for home consumptionOutside GSTHigh — Customs bonded warehouse supply
Para 8(aa)Supply of goods warehoused in SEZ/FTWZ before clearance for exports or to DTA (Finance Act 2025 w.e.f. 01.10.2025 retrospective from 01.07.2017)Outside GSTHigh — SEZ/FTWZ goods supply disputes
Para 9–10Co-insurance apportionment by lead insurer to co-insurer; ceding commission/reinsurance commission in reinsurance (inserted Finance Act 2024 w.e.f. 01.11.2024)Outside GSTHigh — Insurance industry disputes
§143 — Job Work Procedure
🔧 Section 143 — Job Work
Principal sends inputs/capital goods for job work without tax payment
📝 A manufacturer (principal) can send raw materials or machines to a job worker (processor) without paying GST on that movement. But there are strict time limits to bring them back — or supply directly from job worker's place. Miss the deadline → deemed supply → GST payable from day of sending out.
§143(1) — Conditions for Tax-Free Movement
INPUTS
Must be brought back OR supplied from job worker's place
within 1 Year of sending out
§143(3) — NOT returned/supplied in 1 year → Deemed supply by principal to job worker on day of sending out
💡 Textile principal sends grey fabric to processing unit. If not returned in 1 year — GST payable on fabric as if sold to job worker on day 1 of dispatch.
CAPITAL GOODS
(other than moulds/dies/jigs/fixtures/tools)
within 3 Years of sending out
§143(4) — NOT returned/supplied in 3 years → Deemed supply by principal to job worker on day of sending out
💡 Machine sent for repair to job worker. Not returned in 3 years → deemed sold to job worker → GST + interest from date of sending.
EXEMPT FROM LIMITS
Moulds, dies, jigs, fixtures, tools — NO time limit for capital goods
📝 These are permanent tooling items that often stay at the job worker permanently. Legislature specifically carved them out of the 3-year limit.
⚠️ CRITICAL PROVISO — §143(1)(b)
Principal CANNOT supply goods directly from job worker's place of business UNLESS:
(i) Job worker is registered under §25, OR
(ii) Principal has declared job worker's place as additional place of business
Exception: Notified goods by Commissioner
📝 If your job worker is unregistered AND you haven't declared his premises in your GST registration as your additional place — you cannot directly supply from his place. You must first bring goods back to your registered premises.
💡 Principal in Mumbai supplies embroidered sarees directly from job worker in Surat. Job worker unregistered, not declared as APOB → illegal supply → GST liability + ITC risk + potential demand under §73/74.
🔍 GP Singh: Provisos must be strictly construed — they carve out exceptions to the main rule. The conditions in the proviso to §143(1)(b) are MANDATORY, not directory. Non-compliance renders the supply itself illegal. State cannot be asked to ignore mandatory conditions. (Principles of Statutory Interpretation, 13th Ed.)
SECOND PROVISO (inserted 2019)
Commissioner may extend 1 year / 3 year period by further 1 year / 3 years on sufficient cause shown
📝 If you can show genuine reason (dispute, quality issues, business circumstances), you can apply for extension. But you need to apply BEFORE the original period expires.
§143(5) — Waste and Scrap from Job Work
If job worker is registered → job worker can supply waste/scrap from his place directly, on payment of tax
If job worker is NOT registered → principal must supply waste/scrap (and pay tax)
📝 Scrap/waste generated during job work must still attract GST. If job worker is unregistered, the principal is responsible for paying GST on such scrap/waste even though he doesn't physically have it.
EXPLANATION
"Input" for job work purposes includes intermediate goods arising from treatment/process carried out on inputs by the principal OR the job worker
📋 Corresponding Rules: Rules 45–46 CGST Rules, 2017 (ITC-04 — Challan for job work)
§144 — Presumption as to Documents | §145 — Admissibility of Electronic Records
📄 Evidentiary Provisions — §§144 & 145
Documents, Electronic Records & Presumptions in Proceedings
SectionTriggerWhat Court/Authority MUST DOWhat Can Be Rebutted?Litigation Point
§144
Presumption as to Documents		 Document (a) produced by person under this Act; (b) seized from custody; (c) received from outside India — tendered as evidence in prosecution Court SHALL presume:
(i) Truth of contents
(ii) Signatures are genuine
(iii) Document executed/attested by stated person

Court SHALL admit even if not duly stamped		 "Unless the contrary is proved" — burden shifts to accused/person to rebut presumption of truth, signature, execution ⚠️ Assessee Risk: Seized documents presumed true. To challenge — affirmatively prove forgery, tampering, or that you didn't sign/execute. Mere denial insufficient.
§145(1)
Electronic Records Admissible		 Any of: (a) microfilm; (b) facsimile copy; (c) computer printout; (d) electronically stored data including hard copies Deemed to be a document for all purposes of CGST Act. Admissible without further proof or production of original. Evidence of contents of original. — ✓ Dept uses: GSTN portal data, GSTR-2A/2B, e-invoices, e-way bills — all admissible. ✓ Assessee uses: Email correspondences, digital invoices, electronic ledger data — also admissible.
§145(2)
Certificate for Computer Records		 When using computer-generated documents as evidence Certificate identifying document and describing production manner + details of device used = evidence of any matter stated in certificate. Sufficient if stated to best of knowledge and belief. — 📌 Practical: When Dept relies on GSTN-generated data — ask for certificate u/s 145(2). If no certificate, challenge admissibility. Certificate must describe HOW data was generated/stored.
🔍 GP Singh: "Presumptions in penal statutes are to be strictly construed. The presumption u/s 144 is a rebuttable presumption — the words 'unless the contrary is proved' create a reversible burden. The standard of proof to rebut is on balance of probabilities in civil proceedings, but beyond reasonable doubt would apply in criminal prosecution contexts." — Read with Indian Evidence Act §4 on rebuttable presumptions.
⚔️ WORD WAR — "SHALL PRESUME" vs "MAY PRESUME"
SHALL PRESUME (§144)
Mandatory — court has NO discretion to ignore the presumption. It MUST treat contents as true unless rebutted. Much stronger position for prosecution/Dept.
MAY PRESUME (Indian Evidence Act §114)
Discretionary — court may or may not draw the presumption depending on facts and circumstances. Weaker for Dept — assessee can argue court should not draw it.
§144 uses SHALL — mandatory, not discretionary. This means you CANNOT ask the adjudicating authority to ignore a seized document. You must affirmatively rebut it.
§146 — Common Portal | §147 — Deemed Exports
🌐 Common Portal (§146) & Deemed Exports (§147)
§146 — COMMON PORTAL
Government may notify Common GST Electronic Portal for:
• Registration
• Payment of tax
• Furnishing returns
• Computation & settlement of IGST
• Electronic Way Bill
• Such other functions as prescribed
📝 This is the legal authority for creating the GSTN portal (www.gst.gov.in). The portal IS the gateway for all GST compliance. Technical failures on the portal have been the subject of many HC petitions seeking extension of deadlines.
💡 Portal error on last date of GSTR-3B filing → assessee approached HC citing §146 read with §168A — portal is government's own system; its failure cannot penalise assessee.
§147 — DEEMED EXPORTS
Government may notify supplies as "deemed exports" where:
✓ Goods do NOT leave India
✓ Payment received in INR or convertible forex
✓ Goods manufactured in India
📝 Deemed exports = domestic supplies that are treated like exports for the purpose of refunds and benefits. The actual goods stay in India but the supplier gets export-like treatment (refund of GST paid).
💡 Supply to EOU / supply against Advance Authorisation / supply to project under global tender — notified as deemed exports. Supplier can claim refund of GST paid. Buyer cannot claim ITC on same goods.
🔍 GP Singh: "Deeming provisions must be construed strictly — only what is expressly included within the fiction is deemed. The fiction cannot be extended beyond its stated purpose." — Strictly interpret which goods/supplies fall within notification u/s 147.
📋 Common Portal: Notified via Notification No. 4/2017-CT | Deemed Exports: Notification No. 48/2017-CT dated 18.10.2017 and subsequent amendments
§148 — Special Procedure | §148A — Track and Trace Mechanism (Finance Act 2025)
⚙️ §148 Special Procedure & §148A Track and Trace
§148 — SPECIAL PROCEDURE
Govt + Council recommendations → notify:
• Certain classes of registered persons
• Special procedures for: Registration / Return / Payment / Administration
Subject to conditions and safeguards
📝 This is the enabling power for creating special simplified or alternative GST procedures for specific industries or categories. Used for composition scheme modifications, sector-specific notifications etc.
💡 Notification No. 66/2017-CT under §148 — composition dealers allowed to pay tax on outward supply at time of supply in §12(a) manner — no advance tax on receipt.
§148A — TRACK & TRACE (w.e.f. 01.10.2025)
Inserted by Finance Act 2025 — New Anti-Evasion Tool:
§148A(1): Govt notifies GOODS and PERSONS for track & trace
§148A(2): Prescribes unique identification marking system
§148A(3): Persons must:
(a) Affix unique ID marking
(b) Furnish information & maintain records
(c) Furnish machinery details (capacity, duration of operation)
(d) Pay system charges
📝 Track & trace = barcodes, QR codes, or other unique markings on specified goods (likely tobacco, pharma, alcoholic beverages in GST scope). Every item is traceable from manufacturer to retailer. Evasion becomes detectable instantly.
⚠️ KEY COMPLIANCE OBLIGATIONS
Failure to affix → violation → prosecution / demand risk
Failure to furnish machinery details → deemed suppression possible
Pay system charges → mandatory cost of compliance
💡 Tobacco manufacturer must affix unique QR code on each pack. If packets in circulation don't have marking → clandestine manufacture presumed → demand under §74 (fraud/suppression).
§149 — GST Compliance Rating | §150 — Obligation to Furnish Information Return
📊 §149 GST Compliance Rating + §150 Information Returns
§149 — COMPLIANCE RATING
Every registered person MAY be assigned GST compliance rating
Parameters: as prescribed
Updated: periodically
Published: publicly in manner prescribed
Communicated: to registered person
📝 Like a credit score but for GST compliance. Based on timely filing, payment record etc. Low rating = bad reputation + potential ITC denial by recipients dealing with you. Though not yet fully operational, the provision is live.
🔍 GP Singh: "The word 'may' in §149(1) — 'may be assigned' — is discretionary, not mandatory. The government is not obligated to assign compliance ratings. The provision is merely enabling." — Contrast with §§73/74 where 'shall' is used for mandatory demands.
§150 — INFORMATION RETURNS
WIDE LIST of entities MUST furnish information return:
→ Taxable persons
→ State/Central tax authorities (VAT, Excise, Customs)
→ Income Tax authority
→ Banking companies
→ Electricity boards/licensees
→ Registrar of properties (Registration Act)
→ Companies Registrar
→ Motor Vehicles registering authority
→ Land Acquisition Collector
→ Stock Exchanges, Depositories
→ RBI officers
→ GSTN
→ UIN holders
→ Any other person notified
📝 This creates a 360° information network around taxpayers. GST authorities can cross-check your sales with data from stock exchange, property registrar, bank, electricity board. Mismatches → notices.
💡 Assessee declared ₹50L turnover in GST but property registrar shows land sale of ₹2Cr → mismatch → SCN for escaped supply. OR electricity consumption shows large scale production but GST returns show minimal output → §74 demand.
NON-COMPLIANCE CONSEQUENCES
Defective return: 30-day window to rectify → if uncured → treated as NOT furnished
Notice for non-filing: 90 days from notice to furnish
📋 §150: Rules for information return format — to be prescribed under CGST Rules
§151 — Power to Call for Information | §152 — Bar on Disclosure
🔎 §151 Investigation Power + §152 Safeguard
§151 — POWER TO CALL INFO (amended 2022)
Commissioner or authorised officer may by ORDER direct ANY PERSON to furnish:
• Information relating to ANY MATTER dealt with under this Act
• Within specified TIME
• In specified FORM
• In specified MANNER
📝 This is very broad. It says "any person" — not just registered taxable persons. A transporter, a CA, a bank — anyone can be asked. The order must specify time, form and manner. Before 2022 amendment, this was restricted to statistics collection only.
📖 "ANY PERSON"
Widest possible ambit — not limited to 'taxable person' or 'registered person'. Includes third parties, service providers, professionals.
Legislature deliberately chose "any person" over "taxable person" to enable third-party investigation. GST Council specifically amended §151 in 2022 to remove statistical restriction.
⚡ Action: When responding to §151 order, check: (1) Is there a proper ORDER (not mere letter)? (2) Is it by Commissioner or authorised officer? (3) Is it about a "matter dealt with in connection with this Act"?
§152 — BAR ON DISCLOSURE (SAFEGUARD)
Information given u/s 150 or 151 CANNOT:
✓ Be published so as to identify particular person — WITHOUT written consent
✓ Be used for ANY proceedings under this Act — WITHOUT opportunity of hearing to person concerned

Exception (§152(3)):
Publication of class of taxable persons or class of transactions if Commissioner deems public interest
📝 Even though Dept has power to collect information, it cannot weaponise it without following due process. Before using information obtained from a third party against you in a tax proceeding — you must be given a chance to explain or rebut.
💡 Department collects bank statement of assessee from bank u/s 151. Uses it in SCN without giving assessee chance to explain banking entries → assessee argues violation of §152(1) → opportunity of hearing MUST be given before using that information.
🔍 GP Singh: The proviso "without giving opportunity of being heard to the person concerned" (inserted by Finance Act 2021) is a mandatory safeguard. A proceeding conducted without this opportunity is violative of natural justice and the express mandate of §152.
⚔️ WORD WAR — §151 before and after Finance Act 2021
PRE-2022 §151
"Power to collect statistics" — limited to statistics through notification by Commissioner. Could only call for statistical data. Very narrow scope.
POST-2022 §151
"Power to call for information" — any information, any matter, any person, by order. No restriction to statistics. Massively expanded investigative power.
Legislature deliberately substituted 'statistics' with 'information' and 'notification' with 'order'. This expansion means Dept can now directly order any person to produce information — much stronger than before.
§153–156 — Expert Assistance | Samples | Burden of Proof | Public Servants
SectionPower / RuleKey ConditionsPlain EnglishLitigation Point
§153
Expert Assistance		 Officer ≥ Asst. Commissioner may take assistance of ANY expert at any stage of scrutiny / inquiry / investigation / proceedings Having regard to: (a) nature and complexity of case; (b) interest of revenue Like hiring a specialist consultant (valuer, chemist, CA) to help with a complex case. Challenge expert used: (1) Was proper appointment made? (2) Was assessee given copy of expert report? (3) Was natural justice followed before using report in order?
§154
Power to Take Samples		 Commissioner or authorised officer may take samples of goods from taxable person's possession where necessary MUST provide receipt for samples taken Officer can take physical samples of goods (e.g., chemicals, food products, textiles) for testing. Receipt is mandatory — no receipt = no valid sample. No receipt given → challenge validity of sample. Test results based on unsigned/undated samples → challenge reliability. Demand based on sample report → demand copy of lab test report before responding.
§155
Burden of Proof — ITC		 WHERE any person claims entitlement to ITC under CGST Act — burden of PROVING such claim lies on SUCH PERSON No exception or qualification — absolute rule In ITC disputes, YOU (the taxpayer) must prove you are entitled to credit. The Department does not need to prove you are NOT entitled. This is the reverse of the normal Evidence Act position. VERY HIGH IMPORTANCE. Dept cites §155 in every ITC denial. Counter: prove supplier existed (registration certificate), goods received (transport documents, stock records), payment made (bank records), tax paid by supplier (GSTR-2A/2B). Mere 2A/2B match is not sufficient by itself if supplier is fraudulent — assessee must also show due diligence.
§156
Public Servants		 ALL persons discharging functions under CGST Act = public servants within meaning of §21 IPC (now BNS) No qualification — applies to ALL persons, including GSPs, GST practitioners, authorised representatives discharging statutory functions Treating everyone in the GST machinery as a government servant for prosecution purposes. Bribery, corruption, misconduct → IPC/BNS provisions apply. Relevant when challenging officer misconduct; also relevant for GST practitioners acting in official capacity.
📖 BURDEN OF PROOF — §155 — Key Word: "PROVES"
Black's Law Dictionary: "Burden of proof" = obligation of a party to establish facts to a required degree of certainty. Under §155, the taxpayer must affirmatively establish EACH ingredient of ITC entitlement.
SC in State of Karnataka v. Ecom Gill Coffee Trading: "Mere production of purchase invoices is not sufficient to prove ITC claim. The purchasing dealer must show that goods were actually received, used for business, and that the selling dealer is genuine and tax has actually been paid." — This ratio directly applies to §155 CGST Act.
⚡ Action in Hearing: When Dept denies ITC citing §155, counter by: (1) placing ALL documents on record, (2) showing supplier's GST registration, (3) showing goods receipt notes, (4) showing bank payment proof, (5) showing GSTR-2A/2B reflection. Then argue: burden discharged — Dept must now show specific fraud/non-payment.
§157 — Protection of Officers | §158 — Disclosure of Information by Public Servant
🛡️ §157 Officer Protection + §158 Confidentiality of Taxpayer Data
§157 — GOOD FAITH PROTECTION
No suit, prosecution or legal proceedings against:
• President/Members of Appellate Tribunal
• Officers of Appellate Tribunal
• Any officer appointed/authorised under the Act
For anything done or intended to be done in good faith under the Act
📝 Officers are protected from personal legal action when they act honestly under the Act. This prevents taxpayers from suing every officer personally. BUT — it protects only "good faith" actions. If officer acts maliciously, corruptly, or outside statutory authority — no protection.
🔍 GP Singh: "Good faith protection under statutory provisions must be read strictly. 'Good faith' means honest belief — but an officer acting beyond his statutory powers cannot claim good faith protection even if he honestly believed he was authorised." — Vimalabai Deshpande v. State.
§158 — CONFIDENTIALITY RULE
ALL particulars in statements / returns / accounts / documents / evidence in GST proceedings = CONFIDENTIAL
Cannot be disclosed except in 12 specified situations
12 EXCEPTIONS TO CONFIDENTIALITY
(a) Prosecution under IPC/Prevention of Corruption Act/any law
(b) To Central/State Govt for implementing this Act
(c) Process for service of notice or recovery
(d) Civil court in suit where Govt is party (arising from GST proceedings)
(e) Audit officer (tax records / refunds)
(f) Inquiry into officer misconduct
(g) Central/State Govt officer to realise tax/duty
(h) Lawful exercise by public servant / statutory authority
(i) Disciplinary action against CA/CS/Advocate/CMA practitioner
(j) Data entry/automated system agency (contractually bound)
(k) Govt officer for purposes of other law
(l) Class of taxable persons or transactions — public interest publication
📝 Your GST returns, accounts and declarations to the tax department cannot be freely shared or used against you outside the 12 listed purposes. Key for you: Exception (i) means your data CAN be shared with ICAI if you are under disciplinary action.
📋 §158 also read with §133 (witnesses and summons) — confidentiality runs through all three
§158A — Consent-Based Sharing of Information (Finance Act 2023 w.e.f. 01.10.2023)
🔗 §158A — Consent-Based Data Sharing from Common Portal
Notwithstanding §§133, 152, 158 — new consent framework
📝 The government can now share your GST data (registration details, returns, invoice data) with other government systems — but ONLY WITH YOUR CONSENT. Effectively enables connected e-governance: GSTN data can flow to banks for loans, to income tax dept, to MSME schemes etc. But if government/portal makes an error in sharing — no liability on them.
WHAT CAN BE SHARED
(a) Registration application particulars + returns (GSTR-39/44)
(b) Invoice particulars uploaded on portal + outward supply data (§37) + documents under §68 (e-way bill)
(c) Such other details as prescribed
WHOSE CONSENT
Supplier: consent for (a), (b), (c)
Recipient: consent for (b) and (c) where recipient identity info is included
NO LIABILITY ON GOVT
No action against Government or common portal for any liability arising from sharing
No impact on tax liability for relevant supply or return
📝 Government has fully indemnified itself. If your data is shared incorrectly and causes you harm — you cannot sue the portal or Government. This is a significant statutory immunity.
§159 — Publication of Information | §160 — Proceedings Not Invalid on Certain Grounds
📢 §159 Naming + §160 Saving of Defective Proceedings
§159 — PUBLICATION OF NAMES
Commissioner/authorised officer may cause to be published:
• Name of any person
• Other particulars relating to proceedings/prosecution
If necessary or expedient in PUBLIC INTEREST
⚠️ RESTRICTION — CANNOT PUBLISH UNTIL:
Time for appeal to Appellate Authority u/s 107 has EXPIRED without appeal, OR
Appeal presented has been DISPOSED OF
📝 Even if penalty is confirmed — they cannot publicly name you while your appeal is pending. The moment you file appeal under §107, naming is barred until appeal is decided. Explanation: for firms/companies — partner/director names can also be published if circumstances justify.
💡 Department issues notice for ₹2Cr GST fraud. Immediately publishes assessee's name as "fraudster" before appeal period expires → violation of §159(2) → assessee can approach HC for injunction against publication.
§160 — SAVINGS CLAUSE FOR DEFECTIVE PROCEEDINGS
Proceedings are NOT invalid merely because of:
• Mistake
• Defect
• Omission
IF they are in SUBSTANCE AND EFFECT in conformity with the intent, purposes and requirements of the Act
📝 This is Dept's "saving clause" — they use it to argue that a defective SCN or order should be upheld because it substantially complied with the law. But this does NOT save proceedings that violate fundamental principles of natural justice or jurisdictional requirements.
§160(2) — SERVICE CANNOT BE CHALLENGED
Service of notice/order cannot be questioned if:
(a) Already ACTED UPON by person, OR
(b) Defect not raised at or before beginning of proceedings
🔍 GP Singh: §160 saves only PROCEDURAL or FORMAL defects — mistakes, omissions, minor errors. It cannot save JURISDICTIONAL defects or fundamental violations of natural justice. If SCN lacks DIN, lacks jurisdiction, or was issued beyond limitation — §160 offers no refuge to the Dept. Defects going to the root of jurisdiction are NOT saved.
💡 SCN issued without DIN (Document Identification Number) as required by CBIC circular → Dept says §160 saves it → assessee counters: DIN is mandatory, not procedural; its absence goes to root of validity; §160 inapplicable. HC has accepted this in multiple jurisdictions.
§161 — Rectification of Errors | §162 — Bar on Civil Court Jurisdiction
✏️ §161 Rectification + §162 Civil Court Bar
Aspect§161 — Rectification of Errors§162 — Bar on Civil Courts
Who Can Use Any authority who passed the decision/order/notice/certificate/document. Can also be moved by: any officer under CGST/SGST/UTGST Act, or AFFECTED PERSON. General bar — prevents ALL civil courts from deciding questions arising under CGST Act.
Time Limit Within 6 months from date of issue of decision/order/notice/certificate.
Proviso 1: No rectification AFTER 6 months (general rule).
Proviso 2: 6-month limit does NOT apply for CLERICAL or ARITHMETICAL errors from accidental slip or omission.		 No time limit for the bar itself — ongoing.
Trigger Error which is APPARENT ON FACE OF RECORD — either suo motu or on notice. Any question arising from or relating to anything done or purported to be done under the Act.
Limitation/Restriction Where rectification ADVERSELY AFFECTS any person → AUDI ALTERAM PARTEM (principles of natural justice) must be followed. Opportunity of hearing is mandatory. EXCEPTIONS: §§117 and 118 (Appeals to High Court and Supreme Court on questions of law from Appellate Tribunal decisions). HC Writ jurisdiction also survives.
Litigation Use Assessee can file rectification application within 6 months for: wrong computation, wrong PAN/GSTIN, wrong address, wrong tax amount. Dept can use to enhance demand (but must give hearing). Clerical errors can be corrected ANYTIME by assessee. Civil court cannot entertain injunctions against GST proceedings. But HC writ petition (Article 226/227) remains available. Filing civil suit for GST-related matter → Dept can apply for dismissal citing §162.
🔍 GP Singh on "Error Apparent on Face of Record": "An error apparent on the face of record means an error that is self-evident from the record itself — no external evidence needed to establish it. A wrong legal conclusion drawn from correct facts is NOT rectifiable under §161. §161 is not an appeal mechanism — it cannot be used to reopen questions of law already decided." — Compare with §73/74 reopening powers which require fresh proceedings.
§§164–168 — Rule Making | Regulations | Laying | Delegation | Board Instructions
📜 Legislative and Administrative Powers — §§164 to 168
SectionPowerKey FeatureLitigation Significance
§164
Power to Make Rules		 Government + Council recommendation → make rules by notification for carrying out provisions of the Act §164(3) CRITICAL: Rules CAN be given RETROSPECTIVE EFFECT from a date NOT EARLIER than date of commencement of CGST Act (01.07.2017) Very High — Retrospective rules challenged as arbitrary. §164(4): Rule violation → penalty up to ₹10,000. Retrospective rules creating new liabilities can be challenged before HC as arbitrary exercise of legislative power.
§165
Power to Make Regulations		 BOARD (CBIC) → make regulations by notification consistent with Act and rules Regulations are secondary legislation below rules; must be consistent with Act and rules. Board cannot exceed Act's framework. Moderate — Regulations can be challenged if ultra vires Act or rules.
§166
Laying before Parliament		 Every rule/regulation/notification must be laid before each House of Parliament within session for 30 days total Parliament can MODIFY or ANNUL. Modification/annulment is WITHOUT PREJUDICE to validity of anything done under it previously. Moderate — If Parliament annuls a rule, past actions under it are still valid.
§167
Delegation		 Commissioner may delegate any power exercisable by any authority/officer to another authority/officer by notification with conditions Delegation can carry conditions. Important for identifying whether officer who issued notice/order had proper delegated authority. High — Challenge delegated power: (1) Was there proper notification? (2) Were conditions of delegation met? (3) Was the person to whom power was delegated eligible?
§168
Board Instructions		 Board may issue orders, instructions or directions to central tax officers for uniform implementation of Act §168(2) CRITICAL: "Commissioner" in specific listed provisions (§5(3), §25(9)(b), §35(3)(4), §37, §39(6), §44, §52(4)(5), §143(1) first proviso) means Commissioner or Joint Secretary POSTED IN THE BOARD acting with BOARD APPROVAL Very High — CBIC Circulars issued under §168 are BINDING ON OFFICERS. Assessee can RELY on circulars to argue against contrary departmental action. If officer acts against circular → ultra vires.
🔍 GP Singh: "Delegated legislation (rules, regulations, notifications) can be challenged on grounds of: (1) Ultra vires the parent Act; (2) Arbitrary and unreasonable; (3) Retrospective with hostile discrimination; (4) Violation of fundamental rights. §164(3) enables retrospective rules — but even this power has constitutional limits under Articles 14 and 19." — CBIC Circulars under §168 are binding on officers and create legitimate expectations in assessees.
§168A — Power to Extend Time Limit in Special Circumstances (Force Majeure)
⏰ §168A — Force Majeure Extension Power
Inserted by Taxation and Other Laws (Relaxation) Act, 2020 — retrospective from 31.03.2020
📝 This section was specifically inserted during COVID-19 to allow the Government to extend any GST deadline when natural or man-made calamities prevent compliance. It has retrospective operation — meaning even if the deadline already passed, the Government can extend it retrospectively.
TRIGGER — FORCE MAJEURE
Definition (Explanation to §168A):
• War
• Epidemic
• Flood
• Drought
• Fire
• Cyclone
• Earthquake
• Any other calamity caused by nature or otherwise affecting implementation of any provision of CGST Act
📝 "Or otherwise" — this is not limited to natural disasters. Man-made calamities (war, epidemic) are equally covered. COVID-19 was an "epidemic" and was specifically covered. The "affecting implementation" qualifier means it must affect actual compliance — not just be inconvenient.
WHAT CAN BE EXTENDED
ANY time limit specified in OR prescribed under OR notified under the Act — for actions which CANNOT be completed or complied with due to force majeure
💡 COVID notifications extended: GSTR filing deadlines, assessment deadlines, anti-profiteering applications, appeal filing periods, ITC claim periods — all using §168A power. Supreme Court in Cognizance for Extension of Limitation: 2022 SCC also extended limitation across proceedings.
RETROACTIVE POWER
§168A(2): Power includes power to give RETROSPECTIVE EFFECT to notification — from a date not earlier than commencement date of CGST Act (01.07.2017)
📝 Government can issue notification TODAY extending a deadline that already passed YESTERDAY. This prevents assessments from being time-barred due to calamities that nobody anticipated.
⚠️ CONTROVERSIAL USE
Dept argued §168A extended assessment limitation periods for FY 2017-18/2018-19 under COVID notifications. SC addressed this in Union of India v. Filco Trade Centre Pvt. Ltd. and related batch matters — limitation period for §73 demands subject to specific analysis.
🔍 GP Singh: "Extension of limitation by executive action (notification) is constitutional only when it is traceable to a statutory provision that itself authorises retrospective extension. §168A explicitly provides this. However, extension notifications must still be interpreted strictly — they extend only what they expressly cover. Vague or unclear extension notifications are to be construed in favour of the assessee." — Critical in current §73/74 demands where Dept claims COVID extension.
📋 Key COVID Extension Notifications: 35/2020-CT, 55/2020-CT, 76/2020-CT | SC in Cognizance for Extension: 2022 SCC | SC in UOI v. Filco Trade: 2022 SCC
§169 — Service of Notice | §170 — Rounding Off
📬 §169 Service of Notice + §170 Rounding Off
§169(1) — Six Modes of Service (Any one of following)
(a)
Direct/Messenger — to addressee, manager, authorised representative, tax practitioner holding authority, person employed in connection with business, adult family member
(b)
Registered Post / Speed Post / Courier with AD — to last known place of business or residence
(c)
Email to address provided at time of registration or as amended
(d)
Making available on COMMON PORTAL
📝 Uploading on portal = service. This means if you don't check portal, you may miss notices. Portal upload = deemed served from upload date.
(e)
Publication in newspaper circulating in locality where taxable person last known to have resided/worked/carried business
(f)
LAST RESORT — Affixing at last known business/residence. If this also not practicable — affixing on NOTICE BOARD of concerned officer
§169(2) — DEEMED SERVICE DATE
Decision/order/summons/notice deemed served on date when tendered, published, or copy affixed as per §169(1)
§169(3) — REGISTERED POST PRESUMPTION
When sent by registered post/speed post → deemed received at EXPIRY OF PERIOD NORMALLY TAKEN BY SUCH POST IN TRANSIT — unless contrary proved
📝 If postal delivery normally takes 5 days, and notice was sent by registered post, it is presumed served 5 days after posting — even without AD card. Assessee must prove non-receipt affirmatively.
§170 — ROUNDING OFF
Tax, interest, penalty, fine, refund → rounded to nearest rupee
Part of rupee ≥ 50 paise → rounded UP to ₹1
Part of rupee < 50 paise → ignored
💡 CRITICAL HEARING POINT on §169: Department issues SCN by uploading on common portal. Assessee claims no notice received. Department argues §169(1)(d) — portal upload = service, §169(2) — deemed served on upload date. COUNTER: Check: (1) Was portal actually functional? (2) Was assessee's email registered? (3) Was assessee given reasonable time after upload? (4) Was this the FIRST notice or follow-up? Courts have held that service by portal alone, without any other mode, especially for old taxpayers unaware of portal, may require corroboration.
§171 — Anti-Profiteering Measure
💰 Section 171 — Anti-Profiteering
Commensurate benefit must pass to consumer on rate reduction or ITC benefit
📝 When GST rate is reduced or when ITC becomes available (reducing costs), the supplier must reduce prices proportionately. If the supplier keeps the benefit — that's "profiteering." Earlier NAA examined cases; now Appellate Tribunal (GSTAT) has been included as "Authority." New sunset clause: Govt can specify date beyond which no new anti-profiteering applications will be accepted.
§171(1) — The Core Obligation
Any reduction in rate of tax on any supply of goods or services OR benefit of ITC → MUST be passed on to recipient by way of commensurate reduction in prices
📝 "Commensurate" = proportionate. If GST on your product is reduced from 18% to 12% (reduction of 6 percentage points), you must reduce the selling price by an equivalent amount. You cannot pocket the benefit.
§171(2) — AUTHORITY TO EXAMINE
Govt + Council recommendation → constitute/empower Authority to examine whether ITC availed / tax rate reduction actually resulted in commensurate price reduction

NEW (Finance Act 2024): Proviso — Govt may notify date beyond which Authority shall not accept new requests for examination
📝 This creates the Anti-Profiteering Authority (originally NAA — National Anti-Profiteering Authority, now Appellate Tribunal per Explanation 2 inserted by Finance Act 2024). The sunset clause means anti-profiteering will eventually end — Govt has set a cutoff date for new applications.
§171(3A) — PENALTY FOR PROFITEERING
(Inserted by Finance Act 2019)
If Authority finds profiteering → person liable to penalty = 10% of profiteered amount

PROVISO: No penalty if profiteered amount deposited within 30 days of order
📝 Even if you're found guilty of profiteering — if you pay the entire profiteered amount within 30 days of the order — NO penalty. This creates a strong incentive to comply immediately with the order.
💡 Hotel found to have profiteered ₹1Cr by not reducing rates after GST reduction. Authority order: pay ₹1Cr to consumer welfare fund. If hotel pays within 30 days → no 10% penalty. If delayed beyond 30 days → additional ₹10L penalty payable.
EXPLANATION 1 — DEFINITION OF "PROFITEERED"
Amount determined on account of:
NOT passing benefit of reduction in rate of tax, OR
NOT passing benefit of ITC
to recipient by way of commensurate reduction in price
EXPLANATION 2 (Finance Act 2024)
"Authority" shall include "Appellate Tribunal" (GSTAT)
📝 After NAA was abolished, GSTAT has been designated as the authority. This means anti-profiteering cases will now be before GSTAT — same body that hears GST appeals.
🔍 GP Singh: Anti-profiteering provisions must be strictly construed as they impose penal consequences. "Profiteered" is a defined term — only what falls within Explanation 1 constitutes profiteering. The Authority cannot expand the definition. The methodology for computing "profiteered amount" must be consistent and fair — arbitrary computation is challengeable as ultra vires.
📋 Corresponding Rules: Rules 122–137 CGST Rules, 2017 (Pages 297–303) | National Anti-Profiteering Authority — now merged with GSTAT u/s 171 Explanation 2
§172 — Removal of Difficulties | §173 — Amendment of Finance Act 1994 | §174 — Repeal and Saving
🔚 §§172–174 — Transitional, Repeal and Saving Provisions
§172 — REMOVAL OF DIFFICULTIES
Govt + Council recommendation → general or special order in Official Gazette to remove difficulties in implementing provisions
Condition: Order must be consistent with provisions of Act
Time Limit: Within FIVE YEARS of commencement (amended from 3 years by Finance Act 2020)
📝 When Parliament enacts a law, unexpected practical difficulties arise. §172 allows quick correction by government order — without going through full parliamentary amendment. But only within 5 years. Orders under §172 must be placed before Parliament.
§173 — SERVICE TAX CHAPTER OMITTED
Chapter V of Finance Act, 1994 — OMITTED — i.e., Service Tax as a separate levy is abolished
Save as otherwise provided in this Act
📝 Service tax (the predecessor to GST for services) was levied under Chapter V of Finance Act 1994. GST replaced it. §173 formally killed service tax. But the saving clause means pending service tax cases, liabilities, recoveries continue under old law.
§174 — REPEAL OF FIVE LAWS
Following Acts REPEALED on GST commencement:
1. Central Excise Act, 1944 (except goods in Entry 84, Union List)
2. Medicinal & Toilet Preparations (Excise Duties) Act, 1955
3. Additional Duties of Excise (Goods of Special Importance) Act, 1957
4. Additional Duties of Excise (Textiles and Textile Articles) Act, 1978
5. Central Excise Tariff Act, 1985
§174(2) — SAVINGS PROVISIONS
Despite repeal:
(a) Nothing already not in force is revived
(b) Previous operation of repealed Acts protected
(c) All rights/privileges/obligations/liabilities under repealed Acts protected
(d) All dues, penalties, interest under repealed Acts can still be demanded
(e) All investigations/proceedings/adjudications can continue
(f) All appeals/reviews under repealed Acts continue
IMPORTANT EXCEPTION: Tax exemption granted as incentive against investment through notification → NOT continued if notification rescinded on/before appointed day
📝 Legacy service tax / central excise cases from pre-GST era continue under old law. You can still face demands, file appeals, get refunds under those old laws — even though the laws themselves are repealed. The repeal didn't wipe out the past.
🔍 GP Singh: "§6 General Clauses Act saves ongoing rights and proceedings upon repeal. §174(3) expressly saves §6 GCA application. This means all rights and liabilities under repealed Acts are fully protected. Any demand for pre-GST period must be assessed under pre-GST law — not CGST Act."
📋 §174 read with §6 General Clauses Act, 1897 | Transition period proceedings: service tax/excise matters — continue under Finance Act 1994 / Central Excise Act as saved law
SCHEDULE I [See §7] — Activities Treated as Supply Even Without Consideration
⚠️ Schedule I — Deemed Supply Without Consideration
These 4 situations ARE supply even if no money changes hands
📝 Normally GST applies only when there is "consideration" (payment). But Schedule I lists 4 exceptions where even free transactions are taxable. Legislature's rationale: these transactions could be used to evade GST by claiming "no payment." So they are deemed supply regardless.
ParaActivity (Supply Without Consideration)Key ConditionPlain EnglishGST TreatmentLitigation Point
Para 1 Permanent transfer or disposal of business assets ITC must have been availed on such assets If you claimed ITC on a machine and later gift it or throw it away permanently → taxable. Supply of goods → taxable → GST on market value Key: Was ITC actually availed? If no ITC availed (ineligible asset) → Para 1 does NOT trigger. Also: "permanent" disposal — temporary removal is not covered.
Para 2 Supply between related persons OR between distinct persons u/s 25 — in course or furtherance of business Proviso: Gifts not exceeding ₹50,000 in value in a FY by employer to employee — NOT supply Free supplies between group companies (head office / branch office / subsidiaries) → taxable. Gifts by employer to employee up to ₹50,000 per year → free of GST. Supply → taxable → open market value (OMV) as value Very High — Head office-branch transactions; holding-subsidiary supplies; employee perquisites exceeding ₹50,000. Challenge: prove it is NOT in "course or furtherance of business."
Para 3 (a) Principal to agent where agent undertakes to supply goods on behalf of principal
(b) Agent to principal where agent undertakes to receive goods on behalf of principal		 Principal-agent relationship must exist AND agent must supply/receive goods on behalf of principal Commission agents who receive/supply goods in their own name for principal → principal's sending/receiving treated as supply. Supply → GST applies on goods at time of transfer to/from agent High — Del credere agents, C&F agents. If agent acts in own name → Para 3 applies. If agent merely facilitates → service supply, not goods supply.
Para 4 Import of services by a person from a related person OR from any of his other establishments outside India — in course or furtherance of business Note: "person" (not "taxable person") — amended 2019. Covers even persons not otherwise taxable in India Indian company receives free services from its foreign parent (legal, IT, management) → taxable in India under RCM even though no invoice/payment. Supply → taxable as import of services → RCM applicable → recipient must self-assess and pay GST Very High — Intra-group service imports; cost-sharing arrangements; head office in foreign country providing services to Indian branch.
🔍 GP Singh: "Deeming provisions in tax law must be strictly construed — they are not to be extended beyond their express terms. Schedule I must be read strictly. 'Related persons' and 'distinct persons u/s 25' have specific statutory definitions — one cannot apply Schedule I to relationships not falling within those definitions." — Read §2(84) for "related persons" definition very carefully before applying Schedule I Para 2.
SCHEDULE II [See §7] — Activities / Transactions to be Treated as Supply of Goods or Supply of Services
📋 Schedule II — Goods vs Services Classification
When an activity could be either — Schedule II decides
📝 Some transactions could be argued as either goods or services — which matters because rates differ. Schedule II settles the argument. It mandatorily classifies certain activities. Once classified, the correct GST rate applies.
ParaActivityClassificationKey Sub-TypesLitigation Relevance
Para 1 Transfer Depends on sub-clause (a) Transfer of TITLE → goods
(b) Transfer of RIGHT without title / undivided share → services
(c) Agreement with deferred title (hire purchase/conditional sale) → goods		 High — Hire purchase: goods supply (ITC on purchase, GST on EMI). Financial lease: goods supply. Operating lease: services. Incorrect classification → wrong rate, ITC mismatch.
Para 2 Land and Building Both = SERVICES (a) Lease/tenancy/easement/licence to occupy LAND → services
(b) Lease/letting of BUILDING (commercial/industrial/residential) for business/commerce → services		 Very High — Commercial rental disputes, resi-commercial mixed use, short-term vs long-term rentals, licence vs lease characterisation.
Para 3 Treatment or Process on Another Person's Goods SERVICES Any treatment/process applied to another person's goods High — Job work, processing, testing, finishing work. Distinct from §143 job work procedure — this classifies it as service for rate purposes.
Para 4 Transfer of Business Assets Depends on sub-clause (a) Goods forming part of business assets transferred/disposed → goods
(b) Goods held for business put to private use / made available to any person for non-business use → services
(c) Cessation as taxable person → deemed supply of all business goods immediately before cessation
EXCEPTIONS to (c): Business transferred as going concern, OR business carried on by personal representative (deemed taxable person)		 High — Asset disposal on cessation, private use of business assets, going concern transfer — Schedule III exemption.
Para 5 Supply of Services — Deemed Classification ALL = SERVICES (a) Renting of immovable property
(b) Construction of complex/building/civil structure for sale — UNLESS consideration received AFTER completion certificate or first occupation (whichever earlier) → construction service
(c) Temporary transfer/permitting use of IP rights
(d) Development/design/programming of IT software
(e) Agreeing to refrain from / tolerate an act / situation / to do an act
(f) Transfer of right to use any goods (whether or not for specified period)		 Very High — Para 5(b): JDA taxation — key trigger for GST on under-construction flats. Para 5(e): Non-compete clauses, non-solicitation agreements — all taxable as services. Para 5(f): Equipment hire, film rights, software licensing.
Para 6 Composite Supplies = SERVICES (a) Works contract (§2(119)) → service
(b) Supply of food/beverage (other than alcoholic liquor) as part of any service or in any other manner → service		 Very High — Works contract: 12%/18% rate depending on type. Restaurant/catering service. Outdoor catering vs food in canteen. All disputes on works contract rate flow through Para 6(a).
⚠️ KEY NOTE — Para 5(b) Construction Service
Competent Authority for completion certificate = Government or any authority authorised under any law. Where certificate not required from such authority — then from: (i) Architect registered with Council of Architecture; (ii) Chartered Engineer registered with Institution of Engineers (India); (iii) Licensed surveyor of respective local body/development/planning authority.
"Construction" includes: additions, alterations, replacements, remodelling of any existing civil structure.
💡 Developer sells flat for ₹50L. Consideration: 30% received during construction, 70% received after OC. Construction service applies only to 30% — 70% collected after OC/first occupation → sale of building → Schedule III Para 5 → outside GST. Common demand: Dept taxes entire ₹50L → counter: §/Schedule II Para 5(b) read with Schedule III Para 5.
SCHEDULE III [See §7] — Activities / Transactions Neither Supply of Goods NOR Supply of Services
🚫 Schedule III — OUTSIDE GST ENTIRELY
These activities are NEITHER goods NOR services — zero GST, no ITC, no compliance
📝 Schedule III is the "negative list" of GST. These activities are completely outside the GST net. No tax, no invoice requirement, no ITC reversal needed. But the boundaries of each paragraph are heavily litigated — Dept often tries to bring activities within GST scope.
ParaActivity ExcludedConditions / LimitsPlain EnglishLitigation Hotspot
Para 1 Services by employee to employer in course of or in relation to employment Must be in course of or in relation to employment. Not for independent contractors. Salary paid by employer is not subject to GST. Employment services are outside GST. High — Employer-employee relationship vs. independent contractor. Labour supply contracts. Employee deputation vs. manpower agency supply.
Para 2 Services by court or tribunal established under any law Explanation 1: "Court" includes District Court, High Court, Supreme Court Court fees, tribunal filing fees — not GST. Arbitration tribunals may or may not qualify depending on whether "established under any law." Moderate — Arbitrators (under Arbitration Act — law) vs. private arbitrators; NCLT/NCLAT vs. private dispute bodies.
Para 3 (a) Functions by MPs/MLAs/Panchayat/Municipality members
(b) Duties by persons holding constitutional posts in that capacity
(c) Duties as Chairperson/Member/Director of Govt-established body — who is not deemed employee		 Must be in their official capacity performing assigned functions/duties Politicians, constitutional office holders performing their functions — not supply. Low
Para 4 Services of funeral, burial, crematorium or mortuary including transportation of the deceased Must be for the deceased — not general transport or commercial services Death services — fully outside GST. Includes transport of body. Low
Para 5 Sale of land AND (subject to Sch II Para 5(b)) sale of building CONDITION: Sale of building is outside GST ONLY if consideration received AFTER issuance of completion certificate (by competent authority) OR after first occupation — whichever is earlier Completed flat sale — no GST. Under-construction flat sale — GST applies on construction portion (Schedule II Para 5(b)). Very High — Most important para in real estate GST. JDA: developer's share of completed flats — whether Para 5 applies on sale of developer's portion. Land undivided share in apartment sale — Para 5 exclusion or not?
Para 6 Actionable claims other than "specified actionable claims" Specified Actionable Claims (substituted w.e.f. 01.10.2023): Lottery, betting, gambling now TAXABLE as "specified actionable claims." Online gaming (real money games) now covered. Regular actionable claims (promissory notes, bills of exchange, insurance claims) — outside GST. But lottery/betting/gambling/online gaming — taxable at 28%. Very High — Online gaming platforms; fantasy sports; casinos. Supreme Court reference on online gaming GST. ₹1.12L Cr demand on gaming industry.
Para 7 Supply of goods from non-taxable territory to another non-taxable territory WITHOUT goods entering India Effective retrospectively w.e.f. 01.07.2017 per Finance Act 2023 (clarified). No refund for tax already collected before retrospective notification. Foreign-to-foreign trade where goods don't touch India. High seas sales outside India — outside GST. High — High seas sales disputes, ship-to-ship transfers, offshore supply contracts.
Para 8(a) Supply of warehoused goods before clearance for home consumption Explanation 2: "Warehoused goods" has same meaning as Customs Act 1962 Goods in customs bonded warehouse can be sold multiple times — no GST until they clear customs for domestic consumption. High — Bonded warehouse transactions; transfer of title before customs clearance.
Para 8(aa) (Finance Act 2025) Supply of goods warehoused in SEZ / FTWZ to any person before clearance for exports OR to Domestic Tariff Area Retrospective w.e.f. 01.07.2017 per Finance Act 2025. Explanation 3: "SEZ", "FTWZ", "DTA" — meanings as per SEZ Act 2005. Goods in SEZ/FTWZ warehouses can be sold/transferred — no GST until they move to DTA for consumption. High — Many pending disputes on SEZ/FTWZ supply of goods; retrospective provision settles pre-existing demands.
Para 9 (Finance Act 2024 w.e.f. 01.11.2024) Activity of apportionment of co-insurance premium by lead insurer to co-insurer in jointly supplied insurance services Condition: Lead insurer must pay CGST/SGST/UTGST/IGST on ENTIRE amount of premium paid by insured When multiple insurers share a risk, the lead insurer divides premium internally — this internal division is not a separate GST transaction. High — Insurance industry; co-insurance arrangements; pending demands on premium apportionment; retrospective or prospective.
Para 10 (Finance Act 2024) Services by insurer to reinsurer for which ceding commission / reinsurance commission is deducted from reinsurance premium Condition: Reinsurer must pay GST on GROSS reinsurance premium (including ceding/reinsurance commission) Reinsurance commission deducted by the cedant (insurer) from premium paid to reinsurer — not separately taxable if reinsurer pays GST on full gross premium. High — Reinsurance industry; pending demands on ceding commissions; clarificatory but effective from 01.11.2024.
⚠️ CRITICAL NOTE — Para 7 & 8 — NO REFUND CLAUSE
For paras 7 and 8 of Schedule III (retrospective effect from 01.07.2017 per Finance Act 2023/2025): It is clarified that NO REFUND shall be made of all tax which has been collected but which would not have been collected had these provisions been in force at all material times.
📝 Even though Para 7 and Para 8 now say these transactions were NEVER taxable from 01.07.2017 — the government has simultaneously blocked all refunds for GST already collected on these transactions. This is unprecedented — a retroactive exclusion from tax that simultaneously bars refund of tax already paid. Challenged in multiple HCs.
🔍 GP Singh: "Retrospective legislation extinguishing vested rights to refund is constitutionally suspect under Article 14 and 265. The no-refund clause in Schedule III amounts to retaining tax without authority of law for the period before the saving clause was enacted. Multiple HC writ petitions have challenged this as arbitrary and unconstitutional." — Currently under examination before various High Courts.
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal — Chapter XXI + Schedules I, II, III | CGST Act 2017
These statutory arguments and principles are for use in personal hearings. State them confidently with section numbers.
Section 155 CGST Act — Burden of Proof on ITC
Statutory Provision | CGST Act, 2017 | Read with Ecom Gill Coffee Trading: SC
Key Ratio
Where any person claims that he is eligible for input tax credit under this Act, the burden of proving such claim shall lie on such person. However, once the assessee places on record all documentation establishing receipt of goods, payment by banking channel, supplier's GST registration, and reflection in GSTR-2A/2B — the initial burden is discharged. At this stage, the Department must demonstrate specific evidence of fraud or non-payment, not merely allege it.
When to Use — Every ITC denial case. Lead with §155 to acknowledge burden, then systematically demonstrate you have discharged it with documentary proof. Turn the provision into a structured checklist argument.
Section 152(1) CGST Act — Bar on Using Information Without Opportunity of Hearing
Statutory Provision | Amended by Finance Act 2021 w.e.f. 01.01.2022
Key Ratio
No information received under section 150 or section 151 shall be used for the purpose of any proceedings under this Act without giving an opportunity of being heard to the person concerned. This is a mandatory statutory safeguard — not merely a principle of natural justice but an express legislative command. Any SCN, order or assessment that relies on information obtained under §150/151 without first giving a hearing opportunity is vitiated ab initio.
When to Use — When Department uses third-party data (bank statements, electricity records, stock exchange data, income tax information) in an SCN without giving you advance opportunity to explain it. Cite §152(1) as an absolute bar on such use.
Section 160 CGST Act — Limitation on Savings Clause
Statutory Provision | Read with GP Singh on Jurisdictional Defects
Key Ratio
Section 160 saves proceedings from being invalid due to mistakes, defects or omissions only if the proceedings are in substance and effect in conformity with the intent, purposes and requirements of the Act. Section 160 does NOT save jurisdictional defects, violations of natural justice, or defects that go to the root of the authority to act. An SCN issued without Document Identification Number (DIN) as mandated by CBIC Circular 128/47/2019-GST, or an SCN issued beyond limitation, or an order passed without opportunity of hearing — these are not curable under §160 because they are not mere formal defects.
When to Use — When Dept argues a defective SCN/order should be upheld under §160. Counter: §160 saves procedural/formal defects only; specify the exact jurisdictional or natural justice violation and argue it is beyond §160's curative scope.
Section 161 CGST Act — Rectification Cannot Reopen Questions of Law
Statutory Provision | Principles of Error Apparent on Face of Record
Key Ratio
Section 161 enables rectification of errors apparent on the face of record. An error apparent on the face of record is self-evident from the record itself — no external evidence is required to establish it. A wrong legal conclusion drawn from correct facts is NOT an error apparent on face of record. Section 161 cannot be used by either the Assessee or Department as an indirect route to reopen questions of law already decided. It is not an appeal mechanism. Further, where rectification adversely affects the assessee, principles of natural justice (audi alteram partem) must be followed — opportunity of hearing is mandatory.
When to Use — When Department seeks to enhance demand using §161 rectification without giving hearing. Also when assessee wants to challenge attempt by Dept to reopen legal issues settled in assessment order through the backdoor of rectification.
Section 168A CGST Act — Force Majeure Extension (COVID Limitation)
Statutory Provision | Inserted by Taxation and Other Laws Act, 2020 | Retrospective from 31.03.2020
Key Ratio
Section 168A empowers the Government to extend time limits in cases of force majeure. Extension notifications issued under §168A (COVID period) must be interpreted strictly — they extend ONLY what they expressly cover. Vague or unclear extension notifications are to be construed in favour of the assessee. Time-limit extensions for §73/74 demands are subject to specific analysis of each notification's scope and the particular time limit sought to be extended. The word "cannot be completed or complied with due to force majeure" is a qualifying condition — extensions are available only for actions that genuinely could not be completed, not as a blanket benefit for the Department.
When to Use — When Dept claims assessment/demand for FY 2017-18 / 2018-19 was within time due to COVID extensions under §168A notifications. Challenge: specify which extension notification applies, whether assessee's case falls within its exact scope, and whether "could not be completed" test is satisfied.
Section 143 CGST Act — Deemed Supply on Expiry of Job Work Period
Statutory Provision | CGST Act, 2017 | Read with Rules 45-46 CGST Rules
Key Ratio
Section 143(3) and §143(4) create deeming fictions — inputs not returned within 1 year and capital goods not returned within 3 years are deemed to have been supplied by the principal to the job worker on the date of sending out. Deeming fictions must be construed strictly — only what is expressly caught by the fiction falls within it. The proviso to §143(1)(b) regarding additional place of business declaration is a mandatory condition, not a directory provision. Non-compliance means the supply directly from job worker's premises is itself a violation — not merely a procedural lapse.
When to Use — In job work demand cases where Dept claims goods were not returned in time. Counter: produce challan records, ITC-04 filings, proof of return or supply. Also use proviso analysis when Dept demands GST on supply directly from job worker's premises — check if job worker was registered or APOB declared.
Schedule III Para 5 — Sale of Completed Building Outside GST
Statutory Provision | Schedule III r/w Schedule II Para 5(b) | CGST Act, 2017
Key Ratio
Schedule III Para 5 excludes sale of land and sale of building from GST — subject to the caveat in Schedule II Para 5(b). Schedule II Para 5(b) brings within GST only those constructions where consideration is received BEFORE issuance of completion certificate by competent authority OR before first occupation — whichever is earlier. Any consideration received AFTER this trigger point is for sale of building (completed property) — which falls under Schedule III Para 5 and is outside GST. The two provisions must be read together — neither can be applied in isolation.
When to Use — When Dept taxes entire flat sale consideration as construction service. Counter: bifurcate consideration — amount received post-OC/first occupation is sale of completed building → Schedule III → no GST. Demand cannot exceed GST on pre-OC consideration only.
Schedule I Para 2 — Related/Distinct Person Supply — Employer-Employee Gift Exception
Statutory Provision | Schedule I r/w §2(84) Definitions | CGST Act, 2017
Key Ratio
Schedule I Para 2 treats supply between related persons or distinct persons (branches/units u/s 25) as taxable supply even without consideration. But the Proviso protects employer-employee relationships — gifts not exceeding ₹50,000 in value in a financial year by an employer to an employee are expressly excluded. "Related person" has a specific definition in §2(84) — the provision cannot be extended to relationships not covered by that definition. For branch-to-branch or HO-to-branch transactions, distinct person status u/s 25 must be specifically established — mere GST registration at same PAN does not automatically create distinct persons without separate registration for each establishment.
When to Use — In branch transfer GST demands. Establish: (1) Are these actually "distinct persons" u/s 25 with separate GSTIN? (2) Is the transfer in "course or furtherance of business"? (3) For employee gifts — is ₹50,000 annual limit crossed? If any of these fail — Schedule I Para 2 does not apply.
Section 144 CGST Act — Rebuttal of Presumption on Seized Documents
Statutory Provision | CGST Act, 2017 | Read with Indian Evidence Act §4
Key Ratio
Section 144 creates a mandatory presumption — the court SHALL presume the truth of contents of seized/produced/received documents. However, this presumption is expressly rebuttable — "unless the contrary is proved." The burden to rebut is on the assessee/accused. The standard of proof to rebut (in civil adjudication proceedings) is on the balance of probabilities. Rebuttal can be established by: affirmative proof of forgery, tampering, that the signature is not genuine, or that the document was created/planted without the assessee's knowledge. Mere denial without affirmative proof is insufficient.
When to Use — When Dept relies on seized documents (parallel books, cash memos, loose papers, computer data) as basis for demand. Strategy: don't just deny — actively place on record contrary evidence. If signature is denied, request forensic examination. If content is disputed, place positive evidence of actual facts.
Section 159(2) CGST Act — Bar on Publication During Appeal
Statutory Provision | CGST Act, 2017
Key Ratio
No publication under section 159 shall be made in relation to any penalty imposed under the Act until: (a) the time for presenting an appeal to the Appellate Authority under section 107 has expired without an appeal having been presented, OR (b) if an appeal has been presented, until the appeal has been disposed of. This is a mandatory statutory bar — not merely directory. Any publication of assessee's name in connection with a penalty while appeal is pending or appeal period has not yet expired is void and actionable by injunction before the High Court.
When to Use — When Department publicly names assessee in press release, website publication, or circular as a tax evader while assessment/penalty order is under appeal or within appeal period. Approach HC immediately for injunction citing §159(2) as an absolute bar.
CBIC Circulars under Section 168 — Binding on Officers
Statutory Provision | §168 CGST Act | Settled principle of administrative law
Key Ratio
Section 168 empowers the Board to issue orders, instructions and directions to central tax officers, and all such officers shall observe and follow such orders, instructions and directions. CBIC Circulars issued in exercise of this power are binding on departmental officers. An officer who acts contrary to a CBIC Circular is acting in excess of authority. The assessee is entitled to rely on CBIC Circulars even if they are retrospectively modified — legitimate expectation created by a circular cannot be defeated without notice and opportunity of hearing. (Ratan Melting: SC — trade circulars binding on Revenue).
When to Use — When officer's action contradicts CBIC Circular on the same issue. Specifically: DIN requirement (Circular 128/2019-GST), limitation periods for demands, ITC eligibility clarifications, HSN classification clarifications. Cite §168 and the specific circular to argue officer's action is ultra vires.
For professional litigation use | Pawan Soni & Associates, Ichalkaranji | Chapter XXI Miscellaneous + Schedules I, II, III — CGST Act 2017
CGST Rules — Ch.I–III

📜 CHAPTER I — PRELIMINARY
TIER 1 — Chapter I Overview: Preliminary (Rules 1–2)
Rule 1 — Short Title & Commencement
Name of rules + effective date: 22 June 2017
Rule 2 — Definitions
Act, Form, Section, SEZ — and fallback to CGST Act definitions
Rule 1 — Short Title & Commencement
📜 Rule 1 — Short Title & Commencement
CGST Rules, 2017
NAME
These rules shall be called the Central Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017
COMMENCEMENT
Effective from 22nd June, 2017
📝 Rules came into force 22 June 2017 — same day as CGST Act provisions were notified for operation.
📋 Parent Statute: CGST Act, 2017 (12 of 2017) | Rule-making power: Section 164 CGST Act
Rule 2 — Definitions
Term DefinedMeaning Under Rule 2Plain EnglishLitigation Point
"Act" Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017 (12 of 2017) When these Rules say "the Act" — they mean the CGST Act. All rule obligations flow from CGST Act authority; ultra vires challenge requires showing parent Act doesn't authorise the rule.
"Form" A Form appended to these rules GST REG-01, CMP-01 etc. are "Forms" — any reference to a Form means the form at the back of these rules. Rejection of application citing non-prescribed Form format — check if Form is truly "appended" or merely notified separately.
"Section" A section of the Act (i.e., CGST Act) When Rules say "under section 25" — that means section 25 of CGST Act, not any other Act. Cross-Act references (Income Tax Act sections) would need express language. Absence of such language is restrictive.
"Special Economic Zone" Same meaning as in clause (za) of section 2 of the Special Economic Zones Act, 2005 (28 of 2005) SEZ definition borrowed from the SEZ Act itself — identical standard applies to GST Rules. SEZ claims under GST Rules must satisfy SEZ Act definition — a dispute about whether premises qualify as SEZ is governed by SEZ Act, not CGST Rules.
Undefined words If a word is used but not defined in the Rules → it has the meaning given in the CGST Act itself. Fallback to parent Act — Rules and Act must be read together as one integrated code. 🔍 GP Singh: Rules made under a parent statute must be read as part of that statute. A word undefined in Rules but defined in the Act carries the Act's meaning — Nagpur Municipal Corporation v. Thermax Ltd.
💰 CHAPTER II — COMPOSITION LEVY (Rules 3–7)
TIER 1 — Chapter II Overview: Composition Levy (Rules 3–7)
Rule 3 — Intimation for Composition Levy
CMP-01 / CMP-02 filing; provisional & new registrants; ITC-03 reversal; 180-day window
Rule 4 — Effective Date
When composition levy begins — beginning of FY or appointed day
Rule 5 — Conditions & Restrictions
7 mandatory compliance conditions; display obligations; annual continuation
Rule 6 — Validity
Continuation conditions; voluntary / suo moto withdrawal; SCN procedure; ITC-01 on exit
Rule 7 — Rates of Tax
Rate table: manufacturers 0.5%, restaurant 2.5%, others 0.5%, §10(2A) 3%
Rule 3 — Intimation for Composition Levy
💰 Rule 3 — Intimation for Composition Levy
How to opt into Composition Scheme under Section 10
SR(1) — Provisional Registrants
Person with provisional registration under Rule 24 who opts for §10
→ File FORM GST CMP-01 on common portal
→ Prior to appointed day (or within 30 days + extension)
📝 Old-law dealers migrating to GST who want Composition — must file CMP-01 before GST starts or within 30 days.
CAUTION
If CMP-01 filed AFTER appointed day → cannot collect any tax from appointed day; must issue Bill of Supply for all supplies made after that day
SR(2) — Fresh Applicants
Person applying for new registration under Rule 8 may opt for §10
→ Give option in Part B of FORM GST REG-01
→ Treated as intimation
📝 New businesses applying for GST registration — tick the box in REG-01 itself to opt into Composition. No separate form needed.
SR(3) — Existing Registrants opting in
Registered person opting for §10 from a new FY
→ File FORM GST CMP-02 electronically
→ PRIOR to commencement of that FY
→ Furnish FORM GST ITC-03 within 60 days of FY commencement
📝 If you are already registered under normal scheme and want to switch to Composition from next April — file CMP-02 before March 31 and reverse ITC on stock within 60 days.
💡 A trader switches to Composition from FY 2024-25. Files CMP-02 by 31 Mar 2024. Must file ITC-03 reversing ITC on closing stock by 30 May 2024 (60 days from 1 Apr 2024).
SR(3A) — Mid-year option (upto 31 Mar 2018)
