Provisional / newly registered persons could opt for §10 from 1st of following month via CMP-02 filed by 31 Mar 2018
ITC-03 within 180 days
SR(4) — Stock Statement
SR(1) filers must submit details of stock (including from unregistered suppliers) in FORM GST CMP-03 within 90 days of exercising option
📝 Migrating dealers must disclose their opening stock — this helps determine what ITC they had and are now reversing.
SR(5) — One-PAN Rule
Intimation in SR(1) or SR(3) or SR(3A) for any place of business → deemed intimation for ALL places of business on same PAN
📝 Composition is PAN-wide — you cannot opt in for one state and normal scheme for another under same PAN.
💡 Trader has GSTIN in Maharashtra and Gujarat (same PAN). Opts CMP-02 for Maharashtra → automatically opted for Gujarat too. Cannot pay normal GST in Gujarat.
📋 Section 10 CGST Act | TRAN-1 cannot be filed after ITC-03 is furnished
Rule 4 — Effective Date for Composition Levy
📅 Rule 4 — When Does Composition Levy Begin?
SR(3) filers → New FY
Option exercised via CMP-02 (existing registrant switching) → Effective from beginning of the financial year for which intimation filed
💡 CMP-02 filed before 31 Mar 2025 for FY 2025-26 → Composition starts from 1 April 2025.
SR(1) filers → Appointed Day
Provisional registrants migrating (CMP-01) → Effective from the appointed day (1 July 2017)
SR(2) filers → Date of Registration
New applicants opting via REG-01 → Effective from date fixed under Rule 10(2) or (3) (date of liability or date of grant)
📝 New registrant opting Composition gets it from the date GST registration itself becomes effective — not from application date.
Rule 5 — Conditions & Restrictions for Composition Levy
⚠️ Rule 5 — Mandatory Conditions for Composition Scheme
📝 These are ALL conditions that must be satisfied simultaneously. Failure of even ONE condition disqualifies the person from the scheme — making them liable for full GST from the date of default.
ConditionWhat Rule 5(1) RequiresPlain EnglishLitigation Risk if Violated
(a)Neither a casual taxable person nor a non-resident taxable personOnly regular residents can use Composition. Casual/NR dealers excluded.Composition opted by casual dealer = void ab initio. Full tax + penalty.
(b)Goods in stock on appointed day must NOT have been purchased via: inter-State trade, imports, from outside-State branch, or from agent/principal outside State [for SR(1) filers]Opening stock (on GST day) must be local — inter-state purchases disqualify.Department checks VAT records for inter-state purchases in opening stock. If found → Composition disqualified from beginning.
(c)Goods in stock must not have been purchased from unregistered supplier — unless tax paid under §9(4)Stock from unregistered dealers must have RCM paid; otherwise disqualified.High-risk: Many traders ignored RCM on unregistered purchases. Common audit trigger.
(d)Must pay tax under §9(3) and §9(4) (RCM) on inward supplies of goods/servicesEven under Composition — you MUST pay RCM on notified supplies. Composition does not exempt you from RCM.🔍 GP Singh: "Shall" in Rule 5(1)(d) is mandatory. Non-payment of RCM by composition dealer = dual violation: §9 and Rule 5. Full normal tax + interest + penalty.
(e)Was NOT engaged in manufacture of notified goods under §10(2)(e) during the preceding financial yearManufacturers of pan masala, tobacco etc. (notified goods) cannot opt Composition even if they stop manufacturing. Prior year matters.Look-back to previous FY: even if manufacturing stopped before current FY, disqualified.
(f)Must mention words "composition taxable person, not eligible to collect tax on supplies" at TOP of every Bill of SupplyBuyers are warned that ITC is not available to them. Failure to display = wrong representation to buyers.If tax invoice (not Bill of Supply) issued → ITC collected from buyer = fake credit chain. §122 penalty + cancellation risk.
(g)Must display words "composition taxable person" on every notice or signboard at all business premisesPhysical public disclosure at every place of business.Absence of signboard → prima facie evidence of intent to deceive buyers. Supports penalty under §122.
SR(2) — Auto-renewalRegistered person paying under §10 need NOT file fresh intimation every year — continuous as long as conditions satisfiedOne-time opt-in is sufficient — no annual renewal of Composition option required.GST department cannot demand annual CMP-02 filing as condition for continuation. Rule 5(2) protects taxpayer.
Rule 6 — Validity of Composition Levy
⚖️ Rule 6 — When Does Composition Levy Continue / Lapse?
SR(1) — Valid till conditions met
Option remains valid as long as ALL conditions in §10 and Rules are satisfied
SR(2) — Breach triggers full tax
Day he ceases to satisfy any condition → liable for normal §9 tax from that day
→ Issue tax invoice for all subsequent supplies
→ File FORM GST CMP-04 within 7 days of such event
📝 The moment ANY condition is broken — you're out. You must file CMP-04 within 7 days to inform department. Delay creates additional penalty exposure.
💡 Composition dealer crosses turnover threshold on 15 July. Must file CMP-04 by 22 July. From 15 July onwards — issue tax invoices, charge GST, file GSTR-1.
SR(3) — Voluntary Withdrawal
Intends to withdraw → file FORM GST CMP-04 with date of withdrawal on common portal
SR(4)-(5) — SCN Procedure (Proper Officer)
If PO believes person was not eligible or has contravened → Issue SCN in FORM GST CMP-05
→ Reply in CMP-06 within 15 days
→ Order in CMP-07 within 30 days of reply
🔍 GP Singh: Show-cause notice must state all grounds with clarity. Vague SCN in CMP-05 is challengeable — denial of natural justice. Reply opportunity in CMP-06 is a procedural right, not merely directory.
SR(6) — ITC-01 on Exit
On withdrawal (SR(2), SR(3)) or denial order (CMP-07) → electronically furnish FORM GST ITC-01 within 30 days — details of input/semi-finished/finished goods stock
📝 When you exit Composition — you can claim ITC on stock. File ITC-01 within 30 days to avail this credit.
💡 Dealer exits Composition on 1 Oct. Has stock of ₹10L. Files ITC-01 by 31 Oct claiming ITC on that stock. This credit becomes available for set-off against future output tax.
SR(7) — One-PAN Rule on Exit
Withdrawal / denial for any one place → deemed withdrawal for ALL places on same PAN
📋 Section 10 CGST Act | Forms: CMP-04, CMP-05, CMP-06, CMP-07, ITC-01
Rule 7 — Rate of Tax — Composition Levy
Sl.Section Under which OptedCategory of Registered PersonRate of Tax (effective w.e.f. 01.04.2020)Litigation Points
1 §10(1) & (2) Manufacturers (other than manufacturers of notified goods) 0.5% of turnover in State/UT Turnover = taxable + exempt. Manufacturing classification disputed — process not amounting to manufacture = 0.5% applies.
2 §10(1) & (2) Suppliers making supplies under Schedule II Para 6(b) — Restaurant/food service 2.5% of turnover in State/UT Restaurant services under Composition capped at 2.5%. Distinction from entry 3 (other suppliers) crucial.
3 §10(1) & (2) Any other supplier eligible for composition under §10(1) and (2) 0.5% of turnover of taxable supplies of goods and services in State/UT Note: only on taxable turnover (not exempt). Important distinction from entries 1 and 2 where base is total turnover.
4 §10(2A) Persons not eligible under §10(1)/(2) but eligible under §10(2A) — service providers opting composition 3% of turnover of supplies of goods and services in State/UT 🔍 Section 10(2A) was inserted to bring service providers into Composition. Turnover limit: ₹50L. Rate highest among Composition categories.
⚠️ NOTE All Composition rates are applied on STATE/UT-wise turnover only. CGST = half the above rate; SGST = other half. IGST not applicable for Composition dealers (inter-state supply disqualifies from Composition).
📝 CHAPTER III — REGISTRATION (Rules 8–26)
TIER 1 — Chapter III Overview: Registration (Rules 8–26)
Rule 8
Application — REG-01, PAN validation, Aadhaar auth, biometric verification
Rule 9
Verification & approval — 7 days (normal) / 30 days (physical verification) / deemed approval
Rule 9A
Electronic auto-grant via data analysis & risk parameters (w.e.f. 01.11.2025)
Rule 10
REG-06 certificate; GSTIN structure; effective date; retroactive effect if applied within 30 days
Rule 10A
Bank details to be furnished within 30 days of registration or before first GSTR-1
Rule 10B
Aadhaar authentication for revocation, refund, IGST refund on exports
Rule 11
Separate registration for multiple places in same State — conditions; cross-section bar on Composition
Rule 12
TDS (§51) / TCS (§52) registration — REG-07; 3-day grant; cross-territory provision
Rule 13
Non-resident taxable person — REG-09; advance deposit; 5-day advance filing; authorised signatory must be India resident
Rule 14
OIDAR services & online money gaming from outside India — REG-10
Rule 14A
New (w.e.f. 01.11.2025) — Option for taxpayers with monthly output tax liability below ₹2.5L
Rule 15
Extension of casual/NR registration period — REG-11; must pay advance tax
Rule 16
Suo moto registration by officer — REG-12; temporary; 90-day regularisation window
Rule 16A
Temporary Identification Number for non-registerable persons needing to make payments
Rule 17
UIN for UN bodies, embassies, notified persons — REG-13; pan-India applicability
Rule 18
Mandatory display of registration certificate + GSTIN at all places of business
Rule 19
Amendment of registration — REG-14; core vs non-core changes; 15-day timeline; PAN change = new registration
Rule 20
Voluntary cancellation application — REG-16; 30 days; stock details required
Rule 21
Grounds for suo moto cancellation — 9 heads including fake invoices, §171 violation, Rule 86B
Rule 21A
Suspension — auto on cancellation application / PO discretion / data analytics mismatch; REG-31; bars taxable supply
Rule 22
Cancellation procedure — SCN REG-17; reply REG-18; order REG-19/20; drop if returns filed
Rule 23
Revocation — REG-21 within 90 days (+180 days extension); returns must be filed; 30-day compliance window post-revocation
Rule 24
Migration from existing law — provisional registration REG-25; confirmation REG-26; cancellation of non-liable persons
Rule 25
Physical verification of premises — REG-30; before or after grant; 15 working days for report upload
Rule 26
Method of authentication — DSC / EVC; entity-wise signatories; notices to be digitally signed
Rule 8 — Application for Registration
📋 Rule 8 — How to Apply for GST Registration
Section 25 CGST Act
SR(1) — GENERAL APPLICANTS (liable under §25(1) or voluntary under §25(3))
Exceptions: NR taxable person, TDS (§51), TCS (§52), OIDAR/Online Gaming (§14/14A IGST)
📝 Four categories of persons are excluded from this standard application process — they have their own specialised rules (Rules 12, 13, 14).
StepAction RequiredForm / PortalTime Limit / TriggerKey Condition
1Declare PAN + State/UT in Part A of applicationFORM GST REG-01 Part A on common portalBefore applying for registrationISD must make separate application for ISD registration
2PAN validated online by common portal via CBDT database; mobile + email verified via OTPCommon portal → CBDT linkDuring Part A filingPAN must be valid and linked with mobile/email for OTP
3Temporary Reference Number (TRN) generated and communicated to mobile + emailCommon portalOn successful verificationTRN used to submit Part B
4Submit Part B of REG-01 with documents using TRN (electronically signed/EVC)FORM GST REG-01 Part BUsing TRNDocuments as specified in REG-01
4A Aadhaar Authentication [for persons opting Aadhaar auth and not notified under §25(6D)]
Date of submission = date of Aadhaar auth OR 15 days from Part B submission, whichever earlier		 Common Portal While submitting Part B Proviso: If risk-profiled → biometric-based authentication + photograph + document verification at Facilitation Centre. Application complete only after this.
4A (non-Aadhaar) Person not opting Aadhaar → photograph + document verification at Facilitation Centre Facilitation Centre notified by Commissioner On submission Application deemed complete only after successful verification
4B Central Govt may by notification specify States where biometric/physical verification proviso does NOT apply — Per notification Creates State-wise differential treatment
5 Acknowledgement issued electronically in FORM GST REG-02 FORM GST REG-02 On receipt of Part B / 4A completion For casual taxable person — REG-02 only after advance tax deposit
⚔️ WORD WAR — "shall" vs "may" in Rule 8(4A)
ACTUAL WORD: "shall" (authentication)
Aadhaar authentication is mandatory — not optional — for persons who opt for it and are not notified under §25(6D)
IF "may" were used
Authentication would be discretionary; department could not insist; applicant could bypass
VERDICT: "Shall" = Mandatory compliance. Non-compliance = Application NOT complete. Registration NOT granted.
📋 Section 25 CGST Act | REG-01, REG-02 | Section 25(6D): Persons notified for exemption from Aadhaar auth
Rule 9 — Verification & Approval of Application
🔍 Rule 9 — What Happens After You Apply?
Section 25 CGST Act
SR(1) NORMAL TRACK
Documents found in order → approve registration within 7 working days from date of submission
📝 Standard timeline: PO has 7 working days from complete application to grant registration.
💡 Application submitted with all docs on 1 June → registration must be granted by 10 June (7 working days). Failure → deemed approved under SR(5)(a).
SR(1) PROVISO — Physical Verification Track (30 days)
Triggered when:
(a) Failed/opted-out of Aadhaar auth; OR
(aa) Aadhaar done but risk-profiled for physical verification; OR
(b) PO deems physical verification fit (with AC/above approval)
→ Registration within 30 days of submission after physical verification (Rule 25)
📝 Three situations extend timeline from 7 to 30 days — all involving physical verification of business premises.
🔍 GP Singh: "With approval of officer not below rank of AC" for clause (b) is a safeguard condition. PO cannot suo motu extend timeline to 30 days without AC approval. If done without approval → 7-day rule applies; deemed approval may arise.
SR(2) — DEFICIENCY NOTICE
Application deficient in info/documents OR clarification needed
→ Issue FORM GST REG-03 within 7 working days (normal) / 30 days (physical verification track)
→ Applicant to furnish clarification in FORM GST REG-04 within 7 working days of notice
📝 If officer is not satisfied with documents — send REG-03 notice. Applicant gets 7 more days to clarify. "Clarification" includes correction of particulars (except PAN, State, mobile, email).
SR(3) — AFTER SATISFACTORY REPLY
Satisfied with clarification → approve within 7 working days of receipt of clarification
SR(4) — REJECTION
No reply / unsatisfactory reply → may reject with reasons in writing → inform in FORM GST REG-05
🔍 "May" in SR(4) (substituted from "shall" in 2020) = Rejection is now discretionary, not automatic. Before rejection, officer should provide sufficient opportunity. Rejection without reasons = violates natural justice.
SR(5) — DEEMED APPROVAL
If PO fails to act:
(a) Within 7 days (normal track); OR
(b) Within 30 days (physical verification track); OR
(c) Within 7 days of clarification receipt
→ Registration deemed approved
📝 Powerful safeguard for taxpayer: if officer sleeps on the file, registration is automatically granted. You can proceed to access the portal.
💡 If application filed 1 June (normal track) and no action by 10 June → send email to PO + department + file a writ noting deemed approval. GSTIN should be accessible on portal.
📋 Forms: REG-03 (notice), REG-04 (reply), REG-05 (rejection) | Rule 25 (physical verification) | Section 25(6D)
Rule 9A — Electronic Grant of Registration (w.e.f. 01.11.2025)
🤖 Rule 9A — Automated Electronic Registration
Inserted: Notification 18/2025-CT dated 31.10.2025 | w.e.f. 01.11.2025
NOTWITHSTANDING Rule 9
Any person who has applied for registration under Rule 8, Rule 12 or Rule 17 → upon identification on common portal based on data analysis and risk parameters → be granted registration electronically by common portal within 3 working days from date of application
📝 System-driven automatic registration for low-risk applicants — bypasses manual PO processing entirely. Portal itself grants GSTIN within 3 days based on AI/data analysis.
LITIGATION IMPACT
If electronic registration is granted under Rule 9A but later cancellation is sought under Rule 21/22 — the basis is the same risk parameters. Cancellation must show the risk parameters' output changed or new evidence emerged — cannot cancel merely on suspicion.
Rule 10 — Issue of Registration Certificate
📄 Rule 10 — GSTIN & Registration Certificate
FORM GST REG-06
GSTIN Structure
15-character number:
(a) 2 chars — State code
(b) 10 chars — PAN or TAN
(c) 2 chars — Entity code
(d) 1 char — Checksum
💡 27ABCDE1234F1Z5 → 27 = Maharashtra, ABCDE1234F = PAN, 1 = 1st entity in state, Z = entity code, 5 = checksum
SR(2) — RETROSPECTIVE EFFECT
Application submitted within 30 days of becoming liable → Registration effective from date of liability
📝 Applied within 30 days of crossing threshold → ITC available from date of liability. Input tax on purchases between liability date and registration date is not lost.
💡 Turnover crosses ₹40L on 1 July. Application filed 20 July (within 30 days). Registration effective from 1 July — ITC on July purchases recoverable.
SR(3) — LATE APPLICATION
Application submitted AFTER 30 days from liability → Registration effective from date of grant only (Rule 9 timeline)
📝 Late application = no retrospective credit. ITC for the period from liability date to registration grant date = lost permanently.
💡 Turnover crosses threshold 1 July. Application filed 15 August (after 30 days). Registration granted 22 August. No ITC available for July 1 – August 22. Also liable for unregistered period output tax.
SR(4) — Authentication
Registration certificate shall be duly signed or verified through EVC by proper officer
SR(5) — Deemed Approval Certificate
Where registration deemed approved under Rule 9(5) → Registration number communicated; certificate available on portal within 3 days after expiry of Rule 9(5) period
📋 FORM GST REG-06 | Section 25(12) CGST Act | Rule 9(5) deemed approval | Also applies to Rule 9A, Rule 14A grants (inserted 01.11.2025)
Rule 10A — Bank Account Details | Rule 10B — Aadhaar Authentication
Rule 10A — Bank Account Details
OBLIGATION After REG-06 issued and GSTIN assigned → furnish bank account details on common portal
DEADLINE Within 30 days from date of registration grant — OR — before filing first GSTR-1 / using IFF, whichever is EARLIER
EXCEPTION Does NOT apply to persons registered under Rule 12 (TDS/TCS) or Rule 16 (suo moto)
PROVISO For proprietorship: PAN of proprietor must also be linked with Aadhaar
💡 Registration granted 1 June. First GSTR-1 due 11 July. Bank details must be filed by 1 July (30 days) OR before filing GSTR-1 — whichever comes first = 1 July.
📋 Non-compliance → suspension under Rule 21A(2A) possible | Violation of Rule 10A is ground for cancellation under Rule 21(d)
Rule 10B — Aadhaar Authentication for Registered Person
WHO MUST AUTHENTICATE
Registered person (other than §25(6D) notified) must authenticate Aadhaar of:
• Individual: proprietor
• Partnership: any partner
• HUF: Karta
• Company: MD or any whole-time Director
• Association/Society: any member of managing committee
• Trust: any trustee
• + Authorised signatory (in all cases)
PURPOSES (Table)
1. Application for revocation of cancellation (REG-21, Rule 23)
2. Refund application (RFD-01, Rule 89)
3. IGST refund on goods exported out of India (Rule 96)
📝 Aadhaar authentication is a PREREQUISITE for these three purposes — not optional. Without it, the refund/revocation application cannot be filed.
IF NO AADHAAR ASSIGNED Submit: (a) Enrolment ID slip + (b) Bank passbook with photo / Voter ID / Passport / DL — and authenticate within 30 days of Aadhaar allotment
Rule 11 — Separate Registration for Multiple Places of Business
🏢 Rule 11 — Multiple GSTINs in Same State/UT
Section 25(2) CGST Act
CONDITIONS for separate registration (all must be met):
(a) Person has MORE than one place of business as defined in §2(85)
📝 Need actual multiple distinct business locations — single large premises with different floors does not qualify.
(b) Composition BAR If ANY one place of business pays under §9 (normal) → NO other place can pay under §10 (Composition)
📝 You cannot mix normal and Composition in different GSTINs of the same person within the State.
🔍 Explanation: If any separately registered place becomes ineligible for §10 → ALL other registered places of that person become ineligible too. The ineligibility is contagious across the PAN.
(c) Inter-unit supplies taxable Supply of goods/services BETWEEN separately registered places of same person = taxable supply → issue tax invoice or Bill of Supply
💡 Factory (GSTIN-1) transfers goods to its own showroom (GSTIN-2) in same state → GST applicable on such transfer. Forms a taxable supply between two registered units of same PAN.
📋 Section 25(2) | §2(85) definition of "place of business" | Rule 9 & 10 apply mutatis mutandis to these applications
Rule 12 — TDS/TCS Registration | Rule 13 — Non-Resident Taxable Person
Rule 12 — TDS (§51) / TCS (§52) Registration
AspectProvision
FormFORM GST REG-07 (apply electronically on common portal)
TimelineGrant within 3 working days after due verification — FORM GST REG-06
Cross-territory (SR 1A)Person deducting/collecting in a State where they have NO physical presence → mention that State in PART A of REG-07; principal place State in PART B
CancellationIf no longer liable → PO may cancel REG-06 → communicated in FORM GST REG-08. Procedure of Rule 22 applies.
💡 Central Government Ministry in Delhi deducting TDS on contracts for work done in Maharashtra → REG-07 PART A: Maharashtra, PART B: Delhi. Gets Maharashtra GSTIN for TDS purposes.
Rule 13 — Non-Resident Taxable Person (NRTP)
AspectProvision
FormFORM GST REG-09 (with attested copy of valid passport)
TimingApplication at least 5 days PRIOR to commencement of business
Foreign entityAlong with tax identification number / unique number of country of origin / PAN if available
Advance DepositTRN generated for advance tax deposit under §27; REG-02 acknowledgement only AFTER advance deposit in electronic cash ledger
SignatoryApplication signed/EVC by authorised signatory who is an Indian resident with valid PAN
VerificationRule 9 & 10 apply mutatis mutandis
📝 NRTP = Foreign person making taxable supplies in India temporarily (e.g., foreign artiste performing in India, foreign company at trade fair). Must apply 5 days before starting AND pay advance tax.
Rule 14 — OIDAR / Online Money Gaming | Rule 14A — Low Output Tax Option
Rule 14 — OIDAR & Online Money Gaming (Outside India)
Online information and database access/retrieval (OIDAR) services from outside India to non-taxable online recipients in India — OR — Online money gaming from outside India to persons in India
→ Apply in FORM GST REG-10 on common portal
Registration granted subject to conditions, restrictions and officer as notified by Central Government on Council recommendations
📝 Foreign digital service providers (Netflix, gaming platforms) must register in India for GST. REG-10 is their special form.
📋 Section 14 IGST Act (OIDAR) | Section 14A IGST Act (Online Money Gaming) — inserted w.e.f. 01.10.2023
Rule 14A — Option for Low Output Tax Taxpayers
Inserted: Notification 18/2025-CT | w.e.f. 01.11.2025
ELIGIBILITY Monthly output tax liability on B2B supplies (CGST+SGST/IGST+Cess) does NOT exceed ₹2,50,000/month
EXCLUSIONS (a) Persons not opting Aadhaar auth (§25(6D) notified excluded); (b) Cannot have multiple registrations in same State under this rule against same PAN (notwithstanding Rule 11)
GRANT Upon successful Aadhaar auth → registered electronically within 3 working days
WITHDRAWAL File in FORM GST REG-32 on common portal
Pre-conditions for filing withdrawal: returns for 3 months (if before 1 April 2026) / 1 tax period (on or after 1 April 2026) + all returns from effective date
Cannot withdraw if cancellation proceedings initiated
📝 New simpler registration pathway for small B2B suppliers. Low paperwork. Electronic grant in 3 days. Designed to reduce compliance burden.
Rules 15–18 — Extension | Suo Moto | TIN | UIN | Display
RuleSubjectKey ProvisionFormLitigation Point
R.15 Extension — Casual / NRTP Before end of validity → apply for extension via REG-11. Acknowledged only on payment of additional advance tax under §27(2). FORM GST REG-11 No advance tax payment = no acknowledgement = no extension. Extension not automatic.
R.16 id="r16" Suo Moto Registration by Officer During survey/enquiry/inspection → PO finds unregistered liable person → grant temporary registration by order in REG-12; effective from date of order. Within 90 days → person must apply under Rule 8 / Rule 12. (If appeal pending → 30 days from appellate order upholding liability). Rule 9/10 apply on regularisation; GSTIN effective from date of suo moto order. FORM GST REG-12 🔍 Suo moto registration does NOT mean acceptance of liability — it is a provisional measure. Taxpayer can appeal the suo moto order while simultaneously applying under Rule 8 within 90 days. GSTIN dates back to order date — creating retrospective liability if upheld.
R.16A Temporary Identification Number Person NOT liable for registration but required to make any payment under the Act → PO may grant Temporary ID Number via Part B of REG-12. Part B of REG-12 E.g., person making payment of penalty/demand under §74 without being registered. Allows payment without obtaining full GSTIN.
R.17 Unique Identity Number (UIN) Persons under §25(9): UN bodies, multilateral financial institutions, embassies etc. → apply in REG-13. UIN is pan-India applicable (SR 1A). Grant in REG-06 within 3 working days. External Affairs Ministry can recommend. FORM GST REG-13 UIN entitles holder to refund of GST paid on purchases/inward supplies. Non-registration = cannot claim refund. Diplomatic missions frequently miss this.
R.18 Display of Registration Certificate & GSTIN SR(1): Display registration certificate at PROMINENT location at principal + every additional place of business. SR(2): Display GSTIN on name board at ENTRY of principal + every additional place of business. — Non-display = §122 penalty exposure. During inspection/survey — non-display treated as evasion indicator. Penalty + basis for further scrutiny.
Rule 19 — Amendment of Registration
✏️ Rule 19 — How to Amend Your Registration
Section 28 CGST Act
TRIGGER Any change in particulars of REG-01 / REG-07 / REG-09 / REG-10 / CMP-02 intimation / REG-13 → submit application in FORM GST REG-14 within 15 days of such change
CORE CHANGES (PO approval needed — 15 working days)
(i) Legal name of business
(ii) Principal / additional place of business address
(iii) Addition / deletion / retirement of partners / directors / trustees / CEO
→ PO approves within 15 working days in FORM GST REG-15
→ Effective from date of occurrence of event
📝 Core changes are retroactive — amendment takes effect from the actual day the change happened, not from when PO approved it.
🔍 GP Singh: "Shall take effect from the date of the occurrence of the event" — mandatory provision. PO cannot make a core amendment effective from a later date. Any order doing so is liable to be set aside.
AUTOMATIC AMENDMENTS
Changes other than core changes → certificate of registration stands amended upon submission of REG-14 on common portal — no PO action needed
📝 Minor changes (e.g., email address change, additional place of business addition) are self-operative — portal updates automatically.
PAN CHANGE = New Registration
Change in business constitution resulting in change of PAN → must apply for FRESH registration in REG-01
(Not an amendment — it is a new person in law)
💡 Proprietorship converts to partnership = new PAN = new GST registration required. Existing GSTIN is cancelled; input credit must be transferred via ITC-02.
PAN-WIDE NAME CHANGE
Change in legal name (sub-clause i) and change in partner/director (sub-clause iii) in any State → applicable to ALL registrations on same PAN across all States
SR(1A) No amendment shall be effective from a date EARLIER than the date of submission of REG-14 — EXCEPT with the order of the Commissioner with reasons in writing. Retroactive amendment requires Commissioner-level order.
📋 Section 28 CGST Act | Forms: REG-14 (application), REG-15 (order), REG-03/04 (notice/reply), REG-05 (rejection)
Rule 20 — Cancellation Application | Rule 21 — Grounds for Cancellation
Rule 20 — Voluntary Cancellation Application
WHO Registered person (other than Rule 12 / Rule 17 registered) seeking cancellation under §29(1)
HOW File FORM GST REG-16 electronically within 30 days of occurrence of event warranting cancellation
WHAT TO INCLUDE Details of: inputs held in stock / inputs in semi-finished or finished goods in stock / capital goods in stock on cancellation date; liability thereon; payment details against such liability; relevant supporting documents
📝 When you close business or fall below threshold — apply for cancellation within 30 days. Show all stock details and confirm tax liability has been discharged.
💡 Manufacturer closes factory. Holds ₹5L closing stock. Files REG-16 within 30 days. Must show ITC reversal on closing stock u/r 44(6) in the application.
Rule 21 — Suo Moto Cancellation Grounds
ClauseGround for Cancellation
(a)Does not conduct business from declared place of business
(b)Issues invoice/bill without supply of goods/services — fake invoicing
(c)Violates §171 (anti-profiteering) or its rules
(d)Violates Rule 10A (bank account furnishing)
(e)Avails ITC in violation of §16
(f)Outward supplies in GSTR-1 exceed outward supplies in valid return (GSTR-3B) for one or more tax periods
(g)Violates Rule 86B (restriction on ITC utilisation)
(ga)Violates third or fourth proviso to Rule 23(1) (returns filing conditions for revocation)
(h)Monthly filer: has not furnished returns for continuous 6 months
(i)Quarterly filer: has not furnished returns for continuous 2 tax periods
🔍 Rule 21 grounds are exhaustive for suo moto cancellation — PO cannot go outside this list. However, §29 itself has additional grounds. "Is liable to be cancelled" language = PO must still follow SCN procedure under Rule 22 before cancellation (except suspension first).
Rule 21A — Suspension of Registration
⏸️ Rule 21A — Suspension: The Interim Step Before Cancellation
📝 Suspension is like being put "on hold" — not yet cancelled, but cannot issue tax invoices or charge GST. Introduced to allow department to investigate before finally cancelling. Three triggers for suspension.
SR(1) — VOLUNTARY CANCELLATION APPLICATION
When registered person files cancellation application under Rule 20 → registration automatically suspended from date of application OR date from which cancellation sought, whichever is LATER
📝 Filing cancellation application itself suspends registration — no separate order needed.
SR(2) — PO DISCRETIONARY SUSPENSION
PO has reasons to believe registration is liable to be cancelled under §29 or Rule 21 → may suspend with effect from date determined by him pending Rule 22 cancellation proceedings
🔍 GP Singh: "Reasons to believe" is a subjective satisfaction but must be based on objective materials. Mere suspicion or generalized allegation is not sufficient. Suspension challenged on this ground often succeeds in High Court writs.
SR(2A) — DATA ANALYTICS TRIGGER
Comparison of GSTR-3B with GSTR-1 or inward supplies derived from suppliers' GSTR-1 shows significant differences/anomalies → OR Rule 10A contravention → Registration suspended; intimated in FORM GST REG-31
→ Must explain within 30 days why registration should not be cancelled
📝 System-driven suspension — common in practice. Mismatch between GSTR-1 and 3B detected by AI triggers automatic suspension + show-cause notice via REG-31.
💡 Dealer's GSTR-1 shows ₹50L supplies but GSTR-3B shows ₹30L. System flags ₹20L gap. REG-31 issued. Dealer must explain gap within 30 days or face cancellation.
Consequences of Suspension
SR(3) Cannot make any taxable supply during suspension → cannot issue tax invoice → cannot charge GST
[Explanation: "shall not make taxable supply" = shall not issue tax invoice]
💡 Suspended dealer who issues tax invoices during suspension → fake invoice charge → §122 penalty. Criminal liability cannot be ruled out.
SR(3A) No refund shall be granted under §54 during period of suspension
📝 Pending refund claims get stuck during suspension — even if perfectly legitimate. Must resolve suspension first.
SR(4) Suspension automatically revoked upon completion of Rule 22 cancellation proceedings (revocation effective from date suspension began)
PROVISOS to SR(4)
• SR(2) suspension (non-return filing under §29(2)(b)(c)) → deemed revoked on furnishing all pending returns
• SR(2) suspension (Rule 10A) → deemed revoked on compliance with Rule 10A
• PO may revoke anytime during pendency if he deems fit
📋 FORM GST REG-31 (intimation under SR 2A) | §29(2) CGST Act | Rule 22 (cancellation procedure) | SR(5) — §31(3)(a) and §40 apply to supplies during suspension period post-revocation
Rule 22 — Cancellation of Registration (Procedure)
⚖️ Rule 22 — Cancellation Procedure
Section 29 CGST Act
SR(1) — SCN
PO has reasons to believe registration is liable to be cancelled → issue SCN in FORM GST REG-17 within 7 working days
🔍 "Reasons to believe" — objective material must exist. Vague or templated SCN without specific reasons = challengeable. Many HCs have quashed such SCNs.
SR(2) — Reply
Reply to SCN in FORM GST REG-18 within period specified in SCN (7 working days from service)
SR(3) — Cancellation Order
Where application filed under Rule 20 OR reply to SCN → PO issues order in FORM GST REG-19 within 30 days from Rule 20 application / reply
Effective from date to be determined by PO
Directs arrear tax/interest/penalty payment under §29(5)
📝 Important: PO chooses the effective date. Can cancel retrospectively — creating large tax demands for the period of cancelled registration.
SR(4) — DROP PROCEEDINGS
Reply satisfactory → PO drops proceedings in FORM GST REG-20
Special proviso: If person furnishes ALL pending returns + pays ALL tax dues + interest + late fee (for non-return filing cases) → PO shall drop proceedings and pass REG-20
🔍 "Shall drop" in proviso is mandatory on PO once returns filed and dues paid. PO cannot insist on hearing or impose additional conditions. Any order not dropping proceedings after full compliance = illegal.
📋 Forms: REG-17 (SCN), REG-18 (reply), REG-19 (cancellation order), REG-20 (drop order) | Rule 21A (suspension applies during proceedings) | §29(5) arrear dues
Rule 23 — Revocation of Cancellation of Registration
🔄 Rule 23 — Getting Registration Back
Section 30 CGST Act
📝 Revocation = asking the department to reverse the cancellation order and restore your registration. Only available when PO cancelled on his own motion (not when you cancelled voluntarily).
ELIGIBILITY
Only persons whose registration cancelled by PO on his own motion → May apply (subject to Rule 10B Aadhaar auth)
TIME LIMIT
Apply in FORM GST REG-21 within 90 days from date of service of cancellation order
EXTENSION Commissioner or authorised officer (not below Addl. Commissioner / Jt. Commissioner) may extend by up to 180 DAYS for sufficient cause shown
💡 Registration cancelled 1 Jan. REG-21 must be filed by 1 April (90 days). If genuine cause (hospitalisation, natural calamity) — can get extension up to 30 September (additional 180 days).
PRE-CONDITIONS (Non-return filers)
No application for revocation if cancelled for failure to furnish returns — UNLESS:
• All pending returns furnished
• Tax due per returns paid + interest + penalty + late fee
📝 File all your pending returns first, pay all dues — only then can you even apply for revocation. No returns = no revocation application allowed.
🔍 This precondition is mandatory. Cannot be waived by PO or Commissioner. HC writs challenging this precondition have generally failed — courts hold it is a reasonable restriction to prevent habitual defaulters.
POST-REVOCATION COMPLIANCE
(i) All returns from date of cancellation order to date of revocation order → file within 30 days of revocation order
(ii) Retrospective cancellation → file all returns from effective cancellation date to revocation within 30 days
📋 FORM REG-21 (application), REG-22 (revocation order), REG-23 (show cause for rejection), REG-24 (reply), REG-05 (rejection) | Section 30 CGST Act | Rule 10B (Aadhaar auth prerequisite)
Rule 24 — Migration | Rule 25 — Physical Verification | Rule 26 — Authentication
Rules 24, 25, 26 — Migration / Verification / Authentication Framework
RuleSubjectKey ProvisionFormLitigation / Practical Point
R.24(1) Provisional Registration — Migration Every person registered under existing law (VAT/Service Tax/Central Excise) with PAN → enrol on common portal → granted provisional registration → REG-25 certificate with GSTIN REG-25 (provisional cert) Single PAN = single provisional registration only. Centrally-registered Service Tax assessees: one provisional in one State only.
R.24(2) Confirmation of Migration Submit information in REG-26 (electronically, with EVC) within 3 months or extended period. If correct and complete → get REG-06. If incorrect/incomplete → SCN in REG-27 → cancellation order REG-28. REG-27 may be withdrawn via REG-20 if no cause exists. REG-26, REG-27, REG-28 Failure to submit REG-26 in time = cancellation of provisional registration. Late filing possible with Commissioner extension.
R.24(3A) Deemed Migration Registration Certificate not made available within 15 days of REG-26 submission AND no REG-27 notice issued → registration deemed granted; certificate available on portal. — Analogous to deemed approval under Rule 9(5). Protects taxpayer from bureaucratic delay.
R.24(4) Cancellation by Non-Liable Persons Persons not liable to register under GST may apply for cancellation of provisional registration in REG-29 by 31 March 2018. REG-29 Mostly historical now. Composition dealers under existing law who crossed threshold post-GST.
R.25(1) Physical Verification — Post-Grant PO satisfied physical verification needed after grant → get verification done → upload verification report + documents + photographs in FORM GST REG-30 within 15 working days of verification FORM GST REG-30 🔍 Post-grant verification is additional scrutiny — not automatically triggered. Must have specific reasons. Verification report in REG-30 is part of record and can be challenged if adverse findings based on incorrect observation.
R.25(2) Physical Verification — Pre-Grant Physical verification required before grant (proviso to Rule 9(1) cases) → upload REG-30 at least 5 working days BEFORE completion of 30-day period in proviso to Rule 9(1) FORM GST REG-30 Verification report must be on record before 30-day deadline expires. If uploaded late → application moves to deemed approval territory.
R.26(1) Method of Authentication — Documents All applications, replies, returns, appeals etc. submitted electronically with DSC (Digital Signature Certificate) or through Electronic Verification Code (EVC) or other Board-notified signature/verification mode — Documents without DSC/EVC = not validly submitted. Department's notice without DSC/EVC = similarly challengeable (though often courts take a pragmatic view).
R.26(2) Entity-wise Signing Authority Individual: self / authorised person / guardian (if incapacitated). HUF: Karta / authorised signatory. Company: CEO / authorised signatory. Government: authorised officer. Firm: any partner (non-minor) / authorised signatory. Association/Trust: member / trustee / authorised signatory. — 🔍 Returns/replies signed by unauthorised person = invalid. Challenge any adverse order if it was preceded by notice not addressed to proper signatory or if reply was filed by unauthorised person and not accepted.
R.26(3) Authentication of Notices / Certificates / Orders All notices, certificates and orders under this Chapter to be issued electronically by PO or authorised officer through DSC or E-signature (under IT Act 2000) — Notices not properly authenticated = challengeable. Unsigned or non-DSC/E-signature notices from department = not valid notices. Strong ground for quashing before HC under Article 226.
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal — CGST Rules Chapters I, II & III (Rules 1–26)
These statutory principles and interpretive points are for use in personal hearings, appellate proceedings and writ petitions. State them confidently with rule number and provision.
Rule 9(5) — Deemed Approval of Registration
CGST Rules, 2017 | Section 25 CGST Act | Statutory Right
Key Principle
If the proper officer fails to take any action within 7 working days from date of submission (normal track) or within 30 days (physical verification track) or within 7 working days from receipt of clarification — the application for registration shall be deemed to have been approved. The deemed approval is self-executing and requires no order from the department.
When to Use — When department refuses to grant registration despite complete application and expiry of prescribed time. Also use in litigation where department challenges validity of GSTIN claiming registration was not formally granted.
Rule 10(2) vs Rule 10(3) — Effective Date Battleground
CGST Rules, 2017 | Section 25 CGST Act | Statutory Provision
Key Principle
Where application for registration has been submitted within 30 days of becoming liable to registration, registration is effective from the date on which the person becomes liable to registration. Where application submitted after 30 days — effective date is only the date of grant. This distinction determines ITC eligibility and output tax liability for the intervening period.
When to Use — When department denies ITC on purchases made between liability date and registration date. Cite Rule 10(2) to establish that registration (having been applied within 30 days) dates back to liability date — ITC on that period's purchases is allowable.
Rule 22(4) Proviso — Mandatory Drop of Cancellation Proceedings
CGST Rules, 2017 | Section 29 CGST Act | Mandatory Statutory Obligation
Key Principle / Ratio
Where a person, instead of replying to the show cause notice for cancellation (for non-filing of returns under §29(2)(b) or (c)) furnishes all the pending returns and makes full payment of tax dues along with applicable interest and late fee — the proper officer SHALL drop the proceedings and pass an order in FORM GST REG-20. The use of "shall" makes this a mandatory obligation on the proper officer — not discretionary.
When to Use — When department refuses to drop cancellation proceedings despite full compliance with pending returns and dues. PO has no discretion once this compliance is established. Cite this provision and cite Craies on Statute Law principle that "shall" imposes a mandatory duty.
Rule 3(5) — One-PAN, Composition for All Places
CGST Rules, 2017 | Section 10 CGST Act | Statutory Provision
Key Principle
Any intimation under Rule 3(1) or Rule 3(3) or Rule 3(3A) in respect of any place of business in any State or Union territory shall be deemed to be an intimation in respect of all other places of business registered on the same Permanent Account Number. A person cannot be in Composition for one State and under normal scheme for another State.
When to Use — When department issues notice claiming person opted Composition in one State but continued normal scheme in another State under same PAN. Cite Rule 3(5) — such selective opting is not permissible. Similarly, when taxpayer challenges blanket composition imposition — if they opted for one State, all States are covered.
Rule 21A(3) — Suspension Bars Only Tax Invoice; Does NOT Bar All Business
CGST Rules, 2017 | Section 29 CGST Act | Explanation to Rule 21A(3)
Key Principle
The expression "shall not make any taxable supply" during suspension means that the registered person shall not issue a tax invoice and accordingly shall not charge tax on supplies made by him during the period of suspension. It does NOT mean the person cannot carry on business or make supplies — merely that no tax invoice can be issued and no GST charged.
When to Use — When department issues "shut down business" or "stop all operations" directions during suspension, claiming Rule 21A(3) bars all supply. Counter: Rule 21A(3) Explanation restricts only issuance of tax invoice — person may continue business under Bill of Supply. Also use when buyer's ITC is challenged because supplier was suspended — establish that suspension bars tax invoice, not business.
Rule 8(4A) — "Reasons to Believe" / "Data Analysis and Risk Parameters" — Must Be on Record
CGST Rules, 2017 | Section 25 CGST Act | GP Singh Principle
Key Principle
The proviso to Rule 8(4A) requires that biometric authentication / physical verification is triggered where the person "is identified on the common portal, based on data analysis and risk parameters." Similarly, Rule 9(1)(aa) applies to persons "identified on the common portal, based on data analysis and risk parameters." The data analysis output / risk profiling must be an objective, documentable determination — not a mere subjective assessment by officer. Decisions based on undisclosed data analysis without allowing assessee to rebut the risk profiling finding violate natural justice.
When to Use — When registration is refused or delayed citing "risk parameters" without disclosure of the basis. Challenge via RTI or in writ: demand disclosure of data analysis output. If data analysis is wrong (e.g., system misread PAN) — restore to normal track processing. Also use when cancellation is based on data analytics mismatch under Rule 21A(2A) — system output must be disclosed to taxpayer.
Rule 26(3) — Authentication of Notices / Orders — Mandatory Electronic Signing
CGST Rules, 2017 | IT Act, 2000 | Procedural Requirement
Key Principle
All notices, certificates and orders under the provisions of Chapter III shall be issued electronically by the proper officer or any other officer authorised to issue such notices, certificates or orders, through digital signature certificate or through E-signature under the provisions of the Information Technology Act, 2000 or verified by any other mode of signature or verification as notified by the Board.
When to Use — When departmental notices (registration cancellation, rejection) are issued as unsigned system-generated documents or without DSC/EVC. Challenge that notice without authentication under Rule 26(3) is not a valid notice in law — proceedings vitiated ab initio. High Courts of Gujarat, Rajasthan and Kerala have accepted this argument in registration matters.
Rule 5(2) — Composition: No Annual Renewal Required
CGST Rules, 2017 | Section 10 CGST Act | Statutory Right
Key Principle
The registered person paying tax under Section 10 (composition scheme) may not file a fresh intimation every year and may continue to pay tax under the said section subject to the provisions of the Act and these rules. The composition option once exercised is continuous — there is no requirement of annual renewal via CMP-02.
When to Use — When department raises demand on the basis that taxpayer did not file fresh CMP-02 for subsequent years. Cite Rule 5(2) — continuation is automatic as long as conditions are satisfied. No annual renewal is required by law.
Rule 23 — Revocation Period: 90 Days + 180 Days Extension
CGST Rules, 2017 | Section 30 CGST Act | Statutory Provision (amended w.e.f. 01.10.2023)
Key Principle
Application for revocation shall be submitted within 90 days from the date of service of the cancellation order. This period may, on sufficient cause being shown and for reasons to be recorded in writing, be extended by the Commissioner or officer not below the rank of Additional Commissioner or Joint Commissioner, for a further period not exceeding 180 days.
When to Use — When revocation application filed beyond 90 days but within extended 270-day period, and department rejects on limitation grounds. Cite the extension provision — extension is within competence of Addl. Commissioner / Joint Commissioner. Also cite Bhavesh Ishwarlal Lakhani v. State of Gujarat for principle that revocation remedy must be given fair opportunity.
Rule 19(5) — Deemed Amendment of Registration
CGST Rules, 2017 | Section 28 CGST Act | Statutory Provision
Key Principle
If the proper officer fails to take any action within 15 working days from the date of submission of the amendment application or within 7 working days from the date of receipt of reply to show cause notice — the certificate of registration shall stand amended to the extent applied for and the amended certificate shall be made available to the registered person on the common portal.
When to Use — When department delays processing of REG-14 amendment application beyond 15 working days. Amendment is automatic and self-executing under Rule 19(5) — no further action by PO is required or expected. Person can rely on deemed amendment for all subsequent compliances.
For professional litigation use | Pawan Soni & Associates, Ichalkaranji
CGST Rules — Ch.IV–V (Valuation, ITC)

⚖️ CGST Rules — Chapter IV (Valuation) & Chapter V (ITC)

Rules 27–45 | Source: CGST Rules, 2017 (Bare Law) | CA Pawankumar Soni, Ichalkaranji

Permitted / Valid / Eligible
Prohibited / Blocked / Reversed
Condition / If / Unless
Option / Alternative route
Neutral Reference / Statutory
Special Rule / Exception
📝 Plain English
💡 Example
🔍 GP Singh Interpretation
📋 Rule Cross-Reference
📖 Legal Dictionary
⚔️ Word War
📋 Index of Rules
📘 Chapter IV — Valuation
Rule 27 — Non-Monetary Consideration
Rule 28 — Related / Distinct Persons
Rule 29 — Agent Supply
Rule 30 — Cost-Based Value
Rule 31 — Residual Method
Rules 31A/B/C — Lottery, Gaming, Casino
Rule 32 — Special Supplies
Rule 32A — Kerala Flood Cess
Rule 33 — Pure Agent
Rule 34 — Rate of Exchange
Rule 35 — Tax-Inclusive Value
📗 Chapter V — Input Tax Credit
Rule 36 — Documentary Requirements for ITC
Rules 37 & 37A — ITC Reversal (Non-Payment)
Rule 38 — Banking Company ITC
Rule 39 — ISD Distribution
Rule 40 — Special Circumstances ITC
Rules 41 & 41A — Transfer of Credit (M&A)
Rule 42 — ITC on Inputs/Services (Apportionment)
Rule 43 — ITC on Capital Goods (Apportionment)
Rules 44 & 44A — Reversal on Cancellation/Gold Dore
Rule 45 — Job Work Conditions
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal
📘 CHAPTER IV — DETERMINATION OF VALUE OF SUPPLY (Rules 27–35)
Rule 27 — Value of Supply Where Consideration is Not Wholly in Money
💰 Rule 27 — Non-Monetary Consideration
When supply is for consideration NOT wholly in money — 4-step cascade
📝 If a buyer pays partly in cash and partly by giving something back (exchange, barter) — the value for GST must still be determined. Rule 27 gives 4 steps in order — if step (a) works, stop; if not, move to (b), and so on.
4-Step Cascade — Apply in Sequence (a) → (b) → (c) → (d)
(a)
Open Market Value
of such supply
📝 OMV = what an unrelated buyer would pay at the same time for the same supply. This is the ideal answer.
💡 New phone ₹20,000 + exchange old phone → OMV = ₹24,000 (price without exchange)
(b) IF OMV N/A
Money consideration + Equivalent money value of non-money consideration
(if known at time of supply)
💡 Laptop ₹40,000 + barter of printer worth ₹4,000 (known) → Value = ₹44,000. OMV of laptop unknown → use (b).
(c) IF (a)&(b) N/A
Value of supply of goods/services of like kind and quality
📝 Comparable transaction in the market — find a similar supply made under similar conditions.
(d) LAST RESORT
Money consideration + equivalent money value as per Rule 30 or Rule 31 (in that order)
📝 If all else fails — cost + 10% (Rule 30) or reasonable means (Rule 31).
📋 Section 15(1) CGST Act | Rule 27 CGST Rules, 2017 | Explanation (a) Rule 35 — OMV definition
Rule 28 — Value of Supply Between Distinct / Related Persons (Not Through Agent)
🏢 Rule 28 — Related / Distinct Person Valuation
Applies to: Sec. 25(4)/(5) distinct persons + related persons | Not agent transactions
Sub-rule (1): Cascade for Goods / Services
(a) Open Market Value
(b) IF N/A Like Kind & Quality
(c) IF N/A Rule 30 or Rule 31 in order
TWO CRITICAL PROVISOS to Sub-rule (1)
PROVISO 1 — Goods for Further Supply
IF goods intended for further supply AS SUCH by recipient → value may (at supplier's option) be 90% of price charged by recipient to his non-related customer
📝 HO sends goods to branch for onward sale — branch's sale price is known. Supplier can value transfer at 90% of that sale price instead of OMV.
💡 HO transfers goods to Mumbai branch. Branch sells to retailer at ₹100. HO can declare value as ₹90 (90% of ₹100) instead of computing OMV separately.
PROVISO 2 — Full ITC Available
IF recipient is eligible for full input tax credit → invoice value = deemed OMV
📝 If the receiving entity can claim full ITC (no restriction), whatever the supplier declares on invoice is automatically accepted as the correct value — no further scrutiny needed.
💡 HO charges management fee ₹50,000 to fully-taxable branch. Branch has full ITC. Invoice value ₹50,000 = OMV — valid even if OMV might be different.
🔍 GP Singh: This proviso uses "shall be deemed" — a deeming fiction. Deeming fictions must be strictly construed. Department CANNOT reopen valuation when full ITC proviso applies.
Sub-rule (2) — Corporate Guarantee [Inserted w.e.f. 26.10.2023 | Retrospective w.e.f. 26.10.2023]
SPECIAL RULE
Supply of services by supplier to a related person located in India by way of providing corporate guarantee to banking company / FI on behalf of recipient:

Value = 1% per annum of the guarantee amount offered, OR actual consideration — whichever is HIGHER
📝 Company A gives a corporate guarantee to a bank so that its subsidiary (related person) can get a loan. This is treated as a service. Value = 1% p.a. of guarantee amount minimum. This was inserted retrospectively — creating major retrospective liability issues.
💡 Parent gives ₹10 crore guarantee to bank for subsidiary loan for 3 years. Annual value = ₹10 crore × 1% = ₹10 lakh per year. GST @ 18% = ₹1.8 lakh per year on this deemed supply.
EXCEPTION — Full ITC Proviso
IF recipient eligible for full ITC → invoice value = deemed value of said supply (inserted 10.07.2024, retrospective w.e.f. 26.10.2023)
📋 Sec. 25(4)(5) CGST Act | Sec. 15 CGST Act | Notif. 52/2023-CT dt. 26.10.2023 | Notif. 12/2024-CT dt. 10.07.2024
Rule 29 — Value of Supply of Goods Through Agent
🤝 Rule 29 — Principal to Agent (Goods)
Value between principal and his agent
(a) PRIMARY
Open Market Value of goods supplied
OR AT OPTION
90% of price charged by agent to his customer (unrelated) for like kind & quality
(where goods for further supply)
📝 Agent sells onward → supplier can value the principal-to-agent transfer at 90% of the agent's sale price to his customer.
💡 Agent sells groundnut at ₹5,000/quintal. Independent supplier sells same to agent at ₹4,550/quintal.
Value = ₹4,550 (OMV) OR at option = 90% × ₹5,000 = ₹4,500
(b) IF N/A
Rule 30 → Rule 31
(in that order)
📋 Sec. 15 CGST Act | Compare Rule 28 (distinct/related) | Rule 29 CGST Rules
Rules 30 & 31 — Cost-Based Value & Residual Method
🧮 Rules 30 & 31 — Last Resort Valuation Methods
Rule 30 — Cost Plus 10%
Value = 110% of cost of production / manufacture / acquisition / provision
📝 If you can't find market value or like-kind value — take your own cost and add 10% profit margin. That becomes the taxable value.
💡 Company provides services in-house with no comparable market price. Cost = ₹1,00,000. Rule 30 value = ₹1,10,000.
🔍 GP Singh: "Where" in Rule 30 — this rule applies only where preceding rules fail. Department cannot skip Rules 27–29 and directly apply Rule 30 to challenge a valuation.
Rule 31 — Reasonable Means
Determined using reasonable means consistent with Sec. 15 principles
📝 True last resort — use any reasonable method consistent with the law's overall principles. No fixed formula.
💡 For services only — supplier may opt for Rule 31 directly, skipping Rule 30. This option is not available for goods.
⚔️ WORD WAR: "May opt" (services, Rule 31) vs mandatory cascade (goods) — discretion vs obligation. Services supplier has a choice; goods supplier does not.
Rules 31A / 31B / 31C — Lottery, Online Gaming & Casino
Rule Supply Type Value / Formula Plain English Key Point
31A(2)
w.e.f. 22.09.2025		 Lottery (Organising State) 100/140 of face value of ticket
OR notified price by Organising State — whichever is HIGHER
(earlier: 100/128 for authorised; 100/112 for run by State)		 Back-calculation from ticket price including GST. GST is embedded in face value — extract it using this formula. Rate changed to 100/140 by Notif. 13/2025 w.e.f. 22.09.2025 — supersedes earlier two-tier system.
31A(3) Betting / Gambling / Horse Racing 100% of face value of bet or amount paid to totalisator Entire bet amount = taxable value. No deduction for prize money. Actionable claim — full face value taxed. No input deduction.
31B
w.e.f. 01.10.2023		 Online Gaming (incl. Online Money Gaming) Total amount paid or deposited with supplier by/on behalf of player (including virtual digital assets) Every deposit into the gaming platform = taxable value. Even if player doesn't play. Amounts returned/refunded are NOT deductible from value — even if player doesn't participate in any event.
31C
w.e.f. 01.10.2023		 Actionable claims in Casino Total amount paid for: (i) purchase of tokens/chips/coins, OR (ii) direct participation in events without tokens Buy chips at casino → taxable on chip purchase price. Winnings used to play further without withdrawal are NOT new deposits. Explanation (31B & 31C): Winnings re-used to play without withdrawal = NOT treated as new deposit from player.
📋 Notif. 03/2018-CT (Rule 31A) | Notif. 51/2023-CT (Rules 31B & 31C) | Notif. 13/2025-CT dt. 17.09.2025 (Rule 31A amended)
Rule 32 — Determination of Value for Specific/Special Supplies (At Option of Supplier)
Sub-rule Supply Type Value / Formula Notes / Plain English
32(2)(a) Foreign Currency Exchange (Forex) — Standard Method Value = (Buying rate or Selling rate − RBI Reference Rate) × Total units of currency
Proviso: If RBI rate N/A → 1% of gross INR amount
Proviso 2: If neither currency is INR → 1% of lesser amount converted to INR		 Difference between market rate and RBI reference rate = margin = taxable value. Yearly option lock-in once exercised.
32(2)(b) Forex — Optional Flat Method (i) ≤₹1 lakh: 1% of gross, minimum ₹250
(ii) ₹1–10 lakh: ₹1,000 + 0.5% of gross
(iii) >₹10 lakh: ₹5,500 + 0.1% of gross, maximum ₹60,000		 At supplier's option — simplified slab method. Once chosen for a FY, cannot be withdrawn during that year.
32(3) Air Travel Agent — Ticket Booking Domestic: 5% of basic fare
International: 10% of basic fare		 "Basic fare" = part of airfare on which commission normally paid to agent. Not total ticket price.
32(4) Life Insurance (a) Gross premium − investment/savings component (if intimated to policyholder)
(b) Single premium annuity: 10% of premium
(c) Others: 25% (1st year) + 12.5% (subsequent years)		 Exception: If entire premium = only risk cover → no GST (this sub-rule doesn't apply). Investment component excluded from value.
32(5) Second-Hand Goods (Used Goods) Value = Selling price MINUS Purchase price
If negative → IGNORE (no GST on that transaction)
Special rule for repossessed goods: Purchase price = borrower's original price − 5% per quarter between purchase and disposal		 Margin scheme for used goods dealers. No ITC must have been availed on purchase of those goods. Negative margin = zero taxable value.
32(6) Vouchers / Tokens / Coupons Value = face value of goods/services redeemable against such token/voucher Taxed at face value of what can be redeemed — not at purchase price of voucher.
32(7) Services between distinct persons (Schedule I) — ITC available Value = NIL (government notified class where ITC available) Where full ITC is available — government can notify NIL value for internal service transfers between distinct persons.
📋 Rule 32 CGST Rules | Sec. 15 CGST Act | Rule 32(5) — Margin Scheme (No ITC condition critical)
Rule 32A — Value of Supply in Cases Where Kerala Flood Cess is Applicable
🌊 Rule 32A — Kerala Flood Cess Exclusion
Inserted vide Notif. 31/2019-CT w.e.f. 01.07.2019
Where Kerala Flood Cess is levied on a supply, the value of such supply for GST purposes = value determined under Sec. 15 CGST Act
BUT Kerala Flood Cess itself is NOT included in that value
📝 Kerala imposed a flood cess on top of GST. This rule ensures that the cess is excluded from the taxable value — so you don't pay GST on the cess amount itself. Cess-on-cess is avoided.
Rule 33 — Value of Supply in Case of Pure Agent
🧑‍💼 Rule 33 — Pure Agent — Disbursements Excluded from Value
4 Conditions must ALL be satisfied for exclusion
📝 If a supplier pays something on behalf of the customer — acting purely as a passthrough — that reimbursement is excluded from the supplier's taxable value. But ALL 4 conditions must be met simultaneously.
CONDITIONS — ALL 4 MANDATORY
(i) Supplier acts as pure agent — payment on authorisation by recipient
(ii) Third-party payment separately indicated on invoice issued to recipient
(iii) Procured services are in addition to supplier's own services
"Pure Agent" — 4-Limb Definition (ALL mandatory)
(a) Contractual agreement to act as pure agent
(b) Neither holds nor intends to hold title to goods/services
(c) Does NOT use such goods/services for own interest
(d) Receives ONLY actual amount — no profit on pass-through
💡 Corporate services firm A handles company B's incorporation. A pays MCA registration fees on behalf of B. A recovers exact fees — separately shown on invoice — in addition to own service fees. A is a pure agent for the MCA fees portion. That portion excluded from A's taxable value.
🔍 GP Singh: "All the following conditions" = conjunctive reading = strict cumulative satisfaction. If even ONE condition fails — exclusion denied. Department regularly disputes condition (ii) — separate indication on invoice is non-negotiable.
📋 Rule 33 CGST Rules | Compare Sec. 15(2)(b) CGST Act — incidental expenses included in value
Rules 34 & 35 — Rate of Exchange & Tax-Inclusive Value
Rule Provision Formula / Rate Plain English
Rule 34(1)
Goods		 Rate of exchange for goods import/export Rate notified by CBIC under Sec. 14, Customs Act, 1962 applicable on date of time of supply (Sec. 12 CGST) Use Customs exchange rate — not RBI rate — for goods valuation.
Rule 34(2)
Services		 Rate of exchange for services Rate per Generally Accepted Accounting Principles (GAAP) on date of time of supply (Sec. 13 CGST) For services — use accounting rates (not Customs rates). Time of supply under Sec. 13 governs.
Rule 35 Where supply value includes tax (tax-inclusive price) Tax Amount = (Value inclusive of tax × Tax rate%) ÷ (100 + sum of applicable tax rates%) Back-calculate tax from inclusive price. MRP-based supplies use this. E.g., MRP ₹118 at 18% GST → Tax = (118 × 18)/(118) = ₹18. Value = ₹100.
KEY DEFINITIONS — Rule 35 Explanation
(a) "Open Market Value" — Full money amount excluding all taxes/cess, between unrelated parties, price is sole consideration, at same time as supply being valued.
(b) "Like Kind & Quality" — Same/closely resembling supply, under similar circumstances, same characteristics, quality, quantity, functional components, reputation.
📗 CHAPTER V — INPUT TAX CREDIT (Rules 36–45)
Rule 36 — Documentary Requirements & Conditions for Claiming ITC
📄 Rule 36 — ITC Documentary Eligibility
Sub-rule (1): Valid Documents | Sub-rule (2): Particulars | Sub-rules (3)&(4): Restrictions
Sub-rule (1) — Valid Documents for ITC [5 types]
(a) Invoice by supplier
u/s 31
(b) Invoice under Sec. 31(f) (RCM)
subject to tax payment
(c) Debit Note
u/s 34
(d) Bill of Entry
(Customs — for imports)
(e) ISD Invoice / ISD Credit Note
(Rule 54(1))
Sub-rule (2) — Incomplete Document: Can ITC still be claimed?
GENERAL RULE
ITC only if ALL specified particulars of Chapter VI are present in document
PROVISO — Relaxation
Even if not all particulars present → ITC allowed IF document contains:
✓ Tax amount charged
✓ Description of goods/services
✓ Total value
✓ GSTIN of supplier & recipient
✓ Place of supply (inter-state)
📝 Minor defects in invoice → ITC not lost if these 5 essentials are present. Strict construction of document requirements is against the assessee; this proviso is a relief.
Sub-rule (3) — Block Credit on Confirmed Fraud Demands
No ITC on tax paid pursuant to any order where demand confirmed on account of fraud / willful misstatement / suppression of facts u/s 74
(Inserted vide Notif. 20/2024-CT dt. 08.10.2024)
📝 If you paid tax under a confirmed fraud demand (Sec. 74 order), the recipient CANNOT claim that tax as ITC. The tainted payment is blocked from flowing into the credit chain.
Sub-rule (4) — GSTR-2B Matching Condition
ITC on invoices/debit notes available ONLY IF:
(a) Supplier has furnished in GSTR-1 / GSTR-1A / IFF, AND
(b) Details communicated to recipient in FORM GSTR-2B (Rule 60)
📝 ITC is locked to GSTR-2B. If supplier has not filed GSTR-1 — invoice won't appear in your GSTR-2B — you CANNOT claim ITC even if you have a valid invoice and paid tax. This is the single most litigated ITC condition.
💡 You received goods, have invoice, paid ₹18 lakh GST to supplier in Nov 2023. Supplier never filed GSTR-1. Invoice absent from your GSTR-2B. ITC of ₹18 lakh blocked per Rule 36(4). Department can demand reversal with interest.
🔍 GP Singh: "Shall not" in sub-rule (4) = mandatory prohibition. But numerous HCs have held that ITC cannot be denied solely for supplier's default if recipient is bona fide — constitutional challenge to Rule 36(4) pending at SC.
📋 Sec. 16(2) CGST Act | Rule 60 CGST Rules (GSTR-2B) | Sub-rule (3): Sec. 74 CGST Act | Notif. 40/2021-CT (Sub-rule 4 substituted)
Rules 37 & 37A — ITC Reversal for Non-Payment of Consideration & Supplier Non-Filing
🔄 Rules 37 & 37A — Two Grounds for Mandatory ITC Reversal
Rule 37 — Non-Payment to Supplier (180 Days)
ITC availed → Supplier not paid (value + tax) within time limit u/s 16(2) second proviso (180 days)
ACTION
Reverse ITC proportionate to amount not paid + interest u/s 50 → in GSTR-3B of tax period immediately following 180 days from invoice date
📝 If you received goods/services and claimed ITC but didn't pay the supplier within 6 months — you must give back the ITC. Proportionate reversal if partial payment.
💡 Invoice dated 01.07.2024. ITC ₹1,80,000 claimed. You paid ₹50,000 only. By 28.12.2024 (180 days), unpaid = ₹1,30,000. Reverse proportionate ITC on ₹1,30,000 in Dec 2024 GSTR-3B.
RE-AVAIL
On subsequently paying the amount — ITC can be re-availed [Rule 37(2)]
DEEMED PAID
Schedule I supplies (without consideration) → deemed paid for Sec. 16(2) purposes
Sec. 15(2)(b) amounts (additions to value) → also deemed paid
Rule 37(4)
Time limit u/s 16(4) does NOT apply to re-availing credit that was reversed earlier
Rule 37A — Supplier Non-Filing (Auto-Reversal)
Inserted vide Notif. 26/2022-CT
TRIGGER
ITC availed in GSTR-3B on invoices reflected in GSTR-1/IFF by supplier
BUT supplier has NOT filed GSTR-3B for that tax period by 30th September following end of financial year
REVERSAL DEADLINE
Recipient must reverse such ITC in GSTR-3B BEFORE 30th November of same financial year
DEFAULT CONSEQUENCE
If NOT reversed by 30th November → added to recipient's liability + interest u/s 50
RE-AVAIL
If supplier subsequently files GSTR-3B → recipient may re-avail the reversed credit in any subsequent GSTR-3B
🔍 GP Singh: Rule 37A creates conditional ITC — the credit is not finally denied but held in abeyance till supplier files. The re-avail right is crucial — tax authorities must not treat initial reversal as permanent denial.
📋 Sec. 16(2) second proviso | Rule 37 CGST Rules | Rule 37A (Notif. 26/2022-CT) | Sec. 50 (interest)
Rule 38 — ITC Claim by Banking Company / NBFC (Section 17(4) Option)
🏦 Rule 38 — Banking / Financial Institution ITC — 50% Method
Applies when entity chooses NOT to comply with Sec. 17(2) — uses Sec. 17(4) option
(a) NO CREDIT for:
(i) Inputs/services for non-business purpose
(ii) Credit attributable to exempt supplies u/s 17(5)
(b) CREDIT for:
Inputs/services in second proviso to Sec. 17(4) not covered under (a)
(c) FIFTY PERCENT RULE:
50% of remaining ITC is admissible
Balance 50% reversed in GSTR-3B
📝 Banks get half the remaining ITC as a simplified rule — avoids complex exempt/taxable computation applicable to normal businesses.
Rule 39 — ISD Distribution of Input Tax Credit
🔀 Rule 39 — Input Service Distributor (ISD) — Distribution Rules
Substituted w.e.f. 01.04.2025 | Notif. 12/2024-CT
📝 ISD is a registered office that receives invoices for common input services used by multiple branches. It distributes ITC to those branches in proportion to their turnover. Rule 39 sets the exact procedure.
Clause Rule / Condition Plain English / Impact
(a) Credit available in a month → distributed in the same month | Details in FORM GSTR-6 No carry-forward of ISD credit to next month — distribute same month.
(b) Amount distributed ≤ Amount available for distribution Cannot over-distribute. ISD cannot pass more credit than it has received.
(c) Credit attributable to ONE recipient → distributed ONLY to that recipient Specific attribution — not shared pro-rata if identifiable to one unit.
(d)&(e) Credit attributable to multiple recipients → distributed pro-rata based on turnover in State/UT during relevant period Formula: C1 = (t1/T) × C
C = total credit; t1 = turnover of R1; T = aggregate turnover of all recipients to whom service attributable
(g) ISD must separately distribute ineligible ITC (u/s 17(5) or otherwise) and eligible ITC Blocked credits must be separately identified — cannot be merged with eligible credits in distribution.
(h)–(j) CGST/SGST distributed to same-State recipients as CGST/SGST | To other-State recipients as IGST Cross-state distribution always as IGST regardless of original tax head.
(k)&(l) ISD issues ISD Invoice (distribution) + ISD Credit Note (reduction) Separate document types — cannot use normal tax invoices for ISD distribution.
1A (NEW) RCM services attributable to one or more persons — related PAN registered person can transfer to ISD via Sec. 9(3)/(4) invoice New RCM-ISD credit flow mechanism effective 01.04.2025 | Notif. 12/2024 + Notif. 13/2025.
📋 Sec. 20 CGST Act | Rule 39 (substituted w.e.f. 01.04.2025) | GSTR-6 | Rule 54(1) — ISD Invoice format | Notif. 12/2024-CT | Notif. 13/2025-CT
Rule 40 — Manner of Claiming ITC in Special Circumstances (Sec. 18)
🆕 Rule 40 — ITC on Opening Stock / New Registration / Composition Exit
Sec. 18(1)(a)–(d) | ITC on goods / capital goods on transition into taxable status
📝 When a person newly becomes liable to GST (new registration, exit from composition, exempt supplies become taxable) — they can claim ITC on stock held on the day before transition. Rule 40 governs the conditions.
Clause Condition / Rule Notes
(a) Capital goods ITC: Claimed after reducing tax @ 5% per quarter (or part thereof) from date of invoice Depreciation equivalent — ITC reduces by 5% for each quarter CG has been held. Use-period deduction.
(b) Declaration in FORM GST ITC-01 within 30 days of becoming eligible [Commissioner may extend] Strict 30-day window. Commissioner can extend. State Commissioner's extension = deemed notified by Central Commissioner.
(c) Declaration must specify inputs/goods/CGs held in stock on day PRECEDING transition date [varies by Sec. 18(1) clause] Day before effective date of liability, registration, RCM liability, or taxable supply commencement — as applicable.
(d) If aggregate ITC (CGST+SGST+UTGST+IGST) > ₹2 lakh → Declaration must be certified by practising CA or CMA Professional certification mandatory above ₹2 lakh. Signature of practicing CA/CMA on ITC-01.
(e) ITC claimed verified against supplier's GSTR-1 / GSTR-1A / GSTR-4 on common portal System-level cross-verification of claimed ITC against supplier-uploaded data.
Sub-rule (2) Capital goods / plant & machinery sold u/s 18(6): ITC to be reduced @ 5% per quarter from date of invoice
If reduced ITC > tax on transaction value → excess becomes output tax liability		 On disposal of capital goods — pay back proportionate ITC. If GST on sale price is less than remaining ITC, the difference is payable as output tax.
📋 Sec. 18(1)(a)(b)(c)(d) CGST Act | Rule 40 CGST Rules | FORM GST ITC-01 | Sec. 18(6) — disposal of CG
Rules 41 & 41A — Transfer of ITC Credit (M&A, Demerger, Separate Registrations)
🏗️ Rules 41 & 41A — Transferring Unutilised ITC in Restructuring / Split Registrations
Rule 41 — Sale / Merger / Amalgamation / Demerger / Lease / Change of Ownership
Transferor: File FORM GST ITC-02 electronically on common portal + request for transfer of unutilised ITC to Transferee
Transferor: Also submit CA/CMA certificate that transfer has specific provision for transfer of liabilities
Transferee: Accept on common portal → ITC in ITC-02 credited to Transferee's electronic credit ledger
DEMERGER: ITC apportioned in ratio of value of assets of new units as per demerger scheme
Explanation: "Value of assets" = total assets of business — whether or not ITC availed on them
📝 On company restructuring — the unused GST credit (like an asset) must be properly assigned to the entity that takes over the business/liability. ITC-02 is the transfer mechanism.
Rule 41A — Separate Registrations for Multiple Places (within same State)
File FORM GST ITC-02A within 30 days of obtaining separate registrations (Rule 11)
ITC transferred in ratio of value of assets held by new entities at time of registration
New entity accepts on portal → ITC-02A amount credited to electronic credit ledger
📝 A business splits one registration into multiple (e.g., separate registrations for each SEZ unit) — it can transfer proportionate ITC to each new registration using ITC-02A.
📋 Rule 41 | Rule 41A (Notif. 03/2019-CT) | FORM GST ITC-02 | FORM GST ITC-02A | Sec. 18 CGST Act
Rule 42 — ITC Apportionment: Inputs & Input Services (Mixed Use)
📊 Rule 42 — ITC on Inputs/Services — Step-by-Step Apportionment Formula
Applies when Sec. 17(1)/(2) — partly business/non-business or taxable/exempt use
📝 When you use inputs/services for both taxable and exempt supplies (or partly for personal use), you can't claim full ITC. Rule 42 gives a formula to compute exactly how much credit you can keep and how much you must reverse.
Symbol Meaning Treatment
T Total ITC on inputs + input services in a tax period Starting pool
T1 ITC for exclusively non-business purposes BLOCKED — not credited
T2 ITC for exclusively exempt supply BLOCKED — not credited
T3 ITC under Sec. 17(5) blocked credits BLOCKED — not credited
C1 Electronic credit ledger = T − (T1+T2+T3) CREDITED to ledger
T4 ITC exclusively for taxable supplies (incl. zero-rated) FULLY ELIGIBLE
C2 Common credit = C1 − T4 Needs further split
D1 Credit towards exempt supplies = (E/F) × C2
E = exempt turnover; F = total State turnover		 MUST BE REVERSED
D2 Credit for non-business use = 5% of C2 (flat) MUST BE REVERSED
C3 Eligible ITC = C2 − (D1+D2) FINAL ELIGIBLE CREDIT
⚠️ REAL ESTATE SPECIAL RULE — Clause (f) Explanation & Proviso to Clause (i)
For supply of services under Schedule II Para 5(b) (construction of apartments):
• T4 = ZERO during construction phase (inputs/services commonly used for booked & unbooked apartments)
• D1 = (E/F) × C2 calculated per project:
E = (exempt carpet area) + (unbooked carpet area)
F = total carpet area of project
Final reconciliation on issuance of completion certificate / first occupation
📝 Builders: During construction, all ITC is treated as common credit — you can't say it's exclusively for taxable supply. Final ITC reversal is computed project-wise when project completes. Ongoing projects have special transition rules.
ANNUAL RECONCILIATION (Sub-rule 2)
Final D1 and D2 computed for full financial year before due date of September GSTR-3B of following year.
If final > monthly provisional → reverse excess + interest from 1st April of succeeding year till payment date.
If final < monthly provisional → claim excess as credit in return not later than September.
📋 Sec. 17(1)(2) CGST Act | Rule 42 | Schedule II Para 5(b) | Notif. 16/2019-CT (real estate amendments) | Explanation 2 — definition of RREP, REP, carpet area, ongoing project
Rule 43 — ITC Apportionment: Capital Goods (Mixed Use)
🏭 Rule 43 — Capital Goods ITC — 5-Year Useful Life Spread
Capital goods used partly for taxable + partly for exempt / non-business use
📝 Capital goods last for years. So ITC on them is spread over 5 years (60 months). Every month you compute what portion of the ITC relates to exempt use and reverse that. Rule 43 gives the formula for this monthly computation.
Category Treatment Formula / Key Rule
(a) CG exclusively for non-business / exempt supplies ITC declared in GSTR-3B — NOT credited to electronic credit ledger. BLOCKED. No ITC at all. During real estate construction phase — all CG treated as common (T4 = zero).
(b) CG exclusively for taxable/zero-rated supplies Full ITC directly to electronic credit ledger No reversal needed. During real estate construction phase — T4 = zero (commonly used).
(c) Common CG (not covered by a or b) Amount 'A' (full tax on invoice) → directly to electronic credit ledger
Valid for 5 years (60 months) from invoice date		 If previously in (a) and now moved to (c) → claim credit after reducing 5% per quarter for period in (a).
(d) Aggregate 'A' values → Tc Tc = aggregate of 'A' for all common CG with remaining useful life in that period Running pool of common CG credit.
(e) Monthly attribution → Tm Tm = Tc / 60 Each month: 1/60th of common CG pool is attributable for that month's analysis.
(g) Exempt attribution → Te Te = (E/F) × Tm (using same E/F as Rule 42) → added to output tax each month with interest Monthly reversal for exempt portion of common capital goods.
Sub-rule (2) — Real Estate Final Reconciliation Tefinal = [(E1+E2+E3)/F] × Tcfinal (project-wise, at completion)
Afinal = A × (months used / 60)		 Final true-up on completion certificate / first occupation — excess reversed (interest from 1st April); shortfall claimed.
EXCLUSIONS FROM EXEMPT SUPPLY (Explanation 1 to Rules 42 & 43)
Exempt supply excludes: (b) Interest/discount on deposits/loans (except for banks/NBFCs) | (d) Duty Credit Scrips (Notif. 35/2017-CT) | Note: Goods sold from Duty Free Shops at arrival terminals (Explanation 3 — Notif. 38/2023)
📋 Sec. 17(1)(2) CGST Act | Rule 43 | Rule 42 (shared definitions) | Notif. 16/2019-CT | Notif. 16/2020-CT | RERA definitions for real estate project, RREP, carpet area, apartment, promoter
Rules 44 & 44A — Reversal of ITC Under Special Circumstances & Gold Dore Bar
↩️ Rules 44 & 44A — ITC Reversal on Registration Cancellation & Special Circumstances
Rule 44 — Reversal Method: Inputs, Semi-Finished Goods, Finished Goods, Capital Goods [Sec. 18(4) / Sec. 29(5)]
(a) INPUTS — Stocks & WIP
ITC computed proportionately on basis of corresponding invoices on which credit was availed
📝 Match stock with original invoices → reverse ITC on those invoices proportionate to remaining stock.
(b) CAPITAL GOODS
ITC in remaining useful life months computed pro-rata on 5-year basis
Example: Used 4 yrs 6 mths 15 days → remaining = 5 months → Reversal = C × 5/60
INVOICES N/A
If original invoices not available → estimate based on prevailing market price on effective date of event
