Amount determined forms part of output tax liability | Details in FORM GST ITC-03 (Sec. 18(4) events) or FORM GSTR-10 (cancellation of registration) | Above ₹2 lakh → CA/CMA certificate mandatory
Sec. 18(6) — Sale of Capital Goods: Special Rule
ITC on CG = Original ITC − 5% per quarter from invoice date
If this REDUCED ITC > tax on transaction value → excess is OUTPUT TAX
Details in FORM GSTR-1
Rule 44A — Gold Dore Bar (CENVAT Credit Carried Forward)
CENVAT credit carried forward for gold dore bar (additional Customs duty u/s 3 Customs Tariff Act) held on 01.07.2017:
Only 1/6th of such credit is retained in electronic credit ledger.
5/6th must be debited from electronic credit ledger at time of supply of such gold dore bar / jewellery made therefrom.
If already supplied → debit within one week of commencement of CGST Rules.
📋 Sec. 18(4) & (6) CGST Act | Sec. 29(5) CGST Act | Rule 44 | Rule 44A (Notif. 22/2017-CT) | FORM GST ITC-03 | FORM GSTR-10
Rule 45 — Job Work: Conditions & Restrictions for Inputs/Capital Goods Sent to Job Worker
🔧 Rule 45 — Job Work — Challan, Reporting & Return Obligations
Read with Sec. 143 CGST Act
SUB-RULE (1) — Challan Requirement
Inputs/semi-finished goods/CGs sent to job worker under cover of challan issued by principal (Rule 55)
Even if goods sent directly to job worker — challan by principal
Goods from one JW to another → challan by principal OR sending JW
Challan may be endorsed by JW for quantity/description when passing to next JW
SUB-RULE (3) — ITC-04 Filing
Details of challans dispatched/received → FORM GST ITC-04 for "specified period" by 25th of month following that period
Commissioner may extend (State extension deemed notified by Centre)
Specified period:
• Turnover > ₹5 cr: 6-month period (Apr–Sep, Oct–Mar)
• Others: Financial Year
SUB-RULE (4) — Deemed Supply Consequence
If inputs/CGs NOT returned to principal within time u/s 143 → deemed supply by principal to job worker on the day goods were sent out
Principal must declare in GSTR-1 + pay tax + interest
📝 Send goods to job worker → don't get back within 1 year (inputs) or 3 years (CG) → law deems you sold them to the job worker on day of dispatch.
EXPLANATION — Value of Exempt Supply for Sec. 17(3)
(a) Land & building → value = stamp duty value
(b) Securities → value = 1% of sale value
📋 Sec. 143 CGST Act (job work time limits) | Rule 45 | Rule 55 (challan format) | FORM GST ITC-04 | Notif. 35/2021-CT (ITC-04 period changed)
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal — CGST Rules Ch. IV & V (Valuation + ITC)
Statutory arguments extracted from Rules 27–45. Use confidently in personal hearings, SCN replies and appellate submissions.
Rule 28 Proviso 2 — Full ITC = Deemed OMV (Invoice Value Final)
Rule 28, Second Proviso | CGST Rules, 2017 | Notif. 52/2023-CT dt. 26.10.2023
Key Ratio / Principle
Where the recipient is eligible for full input tax credit, the value declared in the invoice shall be deemed to be the open market value of the goods or services. The deeming fiction is conclusive — the department has no authority to challenge or substitute the invoice value with a different valuation where the recipient has full ITC eligibility.
When to Use — When Department challenges inter-company pricing between related/distinct persons and issues SCN alleging undervaluation. Cite Rule 28 Proviso 2 to argue: since recipient has full ITC, any invoice value is statutorily treated as OMV. Entire valuation demand falls.
Rule 36(4) — ITC Cannot Be Denied for Supplier's Default (Constitutional Challenge)
Rule 36(4) CGST Rules | Multiple High Court judgments — Calcutta, Madras, Kerala HCs | SC reference pending
Key Ratio / Principle
The ITC chain under Rule 36(4) makes the recipient responsible for the supplier's filing defaults, which is constitutionally questionable. A bona fide purchaser who has paid consideration and GST cannot be denied ITC merely because the supplier failed to file returns. The ITC right crystallises at the time of payment of tax on inward supply — not at the time of supplier's return filing. Department's remedy lies against the defaulting supplier under Section 79, not against the innocent recipient.
When to Use — When Department denies/reverses ITC solely on the ground that supplier has not filed GSTR-3B and invoice is not in GSTR-2B. Assert: (i) ITC is a vested right; (ii) Rule 36(4) cannot be applied to deny credit where tax has been paid; (iii) cite HC judgments on constitutional challenge; (iv) Department must pursue supplier under Sec. 79 first.
Rule 37 — ITC Reversal Proportionate, Not Total; Re-Avail Right Protected
Rule 37(1) & 37(2) CGST Rules, 2017 | Notif. 19/2022-CT (substituted w.e.f. 01.10.2022)
Key Ratio / Principle
Under Rule 37(1) as amended, ITC reversal on non-payment within 180 days is proportionate to the amount not paid to the supplier — not total reversal of all ITC. Further, Rule 37(2) expressly preserves the right to re-avail the reversed credit upon subsequent payment, and Rule 37(4) provides that the limitation period under Section 16(4) does not apply to such re-availed credit.
When to Use — When Department demands total reversal of ITC under Rule 37 on ground of non-payment, or when they deny re-availing on limitation grounds. Assert: (i) reversal is proportionate only; (ii) re-avail right under Rule 37(2) is unconditional; (iii) Sec. 16(4) limitation expressly excluded for re-availed reversed credit.
Rule 37A — Reversal is Provisional, Not Final; Re-Avail on Supplier Filing
Rule 37A CGST Rules | Inserted vide Notif. 26/2022-CT dt. 26.12.2022
Key Ratio / Principle
Rule 37A mandates only provisional reversal of ITC where supplier has not filed GSTR-3B by 30th September. The same rule expressly preserves the right to re-avail such reversed credit in any subsequent GSTR-3B return if the supplier subsequently furnishes the return. The reversal is not a final denial — it is a temporary hold mechanism. The credit cannot be treated as permanently forfeited on account of supplier's delayed filing.
When to Use — When Department converts a Rule 37A provisional reversal into a final demand, or when they deny re-availing on the ground that the reversal became final. Cite: Rule 37A's express re-avail provision — provisional reversal ≠ permanent denial.
Rule 33 — Pure Agent Exclusion: All 4 Conditions Must Be Tested Objectively
Rule 33 CGST Rules, 2017 | Read with Sec. 15 CGST Act
Key Ratio / Principle
Rule 33 excludes from taxable value any expenditure incurred as a pure agent of the recipient, provided all conditions — including that the amount is separately indicated in the invoice and the services are in addition to the supplier's own services — are cumulatively satisfied. The exclusion applies to actual disbursements, not to supplier's own supply of service. A recovery that meets all four limbs of the pure agent definition and all three conditions of Rule 33 is a reimbursement, not a service, and cannot be taxed as part of value.
When to Use — When Department includes government fees, registration charges, statutory levies recovered from client in the taxable value of the supplier's services. Assert Rule 33: separate indication on invoice, actual recovery without mark-up, third-party payment on authorisation = pure agent exclusion.
Rule 32(5) — Margin Scheme for Used Goods: No GST on Loss Transactions
Rule 32(5) CGST Rules, 2017
Key Ratio / Principle
For second-hand goods dealers who have not availed ITC on purchase, the taxable value is the difference between selling price and purchase price. Where such value is negative, it shall be ignored — meaning no GST is payable on that transaction. The rule does not permit carrying forward of negative margins to offset positive margins on other transactions, but it also does not create a liability on loss-making transactions.
When to Use — When Department demands GST on used goods transactions ignoring the margin scheme, or when they aggregate margins and losses. Assert: (i) margin scheme is at supplier's option; (ii) negative margin = no GST that transaction; (iii) condition precedent = no ITC on purchase must be verified transaction-wise.
Rule 42 — Annual True-Up is the Final ITC Position; Monthly Reversal is Provisional
Rule 42(2) CGST Rules | Notif. 16/2019-CT dt. 29.03.2019
Key Ratio / Principle
Rule 42(1) provisional monthly reversal of common credits (D1+D2) is not the final ITC position. Rule 42(2) mandates a final reconciliation before the due date of September return following the financial year. If the annual aggregate of D1+D2 exceeds monthly provisionals — reverse excess with interest from 1st April. If monthly provisionals exceed annual final — claim the excess back. The monthly computation is always provisional in nature and the annual computation is definitive.
When to Use — When Department raises demand on ITC on the basis of monthly provisional D1/D2 figures without waiting for annual reconciliation, or when they impose interest from the month of availing rather than from 1st April of succeeding year. Assert: interest runs from 1st April of succeeding FY, not from month of credit availing.
Rule 28 Sub-rule (2) — Corporate Guarantee: 1% p.a. or Actual — Retrospective Issue
Rule 28(2) CGST Rules | Notif. 52/2023-CT dt. 26.10.2023 (retrospective w.e.f. 26.10.2023) | Notif. 12/2024-CT dt. 10.07.2024
Key Ratio / Principle
The value of supply of services by way of providing corporate guarantee to a banking company on behalf of a related person located in India shall be deemed to be 1% per annum of the guarantee amount or actual consideration, whichever is higher. This rule was inserted with retrospective effect. However, where the recipient of the guarantee service is eligible for full input tax credit, the invoice value (which may be nil) shall be deemed to be the value of supply — thereby nullifying the 1% floor for fully eligible recipients.
When to Use — In corporate guarantee GST demands (very common in 2024–25 assessments). Assert: (i) if subsidiary recipient has full ITC → invoice value (even nil) = deemed value; (ii) if recipient is a bank or NBFC that cannot claim full ITC → 1% applies; (iii) retrospective application challenged on ground of no tax liability arising before rule was inserted.
Rule 40(d) — CA Certificate Mandatory Only Above ₹2 Lakh — Below That, Self-Declaration Sufficient
Rule 40(1)(d) CGST Rules, 2017
Key Ratio / Principle
The requirement of a certificate from a practising Chartered Accountant or Cost Accountant for ITC claimed on opening stock under Section 18(1) is conditional — it applies only where the aggregate value of such ITC claim on account of all tax heads (CGST+SGST+UTGST+IGST) exceeds rupees two lakhs. Below this threshold, the self-declaration in FORM GST ITC-01 is sufficient and no professional certificate is required.
When to Use — When Department denies Sec. 18(1) ITC on opening stock claiming absence of CA certificate even for small claims. Assert: Rule 40(1)(d) — CA certificate mandatory only above ₹2 lakh. Below ₹2 lakh — ITC-01 self-declaration is complete compliance.
Rule 45(4) — Deemed Supply Only on Expiry of Time Limit; Not Earlier
Rule 45(4) CGST Rules | Sec. 143 CGST Act
Key Ratio / Principle
Rule 45(4) creates a deemed supply fiction only when inputs or capital goods are not returned to the principal within the time specified in Section 143 — namely one year for inputs and three years for capital goods. Until expiry of these time periods, there is no supply, no tax event, and no demand. The fiction operates from the date goods were sent out, but the obligation to pay tax arises only on the failure to return within time — not before. Section 143 time limits are the gateway to this fiction.
When to Use — When Department raises GST demand on goods sent to job worker claiming supply has taken place even before expiry of 1/3 year period, or when goods are pending return but time limit has not yet expired. Assert: deemed supply fiction under Rule 45(4) is triggered only on failure to return within Sec. 143 time limit — not before.
For professional litigation use | Pawan Soni & Associates, Ichalkaranji
CGST Rules — Ch.VI–VIII (Rules 46–84)

⚖️ Chapters VI–VIII — Tax Invoice, Accounts & Returns | Rules 46–84

Source: CGST Rules, 2017 (Bare Law – ICAI Nov 2025 Edition) | CA Pawankumar Soni, Ichalkaranji

Permitted / Valid
Prohibited / Condition Breached
Condition / Unless / If
Optional / Proviso
Neutral Reference
📝 Plain English
💡 Example
🔍 GP Singh Interpretation
📋 Rule Cross-Reference
🆕 2024-25 Amendment
📋 Index of Rules — Chapters VI to VIII
📄 CHAPTER VI — TAX INVOICE, CREDIT & DEBIT NOTES (Rules 46–55A)
Rule 46 — Tax Invoice Particulars
Rule 46A — Invoice-cum-Bill of Supply
Rule 47 — Time Limit: Invoice (Services)
Rule 47A — Time Limit: RCM Invoice
Rule 48 — Manner of Issuing Invoice (incl. e-Invoice)
Rule 49 — Bill of Supply
Rule 50 — Receipt Voucher
Rule 51 — Refund Voucher
Rule 52 — Payment Voucher
Rule 53 — Revised Invoice & Credit/Debit Notes
Rule 54 — Tax Invoice: Special Cases
Rule 55 — Delivery Challan (No Invoice)
Rule 55A — Invoice/BOS to Accompany Transport
📚 CHAPTER VII — ACCOUNTS & RECORDS (Rules 56–58)
Rule 56 — Account Maintenance by Registered Persons
Rule 57 — Electronic Records
Rule 58 — Godown/Warehouse/Transporter Records
📊 CHAPTER VIII — RETURNS (Rules 59–84)
Rule 59 — Outward Supplies (GSTR-1 / IFF)
Rule 60 — Inward Supplies (GSTR-2A / GSTR-2B)
Rule 61 — GSTR-3B (Monthly/Quarterly Return)
Rule 61A — Quarterly Return Option
Rule 62 — Composition Supplier Return (GSTR-4)
Rule 63 — Non-Resident Taxable Person (GSTR-5)
Rule 64 — OIDAR / Online Money Gaming (GSTR-5A)
Rules 65–67A — ISD / TDS / TCS / Nil Return
Rule 78 — E-Commerce Matching

Rules 81-84 — Final Return / UIN / GST Practitioner
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal
Rule 46 — Tax Invoice: Mandatory Particulars (Subject to Rule 54)
🧾 Rule 46 — Tax Invoice
Referred to in Section 31 CGST Act | Subject to Rule 54 for special cases
📝 Every registered supplier must issue a tax invoice for every taxable supply. This rule lists ALL details that must appear on that invoice. Missing any mandatory field = defective invoice = ITC claim at risk for the recipient.
Clause Particular Required Key Condition / Note Litigation Point
(a)Name, Address & GSTIN of SupplierMandatory alwaysWrong GSTIN = defective invoice; ITC denial possible
(b)Consecutive Serial Number ≤ 16 charactersMay contain alphabets, numerals, hyphen "-", slash "/" or combination; unique per financial year per seriesMultiple series permitted. Format flexible — but must be unique within FY
(c)Date of IssueMust appear on invoiceDate determines time of supply for GST
(d)Name, Address, GSTIN or UIN of Recipient (if registered)If registered recipientWrong GSTIN of recipient = ITC at risk for recipient
(e)Name, Address of Recipient + Delivery Address + State + State CodeIf recipient is UNregistered AND taxable value ≥ ₹50,000Threshold: ₹50,000 triggers mandatory disclosure of recipient address
(f)Name, Address + Delivery Address + State CodeIf unregistered recipient AND value < ₹50,000 but recipient requests these detailsOptional below ₹50k unless requested — but if requested, mandatory
(f) Proviso🆕 For Online Money Gaming / ECO-supplied taxable services / OIDAR to unregistered recipientIrrespective of value — invoice must contain NAME OF STATE of recipient (deemed address on record)2022-23 Amendment — ECO-supplied services need state name even for small-value transactions
(g)HSN Code for goods or servicesBoard may notify class of persons/supplies for specific digit count; or exempt certain classes6-digit HSN for turnover >₹5 Cr; 4-digit for others (currently notified)
(h)Description of goods or servicesMandatoryVague description = adjudicating officer may question classification
(i)Quantity + Unit / UQC (for goods)UQC = Unique Quantity CodeMissing quantity in goods invoice = defective invoice
(j)Total value of supplyGross value before discountsDistinction between taxable value (k) and total value (j)
(k)Taxable value after discount/abatementNet value on which tax is chargedPre-supply discounts reduce taxable value; post-supply may require credit note
(l)Rate of tax (CGST / SGST / IGST / UTGST / Cess)Separately for each componentWrong rate = wrong tax charged = Section 77 CGST Act for wrong head; refund with limitations
(m)Amount of tax charged (CGST / SGST / IGST / UTGST / Cess)Separately for each componentAmount of ITC claimable by recipient is limited to tax charged in invoice
(n)Place of Supply + State Name (for inter-State supplies)Inter-State trade or commerce onlyWrong place of supply = wrong tax head = IGST vs CGST+SGST dispute
(o)Address of delivery (if different from place of supply)Only when delivery ≠ place of supplyCommon in drop shipment / third-party delivery scenarios
(p)Whether tax payable on Reverse Charge (RCM)"Yes" / "No" declaration mandatoryIf RCM applicable and not mentioned → recipient may not know liability → penalty exposure
(q)Signature or Digital Signature of supplier / authorised representativeManual or digital; not needed for e-invoice (see proviso)E-invoice under IT Act 2000 exempted from physical signature requirement
(r)🆕 QR Code with embedded IRN (Invoice Reference Number)Applicable where e-invoice issued under Rule 48(4)Inserted 2020. E-invoice mandated; IRN-embedded QR code is legally required part of invoice
(s)🆕 Declaration: "We are NOT required to prepare e-invoice under Rule 48(4)"Required if: (i) Turnover > notified threshold in any FY from 2017-18, AND (ii) yet not required to issue e-invoiceInserted 2022. Applies to taxpayers above threshold but claiming exemption from e-invoice — must self-declare on face of invoice
📌 Special Provisos to Rule 46
EXPORT ENDORSEMENT
Export invoice must carry endorsement:
"SUPPLY MEANT FOR EXPORT / SEZ ON PAYMENT OF IGST" OR "UNDER BOND / LUT WITHOUT PAYMENT OF IGST"
+ Name & address of recipient, delivery address, country of destination (in lieu of clause (e) details)
Sub. by Notf. 20/2024-CT dt. 08.10.2024, w.e.f. 01.11.2024 — covers SEZ supply endorsement now alongside export
CONSOLIDATED INVOICE
Registered person may issue consolidated tax invoice at end of each day for unregistered recipients who don't need invoice
Exception: Multiplex cinema screen supplier (must issue per Rule 54(4A))
📝 B2C small transactions — one daily consolidated invoice instead of per-transaction invoice. Saves compliance burden.
e-INVOICE EXEMPTION FROM SIGNATURE
Signature / digital signature NOT required where invoice issued electronically under Information Technology Act 2000
💡 E-invoices authenticated via IRP portal carry legal validity. Physical signature becomes redundant — but the invoice must have valid IRN.
📋 Corresponding Section: Section 31 CGST Act | Cross-Ref: Rule 48 (e-invoice), Rule 54 (special cases), Rule 55A (transport copy)
Rule 46A — Invoice-cum-Bill of Supply
🧾 Rule 46A — Single Document for Mixed Supplies
Notwithstanding Rules 46, 49 & 54
TRIGGER CONDITIONS — BOTH MUST APPLY:
(1) Supplier is supplying BOTH taxable AND exempted goods/services/both
(2) Recipient is an UNREGISTERED person
OUTCOME
A SINGLE "Invoice-cum-Bill of Supply" may be issued for ALL such supplies (taxable + exempted)

Must contain particulars of Rule 46 or Rule 54 (as applicable) AND Rule 49
📝 Saves issuing two documents. Where a hotel charges for room (taxable) and liquor (exempted/State-levied) to a walk-in guest — one combined document works.
💡 Restaurant providing taxable meal + exempted packaged drinking water to unregistered customer — single invoice-cum-bill of supply suffices. No separate bill of supply needed.
Proviso inserted vide Notf. 26/2022-CT dt. 26.12.2022 — clarified that the single document must contain all required particulars of both applicable rules.
📋 Cross-Ref: Rule 46 (tax invoice), Rule 49 (bill of supply), Rule 54 (special cases)
Rule 47 — Time Limit for Issuing Tax Invoice (Services)
⏱️ Rule 47 — When Must Service Invoice Be Issued?
GENERAL RULE
Invoice within 30 days from date of supply of service
📝 Standard: Issue invoice within a month of providing the service.
EXCEPTION: Banking / NBFC / Insurer
Invoice within 45 days from date of supply of service
📝 Extended time for financial institutions due to high volume of transactions.
FURTHER EXCEPTION: Distinct Persons
Insurer / Bank / NBFC / Telecom Operator / Notified suppliers — Making supplies between distinct persons (Section 25)
May issue invoice BEFORE or AT THE TIME supplier records it in books
OR before end of the QUARTER
📝 Inter-branch supplies within same legal entity (different GSTINs) — flexible invoice timing up to quarter-end.
💡 Bank HO (Mumbai GSTIN) provides IT services to Bank Branch (Delhi GSTIN) in Jan 2025 — invoice can be issued up to 31 March 2025.
📋 Section Ref: Section 31(1) CGST Act | Also See: Rule 47A for RCM invoice timing
Rule 47A — Time Limit: Invoice where Recipient Issues Invoice (RCM Self-Invoice)
⏱️ Rule 47A — RCM Self-Invoice Time Limit
🆕 Inserted by Notification No. 20/2024-CT dated 08.10.2024, w.e.f. 01.11.2024
Entire Rule 47A is a 2024 insertion — NEW RULE effective 01.11.2024
TRIGGER: Section 31(3)(f)
Where invoice is required to be issued by the RECIPIENT under clause (f) of sub-section (3) of section 31
(i.e., recipient is a registered person liable to pay tax under RCM — §9(3) or §9(4))
TIME LIMIT
Recipient must issue the invoice within 30 days from the date of RECEIPT of the said supply of goods or services
📝 Under RCM, recipient must self-issue invoice. Now there is a strict 30-day window from receipt of goods/services. Earlier, no specific time limit existed for this in the rules.
💡 Company receives legal services from unregistered advocate on 1 Nov 2024. RCM liability under §9(3). Company must issue self-invoice by 1 Dec 2024 (30 days). Late issuance = procedural non-compliance.
📋 Cross-Ref: Section 31(3)(f) CGST Act | Notwithstanding Rule 47
Rule 48 — Manner of Issuing Invoice (Including e-Invoice)
🖨️ Rule 48 — How Must Invoice Be Issued?
Rule 48(1) — GOODS
Invoice in TRIPLICATE:
• Original → RECIPIENT
• Duplicate → TRANSPORTER
• Triplicate → SUPPLIER
📝 Physical goods need 3 copies. Transporter copy helps verify goods in transit against e-way bill.
Rule 48(2) — SERVICES
Invoice in DUPLICATE:
• Original → RECIPIENT
• Duplicate → SUPPLIER
📝 Services have no physical transport — no transporter copy needed.
Rule 48(3) — FILING
Serial numbers of invoices issued during tax period shall be furnished electronically on Common Portal in FORM GSTR-1
🆕 or FORM GSTR-1A if any
GSTR-1A reference inserted by Notf. 12/2024-CT dt. 10.07.2024
⚡ Rule 48(4) — E-INVOICE (Electronic Invoice via IRP Portal)
Notified class of registered persons shall prepare invoice by including particulars in FORM GST INV-01 after obtaining Invoice Reference Number (IRN) by uploading on Common GST Electronic Portal (IRP)
📝 E-invoice = Invoice registered on GSTN's IRP (Invoice Registration Portal). IRP assigns IRN. Invoice is then valid. Applicable to businesses above ₹5 Crore aggregate turnover (currently).
💡 Manufacturer with turnover ₹20 Cr must upload every B2B invoice to IRP, get IRN, and print QR code on invoice. Invoice without IRN = not treated as invoice at all [Rule 48(5)].
Rule 48(5) — CRITICAL
Invoice issued in ANY MANNER OTHER THAN prescribed in Rule 48(4) by persons to whom it applies
→ Shall NOT be treated as an INVOICE
🔍 GP Singh: "Shall not be treated" is a deeming non-existence. This is a strict provision — no substantial compliance defence available. If e-invoice mandatory and not done, the document is not an invoice in law.
Rule 48(6)
Sub-rules (1) and (2) — triplicate/duplicate copy requirements — do NOT apply where invoice prepared in manner of Rule 48(4) (e-invoice)
📝 e-Invoice replaces paper triplicates. No need for manual copies where IRP-registered.
EXEMPTION POWER
Commissioner may exempt a person or class from Rule 48(4) for specified period — subject to conditions
💡 SEZ suppliers, insurance companies, banking entities — have been notified as exempt from e-invoice at various stages.
📋 Section Ref: Section 31 CGST Act | Notf. 68/2019-CT, 72/2020-CT | Cross-Ref: Rule 46(r) — QR Code, Rule 46(s) — Non-e-invoice Declaration
Rule 49 — Bill of Supply (for Exempt / Composition Supplies)
📋 Rule 49 — Bill of Supply
Section 31(3)(c) CGST Act — Exempt/Composition/Non-taxable supplies
📝 When no GST is charged (e.g., exempt supplies or composition taxpayer), a "Bill of Supply" replaces the tax invoice. No tax amount column — just details of supply.
Mandatory Particulars in Bill of Supply:
ClauseParticularNote
(a)Name, Address & GSTIN of SupplierSame as tax invoice
(b)Serial Number ≤ 16 charactersUnique per FY per series — same format as invoice
(c)Date of issueMandatory
(d)Name, Address & GSTIN/UIN of Recipient (if registered)If recipient registered
(e)HSN Code for goods or servicesBoard may notify digits required
(f)Description of goods or services or bothMandatory
(g)Value of supply after discount/abatementNo tax amount column (key difference from tax invoice)
(h)Signature / Digital Signature of supplier or authorised repNot required for electronic BOS under IT Act 2000
Proviso 1Provisos to Rule 46 apply mutatis mutandisExport endorsement, consolidated BOS, e-signature exemption — all apply
Proviso 2Tax invoice / similar document under any other law for NON-TAXABLE supply → deemed to be Bill of Supply for GST purposesE.g., alcohol sale invoice = deemed BOS
Proviso 3Electronic BOS under IT Act 2000 — no signature required🆕 Inserted Notf. 74/2018-CT
📋 Section: Section 31(3)(c) | Cross-Ref: Rule 46A (invoice-cum-BOS), Rule 54(5)
Rules 50, 51 & 52 — Receipt Voucher, Refund Voucher & Payment Voucher
💰 Three Vouchers: Receipt / Refund / Payment
Section 31(3)(d), (e) & (g) CGST Act
Voucher When Issued Key Particulars Special Rule / Litigation Point
Rule 50
Receipt Voucher		 When advance received before supply [§31(3)(d)] Supplier GSTIN, serial no., date, recipient details, goods/service description, advance amount, rate of tax, tax amount, PoS (inter-State), RCM indicator, signature Rate unknown at time of advance → 18% tax. Nature unknown → treat as inter-State. Rate later determined → adjustment via invoice.
Key: Advance triggers GST liability via receipt voucher
Rule 51
Refund Voucher		 When advance refunded (no supply happened) [§31(3)(e)] All basics + Receipt Voucher number/date + description of goods/service for which refund, refund amount, rate and tax amount paid Only issued when supply does NOT materialise. Cannot claim GST refund on advance refunded without refund voucher. Necessary document for ITC reversal by recipient.
Rule 52
Payment Voucher		 Issued by RECIPIENT in RCM situations [§31(3)(g)] Supplier GSTIN (if registered), serial no., date, recipient GSTIN, goods/service description, amount paid, rate of tax, tax amount, PoS (inter-State), signature Critical in RCM: Recipient is both payer and voucher issuer. Payment Voucher evidences RCM tax liability. Missing this = procedural default. ITC claim of recipient based on this voucher.
💡 Developer receives ₹5 lakh booking advance from unregistered buyer in Oct 2024. Issues Receipt Voucher with 18% GST on advance. Project cancelled in Jan 2025 — full refund given. Developer must issue Refund Voucher; buyer's ITC (if any) must be reversed. Without Refund Voucher, no GST refund to developer.
📋 Section: §31(3)(d), (e), (g) CGST Act
Rule 53 — Revised Tax Invoice & Credit/Debit Notes
🔄 Rule 53 — Revised Invoices & Credit/Debit Notes
Section 31 (Revised) | Section 34 (Credit/Debit Notes)
Rule 53(1) — Revised Tax Invoice
Mandatory particulars:
• Word "Revised Invoice" prominently
• Supplier Name/Address/GSTIN
• Serial No. ≤ 16 chars (unique/FY)
• Date of issue
• Recipient details
• Serial no. & date of original invoice or bill of supply
• Signature/Digital Signature
📝 Used when registration is granted retrospectively — supplier issues revised invoices for the gap period between effective registration date and certificate issuance date.
Rule 53(1A) — Credit/Debit Note
Mandatory particulars:
• Supplier Name/Address/GSTIN
• Nature of document (Credit Note / Debit Note)
• Serial No. ≤ 16 chars
• Date of issue
• Recipient details (GSTIN/address)
• Serial no(s) & date(s) of corresponding tax invoice(s) or bill(s) of supply
• Taxable value + Rate of Tax + Tax amount credited/debited
• Signature/Digital Signature
💡 Supplier reduces price post-supply — issues Credit Note. Recipient's ITC gets reversed. Supplier's output tax reduces. Time limit: Credit Note by 30 Nov of next FY or filing of annual return (whichever earlier) under Section 34.
Rule 53(2) — Revised Invoice for Retrospective Registration
Where registration effective date is EARLIER than certificate issuance date → Registered person MAY issue revised tax invoices for taxable supplies made during the gap period

PROVISO 1: Consolidated revised invoice for unregistered recipients in that period
PROVISO 2: Inter-State supplies > ₹2.5 lakh — consolidated revised invoice per State for unregistered recipients
Rule 53(3) — "INPUT TAX CREDIT NOT ADMISSIBLE"
Any invoice or debit note issued in pursuance of tax payable under Section 74 (fraud/suppression demand), Section 129 (goods in transit detention) or Section 130 (confiscation) shall PROMINENTLY contain the words "INPUT TAX CREDIT NOT ADMISSIBLE"
🔍 GP Singh: "Prominently" is a mandatory qualifier. If these words are absent on a §74/§129/§130 demand document — the document itself is defective. ITC denial based on such demand document can be challenged procedurally.
📋 Section: §31 (Revised Invoice), §34 (Credit/Debit Notes) | Credit Note time limit: 30 Nov of subsequent FY or annual return filing, whichever earlier
Rule 54 — Tax Invoice in Special Cases (ISD / Banking / GTA / Passenger Transport / Cinema)
Sub-Rule Who / Scenario Document Type Key Particulars / Relaxation Litigation Point
54(1) Input Service Distributor (ISD) ISD Invoice or ISD Credit Note ISD Name/Address/GSTIN; Serial No.; Date; Recipient GSTIN; Amount of credit distributed; Signature. Banking ISD — any document by whatever name called, not necessarily serially numbered. ISD must distribute credit only to units that have received the input service. ISD invoice is the basis for ITC claim at recipient unit.
54(1A) Registered person (same PAN + State code as ISD) transferring credit of common input services to ISD Invoice / Credit / Debit Note issued by the registered person (not ISD) 🆕 Contains: GSTIN of registered person, serial no., date, GSTIN of original supplier + invoice number, GSTIN of ISD, taxable value = value of common services, signature. Taxable value = same as value of common services. Inserted by Notf. 03/2018-CT. This is the mechanism for "cross-charge" of common input services within same entity. Critical for ITC flow in large multi-state enterprises.
54(2) Insurer / Bank / NBFC / Telecom (for services) Consolidated Tax Invoice (or any document in lieu thereof — by whatever name called) May issue MONTHLY consolidated invoice at end of month. May be issued/received physically or electronically. Need not be serially numbered. Need not contain recipient address. But must contain other Rule 46 information. Electronic format + no serial number + no address = significant relaxations. Signature not required for electronic issuance under IT Act 2000.
54(3) Goods Transport Agency (GTA) — transport of goods by road in goods carriage Tax Invoice or document in lieu (by whatever name called) Must contain: Gross weight of consignment, consigner name, consignee name, goods carriage registration number, goods description, origin, destination, GSTIN of person liable to pay tax (consigner/consignee/GTA). Plus all Rule 46 information. GTA must mention WHO is paying GST (forward charge / RCM). GSTIN of tax-payer determines whether RCM applies. Missing GSTIN of liable person = defective invoice = ITC risk.
54(4) Passenger Transport Service (any mode) TICKET (by whatever name called) Ticket = invoice. Need not be serially numbered. Need not contain recipient address. Must contain other Rule 46 information. Electronic ticket exempt from signature under IT Act 2000. Airlines, railways, bus operators — ticket is the tax invoice. No separate invoice needed.
54(4A) Cinema Exhibition (Multiplex screens) — admission to cinematograph films ELECTRONIC TICKET only (mandatory) 🆕 Electronic ticket DEEMED to be tax invoice for all purposes of the Act — even without recipient details, as long as other Rule 46 details are present.
Non-multiplex screens — may optionally follow this procedure.		 Inserted Notf. 33/2019-CT, w.e.f. 01.09.2019. Multiplex MUST issue e-ticket. Cannot issue consolidated invoice like regular suppliers. Exception carved out in Rule 46 proviso specifically for multiplex.
54(5) All special case persons above Applicable to documents under Rules 49, 50, 51, 52, 53 as well Sub-rules 54(2) and 54(4) apply mutatis mutandis to Bill of Supply, Receipt Voucher, Refund Voucher, Payment Voucher and Revised Invoice/Credit-Debit Note issued by these special case suppliers. Consolidated / ticket-based / document-in-lieu approach applies to ALL document types for these special suppliers.
📋 Section: §20 (ISD), §31 | Cross-Ref: Rule 46 (main invoice), Rule 49 (BOS), Rules 50-53 (other documents)
Rule 55 — Delivery Challan (Transportation Without Invoice)
🚚 Rule 55 — When Can Goods Move Without Invoice?
Delivery Challan in lieu of Invoice
Permissible Situations for Delivery Challan in lieu of Invoice:
(a) Liquid gas — quantity unknown at removal from supplier's premises
(b) Goods sent for JOB WORK
(c) Goods transported for reasons OTHER THAN supply
(d) Such OTHER supplies as Board may notify
Delivery Challan — Required Particulars:
Serial No. ≤ 16 chars | Date & Number | Consigner Name/Address/GSTIN (if registered) | Consignee Name/Address/GSTIN or UIN | HSN Code + Description | Quantity (provisional if unknown) | Taxable Value | Tax Rate & Tax Amount (if supply) | Place of Supply (inter-State) | Signature
Delivery Challan — Copies (for Goods):
Original → CONSIGNEE | Duplicate → TRANSPORTER | Triplicate → CONSIGNER
Special Rules for SKD/CKD / Batch Consignments (Rule 55(5)):
(a) Complete invoice issued BEFORE dispatch of FIRST consignment
(b) Delivery challan for each SUBSEQUENT consignment — referencing the invoice
(c) Each consignment carries copies of corresponding challan + certified copy of invoice
(d) ORIGINAL invoice copy accompanies LAST consignment
📝 SKD = Semi Knocked Down | CKD = Completely Knocked Down. For machinery shipped in parts — invoice on first consignment, challan for subsequent parts. Also applies to batch/lot consignments (2018 amendment).
"In batches or lots" added in Rule 55(5) by Notf. 39/2018-CT dated 04.09.2018 — clarifying batch/lot shipments.
📋 Cross-Ref: Rule 138 (E-way Bill declaration), Rule 55A (invoice/BOS to accompany transport)
Rule 55A — Tax Invoice / BOS to Accompany Transport of Goods
🚛 Rule 55A — Physical Copy Requirement During Transport
RULE
The person-in-charge of the conveyance shall carry a copy of the tax invoice or bill of supply (issued under Rule 46, Rule 46A or Rule 49) in cases where such person is required to carry an e-way bill under these rules.
📝 If you need an e-way bill for transportation, you also need a physical/digital copy of the invoice or BOS. Both documents must accompany the goods. Absence of either = grounds for detention under Section 129.
💡 Truck carries machinery worth ₹2 lakh — needs e-way bill. Driver must also carry tax invoice copy. Checked at checkpoint: no invoice copy = detention risk even if e-way bill present.
📋 Inserted: Notf. 03/2018-CT | Cross-Ref: Rule 138 (e-way bill), Section 129 (detention)
Rule 56 — Account Maintenance by Registered Persons (Sub-Rules 1–18)
📚 Rule 56 — Statutory Record-Keeping Obligations
Section 35(1) CGST Act | Comprehensive Accounts
Sub-RuleWhoWhat Must Be MaintainedKey Litigation Point
56(1)Every registered personTrue & correct account of: goods/services imported/exported; supplies attracting RCM; supporting documents (invoices, BOS, delivery challans, credit notes, debit notes, receipt vouchers, payment vouchers, refund vouchers)All 7 types of documents must be maintained. Missing any type = default under §35 → penalty
56(2)Every registered person (excl. Composition)Stock accounts: opening balance, receipt, supply, goods lost/stolen/destroyed/written off/gifted/free samples, closing stock — including raw materials, finished goods, scrap, wastageStock reconciliation critical. Goods lost without accounting = deemed supply under §17(5)(h) → ITC reversal
56(3)Every registered personSeparate account of advances received, paid and adjustments theretoAdvance receipts trigger GST via receipt voucher. Separate ledger required for tracking.
56(4)Every registered person (excl. Composition)Account of: Tax payable (incl. RCM), tax collected, input tax, ITC claimed, register of tax invoices/credit notes/debit notes/delivery challans issued or receivedMost critical record for audit. ITC reconciliation with GSTR-2B must match. Unexplained differences → demand.
56(5)Every registered personParticulars of: (a) Supplier names & addresses, (b) Customer names & addresses (where required), (c) Complete address of ALL premises where goods are stored including transit storageSub-rule (6): If goods found at undisclosed premises → DEEMED SUPPLY by registered person → tax payable. Storage address disclosure is mandatory.
56(6)Proper OfficerGoods at undisclosed premises without valid documents → Tax determined AS IF those goods have been supplied by the registered personDeeming fiction: undisclosed storage = supply = tax demand. Common in search/inspection proceedings under §67.
56(7)Every registered personBooks at PRINCIPAL place of business AND all additional places of business. Electronic records also acceptable.Books must be at registered/additional place — not elsewhere. Remote access to electronic records permissible.
56(8)Every registered personNo erasure/overwriting. Incorrect entries → scored out with attestation → correct entry recorded. Electronic records → LOG of every edit/deletion maintained.Electronic edit log is mandatory. Inability to produce edit log = adverse inference in investigation.
56(9)Manual booksEach volume must be serially numberedMinor compliance requirement; absence may be noted in inspection proceedings.
56(10)Presumption RuleDocuments found at premises other than those in registration certificate → PRESUMED to be maintained by the registered person (unless proved otherwise)Reverse presumption — burden on taxpayer to prove documents found at third-party premises are not his own.
56(11)Every Agent (§2(5))Authorisation from each principal; description/value/quantity of goods received and supplied on behalf of each principal; accounts furnished; tax paid on principal's behalfAgents must maintain principal-wise separate accounts — amalgamation not permissible.
56(12)ManufacturerMonthly production accounts: raw materials / services used; goods manufactured (including waste and by-products) — with quantitative detailsQuantitative production accounts enable cross-checking of input-output ratios. Discrepancies = demand for unaccounted goods.
56(13)Service providersAccounts showing: quantity of goods used in services; input services utilised; services suppliedService sector record-keeping — works contract builders, caterers, etc.
56(14)Works contract executorSeparate accounts per contract showing: client name/address; goods/services received and utilised per contract; payment received per contract; supplier detailsContract-wise separate accounts mandatory. Mixing across projects = non-compliance.
56(16)All registered personsRecords preserved for period as per Section 36 (72 months from due date of annual return). Manual records at all related places; digital records accessible at all related places.Retention period = 6 years. Earlier rule was 5 years + assessment period. Non-retention = penalty + adverse presumption.
56(17)Carrier / clearing/forwarding agentTrue and correct records of goods handled on behalf of registered person. Must produce details on demand of proper officer.Third-party custodians of goods have statutory duty — cannot plead they are not the taxpayer.
56(18)Every registered personProduce books of account maintained under ANY law on demand of proper officerNot just GST books — even Companies Act / Income Tax books if relevant to GST. Broad demand power.
📋 Section: §35 CGST Act | Preservation period: §36 (72 months) | Search power: §67
Rules 57 & 58 — Electronic Records & Transporter/Warehouse Records
💻 Rules 57–58 — Special Record-Keeping Rules
Rule 57 — Electronic Records
57(1): Proper electronic BACK-UP maintained. If destroyed by accident/natural cause → information restorable within reasonable time.
57(2): Electronic records produceable in HARD COPY or electronically readable format on demand — duly authenticated by registered person.
57(3): On demand — must provide: file details, PASSWORDS of files, explanation for codes used, and SAMPLE PRINT of stored information.
🔍 GP Singh: "Password" disclosure is a unique statutory compulsion. Cannot claim privilege of password in GST proceedings. Non-disclosure = obstruction of inspection.
Rule 58 — Godown/Warehouse/Transporter Records
58(1): Persons required under §35(2) but NOT registered — shall submit business details on common portal in FORM GST ENR-01 (physical / Facilitation Centre). Unique enrolment number generated.
58(1A): 🆕 Transporter registered in multiple States/UTs with same PAN — may apply for UNIQUE COMMON ENROLMENT NUMBER via FORM GST ENR-02 (using one GSTIN). Inserted Notf. 28/2018-CT. Enrolled under 1A — cannot use individual GSTINs for Chapter XVI (e-way bill) purposes.
58(4): Transporter → Records of goods transported, delivered, stored in transit + GSTIN of consigner & consignee for each branch.
Warehouse/Godown owner → Period-wise books for each lot + dispatch/movement/receipt/disposal particulars.
58(5): Godown owner — Store goods identifiable ITEM-WISE and OWNER-WISE. Facilitate physical verification by proper officer on demand.
💡 CFS (Container Freight Station) storing goods for multiple registered persons — must be able to identify which goods belong to whom. Mixed storage without owner-wise segregation = default.
📋 Section: §35(2) | Cross-Ref: Chapter XVI (e-way bill rules)
Rule 59 — Outward Supplies: GSTR-1 / IFF / GSTR-1A
📤 Rule 59 — Outward Supplies Statement
Section 37 CGST Act | GSTR-1 (Monthly/Quarterly) | IFF (First 2 months of quarter)
59(1) — Monthly filers
GSTR-1 filed monthly OR quarterly on Common Portal (directly or through Facilitation Centre)

🆕 Option to amend outward supplies in GSTR-1A before filing GSTR-3B for that period
GSTR-1A amendment option — Inserted Notf. 12/2024-CT dt. 10.07.2024
59(2) — Quarterly filers: IFF
For first and second months of quarter — may furnish details via INVOICE FURNISHING FACILITY (IFF) up to cumulative ₹50 lakh per month
IFF: From 1st of succeeding month till 13th of that month
📝 QRMP scheme (Quarterly Return Monthly Payment) taxpayers — IFF avoids recipient having to wait till quarter-end for ITC availability.
59(6) — BLOCKING CONDITIONS (CANNOT file GSTR-1 if:)
(a) Previous month's GSTR-3B not filed
(b) Quarterly filer — previous tax period GSTR-3B not filed
(d) Rule 88C intimation issued — reply/deposit not done
(e) Rule 88D intimation for excess ITC — reply/payment not done
(f) Bank account details not furnished (Rule 10A)
🔍 GP Singh: These blocking conditions are coercive compliance mechanisms. Blocking GSTR-1 = blocking ITC for all recipients downstream. Revenue's leverage far exceeds the assessee's default in many cases — disproportion may be challenged.
What GSTR-1 / IFF / GSTR-1A Must Include:
CategoryDetails Required in GSTR-1Threshold Change (2024)
B2B inter-State & intra-StateInvoice-wise details for all registered recipients—
B2C inter-State (large invoices)Invoice-wise for unregistered recipients🆕 Threshold reduced from ₹2.5 lakh to ₹1 lakh — Notf. 12/2024-CT w.e.f. 01.08.2024
B2C inter-State (small invoices)Consolidated State-wise for unregistered recipients🆕 Consolidated below ₹1 lakh (earlier ₹2.5 lakh) — Notf. 12/2024-CT w.e.f. 01.08.2024
B2C intra-StateConsolidated rate-wise for unregistered recipients—
Credit/Debit NotesIssued during the month for invoices previously issued—
📋 Section: §37 | Cross-Ref: Rule 60 (GSTR-2B), Rule 61 (GSTR-3B), Rule 61A (quarterly option)
Rule 60 — Inward Supplies: GSTR-2A & GSTR-2B
📥 Rule 60 — Auto-Generated ITC Statements
Section 38 | GSTR-2A (Real-time) | GSTR-2B (Auto-generated statement for ITC)
Sub-RuleSource of DataAuto-Populated inKey Point
60(1)Supplier's GSTR-1 or GSTR-1A (🆕) or IFFGSTR-2A Part A (recipient's view)🆕 GSTR-1A also now populates GSTR-2A — Notf. 12/2024-CT. GSTR-4A (composition recipient), GSTR-6A (ISD recipient) also generated.
60(2)Non-Resident Taxable Person's GSTR-5GSTR-2A Part AImport of services from non-residents visible to recipient.
60(3)ISD's GSTR-6GSTR-2A Part BISD-distributed credit visible to recipient units.
60(4)TDS deductor's GSTR-7GSTR-2A Part CTDS credit available to deductee for claiming in ECL after filing of GSTR-7.
60(5)TCS collector's GSTR-8 (e-commerce operator)GSTR-2A Part CTCS credited to supplier's ECL. Supplier claims this TCS credit.
60(6)IGST paid on import of goods / DTA supplies from SEZ/SEZDGSTR-2A Part DImport IGST paid on Bill of Entry auto-populated — critical for ITC claim on imports.
60(7) — GSTR-2BAuto-generated statement for every month — source: GSTR-1/1A of suppliers + GSTR-5 (NRTP) + GSTR-6 (ISD) + Bill of Entry (imports) + IFF dataGSTR-2B (locked monthly ITC statement)🆕 GSTR-1A data now also feeds GSTR-2B — Notf. 12/2024-CT. GSTR-2B is the primary document for ITC claims. Available one day after GSTR-1 due date. ITC available in GSTR-2B = ITC that can be claimed in GSTR-3B.
💡 A manufacturer's ITC claim in GSTR-3B for January 2025 should be based on GSTR-2B for January 2025. If supplier has not filed GSTR-1, their invoice will not appear in GSTR-2B → ITC not available → Blocked ITC until supplier files. This is the "4-condition" framework under Section 16(2).
📋 Section: §38 | Cross-Ref: Rule 59 (GSTR-1), Rule 61 (GSTR-3B), §16(2) (ITC conditions)
Rule 61 — GSTR-3B: Monthly / Quarterly Return
📊 Rule 61 — GSTR-3B: Summary Return
Section 39(1) | Monthly (20th) | Quarterly QRMP Scheme
WHO MUST FILE GSTR-3B:
Every registered person EXCEPT: (i) Persons under §14 IGST Act, (ii) ISD, (iii) Non-resident taxable person, (iv) Person paying tax under §10 (Composition), (v) Person under §51 (TDS), (vi) Person under §52 (TCS)
MONTHLY FILERS — 61(1)(i)
GSTR-3B by 20th of succeeding month for each month
QUARTERLY (QRMP) — 61(1)(ii)
Due dates vary by State:
• Category 1 States (South/West): 22nd of month after quarter
• Category 2 States (North/East/Central): 24th of month after quarter
(See table of states in Rule 61)
PMT-06 — QRMP Tax Payment
For quarterly filers — tax for first two months of quarter must be deposited via FORM GST PMT-06 by 25th of succeeding month
Month 1: ECL balance may be used
Month 2: ECL balance excluding tax due for Month 1
61(2) — GSTR-3B must discharge liability towards tax/interest/penalty/fees by debiting Electronic Cash Ledger (ECL) or Electronic Credit Ledger
📋 Section: §39 | Cross-Ref: Rule 59 (GSTR-1), Rule 61A (quarterly option), Rule 88C (mismatch), Rule 88D (excess ITC)
Rule 61A — Opting for Quarterly Return (QRMP Scheme)
🔄 Rule 61A — QRMP Scheme: Quarterly Return Option
HOW TO OPT:
Indicate preference electronically on common portal from 1st day of 2nd month of preceding quarter till last day of 1st month of the quarter for which option is being exercised
CONTINUATION
Once opted — continues on quarterly basis for future periods UNLESS:
(a) Becomes ineligible (turnover >₹5 Cr), OR
(b) Actively opts for monthly basis on common portal
INELIGIBILITY CONDITION
If last return due on date of exercising option is NOT furnished → NOT eligible to opt for quarterly filing
📝 Cannot switch to quarterly if you have pending return. Clears returns first, then opts.
MANDATORY EXIT
If aggregate turnover exceeds ₹5 Cr during current FY → Must opt for monthly from first month of succeeding quarter
💡 Quarterly filer crosses ₹5 Cr in Q3 (Oct-Dec) → must switch to monthly from January of that year.
📋 Inserted: Notf. 82/2020-CT | Section: §39(1) proviso | Cross-Ref: Rule 61
Rule 62 — Composition Supplier Returns (CMP-08 & GSTR-4)
📋 Rule 62 — Composition Scheme Return Mechanism
FORM GST CMP-08
Quarterly statement of self-assessed tax payment
Due: 18th of month succeeding the quarter
📝 Composition taxpayer pays tax quarterly via CMP-08 — no monthly GSTR-3B filing.
FORM GSTR-4
Annual return for full financial year
Due: 30th April following end of FY
🆕 From FY 2024-25 onwards: 30th June following end of FY
Due date extended from 30 April to 30 June for FY 2024-25 onwards — Notf. 12/2024-CT dt. 10.07.2024
LIABILITY
Discharged only through Electronic Cash Ledger (ECL)
Composition taxpayers CANNOT use ITC (Electronic Credit Ledger)
🔍 GP Singh: No ITC under composition — "shall discharge" through ECL is mandatory. If excess ITC inadvertently taken before switching to composition → must be reversed under Rule 44.
TRANSITION: LEAVING COMPOSITION SCHEME
Voluntary withdrawal / officer-initiated withdrawal:
• File CMP-08 for the period under composition scheme — by 18th of succeeding month after quarter of withdrawal
• File GSTR-4 for that period — by 30 April (or 30 June from FY 2024-25) following end of that FY
📋 Section: §10 (Composition) | Cross-Ref: Rule 44 (ITC reversal on exit), §16 (ITC conditions)
Rules 63–67A — Special Category Returns (NRTP, OIDAR, ISD, TDS, TCS, Nil)
Rule Who Form Due Date Key Point
Rule 63 Non-Resident Taxable Person (NRTP) GSTR-5 Within 20 days after end of tax period OR within 7 days after last day of registration validity — whichever earlier NRTP has limited registration validity. Tax/interest/penalty/fees to be settled before return deadline.
Rule 64 OIDAR service providers from outside India + Online Money Gaming providers from outside India GSTR-5A By 20th of succeeding calendar month 🆕 Substantially amended by Notf. 51/2023-CT w.e.f. 01.10.2023 — Online Money Gaming from outside India now brought within Rule 64. Earlier only OIDAR was covered.
Rule 65 Input Service Distributor (ISD) GSTR-6 As prescribed (13th of succeeding month typically) ISD files GSTR-6 containing details of credit received and distributed under §20. Recipients' GSTR-2A Part B auto-populated from GSTR-6.
Rule 66 TDS Deductor (person required to deduct under §51) GSTR-7 🆕 On or before the TENTH of succeeding month (inserted Notf. 20/2024-CT w.e.f. 01.11.2024) 🆕 Specific due date "10th of month" now inserted in Rule 66 — Notf. 20/2024-CT. GSTR-7A certificate auto-generated for deductee. Deductee's TDS credit available in ECL after GSTR-7 validation.
Rule 67 E-Commerce Operator (TCS under §52) GSTR-8 10th of succeeding month TCS collected from registered suppliers — declared in GSTR-8. Auto-populated in GSTR-2A Part C for those suppliers. Suppliers claim TCS credit in ECL after validation.
Rule 67A All registered persons filing NIL returns GSTR-3B (Nil) / GSTR-1 (Nil) / CMP-08 (Nil) Same due dates as respective forms NIL return can be filed via SMS (Short Messaging Service) using registered mobile number + OTP. No portal login needed for nil filer.
Rule 68 Non-filers of returns FORM GSTR-3A (Notice) Issued electronically Notice issued to persons who fail to file §39, §44, §45, §52 returns. First step in default proceedings.
📋 Rules 69–77 omitted w.e.f. 01.10.2022 (Notf. 19/2022-CT) — matching provisions under §42, §43 repealed
Rule 78 — Matching: E-Commerce Operator (GSTR-8) vs Supplier (GSTR-1)
🔗 Rule 78 — E-Commerce Supply Matching
Details declared by ECO in FORM GSTR-8 (TCS collected) are MATCHED with corresponding details declared by the SUPPLIER in FORM GSTR-1 (🆕 as amended in FORM GSTR-1A if any)
"as amended in FORM GSTR-1A if any" — inserted by Notf. 12/2024-CT dt. 10.07.2024
Fields matched:
(a) State of Place of Supply
(b) Net Taxable Value
📝 If ECO declares ₹10 lakh as supplies through their platform for a registered supplier, but the supplier only shows ₹8 lakh in GSTR-1 — mismatch triggers notice to supplier.
📋 Section: §52 (TCS by ECO) | Rule 67 (GSTR-8)
Rule 80 — Annual Return (GSTR-9 / GSTR-9A / GSTR-9B / GSTR-9C)
Form Who Due Date Key Point
GSTR-9 All registered persons EXCEPT: (i) Persons under 2nd proviso to §44, (ii) ISD, (iii) TDS/TCS payers (§51/§52), (iv) Casual taxable person, (v) Non-resident taxable person 31st December following end of FY Annual reconciliation of all outward/inward supplies, ITC, tax paid. Discrepancy between GSTR-3B and GSTR-9 = demand risk.
GSTR-9A Person paying tax under §10 (Composition taxpayer) 31st December following end of FY Annual return for composition taxpayers — different format from GSTR-9.
GSTR-9B E-Commerce Operator required to collect TCS under §52 31st December following end of FY Annual statement of TCS collected and remitted.
GSTR-9C Registered persons with aggregate turnover > ₹5 Crore in the FY 31st December following end of FY (along with GSTR-9) Self-certified reconciliation statement (not CA-certified since 2021 amendment). Turnover threshold: >₹5 Cr. Below ₹5 Cr — optional. Extension for Tamil Nadu districts (cyclone areas) in FY 2022-23 — Notf. 02/2024-CT.
🔍 GP Singh on GSTR-9C: The self-certification requirement (replacing CA/CMA audit since 2021) means the registered person personally certifies reconciliation. False certification by the person = potential prosecution under §132 for false statement. The evidentiary value of GSTR-9C in adjudication proceedings is significant.
📋 Section: §44 | Substituted: Notf. 30/2021-CT | §44 second proviso — waiver for certain categories of persons by notification
Rules 81–84 — Final Return, UIN, & GST Practitioner
RuleSubjectKey ProvisionsLitigation Point
Rule 81 Final Return (§45) Every person required to furnish final return under §45 → GSTR-10 electronically through common portal / Facilitation Centre Filed upon cancellation of registration. Must file within 3 months of cancellation order or date of cancellation, whichever later. Non-filing → liability to pay tax + late fee.
Rule 82 UIN Holder Inward Supplies (§55) Person with Unique Identity Number (UIN) claiming refund of inward supply taxes → GSTR-11 along with refund application. For other purposes — GSTR-11 on demand of proper officer. UIN holders = UN agencies, embassies, consulates, notified bodies. Refund mechanism via GSTR-11.
Rule 83 GST Practitioner (GSTP) — Enrolment Eligibility: Indian citizen, sound mind, not insolvent, not convicted. Must satisfy ONE of: (a) Retired Group-B gazetted officer from Indirect Tax / Commercial Tax Dept (≥2 yrs), (b) Sales tax / return preparer ≥5 yrs, (c) Graduate in Commerce/Law/Banking/Business + passed CA/CMA/CS final exam or notified exam. Enrolment via Form GST PCT-01. Practitioner must pass NACIN exam (Rule 83A) within prescribed period. Non-compliance → disqualification from practice.
Rule 83: Activities GSTP can act for registered persons if authorised: (a) Furnish outward supply details, (b) File monthly/quarterly/annual/final return, (c) Make deposit to ECL, (d) File refund claim, (e) File amendment/cancellation of registration, (f) E-way bill information, (g) ITC-04 challan details, (h) Amendment/cancellation of enrolment under Rule 58, (i) Intimation for composition scheme entry/exit GSTP cannot act without written authorisation (PCT-05). Refund / registration applications filed by GSTP require confirmation from registered person before processing.
Rule 83B GSTP Surrender of Enrolment 🆕 GSTP may surrender enrolment via Form GST PCT-06 on common portal / Facilitation Centre. Commissioner / authorised officer may cancel enrolment by order in Form GST PCT-07 after enquiry. Inserted Notf. 33/2019-CT. Voluntary surrender mechanism now available for practitioners wishing to exit.
Rule 84 Conditions for Appearance No person eligible to appear before GST authority on behalf of registered/unregistered person unless enrolled under Rule 83. Must produce Form GST PCT-05 authorisation if required by the authority. Non-enrolled persons cannot represent others before GST adjudication/appellate forums. Only enrolled GSTPs or persons authorised under §116 (legal practitioners, CAs, CMAs, CSs, etc.) can appear.
📋 Section: §45 (Final Return), §55 (UIN), §48 (GST Practitioner), §116 (Authorised Representative)
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal — Rules 46–84 | Tax Invoice, Accounts & Returns
Statutory arguments for use in personal hearings before GST officers, Appellate Authority & GSTAT. State them confidently and cite the Rule/Section.
Rule 48(5) — Invoice Issued in Violation of e-Invoice Mandate = NOT an Invoice
CGST Rules, 2017 | Rule 48(5) | Inserted Notf. 68/2019-CT
Key Statutory Principle
Every invoice issued by a person to whom sub-rule (4) applies [e-invoice mandatory class] in any manner other than the manner specified in the said sub-rule shall not be treated as an invoice.
When to Use — When Department demands ITC on the basis of invoices which should have been e-invoices but were not registered on IRP. Argue: these documents are not "invoices" in law → ITC under §16(2)(a) requires a valid invoice → ITC claim on such documents falls. Equally, use in reverse: if assessee's supplier failed to generate e-invoice for B2B supply, assessee cannot be saddled with supplier's default for ITC denial — argue §16(2)(c) (tax paid by supplier) separately.
Rule 53(3) — "Input Tax Credit Not Admissible" Prominently on Demand Documents
CGST Rules, 2017 | Rule 53(3) | Applicable to §74, §129, §130 Proceedings
Key Statutory Principle
Any invoice or debit note issued in pursuance of any tax payable in accordance with the provisions of section 74 or section 129 or section 130 shall prominently contain the words "INPUT TAX CREDIT NOT ADMISSIBLE".
When to Use — (1) When fighting a demand under §74: If the original demand order or the debit note issued thereof does not carry this legend prominently → procedural defect. (2) When opposing ITC claim by another party based on invoice issued pursuant to §74/§129 demand — cite this rule to argue ITC is statutorily blocked. "Prominently" is mandatory — buried-in-fine-print compliance is insufficient.
Rule 56(6) — Goods at Undisclosed Premises = Deemed Supply
CGST Rules, 2017 | Rule 56(6) | Relevant in §67 Search & Seizure Proceedings
Key Statutory Principle
If any taxable goods are found to be stored at any place(s) other than those declared under sub-rule (5) without the cover of any valid documents, the proper officer shall determine the amount of tax payable on such goods as if such goods have been supplied by the registered person.
When to Use — When Department creates demand after detecting goods at third-party premises (job worker's, transporter's, undisclosed godown). Counter-argument: (a) Was Rule 10A(1) compliance done? (b) Does §143 job work exemption apply? (c) Is there a valid delivery challan under Rule 55? (d) Were the premises "other than declared" — did the taxpayer declare these premises at some point? Each argument can neutralise this deeming fiction.
Rule 59(6) — GSTR-1 Blocking: Disproportionate Coercive Mechanism
CGST Rules, 2017 | Rule 59(6) | Constitutionality Questioned by Various HCs
Key Statutory Argument
A registered person shall not be allowed to furnish the details of outward supplies in FORM GSTR-1 if he has not furnished the return in FORM GSTR-3B for the preceding month [Rule 59(6)(a)]. Similarly for Rule 88C/88D intimations.
When to Use — When GSTR-1 blocked causing recipient's ITC disruption: (1) Argue before proper officer that blocking impacts innocent third-party recipients — not just the defaulting taxpayer. (2) Approach High Court under Article 226 — blocking GSTR-1 for disputed (not admitted) liability under Rule 88C/88D is ultra vires §37. (3) Where blocking persists despite genuine dispute — file WP for lifting block pending adjudication.
Rule 47A — RCM Self-Invoice: 30-Day Mandatory Time Limit (NEW 2024)
CGST Rules, 2017 | Rule 47A | Inserted Notf. 20/2024-CT, w.e.f. 01.11.2024
Key Statutory Principle (2024 Amendment)
Notwithstanding anything contained in rule 47, where an invoice referred to in rule 46 is required to be issued under clause (f) of sub-section (3) of section 31 by a registered person [RCM recipient-issued invoice], he shall issue the said invoice within a period of thirty days from the date of receipt of the said supply.
When to Use — When Department denies ITC on RCM self-invoices on ground of delayed issuance: (1) For supplies received before 01.11.2024 — no specific time limit existed in rules → argue no disqualification for delay. (2) For supplies after 01.11.2024 — 30-day limit is mandatory but question: does late invoice = ITC denial or merely a procedural default with late fee? Argue that §16 conditions for ITC do not include timely issuance of self-invoice as a condition — only tax payment condition under §16(2)(c) is relevant.
Rule 46(s) Declaration + Rule 48(4)/(5): Non-e-Invoice Taxpayer Compliance
CGST Rules, 2017 | Rule 46(s) | Inserted Notf. 14/2022-CT | Rule 48(4)/(5)
Key Statutory Argument
A taxpayer whose aggregate turnover in any preceding financial year from 2017-18 onwards exceeds the notified e-invoice threshold, but who is yet not required to prepare invoice under Rule 48(4), must include on every invoice a declaration: "I/We hereby declare that though our aggregate turnover... is more than the notified turnover, we are not required to prepare an invoice in terms of the provisions of the said sub-rule."
When to Use — When Department alleges e-invoice non-compliance against a taxpayer above threshold who claims exemption (e.g., SEZ supplier, banking company, insurer, GTA, passenger transport): (1) First establish category of exemption from e-invoice. (2) Then argue that the Rule 46(s) declaration on the face of invoice itself is the legal compliance mechanism. (3) Absence of this declaration is a procedural defect in the invoice — but does not make the invoice void under Rule 48(5) since Rule 48(5) applies only to persons "to whom sub-rule (4) applies."
Rule 56(10) — Reverse Presumption: Documents Found at Third-Party Premises
CGST Rules, 2017 | Rule 56(10) | Applicable in Search/Survey Proceedings
Key Statutory Principle
Unless proved otherwise, if any documents, registers, or any books of account belonging to a registered person are found at any premises other than those mentioned in the certificate of registration, they shall be presumed to be maintained by the said registered person.
When to Use — When books/documents found at accountant's office or job worker's premises during search: (1) Presumption runs AGAINST the taxpayer. (2) To rebut: (a) Show documents belong to a different registered person, (b) Show they were placed there for legitimate purpose (audit, tax filing), (c) Show the "premises" was actually declared as additional place of business. This presumption can be rebutted — the words "unless proved otherwise" are the lifeline.
GSTR-2B — Primary Document for ITC Eligibility
CGST Rules, 2017 | Rule 60(7) | GSTR-2B: Auto-generated Statement
Key Statutory Argument
An auto-generated statement containing the details of input tax credit shall be made available to the registered person in FORM GSTR-2B, for every month, electronically through the common portal. [Rule 60(7)]. GSTR-2B is the basis for ITC claims consistent with §16(2)(aa).
When to Use — (1) When Department demands ITC reversal on basis of GSTR-2A mismatch: Argue GSTR-2B (not GSTR-2A) is the relevant document for §16(2)(aa) compliance. GSTR-2A is real-time but dynamic; GSTR-2B is the locked, period-specific document. (2) When supplier files GSTR-1 late but before GSTR-2B locking for that period → ITC available in GSTR-2B → permissible. (3) §16(2)(aa) inserted by Finance Act 2021 — GSTR-2B compliance = ITC eligibility. Ensure you argue based on GSTR-2B, not GSTR-2A.
For professional litigation use | Pawan Soni & Associates, Ichalkaranji | CA Pawankumar Soni
CGST Rules — Ch.IX–X (Rules 85–97A)

⚖️ Chapter IX & X — Payment of Tax & Refund | Rules 85–97A

Source: CGST Rules, 2017 (ICAI Bare Law, Nov 2025 Ed.) | CA Pawankumar Soni, Ichalkaranji

Permitted / Valid
Prohibited / Restricted
Condition / If / Unless
Optional / Discretionary
Neutral Reference
📝 Plain English
💡 Example
🔍 GP Singh Interpretation

📖 Legal Dictionary

🆕 2025 Amendment
📋 Index of Rules — Chapter IX & X

⚔️ Hearing Arsenal
Rule 85 — Electronic Liability Register (FORM GST PMT-01)
📒 Rule 85 — Electronic Liability Register
Linked to Section 49(7) CGST Act | FORM GST PMT-01
Maintained on Common Portal | Every person liable to pay tax / interest / penalty / late fee / any other amount
📝 Think of this as your GST "ledger of what you owe" — everything the government has a claim on you for goes in here first before any payment happens.
📌 DEBITS to Liability Register [Rule 85(2)]
85(2)(a)
Tax / interest / late fee / any other amount payable per return
85(2)(b)
Tax / interest / penalty as determined by proper officer in proceedings under the Act
(Substituted w.e.f. 01.10.2022)
85(2)(d)
Interest accruing from time to time
📝 Interest keeps getting debited continuously — not just at year end. Even if you haven't noticed it, the system is recording it.
📌 CREDITS to Liability Register
85(3)
Payment via ECL (Rule 86) or ECrL (Rule 87) per return → Liability register credited
85(4)
TDS (§51) / TCS (§52) / RCM / §10 composition / interest / penalty / fee — paid only via ECrL
85(5)
Demand in register reduced to extent of relief by appellate authority / GSTAT / court
💡 HC sets aside demand of ₹50L — liability register automatically credited ₹50L.
85(6)
Penalty reduced if taxable person pays tax + interest + penalty specified in SCN / demand order
📝 Reduced penalty applies only when you pay all three components as per the show cause notice — not partial payment.
85(7) Discrepancy noticed by registered person → communicate to jurisdictional officer via FORM GST PMT-04 through common portal
⚔️ Word War: "shall be debited" vs "shall be credited" — mandatory language. These are not administrative entries; they are legal events. Failure to credit after appellate relief creates actionable wrong against the Department.
📋 Corresponding: Section 49(7) CGST Act | FORM GST PMT-01, PMT-04
Rule 86 — Electronic Credit Ledger (FORM GST PMT-02)
💳 Rule 86 — Electronic Credit Ledger (ECL)
FORM GST PMT-02 | ITC Credited and Debited Here
📝 ECL is your ITC wallet. Every rupee of Input Tax Credit you are eligible for gets credited here. You can only use this to pay output tax — not for interest, penalty, or late fee.
86(1)
CREDIT: Every claim of ITC under the Act → credited to ECL
💡 You buy raw materials with GST of ₹10L — ₹10L gets credited to your ECL on GSTR-2B matching.
86(2)
DEBIT: Discharge of liability u/s 49 / 49A / 49B
86(3)
Refund claimed of unutilised ITC u/s 54 → debited from ECL to extent of claim
⟳ Re-credit of ECL — When Does Credit Come Back?
86(4)
Refund rejected (fully/partly) → amount debited re-credited via FORM GST PMT-03 by proper officer
86(4A)
Tax paid wrongly or in excess from ECL → if found admissible → re-credited via PMT-03
86(4B)
Erroneous refund deposited back (DRC-03) → equivalent amount re-credited to ECL via PMT-03A
(Omission of sub-rule (10) of rule 96 reference via Notf. 20/2024)
86(5) Save as provided in this Chapter — NO entry shall be made directly in ECL under any circumstance
🔍 GP Singh: "Shall" in Rule 86(5) is absolute prohibition — any direct entry in ECL without following the prescribed process is void. Revenue cannot unilaterally debit ECL without following Rule 86A procedure.
Explanation: Refund is "rejected" if: (a) appeal finally rejected, or (b) claimant gives undertaking to proper officer not to file appeal
📋 Corresponding: Section 49, 49A, 49B CGST Act | FORM GST PMT-02, PMT-03, PMT-03A, PMT-04
Rule 86A — Conditions of Use of Amount Available in ECL (Blocking of ITC)
🚫 Rule 86A — ITC Blocking Power
Commissioner / Authorised Officer — Not below Asst. Commissioner | Inserted w.e.f. 26.12.2019
📝 This rule gives the Commissioner power to freeze your ITC wallet temporarily if he has reason to believe the ITC was obtained fraudulently. You cannot use the blocked amount to pay tax or claim refund until the block is lifted.
Trigger: "Reasons to Believe" — Credit of ITC is Fraudulent / Ineligible
📖 Legal Dictionary: "Reasons to Believe" — Not mere suspicion; requires objective material. SC in Siemens Ltd. held that reasons must be based on relevant material. Must be recorded in WRITING — oral reasons not sufficient for Rule 86A action.
Grounds for Blocking [86A(1)] — Any of the following:
(a)(i)
Invoice/debit note from non-existent registered person or person not conducting business from registered place
(a)(ii)
Invoice/debit note without actual receipt of goods or services
💡 Fake invoices (bill trading) — goods never moved but ITC claimed.
(b)
Tax on supply NOT paid to Government by supplier
(c)
Registered person availing credit is non-existent or not actually conducting business
(d)
Person is not in possession of tax invoice / debit note / prescribed document under Rule 36
86A(1)
Effect: NOT allow debit of equivalent amount from ECL for tax payment or refund claim
Reasons must be recorded in writing
🔍 GP Singh: Strict construction — "may not allow" is not absolute ban; officer still must apply mind to specific amount of disputed ITC. Blanket blocking of entire ECL without specifying amount is bad in law.
86A(2)
Lifting of block: Commissioner satisfied that conditions no longer exist → allow such debit
86A(3)
Automatic expiry: Restriction ceases after 1 year from date of imposition
📝 Even if the Department does not lift the block, it automatically expires after 1 year. Mark the date when block was imposed — key for litigation.
📋 Inserted: Notification No. 75/2019-CT dated 26.12.2019 | Corresponding: Section 49 CGST Act
Rule 86B — Restriction on Use of ECL (99% Cash Rule)
⚠️ Rule 86B — 99% Cash Payment Restriction
Inserted w.e.f. 01.01.2021 | Applies to Large Taxpayers
📝 If your taxable supply (excluding exempt and zero-rated) in a month exceeds ₹50 lakh, you cannot pay more than 99% of your output tax from ITC — at least 1% must come from cash. This prevents ITC-only payment by large traders.
TRIGGER Taxable supply (excl. exempt + zero-rated) in a month > ₹50 Lakh
→ Cannot use ECL to discharge more than 99% of output tax liability
✅ EXCEPTIONS — Rule 86B Does NOT Apply if ANY of the following:
(a)
Person / proprietor / karta / MD / partners / WTD / committee members paid > ₹1 lakh income tax in each of the last 2 financial years (for which ITR filing time expired)
(b)
Refund received > ₹1 lakh in preceding FY on account of unutilised ITC — zero rated exports [§54(3)(i)]
(c)
Refund received > ₹1 lakh in preceding FY on account of unutilised ITC — inverted duty structure [§54(3)(ii)]
(d)
Paid output tax through ECrL (cash) for > 1% of total output liability cumulatively till the said month in current FY
📝 If cumulatively you've already paid 1%+ in cash throughout the year, the restriction doesn't apply for that month.
(e)
Registered person is:
(i) Government Department
(ii) PSU
(iii) Local Authority
(iv) Statutory Body
RELAXATION Commissioner / authorised officer may remove this restriction after verifications and safeguards as deemed fit
💡 A textile manufacturer with ₹2 Cr/month supply but no IT returns filed by proprietor → Rule 86B applies → must pay ₹2L+ in cash even if ECL has sufficient balance. Non-compliance → return filing blocked / demand raised.
📋 Inserted: Notification No. 94/2020-CT dated 22.12.2020 w.e.f. 01.01.2021 | Corresponding: Section 49 CGST Act
Rule 87 — Electronic Cash Ledger (FORM GST PMT-05 / PMT-06)
Sub-ruleProvisionKey Condition / DetailPlain English / Litigation Point
87(1) ECrL maintained in FORM GST PMT-05 for each person liable to pay tax Credits deposits; Debits payments of tax/interest/penalty/fee/other amounts 📝 Your GST cash wallet — deposits in, payments out.
87(2) Generate challan in FORM GST PMT-06 on common portal Challan valid for 15 days from generation ⚠️ Expired challan = payment not credited. Always check validity before making NEFT/RTGS payment.
87(3) Modes of payment: (i) Internet Banking; (ia) UPI; (ib) IMPS; (ii) Credit/Debit card; (iii) NEFT/RTGS; (iv) OTC up to ₹10,000/challan/tax period UPI and IMPS inserted by Notf. 14/2022 — useful for small payments on the go.
87(4) Unregistered person → pays based on temporary ID generated via common portal as per Rule 16A Inserted by Notf. 07/2025 — date yet to be notified 🆕 2025 AMENDMENT — Rule 16A reference inserted by Notification No. 07/2025-CT dated 23.01.2025. Operationalised when notified.
87(5) NEFT/RTGS/IMPS — mandate form generated with challan; submit to bank Mandate form valid for 15 days from challan generation 📝 For NEFT/RTGS, don't just pay — also submit mandate form to bank.
87(6) On successful credit to Govt. account → Challan Identification Number (CIN) generated by collecting bank CIN = proof of deposit Always save CIN. It is the link between your bank debit and GST credit ledger entry.
87(7) CIN received → ECrL credited; receipt available on common portal — Automatic crediting — no manual action required if bank communicates CIN.
87(8) Bank account debited but CIN not generated / not communicated → taxpayer can represent via FORM GST PMT-07 RBI e-Scroll: ECrL may be updated on basis of RBI e-Scroll if details match PMT-06 challan (Notf. 26/2022) ⚠️ Critical for litigation: Bank failure ≠ taxpayer default. File PMT-07 immediately. RBI e-Scroll route protects taxpayer from being penalised for bank failure.
87(9) TDS/TCS amount claimed by registered person → credited to his ECrL — TDS/TCS appears in ECrL automatically once deductor files GSTR-7.
87(13) Transfer of amount in ECrL to another tax head (IGST/CGST/SGST/UTGST/Cess) or to another distinct person's ECrL Via FORM GST PMT-09
Transfer to distinct person: No transfer allowed if liability exists in liability register		 📝 If you paid CGST by mistake but IGST was payable — use PMT-09 to transfer within the same ECrL. For inter-entity transfer to distinct person — clear all outstanding first.
📋 FORMS: GST PMT-04, PMT-05, PMT-06, PMT-07, PMT-09 | Section 49(1) CGST Act
Rule 88 — Identification Number for Each Transaction
🔢 Rule 88 — Unique Transaction ID Numbers
88(1)
Unique ID generated for each debit/credit to ECrL or ECL
88(2)
Unique ID for discharge of liability → indicated in electronic liability register
88(3)
Unique ID for credits to liability register for reasons OTHER than discharge of liability
📝 Every entry in any GST ledger has a unique ID — like a transaction reference number. This creates an audit trail that cannot be tampered with.
Rule 88A — Order of Utilisation of Input Tax Credit
🔄 Rule 88A — ITC Utilisation Sequence
Inserted: Notification No. 16/2019-CT dated 29.03.2019
📝 This rule tells you which ITC to use first when paying GST. IGST ITC must be used before CGST or SGST ITC — compulsorily. But CGST/SGST ITC can be used in any order only after all IGST ITC is exhausted.
Step 1 — IGST ITC (MANDATORY FIRST USE)
First: towards payment of IGST
Balance remaining → towards CGST or SGST/UTGST in any order
Step 2 — CGST / SGST / UTGST ITC
CONDITION CGST / SGST / UTGST ITC can be used towards IGST / CGST / SGST / UTGST — ONLY AFTER IGST ITC is fully utilised
💡 You have IGST ITC ₹5L and CGST ITC ₹3L. Output: IGST ₹4L + CGST ₹2L. Correct: First use IGST ITC for IGST ₹4L, balance IGST ITC ₹1L for CGST ₹1L, then CGST ITC ₹1L for remaining CGST ₹1L. Wrong: Using CGST ITC first while IGST ITC exists.
⚔️ Word War: "shall first be utilised" vs "may be utilised" — IGST utilisation is MANDATORY (shall), cross utilisation of CGST/SGST is permissive (may). Non-compliance with sequence creates ITC utilisation errors leading to demand + interest.
📋 Inserted: Notification No. 16/2019-CT dated 29.03.2019 | Corresponding: Section 49, 49A, 49B CGST Act
Rule 88B — Manner of Calculating Interest on Delayed Payment of Tax
📊 Rule 88B — Interest Calculation Rules
Inserted w.e.f. 01.07.2017 | Amended by Notf. 12/2024 & 20/2024
88B(1)
Case 1: Late Return Filing
Supplies declared in return, return filed late (not after §73/74/74A proceedings)
Interest on: Tax paid via ECrL (Cash) only
Period: Days delayed beyond due date
Rate: As notified u/s 50(1)
📝 If you paid via ITC, no interest. Only cash-paid tax portion attracts interest for late filing.
Notf. 12/2024: If amount credited in ECrL on/before due date and debited while filing return — that amount NOT counted for interest even if filing is late.
88B(2)
Case 2: All Other Cases
(including post-adjudication, investigation-based, etc.)
Interest on: Entire tax remaining unpaid
Period: Due date of payment → date of payment
Rate: As notified u/s 50(1)
📝 For demands confirmed in proceedings — interest on entire unpaid tax, not just cash portion. ECL ITC utilisation after demand does not save interest.
88B(3)
Case 3: Wrong ITC Availed & Utilised
[Section 50(3)]
Interest on: Amount of ITC wrongly availed and utilised
Period: Date of utilisation → date of reversal / tax payment
Rate: As notified u/s 50(3) — higher rate
📝 Wrong ITC utilised attracts higher interest under §50(3). "Utilised" is determined by when ECL balance falls below wrong ITC amount.
EXPLANATION — Utilisation of Wrong ITC
ITC wrongly availed is deemed "utilised" when ECL balance falls below the wrong ITC amount → extent of fall = amount utilised.
Date of utilisation = (a) date return due / date of actual filing (earlier) if balance falls due to return payment; OR (b) date of debit in all other cases.
💡 Wrongly availed ITC ₹10L. ECL balance ₹8L on return filing date. Balance falls below ₹10L → ₹2L deemed utilised on return due date/filing date (whichever earlier). Interest at 24% p.a. on ₹2L from that date.
📋 Inserted: Notf. 14/2022-CT dated 05.07.2022 w.e.f. 01.07.2017 | Amended: Notf. 12/2024 | Corresponding: Section 50 CGST Act
Rule 88C — GSTR-1 vs GSTR-3B Output Liability Mismatch
⚡ Rule 88C — Mismatch: GSTR-1/1A Output Tax > GSTR-3B Declared Tax
Inserted: Notification No. 26/2022-CT dated 26.12.2022
📝 If you show higher sales in GSTR-1 but pay lower tax in GSTR-3B — the system catches this automatically and sends you an intimation asking you to explain or pay the difference within 7 days.
TRIGGER GSTR-1 (incl. GSTR-1A if any) output tax > GSTR-3B tax payable
by such amount and percentage as recommended by GST Council
Process Flow — FORM GST DRC-01B
Step 1
System intimates registered person of difference via Part A of FORM GST DRC-01B electronically + email
Direction: Pay differential or explain within 7 days
Step 2 — Two Options
(a) Pay differential tax + interest via DRC-03 → furnish details in Part B of DRC-01B; OR
(b) Furnish reply with reasons for unpaid differential in Part C of DRC-01B
Step 3 — If No Action
Amount remains unpaid + no explanation / explanation not acceptable
→ Amount recoverable u/s 79 CGST Act
📝 §79 recovery = attachment, garnishment — the toughest collection tool. Act within 7 days to avoid this.
📋 Inserted: Notification No. 26/2022-CT dated 26.12.2022 | FORM GST DRC-01B | Section 79 CGST Act
Rule 88D — GSTR-2B vs GSTR-3B ITC Availed Mismatch
⚡ Rule 88D — Mismatch: ITC in GSTR-3B > ITC Available in GSTR-2B
Inserted: Notification No. 38/2023-CT dated 04.08.2023
