📝 If you claim more ITC in GSTR-3B than what shows in your GSTR-2B (auto-populated statement), the system sends an intimation. You must either pay back the excess or explain why your ITC is correct within 7 days.
TRIGGER ITC availed in GSTR-3B > ITC available in GSTR-2B
by such amount and percentage as recommended by Council
Process Flow — FORM GST DRC-01C
Step 1
Intimation via Part A of FORM GST DRC-01C + email
Direction: Pay excess ITC + interest or explain within 7 days
Step 2 — Two Options
(a) Pay excess ITC (fully/partly) + interest via DRC-03 → furnish in Part B of DRC-01C; OR
(b) Furnish reply with reasons in Part C of DRC-01C
Step 3 — If No Action
Amount not paid + no explanation / not acceptable
→ Amount liable to be demanded u/s 73 or s 74 or s 74A
(74A inserted by Notf. 20/2024 w.e.f. 01.11.2024)
📝 §74A demands carry higher penalty. An auto-system flag under Rule 88D can escalate to a full SCN under §74A — respond carefully within 7 days.
Section 74A reference inserted by Notification No. 20/2024-CT dated 08.10.2024, w.e.f. 01.11.2024 — escalation path now includes new §74A proceedings (for cases involving fraud/suppression with extended limitation).
📋 Inserted: Notification No. 38/2023-CT dated 04.08.2023 | FORM GST DRC-01C | Section 73/74/74A CGST Act
Rule 89 — Application for Refund (FORM GST RFD-01) | Chapter X
💰 Rule 89 — Refund Application
FORM GST RFD-01 | Chapter X begins
Who can apply? — Any person EXCEPT persons covered under notification u/s 55 (UN, consulates, etc. — they use Rule 95 / RFD-10)
📝 Almost everyone can file for refund under Rule 89 — exporters, inverted duty claimants, excess tax payment cases. Only diplomatic/UN bodies use a separate Rule 95 route.
What can be claimed? — Refund of:
Balance in ECrL u/s 49(6)
Tax paid
Interest paid
Penalty paid
Fees paid
Any other amount paid
EXCEPTIONS IGST paid on goods exported out of India → not filed here → use Rule 96 (shipping bill deemed application)
Special Cases for Filing Refund:
SEZ Supplies
(a) Goods: Filed by supplier after SEZ admission endorsed by specified officer
(b) Services: Filed by supplier with endorsement of receipt + authorised ops
Deemed Exports
Filed by: Recipient (preferably) OR Supplier (if recipient doesn't avail ITC and furnishes undertaking)
Casual/Non-Resident
Refund only after last return filed; adjusted against advance tax u/s 27
89(1A) Person who paid tax on intra-State supply later held to be inter-State → apply for refund u/s 77 within 2 years from date of payment of tax on inter-State supply
(Inserted by Notf. 35/2021)
💡 Paid CGST+SGST on service thinking it's intra-State. Later held to be inter-State → claim IGST refund u/s 77 within 2 years. File RFD-01 under this sub-rule.
89(1B) Refund of additional IGST paid on account of upward revision in export price (post-refund sanction under Rule 96) → File RFD-01 within 2 years from relevant date u/s 54
(Inserted by Notf. 12/2024)
Notf. 12/2024-CT dated 10.07.2024: New sub-rule (1B) allows refund of additional IGST when export price is revised upward after original refund was sanctioned under Rule 96.
📋 FORM GST RFD-01 | Subject to Rule 10B (Aadhaar authentication) | Section 54 CGST Act
Rule 89(2) — Documents Required with Refund Application (Annexure 1 of RFD-01)
ClauseRefund Claim TypeDocument RequiredNotes
(a)Order-based refund (appellate / court / §107(6) / §112(8))Reference number of order + copy of order + payment referenceCovers pre-deposit refund on successful appeal
(b)Export of goodsShipping bill/bill of export number, date + relevant export invoice number/dateExcludes electricity exports
(ba)Export of electricityInvoice details, scheduled energy statement (CERC grid code), agreement for tariff🆕 Inserted by Notf. 14/2022
(bb) & (bc)Upward price revision on exports (post-refund)Export invoices, BRC/FIRC, supplementary invoices/debit notes, payment proof of additional IGST, CA/CMA certificate, contract documents🆕 Inserted by Notf. 12/2024 — new category for export price revision cases
(c)Export of servicesInvoice number/date + BRC/FIRC detailsForeign exchange realisation proof
(d)SEZ supply — goodsInvoice number/date + SEZ endorsement as per Rule 46Endorsement by specified officer of SEZ
(e)SEZ supply — servicesInvoice + SEZ endorsement + payment proof by recipient for authorised operationsProof of authorised operations is critical
(f)SEZ supply (goods or services)Declaration — tax not collected from SEZ unit / developerMust state tax not collected — missing declaration = rejection
(g)Deemed exportsInvoice number/date + such other evidence as notified—
(h)Inverted duty structure ITC refundInvoices received and issued during tax periodRate of tax on inputs > rate on outputs
(i)Finalisation of provisional assessmentReference number + copy of final assessment order—
(j)Intra-State → inter-State reclassification (§77)Statement of transactions considered as intra-State subsequently held inter-State—
(k)Excess payment of tax (and interest)Statement showing excess payment details"And interest, if any" — inserted by Notf. 38/2023
(ka) & (kb)Unregistered person — cancelled/terminated service agreementInvoice copies, payment proof, agreement/registered agreement, cancellation letter, CA/CMA certificate from supplier🆕 Inserted by Notf. 26/2022 — new category for unregistered buyers
(l)All claims ≤ ₹2 lakhSelf-declaration — incidence of tax not passed on to any other personNot required for §54(8)(a)(b)(c)(d)(f) cases
(m)All claims > ₹2 lakhCA/CMA Certificate — incidence not passed on (Annexure 2 of RFD-01)Not required for §54(8) exceptions; Not required for unregistered persons bearing tax incidence (Notf. 26/2022)
📋 FORM GST RFD-01, Annexure 1 & 2 | Section 54 CGST Act
Rule 89(4) & (5) — Refund Formulas: Zero-Rated & Inverted Duty Structure
📐 Rule 89(4) — Zero-Rated Supply (Export) ITC Refund Formula
Supply without payment of tax under Bond/LUT
Refund Amount =
(Turnover of zero-rated supply of goods + Turnover of zero-rated supply of services) × Net ITC
÷ Adjusted Total Turnover
Net ITC
ITC availed on inputs and input services during relevant period
Turnover ZR Goods
Value of ZR supply of goods made without tax (Bond/LUT); or 1.5x domestic value, whichever less
Turnover ZR Services
Payments received in relevant period for ZR services + supply completed for advance received earlier − advance received for supply not yet completed
Adjusted Total Turnover
= State turnover (excl. service turnover) + ZR service turnover + non-ZR service turnover LESS exempt supplies (other than ZR) during relevant period
📝 Adjusted Turnover excludes exempt supplies — so it doesn't dilute the refund by including non-taxable supplies.
EXPORT VALUE NOTE (Notf. 14/2022)
FOB value in Shipping Bill OR Invoice/bill of supply value — whichever is less
📐 Rule 89(5) — Inverted Duty Structure ITC Refund Formula
Maximum Refund Amount =
{(Turnover of inverted rated supply of goods and services) × Net ITC ÷ Adjusted Total Turnover}
LESS
{tax payable on such inverted rated supply of goods and services × (Net ITC ÷ ITC availed on inputs and input services)}
Net ITC: ITC availed on inputs during relevant period (Note: Capital goods ITC not included)
Adjusted Total Turnover & Relevant Period: Same as Rule 89(4)
📝 Inverted duty = Input rate > Output rate. Net ITC here means only inputs (not input services). The formula reduces refund by the proportion of output tax attributable to ITC — ensuring you don't get refund on tax you already paid on output.
💡 Textile manufacturer: Inputs at 12% GST, output fabric at 5%. Higher input rate → ITC accumulates → eligible for inverted duty refund via Rule 89(5). Include only input ITC (raw material), not capital goods ITC in Net ITC.
Rule 90 — Acknowledgement & Scrutiny of Refund Application
📋 Rule 90 — Acknowledgement & Deficiency Communication
90(1)
ECrL Refund Claims
Acknowledgement (RFD-02) auto-generated on portal
Limitation period u/s 54(7) counted from date of filing
90(2)
Other Refund Claims
Application forwarded to proper officer
Scrutiny within 15 days of filing
If complete → RFD-02 acknowledgement issued
Clock for §54(7) starts from date of filing
📝 The 60-day (or 30-day for provisional) timeline under §54 starts from the date on RFD-02 — so get the acknowledgement issued promptly.
90(3)
Deficiencies Found
Communicated via FORM GST RFD-03
Fresh application after rectification
Proviso (Notf. 15/2021)
Period from RFD-01 filing to RFD-03 communication excluded from 2-year limitation for fresh refund claim
90(5) & (6) Withdrawal of refund application before RFD-04/06/05/07/08 issuance → File FORM GST RFD-01W → All debits from ECL/ECrL re-credited
(Inserted by Notf. 15/2021)
📋 FORM GST RFD-02 (acknowledgement), RFD-03 (deficiency), RFD-01W (withdrawal)
Rule 91 — Grant of Provisional Refund
⚡ Rule 91 — Provisional Refund [Section 54(6)]
90% of Claimed Refund Upfront
📝 Before the full scrutiny is done, the government can give you 90% of your claimed refund on a provisional basis — so your cash flow is not stuck for months.
CONDITION Person claiming provisional refund must NOT have been prosecuted for any offence under the Act / existing law where tax evaded > ₹250 lakh, in any of the immediately preceding 5 years
91(2) Proper officer makes order in FORM GST RFD-04 within 7 days of acknowledgement under Rule 90(1) or 90(2)
Based on system-generated risk identification and evaluation
Order not required to be revalidated
(Substituted by Notf. 13/2025-CT dated 17.09.2025 w.e.f. 01.10.2025)
🆕 2025 AMENDMENT — Rule 91(2) substituted by Notification No. 13/2025-CT dated 17.09.2025 w.e.f. 01.10.2025: Provisional refund now based on SYSTEM-GENERATED risk evaluation (not prima facie officer satisfaction). Officer may still decline provisional refund for reasons recorded in writing and proceed directly to Rule 92 order.
91(3) Payment order in FORM GST RFD-05 issued → amount electronically credited to bank account
RFD-05 must be revalidated if refund not disbursed within same financial year
91(4) Central Government disburses based on consolidated payment advice
🔍 GP Singh: "Shall make an order within 7 days" — mandatory timeline. If officer fails to act within 7 days, deemed to have accepted prima facie claim. Delay by officer is actionable for mandamus / interest under §56.
📋 FORM GST RFD-04 (provisional sanction), RFD-05 (payment order) | Section 54(6) CGST Act
Rule 92 — Order Sanctioning Final Refund
✅ Rule 92 — Final Refund Sanction Order (FORM GST RFD-06)
92(1)
Proper officer satisfied that refund u/s 54(5) is due
→ Order in RFD-06 mentioning: amount admissible, provisional amount already paid, adjustment against outstanding demand, balance refundable
92(1A)
Tax paid otherwise than zero-rated/deemed export → refund sanctioned proportionate to cash payment vs total payment for that period
Credit ledger portion → re-credited via PMT-03
📝 If you paid partly by ITC and partly by cash — refund in cash (RFD-06) only for cash portion; ITC portion goes back to ECL via PMT-03.
92(2)
Refund liable to be withheld u/s 54(10)/(11)
→ Order in Part A of RFD-07 with reasons for withholding
On satisfaction — release order in Part B of RFD-07
92(3)
Proper officer NOT satisfied — whole/part not admissible
→ Issue notice in RFD-08 (15 days for reply in RFD-09)
→ After reply: Order in RFD-06
No rejection without hearing opportunity
🔍 GP Singh: "No application shall be rejected without giving opportunity of being heard" — mandatory natural justice. Any refund rejection without hearing = void order.
92(4)
Amount refundable to applicant u/s 54(8)
→ Order in RFD-06 + Payment order in RFD-05
RFD-06 not required to be revalidated
RFD-05 requires revalidation if disbursement delayed to next FY
92(5)
Amount NOT payable to applicant u/s 54(8)
→ Order in RFD-06 + Payment order in RFD-05
Amount credited to Consumer Welfare Fund
📋 FORM GST RFD-06 (final order), RFD-07 (withhold/release), RFD-08 (notice), RFD-09 (reply), PMT-03 | Section 54 CGST Act
Rule 93 — Credit of Rejected Refund Claim Back to ECL
🔄 Rule 93 — Re-credit on Refund Rejection
93(1)
Deficiencies communicated under Rule 90(3)
→ Amount debited u/Rule 89(3) re-credited to ECL
93(2)
Refund rejected under Rule 92 (fully/partly)
→ Amount debited, to extent of rejection, re-credited to ECL via order in FORM GST PMT-03
Explanation Refund deemed rejected if: (a) appeal finally rejected, OR (b) claimant gives written undertaking to proper officer not to file appeal
📝 Critical for litigation: Don't give an undertaking not to appeal without careful thought — once given, the ITC gets re-credited but the door to further contest closes.
📋 FORM GST PMT-03 | Section 54 CGST Act
Rule 94 — Interest on Delayed Refund [Section 56]
💰 Rule 94 — Interest on Delayed Refund
Section 56 CGST Act | Amended by Notf. 38/2023 w.e.f. 01.10.2023
📝 If the government doesn't refund within 60 days of application, interest is automatically payable. Rule 94 tells the officer how to calculate and pay this interest — it is not discretionary.
94(1) Interest due u/s 56 → proper officer makes order along with Payment Order in RFD-05
States: amount of delayed refund, period of delay, amount of interest
Electronically credited to applicant's bank account
94(2) — Periods EXCLUDED from Delay Calculation (Notf. 38/2023)
94(2)(a)
Period beyond 15 days of RFD-08 notice that applicant takes to furnish reply in RFD-09 or submit additional documents
📝 If you take 30 days to reply to the show cause notice (beyond the 15 day mark), those extra 15 days are NOT included in calculating government's delay.
94(2)(b)
Period taken by applicant to furnish correct bank account details or validate the bank account furnished
🔍 GP Singh: §56 interest is mandatory — not directory. Once 60 days elapse from filing, interest accrues automatically. Government cannot avoid interest by arguing officer's workload or system issues.
📋 FORM GST RFD-05 | Section 56 CGST Act | Renumbered/amended by Notf. 38/2023-CT w.e.f. 01.10.2023
Rule 95 — Refund to Special Persons (UN, Consulates, etc.)
🏛️ Rule 95 — Refund of Tax to Notified Persons [Section 55]
UN Agencies, Foreign Consulates, Diplomatic Bodies
95(1) Application in FORM GST RFD-10 once per quarter | Electronically on common portal | Along with FORM GSTR-11 (statement of inward supplies)
Conditions for Refund — ALL must be satisfied:
(a)
Inward supplies received from registered person against tax invoice
(b)
Name and GSTIN / Unique Identity Number of applicant mentioned in tax invoice
(c)
Such other restrictions / conditions in the notification are satisfied
Proviso (Notf. 40/2021) If UIN not mentioned in invoice → refund available only if copy of invoice (attested by authorised representative) submitted along with RFD-10
95(5) Treaty / International Agreement takes precedence over this Chapter if inconsistent
📋 FORM GST RFD-10, GSTR-11 | Section 55 CGST Act | Rule 92 applies mutatis mutandis for sanction
Rule 95B — Refund to Canteen Stores Department (CSD)
🪖 Rule 95B — CSD Refund
Ministry of Defence | Inserted by Notf. 12/2024-CT dated 10.07.2024
🆕 2025 AMENDMENT — Rule 95B inserted by Notification No. 12/2024-CT dated 10.07.2024 — new rule for Canteen Stores Department refund.
95B(1) CSD eligible for 50% of applicable central tax paid on inward supplies of goods received for subsequent supply to Unit Run Canteens / authorised customers
HOW Apply in FORM GST RFD-10A once per quarter electronically on common portal
Dealt like RFD-01 application in accordance with Rule 89
Conditions — all three must be met:
(a) Inward supplies received from registered person against invoice + GSTR-1 + GSTR-3B filed by supplier
(b) CSD's name and GSTIN mentioned in tax invoice
(c) Goods received for subsequent supply to Unit Run Canteens / authorised customers
📋 FORM GST RFD-10A | Inserted: Notification No. 12/2024-CT dated 10.07.2024
Rule 96 — Refund of IGST Paid on Goods / Services Exported Out of India
🌍 Rule 96 — IGST Export Refund
Shipping Bill = Deemed Refund Application
📝 For goods exported with payment of IGST, you don't file a separate refund application. Your shipping bill itself is treated as the refund application — the Customs system and GST system work together automatically to process your refund.
Shipping Bill = Deemed Application [Rule 96(1)] — Conditions for deemed filing:
(a)
Export manifest / report filed by person in charge of conveyance covering shipping bill / bill of export number and date
(b)
Applicant furnished valid GSTR-3B
Mismatch between GSTR-1/1A and Shipping Bill → deemed filed when mismatch rectified by exporter
(c)
Applicant undergone Aadhaar authentication per Rule 10B
(Inserted by Notf. 35/2021 w.e.f. 01.01.2022)
Withholding of Refund [Rule 96(4)] — Any of:
(a)
Request received from jurisdictional Commissioner to withhold u/s 54(10) or 54(11)
(b)
Customs officer determines goods exported in violation of Customs Act, 1962
(c)
Commissioner/Board officer, based on data analysis and risk parameters, considers verification of exporter credentials (incl. ITC availed) essential to safeguard revenue
(Inserted by Notf. 14/2022)
96(5A)/(5B)/(5C) Withheld refund transmitted electronically to GST proper officer via system-generated FORM GST RFD-01 → dealt under Rule 89
96(8) Central Govt. may pay refund directly to Government of Bhutan for exports to Bhutan for notified class of goods → exporter NOT paid in such case
96(9) IGST refund for services exported → file application in FORM GST RFD-01 → dealt per Rule 89 provisions
📋 Shipping Bill = Deemed RFD-01 | FORM GST RFD-01 (for withheld/services) | Section 54 CGST Act | Rule 10B (Aadhaar)
Rule 96A — Export under Bond / Letter of Undertaking (LUT)
📜 Rule 96A — Export of Goods/Services without Payment of IGST
Bond or LUT in FORM GST RFD-11 | Inserted by Notf. 15/2017
📝 If you want to export without paying IGST first (and then claiming refund later), you furnish a Bond or LUT. This is a promise that if goods are not exported or payment is not received, you will pay the tax + interest.
96A(1) Furnish Bond or LUT in FORM GST RFD-11 to jurisdictional Commissioner prior to export
Binding to pay tax + interest u/s 50(1) within:
GOODS
15 days after expiry of 3 months (or extended period by Commissioner) from invoice date — if goods not exported
SERVICES
15 days after expiry of 1 year (or FEMA permitted period + extension by RBI, whichever later) from invoice date — if payment not received in convertible foreign exchange / Indian rupees (where RBI permits)
(Substituted by Notf. 12/2024)
Notf. 12/2024: Service export payment period now linked to FEMA period including RBI extension — more taxpayer-friendly.
CONSEQUENCE Goods not exported within time + no payment → export allowed under LUT withdrawn forthwith + amount recovered u/s 79
RESTORATION Export privilege restored immediately when registered person pays amount due [96A(4)]
96A(6) Provisions apply mutatis mutandis for zero-rated supply to SEZ developer/unit without IGST payment
⚔️ Word War: "forthwith" in Rule 96A(3) vs "immediately" in Rule 96A(4) — "forthwith" means without any delay on withdrawal; "immediately" means instant restoration on payment. Courts have held "forthwith" = same day action required.
📋 FORM GST RFD-11 | Section 16 IGST Act | Section 79 CGST Act | Amended by Notf. 12/2024
Rule 96B — Recovery of Refund Where Export Proceeds Not Realised
⚠️ Rule 96B — Recovery of ITC Refund / IGST Refund on Exports
When Sale Proceeds Not Realised within FEMA Period
📝 You got a refund for exports. But the buyer never paid you (export proceeds not received in India within RBI-allowed time). The government wants its refund money back with interest — plus possible penalty.
TRIGGER [96B(1)] Refund paid but export proceeds NOT realised within FEMA period (including extensions)
→ Deposit refund amount with interest within 30 days of expiry of FEMA period
Otherwise: Recovered u/s 73 or 74 or 74A as erroneous refund + interest u/s 50
(§74A reference inserted by Notf. 20/2024 w.e.f. 01.11.2024)
Notf. 20/2024: Section 74A now added as recovery route — for fraud/suppression cases, this means extended limitation period for recovery of erroneous export refunds.
EXCEPTION RBI writes off requirement of export proceed realisation on merits → refund NOT recovered even if not realised
96B(2) Proceeds realised (even partially) AFTER recovery — applicant produces evidence within 3 months from date of realisation → recovered amount refunded to extent of realisation (within RBI extended period)
💡 Exported goods worth ₹10L. Got IGST refund ₹1.2L. Buyer defaulted — proceeds not realised in 9 months (FEMA period). Must deposit ₹1.2L + interest within 30 days. Later buyer pays — apply within 3 months of receipt to get back the ₹1.2L recovered.
📋 Section 73/74/74A CGST Act | FEMA 1999 | Inserted by Notf. 16/2020-CT dated 23.03.2020
Rule 97 — Consumer Welfare Fund
🏦 Rule 97 — Consumer Welfare Fund
Section 57 CGST Act | Substituted by Notf. 21/2018
📝 When a refund is not payable to the taxpayer (because they passed on the tax burden to consumers), the amount goes into this fund for consumer welfare activities instead of going back to the taxpayer.
Credits to the Fund [97(1)]:
All amounts of duty/CGST/IGST/UTGST/Cess u/s 12C CE Act, §57 CGST, §20 IGST, §21 UTGST, §11 Compensation Act
50% of IGST determined u/s 54(5) (re-credited per §20 IGST Act) deposited in Fund
50% of cess determined u/s 54(5) r/w §11 Compensation Act deposited in Fund
Standing Committee — Key Features:
Constituted by Government Order | Chairman + Vice-Chairman + Member Secretary + other members
Meets generally 4 times a year | Minimum 3 members (besides Chairman/VC) for quorum
10 days' notice for meetings
97(7A) Committee makes available 50% of Fund amount to Board each year for GST consumer awareness publicity — provided Department of Consumer Affairs funds ≥ ₹25 crore p.a.
Accounts subject to audit by CAG
Ordered/directed payments from Fund payable on court/appellate authority direction
📋 Section 57 CGST Act | Section 12C Central Excise Act, 1944 | Consumer Protection Act, 1986
Rule 97A — Manual Filing and Processing
📝 Rule 97A — Manual Override Provision
Inserted by Notification No. 55/2017-CT dated 15.11.2017
📝 Despite all rules requiring electronic filing, this rule saves the day when the portal is down or electronic processing is not possible — manual filing is treated as equivalent to electronic filing.
OVERRIDING PROVISION Notwithstanding anything in this Chapter — any reference to electronic filing of application / intimation / reply / declaration / statement / notice / order / certificate on common portal includes manual filing in such Form as appended to these rules
🔍 GP Singh: "Notwithstanding anything contained" is an overriding clause — it overrides all other rules in the chapter. When portal is down and deadline is approaching, this rule protects the taxpayer. Manual filing within time = valid filing. Department cannot reject on ground of non-electronic filing if system was unavailable.
💡 Portal down on last day of 2-year refund limitation. File RFD-01 manually before the officer. Cite Rule 97A — the manual filing on that date is a valid refund filing and limitation is protected.
📋 Inserted: Notification No. 55/2017-CT dated 15.11.2017 | Applies to entire Chapter X
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal — Ch IX & X | Payment & Refund Rules 85–97A
These statutory arguments are for use in personal hearings, replies to SCN, and before appellate forums. State them confidently with rule / section citations.
Rule 86A — ITC Blocking: Reasons Must Be in Writing + Amount-Specific
CGST Rules, 2017 | Rule 86A(1) | Inserted by Notf. 75/2019 dated 26.12.2019
Key Statutory Argument
The Commissioner or authorised officer is required to have "reasons to believe" — not mere suspicion — and those reasons must be recorded in writing. The block under Rule 86A is restricted to an amount equivalent to the disputed ITC and cannot be a blanket block on the entire Electronic Credit Ledger. Further, under Rule 86A(3), the restriction ceases automatically on expiry of one year from its imposition — no further action is needed by the taxpayer.
When to Use — When Department blocks your entire ECL under Rule 86A without recording reasons in writing or without specifying the disputed amount. Cite to limit the block to the specific ITC amount and to enforce the 1-year automatic expiry.
Rule 86B — 99% Cash Rule Does NOT Apply — Exception Established
CGST Rules, 2017 | Rule 86B | Inserted w.e.f. 01.01.2021
Key Statutory Argument
Rule 86B restricts ITC utilisation to 99% of output liability only where taxable supply exceeds ₹50 lakh per month. The restriction ceases to apply if the registered person or its key management personnel have paid more than ₹1 lakh income tax in each of the preceding two financial years, or if the taxpayer has already discharged 1% of total output tax through electronic cash ledger cumulatively upto that month in the current year. These are exceptions that must be verified before applying the restriction.
When to Use — When Department raises demand for 1% cash shortfall under Rule 86B. Establish which exception applies — income tax paid, refund received, or cumulative cash payment — and cite the applicable proviso to Rule 86B.
Rule 88A — IGST ITC Must Be Used First — Wrong Utilisation Creates Demand
CGST Rules, 2017 | Rule 88A | Inserted by Notf. 16/2019-CT dated 29.03.2019
Key Statutory Argument
Input tax credit on account of integrated tax shall FIRST be utilised towards payment of integrated tax, and the balance may be utilised towards CGST or SGST/UTGST in any order. CGST/SGST/UTGST ITC can be used for integrated tax only AFTER IGST ITC is fully exhausted. Non-compliance with this mandatory sequence results in excess ITC utilisation for one head with corresponding short payment in another — leading to demand with interest.
When to Use — When Department raises demand alleging ITC mis-utilisation or when assessee has utilised CGST ITC while IGST ITC remained available. Cite Rule 88A to explain the mandatory utilisation sequence and correct any errors before they attract demand.
Rule 88B(1) — Interest on Delayed Return: Only on Cash Paid Tax, Not ITC Portion
CGST Rules, 2017 | Rule 88B(1) | Notf. 14/2022 w.e.f. 01.07.2017 | Amended by Notf. 12/2024
Key Statutory Argument
Where a registered person files his return late — but not after commencement of proceedings under Section 73, 74, or 74A — interest under Section 50(1) is payable only on that portion of tax which is paid by debiting the Electronic Cash Ledger. Tax discharged through the Electronic Credit Ledger (ITC) does not attract interest for late return filing. Further, where amounts are credited to the Electronic Cash Ledger on or before the due date of filing but debited while filing the late return, such amount lying in the ledger from due date to debit date is NOT considered for interest calculation.
When to Use — When Department raises interest demand on entire output tax liability for late filed return. Resist inclusion of ITC-discharged portion. Also use the Notf. 12/2024 proviso to exclude interest on amounts already parked in ECrL before due date.
Rule 88C / 88D — DRC-01B / DRC-01C: 7-Day Window is an Opportunity Not a Trap
CGST Rules, 2017 | Rules 88C & 88D | Inserted by Notf. 26/2022 and Notf. 38/2023
Key Statutory Argument
Intimation under DRC-01B (GSTR-1 vs GSTR-3B mismatch) and DRC-01C (GSTR-2B vs GSTR-3B mismatch) provide a 7-day window to pay or explain. The rules are clear: if explanation is furnished and found acceptable by the proper officer, there is NO recovery. Recovery under Section 79 or demand under Sections 73/74/74A arises only where no explanation is given or explanation is not found acceptable — giving the assessee a substantive right to contest the system-generated discrepancy before any coercive action.
When to Use — When Department proceeds to demand/recovery without considering explanation filed within 7 days, or when demand is raised under 74A on basis of DRC-01C mismatch without proper adjudication. Cite to stop coercive action before adjudication under the applicable section.
Rule 90(3) Proviso — Deficiency Period Excluded from 2-Year Limitation
CGST Rules, 2017 | Rule 90(3) Proviso | Inserted by Notf. 15/2021-CT dated 18.05.2021
Key Statutory Argument
The period from the date of filing of the refund claim in FORM GST RFD-01 till the date of communication of deficiencies in FORM GST RFD-03 by the proper officer shall be excluded from the period of two years specified under Section 54(1) in respect of any fresh refund claim filed after rectification of deficiencies. This provision protects the taxpayer who acted in time but faced procedural rejection due to deficiencies — the limitation clock stops during this period.
When to Use — When Department rejects a fresh refund application as time-barred after an earlier application was returned with deficiencies under RFD-03. Cite this proviso to establish that the 2-year period must exclude the deficiency-communication period.
Rule 91(2) — 2025: System Risk-Based Provisional Refund; No Revalidation Required
CGST Rules, 2017 | Rule 91(2) | Substituted by Notification No. 13/2025-CT dated 17.09.2025 w.e.f. 01.10.2025
🆕 2025 Amendment Argument
Rule 91(2) as substituted w.e.f. 01.10.2025 mandates that provisional refund be granted within 7 days of acknowledgement, based on risk identification and evaluation by the system. The proper officer may decline provisional refund only by recording reasons in writing and then proceeding directly to a final order under Rule 92. Further, the order in FORM GST RFD-04 is not required to be revalidated. This shifts the process from officer discretion to system-driven risk evaluation, limiting officer subjectivity.
When to Use — When Department delays or refuses provisional refund under Rule 91 without recording reasons in writing after 01.10.2025. Cite the substituted Rule 91(2) to demand either the provisional refund within 7 days or a written order of refusal with reasons.
Rule 92(3) — Mandatory Natural Justice: No Rejection Without Hearing
CGST Rules, 2017 | Rule 92(3) | Chapter X — Refund
Key Statutory Argument
No application for refund shall be rejected without giving the applicant an opportunity of being heard. This is a mandatory natural justice requirement encoded in the rule itself. Any rejection order passed without issuing notice in FORM GST RFD-08 and without considering the reply in FORM GST RFD-09 is procedurally void and liable to be set aside. The opportunity of hearing is not discretionary — it is a statutory right of the applicant.
When to Use — When refund is rejected by order in RFD-06 without proper SCN in RFD-08 or without giving adequate time (15 days) to reply. Also use before appellate forums where ex-parte rejection orders are challenged.
Rule 94 — Interest on Delayed Refund is Mandatory, Not Discretionary
CGST Rules, 2017 | Rule 94 | Section 56 CGST Act | Amended by Notf. 38/2023 w.e.f. 01.10.2023
Key Statutory Argument
Interest on delayed refund under Section 56 is automatic upon expiry of 60 days from date of application. Rule 94(1) mandates the proper officer to make an order along with a payment order in FORM GST RFD-05 specifying the amount of interest. The word "shall" in Section 56 makes this mandatory and not at the discretion of the officer. The only periods excluded from delay calculation are those attributable to the applicant under Rule 94(2) — delay caused by the Department or its systems is not a valid exclusion.
When to Use — When refund is sanctioned but interest under §56 is not paid. File application citing Rule 94 and §56. Also use when Department argues delay was due to administrative reasons — counter that such reasons are not in the exclusion list of Rule 94(2).
Rule 97A — Portal Failure Cannot Defeat Filing Limitation
CGST Rules, 2017 | Rule 97A | Inserted by Notf. 55/2017-CT dated 15.11.2017
Key Statutory Argument
Notwithstanding anything contained in this Chapter, any reference to electronic filing of an application, intimation, reply, declaration, statement or electronic issuance of a notice, order or certificate on the common portal shall, in respect of that process or procedure, include manual filing of the said application, intimation, reply, declaration or statement or issuance of the said notice, order or certificate. This overriding provision ensures that a taxpayer who manually files within time when the portal is unavailable cannot be denied the benefit of timely filing.
When to Use — When Department rejects refund application as time-barred despite manual filing before the officer on the last date because the portal was down. Cite Rule 97A and produce documentary proof of manual filing (dated acknowledgement from officer).
Rule 96B — Export Proceeds Written Off by RBI: No Recovery of Refund
CGST Rules, 2017 | Rule 96B(1) Proviso | Inserted by Notf. 16/2020-CT dated 23.03.2020
Key Statutory Argument
Where sale proceeds in respect of export goods have not been realised within the FEMA period but the Reserve Bank of India writes off the requirement of realisation of sale proceeds on merits, the refund paid to the applicant shall not be recovered. This RBI write-off protection is absolute — once RBI grants this, the Department cannot recover the export refund regardless of the non-realisation of proceeds.
When to Use — When Department initiates recovery of IGST / ITC refund under Rule 96B citing non-realisation of export proceeds. If RBI has written off the realisation requirement, cite this proviso to absolutely bar any recovery action.
For professional litigation use | Pawan Soni & Associates, Ichalkaranji
CGST Rules — Ch.XI–XV (Rules 98–137)

CGST RULES, 2017 — CHAPTERS XI TO XV

Rules 98 – 137 | Assessment, Audit, Advance Ruling, Appeals & Anti-Profiteering

Source: GST Act & Rules Bare Law — ICAI Nov 2025 Edition  |  CA Pawankumar Soni, Pawan Soni & Associates, Ichalkaranji

LEGEND
Root / Section Header
Level-1 Branch
Permitted / Green
Penalty / Red
Condition / Yellow
Optional / Purple
Reference / Blue
📝 Plain English
💡 Example
🔍 GP Singh
Rule Ref
🆕 2025 Amendment

📋 INDEX — CHAPTERS XI TO XV

CHAPTER XI — ASSESSMENT AND AUDIT (Rules 98–102)
Rule 98 — Provisional Assessment Rule 99 — Scrutiny of Returns Rule 100 — Assessment in Certain Cases Rule 101 — Audit Rule 102 — Special Audit
CHAPTER XII — ADVANCE RULING (Rules 103–107A)
Rule 103 — Qualification of AAR Members Rule 104 — Application to AAR Rule 105 — Certification of AAR Copies Rule 106 — Appeal to AAAR Rule 107 — Certification of AAAR Copies Rule 107A — Manual Filing
CHAPTER XIII — APPEALS AND REVISION (Rules 108–116)
Rule 108 — Appeal to Appellate Authority (APL-01) Rule 109 — Application to AA (APL-03) Rule 109A — Appointment of AA Rule 109B — Revision (RVN-01) Rule 109C — Withdrawal of Appeal Rule 110 — Appeal to GSTAT (APL-05) 🆕 Rule 110A — Single Member Bench 🆕 Rule 111 — Application to GSTAT (APL-07) 🆕 Rule 112 — Additional Evidence Rule 113 — Order of AA/GSTAT 🆕 Rule 113A — Withdrawal from GSTAT 🆕 Rule 114 — Appeal to High Court Rule 115 — Demand Confirmed by Court Rule 116 — Disqualification of AR
CHAPTER XIV — TRANSITIONAL PROVISIONS (Rules 117–121)
Rule 117 — TRAN-1 (ITC Carry Forward) Rule 121 — Recovery of Wrong Credit
CHAPTER XV — ANTI-PROFITEERING (Rules 122–137)
Rule 123 — Standing & Screening Committees Rule 126–127 — Powers & Functions of Authority Rule 128–129 — Proceedings Rule 133 — Order of Authority
HEARING ARSENAL
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal
CHAPTER XI — ASSESSMENT AND AUDIT: OVERVIEW (Rules 98–102) ⬆ Index
RuleSubjectKey Form(s)Litigation Point
98Provisional AssessmentASMT-01 to 09Bond/BG not exceeding 25% of demand; 7-day release window
99Scrutiny of Returns (Sec 61)ASMT-10, 11, 1230-day reply period; acceptance protects; Department must inform if explanation accepted
100Assessment (Sec 62, 63, 64)ASMT-13 to 18, DRC-01, DRC-07Best judgment — must allow 15 days for reply; summary assessment withdrawal window
101Audit (Sec 65)ADT-01, ADT-02Annual or part-year; findings shared in ADT-02; reply right before finalisation
102Special Audit (Sec 66)ADT-03, ADT-04Direction by officer; CA/CWA appointed; findings communicated in ADT-04
Corresponding Sections: §60 (Provisional), §61 (Scrutiny), §62 (Non-filers), §63 (Best Judgment), §64 (Summary), §65 (Audit), §66 (Special Audit)
Rule 98 — Provisional Assessment [Section 60] ⬆ Index
Rule 98 — Provisional Assessment
Sub-rule (1) — Application by Taxpayer
File FORM GST ASMT-01 electronically on common portal Can file directly or via Facilitation Centre Must be accompanied by supporting documents
📝 A taxpayer who is unsure about the rate or value of tax (e.g., in complex classification disputes) can request the officer to allow payment on provisional basis rather than paying wrong tax and seeking refund later.
💡 Eg: Fabric manufacturer unsure whether product is 5% or 12% GST — files ASMT-01 to avoid paying wrong tax and locking up cash.
Sub-rules (2)–(3) — Officer's Process
Officer may issue ASMT-02 seeking additional info Taxpayer files reply in ASMT-03 (personal appearance optional) Order in ASMT-04 allowing provisional payment — states rate/value + bond amount Bond amount: NOT exceeding 25% of amount covered under the bond
Sub-rule (4) — Bond & Security
Bond in ASMT-05 + Bank Guarantee as security Bond under SGST/IGST deemed bond under CGST
📝 "Amount" includes IGST, CGST, SGST/UTGST and Cess all together — so the 25% cap covers the total liability not just one component.
Sub-rules (5)–(7) — Finalisation & Release
Officer issues ASMT-06 calling for finalization records Final order in ASMT-07 (refund or demand) Taxpayer files ASMT-08 for release of security Officer must release security via ASMT-09 within 7 working days of receiving ASMT-08
🔍 "7 working days" is mandatory and directory — delay in releasing security = ground for writ. Word "shall" in sub-rule (7) makes it mandatory: State of UP v Harish Chandra; Mandatory vs Directory per GP Singh's Statutory Interpretation.
Forms: ASMT-01 to ASMT-09 | Section: §60 CGST Act
Rule 99 — Scrutiny of Returns [Section 61] ⬆ Index
Rule 99 — Scrutiny of Returns
Sub-rule (1) — Notice ASMT-10
Return selected for scrutiny based on available data Notice in ASMT-10 informing of discrepancy + seeking explanation Time allowed: NOT exceeding 30 days (extendable by officer) Notice must quantify the amount of tax, interest payable — where possible
📝 Scrutiny means the officer compares your GSTR-3B/1 with internal data (GSTR-2A, e-way bills, etc.) and notices mismatches. He MUST quantify the amount if possible — vague notice is challengeable.
🔍 "Where possible" — officer cannot hide behind this to avoid quantification. GP Singh: provisos and qualifications in a statute must be strictly construed. An ASMT-10 without any quantification and without reason for non-quantification may be challenged.
Sub-rule (2) — Taxpayer's Response (ASMT-11)
Accept discrepancy → pay tax + interest + other amounts → communicate in ASMT-11 OR furnish explanation for discrepancy in ASMT-11
💡 Strategic choice: If discrepancy is genuine clerical error — accept and pay under Sec 73(8) to get protection from penalty. If discrepancy is incorrect — file detailed explanation in ASMT-11 with reconciliation.
Sub-rule (3) — Officer's Finding (ASMT-12)
If explanation acceptable → officer informs in ASMT-12 — matter closed If not acceptable → officer may proceed to Sec 73/74/74A demand
Forms: ASMT-10, ASMT-11, ASMT-12 | Section: §61 CGST Act
Rule 100 — Assessment in Certain Cases [Sections 62, 63, 64] ⬆ Index
Rule 100 — Assessment (Best Judgment / Non-filer / Summary)
Sub-rule (1) — Section 62 Non-filer Assessment
Order in ASMT-13 + Summary in DRC-07 Invoked when registered person fails to file returns even after notice
📝 If you don't file GSTR-3B even after a default notice, the officer can assess your tax liability on the basis of available information. File valid return within 30 days of ASMT-13 to get it withdrawn.
Sub-rule (2) — Section 63 Best Judgment Assessment (Unregistered)
Notice in ASMT-14 + Summary in DRC-01 → with grounds of assessment Must give 15 days time to reply Order in ASMT-15 + Summary in DRC-07
📝 For persons who were liable to register but did not register — officer can assess on best judgment basis. The 15-day reply window is a statutory right — any curtailment = procedural illegality.
🔍 "Best judgment" does not mean arbitrary assessment. SC in Kachwala Gems: estimate must be based on available material, not mere guesswork. If no material = assessment liable to be quashed.
Sub-rules (3)–(5) — Section 64 Summary Assessment
Order in ASMT-16 + Summary in DRC-07 Person can apply for withdrawal in ASMT-17 Order of withdrawal / rejection in ASMT-18
📝 Summary assessment is for emergencies — where delay may harm revenue. But the taxpayer has the right to apply for withdrawal. A summary assessment without sufficient urgency/cause is challengeable.
Substituted by Notification 16/2019-CT w.e.f. 01.04.2019 | Forms: ASMT-13 to 18, DRC-01, DRC-07
Rule 101 — Audit [Section 65] ⬆ Index
Rule 101 — Audit
Sub-rule (1) — Period
Financial year or part thereof or multiples thereof
Sub-rule (2) — Notice ADT-01
Notice in ADT-01 issued before audit — as per Sec 65(3)
Sub-rules (3)–(4) — During Audit
Team verifies: books, turnover, exemptions, deductions, ITC, refunds, rate of tax Officer may inform discrepancies — taxpayer may file reply Officer shall finalise findings after considering reply
📝 The right to file a reply before finalisation of audit findings is a critical procedural safeguard. If officer finalises findings without considering reply = breach of natural justice = ADT-02 liable to be challenged.
Sub-rule (5) — Conclusion: ADT-02
Findings communicated in ADT-02 on conclusion of audit
Forms: ADT-01, ADT-02 | Section: §65 CGST Act
Rule 102 — Special Audit [Section 66] ⬆ Index
Rule 102 — Special Audit
Sub-rule (1) — Direction ADT-03
Direction in ADT-03 to get records audited by CA or CWA specified in direction
📝 Special audit is directed by a proper officer (not lower rank) when complexity of case or volume of ITC demands expert examination. The CA/CWA is specified by the officer — taxpayer cannot choose.
Sub-rule (2) — Report ADT-04
Findings communicated to registered person in ADT-04
Forms: ADT-03, ADT-04 | Section: §66 CGST Act
CHAPTER XII — ADVANCE RULING: OVERVIEW (Rules 103–107A) ⬆ Index
RuleSubjectForm/FeeKey Point
103Qualification of AAR Members—Jt Commissioner and above; Central Govt appoints
104Application to AARARA-01, ₹5,000Filed on common portal; signed per Rule 26
105Certification of AAR copies—Certified by any member of AAR
106Appeal to AAARARA-02 (applicant ₹10,000), ARA-03 (officer — no fee)Against Sec 98(6) ruling; Department files free
107Certification of AAAR copies—Signed by members; sent to all parties + jurisdictional officers
107AManual filing—Notwithstanding all other rules — allows manual in-lieu of electronic
Rules 103–107A — Advance Ruling Key Nodes ⬆ Index
Advance Ruling Framework
Rule 104 — Filing AAR Application
Form ARA-01 on common portal Fee: ₹5,000 deposited per Sec 49 Signed per Rule 26
Rule 106 — Appeal to AAAR
Applicant: ARA-02 on portal, fee ₹10,000 Dept. officer: ARA-03 on portal — NO FEE
📝 Important for litigation: AAR rulings bind only the applicant and jurisdictional officer — they are NOT binding precedents. Never cite AAR against third parties in personal hearings.
🔍 Binding only inter-partes. GP Singh: determinations under special adjudicatory bodies bind only parties before them. AAR ruling against your client in another matter = persuasive only, not mandatory.
Rule 107A — Manual Filing
Non obstante clause: "Notwithstanding anything in this Chapter" Permits manual filing where electronic not possible
🔍 Non obstante clause overrides all other provisions in the Chapter — powerful litigation tool to argue manual filing is valid even when portal-based filing was missed due to technical issues.
Forms: ARA-01, ARA-02, ARA-03 | Sections: §97, §98, §100, §101 CGST Act
CHAPTER XIII — APPEALS & REVISION: OVERVIEW (Rules 108–116) ⬆ Index
RuleSubjectFormKey 2025 Change
108Appeal to FAAAPL-01No 2025 change; electronic filing; provisional acknowledgment immediately
109Dept. Application to FAAAPL-03No 2025 change
109AJurisdiction of FAA—No 2025 change
109BRevision by RA (RVN-01)APL-04No 2025 change
109CWithdrawal of AppealAPL-01/03WNo 2025 change
110Appeal to GSTAT (APL-05)APL-05, APL-02A🆕 Substituted by Notif 12/2024 + Notif 13/2025 — New form APL-02A (Part A & B)
110ASingle Member Bench Procedure—🆕 Inserted by Notif 13/2025 w.e.f. 22.09.2025
111Dept. Application to GSTAT (APL-07)APL-07, APL-02A🆕 Substituted by Notif 12/2024 + Notif 13/2025 — New form APL-02A
112Additional Evidence—No 2025 change
113Order of GSTATAPL-04A🆕 Sub-rule (2) substituted by Notif 13/2025 — New form APL-04A for GSTAT summary
113AWithdrawal from GSTATAPL-05/07W🆕 Inserted by Notif 12/2024
114Appeal to HC (APL-08)APL-08No 2025 change
115Demand confirmed by CourtAPL-04No 2025 change
116Disqualification of AR—No 2025 change
Rule 108 — Appeal to Appellate Authority [APL-01] [Section 107] ⬆ Index
Rule 108 — Appeal to FAA (APL-01)
Sub-rule (1) — Filing
File APL-01 electronically + relevant documents Provisional acknowledgment issued IMMEDIATELY Manual filing permitted ONLY if: Commissioner notifies OR portal fails to show impugned order
📝 Electronic filing is the rule; manual is the exception. If you filed manually without satisfying either condition — the appeal may be treated as not filed. Always verify the condition before manual filing.
Sub-rule (3) — Date of Filing (Critical)
If order uploaded on portal → Final ACK in APL-02 = date of appeal If order NOT on portal → Taxpayer to submit self-certified copy within 7 days Date of provisional ACK = date of filing If self-certified copy submitted AFTER 7 days → date of submission = date of filing (limitation risk)
📝 This rule determines the date of filing for limitation purposes. If you miss the 7-day window for submitting self-certified copy, the date shifts to when you submit the copy — which could make the appeal time-barred.
💡 Client's FAA order dated 10 Jan. Portal not uploaded. Filed APL-01 on 5 April. Self-certified copy submitted on 15 April (10 days late). Date of filing = 15 April. If limitation expires 12 April — appeal is time-barred.
🔍 Provisional acknowledgment ≠ Final acknowledgment. Only final ACK creates the "filed" status. This is a trap — many practitioners assume provisional = filed. Explanation to Rule 108 is clear: appeal treated as filed ONLY when final ACK with appeal number is issued.
Forms: APL-01, APL-02 | Section: §107(1) CGST Act | Amended: Notif 38/2023-CT, Notif 26/2022-CT
Rule 109 — Application by Department to FAA [APL-03] ⬆ Index
Rule 109 — Dept. Application to FAA (APL-03)
Sub-rule (1): APL-03 electronically; provisional ACK immediately Manual filing: same conditions as Rule 108 Sub-rule (2): If order on portal → Final ACK in APL-02 = date of filing If order NOT on portal → Self-certified copy within 7 days; else date of submission = date of filing
📝 Same acknowledgment mechanics as taxpayer's APL-01 appeal. Department's APL-03 application is for cases where the officer (not taxpayer) challenges an order — e.g., where the adjudicating authority ruled in favour of taxpayer.
Forms: APL-03, APL-02 | Section: §107(2) CGST Act
Rules 109A, 109B, 109C — Jurisdiction, Revision & Withdrawal ⬆ Index
Rules 109A / 109B / 109C
109A — Jurisdiction of FAA
Order by Addl/Jt Commissioner → Appeal to Commissioner (Appeals) Order by Dy/Asst Commissioner or Superintendent → Appeal to officer not below Jt Commissioner (Appeals) Taxpayer: 3 months from communication of order Department: 6 months from communication of order
109B — Revision by Revisional Authority
Notice in RVN-01 before adverse revision order Reasonable opportunity of being heard mandatory RA issues summary in APL-04 with final demand confirmed
🔍 Revisional Authority must issue RVN-01 before adverse order — no RVN-01 = violation of audi alteram partem. Revision order without notice = procedural infirmity going to root of jurisdiction.
109C — Withdrawal of FAA Appeal
File APL-01/03W before SCN or before order — whichever earlier After final ACK → withdrawal subject to AA's approval (decide within 7 days) Fresh appeal after withdrawal must be within original limitation period
📝 Withdrawal is permitted but carries a risk — fresh appeal must be filed within original limitation period. Cannot use withdrawal to extend limitation.
Rule 110 — Appeal to GSTAT [APL-05] 🆕 2025 AMENDED ⬆ Index
Substituted by Notification No. 12/2024–CT dated 10.07.2024. Further amended by Notification No. 13/2025–CT dated 17.09.2025, w.e.f. 22.09.2025 — New Form APL-02A (Part A = provisional ACK; Part B = final ACK with appeal number) replaces old APL-02 for GSTAT matters. Manual filing proviso OMITTED.
Rule 110 — Appeal to GSTAT (APL-05)
Sub-rule (1) — Filing
File APL-05 electronically
Provisional ACK now issued in PART A of FORM GST APL-02A (not APL-02). Notif 13/2025 w.e.f. 22.09.2025.
Manual filing proviso OMITTED by Notif 13/2025 — only electronic filing now
📝 With GSTAT now operational, all GSTAT appeal filings are strictly electronic. The earlier provision allowing Registrar to permit manual filing has been deleted. No manual filing option exists at GSTAT level.
Sub-rule (2) — Cross-Objections
Cross-objections in APL-06 electronically
Manual cross-objection proviso OMITTED by Notif 13/2025 w.e.f. 22.09.2025 — only electronic.
Sub-rule (4) — Date of Filing
If order on portal: Final ACK in Part B of APL-02A on removal of defects = date of filing If order NOT on portal: Self-certified copy within 7 days
Part B of APL-02A replaces earlier APL-02 for GSTAT — Notif 13/2025 w.e.f. 22.09.2025. Also: "self-certified copy" changed to "self-attested copy" for post-7-day submission.
If copy submitted AFTER 7 days → date of submission = date of filing
Sub-rule (5) — Fees
₹1,000 per ₹1 lakh of tax/ITC/penalty involved Maximum: ₹25,000 | Minimum: ₹5,000 Order not involving demand → flat ₹5,000 Rectification application (Sec 112(10)) → NO FEE
Forms: APL-05, APL-06, APL-02A | Section: §112(1) CGST Act | 🆕 Amended: Notif 12/2024-CT + Notif 13/2025-CT w.e.f. 22.09.2025
Rule 110A — Single Member Bench Procedure 🆕 INSERTED 2025 ⬆ Index
ENTIRELY NEW RULE — Inserted by Notification No. 13/2025–CT dated 17.09.2025, w.e.f. 22.09.2025. Governs appeals heard by Single Member Bench of GSTAT under Sec 109(8).
Rule 110A — Single Member Bench at GSTAT
Sub-rule (1) — Scheduling by President/Vice-President
President or VP (if authorised) can schedule appeal to Single Member Bench if appeal does NOT involve a question of law. Can act suo motu or on application by parties.
📝 This is new — GSTAT can hear smaller matters through a Single Member Bench to reduce pendency. Key condition: NO question of law. If your matter has a legal question — insist on regular bench.
Sub-rule (2) — Referral Back for Question of Law
If Single Member Bench during hearing concludes that a question of law IS involved — must record reasons in writing and send back to President/VP for reconsideration.
🔍 This creates an opportunity: if your matter is assigned to Single Member Bench, argue at the outset that it involves a question of law (interpretation of statute, scope of ITC, classification, etc.) — to get it referred to a Division Bench with Technical + Judicial Member.
Sub-rule (3) — Prior Decision on Same Issue
If same issue for same taxable person within same State has already been decided by a Division Bench (Technical + Judicial Member) — even if assigned to Single Member Bench, it must be heard by the Division Bench.
📝 This protects consistency. If one period is decided by Division Bench — subsequent periods cannot be decided by Single Member. Useful argument if your client had earlier appeal decided by Division Bench.
Sub-rule (4) — ₹50 Lakh Threshold
For reckoning ₹50 lakh threshold under Sec 109(8) — cumulative tax/ITC/fine/penalty across ALL issues and ALL tax periods in the impugned order is to be considered.
📝 If total demand across all periods exceeds ₹50 lakh — it goes to Division Bench, not Single Member. Always compute cumulative amount across all issues and periods, not period-wise.
💡 Order covers FY 2018-19 (demand ₹30L) and FY 2019-20 (demand ₹25L) = Total ₹55L. Goes to Division Bench despite each year being under ₹50L.
🆕 Entirely NEW Rule | Section: §109(8) CGST Act | Inserted by Notif 13/2025-CT w.e.f. 22.09.2025
Rule 111 — Dept. Application to GSTAT [APL-07] 🆕 2025 AMENDED ⬆ Index
Substituted by Notif 12/2024-CT. Further amended by Notif 13/2025-CT w.e.f. 22.09.2025: APL-02A (Part A = provisional; Part B = final) replaces APL-02 for Dept applications. "Self-certified copy" changed to "self-attested copy". Manual filing proviso OMITTED.
Rule 111 — Dept. Application to GSTAT (APL-07)
Sub-rule (1): APL-07 electronically; provisional ACK in Part A of APL-02A
Part A of APL-02A = provisional ACK (new form). Manual filing proviso omitted. Notif 13/2025 w.e.f. 22.09.2025.
Sub-rule (2): Cross-objections in APL-06 electronically
Manual cross-objection proviso omitted. Notif 13/2025.
Sub-rule (4): If order on portal → Final ACK in Part B of APL-02A = date of filing Self-attested copy (not self-certified) within 7 days if order not on portal
Word changed from "self-certified" to "self-attested" by Notif 13/2025 w.e.f. 22.09.2025 — applicable to both Rule 110 and Rule 111.
Forms: APL-07, APL-06, APL-02A | Section: §112(3) CGST Act | 🆕 Amended: Notif 12/2024-CT + Notif 13/2025-CT
Rule 112 — Additional Evidence Before AA or GSTAT ⬆ Index
Rule 112 — Additional Evidence
General Rule — No New Evidence in Appeal
Appellant cannot produce evidence NOT produced before adjudicating authority / FAA
Exceptions — When Additional Evidence IS Permitted
(a) Evidence that OUGHT to have been admitted but was REFUSED (b) Prevented by SUFFICIENT CAUSE from producing it (c) Evidence RELEVANT to ground of appeal — not produced due to sufficient cause (d) Adjudicating authority made order WITHOUT giving sufficient opportunity to adduce evidence
📝 Ground (d) is powerful — if the adjudicating authority passed order without proper hearing or without giving time to produce documents, the appellate authority must permit additional evidence. This is natural justice argument.
🔍 "Sufficient cause" — same standard as Sec 5 Limitation Act. Courts have liberally interpreted it in adjudicatory contexts. GP Singh: "Sufficient cause" is a beneficent provision; must be construed to advance justice, not defeat it.
Procedural Safeguards for Dept.
Sub-rule (2): Reasons must be recorded in writing before admission Sub-rule (3): Dept. must get opportunity to examine/cross-examine or rebut new evidence Sub-rule (4): AA/GSTAT can suo motu direct production of document/witness
Rule 113 — Order of AA / GSTAT 🆕 2025 AMENDED ⬆ Index
Sub-rule (2) substituted by Notification No. 13/2025–CT dated 17.09.2025 w.e.f. 22.09.2025: GSTAT must now issue summary of its order in NEW FORM GST APL-04A (not APL-04). APL-04 continues for FAA and HC/SC orders.
Rule 113 — Order Summaries
Sub-rule (1): FAA issues summary in APL-04 along with its order under Sec 107(11)
Sub-rule (2): GSTAT must issue (or cause to be issued) summary of its order in FORM GST APL-04A — new distinct form for GSTAT summaries. Earlier form was APL-04 (shared with FAA). Notif 13/2025 w.e.f. 22.09.2025.
📝 New form APL-04A is exclusive to GSTAT. This matters for enforcement — DRC-07 and recovery proceedings will reference APL-04A for GSTAT orders. Always check correct form when filing subsequent proceedings.
Forms: APL-04 (FAA/HC/SC), APL-04A (GSTAT) | Section: §107(11), §113(1) CGST Act
Rule 113A — Withdrawal of Appeal/Application from GSTAT 🆕 INSERTED 2024 ⬆ Index
Inserted by Notification No. 12/2024–CT dated 10.07.2024 — new rule for withdrawal of GSTAT appeals/applications (APL-05/APL-07 matters).
Rule 113A — Withdrawal from GSTAT
File withdrawal application in APL-05/07W any time BEFORE GSTAT's order under Sec 113(1) After final ACK (APL-02A) issued → withdrawal subject to GSTAT's APPROVAL GSTAT to decide withdrawal application within 15 days Fresh appeal/application after withdrawal → must file within original Sec 112(1)/(3) limitation
📝 Unlike FAA withdrawal (7-day decision period), GSTAT has 15 days to decide on withdrawal. Limitation trap same as FAA — fresh appeal must be within original limitation period.
💡 Client settles dispute during GSTAT proceedings and wants to withdraw appeal — file APL-05/07W immediately. Note: settlement does not automatically extend limitation for fresh appeal on other issues.
Form: APL-05/07W | Section: §113(1) CGST Act | 🆕 Inserted by Notif 12/2024-CT
Rules 114–116 — HC Appeal, Court Confirmation, Disqualification of AR ⬆ Index
Rules 114 / 115 / 116
Rule 114 — HC Appeal (APL-08)
Appeal to HC under Sec 117 in Form APL-08 Signed per Rule 26
Rule 115 — Court Confirmation
Jurisdictional officer issues APL-04 showing demand confirmed by HC or SC
Rule 116 — Disqualification of AR
Authorised representatives (other than Sec 116(2)(b)/(c)) can be disqualified for misconduct Commissioner must provide opportunity of being heard before disqualification
📝 Advocates and CAs are protected under Sec 116(2)(b)/(c) — this rule applies only to other categories of representatives. Commissioner cannot disqualify a CA/Advocate under this rule.
Chapter XIV — Transitional Provisions: Rules 117–121 (Key Rules) ⬆ Index
Chapter XIV — Transitional Provisions
Rule 117 — TRAN-1 (ITC Carry-Forward from Pre-GST)
Must file TRAN-1 within 90 days of appointed day (i.e., 01.07.2017) Commissioner may extend by further 90 days on Council recommendation Sub-rule (1A): Extended further up to 31.03.2020 for portal-difficulty cases on Council recommendation Sub-rule (4): Credit allowed at 40%/60% for goods without excise duty documents
📝 TRAN-1 was for claiming pre-GST credit of excise/VAT/service tax. Despite lapse of time, SC in Union of India v Filco Trade Centre allowed TRAN-1 filing by directing portal opening — a landmark ruling protecting transition credits.
🔍 SC in Filco Trade Centre (2022): Right to carry forward ITC is a vested right — cannot be defeated by mere non-availability of portal. Portal glitch = sufficient cause for condonation. Ratio directly applicable in pending TRAN-1 matters.
Rule 120A — Revision of TRAN-1
One revision of TRAN-1 permitted within prescribed time
Forms: TRAN-1, TRAN-2 | Section: §140 CGST Act
Rule 121 — Recovery of Wrongly Availed TRAN-1 Credit ⬆ Index
Rule 121 — Recovery of Wrong TRAN-1 Credit
Credit wrongly availed in TRAN-1 can be verified by officer Recovery proceedings under Sec 73 OR Sec 74 OR Sec 74A — as applicable
Rule 121 amended by Notification No. 20/2024–CT dated 08.10.2024 w.e.f. 01.11.2024: "or section 74A" added — proceedings can now also be initiated under the new Sec 74A (applicable from FY 2024-25 onwards).
📝 Wrong TRAN-1 credit can be recovered in full. The officer must choose the right section — if fraud/suppression is alleged, Sec 74 or 74A; if not, Sec 73. Allegation of suppression in TRAN-1 context must be specifically pleaded with material.
Section: §73/§74/§74A | 🆕 Amended by Notif 20/2024-CT w.e.f. 01.11.2024
CHAPTER XV — ANTI-PROFITEERING: OVERVIEW (Rules 122–137) ⬆ Index
RuleSubjectStatusNote
122Constitution of NAAOMITTEDOmitted by Notif 24/2022-CT w.e.f. 01.12.2022 — NAA dissolved
123Standing & Screening CommitteesActiveCouncil-level Standing Committee + State Screening Committees
124Appointment of NAA Chair/MembersOMITTEDOmitted w.e.f. 01.12.2022
125Secretary to NAAOMITTEDOmitted w.e.f. 01.12.2022
126Methodology for determinationActiveAuthority determines methodology — no fixed formula prescribed
127Functions of AuthorityActiveDetermine, identify, and order — including cancellation
128–129Proceedings (Standing Comm → DGAP)Active2-month SC examination; 6-month DGAP investigation
130–132Confidentiality / Cooperation / SummonsActiveRTI-like confidentiality; DGAP can seek expert opinion; civil court summons power
133Order of AuthorityActive6-month limit from DGAP report; includes refund + 18% interest + penalty + cancellation
134Decision by majorityOMITTEDOmitted w.e.f. 01.12.2022
135Compliance by registered personActiveImmediate compliance or recovery under respective GST Acts
136Monitoring by tax authoritiesActiveAuthority may require CT/ST/UTGST officers to monitor compliance
137Tenure of NAAOMITTEDOmitted w.e.f. 01.12.2022
Rules 123, 126, 127 — Committees, Powers & Functions of Anti-Profiteering Authority ⬆ Index
Anti-Profiteering: Structure & Functions
Rule 123 — Committees
Standing Committee: Constituted by Council — State + Central Govt officers State Screening Committee: One State officer + One Central officer per State
📝 Local/smaller complaints go first to State Screening Committee → then to Standing Committee → then to DGAP for investigation. National complaints go directly to Standing Committee.
Rule 127 — Functions of Authority
(i) Determine whether ITC benefit / rate reduction passed on (ii) Identify the registered person who did NOT pass on benefit (iii)(a) Reduction in prices (iii)(b) Return to recipient + 18% interest from date of collection (iii)(b) If recipient unidentifiable → deposit in Consumer Welfare Fund (Sec 57) (iii)(c) Penalty as specified (iii)(d) Cancellation of registration
Rules 128–129 — Anti-Profiteering Proceedings ⬆ Index
Rules 128–129 — Proceedings Flow
Rule 128 — Standing Committee Examination
SC examines written application within 2 months (extendable by 1 month) Local issues → State Screening Committee first (also 2 months + 1 month extension) If prima facie evidence → refer to DGAP
Rule 129 — DGAP Investigation
DGAP issues Notice before investigation: description of goods/services + facts + time limit for reply DGAP must share evidence from one party with all other parties Investigation must be completed within 6 months + 3 months extension Report submitted to Authority with findings + records
Rule 133 — Order of Anti-Profiteering Authority ⬆ Index
Rule 133 — AP Authority Order
Must determine within 6 months of DGAP report Sub-rule (2): Opportunity of hearing on written request Sub-rule (2A): Authority may seek clarification from DGAP during proceedings Sub-rule (3): If profiteering found → order: price reduction + refund + 18% interest + 50% to CWF + penalty + cancellation Sub-rule (4): If further investigation needed → Authority can refer back to DGAP Sub-rule (5): Authority can suo motu expand scope to other goods/services
📝 Anti-profiteering proceedings are civil in nature but carry quasi-criminal consequences (penalty + cancellation). Natural justice is paramount — always insist on opportunity of hearing per Sub-rule (2).
🔍 "Commensurate reduction" is the battleground phrase. There is no fixed formula — Authority determines methodology (Rule 126). This vagueness has been challenged in Delhi HC (HUL case, Nestle India) — principle of certainty in penal provisions demands clarity in methodology.
Section: §171 CGST Act | DGAP = Director General of Anti-Profiteering (name changed from DG Safeguards by Notif 29/2018-CT)
⚔️ HEARING ARSENAL — RULES 98 TO 137
⬆ Index
These statutory arguments are for use in personal hearings before FAA, GSTAT, and courts. State them confidently with Rule/Section number.
Rule 108 — Explanation (Date of Filing)
CGST Rules, 2017 — Rule 108 Explanation
"An appeal shall be treated as filed only when the final acknowledgement, indicating the appeal number, is issued." — Provisional acknowledgment ≠ Filing. Date of final APL-02/APL-02A = date of appeal.
When to Use: Dept. argues appeal time-barred — argue date of final ACK, not provisional ACK, is the relevant date.
Rule 110A(2) — Question of Law → Division Bench
CGST Rules — Rule 110A(2) | Inserted Notif 13/2025 w.e.f. 22.09.2025
"If Single Member Bench concludes that the appeal may involve a question of law — it shall record reasons in writing and send back the appeal to the President/VP for reconsideration." — Statutory right to Division Bench hearing when question of law is involved.
When to Use: Your GSTAT matter is assigned to Single Member Bench — argue at threshold that matter involves question of law (interpretation of Sec 16(2)(b)/17(5)/classification) to get it transferred to Division Bench.
Rule 110A(4) — Cumulative Threshold for ₹50L
CGST Rules — Rule 110A(4) | Inserted Notif 13/2025
"For reckoning the amount of fifty lakh rupees — the cumulative tax or ITC involved or fine/fee/penalty determined with reference to ALL issues and ALL tax periods covered in the order appealed against." — Period-wise calculation is wrong; cumulative calculation governs.
When to Use: Dept. tries to send matter to Single Member Bench citing each period individually under ₹50L — use cumulative rule to insist on Division Bench.
Rule 112 — Additional Evidence (Ground (d))
CGST Rules — Rule 112(1)(d)
"Where the adjudicating authority made the order appealed against without giving sufficient opportunity to the appellant to adduce evidence relevant to any ground of appeal" — additional evidence MUST be permitted. No discretion.
When to Use: Adjudicating officer passed ex-parte order or refused documents — use Rule 112(1)(d) before GSTAT/FAA to adduce additional evidence including fresh affidavits, purchase register, bank statements.
Rule 99 — ASMT-10 Must Quantify
CGST Rules — Rule 99(1)
"The proper officer shall also, where possible, quantify the amount of tax and interest and any other amount payable in relation to such discrepancy." — Vague ASMT-10 without any attempt at quantification is procedurally defective.
When to Use: Client received ASMT-10 with vague allegation of "ITC mismatch" but no amount stated — argue non-compliance with Rule 99(1) = notice defective = no valid proceeding under Sec 61.
Rule 98(7) — 7 Working Days for Security Release
CGST Rules — Rule 98(7)
"The proper officer SHALL release the security...within a period of seven working days from the date of receipt of the application under sub-rule (6)." — Mandatory timeline; "shall" makes it non-discretionary.
When to Use: Bank Guarantee not released after provisional assessment finalized — demand release citing Rule 98(7); non-release = writ jurisdiction triggered.
SC — Filco Trade Centre — TRAN-1 Vested Right
Union of India v Filco Trade Centre Pvt Ltd | SC 2022 | SLP(C) No.32709/2018
"The right to carry forward transitional credit is a vested right which cannot be defeated merely on account of non-availability or glitches in the portal." — Portal failure = sufficient cause; credit cannot be denied on technical grounds.
When to Use: Department denies TRAN-1 credit on ground of late filing or portal error — cite Filco Trade Centre; SC directed portal reopening. Applies to pending TRAN-1 litigation.
Rule 101(4) — Reply Before Audit Finalisation is Mandatory
CGST Rules — Rule 101(4)
"The proper officer shall finalise the findings of audit after due consideration of the reply furnished." — Officer MUST consider reply before finalising ADT-02. ADT-02 without considering reply = violation of natural justice.
When to Use: SCN after audit where audit findings were not shared or reply was not considered — challenge ADT-02 as procedurally invalid = no valid demand can flow from it.
Rule 107A — Non Obstante for Manual Filing
CGST Rules — Rule 107A
"Notwithstanding anything contained in this Chapter [Chapter XII] — reference to electronic filing includes manual filing in such Form as appended to these Rules." — Manual filing of AAR application is valid despite portal requirements.
When to Use: AAR or AAAR rejects application claiming it was manually filed — cite Rule 107A non obstante clause; manual filing explicitly permitted under this rule.
Rule 109B — No Revision Without RVN-01 Notice
CGST Rules — Rule 109B(1)
"Where the Revisional Authority decides to pass an order in revision that is likely to affect the person adversely — the RA shall serve on him a notice in FORM GST RVN-01 and shall give him reasonable opportunity of being heard." — Adverse revision order without RVN-01 = void ab initio.
When to Use: Revision order passed without prior notice — challenge on jurisdictional ground; RVN-01 notice is a condition precedent, not a mere procedural formality.
For professional litigation use | Pawan Soni & Associates, Ichalkaranji | CA Pawankumar Soni (ICAI M.No. 116591) | GST Litigation Practice
CGST Rules — Ch.XVI (E-Way Rules 138–138F)

🚛 Chapter XVI — E-Way Rules (Rules 138 – 138F)

Source: CGST Rules, 2017 (Bare Law — ICAI Nov 2025 Edition) | CA Pawankumar Soni, Ichalkaranji

Permitted / Valid / Positive
Prohibited / Restriction
Condition / If / Unless
Optional / Exception
Neutral Reference
📝 Plain English
💡 Example
🔍 GP Singh
🟢 2025 Amendment (Latest)
📋 Index — Chapter XVI: E-Way Rules
📊 Tier 1 Overview
Rule 138 — Generation of E-Way Bill
Rule 138(10) — Validity Period
Rule 138(14) — No E-Way Bill Cases
Rule 138A — Documents to Carry
Rule 138B — Verification
Rule 138C — Inspection Report
Rule 138D — Detention Upload
Rule 138E — Restriction on Part A
Rule 138F — Gold/Precious Stones (Intra-State)
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal
📊 Tier 1 — Chapter XVI: E-Way Rules Overview
Rule 138
Core rule — who generates, when, how, validity. Covers all modes (road/rail/air/vessel). Threshold: ₹50,000.
Go to Rule 138 ↓
Rule 138(10)
Validity table — distance-based. Upto 200 km = 1 day. Over-dimensional cargo = 20 km per day.
Go ↓
Rule 138(14)
Exhaustive list of cases where NO e-way bill is required — goods, modes, purposes.
Go ↓
Rule 138A
Documents the person-in-charge of conveyance must carry — invoice, e-way bill / EWB number / RFID.
Go ↓
Rule 138B
Verification power — Commissioner/officer can intercept. RFID readers. Physical verification protocol.
Go ↓
Rule 138C
Inspection report — Part A within 24 hrs, Part B within 3 days. One inspection per conveyance.
Go ↓
Rule 138D
Transporter can upload detention info if vehicle held > 30 minutes.
Go ↓
Rule 138E
Restriction on furnishing Part A if returns not filed — 2 consecutive periods. Relief via EWB-05 application.
Go ↓
Rule 138F 🟢
Intra-State e-way bill for gold, precious stones, etc. Only Part A required — no Part B needed. State-specific.
Go ↓
Rule 138 — Information Prior to Movement / Generation of E-Way Bill
🚛 Rule 138 — E-Way Bill: Who Generates, When & How
CGST Rules, 2017 | Chapter XVI | Core E-Way Rule
RULE 138(1) — TRIGGER
Every registered person causing movement of goods of consignment value exceeding ₹50,000 shall, before commencement of movement, furnish information in Part A of FORM GST EWB-01 electronically on common portal → unique number generated
📝 The ₹50,000 threshold is per consignment (not per invoice). The value includes GST charged in the document but excludes exempt goods if mixed with taxable goods (per Expl. 2).
(i) In relation to a supply
(ii) For reasons other than supply
(job work, branch transfer, return, etc.)
(iii) Due to inward supply from unregistered person
📝 Recipient is deemed to cause the movement if they are known at time of commencement.
➕ Irrespective of Value — EWB Mandatory in Special Cases:
PRINCIPAL → JOB WORKER
Principal in one State → Job worker in another State: E-way bill by principal or job worker (if registered) — irrespective of value
HANDICRAFT GOODS
Transported inter-State by person exempted from registration u/s 24(i)(ii) → E-way bill by that person — irrespective of value
AUTHORISED PERSON
Transporter / E-commerce operator / Courier agency may furnish Part A on authorization from consignor
📋 Rule 138(2) / (2A) — Who Generates based on Mode of Transport:
ROAD (own/hired/public)
Registered person (consignor or consignee) generates EWB in Part B of EWB-01
RAIL / AIR / VESSEL
Supplier or recipient generates EWB either before or after commencement of movement
Note: Railways shall not deliver unless EWB produced at time of delivery
📝 For rail/air/vessel, Part B (vehicle number) not needed before movement — can be updated after. But EWB must be generated.
ROAD — NO EWB by person
If person didn't generate → hands to transporter → transporter generates EWB from Part A information
EXCEPTION — PART B NOT REQUIRED
Where goods transported for distance of upto 50 km within State/UT:
(a) From consignor's place of business to transporter's place — supplier/recipient/transporter may NOT furnish Part B
(b) From transporter's place to consignee's place — details of conveyance need not be updated
📝 The 50 km relaxation is for the "last mile" delivery within the same State — only Part A is sufficient for short local hauls. EWB is invalid for road movement unless Part B is furnished (except these 50 km cases).
💡 A textile trader in Ichalkaranji dispatches goods to Kolhapur (45 km away) — only Part A needed. If Kolhapur to Pune (200+ km) — Part B (vehicle number) must be updated before movement.
🟢 LATEST AMENDMENT — w.e.f. 11.02.2025 (Notification No. 12/2024 – CT dated 10.07.2024)
🟢 2025 AMENDMENT — Rule 138(3) — New Proviso
An unregistered person required to generate EWB [fourth proviso to sub-rule (1)] OR opting to generate EWB — shall submit details electronically on common portal in FORM GST ENR-03 (directly or through Facilitation Centre notified by Commissioner) → upon validation, a unique enrolment number generated and communicated to such person.
📝 Earlier, unregistered persons had no formal enrolment mechanism. Now from 11.02.2025, they must register on portal via ENR-03 to get an enrolment number before they can generate an e-way bill.
💡 An unregistered handicraft seller transporting goods inter-State must now first submit ENR-03 details on common portal to get an enrolment number — then generate EWB. If they skip ENR-03, they cannot generate EWB from 11.02.2025.
📋 Other Key Sub-Rules of Rule 138:
138(4)
On EWB generation → unique EBN made available to supplier, recipient & transporter on common portal
138(5)
Goods transferred from one conveyance to another → update Part B before transfer & further movement
[50 km exception applies for last-leg to consignee]
138(5A)
Consignor/recipient may assign EBN to another transporter for Part B updation
CANNOT assign after transporter has already updated Part B
138(6)
Multiple consignments in one conveyance → transporter may generate Consolidated EWB in FORM GST EWB-02
138(7)
Consignor/consignee did NOT generate EWB + aggregate value > ₹50,000 → Transporter shall generate (except rail/air/vessel) for inter-State supply
[E-commerce/courier can furnish Part A]
138(8)
Part A information → made available to registered supplier for GSTR-1 filing. Unregistered supplier/recipient informed electronically if mobile/email available.
138(9) — CANCELLATION
EWB may be cancelled within 24 hours of generation if goods not transported OR transported differently
CANNOT cancel if verified in transit (Rule 138B)
Unique number valid for 15 days for Part B updation
📝 If EWB is generated but trip cancelled — cancel within 24 hours. After 24 hours, it remains live and mismatches may attract penalty if goods are inspected.
📋 Rules 138(11)(12)(13) — Acceptance / Rejection / Validity across States
138(11)
EWB details made available to supplier (if Part A by recipient/transporter) or recipient (if Part A by supplier/transporter) → must communicate acceptance/rejection
138(12) — DEEMED ACCEPTANCE
No communication within 72 hours of details being available, OR at time of delivery (whichever is earlier) → deemed accepted
📝 Silence = acceptance. If recipient does not reject within 72 hours or before delivery, they are deemed to have accepted the consignment details in the EWB.
138(13)
EWB generated under Rule 138 or State GST Rule 138 → valid in every State and Union territory
📋 Corresponding Forms: FORM GST EWB-01 (E-way Bill), FORM GST EWB-02 (Consolidated EWB), FORM GST ENR-03 (Unregistered person enrolment — 2025)
Rule 138(10) — Validity Period of E-Way Bill
⏱️ Rule 138(10) — E-Way Bill Validity Period Table
Amended — w.e.f. 01.01.2021 (100 km → 200 km per day)
📝 Validity is counted from the time of EWB generation — and "each day" means the period expiring at midnight of the day immediately following the date of generation.
S.No. Distance Validity Period Type of Cargo Litigation Note
1 Up to 200 km One day Normal cargo (not Over Dimensional / multimodal with ship) Goods must reach destination within 1 day. If delayed, transporter must extend validity by updating Part B.
2 For every 200 km or part thereof thereafter One additional day Normal cargo 400 km = 2 days, 401 km = 3 days, etc.
3 Up to 20 km One day Over Dimensional Cargo (ODC) or Multimodal (at least one leg by ship) ODC = cargo exceeding dimensions under Rule 93, Central Motor Vehicles Rules, 1989.
4 For every 20 km or part thereafter One additional day ODC / Multimodal 40 km ODC = 2 days. Very short validity for heavy/special cargo.
⚠️ EXTENSION OPTIONS  (1) Commissioner may extend validity for notified categories of goods.  (2) Transporter may extend validity in exceptional circumstances (including multimodal shipment) by updating Part B.  (3) Validity may also be extended within 8 hours from time of expiry (Notif. 31/2019).
📝 If EWB expires mid-transit due to traffic/breakdown/natural calamity — the transporter must generate another EWB after updating Part B. Expired EWB = No valid document = Risk of detention and penalty u/s 129.
💡 Truck carrying 300 km route — generates EWB on Day 1 at 10 PM. Validity = 2 days = expires midnight of Day 3. If truck breaks down and reaches on Day 4 — EWB expired — officer can detain and levy penalty. Transporter should have extended before expiry or within 8 hours of expiry.
🔍 GP Singh on strict construction: Validity conditions are directory or mandatory depending on context. Courts have held that bona fide delays due to accident/breakdown may not attract penalty if substantiated by evidence — "reason to believe" for evasion must be present before invoking Section 129 merely for EWB expiry.
Rule 138(14) — Cases Where No E-Way Bill is Required
🚫 Rule 138(14) — Exhaustive List: No E-Way Bill Required
📝 This is an exhaustive list — if movement does not fall under any of these clauses, EWB is required. The onus is on the taxpayer to establish that the exemption applies. Officers often contest these at checkposts.
Clause Situation / Category Plain English GST / Litigation Note
(a) Goods specified in Annexure to Rule 138(14) LPG (household), kerosene (PDS), postal baggage, pearls/precious metals/jewellery (Ch. 71), currency, used household effects, coral — all these are exempt from EWB requirement. Note: Imitation Jewellery (HSN 7117) was inserted — does NOT get exemption (amended by Notif. 26/2022).
(b) Goods transported by non-motorised conveyance Bullock cart, cycle, handcart, etc. — no EWB needed. This exemption is frequently misused for small inter-State evasion. Officers are alert to such claims.
(c) Goods from port/airport/ACC/LCS to ICD or CFS for customs clearance Import goods being moved for customs clearance formalities — EWB not needed as goods are under Customs supervision. Bill of entry itself is the document. Customs bond/seal applies.
(d) Movement within areas notified by State GST Rules under Rule 138(14)(d) Each State can notify specific local areas/zones where intra-State EWB not required. Check State notification. State-specific — verify for Maharashtra/Karnataka etc.
(e) Goods (other than de-oiled cake) in Notification 2/2017-CT(Rate) Schedule — GST exempt goods Most agricultural goods, vegetables, grains, milk, eggs, unprocessed goods exempt from GST — also exempt from EWB.
