Common defence: If goods are GST-exempt under Notif. 2/2017-CT(Rate) — no EWB required. Useful for agri traders.
(f) Alcoholic liquor for human consumption, petroleum crude, HSD, petrol, natural gas, ATF These are outside GST. No EWB required under CGST Rules — State may have separate rule. Petroleum products — check State-specific rules.
(g) Supply treated as No Supply under Schedule III Actionable claims (other than lottery/betting), sale of land/building, employee services, judiciary/legislature functions — no EWB. Useful in JDA/real estate disputes — movement of goods between developer and landowner under Schedule III arrangement.
(h) Goods under Customs bond / supervision / seal Transit goods moving between ICD, CFS, customs ports under customs control — EWB not needed. Bill of entry + Customs transit document is sufficient.
(i) Transit cargo from/to Nepal or Bhutan International transit — no GST EWB needed. Covered by customs/bilateral treaty documents.
(j) Goods exempt under Notif. 35/2017-CT(Rate) or 26/2017-IT(Rate) Additional exempt goods notifications — goods specified therein are also EWB-exempt. Cross-check current notifications — amended periodically.
(k) Movement of goods by Defence formation (Ministry of Defence) as consignor or consignee Army/Navy/Air Force movements — no EWB required. Defence exemption is absolute — no officer can detain defence consignments for EWB.
(l) Consignor is Central/State Government or local authority — goods transported by rail Government goods by rail — EWB not required. Government railways movement — administrative exemption.
(m) Empty cargo containers being transported Empty containers — no goods = no EWB required. Useful defence — if officer intercepts empty container truck, no EWB violation.
(n) Goods moved up to 20 km from place of business to weighbridge and back — with delivery challan under Rule 55 Short trip to weigh goods and return — no EWB if delivery challan accompanies and distance ≤ 20 km. Conditions: (a) accompanied by delivery challan as per Rule 55. (b) Distance ≤ 20 km.
(o) Empty cylinders for packing of LPG moved for reasons other than supply (Inserted Notif. 26/2018) Empty LPG cylinders being returned/moved for re-filling — no EWB required. Applicable to gas cylinder distributors. Return of empty cylinders is common — no EWB needed.
⚠️ KEY PRINCIPLE This list is exhaustive, not illustrative. If your goods/movement do not squarely fall in one of these clauses, EWB is required. Courts have consistently held that exemptions must be strictly construed — taxpayer claiming exemption must prove it falls within the clause.
Rule 138A — Documents & Devices to be Carried by Person-in-Charge of Conveyance
📄 Rule 138A — What Must the Driver/Person-in-Charge Carry?
📝 "Person in charge of conveyance" means the driver, pilot, captain, or any person responsible for the vehicle. If these documents are not produced on demand — the officer can detain the goods and invoke Section 129 (detention/penalty) or Section 130 (confiscation).
Rule 138A(1) — Mandatory Documents to Carry:
(a) INVOICE / BOE / DELIVERY CHALLAN
The invoice or bill of supply or delivery challan, as the case may be — original or reference number
📝 The tax invoice is the primary document. If invoice has IRN/QR code (e-invoice) — the QR code produced electronically is sufficient.
(b) E-WAY BILL
Copy of e-way bill in physical form OR e-way bill number in electronic form OR mapped to RFID device embedded on conveyance
📝 Driver need not carry paper printout of EWB — showing EWB number on mobile/tablet is sufficient. If RFID is embedded and mapped — RFID reader will capture it.
💡 Driver shows the EWB number on his GST Common Portal app screen — this is valid. Insisting on paper printout is not required by law.
EXCEPTION — Clause (b) NOT applicable
No need to carry e-way bill in case of movement of goods by rail, air or vessel
IMPORTED GOODS — ADDITIONAL REQUIREMENT
Person in charge of conveyance carrying imported goods → shall also carry a copy of Bill of Entry filed by importer and indicate the BoE number and date in Part A of EWB-01
RULE 138A(2) — E-INVOICE QR CODE
Where invoice issued under Rule 48(4) [e-invoice] → the QR code with embedded IRN produced electronically satisfies the invoice requirement — no physical copy of invoice needed
📝 If the supplier is under e-invoicing — the IRN/QR code is the proof. Officers must accept it on screen. Insisting on physical paper invoice when e-invoice QR code is shown is legally incorrect.
RULE 138A(4) — RFID
Commissioner may require class of transporters to obtain RFID Device and embed on conveyance and map e-way bill prior to movement
RULE 138A(5) — ALTERNATIVE DOCUMENTS
Where circumstances warrant → Commissioner may, by Notification, require person-in-charge to carry instead of e-way bill:
(a) Tax invoice / bill of supply / bill of entry; or
(b) Delivery challan (where goods transported for reasons other than supply)
📋 RFID: Commissioner notified. E-invoice: Rule 48(4). IRN: GST INV-1 form.
Rule 138B — Verification of Documents & Conveyances
🔍 Rule 138B — Verification Powers of Proper Officer
138B(1) — INTERCEPTION POWER
Commissioner or officer empowered by him may authorise proper officer to intercept any conveyance to verify EWB — in physical or electronic form — for both inter-State and intra-State movement
📝 Only an authorised proper officer can intercept. An officer who is NOT authorised has no power to stop and check. This is a valid legal defence.
🔍 GP Singh: Power to intercept is a coercive power — must be exercised by an authorised officer. Any unauthorised interception or detention renders the subsequent proceedings void. Always check whether the intercepting officer has specific authorisation.
138B(2) — RFID READERS
Commissioner shall get RFID readers installed at places where verification is required → verification through RFID readers where EWB has been mapped
138B(3) — WHO CAN PHYSICALLY VERIFY
Physical verification by proper officer as authorised by Commissioner
Proviso: On receipt of specific information on tax evasion — ANY OTHER OFFICER after obtaining approval from Commissioner or authorised officer
📝 Routine physical verification = only authorised officer. But if there is specific intelligence about evasion — any officer with Commissioner's approval can verify. Two different thresholds.
💡 Anti-evasion officer intercepts truck based on informer tip about undervalued goods — this is valid under the evasion proviso. Routine patrol officer stopping trucks without authorisation — NOT valid under Rule 138B(3).
📋 Link: If EWB verified in transit under Rule 138B → EWB cannot be cancelled [Rule 138(9)]. Inspection report must be filed under Rule 138C within 24 hours / 3 days.
Rule 138C — Inspection & Verification of Goods in Transit
📋 Rule 138C — Inspection Report Filing Timelines & One-Inspection Rule
138C(1) — INSPECTION REPORT TIMELINES
Part A of FORM GST EWB-03:
Summary report → within 24 hours of inspection online
Part B of FORM GST EWB-03:
Final report → within 3 days of inspection
📝 Both timelines counted from midnight of the date on which the vehicle was intercepted (not from time of interception).
EXTENSION AVAILABLE
Commissioner or authorised officer may extend Part B timeline for further period not exceeding 3 days on sufficient cause
🔍 GP Singh: Timeline is mandatory — if officer fails to file Part B within 3 days (+3 day extension), the inspection proceedings may be treated as lapsed. Taxpayer can argue no valid final report = no adverse order.
138C(2) — ONE INSPECTION RULE
Where physical verification done at one place within State/UT or any other State/UT → NO FURTHER physical verification of the same conveyance in that State/UT
UNLESS specific information on tax evasion received subsequently
📝 Once a truck is checked at one checkpost within a State, it cannot be stopped and checked again in the same State — unless new intelligence arrives. This prevents harassment at multiple checkposts.
💡 Truck verified at Pune checkpost — cannot be re-verified at Nashik checkpost in Maharashtra without fresh evasion intelligence. If officer at Nashik stops the truck citing "routine check" — cite Rule 138C(2) — one inspection is complete.
Rule 138D — Facility for Uploading Detention Information
🚨 Rule 138D — Transporter's Remedy When Vehicle is Detained
RULE
Where a vehicle has been intercepted and detained for a period exceeding 30 minutes → the transporter may upload the said information in FORM GST EWB-04 on the common portal
📝 This is a safeguard for transporters — if the officer is holding the vehicle without action, the transporter can go online and report the detention. This creates an official record and puts pressure on the officer to proceed lawfully. It is a right — not an obligation.
💡 Officer stops truck at 11 AM, does not complete inspection, vehicle held idle. At 11:31 AM, transporter can upload EWB-04 on portal reporting detention. This creates a timestamp record that is available to higher officers. Useful to later file a complaint for undue detention or to claim compensation for demurrage.
⚠️ NOTE Railway leasing of parcel space does NOT constitute "transportation by railway" under this Chapter — per Explanation inserted by Notif. 14/2018.
📝 If Railways are merely renting out wagon space (not actually transporting), GST EWB rules relating to "transport by rail" exemptions do NOT apply. The movement is treated as road transport for EWB purposes.
Rule 138E — Restriction on Furnishing Information in Part A of EWB-01
🚫 Rule 138E — EWB Part A Restriction for Non-Filers
Inserted vide Notif. 74/2018 — w.e.f. 21.06.2019
📝 If a registered person has not filed returns for the specified period, ALL persons (consignor, consignee, transporter, e-commerce operator, courier agency) are blocked from furnishing Part A of EWB-01 for any outward movement of that registered person's goods. This effectively blocks ALL goods movement for the defaulting supplier.
Persons Subject to Restriction — Two Categories:
(a) COMPOSITION DEALERS + NOTIF. 2/2019
Person paying tax u/s 10 (composition) or availing benefit of Notif. 02/2019-CT(Rate) [real estate scheme] → has NOT furnished statement in FORM GST CMP-08 for 2 consecutive quarters
(b) REGULAR TAXPAYERS
Person other than (a) → has NOT furnished return (GSTR-3B) for 2 consecutive tax periods
(tax period = monthly for regular, quarterly for QRMP)
📝 "Consecutive" is key — one missed return does not trigger this. Two back-to-back missed returns block EWB. Filing one return restores EWB access.
(c) NON-FILER OF OUTWARD SUPPLIES
Person who has NOT furnished statement of outward supplies (GSTR-1) for any 2 months/quarters
(d) SUSPENDED REGISTRATION
Person whose registration has been suspended under Rule 21A(1), (2) or (2A)
📝 Even a temporarily suspended registrant cannot use the EWB portal. This restriction applies from the date of suspension order.
RELIEF MECHANISM — EWB-05 Application
Registered person may apply to jurisdictional Commissioner in FORM GST EWB-05 → Commissioner on sufficient cause, with written reasons → may grant permission by order in FORM GST EWB-06 subject to conditions/restrictions

No rejection order shall be passed without giving reasonable opportunity of being heard.

State/UT Commissioner's grant/rejection = deemed to be Central Commissioner's grant/rejection (and vice versa).
🔍 GP Singh: The EWB-05 application is an administrative remedy — not a statutory appeal. The principles of natural justice (PHOB) still apply before any rejection. "Reasonable opportunity of being heard" cannot be reduced to a sham hearing. Rejection without PHOB is liable to be set aside in writ proceedings.
💡 Manufacturer who missed 2 months' GSTR-3B — goods blocked at his factory, cannot dispatch. Files EWB-05 explaining genuine reason (system error, illness). Commissioner grants EWB-06 with conditions. Without EWB-06 — complete business shutdown for the period. This rule has significant cash-flow implications.
COVID-19 RELAXATIONS (Historical)
Restriction did NOT apply during 20.03.2020 to 15.10.2020 (returns for Feb 2020–Aug 2020 not filed) and 01.05.2021 to 18.08.2021 (returns for Mar 2021–May 2021 not filed) — special relaxations during COVID periods.
🟢 Rule 138F — Intra-State E-Way Bill for Gold, Precious Stones & Precious Metals
Inserted vide Notification No. 38/2023 – CT dated 04.08.2023 | Latest Provision
🟢 RECENTLY INSERTED PROVISION — Notification No. 38/2023-CT dated 04.08.2023 | This rule creates a STATE-SPECIFIC intra-State EWB regime for Gold, Precious Stones & Precious Metals
🟢 Rule 138F — Special Intra-State EWB for Gold & Precious Items
Applicable only when State Commissioner mandates it + consignment value exceeds State threshold
📝 This rule creates an OPTIONAL regime — each State's Commissioner can decide whether to apply this rule. Until a State Commissioner mandates it, Rule 138F does not apply in that State. It is designed specifically to track intra-State movement of high-value goods like gold, diamonds, etc. which are otherwise exempt from the regular EWB requirement (Annexure to Rule 138(14)).
Rule 138F(1) — When Does This Rule Apply? Two Cumulative Conditions:
CONDITION 1
State/UT Commissioner has mandated furnishing of information for intra-State movement of goods against S.No. 4 and 5 of Annexure to Rule 138(14) — i.e., precious metals, gems, jewellery
CONDITION 2
Consignment value exceeds such amount (not below ₹2 lakhs) as notified by State/UT Commissioner in consultation with jurisdictional Principal Chief Commissioner or Chief Commissioner of Central Tax
📝 Minimum threshold is ₹2 lakhs — State can set higher. Below that amount, even gold movement doesn't need intra-State EWB under Rule 138F.
WHO MUST GENERATE
Every registered person causing intra-State movement:
(i) In relation to a supply; OR (ii) For reasons other than supply; OR (iii) Due to inward supply from unregistered person
→ shall before commencement of movement → furnish information in Part A of FORM GST EWB-01 → unique number generated
📝 E-commerce operators and courier agencies can furnish Part A with consignor's authorisation.
🟢 KEY DIFFERENCE FROM REGULAR RULE 138
Under Rule 138F: Part B of EWB-01 (vehicle/conveyance details) is NOT required to be furnished.
After furnishing Part A → EWB is generated without vehicle details.
This simplifies compliance for jewellers/bullion dealers who may not know transport details in advance.
📝 In regular EWB (Rule 138) — you need vehicle number (Part B) for road transport. In Rule 138F — only the goods details (Part A) are needed. Precious metals can move on any vehicle without updating Part B — only cargo identity matters.
Rule 138F(3) — GSTR-1 Integration
Part A information → made available to registered supplier on common portal → can be used for GSTR-1 filing
Rule 138F(4) — CANCELLATION
EWB may be cancelled within 24 hours of generation if goods not transported or transported differently → Cannot cancel if verified in transit (Rule 138B)
Rule 138F(5) — No EWB Even Under Rule 138F
EWB not required even under 138F for:
(a) Goods from customs port/airport/ACC/LCS to ICD/CFS for customs clearance
(b) Goods under customs bond/supervision/seal
Rule 138F(6) — Applicable Rules
Rules 138(10), (11), (12), 138A, 138B, 138C, 138D, 138E apply mutatis mutandis to EWBs generated under Rule 138F
📝 Mutatis mutandis = with necessary changes. All verification, inspection, restriction, and cancellation rules of regular EWB apply equally to Rule 138F EWBs.
🟢 This is a new provision (Aug 2023) — check whether your State's Commissioner has issued notification mandating Rule 138F and the minimum threshold amount. As of Nov 2025, Maharashtra and some other States may have issued such notifications — verify from State GST portal.
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal — Chapter XVI | E-Way Rules 138–138F
These statutory arguments are for use in personal hearings before GST officers, Appellate Authorities & GSTAT. State them confidently and cite the specific sub-rule.
Rule 138B(1) — Authorisation Requirement for Interception
CGST Rules, 2017 | Rule 138B | Applicable in all detention/seizure cases
Key Statutory Argument
Under Rule 138B(1), only a proper officer specifically authorised by the Commissioner can intercept a conveyance to verify the e-way bill. An officer who has not received such authorisation has no power to stop, detain, or inspect the vehicle. Any detention by an unauthorised officer is without jurisdiction — and all subsequent proceedings (seizure, penalty, confiscation) are also without jurisdiction.
When to Use — When challenging detention order under Section 129 — demand proof of the intercepting officer's specific authorisation under Rule 138B(1). If authorisation is not produced, file writ challenging the detention as without jurisdiction.
Rule 138C(2) — One Inspection Rule (Anti-Harassment)
CGST Rules, 2017 | Rule 138C(2) | Applicable at multiple checkposts
Key Statutory Argument
Rule 138C(2) provides that where physical verification of goods in a conveyance has been done at one place within the State or Union territory or in any other State or Union territory, no further physical verification of the same conveyance shall be carried out again in the same State or Union territory — unless specific information relating to evasion of tax is made available subsequently. Re-interception of the same conveyance at a second checkpost within the same State — without fresh evasion intelligence — is prohibited by statute.
When to Use — When a truck that has already been checked at one checkpost is stopped again at another checkpost in the same State. Cite Rule 138C(2) and demand the "specific information on evasion" that triggered the second check. Absence of such specific information makes the second interception illegal.
Rule 138C(1) — Mandatory Timelines for Inspection Report
CGST Rules, 2017 | Rule 138C(1) | Applicable in all detention / seizure proceedings
Key Statutory Argument
Under Rule 138C(1), the proper officer is required to record the summary inspection report in Part A of FORM GST EWB-03 within twenty-four hours of inspection, and the final inspection report in Part B within three days. Timelines commence from the midnight of the date of interception. Failure to file the report within the prescribed time — without seeking extension — renders the inspection proceedings procedurally defective. A final order passed beyond the timeline, without compliance of Rule 138C, cannot stand on procedural grounds.
When to Use — When the officer has detained goods but not filed EWB-03 Part A within 24 hours or Part B within 3 days. Calculate timelines precisely from midnight of date of interception. File representation demanding either release of goods or rejection of detention as time-barred.
Rule 138(9) — Cancellation Window & Verification Bar
CGST Rules, 2017 | Rule 138(9) | Applicable in EWB cancellation disputes
Key Statutory Argument
Rule 138(9) provides that an e-way bill may be cancelled electronically on the common portal within twenty-four hours of generation if goods are either not transported or not transported as per the details in the e-way bill. Once the e-way bill has been verified in transit in accordance with Rule 138B, it cannot be cancelled. The unique number generated under Rule 138(1) is valid for fifteen days for updation of Part B.
When to Use — (a) When assessee wants to cancel EWB for goods not dispatched — must be within 24 hours. (b) When department argues cancellation after verification — not permissible. (c) When Part B not updated — unique number still valid for 15 days from generation.
Rule 138(14) — No E-Way Bill Required — Exhaustive Exemption List
CGST Rules, 2017 | Rule 138(14) + Annexure | Primary defence in EWB detention cases
Key Statutory Argument
Notwithstanding anything contained in Rule 138, no e-way bill is required where the goods or movement falls within the exhaustive categories in Rule 138(14)(a) to (o). These include: goods specified in the Annexure (precious metals, jewellery, currency, etc.), non-motorised conveyance, customs-bonded goods, defence movements, government rail movements, empty cargo containers, weighbridge trips within 20 km with delivery challan, and empty LPG cylinders. This list is exhaustive — any goods not covered therein requires EWB. Conversely, where the movement squarely falls within one of these clauses, EWB is not a legal requirement and no penalty can be levied for its absence.
When to Use — First line of defence in any EWB detention case. Match the specific clause — prove goods/movement falls within one of the 15 categories. Prepare brief with applicable notification number and HSN code where needed.
Rule 138(12) — Deemed Acceptance / 72-Hour Rule
CGST Rules, 2017 | Rule 138(12) | Applicable in ITC matching and recipient liability disputes
Key Statutory Argument
Under Rule 138(12), where the person to whom the EWB information has been made available does not communicate acceptance or rejection within seventy-two hours of the details being made available, or at the time of delivery — whichever is earlier — it shall be deemed that the said details have been accepted. This deemed acceptance is a statutory fact — recipient cannot later claim the goods were not received or that the EWB details were wrong, if the 72-hour window has passed without rejection.
When to Use — When department denies ITC to recipient claiming goods not received — but EWB shows delivery and recipient didn't reject within 72 hours. Cite Rule 138(12) — deemed acceptance = delivery is established by statutory deeming fiction.
Rule 138A(2) — E-Invoice QR Code is Valid Document
CGST Rules, 2017 | Rule 138A(2) | Applicable in e-invoice interception disputes
Key Statutory Argument
Where invoice is issued under Rule 48(4) [e-invoicing], the Quick Response (QR) code having an embedded Invoice Reference Number (IRN) produced electronically is sufficient for verification by the proper officer in lieu of the physical copy of the invoice. No officer can insist on production of a physical paper invoice where e-invoicing is applicable. Production of IRN/QR code on screen satisfies the requirement of Rule 138A(a).
When to Use — When officer detains vehicle insisting on physical invoice but driver shows e-invoice QR code on phone/tablet. Cite Rule 138A(2) — QR code with IRN = valid document. Demand release of goods on this statutory provision.
🟢 Rule 138(3) — ENR-03 Requirement for Unregistered Persons (w.e.f. 11.02.2025)
CGST Rules — Notification No. 12/2024-CT dated 10.07.2024 | w.e.f. 11.02.2025 | Latest 2025 Amendment
Key Statutory Argument
With effect from 11 February 2025, any unregistered person required to generate an e-way bill (under the fourth proviso to Rule 138(1)) or opting to generate an e-way bill, must first submit their details electronically in FORM GST ENR-03 on the common portal — directly or through a Facilitation Centre notified by the Commissioner. Upon validation, a unique enrolment number is generated and communicated to such person. Without a valid enrolment number under ENR-03, an unregistered person cannot generate an e-way bill from 11.02.2025.
When to Use — (a) When unregistered handicraft seller is detained for movement without EWB — check whether they have ENR-03 enrolment from 11.02.2025 onwards. (b) When investigating whether unregistered supplier has complied with new ENR-03 requirement before generating EWB. This is a 2025 compliance point that many traders are unaware of.
Rule 138E — EWB Restriction Does Not Apply if Returns Filed
CGST Rules, 2017 | Rule 138E | Applicable in return-default / EWB blocking disputes
Key Statutory Argument
The restriction under Rule 138E on furnishing Part A of EWB-01 applies only where the registered person has not furnished returns for TWO CONSECUTIVE tax periods. Filing of even one pending return — making the consecutive default condition non-applicable — should restore EWB access. Further, before rejecting an EWB-05 application for restoration of EWB access, the Commissioner must afford a reasonable opportunity of being heard. No order of rejection shall be passed without such opportunity.
When to Use — When client's EWB access is blocked — first check: is it truly 2 consecutive periods? File pending returns immediately — often restores access automatically. If EWB-05 is rejected without hearing — challenge the rejection in writ as violation of natural justice.
For professional litigation use | Pawan Soni & Associates, Ichalkaranji
CGST Rules — Ch.XVII–XIX (Insp., Demands, Offences)

⚖️ CGST Rules 2017 — Chapter XVII, XVIII & XIX

⚠️ Rules marked AMDT 2025 are inserted/substituted by Notification No. 11/2025-CT dated 27.03.2025

Permitted / Valid
Prohibited / Penalty
Condition / If / Unless
Discretionary / Optional

📋 Rule Reference

Rule 139 — Inspection, Search & Seizure
Rule 140 — Bond & Security for Release
Rule 141 — Procedure: Seized Goods
📌 Chapter XVIII — Demands & Recovery

Rule 142A — Recovery under Existing Laws

⚔️ Hearing Arsenal
🔍 Chapter XVII — Inspection, Search & Seizure | Rules 139–141 | Overview
Rule 139 — Authorization + Procedure for Inspection/Search/Seizure Rule 140 — Provisional Release on Bond (INS-04) Rule 141 — Perishable/Hazardous Goods — Disposal Procedure
Rule 139 — Inspection, Search & Seizure (§67)
🔍 Rule 139 — Authorization for Inspection / Search / Seizure
Section 67 CGST Act | FORM GST INS-01 to INS-03
PRE-CONDITION
Officer not below rank of Joint Commissioner must have reasons to believe that a place needs to be visited for inspection / search / seizure u/s 67
🔍 "Reasons to believe" — GP Singh: This is an objective test, not merely subjective satisfaction. SC in ITO v. Lakhmani Mewal Das held that reasons must be based on relevant material and be rational — they must be capable of scrutiny. A court can examine whether the reasons have a rational nexus with the belief. Mere suspicion ≠ reasons to believe.
📝 The JC cannot just decide on a hunch — there must be tangible information or intelligence forming the basis for the authorization. This is your first line of attack if the search is challenged.
5 Sub-rules of Rule 139 — Step-by-step Procedure
R.139(1)
JC issues authorization in INS-01 to subordinate officer to conduct inspection / search / seizure
📝 Authorization must pre-exist the visit. If the officer walks in without INS-01 in hand, the entire action is unauthorized — grounds for writ.
🔍 GP Singh: Coercive provisions must be strictly construed. Any deviation from mandatory procedural precondition renders the action void — not merely voidable.
R.139(2)
If goods/documents/books/things are liable to seizure u/s 67(2) → officer makes order of seizure in INS-02
R.139(3)
Officer may entrust custody of seized goods to owner/custodian for safekeeping — person shall NOT remove/part/deal without prior permission
📝 The goods stay with you physically but legally they belong to the department. Moving them without permission = contempt of authority.
R.139(4)
Where seizure is NOT practicable → officer issues order of prohibition in INS-03 — owner cannot deal with goods without permission
📝 An INS-03 freezes the goods in place without physically taking them. Useful for bulky or fixed assets.
R.139(5)
Officer must prepare inventory of seized goods/documents — containing description, quantity/unit, mark/model — signed by person from whom seized
📝 Mandatory inventory protects both sides — it prevents the department from adding to or removing items and protects the assessee from false claims.
🔍 GP Singh: Non-preparation of inventory or unsigned inventory is not a mere irregularity — it goes to the root of the validity of seizure. A safeguard provision must be strictly enforced.
📋 Corresponding Section: §67 CGST Act | Forms: INS-01, INS-02, INS-03
Rule 140 — Bond & Security for Provisional Release of Seized Goods
🔓 Rule 140 — Provisional Release of Seized Goods
Bond + Security | FORM GST INS-04
MECHANISM
Seized goods may be released on provisional basis upon:
① Execution of a bond for value of goods in INS-04
② Furnishing bank guarantee equivalent to applicable tax + interest + penalty
📝 You give a bond (promise to pay) for the full value of goods AND a bank guarantee for the tax/interest/penalty. This is how a business recovers seized stock to continue operations while the dispute plays out.
💡 Goods worth ₹50 lakh seized. GST liability assessed at ₹9 lakh. Bond: ₹50 lakh; Bank Guarantee: ₹9 lakh (+ interest + penalty). On furnishing both → goods released provisionally. Business can resume use of goods.
EXPLANATION
"Applicable tax" includes: Central Tax + State Tax OR Central Tax + UT Tax AND Cess u/s GST (Compensation to States) Act, 2017
FAILURE CONSEQUENCE — R.140(2)
If person fails to produce goods at appointed date and place → Security (bank guarantee) shall be encashed and adjusted against tax + interest + penalty + fine
📝 The bank guarantee is not a mere formality — it gets called if you don't comply. This is a strict consequence provision.
📋 Form: INS-04 | Section: §67(6) CGST Act
Rule 141 — Procedure for Perishable / Hazardous Seized Goods
⚠️ Rule 141 — Special Procedure for Perishable / Hazardous Goods
FORM GST INS-05
R.141(1) — PAYMENT ROUTE
Person pays HIGHER of:
① Market price of goods, OR
② Tax + interest + penalty payable
→ Goods released in INS-05 on proof of payment
📝 Pay the tax liability or the market value — whichever is more — and the goods come back immediately. Designed to prevent perishables from rotting in custody.
💡 Perishable food goods worth ₹5 lakh seized. GST demand ₹3 lakh. Person pays ₹5 lakh (higher) → INS-05 issued → goods released forthwith.
R.141(2) — NON-PAYMENT ROUTE
If person fails to pay → Proper officer may dispose of goods
→ Amount realized adjusted against tax + interest + penalty + any other amount
📝 The department doesn't hold perishables indefinitely — they will sell/dispose and credit the proceeds against your liability. Any surplus? You may not get it back easily.
🔍 GP Singh: "May dispose" — the word "may" confers discretion on the proper officer. SC in numerous cases held that discretion must be exercised reasonably. An unreasonable disposal at throwaway price can be challenged in writ.
NOTE "Proper officer" — substituted by Notification 16/2020-CT dated 23.03.2020 (was "Commissioner" earlier). Delegation of power downward.
📋 Form: INS-05 | Section: §67 CGST Act
⚖️ Chapter XVIII — Demands & Recovery | Rules 142–161 | Overview
Rule 142 — DRC-01 Framework Rule 142A — Existing Law Demands Rule 142B — DRC-01D (s.79) Rule 143 — Deduction from Money Owed Rule 144 — Auction of Goods Rule 144A — Transit Goods Auction Rule 145 — Third Person Recovery Rules 146–147 — Decree/Property Rules 148–153 — Safeguards Rule 154 — Proceeds Distribution Rules 155–161 — Recovery Routes Rule 159 — Provisional Attachment
Rule 142 — Notice & Order for Demand | DRC-01 Framework (§73/§74/§74A)
📋 Rule 142 — The Complete DRC Framework
Demand Initiation → Voluntary Payment → Order → Conclusion of Proceedings
📝 Rule 142 is the procedural backbone of every GST demand. Every SCN, every reply, every payment before order, every conclusion of proceeding passes through this rule. Master this flow and you master GST demand procedure.
Sub-rule Provision / Action Form Used Plain English GP Singh / Litigation Applicable Sections
R.142(1)(a) Proper officer serves notice with DRC-01 summary electronically DRC-01 Summary of the SCN demand is uploaded online — you get electronic notice. DRC-01 without underlying valid SCN = void. Check service of notice first. §52, §73, §74, §74A, §76, §122–§130
R.142(1)(b) With statement u/s §73(3)/§74(3)/§74A(3) — DRC-02 summary electronically DRC-02 When multiple periods covered by one SCN via statement — DRC-02 specifies amounts period-wise. §73(3), §74(3), §74A(3)
R.142(1A) Pre-notice communication — before serving SCN, proper officer may communicate details of tax/interest/penalty in Part A of DRC-01A DRC-01A Part A This is the "show-cause before show-cause" — informal intimation before formal SCN. A chance to pay and close the matter without formal proceedings. Failure to give DRC-01A opportunity may render SCN challengeable on principles of natural justice if assessee can show prejudice. §73(1), §74(1), §74A(1)
R.142(2) If person pays tax+interest (§73(5)) or tax+interest+penalty (§74(5)/§74A clause(i)) before notice → files DRC-03, gets DRC-04 acknowledgement DRC-03, DRC-04 Voluntary pre-notice payment extinguishes proceedings. DRC-04 is the acknowledgement — the proceeding ends here without any SCN being served. Best exit strategy — pay under §73(5) (no penalty) before SCN. DRC-04 is your proof that proceedings concluded. Preserve it carefully. §73(5), §74(5), §74A clause(i)
R.142(2A) If partial payment or submission against DRC-01A → file Part B of DRC-01A; proper officer responds in Part C of DRC-01A DRC-01A Parts B & C You can partly pay or contest the pre-notice intimation. The officer then accepts or rejects via Part C — a mini-adjudication before the formal SCN. §73(1), §74(1), §74A(1)
R.142(2B) If amount paid via DRC-03 against a demand but NOT adjusted → file DRC-03A to credit it to Liability Register against the specific debit entry DRC-03A DRC-03 payment must be linked to the specific demand — DRC-03A is the linking application. Without this, payment floats unattributed and recovery continues. Critical: DRC-03A cannot be filed if DRC-05 already issued. Once proceedings concluded, no re-attribution. §52, §73, §74, §74A, §76, §122–§130
R.142(3) If payment made within 30 days of notice (§73(8)/§74(8)/§74A clause(ii)) → files DRC-03, officer issues DRC-05 concluding proceedings DRC-03, DRC-05 Pay within 30 days of SCN receipt with reduced penalty → proceedings conclude with DRC-05. Best post-SCN settlement route. DRC-05 = Discharge Certificate. Get it. Keep it. It is your protection against re-opening. §73(8), §74(8), §74A clause(ii), §129(1)
R.142(4) Reply to SCN / representation → filed in DRC-06 DRC-06 DRC-06 is your formal reply to the SCN. All your arguments, evidence, submissions go here. This is the most important document you will file. DRC-06 must be timely. Delayed or absent DRC-06 → order passed ex-parte. Ex-parte order is harder to challenge at appeal stage. §73(9), §74(9), §74A(6), §76(3)
R.142(5) Summary of order uploaded in DRC-07 specifying tax/interest/penalty payable DRC-07 DRC-07 is the demand notice for recovery. Once DRC-07 is uploaded, the clock for recovery starts. Check DRC-07 carefully — errors in amounts vs the actual order = ground for rectification u/s 161 or DRC-08. §52, §62, §63, §64, §73–§76, §122–§125, §127, §129, §130
R.142(6) Order in DRC-07 = Notice for Recovery DRC-07 No separate recovery notice needed after DRC-07. The demand order IS the recovery notice. Recovery proceedings under Rule 143–161 can begin immediately. §79 CGST Act
R.142(7) Rectification order u/s 161 → uploaded in DRC-08 DRC-08 If officer finds an error in his own order — arithmetic mistake, clerical error — he can rectify in DRC-08. This is not an appeal — it is self-correction within 3 months. §161 CGST Act
📋 All Forms: DRC-01 to DRC-08 | Sections: §73, §74, §74A, §76, §79, §161 CGST Act
Rule 142A — Recovery of Dues under Existing Laws (Pre-GST Demands)
⚖️ Rule 142A — Pre-GST Era Demands → Recovery under GST
DRC-07A | Inserted vide Notification 60/2018-CT dated 30.10.2018
SCOPE
Orders under existing laws (Pre-GST: Service Tax, VAT, Central Excise) creating demand of tax/duty/penalty/fee that becomes recoverable after appointed day → can be uploaded for recovery under GST machinery
📝 Pre-GST demands that couldn't be collected before July 1, 2017 can now be recovered using the GST recovery machinery. The government doesn't lose its old tax dues just because GST replaced the old laws.
R.142A(1)
Summary of order uploaded in DRC-07A → posted in Part II of Electronic Liability Register in PMT-01
R.142A(2)
If demand rectified/modified/quashed in appeal/review/revision under existing laws → summary uploaded and PMT-01 updated accordingly
📝 Win your old-law appeal → the GST liability register is automatically updated. You need to upload the order via the new form.
📋 Form: DRC-07A | PMT-01 | Applicable to pre-GST era demands recoverable after 01.07.2017
Rule 142B — Intimation of Amounts Recoverable u/s 79 (DRC-01D)
⚡ Rule 142B — Fast-Track Recovery Intimation via DRC-01D
Inserted vide Notification 38/2023-CT dated 04.08.2023
📝 Where tax/interest has become payable under §75/Rule 88C and remains unpaid — a new direct route exists. The officer sends DRC-01D intimating the defaulter to pay within 7 days. This DRC-01D itself IS the notice for recovery — no separate steps needed.
TRIGGER
Amount of tax/interest recoverable u/s 79 — under §75 read with Rule 88C — remains unpaid
R.142B(1)
Officer intimates details in DRC-01D electronically → directs person to pay within 7 days
📝 Only 7 days to respond! Much faster than normal demand proceedings. Posted in PMT-01 liability register.
R.142B(2)
DRC-01D = Notice for Recovery itself
R.142B(3)
If unpaid after 7 days → officer proceeds under Rules 143/144/145/146/147/155/156/157/160
📝 All recovery modes open up automatically after the 7-day window. No further notice. Act fast if you receive DRC-01D.
💡 Rule 88C notices for GST mismatch unpaid → DRC-01D issued → 7 days to pay → if unpaid, attachment of bank account / property can follow directly under Rules 144/147/159.
📋 Form: DRC-01D | Section: §79, §75 CGST Act | Rule 88C
Rules 143–145 — Recovery Methods: Deduction / Goods Sale / Third Person
RuleMethodMechanismFormKey Safeguard / Litigation Point
Rule 143
§79(1)(a)		 Recovery by deduction from money owed to defaulter Officer directs a "specified officer" (Central/State/UT Govt, Board, Corporation, public sector body) to deduct amount from any money owing to defaulter. "Specified officer" broadly covers all government entities and PSUs. DRC-09 Scope of "specified officer" is very wide — includes all PSU banks and government companies. If defaulter has contracts with any public body, those payments can be intercepted.
Rule 144
§79(1)(b)		 Recovery by sale of goods under officer's control Officer prepares inventory + market value estimate → sells only so much as needed for recovery + admin costs → auction/e-auction with 15 days' notice in DRC-10 → successful bidder pays in 15 days → DRC-12 certificate issued. Bid deposit can be specified. DRC-10, DRC-11, DRC-12 15-day auction notice mandatory (except perishables). Challenge auction if notice period compressed without perishable/hazardous justification. Pre-bid deposit forfeited if successful bidder defaults.
Rule 145
§79(1)(c)		 Recovery from third person Officer serves notice on "third person" (anyone holding money belonging to defaulter — bank, debtor, tenant, etc.) directing deposit of specified amount. Third person paying gets DRC-14 certificate of discharge. DRC-13, DRC-14 DRC-14 protects the third person — they pay without incurring liability to the defaulter. But notice must correctly identify the amount — excess attachment of third person's own money is challengeable.
📋 Forms: DRC-09, DRC-10, DRC-11, DRC-12, DRC-13, DRC-14 | Section: §79 CGST Act
Rule 144A — Recovery of Penalty by Sale of Transit Goods (§129)
🚚 Rule 144A — Auction of Detained/Seized Transit Goods for Penalty Recovery
Inserted vide Notification 40/2021-CT dated 29.12.2021 | Section 129
TRIGGER
Person transporting goods or owner fails to pay penalty u/s 129(1) within 15 days of receipt of copy of order u/s 129(3)
Officer prepares inventory + market value estimate → initiates auction via DRC-10 notice (15-day notice minimum)

PROVISO: If penalty paid (incl. custody/handling expenses) AFTER 15 days but BEFORE DRC-10 notice → officer cancels auction and releases goods
📝 There is a last-chance window between Day 15 and the DRC-10 notice — pay all dues during this window and your goods come back before the auction is advertised.
STAY PROTECTION — R.144A(8)
If appeal filed u/s 107(6) → recovery proceedings under Rule 144A are deemed stayed
EXCEPTION: Stay NOT available for perishable/hazardous goods
📝 File your appeal and the auction stops automatically — except for perishables. This is a built-in statutory stay. You don't need to separately apply for a stay if goods are non-perishable.
💡 E-way bill violation. Goods detained. Penalty order u/s 129(3) passed. Transporter doesn't pay within 15 days → auction initiated. But transporter files appeal u/s 107 → auction automatically stayed. Goods remain with department but cannot be sold during appeal.
📋 Form: DRC-10, DRC-12 | Section: §129 CGST Act | Notification 40/2021-CT
Rules 146–147 — Recovery through Court Decree & Sale of Property
RuleMethodKey FeaturesForm
Rule 146
§79(1)(d)		 Recovery through execution of civil court decree Officer sends request in DRC-15 to civil court → court executes attached decree → net proceeds credited for settlement. Useful when defaulter has a civil decree in his favour from which the government can recover. DRC-15
Rule 147
§79(1)(b)		 Recovery by sale of movable and/or immovable property Attachment: Officer prepares list → estimates market value → issues attachment/distraint order + notice for sale in DRC-16. Encumbrance placed with Revenue/Transport Authority.

For immovable property: Order affixed on property; remains till sale confirmation.

For movable property: Physical seizure by officer; auction via DRC-17 (15-day notice).

Claim/Objection: Any person can file claim that property is not liable — officer investigates — if satisfied, releases property; if not, rejects and proceeds with auction.

Transfer: DRC-12 certificate → rights/title vest in bidder; stamp duty paid by transferee.

Special: Negotiable instruments/shares may be sold through broker instead of public auction. 		DRC-16, DRC-17, DRC-12
KEY LITIGATION POINT — Rule 147(8)-(11)
Third party claiming ownership of attached property must file claim showing interest ON THE DATE OF ATTACHMENT ORDER. This is your window to protect third-party assets wrongly attached.
📋 Forms: DRC-15, DRC-16, DRC-17, DRC-12 | Section: §79 CGST Act
Rules 148–153 — Auction Safeguards, Attachment of Debts/Shares & Partnership
RuleSafeguard / ProvisionLitigation Significance
Rule 148 No officer involved in sale may bid, acquire or attempt to acquire any interest in property sold — directly or indirectly If any official is found to have bid or influenced the auction → entire sale is void. Check for officer involvement in suspicious low-price auctions.
Rule 149 No sale under Chapter XVIII shall take place on Sunday / general holidays / notified holidays for the area Sales on holidays can be challenged as void. Check auction dates carefully.
Rule 150 Proper officer may seek police assistance for discharge of duties — officer-in-charge of local police station must depute officers Right of proper officer to police escort — relevant in obstruction cases.
Rule 151 Attachment of debts/shares/movable property not in possession of defaulter via DRC-16 written order:
• Debt: prohibits debtor from paying defaulter
• Share: prohibits transfer/dividend
• Other movable: prohibits person in possession from giving it to defaulter
Debtor can pay officer directly — deemed paid to defaulter		 DRC-16 copy must be affixed at officer's office AND sent to debtor/corporation. Non-service renders attachment incomplete. Debtor/corporation paying defaulter after receiving DRC-16 is liable.
Rule 152 If property in custody of court or public officer → officer sends attachment order to that court/officer Covers provident fund, court deposits, government dues payable to defaulter.
Rule 153 Partnership interest attachment → officer makes order charging partner's share in partnership property and profits + appoints receiver for share of profits + may order sale of interest Other partners can redeem the charged interest or purchase at sale — protecting the firm. Individual partner's liability can be recovered from his share of profits including accrued and accruing amounts.
Rule 154 — Disposal of Sale Proceeds (Priority Waterfall)
💰 Rule 154 — Distribution of Sale Proceeds from Auction
Substituted vide Notification 40/2021-CT dated 29.12.2021
📝 When goods/property are auctioned for recovery, the money doesn't all go to the government — there is a strict waterfall/priority order. Whatever is left after all dues goes back to the defaulter.
PRIORITY 1 (First)
Administrative cost of recovery process
PRIORITY 2 (Next)
Amount to be recovered OR penalty payable u/s 129(3), as the case may be
PRIORITY 3 (Next)
Any other amount due under CGST / IGST / UTGST / State GST Acts and rules
PRIORITY 4 (Balance)
Credit to Electronic Cash Ledger of registered owner OR to bank account of unregistered person
R.154(2) — UNCLAIMED BALANCE
If balance cannot be paid within 6 months from date of sale → credited to Consumer Welfare Fund
📝 If the department can't return the surplus to you within 6 months, they credit it to the welfare fund and it's gone. File your claim for surplus proceeds promptly after auction.
💡 Property auctioned for ₹80 lakh. Admin cost ₹2 lakh. Tax demand ₹50 lakh. Other GST dues ₹5 lakh. Surplus: ₹23 lakh → credited to your e-cash ledger. If you de-register: bank account. If unclaimed for 6 months: Consumer Welfare Fund.
📋 Section: §79 CGST Act | Consumer Welfare Fund
Rules 155–161 — Multiple Recovery Routes, Instalment Facility & Liquidation
RuleRecovery RouteMechanismFormKey Condition
Rule 155
§79(1)(e)		 Land Revenue Authority Officer sends certificate to Collector/Commissioner → recovered as if it were land revenue DRC-18 Powerful route — land revenue recovery is coercive and fast. No court involvement. Challenge at writ court if demand itself is disputed.
Rule 156
§79(1)(f)		 Recovery as Fine through Court Officer applies to Magistrate → recovered as if it were a fine under CrPC DRC-19 Criminal court route — only if other methods fail or are inappropriate. Magistrate can order imprisonment for fine default.
Rule 157 Recovery from Surety If any person has become surety for defaulter → proceedings against surety as if surety were the defaulter — Guarantors/sureties beware — you are treated as the defaulter itself. Surety's property can be attached and sold under all recovery rules.
Rule 158
§80		 Payment in Instalments Taxable person files DRC-20 → Commissioner calls jurisdictional officer report on financial ability → Commissioner grants up to 24 monthly instalments via DRC-21 DRC-20, DRC-21 NOT available if: (a) existing default where recovery is on, OR (b) instalment was given in preceding FY, OR (c) amount less than ₹25,000. These three disqualifications are absolute.
Rule 160
§88		 Recovery from Company in Liquidation Commissioner serves claim on liquidator in DRC-24 for tax/interest/penalty/any amount due under Act DRC-24 GST dues have first charge u/s 82 on assets. Liquidator must pay GST before other creditors except those with prior statutory charges.
Rule 161
§84		 Continuation of Recovery on Reduction/Enhancement Intimation/notice for reduction or enhancement of demand u/s 84 issued in DRC-25 DRC-25 If appeal court reduces or enhances demand → DRC-25 issued to adjust recovery proceedings accordingly. "Intimation" replaced "order" by Notification 26/2022-CT dated 26.12.2022.
INSTALMENT ELIGIBILITY CHECKLIST — Rule 158
✅ Amount ≥ ₹25,000 | ✅ No existing default in recovery | ✅ No instalment given in preceding FY | ✅ Genuine financial hardship | → Max 24 monthly instalments possible
Rule 159 — Provisional Attachment of Property (§83)
🔒 Rule 159 — Provisional Attachment
Section 83 CGST Act | DRC-22 / DRC-22A / DRC-23
📝 Provisional attachment is the most feared recovery power — the Commissioner can freeze your bank account and property before any final order is passed, just to protect revenue. But it has strict procedural requirements that must be challenged if violated.
R.159(1)
Commissioner passes order in DRC-22 mentioning details of property attached
🔍 GP Singh: §83 is a pre-adjudication coercive power — courts have consistently held it must be exercised sparingly and only when there is a real apprehension of the taxable person disposing of assets. SC in Radha Krishan Industries held provisional attachment must be in proportion to the tax demand — attaching entire business assets disproportionate to tax demand = abuse of power.
R.159(2)
Copy of DRC-22 sent to Revenue/Transport Authority to place encumbrance
AUTO EXPIRY: Encumbrance removed on written instruction OR on expiry of 1 year from issuance
📝 Provisional attachment automatically expires after 1 year! If adjudication is not completed, the attachment lapses by operation of law. Track the 1-year anniversary.
R.159(3)–(4)
If property is perishable/hazardous → person pays LOWER of: market price OR amount payable → released in DRC-23.
If failure to pay → Commissioner may dispose
R.159(5)
Person may file objection in DRC-22A that property is NOT liable to attachment → Commissioner gives opportunity of being heard → may release via DRC-23
💡 Third party's property wrongly attached (e.g., property transferred before SCN) → file DRC-22A immediately showing it's not the defaulter's property → Commissioner should release.
R.159(6)
If Commissioner satisfied that property is no longer liable to attachment → releases via DRC-23
⚠️ CRITICAL LITIGATION POINTS
(1) DRC-22 must state reasons — a blanket attachment without reasons = challengeable in writ
(2) 1-year auto-expiry under R.159(2) — demand it if adjudication is prolonged
(3) DRC-22A is your right — use it immediately on receipt of DRC-22
(4) Attachment of third party's bank account: SC in M/s Everest Industries held this violates Art.19(1)(g) if not the assessee's own account
📋 Forms: DRC-22, DRC-22A, DRC-23 | Section: §83 CGST Act | Notification 52/2023-CT
⚔️ Chapter XIX — Offences & Penalties | Rules 162–164 | Overview
Rule 162 — Compounding of Offences (CPD-01/02) Rule 163 — Consent-based Sharing (§158A) ⭐ Rule 164 — Section 128A Waiver Procedure (AMDT 2025)
Rule 162 — Procedure for Compounding of Offences (§138)
⚖️ Rule 162 — Compounding of Offences under Section 138
Application → Scrutiny → Order → Payment → Immunity
📝 Compounding = paying a sum of money to end a criminal prosecution under GST. It is available before OR after prosecution is launched. Once compounded, prosecution ends. But immunity can be withdrawn if you concealed material facts.
StepActionFormTimeline/Condition
1. Application
R.162(1)		 Applicant files application for compounding to Commissioner — before OR after prosecution CPD-01 No specified timeline — available at any stage of prosecution
2. Report
R.162(2)		 Commissioner calls report from concerned officer on particulars + any other relevant information Internal Before deciding application
3. Order
R.162(3)		 Commissioner, on being satisfied applicant made FULL AND COMPLETE disclosure → allows application with compounding amount and grants immunity OR rejects within 90 days CPD-02 90 days from receipt. No opportunity of hearing = invalid order. Full and complete disclosure is mandatory — concealment = immunity withdrawal.
Compounding Amounts — Rule 162(3A) [Inserted vide Notification 38/2023-CT]
Clause (i) Offences
(§132(a),(c),(d),(e),(f),(h),(i))		 50%–75% of tax evaded / ITC wrongly availed / refund wrongly taken — Maximum cap at 75% (if punishable u/s 132(1)(i)) or 60% (if u/s 132(1)(ii)). Minimum floor at 50% or 40%. Higher of the two clauses applies if multiple offences.
Clause (ii) Offences
(§132(f),(h),(i) and attempts)		 25% of tax evaded / ITC wrongly availed / refund wrongly taken — Lower rate for less serious offences and attempted offences. Fixed at 25% — no sliding scale.
4. Pre-payment
R.162(5)		 Application cannot be allowed unless tax + interest + penalty fully paid FIRST — Pay first, compound second. No compounding without full pre-payment of the underlying liability.
5. Compounding Payment
R.162(6)		 Applicant pays compounding amount within 30 days of CPD-02 order → furnishes proof to Commissioner CPD-02 Miss the 30-day window → CPD-02 order is VOID. Prosecution continues.
6. Immunity Withdrawal
R.162(8)		 Immunity may be withdrawn at any time if Commissioner satisfied that applicant concealed material particulars or gave false evidence → person tried as if no immunity granted — Complete disclosure is non-negotiable. Concealment during compounding = criminal prosecution reinstated in full force.
📋 Forms: CPD-01, CPD-02 | Section: §138 CGST Act | Notification 38/2023-CT
Rule 163 — Consent-based Sharing of GST Information (§158A)
🔗 Rule 163 — Consent-based Data Sharing Framework
Section 158A CGST Act | Inserted vide Notification 38/2023-CT dated 04.08.2023
📝 GST registered persons can share their own GST data (registration details, GSTR-3B, GSTR-1/1A) with external systems — banks, NBFCs, credit rating agencies — but ONLY with consent. This enables GST-based credit scoring and financial services integration.
DATA THAT CAN BE SHARED
(a) FORM GST REG-01 (registration data as amended)
(b) GSTR-3B data for certain tax periods
(c) GSTR-1 / GSTR-1A data — invoices, debit notes, credit notes issued
R.163(1)
Requesting system obtains registered person's consent for sharing → communicates consent + tax period details to common portal
R.163(2) — CHAIN CONSENT
Registered person may give consent ONLY AFTER obtaining consent of ALL recipients to whom invoices/credit notes/debit notes were issued during those periods
📝 You can't share your B2B invoice data without first getting permission from all your customers whose data appears in that data set. The customer's consent is deemed given if you give yours.
R.163(3)
Common portal communicates information to requesting system only after receiving: (a) consent AND (b) tax period / recipient details
📋 Section: §158A CGST Act | Inserted Notification 38/2023-CT | Amendment: Notif 12/2024-CT
Rule 164 — Procedure for Closure of Proceedings under Section 128A (Waiver of Interest/Penalty) ⭐ AMDT 2025
⭐ AMENDMENT NOTE: Rule 164 was inserted by Notification No. 20/2024-CT dated 08.10.2024 (w.e.f. 01.11.2024). Sub-rules (4), (7), (8) and the final Explanation were further amended/inserted by Notification No. 11/2025-CT dated 27.03.2025 (w.e.f. 27.03.2025). These 2025 amendments are highlighted separately below.
🏆 Rule 164 — Section 128A Waiver Procedure
Interest & Penalty Waiver | FY 2017-18 to 2019-20 | SPL Form Series
📝 Section 128A is a one-time amnesty — for demands pertaining to FY 2017-18 to 2019-20, if you pay the TAX (not interest, not penalty) and apply in time, the government waives the interest and penalty. Rule 164 is the full procedure for availing this waiver.
Sub-rule Provision Form Plain English Key Condition / Litigation Amendment Status
R.164(1) For waiver of notice/statement [§128A(1)(a)] → file application with payment details in DRC-03 SPL-01 You have a pending SCN/statement — pay the tax + apply in SPL-01 → interest and penalty waived. Payment details of DRC-03 must match demand in SCN/statement. Mismatch = rejection. Inserted by Notif 20/2024-CT
R.164(2) For waiver of order [§128A(1)(b)/(c)] → file SPL-02 with payment from Electronic Liability Register against the debit entry
PROVISO: If paid via DRC-03, first file DRC-03A to credit against the debit entry, THEN file SPL-02		 SPL-02
+
DRC-03A		 You have an order against you — pay and apply. If payment was via DRC-03 (unlinked), link it first via DRC-03A before SPL-02. Sequence: DRC-03A (if needed) → SPL-02. Wrong sequence = SPL-02 rejected. Check if DRC-05 already issued — if yes, DRC-03A cannot be filed. Inserted by Notif 20/2024-CT
R.164(3) If demand is PARTLY for erroneous refund + partly for other reasons → application only after paying FULL amount of tax demanded on or before notified date SPL-01/02 Mixed demands (part erroneous refund, part other) → cannot cherry-pick. Pay the entire tax demand first. Erroneous refund component cannot be isolated. Full payment is precondition. Inserted by Notif 20/2024-CT
R.164(4)
AMDT 2025		 If demand is PARTLY for §128A period AND partly for other period → application only after paying FULL tax demanded — [now includes: "related to period mentioned in said sub-section and"] SPL-01/02 📝 Mixed-period demand — pay all of it. The 2025 amendment clarified that the payment requirement includes tax for the specifically covered period (FY17-18 to FY19-20) separately identified from the rest. ⭐ 2025 Amendment (Notif 11/2025-CT dated 27.03.2025): Inserted the phrase "related to period mentioned in said sub-section and" to clarify the payment obligation for mixed-period demands. Amended by Notif 11/2025-CT w.e.f. 27.03.2025
R.164(5) Amount payable = amount payable under notice/statement/order under §73 — MINUS — amount not payable per §16(5) or §16(6) — If you have a valid ITC credit u/s 16(5)/(6), reduce the demand first, then calculate the amount to be paid for §128A benefit. Section 16(5) extended ITC credit window — available demands may qualify for deduction before computing §128A payment. Always check this first. Inserted by Notif 20/2024-CT
R.164(6) Application must be filed within 3 months from notified date u/s 128A(1)
PROVISO: For SPL-02 in first proviso cases [Appellate Order] → 6 months from communication of order u/s 73		 SPL-01/02 Strict limitation. 3 months from notified date OR 6 months for appellate order cases. No condonation of delay appears to be available. Mark the deadline in your calendar immediately on receipt of the SPL notice. Missed deadline = no waiver, ever. Inserted by Notif 20/2024-CT
R.164(7)
AMDT 2025		 NEW EXPLANATION: No refund shall be available for any tax, interest, and penalty discharged for the ENTIRE period, prior to commencement of GST (Second Amendment) Rules 2025, in cases where notice/statement/order includes demand PARTLY for §128A period AND partly for other period — 📝 If you've already paid everything (full tax+interest+penalty) for a mixed-period demand BEFORE these 2025 rules, you cannot claim refund of the excess (interest/penalty for §128A period). The waiver is prospective only. ⭐ 2025 Amendment (Notif 11/2025-CT): New Explanation inserted to prevent refund claims for pre-amendment full payments in mixed-period demands. Critical taxpayer alert: do not wait expecting refund of old payments. Inserted by Notif 11/2025-CT w.e.f. 27.03.2025
R.164(8)
AMDT 2025		 NEW PROVISO to R.164(7): In mixed-period demand cases, applicant instead of withdrawing the appeal shall INTIMATE the appellate authority / GSTAT that he does not wish to pursue the appeal for the §128A period → authority shall pass appropriate order for the other period
Explanation: Appeal deemed withdrawn for §128A period on such intimation		 —
FORM GST SPL-08 for undertaking		 📝 For mixed-period demands where you have an ongoing appeal — you don't need to WITHDRAW the entire appeal. Just intimate the appellate authority that you are giving up the §128A period portion. The appeal continues for the non-§128A period. ⭐ 2025 Amendment (Notif 11/2025-CT): Major relief provision! Taxpayers with mixed-period appeals need not sacrifice their appeal for the non-amnesty period. Partial waiver of appeal for amnesty period only. SPL-08 undertaking required within 3 months of appellate authority order (APL-04) if rejection is appealed and then overturned. Inserted by Notif 11/2025-CT w.e.f. 27.03.2025
R.164(8)–(9) If officer finds application liable to rejection → issues notice in SPL-03 within 3 months → applicant replies in SPL-04 within 1 month SPL-03
SPL-04		 The officer must give you a chance before rejecting. SPL-03 is the notice → SPL-04 is your reply. If SPL-03 not issued → order must be passed within 3 months. If SPL-03 issued and you don't reply → officer passes order within 4 months of SPL-03. Silence = adverse order. Always file SPL-04 reply. Inserted by Notif 20/2024-CT
R.164(10)–(11) If eligible → officer issues acceptance order SPL-05:
• For SPL-01 cases: DRC-07 not required for that notice/statement
• For SPL-02 cases: Liability Register modified accordingly		 SPL-05 SPL-05 = Green signal. Proceedings concluded. DRC-07 demand not issued for SPL-01. For SPL-02, liability register updated. Best outcome — get SPL-05 and preserve it. SPL-05 is your amnesty certificate — equivalent to DRC-05 for settlement. Cannot be reopened without fresh proceedings. Inserted by Notif 20/2024-CT
R.164(12)–(13) If not satisfied → rejection order SPL-07 → timelines: SPL-05/07 within 3 months from application (if no SPL-03) OR 3 months from SPL-04 reply / 4 months from SPL-03 (if SPL-03 issued) SPL-07 SPL-07 = Rejection. Can be appealed u/s 107. See R.164(15) for consequences. Track timelines for SPL-05/07. If neither issued within time → deemed approved per R.164(14). Inserted by Notif 20/2024-CT
R.164(14) If proper officer fails to issue order within time → application deemed APPROVED and proceedings deemed CONCLUDED — Deemed approval on officer's inaction! If officer misses the deadline → your waiver application is automatically approved. No further action needed from your side. Track the officer's timeline. If deadline passes without SPL-05 or SPL-07 → issue letter asserting deemed approval. This is a powerful provision. Inserted by Notif 20/2024-CT
R.164(15) If SPL-07 not appealed in time → original appeal against §128A order restored.
If appealed and appellate authority upholds SPL-07 → original appeal restored (on filing SPL-08 undertaking).
If appellate authority overturns SPL-07 → issues SPL-06 accepting application.		 SPL-06
SPL-08		 Complex restoration mechanism. SPL-06 from appellate authority = same as SPL-05. SPL-08 = undertaking not to further appeal the rejection. Don't let SPL-07 go unappealed if you believe you are eligible. The restoration of original appeal is not automatic — you must file SPL-08 undertaking within 3 months of APL-04. Inserted by Notif 20/2024-CT
R.164(16)–(17) (16) If additional tax payment required under 2nd proviso §128A not made in time → waiver under SPL-05/06 becomes VOID
(17) If interest/penalty for erroneous refund period or non-§128A period (mentioned in SPL-05/06) not paid within 3 months → waiver becomes VOID		 — Waiver can be undone! Pay all residual amounts (for non-amnesty periods, erroneous refund etc.) within 3 months of SPL-05/06. Non-payment = SPL-05/06 void = back to square one. After getting SPL-05 — check if any residual liability mentioned. Pay within 3 months. Preserve payment proof. These are the most dangerous post-approval conditions. Inserted by Notif 20/2024-CT
⭐ Summary of 2025 Amendments to Rule 164 (Notification No. 11/2025-CT dated 27.03.2025, w.e.f. 27.03.2025)
Provision AmendedNature of ChangePractical Impact
R.164(4) — Proviso to sub-rule Inserted phrase "related to period mentioned in said sub-section and" — clarifying that in mixed-period demands, the full tax demanded (for §128A period separately identified) must be paid Taxpayers with mixed-period demands must ensure tax for §128A period (FY17-18 to FY19-20) is clearly identified and paid in full. Use revised DRC-03 to attribute correctly.
R.164(7) — New Explanation No refund available for tax/interest/penalty already discharged for entire period before commencement of 2025 Amendment Rules, in mixed-period demand cases Taxpayers who paid full interest+penalty for entire mixed-period demand BEFORE March 27, 2025 cannot now claim refund of interest/penalty for the §128A period. Only prospective relief. Explore whether DRC-03A + SPL-02 remains available for the tax portion.
R.164(8) — New Proviso + Explanation In mixed-period appeal cases — intimation to appellate authority (not withdrawal of appeal) → appeal deemed withdrawn only for §128A period → appellate authority passes order for remaining period MAJOR RELIEF: Taxpayers with mixed-period appeals pending at First Appellate Authority / GSTAT need not withdraw their appeal entirely. Intimate the authority about §128A period waiver intent. Continue appeal for remaining period. Appeal deemed withdrawn for amnesty period on such intimation. File SPL-08 undertaking if required.
📋 Forms: SPL-01 to SPL-08, DRC-03A | Section: §128A CGST Act | Notif 20/2024-CT + Notif 11/2025-CT
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal — CGST Rules Ch. XVII, XVIII & XIX | Rules 139–164
These ratios and statutory arguments are for use in personal hearings. State them confidently and cite the provision.
Rule 139(1) — Authorization Precondition for Search/Inspection
Rule 139(1) CGST Rules 2017 | Section 67 CGST Act | JC Authorization Requirement
Key Ratio / Principle
Authorization in FORM GST INS-01 by an officer not below the rank of a Joint Commissioner is a mandatory precondition for any inspection, search or seizure under Section 67. An officer visiting without a prior authorization or with an authorization signed by an officer below the rank of Joint Commissioner acts without authority of law. "Reasons to believe" forming the basis of authorization are not a mere formality — they must be based on rational, relevant material capable of objective scrutiny. (Principle derived from ITO v. Lakhmani Mewal Das — AIR 1976 SC 1753)
When to Use — When challenging the validity of a search/inspection action. Ask the officer to produce INS-01. Check rank of signing authority. Challenge before writ court if INS-01 was absent or signed by officer below JC rank.
Rule 139(5) — Mandatory Inventory as Safeguard against Abuse
Rule 139(5) CGST Rules 2017 | Section 67 CGST Act
Key Principle
The seizure officer is mandatorily required to prepare an inventory of seized goods, documents, books or things containing inter alia description, quantity or units, mark or model — and get it signed by the person from whom such goods are seized. Non-preparation of inventory, or refusal to provide a signed copy to the person from whom goods are seized, is not a mere procedural irregularity — it goes to the root of the validity of seizure and vitiates the proceedings.
When to Use — When goods seized but inventory not given to you, or unsigned/incomplete inventory is the basis for demand. Challenge: valid seizure requires valid inventory. Without it, the seizure proceedings are fundamentally defective.
Rule 142(1A) + R.142(2A) — Pre-Notice DRC-01A Opportunity is a Natural Justice Requirement
Rule 142(1A) and 142(2A) CGST Rules 2017 | Sections 73, 74, 74A CGST Act
Key Ratio / Principle
Rule 142(1A) mandates that before service of notice under Section 73/74/74A, the proper officer may communicate the details of tax, interest and penalty ascertained by him in Part A of DRC-01A. Sub-rule (2A) provides the opportunity for the registered person to file submissions in Part B. The omission to provide this pre-notice opportunity, where it prejudicially affects the assessee, is a violation of principles of natural justice — particularly the audi alteram partem principle — and vitiates the subsequent SCN proceedings.
When to Use — Where SCN was issued directly without DRC-01A opportunity, resulting in denial of chance to voluntarily pay under §73(5)/§74(5) at lower penalty rate. Argue: the procedural bypass resulted in prejudice — the assessee was denied the reduced-penalty exit route.
Rule 142(3) + DRC-05 — Discharge Certificate Bars Re-Opening
Rule 142(3) CGST Rules 2017 | Section 73(8)/74(8)/74A CGST Act
Key Principle
Where a person chargeable with tax makes payment of tax, interest and penalty within the period specified in sub-section (8) of section 73 or section 74 or the applicable clause of section 74A, and the proper officer issues FORM GST DRC-05 concluding the proceedings, such DRC-05 constitutes a formal closure of the proceedings. Proceedings so concluded cannot be reopened for the same period and the same subject matter — such re-opening would be without jurisdiction and in violation of the fundamental principle against double jeopardy in civil tax proceedings.
When to Use — When the department issues a fresh notice or reopens proceedings for a period/subject matter for which DRC-05 was already issued. Produce DRC-05. Argue: proceedings legally concluded. No surviving jurisdiction to proceed.
Rule 142(2B) / DRC-03A — Right to Link Pre-Existing Payment to Specific Demand
Rule 142(2B) CGST Rules 2017 | Notification 12/2024-CT dated 10.07.2024
Key Principle
Where an amount has been paid by a person through FORM GST DRC-03 but not credited in the Electronic Liability Register against the debit entry created for the specific demand, the person has a statutory right to file an application in FORM GST DRC-03A to credit such payment against the said demand. The department cannot treat such payment as unattributed or credit it to a different demand while recovering the specific demand for which the payment was intended — such action defeats the statutory intent of sub-rule (2B).
When to Use — When DRC-03 payment made (e.g., voluntary payment at time of audit) is not being credited against the specific demand in DRC-07. File DRC-03A immediately. If rejected, argue statutory right to attribution cannot be denied.
Rule 144A(8) — Statutory Stay on Auction of Transit Goods on Filing Appeal
Rule 144A(8) CGST Rules 2017 | Section 129(6) read with Section 107 CGST Act
Key Principle
Where an appeal has been filed by the person under Section 107(6) (25% deposit condition for admitted appeals), the proceedings for recovery of penalty by sale of goods or conveyance detained or seized in transit under Rule 144A shall be deemed to be stayed. This is an automatic statutory stay — no separate application for stay of auction is required. The auction is stayed by operation of law upon filing of the appeal. Exception: perishable/hazardous goods are not protected.
When to Use — When department proceeds to auction detained goods despite appeal being filed under §107. Cite Rule 144A(8). The stay is automatic — no court application needed. Department proceeding with auction despite this rule = contempt of statutory provision.
Rule 159(2) — Provisional Attachment Auto-Expires in 1 Year
Rule 159(2) CGST Rules 2017 | Section 83 CGST Act | Notification 52/2023-CT
Key Principle
The encumbrance placed by the Commissioner on movable or immovable property pursuant to an order of provisional attachment in FORM GST DRC-22, shall be removed either on written instructions from the Commissioner OR on expiry of a period of one year from the date of issuance of the order under sub-rule (1), whichever is earlier. Provisional attachment does not survive beyond one year as a matter of law — an attachment continuing beyond one year without a fresh order is an unauthorized encumbrance challengeable in a writ petition.
When to Use — When provisional attachment persists for more than 1 year without completion of adjudication. File writ petition citing R.159(2). The attachment has auto-expired. The bank/authority is under no obligation to continue the freeze. Demand removal in writing citing the provision.
Rule 159(5) / DRC-22A — Right to Object to Provisional Attachment
Rule 159(5) CGST Rules 2017 | Radha Krishan Industries v. State of H.P. — SC (2021) 6 SCC 771
Key Ratio
SC in Radha Krishan Industries held that provisional attachment under §83 must be exercised sparingly, in cases where there is a real apprehension of the taxable person defeating recovery by disposing of assets, and must be proportionate to the tax demand. Attaching all assets of a business rendering it unable to function is an abuse of the provision. Rule 159(5) gives the statutory right to file DRC-22A objection to the attachment. Commissioner must give opportunity of hearing and may release the property if not liable to attachment.
When to Use — When bank accounts or business assets are provisionally attached and business cannot function. File DRC-22A immediately. Cite Radha Krishan Industries — argue disproportionality. Also argue: attachment without assessment of tax liability is premature and coercive.
Rule 158 — Instalment Facility as a Right (when conditions met)
Rule 158 CGST Rules 2017 | Section 80 CGST Act
Key Principle
Section 80 provides that on an application, the Commissioner may, for reasons to be recorded in writing, extend the time for payment or allow payment in instalments. Rule 158 implements this by allowing up to 24 monthly instalments. Where all eligibility conditions are met — amount ≥ ₹25,000, no existing default under recovery, no instalment in preceding FY — the denial of instalment facility without recorded reasons is an arbitrary exercise of discretion challenged under Article 14.
When to Use — When client has genuine financial hardship and demand is ≥ ₹25,000 with no prior defaults. File DRC-20. If Commissioner denies without reasons — challenge. The law requires reasons to be recorded in writing. Unreasoned denial = reviewable.
Rule 164(8) [2025 Amendment] — Partial Appeal Waiver for Mixed-Period §128A Cases ⭐ AMDT 2025
Rule 164(8) CGST Rules 2017 | Notification 11/2025-CT dated 27.03.2025 | Section 128A CGST Act
Key Statutory Principle
Where a notice, statement or order under §128A includes a demand of tax partially for the period covered (FY2017-18 to 2019-20) and partially for other periods, the applicant seeking §128A amnesty benefit shall NOT be required to withdraw the appeal entirely. Instead, the applicant shall intimate the appellate authority or GSTAT that he does not wish to pursue the appeal for the §128A covered period — whereupon the appeal shall be deemed to be withdrawn for that period, and the appellate authority shall pass appropriate orders for the remaining non-§128A period. The applicant's right of appeal for the non-§128A period is fully preserved.
When to Use — In any mixed-period appeal case where client wants §128A amnesty for FY17-19 portion but wants to continue appeal for other years (e.g., FY2020-21 portion). Intimate the appellate authority in writing citing Rule 164(8). The authority cannot insist on full withdrawal. Appeal for non-amnesty years continues independently.
Rule 164(14) — Deemed Approval on Officer's Inaction
Rule 164(14) CGST Rules 2017 | Section 128A CGST Act | Notification 20/2024-CT
Key Principle
Where the proper officer fails to issue an order under sub-rule (10) or sub-rule (12) within the time limit specified in sub-rule (13), the application in FORM GST SPL-01 or FORM GST SPL-02, as the case may be, shall be deemed to be approved and the proceedings shall be deemed to be concluded. This is a self-executing statutory provision — no court order is required to give effect to deemed approval. The department cannot contend that proceedings are ongoing after the expiry of the specified time period.
When to Use — When SPL-01/SPL-02 filed and neither SPL-05 nor SPL-07 issued by the proper officer within the prescribed time (3 months from application if no SPL-03; or 3/4 months from SPL-04/SPL-03 respectively). Write to the jurisdictional officer asserting deemed approval under Rule 164(14). If department issues demand despite deemed approval, challenge by writ citing this provision.
Rule 162(3)/(6) — Compounding Order Void on Non-Payment within 30 Days
Rule 162(3) and (6) CGST Rules 2017 | Section 138 CGST Act
Key Principle
The compounding of an offence under Section 138 and Rule 162 is a complete procedure — mere issuance of CPD-02 order does not extinguish prosecution. The applicant must pay the compounding amount within 30 days of CPD-02. If the compounding amount is not paid within this period, the CPD-02 order shall be vitiated and rendered void — and prosecution may be revived. The immunity is conditional, not absolute, until actual payment is made and proof furnished to the Commissioner.
When to Use — If department claims CPD-02 was issued but client never paid — argue compounding order itself is void and prosecution should be treated as not compounded, entitling fresh consideration. Conversely, if client has paid, ensure DRC-03/payment proof was furnished to Commissioner in time.
For professional litigation use | Pawan Soni & Associates, Ichalkaranji | Rules 139–164 CGST Rules 2017 | ⭐ 2025 Amendments incorporated as of Notification No. 11/2025-CT dated 27.03.2025
IGST Act, 2017 — Complete

⚡ Integrated Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017 — Complete Bare Law

Source: CGST Act, 2017 / GST Litigation Reference | CA Pawankumar Soni, Ichalkaranji

Permitted / Valid
Prohibited / Restricted
Condition / If / Unless
Optional / Discretionary
Neutral Reference
📝 Plain English
💡 Example
🔍 GP Singh Node
Rule Cross-Reference
🆕 Latest Amendment (2024–25)
📖 Legal Dictionary
📋 Index — IGST Act, 2017 (Sections 1–15)
CHAPTER I — PRELIMINARY

§2 Definitions (25 Definitions)
CHAPTER II — ADMINISTRATION

CHAPTER III — LEVY & COLLECTION
