⚔️ Hearing Arsenal
🗺️ TIER 1 — Birds-Eye View: IGST Act, 2017 Chapter-wise Overview
Chapter Sections Subject Matter Core Purpose / Litigation Significance
I — Preliminary §1, §2 Short title, extent, commencement & 25 key definitions Foundation — definitions control scope of every provision. "Intermediary", "fixed establishment", "OIDAR" — gateway words for disputes.
II — Administration §3, §4 Appointment of officers; Cross-authorization of State tax officers Jurisdictional validity of proceedings. State officer acting as IGST proper officer must satisfy §4 conditions.
III — Levy & Collection §5, §6, §6A IGST levy on inter-State supply; exemption powers; general practice waiver Core charging section. Rate cap 40%. Reverse charge. E-commerce. Excluded goods (petroleum). Amendment 2024 — ENA/rectified spirit.
IV — Nature of Supply §7, §8, §9 Inter-State vs Intra-State determination; territorial waters deeming Most litigated — dual-control disputes, SEZ supplies, supply to tourist, cross-UT supply. Nature determines which tax applies.
V — Place of Supply §10–§14A Place of supply for goods (domestic, imports, exports) and services (domestic & cross-border) including OIDAR, online money gaming Most complex chapter. 14 different rules for services alone. Each sub-section is a separate battleground in disputes.
VI — Tourist Refund §15 Refund of IGST to foreign tourists on goods taken out of India Narrow provision — applies only to tourists (non-residents, stay < 6 months).
Section 1 — Short Title, Extent & Commencement
⚡ IGST Act, 2017 — Section 1
Short Title | Extent | Commencement
§1(1)
"Integrated Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017"
📝 Short name — used in all citations. Abbreviated as IGST Act or IGSTA.
§1(2)
Extends to whole of India
[J&K extension added vide Amendment Act 2017, w.e.f. 08.07.2017]
📝 Originally excluded J&K — that exclusion was omitted making IGST applicable pan-India uniformly.
§1(3)
Commencement: notified date by Central Govt
• §§1, 2, 3, 14, 20, 22 → 22.06.2017
• §§4–13, 16–19, 21, 23–25 → 01.07.2017
📝 Proviso allows different dates for different sections — hence some provisions came into force 9 days before the main Act. Different commencement = different limitation periods.
Enforcement Notifications: No. 1/2017-IT (22.06.2017) | No. 3/2017-IT (28.06.2017)
Section 2 — Definitions (25 Key Definitions)
Clause Term Defined Core Meaning Plain English Litigation Significance GP Singh / Amendment Note
§2(1) Central Goods and Services Tax Act CGST Act, 2017 (12 of 2017) The parent Act — IGST Act cross-references CGST for supply, ITC, valuation etc. All undefined terms in IGST → travel to CGST Act (§2(24)).
§2(2) Central tax Tax levied under CGST Act CGST — the Centre's share of dual GST. Distinction: IGST ≠ CGST + SGST. IGST is a separate levy.
§2(3) Continuous journey Single or multiple tickets issued at same time by one/more suppliers with no stopover between legs. Stopover = disembark to transfer or break journey for period then resume. Multiple-leg flight with no break = continuous journey. Relevant for place of supply under §12(9). Critical for airline/travel agent disputes on POS. Single ticket via Mumbai-Delhi-London = continuous journey even if different airlines. 🔍 "Stopover" explanation is integral to determine IGST vs no-tax on transit passengers.
§2(4) Customs frontiers of India Limits of customs area as defined in §2(11) Customs Act, 1962 The invisible line at airport/port where Indian customs jurisdiction begins. Imported goods till customs frontier = inter-State supply (§7(2)). Pre-customs-clearance title transfer disputes.
§2(5) Export of goods Taking goods OUT of India to a place OUTSIDE India Exports = zero-rated. Simple directional test — out of India. High seas sales, bonded warehouse supplies — whether "out of India" before customs frontier crossed.
§2(6) Export of services Supply satisfying ALL 5 conditions simultaneously:
(i) Supplier in India
(ii) Recipient outside India
(iii) POS outside India
(iv) Payment in forex (or INR where RBI permits)
(v) Not distinct persons (Expl. 1 to §8)		 All 5 must be met. Failure of any ONE = NOT export = IGST payable. Most litigated definition in IGST Act. BPO/IT services, intragroup services — condition (v) is most litigated: related company disputes on "distinct persons." INR payment allowed in some cases after 2018 amendment. 🔍 Conjunctive "and" connects all 5 conditions — GP Singh: "and" must be read disjunctively only if absurdity results. Here — no absurdity — all 5 are mandatory.
§2(7) Fixed establishment Place (other than registered place of business) with:
• Sufficient degree of permanence
• Suitable structure in terms of human & technical resources
To supply OR receive services for its own needs		 A branch office or project site that is SUFFICIENTLY PERMANENT to supply or receive services. Not every temporary presence qualifies. Critical for §2(14)/(15) — location of recipient/supplier of services. BPO/IT — whether overseas delivery centre = fixed establishment in India. If yes → not export. 📖 "Sufficient degree of permanence" — not defined; must be interpreted from EU GST jurisprudence: CJEU Berkholz, Faaborg-Gelting cases. Indian HC yet to crystallize the test.
§2(10) Import of goods Bringing goods INTO India from OUTSIDE India Mirror of export definition — directional test. Applies till customs frontier. High seas sale buyer becomes importer — IGST at customs stage on CIF + customs duty value.
§2(11) Import of services Supply where:
(i) Supplier outside India
(ii) Recipient in India
(iii) POS in India		 All 3 must be met. Import = taxable under RCM (§5(3)). Unlike export of services — import has only 3 conditions. Software subscriptions, cloud services, royalties paid to foreign companies — trigger RCM on recipient in India. No forex condition for import.
§2(12) Integrated tax IGST levied under this Act The actual tax name. Distinct from CGST, SGST, UTGST. IGST credit can be used to pay CGST/SGST. Order of utilization critical in refund claims.
§2(13) Intermediary Broker, agent or other person who:
• Arranges/facilitates supply of goods/services/securities
BETWEEN two or more persons
• Does NOT supply on own account
Also includes: arranging supply of securities		 Key test: Does the person supply on OWN ACCOUNT? If yes → not intermediary. If only facilitates between others → intermediary. Intermediary's POS is supplier's location (§13(8)(b)) — NOT recipient's location. Most litigated — BPO/call centers, IT-enabled services, commission agents. "Intermediary" POS is in India (where intermediary is) → IGST payable. "Own account supplier" POS is where recipient is → may be export. Crux of BPO disputes. 📖 SC in Sutherland Global Services upheld broad interpretation. AAR rulings split. 🔍 "By whatever name called" — GP Singh: substance over label. Even if called "service provider" — if functioning as middleman → intermediary.
§2(14) Location of recipient of services (a) Place of business where registration obtained
(b) Fixed establishment (if supply received there)
(c) Most concerned establishment (if multiple)
(d) Usual place of residence (if none of above)		 Hierarchical test — 4 steps in order. First matching step gives location. Relevant for POS determination under §12 and §13. Cross-border supply to multi-state recipient — which establishment is "most concerned"? Complex in insurance, banking, IT services.
§2(15) Location of supplier of services (a) Registered place of business
(b) Fixed establishment
(c) Most concerned establishment
(d) Usual place of residence — same hierarchy as §2(14)		 Mirror of §2(14) from supplier's side. Determines POS in §13(8) for intermediary, banking, transport hire. Foreign company with Indian branch — is location supplier the Indian branch (IGST) or foreign HO (potentially outside India)?
§2(16) Non-taxable online recipient (NTOR) ANY unregistered person receiving OIDAR services in taxable territory. "Unregistered person" excludes person registered solely under §24(vi) of CGST Act.
[Amended w.e.f. 01.10.2023 — Fin Act 2023]		 Used in §14 — OIDAR supplier from outside India must pay IGST if recipient is NTOR. Earlier definition limited to non-business recipients — now covers all unregistered persons. Netflix, Spotify, cloud SaaS to unregistered Indian users — supplier from non-taxable territory must register & pay IGST under §14. 🆕 Finance Act 2023 expanded definition significantly — earlier only non-business/non-govt unregistered persons; now ALL unregistered persons.
§2(17) OIDAR Services Services mediated by IT over internet/electronic network whose nature renders supply essentially impossible without IT. Includes:
(i) Internet advertising
(ii) Cloud services
(iii) e-Books, movies, music, software via telecom/internet
(iv) Data/information electronically
(v) Digital content (movies, TV, music)
(vi) Digital data storage
(vii) Online gaming (excl. online money gaming)
[Item (vii) amended w.e.f. 01.10.2023]		 Broad category — almost all digital services. The "essentially impossible without IT" test was deleted by Finance Act 2023 (making it even broader). Online gaming (non-monetary) = OIDAR. Online money gaming split into §14A. Critical for foreign gaming/streaming companies' IGST liability. 🆕 Finance Act 2023: (a) deleted "essentially automated/no human intervention" qualifier — broader scope; (b) online gaming split — money gaming now in §14A separately.
§2(18) Output tax IGST chargeable on taxable supply made by taxable person or agent. Excludes tax payable on reverse charge basis. What you owe on YOUR sales. RCM liability is separately tracked — not "output tax" of the seller. ITC utilization sequence — output tax liability comes first. RCM cannot be set off against output tax in same way.
§2(19) Special Economic Zone Same meaning as SEZ Act, 2005 (28 of 2005) Supplies TO SEZ developer/unit = inter-State supply → zero-rated → no IGST payable (or refund). High dispute: Whether supply is "to" SEZ developer or merely to a person who happens to be in SEZ.
§2(21) Supply Same meaning as §7 of CGST Act IGST borrows the supply definition entirely from CGST Act. No separate IGST definition of supply. Classification disputes on supply → apply §7 CGST and then determine inter-State/intra-State nature under §7/§8 IGST.
§2(22) Taxable territory Territory to which IGST Act applies (i.e., India) India = taxable territory for IGST purposes. Used in §13(12) OIDAR deeming provision.
§2(23) Zero-rated supply Has meaning assigned in §16 IGST Act (export of goods/services + SEZ supplies) Zero-rated = supply on which IGST is 0% AND ITC is fully available. Different from exempt supply (ITC blocked). Critical difference: zero-rated supplier gets ITC refund. Exempt supplier does not. Wrong classification = blocked refund. 🔍 GP Singh: "Zero-rated" is a defined legal term — cannot be read as equivalent to "exempt." Statute must be given its exact meaning.
§2(24) Words not defined in IGST Any undefined word → takes meaning from CGST Act / UTGST Act / Compensation Act IGST is a compact Act — relies heavily on CGST Act for most definitions. Always check CGST first when IGST term is undefined.
🆕 2023–24 Amendments to §2 Definitions (all notified w.e.f. 01.10.2023):
§2(16) NTOR — expanded from "non-business govt/individual" to ALL unregistered persons. | §2(17)(vii) Online gaming amended — online money gaming removed from OIDAR, placed in new §14A. | §2(17) — "essentially automated" qualifier deleted from OIDAR definition — scope broadened.
IGST Act §2 → cross-refer CGST Act §2 for all undefined expressions | No separate IGST Rules for definitions
Section 3 & 4 — Administration: Appointment & Cross-Authorisation of Officers
⚙️ Chapter II — Administration
§3 Appointment | §4 Cross-Authorisation
§3 — Appointment of Officers
Board may appoint central tax officers as it thinks fit for exercising powers under IGST Act
📝 "Board" = CBIC. Central officers get IGST powers by appointment. Their jurisdiction is inter-State supplies.
🔍 "As it thinks fit" — GP Singh: "may" in delegation provision = discretionary. Board retains control over scope. Appointment must be in writing — oral authorization insufficient.
§4 — Cross-Authorisation
State GST / UTGST officers are authorised to act as proper officers under IGST Act
Subject to: exceptions & conditions notified by Central Govt on GST Council recommendation
📝 State officers can exercise IGST powers — but only within limits set by Central Govt notification. Without notification specifying the scope, a State officer has no automatic IGST jurisdiction.
💡 A State GST inspector conducts search under IGST Act. If the Central Govt notification does not include "search" within State officer's IGST powers → search is without jurisdiction → entire proceedings challengeable.
🔍 "Without prejudice to the provisions of this Act" — GP Singh: these words preserve existing IGST officer powers. §4 is ADDITIONAL authorization, not substitution. Both can exercise powers within their notified scope.
§4 cross-authorization: See Notification No. 39/2017-CT and equivalent state notifications specifying scope | Challenge jurisdictional validity where State officer exceeds notified scope
Section 5 — Levy & Collection of IGST
💰 Section 5 — Levy & Collection of Integrated Tax
Core Charging Section of IGST Act
§5(1) — General Rule: IGST on All Inter-State Supplies
IGST levied on ALL inter-State supplies of goods or services or both at such rates not exceeding 40% as notified by Central Govt on GST Council recommendation | Paid by taxable person | Valuation as per §15 CGST Act
EXCLUSIONS from §5(1) levy:
(a) Alcoholic liquor for human consumption — excluded from IGST
(b) 🆕 2024 AMEND Un-denatured extra neutral alcohol (ENA) / rectified spirit used for manufacture of alcoholic liquor — excluded w.e.f. 01.11.2024 vide Finance (No.2) Act, 2024
🆕 LATEST AMENDMENT — Finance (No.2) Act, 2024 (w.e.f. 01.11.2024): Un-denatured extra neutral alcohol and rectified spirit used for manufacture of alcoholic liquor for human consumption is NOW EXCLUDED from IGST levy. This was a major litigation issue — ENA used for potable liquor manufacture was being taxed under GST despite the end product being outside GST. Amendment resolves this dispute prospectively.
💡 A distillery in Maharashtra purchases ENA from Rajasthan for manufacturing whisky. Earlier — IGST on inter-State ENA purchase was disputed. From 01.11.2024 — no IGST on such purchase since ENA for alcoholic liquor manufacture is excluded. Pending demands for pre-amendment period remain open.
PROVISO to §5(1) — Imported Goods: IGST at Customs Stage
IGST on imported goods (other than notified goods) → levied & collected under §3 Customs Tariff Act, 1975 | At customs stage | On value as determined under Customs Tariff Act | At point when customs duties leviable under §12 Customs Act, 1962
📝 Imports don't go through GST registration/return system for IGST — it is collected at the border alongside customs duty by Customs. The customs-cleared IGST then flows as ITC into the importer's GSTR-2B.
💡 Machine imported from Germany. Customs duty (BCD) + IGST collected at port of import by Customs. Importer gets ITC of this IGST in GSTR-2B. ITC dispute: whether IGST on capital goods is available without restriction.
§5(2) — Petroleum Deferred
IGST on petroleum crude, HSD, motor spirit (petrol), natural gas, ATF → NOT yet levied | Levy to commence from such date as notified by Central Govt on GST Council recommendation
📝 Petroleum products are "in GST" but levy has NOT been commenced. They remain under the old VAT/excise regime. No notification has been issued till date. These 5 products are the major exclusions from GST.
Reverse Charge Mechanism (RCM) under IGST Act
§5(3) — Notified Supply-based RCM
Govt may notify categories of supply where tax payable by RECIPIENT on reverse charge basis. Recipient treated as if liable to pay tax. Applies to ALL provisions of IGST Act.
💡 Legal services by advocate to registered business — IGST payable by recipient under RCM. Also: GTA services.
§5(4) — Unregistered Supplier RCM
Class of registered persons who in respect of SPECIFIED categories of goods/services received from UNREGISTERED supplier → shall pay tax on RCM basis.
[Substituted in 2018]
📝 When you (registered) buy certain goods/services from an unregistered person → you (the buyer) pay IGST as if you were the seller. Prevents revenue leakage.
§5(5) — E-Commerce Operator Liability
Govt may notify categories of services where IGST on inter-State supplies through e-commerce operator shall be paid by the ECO
ECO without physical presence: person representing ECO liable
Neither physical presence NOR representative: ECO must appoint person in India for tax payment
📝 Zomato, Swiggy, Ola — pay IGST on restaurant/transport services supplied through their platform. If they're foreign with no Indian presence, they must still appoint someone to pay tax.
💡 Foreign ECO with no Indian office supplies software via its marketplace. Must appoint Indian representative. If doesn't — that representative is personally liable. No escape via "no presence" argument.
§5(3) Notifications: 9/2017-IT, 10/2017-IT | §5(5) ECO Notifications: 17/2017-IT | Rate Notifications: 1/2017-IT, 2/2017-IT | CGST Rules 142–161 for valuation
Section 6 — Power to Grant Exemption | Section 6A — General Practice Waiver
🛡️ Section 6 — Exemption Powers & §6A General Practice
§6(1) — General Notification Exemption
Satisfied in public interest → on GST Council recommendation → exempt by notification — absolute OR conditional | Category-wide
📝 The standard route — govt issues exemption notification (like Notification 12/2017-IT for services). Conditional exemptions come with strings attached — failure = tax payable.
🔍 "Public interest" — GP Singh: discretion of govt subject to judicial review only on Wednesbury unreasonableness. Assessee cannot claim exemption as of right without meeting all conditions.
§6(2) — Special Order Exemption
Exceptional circumstances → by special order in EACH CASE → exempt specific goods/services
📝 One-off exemption for unique situations. Used sparingly. Pandemic exemptions (ventilators, vaccines) are examples.
§6(3) — Retrospective Clarification
Govt may insert Explanation in notification within 1 year of §6(1) notification to clarify scope — effective from date of original notification
📝 The government can retroactively clarify what the exemption meant — within 1 year. Cannot use to restrict exemption that was validly availed.
🔍 GP Singh: Retrospective clarification valid only if it clarifies — not if it modifies. If it takes away vested rights → courts may strike down as substantive amendment dressed as clarification.
§6 Explanation — No Collection Beyond Effective Rate
If absolute exemption granted → registered supplier SHALL NOT collect tax in excess of effective rate. Collecting excess = offence.
💡 Supplier gets absolute exemption on supply but still charges GST in invoice — amount collected is not "output tax" but illegal collection under §76 CGST. Must be deposited within 30 days.
🆕 §6A — INSERTED BY FINANCE (NO.2) ACT, 2024 (w.e.f. 01.11.2024)
Power NOT to Recover IGST — General Practice Waiver
Notwithstanding anything in IGST Act — if Govt satisfied that:
(a) A practice was/is generally prevalent regarding levy of IGST (including non-levy) on any supply; AND
(b) Such supplies were/are liable to IGST (which was not levied) OR liable to HIGHER IGST than levied under that practice —
→ Govt may, on GST Council recommendation, by notification, direct that SHORT/NON-LEVIED IGST shall NOT be required to be paid in respect of such supplies.
📝 This is a prospective and retrospective shield. If an industry-wide wrong practice existed (e.g., nobody charged IGST on a certain service), the Govt can officially waive recovery for past period. This protects bonafide taxpayers caught in legitimate industry-wide practices.
💡 Assume the entire real-estate industry did not pay IGST on a particular type of development rights transfer based on general understanding. If Govt invokes §6A → all show cause notices for past periods on that practice → waived. This section mirrors §11C of Customs Act.
🔍 GP Singh: "Notwithstanding anything in this Act" — overriding clause. If §6A notification issued → it prevails even over §74/§73 demand provisions. Assessee can cite §6A notification as absolute defence.
§6 Exemption Notifications: 9/2017-IT (goods), 12/2017-IT (services) | §6A modeled on §11C Customs Act | No IGST Rules for §6A
Section 7 — Inter-State Supply (IGST Trigger)
🗺️ Section 7 — When is a Supply INTER-STATE?
IGST applies | Most litigated section
§7(1)(a) — Goods: Two Different States
Supplier in State A → POS in State B → Inter-State
§7(1)(b) — Goods: Two Different UTs
Supplier in UT A → POS in UT B → Inter-State
§7(1)(c) — Goods: State + UT
Supplier in State → POS in UT or vice versa → Inter-State
§7(2) — Imports Till Customs Frontier
Supply of goods imported into India → till they cross customs frontiers of India → deemed inter-State
📝 Goods on a ship in Indian waters → still "inter-State" until customs cleared. High seas sale is inter-State.
§7(3) — Services: Different States/UTs
Location of supplier & POS in different States, different UTs, or State + UT → Inter-State service supply
§7(4) — Import of Services
Services imported into India → deemed inter-State supply → IGST via RCM
§7(5) — Residual Inter-State
(a) Supplier in India, POS outside India
(b) Supply to/by SEZ developer/unit
(c) In taxable territory, not intra-State and not covered elsewhere in this section
→ ALL treated as inter-State
📝 §7(5)(c) is the catch-all — if supply can't be clearly classified as intra-State, it defaults to inter-State. SEZ supplies are always inter-State regardless of location.
LITIGATION POINT — §7(5)(b) SEZ Supply
Supply TO SEZ developer/unit = inter-State = IGST (zero-rated). Supply BY SEZ to DTA = also inter-State = IGST. Supply within SEZ = intra-State = CGST + SGST (within same State). Confirmation from Circular 48/2018-GST.
Subject to §10 IGST (POS for goods) and §12 IGST (POS for services) | Circular 48/22/2018-GST clarifies SEZ supplies
Section 8 — Intra-State Supply (CGST + SGST Trigger)
🏠 Section 8 — When is a Supply INTRA-STATE?
CGST + SGST/UTGST applies
§8(1) — Goods
Supplier location + POS of goods in SAME State or SAME UT → Intra-State
Proviso — NOT intra-State despite same location:
(i) Supply to/by SEZ developer/unit
(ii) Goods imported till customs frontier
(iii) Supply to tourist (§15)
📝 Three forced exceptions — even if supplier and POS are in same state, SEZ/import/tourist supplies are always inter-State (IGST).
§8(2) — Services
Supplier location + POS of services in SAME State or SAME UT → Intra-State
Proviso — NOT intra-State:
Supply to SEZ developer/unit
📝 Services to SEZ = always inter-State (zero-rated IGST), even if SEZ is in the same state as supplier.
Explanation 1 — Distinct Persons for IGST Purposes
(i) Establishment in India + establishment outside India → distinct persons
(ii) Establishment in one State/UT + establishment in another State/UT → distinct persons
(iii) Establishment + another establishment NOT REGISTERED in same State/UT → distinct persons
📝 Distinct person = supply between branches/offices of same company in different states = taxable supply even though same legal entity. Each GSTIN is treated as a separate "person" for GST purposes. This is the basis for all intra-entity cross-charge disputes.
💡 Tata Consultancy Services HO in Mumbai provides shared services to branch in Bengaluru. Both are "distinct persons" — cross-charge is a taxable supply. If no invoice raised → §74 risk for suppression.
🔍 GP Singh: Explanation 1 creates a deeming fiction. Deeming provisions must be strictly construed — only for the purpose for which deemed. "Distinct persons" for inter/intra-State classification — does not make them "distinct persons" for all GST purposes (e.g., ITC eligibility remains based on actual use).
Explanation 2
Person carrying on business through branch or agency or representational office in any territory = having an establishment in that territory
Section 9 — Supplies in Territorial Waters
🌊 Section 9 — Territorial Waters: Deeming State Location
📝 Territorial waters don't belong to any State — they are a Union subject. But for GST, we need to assign them to a State for revenue distribution. §9 does this by deeming the nearest coastal state as the location.
§9(a)
If location of SUPPLIER is in territorial waters → supplier's location deemed to be in the coastal State/UT nearest to baseline point
§9(b)
If PLACE OF SUPPLY is in territorial waters → POS deemed to be in the coastal State/UT nearest to baseline point
PRACTICAL APPLICATION
Offshore oil drilling rigs, fishing vessels, maritime services. GST revenue goes to nearest coastal state. Important for petroleum royalties and offshore contracts.
🔍 "Nearest point of appropriate baseline" — GP Singh: deeming fictions must be applied strictly. Only the specific determination (State attribution) is deemed — not the entire tax character of the supply.
Section 10 — Place of Supply of Goods (Domestic — Not Import/Export)
Sub-section Scenario Place of Supply Plain English Litigation Point
§10(1)(a) Supply involves MOVEMENT of goods (by supplier, recipient or third party) Location where goods movement TERMINATES for delivery to recipient Destination rule — where goods reach the buyer. Most common rule. Dispute: what if delivery terminates at recipient's warehouse in State A but goods are for use in State B? POS = State A.
§10(1)(b) Bill-to-ship-to: Goods delivered to THIRD PERSON on direction of another, before/during movement, via transfer of title documents Principal place of business of the THIRD PERSON (the person who directed delivery) The "middleman" rule — if A tells B to send goods directly to C, A's POS is A's address (not C's). A is deemed to have received goods first. Bill-to-ship-to transactions. E-commerce drop-shipping. High dispute on who is "third person" — buyer or purchaser of rights.
§10(1)(c) No movement of goods (goods stationary at time of supply) Location of goods at time of delivery No delivery movement — POS is where goods already are. Sale of immovable property fixtures, goods at warehouse (not moved), transfer of title documents for goods in storage.
§10(1)(ca) 🆕 Supply to UNREGISTERED PERSON — address recorded in invoice Location as per ADDRESS IN INVOICE. If no address → location of supplier.
Expl: Recording State name = recording address		 📝 For B2C supplies — invoice address controls. Mere State name in invoice = sufficient address for POS.
[Inserted by IGST Amendment Act 2023 w.e.f. 01.10.2023]		 🆕 2023 insertion. Resolves B2C supply POS disputes. E-commerce platforms delivering to unregistered buyers — state name in delivery address = POS. Overrides §10(1)(a)/(c) for such supplies.
§10(1)(d) Goods assembled or installed at site Place of installation or assembly Where you put it together → POS. EPC contracts, plant installation. Works contracts with multi-State installation — POS is installation State. Supply of prefabricated goods + installation — composite supply — POS = site.
§10(1)(e) Goods supplied ON BOARD a conveyance (vessel, aircraft, train, motor vehicle) Location where goods TAKEN ON BOARD Goods loaded onto ship/plane — POS is the loading port/airport. Duty-free shops on aircraft/ships. Ship chandelling. POS = port of loading regardless of vessel's destination.
§10(2) POS cannot be determined under §10(1) As prescribed (IGST Rules) Fallback — go to IGST Rules for determination. Residual rule — rarely applied. If applicable, rules may allow POS by agreement/apportionment.
🆕 §10(1)(ca) inserted by IGST Amendment Act, 2023 (w.e.f. 01.10.2023): For supplies to unregistered persons — POS is address in invoice (even just the State name). This overrides §10(1)(a) and (c) for B2C transactions. Resolves e-commerce POS disputes where unregistered buyer's exact address may not be recorded.
§10 applies only to domestic goods — not import (§11) | For goods on board vessel in territorial waters → §9 first, then §10
Section 11 — Place of Supply of Goods: Imports & Exports
✈️ Section 11 — POS for Import/Export of Goods
Simple directional rule
§11(a) — IMPORT
POS = Location of Importer
📝 Goods coming into India → POS is wherever the importer is registered/located in India. IGST collected at customs stage by Customs dept.
💡 Mumbai-based company imports machinery. POS = Maharashtra. IGST at port of import → collected by Customs → flows as ITC to Maharashtra GSTIN of importer.
§11(b) — EXPORT
POS = Location outside India
📝 Goods going out of India → POS is outside India = inter-State supply = zero-rated. No IGST payable. Full ITC refund available.
💡 Textile exporter in Surat ships garments to US buyer. POS = USA (outside India). Zero-rated. Claim refund of IGST paid on inputs or export under LUT without paying IGST.
Section 12 — Place of Supply of Services (Both Supplier & Recipient in India)
🏢 Section 12 — POS for Services: DOMESTIC (Both Parties in India)
14 specific rules + 1 general rule
📝 §12 applies ONLY when BOTH supplier and recipient of services are located in India. If either is outside India → §13 applies. §12 has 14 separate sub-sections each defining POS for specific categories.
Sub-section Service Category Place of Supply Key Conditions / Notes Litigation Point
§12(2)(a) General Rule — Registered Recipient Location of the REGISTERED RECIPIENT Default rule for B2B services. Covers all services not in §12(3)–§12(14). Most B2B disputes resolved here. If recipient has multiple GSTINs — which one received the service?
§12(2)(b) General Rule — Unregistered Recipient (i) Location of recipient if address on record; (ii) Location of supplier if no address B2C general rule. Supplier's location = POS when buyer address not available. E-commerce supplies to unregistered persons — importance of recording buyer address in invoice.
§12(3) Immovable Property Services
(architects, surveyors, engineers, interior decorators, estate agents, construction co-ordination, rights to use immovable property, lodging accommodation in hotels/clubs/campsites/houseboats, event venue)		 Location where the IMMOVABLE PROPERTY is located or intended to be located If property in multiple states → apportioned proportionally. If property outside India → POS = location of recipient. Most critical for real estate disputes. GST on construction services, JDA, development rights — POS is property location. Cross-State property = cross-State IGST dispute.
§12(4) Restaurant, catering, personal grooming, fitness, beauty, health (incl. cosmetic surgery) Where services are ACTUALLY PERFORMED Performance-based rule. No general/default rule override. Multi-city restaurant chains — each outlet's State is POS for that outlet's services.
§12(5) Training & performance appraisal Registered: location of recipient | Unregistered: where actually performed B2B training → recipient's GSTIN location. B2C → venue. Online training to unregistered students — POS = where students actually attend (if B2C). Pan-India webinar → complex apportionment.
§12(6) Admission to cultural/artistic/sports/scientific/educational events, amusement parks Place where event is held / park is located Ticket-based events. POS = venue State. Online ticketing companies — POS = venue location even if ticket sold online from another State.
§12(7) Organisation of events (conferences, fairs, exhibitions, celebrations, sponsorship) Registered: location of recipient | Unregistered: where event actually held (if outside India → recipient location) Different rules for organiser vs attendee. Sponsorship to events → recipient (sponsor's) location. MICE events — event management company POS vs sponsor's POS are different. Sponsorship = recipient location.
§12(8) Transportation of goods (incl. mail/courier) Registered: location of recipient | Unregistered: where goods handed over for transportation GTA, courier companies. B2B → recipient State. B2C → origin point. International courier (domestic leg) → where goods handed over. GTA — registered recipient's GSTIN location → IGST if cross-State.
§12(9) Passenger transportation service Registered: location of recipient | Unregistered: place of EMBARKATION for continuous journey Return journey = separate journey. Future-use tickets where embarkation unknown → §12(2) applies. Airlines — passenger POS = embarkation city. Corporate travel booked by company → recipient's GSTIN location.
§12(10) Services ON BOARD conveyance (vessel, aircraft, train, motor vehicle) First scheduled point of DEPARTURE of conveyance Inflight food/services. First departure city = POS regardless of journey. Catering services supplied on board flight from Delhi to Mumbai → POS = Delhi (first departure).
§12(11) Telecommunication services (fixed line, leased circuit, mobile post-paid, pre-paid via voucher) Complex: (a) Fixed line → where installed; (b) Post-paid mobile → billing address; (c) Pre-paid via agent → agent address; (d) Otherwise → recipient address or supplier address Pre-paid via online banking → location of recipient on supplier's records. Roaming charges, cross-State SIMs, online recharges — multiple POS possibilities. Telecom sector disputes with state govts on revenue sharing.
§12(12) Banking & financial services including stock broking Location of RECIPIENT on records of supplier. If not on records → location of supplier. KYC records determine POS for banking. If no KYC → bank's location. Multi-State banking — home branch KYC determines POS. Online banking without branch = supplier's location.
§12(13) Insurance services Registered: location of recipient | Unregistered: location of recipient on supplier's records Policy records determine POS. B2B → policy-holder's GSTIN. Corporate group policies — which GSTIN takes the ITC? POS = policyholder's GSTIN location.
§12(14) Advertisement services to Central/State Govt / statutory body / local authority for specific states Each State/UT identified in contract gets proportional POS based on dissemination value Pan-India govt ad campaigns — POS apportioned State-wise. Each state pays for its share. DAVP campaigns for multiple States — invoice per State or apportionment clause in contract required. Failure → single POS dispute.
§12 applies where BOTH supplier and recipient are in India | If either is outside India → apply §13 | Circular 103/2019-GST on place of supply of various services
Section 13 — Place of Supply: Cross-Border Services (Supplier OR Recipient Outside India)
🌐 Section 13 — POS for Cross-Border Services
Applies when supplier OR recipient is OUTSIDE India
📝 §13 is the key section for determining whether a service is exported (zero-rated) or imported (RCM). The general rule is: POS = location of recipient. But 8 specific exceptions exist where POS is determined differently.
Sub-section Service Category Place of Supply Plain English Litigation / Hearing Point
§13(2) General Rule — All services not in §13(3)–§13(13) Location of the RECIPIENT of services
Proviso: If recipient location unavailable → location of supplier		 Default = follow the money/recipient. Most IT/ITeS, BPO, professional services fall here. IT export disputes — if recipient is in USA and Indian company supplies → POS = USA = export. But if related company → "distinct persons" deeming → POS may be India.
§13(3)(a) Services on goods requiring physical availability — repair, maintenance, processing Location where services ACTUALLY PERFORMED
If remote (electronic means) → where goods situated at time of supply
Proviso: Goods temporarily imported for repair & re-exported → POS = outside India (EXEMPT)		 Send machine to India for repair → POS = India. If repaired remotely while machine is in India → POS = India. If imported temporarily and re-exported after repair → POS outside India (2018 amendment). Warranty repairs, ship/aircraft repairs in India. Temporary importation + repair + re-export → zero-rated. Failure to re-export → POS = India → IGST liability.
§13(3)(b) Services requiring physical presence of recipient WITH supplier (e.g., medical, advisory in-person) Location where services ACTUALLY PERFORMED Medical tourism — patient comes to India for treatment → POS = India (even if patient is foreign). IGST payable at Indian rate. Foreign patients treated in India — IGST payable even to foreign patients. Dispute: whether hospital exempt or IGST-able on foreign patient services.
§13(4) Immovable property services (architects, estate agents, accommodation, construction) Where immovable property is located or INTENDED TO BE LOCATED Same as §12(3) — property location controls. Even for foreign architects designing Indian property. Foreign architect designing Indian building → POS = India → IGST payable by RCM on recipient in India.
§13(5) Events — admission/organisation (cultural, sporting, educational, entertainment) Where event is ACTUALLY HELD IPL held in India — even foreign broadcast rights arrangement → POS = India for on-ground services. Foreign sponsors attending Indian events — event held in India = IGST. International sporting events in India — all related services: POS = India.
§13(6) §13(3)/(4)/(5) services at MULTIPLE LOCATIONS including taxable territory Location IN TAXABLE TERRITORY (India) If any part of service is in India (among multiple locations) → entire POS = India. India wins. Multi-location services — even partial India presence → entire service taxed in India. No apportionment allowed here.
§13(7) §13(3)/(4)/(5) services in MULTIPLE States/UTs Apportioned to each State/UT proportionally If service spans multiple Indian states → apportion value per state. Pan-India construction project → value apportioned to each state where work performed.
§13(8)(a) Services by banking company / financial institution / NBFC to account holders Location of SUPPLIER of services (not recipient) Bank's POS = bank's location (not depositor's location). Bank in India → POS = India always. Foreign account holders of Indian banks — POS = India (not their country). IGST payable on these services.
§13(8)(b) INTERMEDIARY SERVICES Location of SUPPLIER of services (intermediary's location) Intermediary's POS = where the intermediary is. If intermediary in India → POS = India → NOT export even if principal is overseas. Most litigated rule in IGST Act. BPO/call center companies claiming export — if classified as intermediary → POS = India → IGST payable → no refund. SC, HC, AAR all split.
§13(8)(c) Hiring of means of transport (excl. aircraft and vessels exceeding 1 month) up to 1 month Location of SUPPLIER Short-term vehicle/transport hire → POS = lessor's location. Over 1 month → general rule (§13(2)) applies. Short-term yacht/car hire by foreign tourists from Indian company → POS = India → IGST payable.
§13(10) Passenger transportation services Place where passenger EMBARKS for continuous journey Embarkation point = POS. Onward+return ticket → return = separate journey. International flights from India — POS = Indian airport (embarkation) → IGST on portion for international leg if applicable.
§13(11) Services on board conveyance (in-flight, in-train) First scheduled point of departure Same as §12(10) — first departure = POS.
§13(12) OIDAR Services (cross-border) Location of RECIPIENT
Explanation: Recipient deemed in taxable territory if ANY 2 of 7 conditions met		 Foreign Netflix to Indian user → POS = India (user's location). Even if user gives wrong address — 7 deeming conditions catch them. Foreign OIDAR suppliers — IGST via simplified registration scheme. Revenue from Indian users taxable in India. 7 deeming conditions ensure no avoidance via fake addresses.
§13(13) Anti-avoidance / Uniform application Govt may notify any description of services or circumstances to prevent double taxation, non-taxation, or ensure uniform application → POS = place of effective use and enjoyment Fallback power to fix anomalies. Not yet widely used but available to plug gaps. Regulatory tool — watch for notifications under this sub-section that may override §13(2).
⚠️ THE INTERMEDIARY TRAP — §13(8)(b)
The single most disputed provision in IGST Act. Classification as intermediary → POS = India → no export → IGST payable → no zero-rating refund. Courts and AAR have struggled with BPO, IT support companies, commission agents. Key test: Does the person supply on OWN ACCOUNT or merely facilitate between others? Own account = §13(2) general rule (POS = recipient's location) → export possible. Facilitator = §13(8)(b) intermediary → POS = India → not export.
§13 applies only when supplier OR recipient is outside India | IGST Circular 159/2021-GST on intermediary | Circular 161/2021-GST on cross-border services
Section 14 — Special Provision for OIDAR Services by Non-Taxable Territory Supplier
💻 Section 14 — OIDAR Services: Foreign Supplier to Indian Unregistered Recipient
Netflix/Spotify/SaaS model — direct tax liability on foreign supplier
§14(1) — Who Pays Tax?
Rule: OIDAR services by person in NON-TAXABLE TERRITORY → received by NON-TAXABLE ONLINE RECIPIENT (NTOR = unregistered Indian) → FOREIGN SUPPLIER is the person liable to pay IGST
Exception for Intermediary: Where services supplied through intermediary in non-taxable territory — intermediary DEEMED to be recipient (buying from supplier) and re-supplying to NTOR
Unless intermediary proves all 4 conditions:
(a) Invoice clearly identifies service and its supplier in non-taxable territory
(b) Intermediary does NOT authorise charge, collect or be responsible for payment between NTOR and supplier
(c) Intermediary does NOT authorise delivery
(d) Intermediary does NOT set terms/conditions of supply — those are set by actual supplier
📝 If the 4 conditions are met → intermediary is a PURE pass-through → actual foreign supplier pays IGST. If conditions NOT met → intermediary is deemed buyer+re-supplier → intermediary pays IGST as if it supplied from India. Anti-abuse rule.
§14(2) — Simplified Registration Scheme
Foreign OIDAR supplier liable under §14(1) → shall register under SIMPLIFIED REGISTRATION SCHEME (notified by Govt) for IGST payment
If representative in India: representative registers & pays IGST
No physical presence + no representative: supplier appoints person in India → that person liable to pay
📝 Foreign Netflix/Spotify doesn't need full GST registration (GSTR-1, GSTR-3B). They register under a simplified scheme and file quarterly returns. If they don't register → Indian representative or appointed person is liable.
💡 Spotify AB (Sweden) supplies music streaming to unregistered Indian users (₹119/month plan). POS = India (§13(12)). Spotify must register under simplified scheme and pay IGST on collections from Indian users. Alternatively, if Indian payment gateway controls billing → §14(1) intermediary provisions may apply.
Simplified Registration Scheme: Notification 10/2017-IT | IGST Circular on OIDAR: Circular 204/2023-GST | Definition of NTOR: §2(16) IGST Act as amended 01.10.2023
Section 14A — Online Money Gaming: Foreign Supplier [🆕 Inserted Oct 2023]
🆕 INSERTED by IGST Amendment Act, 2023 — w.e.f. 01.10.2023
Section 14A — Special Provision for Online Money Gaming Supplier Outside Taxable Territory
§14A(1) — Liability
Foreign supplier of online money gaming (as defined in §2(80B) CGST Act) NOT located in taxable territory → supplying to person IN taxable territory → LIABLE to pay IGST on such supply
📝 Online money gaming = real-money gaming (fantasy sports, online poker, rummy for real stakes etc.). Foreign operators offering such services to Indian players → must pay IGST on 28% (highest slab).
💡 Betway (Malta) offers cricket fantasy league to Indian users. Online money gaming by foreign entity to Indian person → §14A liability. Must register or face website blocking.
§14A(2) — Registration
Same as §14(2) — Simplified Registration Scheme
Representative in India → registers & pays
No presence → must appoint Indian person
§14A(3) — Website Blocking Penalty
Failure to comply with §14A(1) or §14A(2) → liable to have computer resource/website BLOCKED for access by public in India
Notwithstanding anything in §69A IT Act, 2000
📝 This is the enforcement weapon — non-compliant foreign gaming sites can be BLOCKED in India. Government has actually used this power against non-compliant operators.
💡 Several offshore gaming platforms were blocked in Jan 2024 for non-compliance with §14A(2). Blocking is immediate — no prior notice required under §69A IT Act overriding provision.
🆕 §14A — Background: Online money gaming (real-money games) were brought under 28% GST on full face value (not GGR) from 01.10.2023. Foreign operators were a concern — §14A creates specific liability and enforcement mechanism (website blocking) for non-compliant offshore operators. This is India's extraterritorial GST reach.
Online money gaming defined in §2(80B) CGST Act | Taxed at 28% on face value | Blocking authority under §69A IT Act read with §14A IGST Act
Section 15 — Refund of IGST to International Tourist
✈️ Section 15 — Tourist Refund Scheme
IGST paid on goods taken out of India by tourist
RULE
IGST paid by TOURIST leaving India on any supply of GOODS taken out of India by him → shall be REFUNDED
In such manner & subject to such conditions and safeguards as may be prescribed
DEFINITION — "Tourist"
A person NOT normally resident in India
Who enters India for stay of NOT MORE THAN 6 MONTHS
For legitimate NON-IMMIGRANT purposes
📝 Tourist refund = duty-free equivalent under GST. Foreign visitor buys goods in India, pays IGST, takes them home → gets refund at airport. Similar to VAT refund schemes in Europe. Rules yet to be fully operationalized.
💡 Japanese tourist buys silk sarees from Varanasi, pays IGST. Takes sarees out of India at Delhi airport. Can claim refund of IGST paid. Conditions: goods must actually leave India; tourist must depart within 6 months of entry.
LIMITATION
Applies only to GOODS — NOT services
Tourist must be NON-RESIDENT and entry for non-immigrant purpose
NOT available to: NRIs on home visits (if NRI is "not normally resident" may qualify), long-stay foreigners (over 6 months)
🔍 "Not normally resident in India" — undefined in IGST Act. GP Singh: must be interpreted by residence in usual sense. FEMA definition of NRI or IT Act §6 may be borrowed. Circular 175/2022-GST acknowledges operationalization challenges.
§15 scheme not yet operationalized via Rules | IGST Rules 2017 do not contain tourist refund procedure yet | Watch for notification
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal — IGST Act, 2017
These statutory principles and ratios are for use in personal hearings before GST authorities, Appellate Authority, and GSTAT. State them confidently with section citations.
Section 2(6) IGST — Export of Services: All 5 Conditions are Mandatory (Conjunctive)
Statutory Provision | IGST Act, 2017 | Read with §13(2)
Key Statutory Argument
"Export of services" requires satisfaction of ALL FIVE conditions under §2(6) simultaneously. The word "and" connecting clauses (i) through (v) is conjunctive — not disjunctive. Failure of any single condition — particularly condition (v) on 'distinct persons' or condition (iv) on foreign exchange payment — renders the supply a taxable domestic supply, not an export. No partial compliance suffices.
When to Use — When opposing refund claim for alleged export of IT/BPO services where related company (distinct person) or INR payment issues exist. Also when defending against demand where department denies export status.
Section 13(8)(b) IGST — Intermediary POS: Supplier's Location (India)
Statutory Provision | Most Litigated Rule in IGST Act | Read with §2(13)
Key Statutory Argument
The place of supply of intermediary services is the location of the supplier of services — i.e., where the intermediary is located. If the intermediary is in India, the POS is India — IGST is payable. This is NOT export of services regardless of where the principal or ultimate recipient is located. The decisive test is: does the person supply goods/services on its OWN ACCOUNT? If yes — not intermediary (§13(2) applies). If the person merely arranges or facilitates between two others — it is an intermediary — §13(8)(b) applies.
When to Use — In hearings defending against intermediary classification of IT/BPO companies. Also when challenging refund claims of companies wrongly claiming export status while functioning as intermediaries.
Section 5(1) IGST — Exclusion of Alcoholic Liquor & ENA [Finance (No.2) Act 2024]
Statutory Provision as Amended | w.e.f. 01.11.2024
Key Statutory Argument (Latest Amendment)
Un-denatured extra neutral alcohol and rectified spirit used for manufacture of alcoholic liquor for human consumption has been expressly excluded from the levy of IGST under §5(1) with effect from 01.11.2024 vide the Finance (No.2) Act, 2024. Any demand for IGST on inter-State supply of such ENA/rectified spirit relating to period from 01.11.2024 onwards is without jurisdiction — the charging section itself excludes this commodity.
When to Use — In defending demands for IGST on ENA/rectified spirit supplied inter-State for liquor manufacture from November 2024. Cite the specific amendment date to limit relief to post-amendment period while keeping pre-amendment disputes open.
Section 6A IGST — General Practice Waiver: Non-Recovery Power [Finance (No.2) Act 2024]
Statutory Provision — Newly Inserted | w.e.f. 01.11.2024 | Modeled on §11C Customs Act
Key Statutory Argument
Section 6A of the IGST Act, inserted by the Finance (No.2) Act 2024, empowers the Government to waive recovery of IGST that was not levied or was short-levied due to a generally prevalent trade practice — provided the Government issues a notification to that effect. Once such notification is issued, it operates notwithstanding anything contained in the IGST Act — overriding even demand provisions. This is the legislative equivalent of a general amnesty for industry-wide bonafide mistakes.
When to Use — When facing demands arising from industry-wide practices where the entire sector followed the same treatment (e.g., non-charging of IGST on certain cross-border service categories). Argue that §6A notification should be sought / may be issued before demand is confirmed.
Section 7(5)(b) IGST — SEZ Supply: Always Inter-State
Statutory Provision | IGST Act §7(5)(b) | Circular 48/22/2018-GST
Key Ratio
Any supply of goods or services or both to or by a Special Economic Zone developer or unit is always treated as inter-State supply under §7(5)(b) of the IGST Act, regardless of whether the supplier and the SEZ are located in the same State. This is a mandatory deeming provision — not discretionary. Consequently, all supplies to SEZ units attract IGST (even if supplier is in same State) and are zero-rated under §16 IGST Act.
When to Use — When opposing demands for CGST+SGST on supplies to SEZ units. Also when claiming zero-rating refund for supplies made to SEZ from same State as supplier — department sometimes incorrectly treats as intra-State.
Section 8 Explanation 1 — Distinct Persons: Deeming Fiction Strictly Construed
Statutory Provision | IGST Act §8 Explanation 1 | GP Singh Principle on Deeming Fictions
Key Ratio
Explanation 1 to §8 IGST Act creates a deeming fiction — establishments of the same person in different States/UTs are treated as "distinct persons" for the purposes of the IGST Act. GP Singh's Principles of Statutory Interpretation: a deeming provision must be applied strictly for the purpose for which it is deemed, and not extended beyond. "Distinct persons" under §8 Explanation 1 is for the purpose of determining inter-State/intra-State character of supply — it does not make them "distinct persons" for all GST purposes such as ITC eligibility or anti-profiteering.
When to Use — When department seeks to use "distinct persons" fiction for purposes beyond supply classification (e.g., denying ITC on cross-charge basis claiming "distinct persons" means independent entities). Argue strict construction of deeming fiction.
Section 14A(3) IGST — Website Blocking for Non-Compliant Online Money Gaming Operators
Statutory Provision | Inserted 01.10.2023 | Enforcement Weapon
Key Statutory Power
Section 14A(3) of the IGST Act provides that in case of failure by a foreign online money gaming supplier to comply with §14A(1) (liability to pay IGST) or §14A(2) (simplified registration), any computer resource used for such supply shall be liable to be blocked for access by the public in India, notwithstanding anything in §69A of the Information Technology Act, 2000. This blocking power operates independently of the IT Act framework — creating a parallel enforcement mechanism under GST law.
When to Use — As a threat/compliance argument when advising foreign online gaming clients on India GST registration. Also relevant in any litigation involving whether the blocking action is proportionate — §14A(3) gives statutory backing to blocking without need for separate IT Act proceedings.
Section 9 IGST — Territorial Waters Deeming: Nearest Coastal State
Statutory Provision | IGST Act §9 | Offshore sector disputes
Key Statutory Argument
Notwithstanding anything in the IGST Act, where the location of the supplier or the place of supply is in the territorial waters, it shall be deemed for the purposes of this Act to be located in the coastal State or Union territory nearest to the baseline point. This deeming provision conclusively determines jurisdiction — there is no room for the supplier to argue that territorial waters are outside any State's jurisdiction.
When to Use — In offshore oil/gas sector disputes, ship chandelling, marine services disputes where the department of one coastal State claims jurisdiction and the supplier challenges on grounds of being "in territorial waters" which is a Union subject.
Section 4 IGST — Jurisdictional Challenge: State Officer's Cross-Authorization Scope
Statutory Provision | IGST Act §4 | Administrative Law Principle
Key Jurisdictional Argument
Section 4 of the IGST Act authorizes State GST/UTGST officers to act as proper officers under the IGST Act "subject to such exceptions and conditions as the Government shall, on the recommendations of the Council, by notification, specify." This authorization is conditional — not blanket. A State officer exercising IGST powers in respect of matters not covered by the relevant notification exceeds jurisdiction. Any SCN, search, seizure or demand made by a State officer without valid §4 cross-authorization in respect of that category of IGST matter is without jurisdiction and liable to be challenged.
When to Use — When a State GST officer issues SCN for IGST matters where the notification does not specifically include that power. Check the cross-authorization notification carefully — this is a powerful jurisdictional challenge.
For professional litigation use | Pawan Soni & Associates, Ichalkaranji
IGST Act — Ch.VII–IX (Sec.16–25)

⚡ IGST Act, 2017 — Chapters VII to IX

Sections 16–25 | Zero Rated Supply · Apportionment of Tax · Miscellaneous

Source: IGST Act, 2017 | ICAI November 2025 Edition | CA Pawankumar Soni, Ichalkaranji

Permitted / Valid
Prohibited / Void
Condition / If / Unless
Optional / Proviso

💡 Example
