⚔️ Hearing Arsenal
📊 Tier 1 — Chapter-Wide Overview | IGST Act Chapters VII–IX (§§16–25)
Section Chapter Provision / Heading Key Trigger / Condition Litigation Relevance Amendment Flag
§16(1) VII Definition of Zero Rated Supply Export of goods/services OR supply to SEZ developer/unit for authorised operations Core definition — determines eligibility for LUT/bond route and refund 🔶 "for authorised operations" inserted — Finance Act 2021, w.e.f. 01.10.2023
§16(2) VII ITC on Zero Rated Supply Subject to §17(5) CGST Act ITC available even if zero-rated supply = exempt supply — critical argument point
§16(3) VII Refund — LUT/Bond Route Without payment of IGST, on bond/LUT — refund of unutilised ITC Amended substantially — merged two options into single unified refund route 🔶 Substituted — Finance Act 2021, w.e.f. 01.10.2023 + Proviso added — Finance (No.2) Act 2024, w.e.f. 01.11.2024
§16(4) VII Govt Power to Specify Class of Persons/Goods On Council's recommendation + notification Can enable IGST-payment-based export refund for specified class 🔶 §16(4)(i) and (ii) amended — Finance (No.2) Act 2024, w.e.f. 01.11.2024
§16(5) VII No Refund — Export Duty Goods Zero rated supply of goods subject to export duty Absolute bar on ITC refund AND IGST refund if goods attract export duty 🔶 NEW sub-section — Finance (No.2) Act 2024, w.e.f. 01.11.2024
§17(1) VIII Apportionment of IGST to Central Govt Six specified categories (unregistered person, composition, no ITC availed, lapsed ITC, imports) Determines IGST split between Centre and States — not directly a taxpayer right issue
§17(2) VIII Balance Apportionment to States/UTs After Central Govt share, balance apportioned to supply State Proviso — if supply place cannot be determined: proportion to total supplies
§17(2A) VIII Ad Hoc Apportionment — 50:50 Amount not covered under (1)/(2) — Council's recommendation Temporary ad hoc split — adjusted later 🔶 Inserted — IGST (Amendment) Act 2018, w.e.f. 01.02.2019
§17(3–5) VIII Interest, Penalty, Refund — mutatis mutandis Applies same apportionment logic to interest/penalty/compounding Refund apportioned to State reduces future State allocation
§17A VIII Transfer of Certain Amounts (ECL Cross-Transfer) Amount transferred from IGST ECL to SGST/UTGST ECL Government transfers equivalent to State/UT tax account 🔶 New section — Finance (No.2) Act 2019, w.e.f. 01.01.2020
§18 VIII Transfer of ITC (IGST credit utilisation) IGST credit used for CGST/UTGST/SGST — corresponding transfer from IGST account Basis for cross-credit utilisation mechanism — refund offset calculations
§19 VIII Wrong Tax Correction (IGST ↔ CGST+SGST) (1) IGST paid on intra-State → refund; (2) CGST+SGST paid on inter-State → no interest Critical litigation tool — no interest payable in reverse scenario
§20 IX Application of CGST Act — 25 provisions mutatis mutandis — all 25 CGST provisions apply to IGST with modifications TDS rate 2%; TCS rate not exceeding 2%; value includes all duties; penalty = sum total; Appeal cap: ₹40 crores 🔶 Appeal cap amended to ₹40 crores — Finance (No.2) Act 2024, w.e.f. 01.11.2024
§21 IX Import of Services — post appointed day Taxable regardless of when transaction initiated — if service imported on/after appointed day Partial prior tax payment = only balance IGST payable; no double taxation
§22–24 IX Rule-Making, Regulation-Making, Parliamentary Laying Government / Board powers with retrospective effect allowed (not earlier than Act commencement) Retrospective rules possible — challenge if adverse
§25 IX Removal of Difficulties Within 5 years from Act commencement — government order published in Official Gazette Orders must be consistent with Act; laid before Parliament 🔶 Period changed from 3 years to 5 years — Finance Act 2020, w.e.f. 30.06.2020
§16(1) — Definition of "Zero Rated Supply" | Chapter VII
⚡ Section 16(1) — Zero Rated Supply
IGST Act, 2017 | Chapter VII
📝 Zero-rated supply means even though no tax is charged on the output, the supplier can still claim full ITC on inputs used for making such supply. It is the most favourable GST treatment possible for exporters and SEZ suppliers.
"Zero Rated Supply" means any of the following supplies:
§16(1)(a)
Export of goods or services or both
📝 Any export — whether goods leave India or services are provided to overseas recipient — is zero rated. No GST on output but ITC on inputs is fully available.
💡 Software exporter invoices US client in USD. No IGST on invoice. But IGST/CGST+SGST on office rent, internet, laptops → fully creditable.
🔍 GP Singh: "Both" = disjunctive "or" extends scope. Supply of goods AND services together in a single transaction (composite export) = zero rated. Cannot narrowly restrict to either goods-only or services-only export.
§16(1)(b)
Supply of goods or services or both to
SEZ Developer OR SEZ Unit
AMDT 2021/2023
For authorised operations only
📝 Supply to an SEZ person is zero rated — but only if the goods/services are meant for their authorised operations. Supply for personal use or non-authorised purposes ≠ zero rated.
💡 IT company in SEEPZ is an SEZ unit. Supplier provides server hardware for IT operations → zero rated. But cafeteria food supplied to SEZ employees → NOT zero rated (not authorised operation).
🔍 GP Singh: "Authorised operations" — restrictive phrase inserted by Finance Act 2021 (w.e.f. 01.10.2023). Purposive construction: supplies benefitting employees or personal use of SEZ persons cannot claim zero-rating benefit. Burden on supplier to prove nexus with authorised operation.
🔶 AMDT — Finance Act 2021 | Notif. No. 27/2023-CT | w.e.f. 01.10.2023
Words "for authorised operations" inserted in §16(1)(b). Restricts zero-rating for SEZ supplies to only those used for authorised operations — personal/non-operational supplies are excluded.
📋 Corresponding Rules: CGST Rules — Rule 89 (Refund Application) | Rule 96 (Refund of IGST on Export of Goods)
§16(2) — ITC Availability on Zero Rated Supply
🔑 Section 16(2) — ITC Credit on Zero Rated Supplies
✓ PERMITTED
Credit of input tax may be availed for making zero-rated supplies —
Notwithstanding that such supply may be an exempt supply
Subject to §17(5) of CGST Act
📝 Normally, ITC is blocked on exempt supplies (Section 17(2) CGST). But this section carves out an exception — zero rated supplies are treated as if they are taxable for ITC purposes, even if the actual supply is exempt (e.g., export of exempt goods).
💡 Exporter exports fresh vegetables (exempt goods). Normally, ITC on transport/cold storage used for exempt goods = blocked. BUT because export = zero rated supply, §16(2) overrides — ITC on inputs for this export is available.
OVERRIDES
§17(2) CGST Act
(ITC block on exempt supplies)
STILL SUBJECT TO
§17(5) CGST Act
(Blocked credit list — motor vehicles, personal consumption, etc.)
📝 Even for zero rated supplies, if the input is in the blocked credit list of §17(5) (like motor vehicles), ITC remains blocked. §16(2) does not override §17(5).
🔍 GP Singh: "Notwithstanding" = non-obstante clause. Must be read broadly — it overrides §17(2) specifically. But "subject to §17(5)" = explicit carve-out preserved by legislature. Courts cannot extend the "notwithstanding" to also override §17(5). Two phrases in same provision — "notwithstanding" expands; "subject to" contracts.
📋 Corresponding Rules: Rule 89(4) — Refund of ITC on Zero Rated Supplies | CGST Act §17(2) and §17(5) cross-reference
§16(3) — Refund Mechanism for Zero Rated Supplies 🔶 SUBSTITUTED
💰 Section 16(3) — Zero Rated Supply Refund Route
Substituted — Finance Act 2021 | w.e.f. 01.10.2023
🔶 SUBSTITUTED — Finance Act 2021 | Notif. 27/2023-CT | w.e.f. 01.10.2023
Prior to substitution, §16(3) had TWO options: (a) LUT/Bond route with ITC refund, or (b) Payment of IGST with refund of IGST paid. Now merged into UNIFIED single route.
CURRENT POSITION — POST 01.10.2023
A registered person making zero rated supply shall be eligible to claim:
Refund of unutilised ITC on supply of goods or services or both,
without payment of integrated tax
on bond or LUT, in accordance with §54 CGST Act
📝 The unified route means: Give an LUT or bond → don't pay IGST on exports → claim refund of accumulated ITC. Earlier there was a dual option — paying IGST first and then claiming IGST refund. That option has been removed from §16(3) itself but preserved in §16(4) for government-notified class of persons.
🔶 NEW PROVISO — Finance (No.2) Act 2024 | Notif. No. 17/2024-CT | w.e.f. 01.11.2024

Proviso to §16(3) — Export Proceeds Realisation Condition:

A registered person making zero rated supply of goods shall —
in case of non-realisation of sale proceeds —
be liable to deposit the refund so received under this sub-section
along with applicable interest under §50 CGST Act
within 30 days after expiry of FEMA time limit for receipt of foreign exchange remittances
📝 In simple terms: You exported goods, got refund of ITC. But if the export proceeds (foreign payment) do not come in within the FEMA prescribed time limit → you must return the refund + pay interest within 30 days of expiry of that FEMA period. FEMA = Foreign Exchange Management Act, 1999.
💡 Exporter receives ITC refund of ₹10 lakhs in August 2024. FEMA period for export proceeds = 9 months = expires May 2025. If USD payment from foreign buyer not received by May 2025 → Exporter must deposit ₹10 lakhs + interest within 30 days = by June 2025.
🔍 GP Singh: "Shall be liable to deposit" — mandatory, not discretionary. Non-compliance triggers interest under §50 CGST Act (currently 18% p.a.). Limitation period starts running from 31st day after FEMA period expiry. Challenge angle: FEMA timelines are RBI-governed and can be extended — if RBI extension obtained, does the 30-day countdown restart? Yes — law says "expiry of time limit prescribed under FEMA" which would include extended period.
📋 Rule 89 CGST Rules (Refund Application) | Rule 96A (LUT) | FEMA (Export of Goods and Services) Regulations 2015 — RBI Master Circular on Export of Goods/Services
§16(4) — Government Power to Specify Class / Goods for IGST-Payment Route 🔶 AMENDED
🏛️ Section 16(4) — Government Notification Power
On Recommendation of GST Council
POWER
Government may, on recommendation of Council, by notification, specify —
subject to conditions, safeguards and procedures:
§16(4)(i)
A class of persons who may:
Make zero rated supply on payment of IGST
AND
Claim refund of tax so paid
AMDT 2024
in accordance with §54 CGST Act or rules thereunder
📝 For certain specified types of exporters, Government can allow them to pay IGST on exports and then get a refund of that IGST — instead of the LUT/bond route.
🔶 AMDT — Finance (No.2) Act 2024 | w.e.f. 01.11.2024
Words "in accordance with the provisions of section 54 of the CGST Act or the rules made thereunder" inserted — aligning refund claim to §54 CGST framework.
§16(4)(ii)
A class of goods or services or both
AMDT 2024
on zero rated supply of which, supplier shall pay IGST and claim refund of tax so paid, in accordance with §54 CGST Act or rules thereunder
📝 For specific goods/services, Government can mandate the IGST-payment route (instead of LUT). Earlier worded narrowly for "goods" only; now covers goods or services or both.
🔶 AMDT — Finance (No.2) Act 2024 | w.e.f. 01.11.2024
Clause substituted — now covers "goods or services or both" (earlier only "goods"). Also aligns refund route to §54 CGST framework explicitly.
🔍 GP Singh: "May" in "Government may...specify" = discretionary. Government is not obligated to create these classes. But once a notification is issued under §16(4), the specified class of persons/goods must follow the IGST-payment route — they cannot opt for LUT route. The power is regulatory, not adjudicatory — delegated legislation governs.
📋 Corresponding: Rule 96 CGST Rules (Refund of IGST paid on export) | Notifications issued by CBIC under §16(4) from time to time
§16(5) — No Refund on Export Duty Goods 🔶 NEW SUB-SECTION — Finance (No.2) Act 2024
🚫 Section 16(5) — Absolute Bar on Refund | Export Duty Goods
NEWLY INSERTED — Finance (No.2) Act 2024 | w.e.f. 01.11.2024
🔶 NEW INSERTION — Finance (No.2) Act 2024 | Notif. No. 17/2024-CT dated 27.09.2024 | w.e.f. 01.11.2024
✗ ABSOLUTE BAR — Notwithstanding anything in §16(3) and §16(4)

NO REFUND of —
(a) Unutilised ITC on account of zero rated supply of goods, OR
(b) Integrated tax paid on account of zero rated supply of goods

— shall be allowed where such zero rated supply of goods are subject to export duty
📝 Some goods attract export duty (customs duty on export) — like iron ore, rice, etc. If you export such goods, you cannot claim ITC refund OR IGST refund, even though the supply is zero rated. The zero-rating benefit (of refund) is taken away by export duty policy — Government does not want to incentivise export of such goods.
💡 Exporter exports raw cotton (subject to export duty). Even though export = zero rated supply, exporter CANNOT claim: (a) refund of accumulated ITC, or (b) refund of IGST paid on export. Both routes blocked.
OVERRIDES
§16(3) — LUT/Bond refund route
&
§16(4) — IGST-payment refund route
📝 "Notwithstanding" = this sub-section prevails over both §16(3) and §16(4). No escape — if export duty applies on the goods, no refund at all.
SCOPE
Only for goods subject to export duty
NOT for services
📝 Export duty is levied only on goods. Services have no export duty concept. So §16(5) affects only goods exporters, not service exporters.
ITC FATE
Blocked ITC cannot be refunded — but may be
carried forward for set-off against future domestic IGST/CGST liability (if any)
📝 The ITC is not lost — it sits in the credit ledger. If the exporter also has domestic sales, the accumulated ITC can be set off against future tax liability. But refund in cash — blocked.
🔍 GP Singh: "Notwithstanding anything contained in sub-sections (3) and (4)" — strongest non-obstante clause. §16(5) is a special provision prevailing over general provisions in (3) and (4). "Subject to export duty" — the trigger is the goods being dutiable, not the actual payment of duty. Even if exporter gets an exemption from export duty for a particular shipment, the classification of goods as "subject to export duty" is what matters — check the tariff schedule.
📋 Export Duty: Customs Tariff Act, 1975 — Schedule II (Export Duty List) | Common export duty goods: Rice (de-husked), Iron ore, some petroleum products
§17(1) — Apportionment of IGST to Central Government | Chapter VIII
🏛️ Section 17(1) — IGST Apportionment to Centre
Chapter VIII — Apportionment of Tax & Settlement of Funds
📝 IGST is a dual tax — part belongs to Centre, part to State where consumption happens. §17 governs the mechanical splitting and transfer of collected IGST. Not directly a taxpayer right but relevant in inter-governmental disputes and refund scenarios.
Out of IGST paid to Central Govt — the amount at rate equivalent to central tax on similar intra-State supply shall be apportioned to Centre in these 6 categories:
Clause Category Plain English
§17(1)(a) Inter-State supply to unregistered person OR composition taxpayer B2C inter-State sales / supply to composition dealers — Centre keeps its portion
§17(1)(b) Inter-State supply where recipient is not eligible for ITC Registered recipient who cannot claim ITC — Centre gets central tax equivalent
§17(1)(c) Inter-State supply where ITC not availed within specified period → remains in IGST account after annual return due date Lapsed/unclaimed ITC — allocated to Centre after annual return deadline passes
§17(1)(d) Import of goods/services by unregistered person OR composition taxpayer Imports by B2C / composition taxpayers — Centre keeps its share
§17(1)(e) Import where registered person is not eligible for ITC Importer cannot use ITC — Centre gets central tax equivalent
§17(1)(f) Import where ITC not availed within specified period → lapsed Unclaimed import ITC after annual return deadline → allocated to Centre
📋 Mechanism: §17(4) prescribes manner and timeline for transfer from IGST account to central tax account
§17(2) / §17(2A) / §17(3–5) / §17A — State Apportionment, Ad Hoc & Refund Provisions
⚖️ Section 17(2) to 17A — Balance Apportionment, Ad Hoc & Refund
§17(2) — Balance to States/UTs
Balance IGST (after Central Govt share) apportioned to State where supply takes place
PROVISO 1
If place of supply cannot be determined separately → apportion in proportion to total supplies made to each State in that FY
PROVISO 2
If taxable person not identifiable → apportion to all States in proportion to State tax collected by each State in preceding FY
📝 When you cannot trace who supplied or where supply happened, the IGST is split based on each State's share of total GST collection in the previous year. Like dividing an unaddressed parcel based on each city's share of mail volume.
§17(2A) — Ad Hoc Apportionment AMDT 2019
🔶 Inserted — IGST Amendment Act 2018 | w.e.f. 01.02.2019

Amount not apportioned under (1) or (2) may — for time being — on Council's recommendation — be apportioned:
50% to Central Govt
50% to State Govts / UTs
on ad hoc basis — adjusted later against (1)/(2) amounts
📝 A practical safety valve — if the normal apportionment formulas in (1) and (2) haven't resolved all IGST amounts, do a 50:50 split for now, and fix it later when data is available.
§17(3) to §17(5)
§17(3)
Same apportionment rules apply mutatis mutandis to interest, penalty & compounding amount realised with the tax
📝 Mutatis mutandis = with necessary modifications. The same Centre-State split formula applies not just to tax but also to interest and penalty collected alongside.
§17(4)
After apportionment — IGST account reduced by apportioned amount → Central Govt transfers:
• To central tax account (Centre share)
• To State tax account (State share)
§17(5)
If IGST apportioned to State/UT is later refunded to any person → reduce that refunded amount from future apportionment to that State/UT
📝 If a refund was given out of State's share, the State's future allocation is reduced by that refund amount. Prevents double benefit — refund reduces the State's credit balance.
🔶 §17A — Transfer of Certain Amounts | Finance (No.2) Act 2019 | w.e.f. 01.01.2020

Where any amount has been transferred from the electronic cash ledger under IGST Act to electronic cash ledger under SGST Act or UTGST Act —
Government shall transfer to the State tax account or Union territory tax account an amount equal to the amount transferred from the electronic cash ledger —
in such manner and within such time as may be prescribed.
📝 §17A plugs a practical gap: When a taxpayer transfers money from their IGST cash ledger to their SGST cash ledger (a feature allowed in GST portal), the government must correspondingly move money from IGST account to the respective State tax account. This ensures the State actually receives the funds, not just the taxpayer's ledger entry.
📋 GST Portal functionality: Cross-ledger transfer | Corresponding: §49(4) CGST Act (cross-utilisation of ITC)
§18 — Transfer of Input Tax Credit (ITC Cross-Utilisation)
🔄 Section 18 — Transfer of ITC | IGST Credit Utilised for Other Taxes
📝 IGST credit (accumulated from imports or inter-State purchases) can be used to pay CGST, UTGST, or SGST. When this happens, the IGST collected amount is reduced, and an equivalent is transferred to the respective central/UT/State tax account. This is the plumbing behind cross-utilisation of ITC.
§18(a)
IGST credit utilised for Central Tax payment
[per §49(5) CGST Act]
IGST account reduced by credit utilised → Central Govt transfers equivalent to central tax account
§18(b)
IGST credit utilised for Union Territory Tax payment
[per §9 UTGST Act]
IGST account reduced → Central Govt transfers equivalent to union territory tax account
§18(c)
IGST credit utilised for State Tax payment
[per respective SGST Act]
IGST account reduced → amount apportioned to appropriate State Govt → transferred to State tax account
EXPLANATION
"Appropriate State" = State or Union territory where taxpayer is registered or liable to be registered under CGST Act
📋 §49(5) CGST Act — Cross-utilisation priority: IGST credit can be used for IGST, CGST, SGST/UTGST (in that order). §18 IGST Act provides the backend accounting mechanism.
§19 — Wrong Tax Correction | IGST ↔ CGST+SGST
⚠️ Section 19 — Tax Wrongfully Collected & Paid
Wrong classification: Inter-State vs Intra-State
§19(1) — IGST paid → Later held Intra-State
SITUATION
Registered person paid IGST treating supply as inter-State
→ Subsequently held to be intra-State supply
REMEDY
Eligible for refund of IGST so paid
in such manner & subject to conditions as prescribed
📝 You paid the wrong tax. Now pay CGST+SGST (the correct tax for intra-State). You will get IGST refund — but you must first determine refund procedure, and limitation applies.
💡 Manufacturer in Maharashtra supplies goods to customer also in Maharashtra, but raises IGST invoice (wrong). Later adjudication holds: intra-State supply. → IGST refund + CGST+SGST liability (with interest if paid late).
§19(2) — CGST+SGST paid → Later held Inter-State
SITUATION
Registered person paid CGST + SGST/UTGST treating supply as intra-State
→ Subsequently held to be inter-State supply
RELIEF
NOT required to pay any interest on the IGST amount payable (the correct tax)
📝 You paid CGST+SGST in good faith thinking it was intra-State. Later found to be inter-State. You must now pay IGST — but you are EXEMPTED from interest on that IGST. The wrongly paid CGST+SGST can be refunded per §77 CGST Act.
💡 Gujarat supplier sends goods to Rajasthan but treats as intra-State (e.g., agent holds stock in both states). Pays CGST+SGST. GST officer later holds: inter-State. Supplier must pay IGST — but NO INTEREST on IGST. CGST+SGST refunded per §77 CGST.
⚔️ LITIGATION GOLD — §19(2) with §77 CGST Act
§19(2) + §77 CGST Act together = Complete protection in wrong-head payment scenarios. No interest on IGST (§19(2)) + Refund of CGST/SGST (§77 CGST) without insisting on unjust enrichment where tax is charged separately.
🔍 GP Singh: "Shall not be required to pay any interest" in §19(2) — absolute exemption. Department cannot demand interest on IGST in this scenario. Interest demands in such cases are per se illegal. The provision recognizes the genuine bona fide error in determining place of supply — which is often genuinely ambiguous.
📋 Corresponding: §77 CGST Act (Refund of tax wrongfully collected) | CBIC Circular No. 192/04/2023-GST dated 17.07.2023 — Clarification on wrong head payment scenarios
§20 — Application of CGST Act Provisions (mutatis mutandis) | Chapter IX
📋 Section 20 — CGST Act Provisions Applied to IGST
Chapter IX — Miscellaneous | 25 Provisions + 5 Provisos
📝 IGST Act is a lean statute. Most procedural machinery (returns, audit, demands, appeals, offences) is borrowed from CGST Act with "mutatis mutandis" application — meaning with necessary changes. This single section incorporates 25 chapters of CGST Act into IGST with modifications listed in the provisos.
Sl. CGST Provision Applied Key Modification / IGST Specific Rule Litigation Point
(i)Scope of supplyApplied as-isSchedule I, II, III apply equally for IGST
(ii)Composite & Mixed SupplyApplied as-isNature of supply determination same
(iii)Time & Value of SupplyApplied as-isIGST valuation = CGST valuation rules
(iv)Input Tax CreditApplied as-is§16–17 CGST ITC rules apply for IGST credit also
(v)RegistrationApplied as-isCGST registration = IGST registration
(vi)Tax Invoice, Credit & Debit NotesApplied as-isSame invoice rules; IGST shown separately
(vii)Accounts & RecordsApplied as-is
(viii)Returns (other than late fee)Applied — but late fee provisions differIGST late fee: check separate IGST notification
(ix)Payment of TaxApplied as-isIGST paid through common portal
(x)Tax Deduction at Source (TDS)AMDT Proviso: TDS rate = 2% (vs 1% each for CGST+SGST = 2% total)
For IGST: 2% single rate deduction		TDS on inter-State contracts: 2% IGST (not 1%+1%)
(xi)Collection of Tax at Source (TCS — e-commerce)PROVISO TCS rate: not exceeding 2% on net value of taxable suppliesECO operators: IGST TCS at notified rate (max 2%)
(xii)AssessmentApplied as-is§62–65 CGST apply — scrutiny, best judgment, special audit
(xiii)RefundsApplied as-is (§54 CGST)IGST refund = §54 CGST procedure
(xiv)AuditApplied as-is§65-66 CGST — Dept audit / special audit
(xv)Inspection, Search, Seizure & ArrestApplied as-is§67-72 CGST apply for IGST matters
(xvi)Demands & RecoveryApplied as-is§73-79 CGST — show cause notice, adjudication
(xvii)Liability to Pay in Certain CasesApplied as-is§85-94 CGST — liability of agent, liquidator, partner, etc.
(xviii)Advance RulingApplied as-isAAR/AAAR covers IGST questions
(xix)Appeals & Revision🔶 AMDT 2024 Proviso: Max pre-deposit for appeal before AA or GSTAT = ₹40 crores per appeal
Earlier: ₹50 crores (AA) / ₹100 crores (GSTAT)		₹40 crore cap for IGST appeals — significant reduction
(xx)Presumption as to DocumentsApplied as-is§81 CGST — documents produced by Dept presumed authentic
(xxi)Offences & PenaltiesProviso: Penalty = Sum total of penalties under CGST Act AND SGST/UTGST ActNo double counting — IGST penalty = CGST penalty + SGST penalty
(xxii)Job WorkApplied as-is§143 CGST — job work provisions apply for IGST
(xxiii)Electronic CommerceApplied as-is§52 CGST — ECO obligations apply
(xxiv)Transitional ProvisionsApplied as-is§139-142 CGST
(xxv)Miscellaneous Provisions (imposition of interest and penalty)Also: Proviso: Value of IGST supply includes all duties, cesses, fees under any other law (charged separately by supplier)Inclusive valuation — customs duty, cess, statutory fees form part of IGST value
🔶 AMDT — Finance (No.2) Act 2024 | w.e.f. 01.11.2024
Fifth Proviso to §20 substituted: Maximum pre-deposit payable for each appeal before Appellate Authority or Appellate Tribunal = ₹40 crore.
Earlier: ₹50 crores (before AA) and ₹100 crores (before GSTAT). Now unified at ₹40 crores per appeal for IGST purposes.
🔍 GP Singh on "mutatis mutandis": This phrase is not a blank cheque to apply all CGST provisions unchanged. The phrase itself means "with such modifications as are necessary." Courts have held that provisions fundamentally incompatible with the structure of IGST (e.g., a purely intra-State concept) cannot be applied mutatis mutandis. Challenge angle: If CGST provision by its nature applies only to intra-State transactions, its mutatis mutandis application to IGST may be argued as inapplicable.
📋 §20 is the master borrowing provision — virtually the entire CGST Act's procedural law is imported into IGST. Every IGST litigation involves identifying which CGST provision applies and whether any modification is needed.
§21 — Import of Services Made On or After Appointed Day
🌐 Section 21 — Import of Services — Post Appointed Day Taxability
📝 This section prevents tax avoidance by ensuring that if service import happens after GST came into force (1 July 2017), it is taxable under IGST — even if the contract was signed or advance was paid before GST. Only if tax was fully paid under the old law (Service Tax) is there an exemption.
Import of services on/after appointed day = Taxable under IGST
Regardless of when the transaction was initiated
EXCEPTION 1
Tax on such import was paid in full under the existing law (Service Tax)
→ NO TAX under IGST Act
📝 Full Service Tax paid on the import before GST → No double taxation. Import is exempt from IGST.
EXCEPTION 2
Tax paid in part under existing law
→ Balance amount of IGST payable
📝 If partial Service Tax was paid earlier, only the remaining IGST is now due. No duplication on the already-taxed portion.
EXPLANATION
Transaction "initiated before appointed day" if either:
• Invoice issued before appointed day, OR
• Payment (full or part) received/made before appointed day
📝 For deciding whether a transaction is "before GST day," it's enough if the invoice was raised OR any payment was made — even partial. Both qualify. But the actual import/receipt of service on or after GST day → IGST applies.
💡 Indian company signed agreement with US consultant in April 2017 (pre-GST). US consultant renders services in August 2017 (post-GST). No service tax was paid. → Full IGST applicable on the import, even though contract was pre-GST. | Another case: Partial service tax paid in June 2017 on advance → Only balance IGST on remaining value.
📋 §2(11) IGST Act — "Import of services" definition | Appointed Day = 01.07.2017 | Corresponding: §142 CGST Act — Transitional provisions for pending transactions
§22–24 — Power to Make Rules, Regulations & Parliamentary Laying
📜 Sections 22–24 — Legislative Machinery
§22 — Power to Make Rules
§22(1)
Government may, on Council recommendation, by notification, make rules for carrying out the Act
§22(2)
Rules may cover any matter required or permitted under the Act
§22(3)
Power includes power to give retrospective effect — not earlier than date Act came into force
📝 Rules can be made with retrospective effect — but cannot go before 1 July 2017 (when IGST came into force). Challenge angle: Retrospective rules that impose new obligations or worsen taxpayer's position should be challenged as ultra vires.
§22(4)
Contravention of rules = Penalty up to ₹10,000
§23 — Power to Make Regulations
Board may, by notification, make regulations
consistent with this Act and rules
to carry out the provisions of the Act
📝 The Board (CBIC) can make regulations — which are below rules in the legal hierarchy. Regulations are operational details. Regulations cannot override the Act or the Rules.
🔍 GP Singh: Regulations = Subordinate to Rules = Subordinate to Act. If a Regulation conflicts with a Rule, the Rule prevails. If a Rule conflicts with the Act, the Act prevails.
§24 — Laying Before Parliament
Every rule, regulation, and notification under this Act — shall be laid before each House of Parliament for 30 days (in one or more sessions)
IF PARLIAMENT MODIFIES / ANNULS
Rule/Regulation/Notification has effect only in modified form OR is of no effect — as Parliament decides
SAVING
Modification/annulment does NOT invalidate anything done earlier under that rule/notification
📝 Parliamentary oversight mechanism. Even after a rule is struck down by Parliament, past actions taken under it remain valid — no retrospective invalidity. This protects taxpayers who acted in good faith under the rule.
📋 IGST Rules, 2017 — made under §22. CBIC Regulations — made under §23. All notifications issued under IGST Act — subject to §24 Parliamentary scrutiny.
§25 — Removal of Difficulties 🔶 PERIOD AMENDED
🔧 Section 25 — Removal of Difficulties Order
📝 If any part of the Act creates practical difficulty in implementation, the Government can issue a special order (gazetted) to fix the difficulty — without amending the Act. This is a quick-fix power with conditions and a sunset period.
§25(1) — Power
Government may, on Council's recommendation, by general or special order published in Official Gazette, make provisions for removing difficulty
CONDITIONS
Order must be:
(a) Not inconsistent with this Act, rules or regulations
(b) Necessary or expedient for removing difficulty
SUNSET
No order shall be made after expiry of 5 years from commencement of this Act
AMDT 2020
🔶 Finance Act 2020 | Notif. 4/2020-CT | w.e.f. 30.06.2020
Period changed from 3 years → 5 years from commencement
📝 The 5-year window runs from July 2017. So removal of difficulties orders could be issued until approximately July 2022. After that, any difficulty must be addressed by amendment to the Act itself.
§25(2) — Parliamentary Oversight
Every order made under §25(1) shall be laid, as soon as may be, before each House of Parliament
📝 Even these difficulty-removal orders must be placed before Parliament — ensuring democratic accountability for executive action under this provision.
🔍 GP Singh: An order under §25 cannot override or modify the Act itself — it can only fill gaps or remove operational difficulties. If an order effectively amends the Act or creates new obligations, it exceeds the power under §25 and is ultra vires. This is a powerful challenge in litigation.
📋 §172 CGST Act — equivalent provision (period also amended to 5 years). IGST Act §25 and CGST Act §172 are parallel provisions.
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal — IGST Act Chapters VII–IX | §§16–25
These statutory ratios are for use in personal hearings before GST Officer / First Appellate / GSTAT. State them confidently and cite the section number.
Section 16(2) IGST Act — ITC on Zero Rated Supply (Even if Exempt)
Statutory Provision | IGST Act, 2017 | Read with §17(2) CGST Act
Key Ratio / Statutory Principle
Credit of input tax may be availed for making zero rated supplies, notwithstanding that such supply may be an exempt supply — subject to §17(5) CGST Act. The non-obstante clause in §16(2) IGST Act overrides the ITC restriction in §17(2) CGST Act. Zero rated supplies are NOT to be treated as exempt supplies for the purpose of ITC eligibility.
When to Use — When Department denies ITC on inputs/input services used for export or SEZ supply on the ground that such supply is "exempt." Cite §16(2) IGST Act to establish that the ITC restriction on exempt supplies (§17(2) CGST) does not apply to zero rated supplies. ITC is fully available.
Section 16(1)(b) IGST Act — "For Authorised Operations" Restriction (Post 01.10.2023)
Statutory Provision | IGST Act | As amended by Finance Act 2021 | Effective 01.10.2023
Key Principle
Zero rating for supply to SEZ developer or SEZ unit is available only when such supply is for "authorised operations." Supplies to SEZ persons for personal consumption or non-authorised purposes do not qualify for zero rating. The burden lies on the supplier to establish the nexus between the supply and the authorised operations of the SEZ unit/developer.
When to Use — (a) When Department challenges zero rating on supply to SEZ — argue that the supply was indeed for authorised operations. Produce the SEZ unit's authorised operations letter and show the nexus. (b) When assessing liability on supplies to SEZ that are not for authorised operations — concede zero rating is not available and compute IGST.
Section 16(5) IGST Act — No Refund Where Export Duty Applicable (NEW — w.e.f. 01.11.2024)
Statutory Provision | Finance (No.2) Act 2024 | Notification No. 17/2024-CT dated 27.09.2024
Key Principle
Notwithstanding anything contained in §16(3) and §16(4), no refund of unutilised ITC or IGST paid on zero rated supply of goods shall be allowed where such zero rated supply of goods are subject to export duty. The "subject to export duty" test is based on the classification of goods — not on whether export duty was actually levied or exempted for a specific shipment.
When to Use — When exporters of export duty-liable goods claim ITC refund or IGST refund. Counter the refund claim by establishing that the goods are classified under the export duty schedule — refund is absolutely barred by §16(5) regardless of the route (LUT or IGST payment). Also useful when advising exporter clients before they plan their export to avoid refund blocking.
Section 16(3) Proviso IGST Act — FEMA Non-Realisation → Deposit Refund (NEW — w.e.f. 01.11.2024)
Statutory Provision | Finance (No.2) Act 2024 | Notification No. 17/2024-CT
Key Principle
A registered person making zero rated supply of goods who has received ITC refund — shall, in case of non-realisation of sale proceeds within the FEMA prescribed time limit — be liable to deposit the refund along with applicable interest under §50 CGST Act within thirty days after expiry of the FEMA time limit. If RBI extends the FEMA realisation period, the 30-day countdown restarts from the expiry of the extended period.
When to Use — When Department issues notice for recovery of ITC refund on ground of non-realisation of export proceeds. First check: (a) Has the FEMA period actually expired? (b) Has RBI extension been obtained? (c) Is the 30-day window still open? If yes to (b) or (c), recovery demand is premature. Also: Interest under §50 CGST runs from the 31st day after FEMA expiry — not from date of refund receipt.
Section 19(2) IGST Act — No Interest When CGST+SGST Paid on Inter-State Supply
Statutory Provision | IGST Act, 2017 | Read with §77 CGST Act
Key Ratio — Litigation Gold
A registered person who has paid central tax and State tax on a transaction subsequently held to be an inter-State supply shall NOT be required to pay any interest on the amount of integrated tax payable. The legislature has specifically exempted interest in this scenario — recognising that determination of place of supply can be genuinely ambiguous. Department's demand for interest in such cases is per se illegal.
When to Use — When Department raises IGST demand (with interest) on supplies that were originally treated as intra-State and CGST+SGST was paid. Cite §19(2) IGST Act — no interest is payable on the IGST demanded. Then cite §77 CGST Act for refund of the wrongly paid CGST+SGST. Combined effect: re-characterise without interest burden. This is one of the most powerful protections in IGST Act for wrong-classification scenarios.
Section 20 IGST Act — mutatis mutandis Application of CGST | ₹40 Crore Appeal Cap
Statutory Provision | Finance (No.2) Act 2024 | Effective 01.11.2024
Key Principle — Appeal Pre-Deposit
Under §20 IGST Act read with the fifth proviso (as substituted by Finance (No.2) Act 2024), the maximum amount payable for each appeal filed before the Appellate Authority or the Appellate Tribunal under IGST Act is ₹40 crore per appeal. This is reduced from the earlier ₹50 crore (before AA) and ₹100 crore (before GSTAT). The cap applies per appeal, not per matter.
When to Use — In large IGST disputes (inter-State supply, import, SEZ matters) where Department demands very high pre-deposit for appeal admission. Cite the ₹40 crore cap — applicable w.e.f. 01.11.2024. Any appeal filed on/after 01.11.2024 gets the benefit of the new reduced cap. For pending appeals, clarify applicability as on date of filing.
Section 20 IGST Act — mutatis mutandis: Limitations on Application of CGST Provisions
Statutory Principle | IGST Act, 2017 | Based on GP Singh's interpretation of "mutatis mutandis"
Key Principle
The expression "mutatis mutandis" in §20 IGST Act does not permit a wholesale import of all CGST provisions into IGST without modification. It means "with such necessary changes." A CGST provision that is fundamentally incompatible with the inter-State character of IGST, or that makes sense only in the context of intra-State supplies, cannot be mechanically applied to IGST without the modifications that the nature of the subject matter demands.
When to Use — When Department mechanically applies a CGST provision (e.g., a procedural requirement specific to intra-State transactions) to an IGST scenario without considering the necessary modifications. Argue that mutatis mutandis application requires modification — and the un-modified CGST provision cannot be applied as-is to the IGST facts of the case.
Section 22(3) IGST Act — Retrospective Rules Cannot Precede Act Commencement
Statutory Provision | IGST Act, 2017
Key Principle
The power to make rules conferred by §22 includes the power to give retrospective effect to the rules — but from a date not earlier than the date on which the provisions of this Act came into force. Rules cannot be given retrospective effect prior to 01.07.2017. Any rule made with retrospective effect to a date before 01.07.2017 is ultra vires and void to that extent.
When to Use — When a newly issued CGST/IGST Rule or Notification purports to apply retrospectively to periods before 01.07.2017, or when the retrospective application of a rule significantly prejudices the taxpayer. Argue: retrospective application is limited by §22(3) — cannot go before IGST Act commencement. Any demand raised on the basis of a retrospective rule must be tested for this constitutional and statutory limit.
Section 21 IGST Act — Import of Services: Partial Prior Tax → Only Balance IGST Payable
Statutory Provision | IGST Act, 2017
Key Principle
Where import of services is made on or after the appointed day but tax on such import had been paid in part under the existing law (Service Tax), only the balance amount of IGST is payable. Full IGST cannot be demanded if partial Service Tax was already paid — this would result in double taxation which §21 specifically prevents.
When to Use — In assessments covering the transitional period (2017-2018) where cross-border service contracts straddled the GST implementation date. If Service Tax was paid on any portion — cite §21 proviso — Department can demand only the balance IGST, not full IGST on the entire amount. Document the Service Tax payments carefully to support this argument.
Section 25(1) IGST Act — Removal of Difficulties Orders Cannot Override the Act
Statutory Provision | IGST Act, 2017 | Read with §172 CGST Act
Key Principle
An order under §25 IGST Act for removal of difficulties must be not inconsistent with the provisions of this Act or the rules or regulations made thereunder. Such an order cannot create new obligations, modify the statutory framework, or effectively amend the Act. Any order under §25 that goes beyond filling gaps or removing operational difficulties and instead modifies the substantive provisions of the Act exceeds the power conferred and is ultra vires.
When to Use — When Department relies on a removal of difficulties order or similar executive order to impose obligations not found in the Act/Rules — or to override a taxpayer-favourable provision. Challenge: The order must be "not inconsistent" with the Act — if it effectively amends or contradicts the Act, it is void. Parliamentary oversight under §25(2) further confirms the subordinate nature of such orders.
For professional litigation use | Pawan Soni & Associates, Ichalkaranji | IGST Act — Chapters VII–IX (§§16–25) | ICAI Nov 2025 Edition
IGST Rules — Rules 1–9

📐 IGST Rules, 2017 — Rules 1 to 9

🟣 Amendment Key
AMDT — Nodes highlighted in purple represent insertions/amendments made after original enactment.
Rule 3 inserted vide Notification No. 12/2017-IT dated 15.11.2017 w.e.f. 01.07.2017 | Rules 4–9 inserted vide Notification No. 04/2018-IT dated 31.12.2018 w.e.f. 01.01.2019 | Rule 3(h) clause inserted vide Notification No. 04/2018-IT dated 31.12.2018 w.e.f. 01.01.2019
Permitted / Valid
Prohibited / Condition Not Met
Condition / If / Unless
Optional / Discretionary

📝 Plain English
💡 Example

Rule Reference

📌 TIER 1 — Chapter Overview: IGST Rules, 2017 — Rules 1 to 9
Rule No. Rule Title Governing Section (IGST Act) Core Purpose Amendment Status
Rule 1 Short Title & Commencement §22 IGST Act r/w §20 Naming and effective date — deemed in force from 22.06.2017 Original enactment
Rule 2 Application of CGST Rules §20 IGST Act CGST Rules apply mutatis mutandis to IGST for matters covered under §20 Original enactment
Rule 3 Apportionment of Advertisement Services — Govt. Recipient (§12(14)) §12(14) IGST Act 9 media-specific formulae for state-wise value split: newspapers, pamphlets, hoardings, trains, utility bills, railway tickets, radio, TV, internet, SMS 🟣 AMDT: Notfn. 12/2017-IT dt. 15.11.2017 w.e.f. 01.07.2017
Rule 3(h) further amended vide Notfn. 04/2018-IT dt. 31.12.2018
Rule 4 Apportionment — Immovable Property Services (§12(3)) §12(3) IGST Act 3 methods: Nights-based (hotels), Area-based (property spans states), Time-spent (houseboats) 🟣 AMDT: Notfn. 04/2018-IT dt. 31.12.2018 w.e.f. 01.01.2019
Rule 5 Apportionment — Event Organisation Services (§12(7)) §12(7) IGST Act GAAP-based apportionment when event held in multiple states and consolidated charge 🟣 AMDT: Notfn. 04/2018-IT dt. 31.12.2018 w.e.f. 01.01.2019
Rule 6 Apportionment — Leased Circuit Services (§12(12)) §12(12) IGST Act Point-count method: each start/end/intermediate point = 1 point; value split by state-wise points 🟣 AMDT: Notfn. 04/2018-IT dt. 31.12.2018 w.e.f. 01.01.2019
Rule 7 Cross-Border: Goods-related / Physical Presence Services (§13) §13 IGST Act 3 methods by scenario: equal division (same goods), invoice-ratio (different goods), GAAP (individual services) 🟣 AMDT: Notfn. 04/2018-IT dt. 31.12.2018 w.e.f. 01.01.2019
Rule 8 Cross-Border: Immovable Property Services (§13) §13 IGST Act Apply Rule 4 mutatis mutandis for cross-border immovable property service scenarios 🟣 AMDT: Notfn. 04/2018-IT dt. 31.12.2018 w.e.f. 01.01.2019
Rule 9 Cross-Border: Event Admission/Organisation Services (§13) §13 IGST Act Apply Rule 5 mutatis mutandis for cross-border event service scenarios 🟣 AMDT: Notfn. 04/2018-IT dt. 31.12.2018 w.e.f. 01.01.2019
📌 Parent Act: IGST Act, 2017 (13 of 2017) | Enabling Power: §22 r/w §20 IGST Act | Place of Supply Sections: §12 & §13 IGST Act
Rule 1 — Short Title & Commencement
📐 Rule 1 — IGST Rules, 2017
Short Title & Commencement | §22 IGST Act r/w §20
Rule 1(1)
Called: "The Integrated Goods and Services Tax Rules, 2017"
Rule 1(2)
Deemed to have come into force on
22nd June, 2017
📝 The rules were given retrospective effect — they operate as if they existed since 22.06.2017, even if notified later.
🔍 GP Singh: Deeming provisions must be construed strictly. "Deemed to have come into force" creates a legal fiction — courts cannot look beyond the deemed date for any compliance obligation under these Rules.
📌 Enabling Power: §22 IGST Act, 2017 (13 of 2017) read with §20 IGST Act
Rule 2 — Application of Central Goods and Services Tax Rules
⚙️ Rule 2 — Application of CGST Rules to IGST
§20 IGST Act | Mutatis Mutandis Application
📝 "Mutatis mutandis" means the CGST Rules apply to IGST with necessary modifications — wherever CGST Rules say "central tax," read it as "integrated tax." You don't need a separate set of procedural rules for IGST — CGST Rules serve the purpose.
TRIGGER — Provisions specified in §20 of IGST Act are governed by CGST Rules 2017
APPLICATION — CGST Rules 2017 apply so far as may be in relation to integrated tax as they apply in relation to central tax
⚔️ WORD WAR — "so far as may be" vs "as if"
✅ USED: "so far as may be"
Allows modification/adaptation — legislature anticipated that CGST Rules may not fit IGST perfectly. Gives flexibility in application.
❌ NOT USED: "as if"
"As if" creates a strict legal fiction — leaves no room for adaptation or modification. Would make every CGST Rule mandatory for IGST without exception.
⚖️ Litigation Point: "So far as may be" = Rules apply with modifications needed for IGST context. If a CGST Rule is procedurally incompatible with IGST, it need not be applied. Assessee can argue inapplicability of a harsh CGST Rule to IGST proceedings on this ground.
📌 §20 IGST Act specifies which CGST provisions apply to IGST | CGST Rules 2017 — all chapters as applicable
🟣 Rule 3 — Advertisement Services to Govt. | §12(14) IGST Act [AMDT: Notfn. 12/2017-IT & 04/2018-IT]
🟣 AMDT — INSERTED
Entire Rule 3 inserted vide Notification No. 12/2017-IT dated 15.11.2017 w.e.f. 01.07.2017
Rule 3(h) — internet clause further amended vide Notification No. 04/2018-IT dated 31.12.2018 w.e.f. 01.01.2019
📺 Rule 3 — State-wise Value Apportionment
Advertisement Services to Central/State Govt / Statutory Body / Local Authority
§12(14) IGST Act | Absence of Contract specifying State-wise value
📝 When a government ad agency runs campaigns across multiple states via one consolidated order, IGST cannot be charged as one amount to one state. Rule 3 gives 9 media-specific formulas to split the value — so each state pays its share of IGST. Without this, the place of supply for multi-state ad services to Govt would be unresolvable.
Clause Media Type Apportionment Formula Reference Source Key Illustration
(a) Newspapers & Publications Amount payable for editions published in each State/UT = value for that State Release order / edition-wise amounts Separate invoices per edition (Delhi, Pune, Mumbai, Lucknow, Jaipur editions of one newspaper)
(b) Printed Material
(pamphlets, leaflets, diaries, T-shirts)		 Amount payable for distribution in each State = that State's value. Breakup ratio = distribution ratio Ministry breakup provided at print order stage 1L pamphlets: 20K (Haryana) : 50K (UP) : 30K (Rajasthan) → ratio 2:5:3 → ₹20K / ₹50K / ₹30K
(c)(i) Hoardings (Non-Train) Amount payable for hoardings in each State = that State's value Release order with city/location-wise breakup Airport hoardings in Delhi, Mumbai, Chennai, Kolkata — amount per city = value for that State/UT
(c)(ii) Advertisements on Trains Length of railway track in each State for that train / Total track length → ratio applied to amount payable www.indianrail.gov.in (track length data) Hazrat Nizamuddin–Vasco Goa Express: ratio 0.5:0.5:2:2:3:3:1 for 7 states → ₹1.2L split accordingly
(d)(i) Utility Bills (Oil & Gas) Amount payable for bills of consumers in each State/UT = that State's value Consumer address database of oil/gas company —
(d)(ii) Railway Tickets Ratio of number of railway stations in each State → applied to total amount payable Railway website www.indianrail.gov.in MP : Chhattisgarh stations = 713:251; ₹9,640 → ₹7,130 (MP) : ₹2,510 (CG)
(e) Radio Stations (FM) Amount payable to each radio station located in / named after a State = that State's value Release order breakup per station OP Kolkata, OP Bhubaneswar, OP Patna, OP Ranchi, OP Delhi — Delhi station = Delhi value (even if studio elsewhere)
(f) Television Channels Broadcast Audience Research Council (BARC) viewership figures for each State → ratio applied. Population ratio used when BARC clubs States together BARC; last week of prior quarter; Census for population ratio Delhi:UP:Uttarakhand:Bihar:Jharkhand viewership ratio = 5:9:1:4:1 → ₹20L split = 5L:9L:1L:4L:1L
(h) 🟣 Internet Advertisements
AMDT 04/2018-IT		 Service deemed provided all over India. TRAI internet subscriber figures for each State → ratio applied. Population ratio used when TRAI clubs States. www.trai.gov.in; Last quarter of prior financial year; Census for population ratio 🟣 AMDT: "Service deemed provided all over India" — inserted w.e.f. 01.01.2019. Before this amendment, internet ads did not have this deemed pan-India provision.
(i) SMS / Short Messaging Service TRAI telecom subscriber figures by telecom circle → population ratio applied when circle spans multiple States www.trai.gov.in; prior quarter figures Maharashtra telecom circle includes Maharashtra + Goa → separate subscriber calculation using population ratio (Census)
🟣 KEY RULE ARCHITECTURE
Trigger: Service to Central Govt / State Govt / Statutory Body / Local Authority under §12(14) IGST Act in absence of contract specifying state-wise value.
Consequence: Supplier must issue separate invoices per State / UT based on the applicable formula above.
💡 If ABC gives a consolidated ₹50L order to a TV channel for a pan-India campaign, the TV channel cannot issue one invoice for ₹50L to one state. It must split using BARC viewership data and issue state-wise invoices. Failure = wrong place of supply = CGST/SGST charged instead of IGST or vice versa.
📌 §12(14) IGST Act — Place of supply for advertisement services to Govt. | BARC: Broadcast Audience Research Council | TRAI: Telecom Regulatory Authority of India
🟣 Rule 4 — Immovable Property Services Spanning Multiple States | §12(3) [AMDT: Notfn. 04/2018-IT]
🟣 AMDT — INSERTED
Inserted vide Notification No. 04/2018-IT dated 31.12.2018 w.e.f. 01.01.2019
🏗️ Rule 4 — Value Apportionment: Immovable Property in Multiple States
§12(3) IGST Act | Absence of Contract for State-wise value
📝 When a hotel, architect, or contractor provides services for a property that straddles two states — or a hotel chain charges one consolidated amount for stays in multiple states — Rule 4 tells you how to split the value between those states.
📋 Services Covered under Rule 4
(a) Services directly in relation to immovable property
(architects, interior decorators, surveyors, engineers, estate agents, grant of rights to use, construction)
(b) Lodging accommodation (hotel, inn, guest house, homestay, club, campsite, houseboat)
(c) Accommodation for marriage / reception / social / cultural / religious / business function & related services
(d) Services ancillary to (a), (b), (c)
⚖️ Three Apportionment Methods
Method (i)
Hotels / Inns / Guest Houses / Clubs / Campsites
(EXCEPT single property in 2+ contiguous States)

Formula: Number of Nights stayed in property in each State
📝 If you stay 2 nights in Delhi hotel + 1 night in Agra hotel (same chain), value splits 2:1 between Delhi and UP.
💡 Hotel chain X charges ₹30,000 consolidated. Delhi: 2 nights, Agra: 1 night → Delhi value ₹20,000; UP value ₹10,000. Separate invoices issued.
Method (ii)
All other immovable property services + Single property in 2+ contiguous States

Formula: Area of immovable property in each State / UT
📝 A 20,000 sq.ft. plot straddling two states: 12,000 sq.ft in S1, 8,000 sq.ft in S2 → site preparation value splits 12:8 (or 3:2).
💡 Architect charges ₹5L for design of a property in S1 & S2 (area ratio 3:2) → ₹3L attributed to S1, ₹2L to S2. Separate invoices.
Method (iii)
Houseboat or other vessel

Formula: Time spent by boat/vessel in each State (as declared by service provider)
📝 A houseboat that spends 22 hours in Kerala and 2 hours in Karnataka → 24-hour stay value splits 22:2 (11:1). Service provider's declaration controls.
💡 Company C's houseboat: 22 hours Kerala + 2 hours Karnataka. Ratio 11:1 governs value split. Guest issues: what if declaration is wrong? Penal consequences for supplier.
📌 §12(3) IGST Act — Place of supply of services in relation to immovable property | Rule 4 also applied mutatis mutandis for cross-border scenarios under Rule 8
🟣 Rule 5 — Event Organisation Services Spanning Multiple States | §12(7) [AMDT: Notfn. 04/2018-IT]
🟣 AMDT — INSERTED
Inserted vide Notification No. 04/2018-IT dated 31.12.2018 w.e.f. 01.01.2019
🎭 Rule 5 — Value Apportionment: Event Services — Multi-State
§12(7) IGST Act | Unregistered Recipient | Consolidated Charge
Conditions — ALL must be satisfied
COND 1
Services are: (a) Cultural / artistic / sporting / scientific / educational / entertainment events OR (b) Conferences, fairs, exhibitions, celebrations OR (c) Ancillary services / sponsorship assignment
COND 2
Recipient = person other than registered person
(Unregistered recipient)
COND 3
Event held in India in more than one State/UT
COND 4
Consolidated amount charged for all such services
(No contract specifying state-wise value)
FORMULA
Apportionment by application of Generally Accepted Accounting Principles (GAAP)
📝 GAAP means standard accounting methods — usually based on cost incurred, revenue recognised, or event-based metrics per state. The law does not prescribe a specific formula — it leaves it to GAAP, which gives flexibility but also creates disputes with department.
🔍 GP Singh: "Generally accepted" = a standard that is widely followed and recognised. A taxpayer following one GAAP-compliant method cannot be asked to follow another merely because the department prefers it, unless the chosen method is patently unreasonable.
💡 Event company E: 3 events in S1 + 2 events in S2 for unregistered recipient R. Total charge ₹10L. GAAP ratio: 3:2. S1 value = ₹6L, S2 value = ₹4L. Separate invoices for each state.
📌 §12(7) IGST Act | Rule 5 applied mutatis mutandis for cross-border events under Rule 9
🟣 Rule 6 — Leased Circuit Services Spanning Multiple States | §12(12) [AMDT: Notfn. 04/2018-IT]
🟣 AMDT — INSERTED
Inserted vide Notification No. 04/2018-IT dated 31.12.2018 w.e.f. 01.01.2019
🔌 Rule 6 — Value Apportionment: Leased Circuit Services
§12(12) IGST Act | Point-Count Method
📝 A leased circuit is a dedicated private telecom link between offices. When this circuit passes through multiple states, Rule 6 uses a simple point-count: each endpoint and each intermediate point = 1 point. States get value in proportion to points within them.
📍 How to Count Points in a Circuit
Starting Point
Always = 1 point
End Point
Always = 1 point
Intermediate Point
= 1 point ONLY IF benefit of leased circuit is also available at that intermediate point
(not just a transit node)
📝 A signal relay station that just passes the signal through is NOT a point. Only locations where someone actually uses the circuit count.
FORMULA
Value for each State = (Points in that State / Total Points) × Total consolidated charge
ScenarioCircuit RoutePoints per StateValue Ratio
Illus. 1Delhi → Mumbai (2 points)Delhi (UT): 1, Maharashtra: 11:1 (Delhi:Maharashtra)
Illus. 2Chennai → Bengaluru → Mysuru (Bengaluru is intermediate point beneficiary)Tamil Nadu: 1, Karnataka: 21:2 (TN:Karnataka)
Illus. 3Kolkata → Patna → Guwahati (all 3 are full access points)West Bengal: 1, Bihar: 1, Assam: 11:1:1
📌 §12(12) IGST Act — Place of supply for leased circuit / telecommunication services
🟣 Rule 7 — Cross-Border: Goods-Related / Physical Presence Services | §13 [AMDT: Notfn. 04/2018-IT]
🟣 AMDT — INSERTED
Inserted vide Notification No. 04/2018-IT dated 31.12.2018 w.e.f. 01.01.2019
🌐 Rule 7 — Cross-Border: Apportionment
Services requiring Physical Goods or Physical Presence | §13 IGST Act
📝 When supplier OR recipient is outside India and the service involves physically working on goods (like testing a machine) or a person's physical presence (like a makeup artist), and this work happens in multiple Indian states — Rule 7 splits the value.
Three Apportionment Methods under Rule 7
Method (i)
Services on SAME goods performed in multiple States

Formula: Equal division of value across all States
📝 Same type of good being serviced in two states → split value 50:50 (or equally across however many states).
💡 Company C (Kolkata) tests a dredging machine — testing in Odisha AND Andhra Pradesh. Value split equally between Odisha and AP.
Method (ii)
Services on DIFFERENT goods in different States

Formula: Invoice value ratio of goods in each State
📝 Two different cars being serviced — use their respective invoice values to split the service fee between states.
💡 Company C (Delhi): services car J in Delhi + car A in Gurugram. Value of J service = Delhi portion; value of A service = Haryana portion. Ratio = J's invoice: A's invoice.
Method (iii)
Services to individuals (physical presence services) in multiple States

Formula: Generally Accepted Accounting Principles (GAAP)
📝 A makeup artist or coach who works in multiple states — use GAAP to split the consolidated fee. Usually time-based or session-based.
💡 Makeup artist M provides services to actor A — some scenes in Mumbai, some in Goa. Value apportioned to Maharashtra and Goa by GAAP (e.g., days of shooting).
📌 §13 IGST Act — Place of supply where supplier or recipient is outside India
🟣 Rules 8 & 9 — Cross-Border Referential Rules | §13 IGST Act [AMDT: Notfn. 04/2018-IT]
🟣 AMDT — INSERTED
Both Rules inserted vide Notification No. 04/2018-IT dated 31.12.2018 w.e.f. 01.01.2019
🔗 Rules 8 & 9 — Cross-Border Referential Framework
§13 IGST Act | Mutatis Mutandis Application of Rules 4 & 5
📝 Rules 8 and 9 are elegantly simple. Instead of creating new formulas for cross-border scenarios, they simply say: apply the domestic rules (Rule 4 for immovable property, Rule 5 for events) with necessary modifications. "Mutatis mutandis" is the legal shorthand for this.
🟣 Rule 8
Cross-Border Immovable Property Services (§13)

Where supplier or recipient is OUTSIDE India
and service relates to immovable property located in more than one State/UT

Method: Apply Rule 4 mutatis mutandis
📝 Foreign architect designing a hotel property straddling two Indian states → same area-based formula as Rule 4 applies.
💡 UK-based interior design firm redesigns a mall spread across Telangana and AP (area 40:60). Indian IGST value splits 40:60. Apply Rule 4(ii).
🟣 Rule 9
Cross-Border Event / Admission Services (§13)

Where supplier or recipient is OUTSIDE India
and service relates to cultural / artistic / sporting / scientific / educational / entertainment event in more than one State/UT

Method: Apply Rule 5 mutatis mutandis
📝 Foreign event management company organising a pan-India music festival across 3 states → same GAAP formula as Rule 5 applies.
💡 Singapore-based company organises medical conference across Delhi and Mumbai. GAAP ratio (e.g., 2:3 events) governs value split. Rule 9 → Rule 5 → GAAP method.
⚔️ WORD WAR — "mutatis mutandis" in Rules 8 & 9
✅ WHAT IT MEANS
"With necessary modifications" — Rules 4 and 5 apply with adaptations required for cross-border context (e.g., foreign currency amounts, foreign principal/recipient identity).
❌ WHAT IT DOES NOT MEAN
It does not mean rules apply identically without modification. A cross-border supplier cannot be forced to comply with domestic-only procedural steps that are physically impossible for them.
⚖️ Litigation Point: If Department applies Rules 8/9 rigidly identical to Rules 4/5 without recognising cross-border modifications needed, assessee can argue "mutatis mutandis" permits adaptation — GST cannot be demanded on a formula that is procedurally impossible to follow for a foreign entity.
📌 §13 IGST Act — Place of supply where location of supplier or recipient is outside India | Rules 8 & 9 are referential rules applying Rules 4 & 5 to cross-border scenarios
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal — IGST Rules 1–9
Statutory arguments for use in personal hearings before GST authorities, Appellate authorities & GSTAT. State them confidently and cite the Rule / Notification.
Rule 2 IGST Rules — "So far as may be" Flexibility
IGST Rules, 2017 | Original enactment | Read with §20 IGST Act
Key Statutory Principle
CGST Rules 2017 apply to integrated tax "so far as may be" in relation to integrated tax as they apply in relation to central tax. The phrase "so far as may be" is not the same as "as if" — it allows adaptation and modification. A CGST procedural rule that is incompatible with the nature of IGST transactions need not be applied blindly to IGST proceedings.
When to Use — When Department mechanically applies a CGST Rule procedure to your IGST matter and demands compliance with a step that makes no sense for an integrated tax transaction. Cite Rule 2 to argue that CGST Rules apply only "so far as may be" — not as absolute mandates.
Rule 3 IGST Rules — Mandatory State-wise Invoicing for Govt. Ad Services
IGST Rules, 2017 | Notfn. 12/2017-IT dt. 15.11.2017 | §12(14) IGST Act
Key Statutory Principle
Rule 3 mandates that for advertisement services to Central / State Government / Statutory Body / Local Authority where the service is disseminated across multiple States without a contract specifying state-wise value, the supplier must compute the value attributable to each State using the prescribed media-specific formula and issue separate invoices per State / UT. A single consolidated invoice for a multi-state government advertisement campaign is not in compliance with Rule 3.
When to Use — When Department issues demand for wrong state's IGST/CGST+SGST because supplier issued one invoice for a multi-state Govt. campaign, or when assessee claims ITC on invoice not split state-wise as required. Cite Rule 3 to establish the correct place of supply and value per state.
Rule 3(h) IGST Rules — Internet Advertisement Deemed Pan-India
IGST Rules, 2017 | Notfn. 04/2018-IT dt. 31.12.2018 w.e.f. 01.01.2019
Key Statutory Principle (Amendment)
With effect from 01.01.2019, internet advertisement services to the Government are deemed to have been provided all over India. The value attributable to each State is calculated on the basis of internet subscribers in each State as per TRAI data (last quarter of prior financial year). This deeming provision applies specifically to internet advertisements — prior to 01.01.2019, this deeming did not exist.
When to Use — When period of dispute involves internet advertisement to Govt. and there is a question of place of supply or value attribution. Pre-01.01.2019: argue Rule 3(h) amendment not applicable; no deemed pan-India provision existed. Post-01.01.2019: apply TRAI subscriber-based apportionment.
Rule 4 IGST Rules — Area-Based Apportionment for Cross-State Immovable Property
IGST Rules, 2017 | Notfn. 04/2018-IT dt. 31.12.2018 w.e.f. 01.01.2019 | §12(3) IGST Act
Key Statutory Principle
When services in relation to immovable property (including by architects, engineers, interior decorators, hotels, and those providing right to use property) are supplied in respect of property located in more than one State or Union territory, and no contract exists for separately determining state-wise value, the value shall be apportioned on the basis of: (i) number of nights stayed (for hotels — except single property in contiguous states), (ii) area of property in each state (all other cases), or (iii) time spent in each state (for houseboats). Supplier is obligated to issue separate invoices per state.
When to Use — When a hotel chain or architect/contractor is assessed for charging wrong head of tax (CGST+SGST vs IGST) on a multi-state property service. Cite Rule 4 to establish the correct formula and argue that the tax demand is incorrectly computed because state-wise apportionment was not followed.
Rule 6 IGST Rules — Point-Count Method for Leased Circuits
IGST Rules, 2017 | Notfn. 04/2018-IT dt. 31.12.2018 w.e.f. 01.01.2019 | §12(12) IGST Act
Key Statutory Principle
For leased circuit services installed across more than one State or Union territory with a consolidated charge, the value attributable to each State is determined by the number of "points" in that State — a starting point, an end point, and intermediate points (only where the benefit of the leased circuit is actually available, not mere relay nodes). An intermediate transit node that does not provide usable circuit access is NOT a point for this purpose.
When to Use — When a telecom or IT company providing leased circuits is assessed for wrong tax or wrong apportionment. Cite Rule 6 point-count methodology. Key argument: only active access points count — not passive relay points. Use this to reduce the number of points counted in a state with a higher tax rate.
Rules 8 & 9 — Mutatis Mutandis = Adaptation, Not Identical Application
IGST Rules, 2017 | Notfn. 04/2018-IT dt. 31.12.2018 w.e.f. 01.01.2019 | §13 IGST Act
Key Statutory Principle
Rules 8 and 9 direct that Rules 4 and 5 shall apply "mutatis mutandis" to cross-border immovable property and event services respectively. "Mutatis mutandis" means with necessary modifications — it does not mean identical application. Where a domestic compliance step under Rule 4 or Rule 5 is procedurally impossible for a foreign supplier or recipient (e.g., Indian registration, Indian bank account), the "mutatis mutandis" instruction must be read to allow adaptation to the cross-border reality.
When to Use — When Department demands that a foreign service provider comply with every step of Rule 4/5 in a cross-border scenario with no modification. Cite Rules 8/9 and argue "mutatis mutandis" requires adaptation — an impossible compliance cannot be demanded. Useful in cases involving foreign architects, foreign event companies operating in India.
Rule 1(2) — Deemed Commencement: Retrospective Effect
IGST Rules, 2017 | Original enactment | w.e.f. 22.06.2017
Key Principle
The IGST Rules, 2017 are deemed to have come into force on 22nd June, 2017 — not the date of notification. This creates a legal fiction of retrospective operation. Under settled principles of statutory interpretation, a deeming provision must be strictly construed — the legal fiction created by "deemed to have come into force" is limited to what the provision requires and cannot be extended by analogy or implication.
When to Use — When Department demands compliance with IGST Rules for a period between 01.07.2017 and the date of notification of those Rules. Counter-argument: Rules are deemed in force from 22.06.2017 — compliance obligation existed from that date. Alternatively, where assessee argues Rules were not in force for a pre-notification period, Department can cite deemed commencement.
For professional litigation use | Pawan Soni & Associates, Ichalkaranji | IGST Rules 2017 — Rules 1 to 9 | Source: ICAI Bare Law Nov 2025 Edition
