⚔️ Hearing Arsenal
📘 PART A — THE UNION TERRITORY GOODS AND SERVICES TAX ACT, 2017 (No. 14 of 2017)
§1–2 — Short Title, Extent & Definitions | UTGST Act 2017
🏛️ UTGST Act, 2017 — No. 14 of 2017
Levy of tax on intra-UT supplies | Enacted 12th April 2017
📝 UTGST applies to Union Territories WITHOUT a legislature — like Andaman, Lakshadweep, Chandigarh, Ladakh, Dadra & Nagar Haveli. States with their own legislature use SGST. Think of UTGST as the "State GST" for Union Territories.
§1(2) — EXTENT
Extends to UTs of Andaman & Nicobar Islands, Lakshadweep, Dadra & Nagar Haveli and Daman & Diu, Ladakh, Chandigarh and other territory
🔶 AMDT — Finance Act 2020 (w.e.f. 26.01.2020)
"Dadra and Nagar Haveli and Daman and Diu" substituted in place of two separate UTs — merger of the two UTs into one. Prior: "Dadra and Nagar Haveli, Daman and Diu" (two separate entries).
§2 — Key Definitions (10 terms)
ClauseTermDefinitionPlain EnglishLitigation Point
§2(1)Appointed DayDate on which UTGST provisions come into forceThe start date of UTGST — 22 June 2017 for §§1,2,3,4,5,17,21,22; 1 July 2017 for restRelevant for transitional ITC claims; base year calculations
§2(2)CommissionerCommissioner of Union territory tax appointed under §3The top UTGST officer — equivalent to State's Principal Commissioner in SGSTAll delegation, powers flow from Commissioner
§2(3)Designated AuthorityAuthority notified by the CommissionerA specific officer notified for a specific purpose — e.g., for processing advance rulingLook for notification before assuming authority
§2(4)Exempt SupplySupply attracting nil tax OR exempt under §8 UTGST OR §6 IGST — includes non-taxable supplyThree categories: nil-rated, exempted by notification, or non-taxable. All three different.Crucial for ITC reversal u/s 17(2) CGST — definition must be read carefully
§2(5)Existing LawAny law relating to levy on goods/services passed before UTGST commencementPre-GST laws — VAT, CST, Entry Tax, Service Tax etc.Governs transitional provisions §§17–20
§2(6)GovernmentAdministrator or any Authority or officer authorised by Administrator by Central GovtIn UTs, there is no elected government — the Administrator (Lt. Governor or similar) is the GovernmentAll notifications, exemptions issued by "Government" = Administrator
§2(7)Output TaxUTGST charged on taxable supply by registered person or his agent — EXCLUDES RCM liabilityTax you collect from your buyer. Does NOT include tax you pay on RCM purchases.ITC of UTGST cannot be used to pay CGST (§9(c)) — output tax definition confirms this
§2(8)Union TerritoryTerritory of (i) A&N Islands (ii) Lakshadweep (iii) Dadra & NH & D&D (iv) Ladakh (v) Chandigarh (vi) other territory — each treated SEPARATELYEach UT is a separate jurisdiction for UTGST. Supplier in Chandigarh and buyer in Ladakh = inter-UT = IGST applies.Inter-UT supply → IGST, not UTGST
§2(9)Union Territory TaxTax levied under UTGST ActThe actual UTGST cess/tax — equivalent to CGST component in total GSTRate cannot exceed 20% (§7(1))
§2(10)Words & ExpressionsUndefined terms in UTGST take meaning from CGST/IGST/SGST/Compensation Cess ActsUTGST borrows definitions — "taxable supply", "registered person" etc. all come from CGST ActAlways read CGST definitions alongside UTGST
📋 Corresponding Provisions: CGST Act §2 (Definitions) | IGST Act §2 | Enforced via Notification No. 1/2017-UT Tax dated 21.06.2017 and No. 3/2017-UT Tax dated 28.06.2017
§3–6 — Administration | Officers, Powers & Authorisation
🏢 Chapter II — ADMINISTRATION | §§3–6
§3 — OFFICERS
Administrator appoints Commissioners & other officers by notification — deemed proper officers for specified purposes

Existing law officers = deemed appointed under UTGST
🔍 GP Singh: "Deemed" = legal fiction. Existing law officers are treated AS IF newly appointed — they get all UTGST powers automatically without fresh notification.
§4 — AUTHORISATION
Administrator may by ORDER authorise any officer to appoint UT tax officers BELOW rank of Asst. Commissioner for administration
📝 The Administrator can delegate his appointment power downwards — but only for officers below Asst. Commissioner. The Asst. Commissioner and above must be appointed by Administrator directly.
§5 — POWERS
(1) Officers exercise powers subject to Commissioner's conditions
(2) Senior officers may exercise powers of subordinates
(3) Commissioner may delegate powers downward
(4) Appellate Authority CANNOT exercise powers of other UT officers
🔍 GP Singh: §5(4) — "shall not" = mandatory prohibition. Appellate Authority is a separate stream — it cannot become a first-instance adjudicating authority under any circumstance.
§6 — CENTRAL TAX OFFICERS AS PROPER OFFICERS
CGST officers authorised as proper officers for UTGST — on Council's recommendations

§6(2)(b): If CGST officer initiates proceedings → UTGST officer CANNOT initiate proceedings on SAME subject matter

§6(3): Appeals/Rectification of UTGST orders → NOT before CGST officers
📝 Since UTs don't have separate full state tax machinery, CGST officers run UTGST also. But there's a strict bar: one set of proceedings per subject — no double jeopardy between CGST and UTGST wings.
💡 If CGST officer issues SCN for FY 2021-22 on ITC mismatch → UTGST officer CANNOT issue another SCN on the same ITC mismatch for the same period.
📋 Corresponding: CGST §§3–6 (parallel structure) | §6(2)(b) → No parallel proceedings rule
§7 — Levy and Collection of UTGST | Chapter III
💰 Section 7 — Levy and Collection of Union Territory Tax
§7(1) — CORE LEVY
UTGST levied on ALL intra-State supplies of goods or services or both
Rate: Not exceeding 20% (as notified by Central Govt on GST Council recommendation)
Paid by: Taxable person
🔶 AMDT 2024 — Finance (No.2) Act 2024 | Notif. 17/2024-CT dt. 27.09.2024 | w.e.f. 01.11.2024
EXCEPTIONS added to §7(1): UDGST NOT levied on:
(a) Alcoholic liquor for human consumption [existing]
(b) Un-denatured extra neutral alcohol (ENA) or rectified spirit — used for manufacture of alcoholic liquor for human consumption [NEW — AMDT 2024]
Reason: ENA/RS used in alcohol manufacture now excluded from GST levy — parity with alcoholic liquor exclusion.
§7(2) — PETROLEUM PRODUCTS
UTGST on petroleum crude, HSD, petrol, natural gas, ATF → levied from date to be notified by Central Govt on GST Council recommendation
[Not yet levied as on Nov 2025]
📝 These 5 products are "parked" outside GST for now — they still attract State VAT and Central Excise. When the Council decides, UTGST will also apply on them.
§7(3) — RCM: RECIPIENT PAYS
Central Govt may notify categories of supply where TAX paid by RECIPIENT of goods/services on reverse charge basis — all Act provisions apply to such recipient AS IF he is the supplier
🔍 GP Singh: "As if" = deeming fiction. The recipient becomes the deemed supplier for all compliance purposes — registration, payment, returns, ITC claims flow accordingly.
§7(4) — RCM FROM UNREGISTERED SUPPLIER
[Substituted vide UTGST Amendment Act 2018, w.e.f. 01.02.2019]
Govt may notify REGISTERED persons who must pay RCM when receiving specified supplies from UNREGISTERED supplier
🔶 AMDT 2018→2019
Prior: Blanket RCM on all purchases from unregistered suppliers.
Now: Only notified categories — significant narrowing of RCM scope.
§7(5) — E-COMMERCE RCM
Govt may notify services where ECO (e-commerce operator) pays tax as if it is the SUPPLIER — even if ECO has no physical presence in taxable territory → representative or appointed person liable
📝 Like Swiggy/Zomato paying GST on restaurant services, or Ola/Uber on cab rides. ECO collects from customer and deposits tax.
💡 Foreign ECO with no India presence → must appoint India representative who is liable to pay UTGST on all supplies through the ECO platform.
📋 Parallel: CGST §9 | IGST §5 | RCM Notifications: Notif. 13/2017-CT(R), 10/2017-IGST(R)
§8 — Power to Grant Exemption | §8A — General Practice (NEW 2024)
🛡️ §8 — Power to Grant Exemption from UTGST
§8(1) — GENERAL NOTIFICATION
Central Govt on Council recommendation → by NOTIFICATION → exempt goods/services absolutely or subject to conditions from whole/part of UTGST
"Public interest" is the trigger
🔍 GP Singh: "Public interest" — not defined. Courts have held it is a wide phrase giving wide discretion to Government. But arbitrary use without Council recommendation is challenged.
§8(2) — SPECIAL ORDER
Central Govt on Council recommendation → by SPECIAL ORDER in each case → exempt any goods/services in exceptional circumstances (reasons stated in order)
📝 Individual exemptions for exceptional cases — like exempting supplies to flood-hit UT. Requires specific order with reasons, not just a general notification.
§8(3) — EXPLANATION IN NOTIFICATION
Within 1 YEAR of §8(1) notification or §8(2) order → Govt may insert EXPLANATION to clarify scope → deemed always to have been part of original notification
📝 Retrospective clarification power. Government can explain what an exemption means within 1 year — and the explanation is treated as always being there.
§8(4) — CGST EXEMPTION = UTGST EXEMPTION
Any CGST exemption notification/order u/s 11 CGST → deemed to be notification/order under UTGST Act
Auto-mirror provision
📝 If CGST exempts something (like health services), UTGST automatically exempts it too — no separate UT notification needed. Perfect symmetry.
⚠️ EXPLANATION TO §8
Where exemption granted absolutely → registered supplier SHALL NOT collect tax in excess of effective rate on such supply
🔶 NEW SECTION 8A — INSERTED vide Finance (No.2) Act 2024 | w.e.f. 01.11.2024
🔶 §8A — Power NOT to Recover UTGST not levied / short-levied — as result of GENERAL PRACTICE

If Government satisfied that:
(a) A practice was/is generally prevalent regarding levy of UTGST (including non-levy) on any supply, AND
(b) Such supplies were/are liable to UTGST (not being levied) OR liable to HIGHER UTGST (being short-levied)

→ Government may, on Council recommendation, by notification DIRECT that:
The whole UTGST payable OR excess UTGST — but for said practice — SHALL NOT be required to be paid
📝 This is a "General Practice Protection" provision. If an entire industry was following a wrong tax practice (e.g., not charging GST on a particular activity), the Government can officially waive the liability for past period — no recovery from anyone who followed that practice.
💡 Example: If industry-wide practice was not to charge UTGST on certain services like "facilitation services for renting" — and Revenue now wants to demand UTGST for past 3 years — §8A allows Govt to notify that past liability is waived because of general practice.
🔍 GP Singh: "Satisfied" = subjective satisfaction of Government. Not open to judicial challenge on merits — only if mala fide or irrelevant consideration. The "recommendation of the Council" is a mandatory pre-condition — without it, the notification is ultra vires.
📋 Parallel: CGST §11 (Exemption) | CGST §11A [analogous to §8A] | Exemption Notifications: 12/2017-CT(R), 9/2017-IGST(R)
§9 — ITC Utilisation Order | §9A & §9B — New Provisions (AMDT 2019)
🔄 §9 — Payment of Tax — ITC Utilisation Order (UTGST)
§9(a) — IGST ITC
IGST ITC used first against IGST
↓ Remaining → CGST → SGST/UTGST (in that order)
§9(b) — UTGST ITC
UTGST ITC used first against UTGST
↓ Remaining → IGST

Proviso [AMDT 2019]: UTGST ITC → IGST only when CGST ITC balance NOT AVAILABLE for IGST payment
🔶 AMDT 2019
Priority clarified — CGST ITC must first be exhausted before UTGST ITC is used for IGST payment.
§9(c) — ABSOLUTE BAR
UTGST ITC SHALL NOT be used towards payment of CGST
📝 Cross-utilisation banned: UTGST ITC cannot pay CGST. CGST ITC cannot pay UTGST. They are two separate streams — no cross-contamination.
🔍 GP Singh: "Shall not" = mandatory prohibition. Any attempted use of UTGST ITC for CGST payment is void ab initio. Return filing system should reject it technically.
🔶 §9A & §9B — INSERTED vide UTGST Amendment Act 2018 | Notif. 1/2019-UTT dated 29.01.2019 | w.e.f. 01.02.2019

§9A — Notwithstanding §9: UTGST ITC shall be utilised towards IGST or UTGST payment only after IGST ITC has first been fully utilised

§9B — Notwithstanding this Chapter (subject to §9(c)): Government may, on Council recommendation, PRESCRIBE the order and manner of utilisation of ITC (IGST + CGST + SGST/UTGST) towards payment of any such tax
📝 §9A adds a new priority rule: exhaust IGST ITC completely before touching UTGST ITC. §9B gives Government power to prescribe the overall order by Rules — creating flexibility for future changes without amending the Act.
💡 Chain: Taxpayer has IGST ITC ₹10L, UTGST ITC ₹5L, CGST ITC ₹3L. IGST liability ₹12L → Use all IGST ITC (₹10L) first → then CGST ITC (₹2L) → balance ₹nil IGST → only now can UTGST ITC be used for UTGST liability.
📋 Parallel: CGST §49, §49A, §49B | CGST Rule 88A — ITC Utilisation Order
§10 — Transfer of ITC | §11 — Officers to Assist
📤 §10 — Transfer of UTGST ITC to IGST Account + §11 — Assistance
§10 — ITC TRANSFER MECHANISM
When UTGST ITC used for paying IGST (u/s 5(5) CGST, via valid GSTR-3B) → Amount collected as UTGST reduced by that amount → Central Govt transfers equivalent amount from UT Tax Account to Integrated Tax Account (within prescribed time & manner)
📝 This is an accounting / inter-government fund transfer. When UT taxpayer uses UTGST credit to pay IGST, the back-end accounting moves money from UT's bucket to Centre's IGST bucket to maintain revenue accuracy.
§11 — OFFICERS TO ASSIST
(1) ALL these officers shall assist UTGST proper officers in implementation:
• Police Officers
• Railway Officers
• Customs Officers
• Revenue collection officers
• Village officers
• Central Tax Officers
• State Tax Officers

(2) Govt may by notification empower ANY OTHER class of officers to assist when called by Commissioner
📝 Multi-agency coordination. The UTGST officer investigating a case can call on police, customs, railways for help. This is important in search-and-seizure operations.
🔍 GP Singh: "Shall assist" = mandatory duty on those officers. If a police officer refuses to assist a GST officer during a search, it is breach of statutory duty under §11.
§12–13 — Tax Wrongfully Collected | Recovery | Chapter VI
⚠️ Chapter VI — Demands & Recovery | §§12–13
§12(1) — WRONG HEAD REFUND
Registered person paid CGST + UTGST treating supply as intra-State → Later held to be inter-State → REFUNDED the CGST + UTGST (wrong tax paid)
💡 Supplier in Chandigarh supplies to Chandigarh buyer — charges CGST+UTGST. Later found buyer is in Delhi — supply was inter-State. Supplier gets refund of CGST+UTGST and now pays IGST.
§12(2) — NO INTEREST
Person paid IGST treating supply as inter-State → Later held to be intra-State → NOT required to pay INTEREST on CGST + UTGST now payable
📝 No interest penalty for bona fide wrong classification where tax was paid (just in wrong head). Only the correct tax needs to be paid — no interest.
🔍 GP Singh: "Shall not be required" = mandatory benefit. Department cannot demand interest in this situation. It is not discretionary — it is a statutory right of the taxpayer.
§13 — RECOVERY BY CGST OFFICER
(1) Proper officer of CENTRAL TAX may recover UTGST arrears as if they were arrears of central tax — and credit recovered amount to UT Tax account

(2) Where recovered amount < total dues (CGST + UTGST) → credited PRO-RATA in proportion to respective dues
📝 Cross-recovery mechanism. A CGST officer can chase and recover pending UTGST as part of a single recovery drive — no need for separate UT recovery officer. Very practical for shared officer infrastructure.
📋 Parallel: CGST §77 (Wrong Head), CGST §79 (Recovery)
§14–16 — Advance Ruling Authority & Appellate Authority | Chapter VII
📜 Chapter VII — ADVANCE RULING | §§14–16
§14 — DEFINITIONS
"Advance Ruling" = decision by Authority / Appellate Authority on questions u/s 97(2) or 100(1) CGST Act
"Applicant" = any person registered or desirous of registration
"Application" = filed u/s 97 CGST Act
§15 — CONSTITUTION OF AAR
Central Govt by notification constitutes [UT Name] Authority for Advance Ruling
2 Members: (i) Officer of Central Tax + (ii) Officer of UT Tax

Proviso: Central Govt may notify AAR located in any State/UT to act as AAR for UTGST purposes
📝 Small UTs may share AAR with a nearby State. For example, Chandigarh AAR may be notified to also handle Ladakh cases rather than constituting a separate body.
§16 — CONSTITUTION OF AAAR
Appellate Authority for Advance Ruling:
Members: (i) Chief Commissioner of Central Tax (Board designated) + (ii) Commissioner of UT Tax having jurisdiction over applicant

Proviso: AAAR of another State/UT may be notified to act as UTGST AAAR
💡 If taxpayer in Lakshadweep gets adverse AAR ruling → appeal to AAAR → AAAR of Lakshadweep (or shared AAAR as notified) — headed by Chief Commissioner of Central Tax.
📋 Parallel: CGST §§95–106 (Advance Ruling Chapter) | Questions: §97(2) CGST | CGST Rule 104
§17–20 — Transitional Provisions | Chapter VIII
🔁 Chapter VIII — TRANSITIONAL PROVISIONS | §§17–20
📝 These sections deal with the switchover from pre-GST regime (VAT/Service Tax/Excise) to UTGST. Key issues: migrating existing ITC, handling goods sent for job work before GST, price revisions, and handling pre-GST assessment proceedings.
SectionSubjectKey ProvisionCondition / LimitLitigation Point
§17Migration of Existing TaxpayersVAT-registered persons with PAN → provisional UTGST registration certificate issued | Final certificate on prescribed conditionsIf registration cancelled later (no liability) → deemed never issuedProvisional registration carries full UTGST rights; failure to convert to final within time = lapse
§18(1)ITC Carryforward — Closing BalanceRegistered person (non-composition) may carry forward VAT + Entry Tax credit from last pre-GST return to e-credit ledger | Within 90 days of appointed dayNOT allowed if: credit not admissible under UTGST | Returns not filed for 6 months | Credit on exempted goods90-day deadline is strict. Late carry-forward claim rejected.
§18(2)ITC on Capital Goods (unavailed)Credit on capital goods not carried forward in return (but admissible under existing law) → may be taken in e-credit ledgerMust be admissible under BOTH existing law AND UTGST ActDouble-admissibility test — if blocked under CGST §17(5), cannot be availed under transition
§18(3)ITC on Inputs in Stock — Non-registered/Exempted SellersSellers previously not liable or dealing in exempted goods → credit on input stock on appointed dayConditions: Used for taxable supplies | Eligible under UTGST | Invoice available | Invoice within 12 months of appointed day12-month invoice age test — older invoices disqualified
§18(4)–(6)Various ITC scenariosMixed suppliers (taxable+exempt), fixed rate payers, goods received after appointed day — all have specific ITC transition rules§18(5): 30-day deadline (extendable by 30 days) for inputs received post-appointed day with pre-appointed day invoicesEach scenario has its own conditions — wrong sub-section claim = rejection
§19Job Work — Goods Sent Before Appointed DayInputs/semi-finished/goods sent for job work before appointed day → returned on/after appointed day → NO TAX if returned within 6 months6 months extendable by 2 months by Commissioner | Non-return → ITC recovery under §142(8)(a) CGSTGoods not returned within time → ITC recovery demanded. Time limit is critical.
§20(1)Goods Returned After Appointed DayTax paid goods sold ≤6 months before appointed day → returned by non-registered person → REFUND of pre-GST tax | If returned by registered person → deemed supply6-month window from appointed day | Goods must be identifiableRefund of old VAT possible if conditions met — but deemed supply trap for B2B returns
§20(2)–(12)Price Revisions, Pending Proceedings, Approval Basis GoodsUpward price revision → supplementary invoice within 30 days | Downward revision → credit note within 30 days | Pending appeals/assessments → disposed under existing law | Approved goods rejected within 6 months → no tax30-day window for supplementary invoice/credit note | 6 months for approval basis goods returnDebit note for upward revision = deemed outward supply under CGST Act | Credit note benefit only if recipient reduces ITC
📋 Parallel: CGST §§139–142 (Transitional) | CGST Rules 117–120C | Tran-1 and Tran-2 Forms
§21 — Application of CGST Provisions | Chapter IX (Miscellaneous)
⚙️ §21 — Mutatis Mutandis Application of CGST Act to UTGST
📝 Mutatis mutandis = "with necessary changes". The CGST Act's detailed compliance framework (returns, assessment, offences etc.) applies to UTGST — just replace "CGST" with "UTGST", "central tax" with "Union territory tax" etc. This avoids repeating 174 sections in the UTGST Act.
📖 Legal Dictionary: "Mutatis mutandis" — Latin phrase meaning "with the necessary changes having been made" or "with things changed that need to be changed." It is a term of convenience in legislative drafting. Courts have held (State of Punjab v Devans Modern) that it imports only such changes as are absolutely necessary — the borrowed provision is not rewritten wholesale.
27 Topics from CGST Act apply to UTGST via §21
#Topic Applied from CGST ActModification for UTGST
(i)–(ii)Scope of Supply | Composition LevyReferences to "central tax" → "Union territory tax"
(iii)–(iv)Composite & Mixed Supply | Time & Value of SupplyReferences to "Commissioner" → "Commissioner of UT Tax"
(v)–(vi)Input Tax Credit | RegistrationReferences to "officers of central tax" → "officers of UT tax"
(vii)–(ix)Tax Invoice, Credit/Debit Notes | Accounts & Records | ReturnsReferences to "SGST/UTGST Act" → "CGST Act"
(x)–(xii)Payment of Tax | TDS | TCSReferences to "State tax/UT tax" → "central tax"
(xiii)–(xv)Assessment | Refunds | AuditReferences to "Commr of State tax/UT tax" → "Commr of Central Tax"
(xvi)–(xviii)Inspection, Search, Seizure & Arrest | Demands & Recovery | Liability in Certain CasesKey enforcement provisions — all apply with UT tax reference changes
(xix)–(xxi)Advance Ruling | Appeals & Revision | Presumption as to DocumentsSame framework, UT tax context
(xxii)–(xxvii)Offences & Penalties | Job Work | E-Commerce | Settlement of Funds | Transitional | Miscellaneous (including imposition of fine & penalty)Full CGST machinery applies mutatis mutandis
📋 This section makes CGST Act §§1–174 operational for UTGST — CGST Act is the "parent" law; UTGST Act is the thin "mirror" statute.
§22–26 — Rules, Regulations, Laying, Directions & Removal of Difficulties
📜 §§22–26 — Miscellaneous Provisions | UTGST Act
§22 — RULE-MAKING POWER
Central Govt on Council recommendation → by notification → make rules for UTGST
Includes power to give RETROSPECTIVE EFFECT from date not earlier than commencement
Penalty for rule contravention: up to ₹10,000
🔍 GP Singh: Retrospective rules — valid if legislature authorised (as here). But rules cannot override the Act itself even retrospectively. Courts distinguish: procedural retrospectivity (valid) vs. substantive liability creation (needs careful scrutiny).
§23 — REGULATIONS BY BOARD
Board (CBIC) may make regulations consistent with UTGST Act and rules to carry out purposes of the Act
§24 — LAYING BEFORE PARLIAMENT
Every rule / regulation / notification → laid before EACH HOUSE of Parliament within session for 30 days
Both Houses may modify or annul → effective in modified/annulled form thereafter
But: earlier acts done under that rule remain valid (savings clause)
§25 — COMMISSIONER'S DIRECTIONS
Commissioner may issue orders, instructions or directions to UT Tax officers for UNIFORMITY in implementation
ALL officers and persons employed in implementation SHALL observe and follow
🔍 GP Singh: §25 directions are binding on officers — but they cannot override the Act or Rules. If a circular/direction contradicts the Act, the Act prevails (SC in CCE v Ratan Melting).
§26 — REMOVAL OF DIFFICULTIES
Central Govt on Council recommendation → general/special order in Official Gazette → may make provisions for removing difficulties

Time limit: 5 years from commencement
🔶 AMDT — Finance Act 2020
Period extended from "3 years" to "5 years" — giving Government more time to issue removal of difficulty orders.
📗 PART B — THE GST (COMPENSATION TO STATES) ACT, 2017 (No. 15 of 2017)
§1–2 — Short Title & Definitions | Compensation to States Act
💰 GST Compensation to States Act, 2017 — No. 15 of 2017
Compensating States for Revenue Loss arising from GST implementation
📝 States were afraid GST would reduce their VAT revenue. The Constitution guaranteed them compensation for 5 years (Art. 279A — see Part C). This Act implements that guarantee — funded by a special Cess on sin goods (tobacco, aerated drinks, luxury cars).
§2 — Key Definitions
TermDefinition (Simplified)Significance
Cess §2(c)GST Compensation Cess levied under §8 of this ActITC of cess can only be used against cess — not against CGST/SGST/IGST
Compensation §2(d)Amount in form of GST Compensation as determined under §7Formula-based; released every 2 months provisionally
Fund §2(f)GST Compensation Fund u/s 10 — part of Public Account of India, non-lapsableCess proceeds credited here; compensation paid from here
Input Tax §2(g)Cess on supplies made to taxable person + cess on imports including RCM cessInput cess ≠ input CGST. Separate credit stream.
Projected Growth Rate §2(k)14% per annum (see §3)The assumed growth rate for calculating how much revenue States "should have" earned
Transition Period §2(r)5 years from transition date (when State's SGST came into force)Compensation mechanism active only for 5 years — expired June 2022 for most states. Extended beyond by separate notifications/GST Council decisions.
State §2(m)For §§3-7: States as per CGST Act | For §§8-14: Includes Union Territories (UTGST Act states)UTs also get cess proceeds benefit for §§8-14 purposes
§3–6 — Projected Growth Rate, Base Year & Projected Revenue
📊 Compensation Calculation Framework — §§3–6
§3 — PROJECTED GROWTH RATE
14% per annum
Nominal growth rate assumed for subsumed revenues during transition period
📝 Government assumed States would have grown their VAT/CST revenues by 14% each year. If actual GST revenue is less, the gap is compensated.
§4 — BASE YEAR
Financial year ending 31st March 2016 = Base Year for all calculations
📝 FY 2015-16 is Year Zero. All projections start from this base. Revenue collected that year (net of refunds) becomes the starting point.
§5 — BASE YEAR REVENUE
Sum of: VAT + Sales Tax + Purchase Tax + Works Contract Tax + CST + Entry Tax/Octroi/LBT + Luxury Tax + Advertisement Tax + Excise on Medicinal/Toilet Preps (State share)

EXCLUDED from base year: Revenue on petroleum, HSD, petrol, gas, ATF, alcohol — also Entertainment Tax collected by local bodies
💡 Maharashtra's base year revenue = FY16 VAT + CST + LBT + Luxury Tax (all net of refunds, as certified by CAG). Petrol tax excluded.
§6 — PROJECTED REVENUE FORMULA
Projected Revenue for Year N = Base Year Revenue × (1 + 14/100)n

Where n = number of years from base year

Illustration: Base = ₹100; Projected for FY 2018-19 (n=3) = 100 × (1.14)³ = ₹148.15
📝 Simple compound growth formula at 14% pa. The higher this number grows, the more compensation a State is entitled to — if actual GST revenue falls short.
§7 — Calculation and Release of Compensation
💸 §7 — Compensation Payable = Projected Revenue − Actual Revenue
ACTUAL REVENUE (§7(3)(b)) = State SGST collected (net of refunds) + IGST apportioned to State + Taxes under subsumed Acts (net of refunds) as certified by CAG
BI-MONTHLY RELEASE (§7(4))
Provisional compensation calculated & released at end of every 2-month period
Pro-rata basis against projected annual revenue
Illustration: 10 months = 100 × (5/6) = ₹83.33
📝 Every 2 months States get a provisional cheque. Final settlement happens after CAG audits the year's actual revenue. If excess was released → adjusted in next year's payments.
ANNUAL FINAL CALCULATION (§7(2))
Final compensation calculated after receipt of CAG-audited revenue figures
Excess released → adjusted in next financial year payments
REFUND TO CENTRE (§7(6))
If no compensation due in a year AND excess was released in previous year → State must REFUND to Central Govt
Refund credited to Fund
📝 States can be asked to refund excess compensation. This became relevant when States' GST revenues exceeded projections in certain high-growth years.
§8 — Levy of Compensation Cess | §8A — General Practice Protection (NEW 2024)
🏷️ §8 — Levy and Collection of GST Compensation Cess
§8(1) — BASIS OF LEVY
Cess on intra-State supplies (as per CGST §9) AND inter-State supplies (as per IGST §5) of notified goods/services
Purpose: Providing compensation to States for GST transition revenue loss
Period: 5 years from GST implementation date (or such period as prescribed on Council recommendation)
Proviso: Composition taxpayers (§10 CGST) EXEMPT from cess
§8(2) — RATE & BASIS
Cess on goods/services specified in SCHEDULE — column (4) gives maximum rate (value / quantity / combination)
Value for cess = §15 CGST Act for all intra/inter-State supplies
Imports: Cess levied u/s 3 Customs Tariff Act at point of customs duty
Cess Schedule — Key Rates (as on Nov 2025)
S.No.GoodsTariffMax Cess RateNotes on Amendments
1Pan Masala2106 90 2051% of retail sale price per unit🔶 Substituted — Finance Act 2023 (w.e.f. 01.04.2023). Prior: 135% ad valorem. Now: RSP-based.
2Tobacco & manufactured tobacco substitutes including tobacco productsChapter 24₹4,170 per 1000 sticks OR 290% ad valorem OR combination (not exceeding ₹4,170 + 290%) OR 100% of RSP🔶 Substituted — Finance Act 2023 (w.e.f. 01.04.2023). Complex formula retained with RSP as cap.
3Coal, briquettes, solid fuels from coal/lignite2701, 2702, 2703₹400 per tonneUnchanged — specific rate
4Aerated waters2202 10 1015% ad valoremUnchanged
4AMotor vehicles for transport of not more than 13 persons including driver8702 10, 20, 30, 90🔶 25% ad valorem🔶 Inserted vide GSTCS Amendment Act 2017 (No.9 of 2017) w.e.f. 02.09.2017
5Motor cars & other vehicles (principally for person transport, including station wagons, racing cars) — less than 10 persons8703🔶 25% ad valorem🔶 Substituted — GSTCS Amendment Act 2017 (No.9 of 2017) w.e.f. 02.09.2017. Prior: 15% ad valorem.
6Any other supplies (residual)All other15% ad valoremCatch-all for notified supplies not in S.No. 1-5
🔶 NEW SECTION 8A — INSERTED vide Finance (No.2) Act 2024 | Notif. 17/2024-CT dt. 27.09.2024 | w.e.f. 01.11.2024
🔶 §8A — Power NOT to Recover Cess not levied / short-levied — as result of GENERAL PRACTICE

Exactly mirrors §8A of UTGST Act. If Government satisfied:
(a) Practice was/is generally prevalent regarding levy of CESS (including non-levy)
(b) Supplies liable to cess (not being levied) OR liable to HIGHER cess (being short-levied)

→ Government may, on Council recommendation, notify that: Such cess / excess cess shall NOT be required to be paid
💡 Example: Pan Masala manufacturers followed trade practice of computing cess on MRP net of discounts — Revenue now demands cess on full MRP for past 3 years. §8A can be used to waive such past demand if the erroneous practice was "generally prevalent."
🔍 GP Singh: "Generally prevalent" — a question of fact. The taxpayer invoking §8A protection must demonstrate that the practice was widespread in the industry — not just their individual practice. Evidence: trade circulars, industry association correspondence, parallel treatment by others.
📋 Cess Notification: Notif. 1/2017-Compensation Cess | Cess Rules 2017 | Amendment Notifications
§9–11 — Returns & Refunds | Compensation Fund | Other Cess Provisions
📋 §§9–11 — Returns, Fund Management & Cess Provisions
§9 — RETURNS, PAYMENTS, REFUNDS
Every taxable person making taxable supply under this Act shall:
(a) PAY cess
(b) FURNISH returns with GSTR filings under CGST Act
(c) APPLY for refunds of cess

CGST Act provisions for returns and refunds apply mutatis mutandis for cess
📝 No separate cess return forms. Cess is reported within the same GSTR forms. The cess column in GSTR-1 and GSTR-3B captures compensation cess separately.
§10 — GST COMPENSATION FUND
Non-lapsable fund in Public Account of India
Cess proceeds + other Council-recommended amounts credited here
§10(3): At end of transition period → 50% of unutilised Fund → Consolidated Fund of India | Remaining 50% → distributed to States in ratio of their total revenue from SGST/UTGST in last year of transition period

§10(3A): Interim distribution during transition period also possible on Council recommendation
🔶 §10(3A) — AMDT 2019
Inserted vide GSTCS Amendment Act 2018 (w.e.f. 01.02.2019): Allows Council to recommend interim utilisation of Fund even during transition period. Also: Shortfall recovery mechanism from Centre (50%) and States (50%) if Fund insufficient.
§11 — OTHER PROVISIONS RELATING TO CESS
(1) CGST Act provisions (assessment, ITC, non-levy, short-levy, interest, appeals, offences, penalties) apply mutatis mutandis to intra-State cess

(2) IGST Act provisions apply mutatis mutandis to inter-State cess

Proviso: ITC of cess → used ONLY towards payment of CESS. Cannot be used for CGST/SGST/IGST.
IMPORTANT
Cess ITC ≠ CGST ITC. Total ring-fencing. Cess credit can only offset cess liability.
§10(4)–(5) — CAG AUDIT — Fund accounts audited by CAG at specified intervals | Audit report placed before each House of Parliament — ensuring parliamentary oversight of cess fund utilisation
§12–14 — Rules, Laying & Removal of Difficulties
📜 §§12–14 — Legislative Infrastructure
§12 — RULE-MAKING
Central Govt on Council recommendation → rules for specific matters: base year revenue conditions | Fund refund manner | Cess levy manner & period | Returns & refund forms
§13 — LAYING OF RULES
Every rule → laid before each House of Parliament for 30 days (in session) | Both Houses may modify/annul | Earlier acts under that rule remain valid
§14 — REMOVAL OF DIFFICULTIES
Central Govt on Council recommendation → order in Official Gazette → remove difficulties
Time limit: 5 years from commencement
🔶 AMDT — Finance Act 2020
"Five years" substituted for "three years" — more time for difficulty-removal orders.
📙 PART C — CONSTITUTION (ONE HUNDRED AND FIRST AMENDMENT) ACT, 2016 (8th September 2016)
§1–2 — Art. 246A (New) | Dual Power to Make GST Laws
🏛️ Article 246A — Special Provision for GST | Constitutional Foundation
📝 Before this amendment, Article 246 gave Parliament exclusive power on Union List and States power on State List. GST required BOTH Centre and States to tax the SAME supply. Article 246A created a new concurrent-type power specifically for GST — overriding Articles 246 and 254.
ART. 246A(1) — DUAL POWER
Notwithstanding Art. 246 & 254 → Parliament AND Legislature of every State have power to make laws on GST imposed by Union or State
Applicable to all supplies except inter-State
🔍 GP Singh: "Notwithstanding" = overriding provision. Art. 246A trumps Art. 246 entirely for GST. Earlier concurrent list concepts under Art. 254 also displaced. This creates a unique constitutional framework — dual but coordinated taxation.
ART. 246A(2) — PARLIAMENT EXCLUSIVE for INTER-STATE
Parliament has EXCLUSIVE power to make laws on GST where supply takes place in course of INTER-STATE TRADE OR COMMERCE
📝 For inter-State supplies (IGST), only Parliament can legislate — States have no power. IGST Act is therefore purely a Central Act. States cannot levy their own inter-State GST.
💡 Maharashtra cannot pass a law taxing supply from Pune to Delhi — that's Parliament's domain via IGST Act. Maharashtra can only tax Pune to Nashik supply.
EXPLANATION TO ART. 246A
Provisions take effect for petroleum crude, HSD, petrol, natural gas, ATF (referred in Art. 279A(5)) → from date recommended by GST Council
[Petroleum products not yet brought under GST]
§§3–8 AMENDMENTS to Art. 248, 249, 250, 268, 268A, 269 — Consequential amendments: Art. 248 (residual power) qualified by 246A; Art. 249 & 250 (Parliament's national interest/Emergency powers) extended to GST; Art. 268 (duties of excise on medicinal preparations) — "duties of excise on medicinal and toilet preparations" words OMITTED; Art. 268A (service tax sharing) — OMITTED entirely; Art. 269 (goods consignment) — "except as provided in Art. 269A" inserted
§9 — Art. 269A (New) | Levy of GST on Inter-State Trade
🌐 Article 269A — IGST: Levy, Collection & Apportionment
ART. 269A(1) — LEVY BY CENTRE
GST on inter-State supplies → levied & collected by Government of India → apportioned between Union & States as Parliament may provide by law

Explanation: Import into India = deemed inter-State supply
📝 IGST is collected 100% by Centre then shared with States via formula. The Centre acts as collection agent for the whole of India — States don't collect IGST.
ART. 269A(2)–(4) — NOT PART OF CONSOLIDATED FUND
(2) Amount apportioned to State under (1) → NOT part of Consolidated Fund of India
(3) Where State SGST ITC used for paying IGST → such amount NOT part of CFI
(4) Where IGST ITC used for paying State SGST → NOT part of State Consolidated Fund
📝 Complex accounting: IGST is collected centrally but doesn't fully belong to Centre. Cross-utilisation of credits creates inter-government fund flows outside the normal Consolidated Fund mechanism.
ART. 269A(5) — PLACE OF SUPPLY
Parliament may by law formulate principles for determining PLACE OF SUPPLY when supply of goods/services takes place in course of inter-State trade or commerce
📝 This is the constitutional basis for IGST Act's Chapter IV (Sections 10–14) — Place of Supply Rules. Without Art. 269A(5), Parliament would have no constitutional authority to determine where inter-State supplies "happen."
💡 The entire Place of Supply framework — §10 (goods), §12 (services B2B), §13 (services B2C) of IGST Act — derives its constitutional validity from Art. 269A(5).
§12 — Art. 279A (New) | GST Council — Constitution & Powers
⚖️ Article 279A — GST Council — The Constitutional Heart of GST
📝 The GST Council is the apex decision-making body for all GST policy. It is constitutionally mandated — not just a statutory body. Its recommendations carry immense authority, though the Supreme Court (Mohit Minerals, 2022) held they are "recommendatory" not binding on Parliament/States.
ART. 279A(1) — CONSTITUTION
President constitutes GST Council within 60 days of commencement of 101st Amendment Act
ART. 279A(2) — COMPOSITION
(a) Union Finance Minister — Chairperson
(b) Union Minister of State (Revenue/Finance) — Member
(c) Minister in charge of Finance/Taxation from EACH STATE — Members
ART. 279A(4) — RECOMMENDATIONS
Council recommends on:
(a) Taxes to be subsumed
(b) Goods/services taxable/exempt
(c) GST model laws, IGST apportionment principles
(d) Threshold limits
(e) GST Rates (floor rates with bands)
(f) Special rates for natural calamity/disaster
(g) Special provisions for NE States, J&K, Himachal, Uttarakhand
(h) Any other GST matter
ART. 279A(9) — VOTING
3/4th majority of weighted votes of members present & voting
Central Govt vote weightage: 1/3 of total votes cast
All State Govts together: 2/3 of total votes cast
📝 Centre and States together must agree — Centre alone cannot outvote all States (1/3 vs 2/3). This ensures true federal cooperation. Any decision needs both Centre and majority of States on board.
🔍 GP Singh: "Majority of not less than 3/4" is a supermajority requirement. Even if Centre and some States agree, if 3/4 weighted threshold not crossed, recommendation is invalid. Courts can examine if the 3/4 threshold was met.
ART. 279A(11) — DISPUTE RESOLUTION MECHANISM
GST Council shall establish mechanism to adjudicate disputes between:
(a) Centre vs one or more States | (b) Centre & some States vs other States | (c) Two or more States
Arising from Council recommendations or their implementation
ART. 279A(5) — PETROLEUM PRODUCTS
Council to recommend the DATE on which GST levied on petroleum crude, HSD, petrol, natural gas, ATF
[Date not yet recommended as of Nov 2025]
🔶 SC Ruling — Mohit Minerals Pvt. Ltd. v. UoI [2022 SCC Online SC 657]
Supreme Court held: GST Council recommendations are NOT binding on Parliament or State Legislatures — they are "recommendatory" in nature. Parliament retains full legislative freedom to accept, modify or reject Council recommendations. The cooperative federal structure of GST does not create a "constitutional compulsion" to accept Council decisions verbatim.
§14, §17 — Art. 366 Definitions | 7th Schedule Amendments
📜 Art. 366 Insertions + 7th Schedule — Legislative Lists
ART. 366 — NEW DEFINITIONS
§14 inserts:
(12A): "goods and services tax" = any tax on supply of goods/services or both — EXCLUDING tax on supply of alcoholic liquor for human consumption
(26A): "Services" = anything other than goods
(26B): "State" for Arts 246A, 268, 269, 269A, 279A includes Union territory WITH Legislature (J&K, Delhi, Puducherry)
📝 Three key constitutional definitions. "Services" was never defined in Constitution before this. Now anything that is not goods = service. Alcoholic liquor explicitly excluded from GST definition itself — constitutional, not just statutory.
📖 Legal Dictionary: "Goods" was defined in Art. 366(12) as including all materials, commodities and articles. "Services" = everything else. This mutually exclusive definition is critical — no overlap between goods and services in constitutional sense.
7th SCHEDULE CHANGES — §17
UNION LIST (List I):
Entry 84 SUBSTITUTED: Duties of excise only on petroleum crude, HSD, petrol, gas, ATF, tobacco & tobacco products
Entries 92 (newspaper ad tax) & 92C (service tax) OMITTED

STATE LIST (List II):
Entry 52 (taxes on entry of goods) OMITTED
Entry 54 SUBSTITUTED: Taxes on sale of petroleum crude, HSD, petrol, gas, ATF, alcoholic liquor for human consumption
Entry 55 (taxes on advertisement) OMITTED
Entry 62 SUBSTITUTED: Taxes on entertainment — only to extent levied by Panchayat/Municipality/Regional/District Council
📝 The legislative lists were cleaned up — entries for taxes subsumed in GST were deleted/narrowed. Centre can no longer impose service tax (Entry 92C gone). States can no longer impose entry tax (Entry 52 gone) or ad tax (Entry 55 gone).
💡 Before GST: State could tax newspaper advertisements under Entry 55. After: Entry 55 OMITTED — newspaper ads now under GST (services) under Art. 246A. States cannot unilaterally tax them anymore.
§18–20 — COMPENSATION, TRANSITION & REMOVAL OF DIFFICULTIES
§18: Parliament must by law compensate States for revenue loss for 5 years [constitutional obligation — basis for Compensation Act]
§19: Pre-GST laws inconsistent with amended Constitution → continue until amended/repealed by competent authority OR 1 year from commencement (whichever earlier)
§20: President may by order remove difficulties within 3 years of assent
📝 §18 gave constitutional backing to the Compensation Act 2017. §19 ensured existing VAT/Service Tax laws weren't immediately unconstitutional — there was a transition period of 1 year to clean them up.
⚔️ Hearing Arsenal — UTGST + Compensation + Constitution 101st Amendment
These ratios and statutory principles are for use in personal hearings before GST adjudicating authorities, Appellate Authorities, and GSTAT. State them confidently and cite the provision/case.
§6(2)(b) UTGST — No Parallel Proceedings on Same Subject Matter
Statutory Provision | UTGST Act, 2017 | Applicable in shared CGST/UTGST officer situations
Key Principle
Where a proper officer under the CGST Act has initiated any proceedings on a subject matter, no proceedings shall be initiated by the proper officer under the UTGST Act on the same subject matter.
When to Use — When Department issues two sets of notices — one from CGST officer and one framed as UTGST notice — on the same supply/period/ITC claim. File a jurisdictional objection: §6(2)(b) bars the parallel proceeding. Only one proceeding can survive — whichever was initiated first.
§9(c) UTGST — Absolute Bar on Cross-Utilisation (UTGST ITC vs CGST)
Statutory Provision | UTGST Act §9(c) | Mandatory Rule — No Exceptions
Key Principle
Union territory tax shall not be utilised towards payment of central tax. This is not discretionary — it is a statutory prohibition. The ITC credit streams of CGST and UTGST are ring-fenced from each other.
When to Use — When Department demands interest/penalty alleging that assessee wrongly used UTGST ITC to offset CGST demand. Cite §9(c) to establish that such cross-use is statutorily void and the correct remedy is for assessee to pay CGST separately — but no penalty for a coding/filing error if the intent was bona fide.
§12(2) UTGST — No Interest on Wrong-Head Payment (IGST Paid Instead of CGST+UTGST)
Statutory Provision | UTGST Act §12(2) | Right of Taxpayer — Mandatory, not Discretionary
Key Principle
A registered person who has paid integrated tax on a transaction considered by him to be an inter-State supply, but which is subsequently held to be an intra-State supply, shall not be required to pay any interest on the amount of the central tax and the Union territory tax payable.
When to Use — When Department reclassifies inter-State supply as intra-State and demands CGST+UTGST with interest for the period. Cite §12(2): interest waiver is statutory — not an administrative concession. Department has no discretion to demand interest in this scenario. The taxpayer must pay the tax differential but interest cannot be levied.
§8A UTGST / §8A GST Compensation Act — General Practice Waiver (AMDT 2024)
Statutory Provision | Finance (No.2) Act 2024 | w.e.f. 01.11.2024 | Industry-Wide Protection
Key Principle
Where a practice was generally prevalent regarding levy of Union territory tax (including non-levy thereof) on any supply of goods or services or both, the Government may direct that the whole of the Union territory tax payable, but for the said practice, shall not be required to be paid in respect of supplies made following that practice.
When to Use — When demand relates to a period and practice that was industry-wide — e.g., non-levy on certain transactions where the entire trade followed the same approach. Argue: (a) the practice was generally prevalent (show trade circulars, parallel assessments of other taxpayers, industry body correspondence), (b) §8A gives Government power to waive such liability — file representation for notification under §8A. In hearing: cite §8A to challenge demand in limine pending §8A notification.
§11(1) GST Compensation Act — Cess ITC Ring-Fencing
Statutory Provision | GST Compensation to States Act 2017 §11 | Proviso — Mandatory
Key Principle
The input tax credit in respect of cess on supply of goods and services leviable under section 8 shall be utilised only towards payment of said cess on supply of goods or services leviable under the said section.
When to Use — When assessee has accumulated cess ITC and wants to use it against CGST/IGST liability, OR when Department alleges that cess ITC was wrongly used against non-cess liability and demands penalty. Cite §11 proviso: cess ITC is completely ring-fenced — it cannot lawfully pay CGST or IGST. The error (if any) is technical, not fraudulent.
Article 279A(9) — GST Council Decision-Making | 3/4th Majority Requirement
Constitutional Provision | Art. 279A(9) | Constitution of India
Key Principle
Every decision of the Goods and Services Tax Council shall be taken at a meeting by a majority of not less than three-fourths of the weighted votes of the members present and voting. The vote of the Central Government shall have a weightage of one-third of the total votes cast, and the votes of all the State Governments taken together shall have a weightage of two-thirds of the total votes cast.
When to Use — When challenging validity of a GST notification/rate change on ground that it was not supported by proper Council recommendation. Argument: if the Council meeting records do not show 3/4 majority was achieved, the "recommendation" is constitutionally defective — and the notification based on that recommendation is ultra vires.
Mohit Minerals Pvt. Ltd. v. Union of India — Council Recommendations are Recommendatory
Supreme Court of India | 2022 SCC Online SC 657 | 9-Judge Bench Constitution Reference
Key Principle
The recommendations of the GST Council are the product of a collaborative dialogue between the Union and States. Parliament and State Legislatures are not constitutionally obligated to enact legislation in terms of the recommendations of the GST Council. The GST Council recommendations are recommendatory and not binding.
When to Use — When Department argues that a GST Council recommendation automatically creates a legal obligation and the taxpayer cannot challenge the resulting notification. Counter: Council recommendations are not law — the notification itself must be tested for vires under Art. 246A. The recommendation is a pre-condition for the notification, not a substitute for independent legislative validity.
Art. 246A — Concurrent GST Power | Art. 269A — IGST: Constitutional Basis
Constitutional Provisions | Constitution (101st Amendment) Act 2016
Key Principle for Jurisdictional Arguments
Article 246A — Notwithstanding anything contained in articles 246 and 254, Parliament, and, subject to clause (2), the Legislature of every State, have power to make laws with respect to goods and services tax. Parliament has EXCLUSIVE power to make laws with respect to goods and services tax where the supply takes place in the course of inter-State trade or commerce.
When to Use — In disputes about whether a supply is intra-State or inter-State: cite Art. 246A(2) — only Parliament can tax inter-State supplies. If State SGST officer demands SGST on what is actually an inter-State supply (which was already subjected to IGST), raise the constitutional bar: §12(1) CGST / §12(1) UTGST plus Art. 246A(2). State has no jurisdiction over inter-State supplies.
§18(1) UTGST — 90-Day Limit for ITC Carryforward | Strict Interpretation
Statutory Provision | UTGST Act §18(1) | Transitional ITC Disputes
Key Principle
A registered person shall be entitled to take, in his electronic credit ledger, credit of the amount of Value Added Tax and Entry Tax carried forward in the return relating to the period ending with the day immediately preceding the appointed day, furnished by him under the existing law, not later than ninety days after the said day. Credit not claimed within 90 days is permanently lost.
When to Use — In transitional ITC disputes (Tran-1 cases). Taxpayer argument: if Tran-1 was filed within 90 days, the credit is valid — Department cannot disallow on ground of technical errors in filing as long as substantive eligibility is established. Conversely: if 90-day deadline missed (no GSTR-3B/Tran-1 filing), credit is permanently barred — this is the statutory cut-off (as held by various HCs).
§21 UTGST + Art. 246A — CGST Framework Applies to UT; No Separate UT Procedure
Statutory Provision | UTGST Act §21 | Procedural Uniformity
Key Principle
The provisions of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act relating to assessment, refunds, audit, inspection, search, seizure and arrest, demands and recovery, liability to pay, advance ruling, appeals and revision, offences and penalties, shall apply mutatis mutandis in relation to Union territory tax as they apply in relation to central tax. All procedural and substantive rights available under CGST Act flow equally into UTGST proceedings.
When to Use — When a UTGST officer claims a different procedure or relaxes/tightens CGST-analogous provisions in UTGST proceedings. Counter: §21 mandates that CGST procedures apply mutatis mutandis — the taxpayer has the same rights of adjudication, appeal, and revision in UTGST proceedings as in CGST. A deviation from CGST procedure in UTGST proceedings is an error of law.
For professional litigation use | Pawan Soni & Associates, Ichalkaranji | CA Pawankumar Soni | Master Prompt v3.0
